lead: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 大手町本店
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 大手町本店
name_en: Shirunashi Tantanmen Senmon Kingken Otemachi Honten
cuisine: 広島汁なし担担麺（汁なし担担麺専門店）
neighborhood: 広島市中区大手町（中電前駅・原爆ドーム/平和記念公園の南、徒歩圏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町3-3-14 武本ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町3-3-14 returned [132.453842, 34.388691], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in Otemachi, central Hiroshima just south of the Peace Memorial Park, consistent with the stated location.
seats_text: About 20 seats (counter-style, small shop).
hours_raw: Tabelog listing: 11:00-20:00 (Mon-Sat & holidays); closed Sunday. Note: other listings show split hours of 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-20:00 on weekdays and 11:00-15:00 on Sat/holidays — hours may vary, confirm on the day.
closed_days: Sundays (occasional irregular closures).
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, famous specialty noodle counter where you order a single signature dish and mix it vigorously yourself; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu are enough, but it is fast-paced and not set up for detailed allergy questions in English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is built on wheat ramen-style noodles (sourced from a Hatsukaichi noodle maker) plus a custom soy sauce and sesame-paste tare, all of which contain wheat. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature shirunashi tantanmen is topped with pork mince (豚ミンチ) and uses a soy-sauce/sesame-based seasoning; no plant-based version is documented.
caveats: Hours vary between published sources (continuous 11:00-20:00 vs split lunch/dinner service); confirm before visiting. Single-dish specialty shop with no vegan or gluten-free accommodation documented.

bio.background: The Otemachi flagship opened in November 2011 in central Hiroshima, just south of the Peace Memorial Park, and grew into one of the shops that carried Hiroshima's soupless dandan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen) to national fame, later expanding to more than a dozen branches across Tokyo, Osaka, Fukuoka and beyond.
bio.specialty: Shirunashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles): medium-thin crimped wheat noodles dressed in a sesame-paste and custom soy-sauce tare with chili oil, pork mince and green onion, finished with their signature Sichuan pepper.
bio.philosophy: A near-obsession with aroma and freshness: the founder fell for the citrus-like fragrance of fresh Sichuan peppercorns on a trip to Chengdu, and the shop grinds its own blend of red and green sansho fresh every single morning at each location, replacing it daily because they believe the fragrance fades over time. Noodles are made by an established Hatsukaichi noodle maker and the soy sauce was developed together with a local brewer.

anecdotes.text: The founder discovered fresh Sichuan peppercorns at a market in Chengdu around the time of opening and was struck by their citrus-like, fruity aroma, which became the heart of the dish.
Part of the ritual is stirring the noodles vigorously about 30 times before eating to coat them evenly, a step the shop turned into a playful signature of the experience.

The shop is credited as one of the first to make Hiroshima's soupless dandan noodles known nationwide, pairing noodles from an established Hatsukaichi maker with a soy sauce custom-developed with a local brewing company.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish uses wheat noodles plus a wheat-containing soy-sauce and sesame tare; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop uses a soy sauce custom-developed with a local brewing company; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature noodles are topped with pork mince and seasoned with a sesame/soy tare, so the dish is not plant-based.

lead: 汁なし担担麺 味源
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 味源
cuisine: 広島汁なし担担麺（brothless dan dan noodles）
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodle shop. The noodles are wheat-based and the sauce relies on soy sauce and sesame, so the core dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Dan dan noodles here are made with minced pork and a soy/sesame-based seasoning, so they are not vegan. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀13-16
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 132.462784, lat 34.395016 — within the central Hiroshima box, near Hatchobori.
seats_text: 18 counter seats; compact storefront that opens into a slightly larger back area.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00, 17:30-20:00; Sat-Sun 11:00-15:00 (weekends lunch only). Sources differ slightly on the evening close time.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a ticket vending machine at the entrance, so little Japanese is needed, but the machine and menu are in Japanese and no English support is documented, so a phrasebook or translation app helps.
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, near Hatchobori Station)

bio.background: A small brothless dan dan noodle shop that opened in November 2022 in Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, run by the owner with a small staff. Behind a modest white storefront with red lanterns and glass doors, the space opens into a tidy back room lined with counter seats.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen) built on a sesame-oil base, with crushed nuts adding texture and a restrained, balanced level of spice and Sichuan-pepper numbness. Diners can dial up the heat themselves using a flower-Sichuan-pepper dispenser at the counter.
bio.philosophy: A direct, unembellished approach that prizes clean, well-executed classic technique over flashy originality, with affordable bowls around 650 yen.

anecdotes.text: Local food writers single it out as one of Hiroshima's best simple, refined takes on brothless dan dan noodles, with effective crushed-nut texture and a sesame-oil foundation.
On Tuesdays the shop offers complimentary half-rice to pair with the noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is a brothless dan dan noodle made with wheat noodles and a soy/sesame seasoning, which conventionally contains wheat-based soy sauce.

caveats: Evening hours vary slightly between sources (one lists 17:30-20:00, another 17:00-21:00); the shop has irregular closures. Phone number is not publicly listed. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations are documented and the core dish contains wheat noodles, soy sauce, sesame, nuts, and pork.

lead: オイスターバー MABUI 袋町
name_ja: オイスターバー MABUI 袋町
cuisine: 牡蠣／オイスターバー（生牡蠣・牡蠣料理、イタリアン・シーフード）
gf_detail: This is an oyster bar serving raw oysters and oyster dishes such as garlic butter saute, ahijo, and oyster gratin, alongside Italian and seafood plates. Cooked dishes commonly use wheat-based ingredients and sauces, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodation is documented. Raw oysters themselves are naturally gluten-free, but cross-contact in a shared kitchen has not been addressed, so a celiac diner would need to ask in person and exercise caution.
vegan_detail: The entire concept centers on oysters and seafood, with dishes built around shellfish, butter, cheese, sea urchin, and other animal products. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and the kitchen is not suitable for a strict plant-based diner.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町2-26 カッパーハウス 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区袋町2-26 via GSI; result lands in central Hiroshima within the expected box (near 本通/立町, a few minutes from the A-Bomb Dome area).
neighborhood: 広島市中区袋町（本通駅・立町駅から徒歩3〜5分、本通周辺）
seats_text: Counter and sofa seating plus table seats (about 20 at tables) and a loft/attic-style area seating up to 6; official site lists roughly 40 seats while the listing platform shows up to 50. Use ~40 as the working figure.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri: 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–24:00 (L.O. food 23:00 / drinks 23:30); Sat/Sun/Holidays: 11:30–24:00. The official site states the kitchen runs through to midnight with lunch from 11:30 and dinner from 17:00; effectively open daily year-round.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays); generally open daily / year-round per the official site.
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, casual oyster bar where a foreign student can comfortably point at menu items and order a tasting set; a few words of Japanese help, but English support is not documented so come ready to gesture and smile.
caveats: Seat count and exact hours differ slightly between the official site and listing pages; treat hours as effectively daily 11:30 to midnight with a possible weekday afternoon break, and confirm any irregular closures before visiting. Dinner courses with all-you-can-drink run roughly 6,000–9,000 yen, so a meal here typically exceeds a budget visit.

bio.background: An oyster bar tucked into the Fukuromachi quarter of central Hiroshima, a few minutes' walk from the Hondori shopping arcade and within easy reach of the city's central sights. The kitchen keeps fresh raw oysters on hand all year, drawing on Hiroshima's celebrated local beds as well as oysters from elsewhere in Japan and abroad.
bio.specialty: Raw oysters served on the half shell with seven to eight varieties available at any given time, alongside a 'tasting flight' comparison set, and signature cooked plates such as garlic-butter sauteed oysters, oyster-and-mushroom ahijo, and an oyster-uni-spinach gratin.
bio.philosophy: A year-round celebration of Hiroshima oysters: shucked to order so guests can taste the differences between regions and growers side by side.

anecdotes.text: The bar markets itself as a place where you can eat fresh raw oysters every day of the year, keeping 7-8 varieties on the counter at once and offering a tasting set so guests can compare them.
A local Hiroshima media feature highlighted it as a spot where you can enjoy raw oysters year-round, including at lunch.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（中南米テイスト融合の鉄板焼き）
gf_detail: 広島風お好み焼きは小麦粉の生地（クレープ状の薄焼き）と中華そばが必須の料理で、本質的にグルテンを含みます。トッピングや鉄板焼きソースにも小麦由来の醤油・ソースが使われるため、グルテンフリー対応の情報は確認できません。セリアック病の方には適しません。
vegan_detail: 看板メニューの「肉玉そば」をはじめ卵・豚肉・中華そば・かつお出汁・お好みソース（魚介・小麦由来）を使うため、ヴィーガン対応の情報は確認できません。名物のハラペーニョトッピングやトルティーヤチップス等の中南米系サイドメニューはありますが、店としてヴィーガン対応を謳ってはいません。来店前に要確認。
address_ja: 広島県広島市西区楠木町1-7-13
geocode_note: 国土地理院ジオコーダーで番地レベルの座標を取得。広島市内ボックス内（lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50）に収まる。横川駅の南東、原爆ドームの北方に位置。
seats_text: 17席（カウンター・鉄板を囲むスタイルの小規模店）
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日（年末年始 12/30-1/3 休業）
cultural_comfort_note: 店主はグアテマラ出身で英語・スペイン語も通じやすく、店内に世界地図を掲げて旅行者と交流するため、外国人留学生でも基本的な日本語の挨拶があれば気軽に立ち寄れます。
caveats: 営業時間が曜日で変則的（火・金はランチのみ、月・水・木はディナーのみ、土日祝休）なので訪問曜日に注意。支払い方法は情報源により食い違いがあり（現金のみとする情報と、カード・QR可とする情報の両方が存在）、現金を用意しておくのが安全。公式サイトは確認できなかった。

bio.chef_name: フェルナンド・ロペス（Fernando Lopez）
bio.origin: グアテマラ（グアテマラシティ、1963年生まれ）
bio.background: 内戦下のグアテマラに生まれ育ち、高校卒業後に出国。米ニューオーリンズの叔父のもとでイタリア料理店・フランス料理店で修業し、包丁さばきや肉の扱いといった料理の基礎を身につけた。ホノルルの高級ホテルでの一時的な仕事で広島出身の真喜子さんと出会って結婚し、1995年に広島へ移住。その後、広島の老舗お好み焼き店『八昌』で修業して独立し、2000年にJR横川駅近くで自身の店を開いた。
bio.specialty: 広島風お好み焼き。看板は『肉玉そば』。他店にはない中南米由来の青唐辛子ハラペーニョのトッピングが名物で、ファヒータやトルティーヤチップスといった中南米系のサイドメニューも揃える。
bio.philosophy: 店内に世界地図を掲げ、自国グアテマラを指し示すことで旅行者と対話する。『戦争になってからでは遅い。広島に来た人が、自分の国が正しい方向に進んでいるのかを裁ける人になってほしい』と語り、被爆地ヒロシマの記憶と、戦争の根本原因への理解を伝えることを大切にしている。

anecdotes.text: 内戦下のグアテマラで育った店主は『毎週のように校門にばらばらの遺体が捨てられていた』と少年時代を振り返り、25歳まで生きられないと思い出国を決意した。広島出身の妻と結婚し、被爆者である義両親や妻のキリスト教コミュニティを通じてヒロシマの被爆の記憶に触れていった。
薬研堀の名店『八昌』で修業して独立し、横川駅近くで20年以上にわたり店を営む。ミシュランガイド広島でビブグルマンに掲載された評判の店。
他店では見られないハラペーニョのトッピングが業界に新風を吹き込み、グアテマラ出身店主ならではの個性として注目されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 広島風お好み焼きは小麦粉の生地と中華そばが基本で、同一の鉄板で複数の品を調理するため、グルテンの交差汚染は避けられない。グルテンフリー対応の記載は確認できなかった。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: お好み焼きには小麦・魚介由来のお好みソースが使われるのが一般的で、本品も例外ではないと考えられる。小麦不使用ソースの提供情報は確認できなかった。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 看板メニューに豚肉・卵・中華そば・かつお出汁が使われ、同じ鉄板で肉や卵を焼くため、植物性のみの調理を分離している情報は確認できなかった。

positives.text: 名物のハラペーニョトッピングやトルティーヤチップス等の中南米系サイドメニューがあり、ベジタリアン/ヴィーガンが食べられる品があるか店主に直接相談しやすい（店主は英語・スペイン語も通じやすい）。

lead: クロワッサンマルシェ
name_ja: クロワッサンマルシェ
cuisine: Macrobiotic vegan / organic natural foods (週替わりマクロビランチ, organic pesticide-free vegetables)
gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, but they sell a gluten-free bread (グルテンフリー食パン) made with rice flour (米粉) instead of wheat. The macrobiotic lunch sets are rice-based (fermented brown rice / 酵素玄米, switching to plain brown rice when sold out) with vegetable sides, so the core lunch is naturally largely rice-based — but individual side dishes may use wheat-containing seasonings (e.g. soy sauce / miso). No published dedicated-fryer or cross-contamination information; ask staff directly about specific dishes.
vegan_detail: The daily lunches are completely vegan (動物性不使用) — two macrobiotic options served on weekdays (around 1,000 yen + tax): a macrobiotic plate (one main plus four vegetable sides with fermented brown rice and gomashio) and a macrobiotic curry plate (red-lentil and seasonal vegetable curry, mild, with a lentil-quinoa salad). Important caveat: the shop also sells packaged natural-food/bento items, and some bento occasionally contain animal products following macrobiotic yin-yang philosophy, so check labels on retail items. The sit-down lunches themselves are vegan.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堺町1-3-4-101
geocode_note: Geocoded from chōme-level address 広島県広島市中区堺町1-3-4 via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box and match the Sakaicho location just west of Honkawa-bashi, a few minutes from Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 広島市中区堺町 (Sakaicho, Naka-ku) — a few minutes west of the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome via Honkawa-bashi bridge; nearest tram stop Dobashi (土橋) on the Hiroden Eba line.
seats_text: About 6 counter seats; a small natural-food shop with a compact eat-in counter.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warmly-run family shop where some basic Japanese (or a translation app) helps, but staff have welcomed international visitors before, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the day's lunch will get you far.

bio.chef_name: 信濃 克爾 (Shinano Katsutoshi) and 信濃 由佳 (Shinano Yuka)
bio.background: Croissant Marche is run by a married couple, Katsutoshi and Yuka Shinano. Their journey began in 1982 when they opened a general goods store called 'Croissant no Mise'; conversations with customers inspired them to create a place built around health-conscious food. They opened the original Croissant Marche in 2006 in Nishi Ward, then relocated in 2014 to the current spot near Peace Memorial Park for better visibility, becoming one of Hiroshima's pioneering plant-based eateries.
bio.philosophy: Their guiding idea is 'a handmade shop without animal products.' Yuka, who could not comfortably eat animal foods, found macrobiotics transformative after her husband took an interest in brown rice — they describe realizing they didn't need to force themselves to eat animal products. They eat fermented brown rice daily and credit their energy to this diet, sourcing vegetables from local farms grown with minimal pesticides in a local-to-table spirit.
bio.specialty: Weekly-changing macrobiotic vegan lunch plates built on fermented brown rice (酵素玄米) with gomashio, plus a red-lentil seasonal-vegetable curry; also house-made breads including a rice-flour gluten-free loaf.

anecdotes.text: The husband was reported to still be working actively at the counter at around 80 years old, the couple attributing their vitality to their plant-based, brown-rice-centered diet.

Their clientele spans ages from 10 to 80, predominantly women, with a growing number of health-conscious younger customers, and before COVID they regularly served international visitors near the Peace Park.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sit-down lunches are completely vegan, but the retail bento and packaged goods sold in the shop occasionally contain animal products following macrobiotic yin-yang principles, so check labels before buying take-out items.

positives.text: Daily lunches are entirely free of animal products and built on local organic, minimal-pesticide vegetables and fermented brown rice.
They sell a gluten-free bread made with rice flour in place of wheat.

caveats: Gluten-free is option-level, not dedicated: a rice-flour GF bread is sold and the lunch base is brown rice, but vegetable sides may use wheat-bearing seasonings and no cross-contamination protocol is published — confirm specific dishes with staff. Retail bento may contain animal products even though the eat-in lunches are vegan. Hours/closed-days and 6-seat counter are confirmed; phone 082-234-8133. Payment method not explicitly published; assumed cash-only for a small independent natural-food shop — verify on arrival.

cuisine: Seafood diner — anago (conger eel) rice and Hiroshima oyster dishes, cafe-style casual
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (next to the post office), within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町590-5
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chome-level point in Miyajima-cho; lands on Miyajima island as expected, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Approximately 50 seats across two floors; tatami seating on the first floor and table seating on the second, plus some counter seating. Child high chairs available.
hours_raw: 11:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:30); hours vary by season (季節により時間変更あり). Some sources list 11:00–21:00.
closed_days: Listed as no fixed closing day (無休), though some sources note irregular closures (不定休). Seasonal hour changes apply.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, family-friendly tourist-area diner where pointing at the photo menu and a few polite words of Japanese will get you a warm welcome, though staff may not speak much English.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature anago (conger eel) rice and oyster dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce and sweet tare, fried oyster sets are breaded in wheat flour, and curry roux also contains wheat — so the core menu is not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood specialist built around conger eel and oysters; menus center on fish, shellfish, and pork (kone-yaki), with wheat-soy sauces throughout. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: The chef-supervision claim (menu by 藤井恵) appeared only in a single aggregator extract and was not corroborated by independent sources, so it is omitted from the diner-facing story. Hours vary seasonally — confirm before a late visit.

bio.background: A cafe-style local diner sitting right in the middle of Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, next to the post office and an easy walk from Itsukushima Shrine. The two-floor space has a warm wooden interior with tatami seating downstairs and tables upstairs, and it has earned a following among women, families, and solo travelers for being unfussy and welcoming.
bio.specialty: Unusually for the island, it serves both of Miyajima's signature ingredients under one roof: grilled conger eel (anago) over rice and Hiroshima oysters prepared as fried sets, rice bowls, and even oyster-cutlet curry. The anago-meshi set and the Miyajima set are the dishes people come for.
bio.philosophy: A quick, comfortable stop for sightseers who want to taste the island's specialties without a long wait — dishes are known for arriving fast.

anecdotes.text: On winter firework nights, when many island restaurants have already closed, this place stays open late and draws long queues of visitors looking for a warm meal.
It is one of the rare spots on Miyajima that puts both anago and oyster dishes on the same menu, where the two specialties are usually sold separately.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago rice and oyster bowls are seasoned with sweet soy-based tare/soy sauce, which in standard Japanese cooking is wheat-based.

cuisine: Wagashi (Japanese confectionery) — momiji manjū and the original deep-fried "age-momiji"
neighborhood: Miyajima / Omotesando shopping street (walking distance from Itsukushima Shrine)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町448-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: The official site lists no eat-in seating at the main shop (a small 4-seat eat-in space is only at the separate Kaigandori-ten branch); a listing elsewhere cites 30 seats, so seating at the honten is unconfirmed. It functions mostly as a takeaway / food-stand confectionery.
hours_raw: 9:00〜17:30頃（※季節により変わります）
closed_days: 不定休（要問合せ／繁忙期は基本営業）— irregular holidays; generally open during peak tourist periods
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, tourist-friendly walk-up confectionery stand on Miyajima's main street; you can point and pay easily, though deep menu questions are best in basic Japanese.
gf_detail: Momiji manjū are made from a wheat-flour castella-style batter, and the signature age-momiji is that same manjū deep-fried in a wheat-flour tempura-style batter, so the core products contain wheat gluten and are not gluten-free. No gluten-free option is documented.
vegan_detail: The classic fillings center on sweet red-bean paste (anko), which can be plant-based, but the castella-style manjū batter typically contains egg (and often dairy/honey), and the shop offers cream/chocolate/cheese fillings too. No item is documented as certified vegan, and the fried items share fryers with non-vegan batters, so a strict vegan should ask directly.

bio.background: Founded in 1912 (Meiji 45) by Yohei and Tora Takeuchi, this Miyajima shop began by making thin-skinned manjū and mochi cakes and has stayed in its original spot on the island for over a century, passing through five generations of the Takeuchi family.
bio.specialty: Momiji manjū and the original deep-fried 'age-momiji'
bio.philosophy: The family describes its work as balancing inherited tradition with new ideas — preserving the craft of momiji manjū while proposing fresh ways for visitors to enjoy it, with artisans united by the wish that every customer eats a delicious manjū.

anecdotes.text: On January 1, 2001, the shop debuted deep-fried momiji manjū — developed by experimenting with a batter to coat their fluffy castella-style cake — and it became one of Miyajima's defining street-food specialties, making this the birthplace of age-momiji.
To keep control of quality the family expanded into making its own bean paste (an anko works opened in 2023) and later a cafe-gallery concept, while keeping the historic main shop on the island.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature item is deep-fried; fried sweet items share frying oil, so plant-based items cannot be guaranteed free of contact with egg/dairy-containing batters.

caveats: Main shop has no documented eat-in seating (mainly takeaway/walk-up); hours are seasonal and holidays are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. A separate seat count (30) appears on a third-party listing but is not confirmed for the honten.

cuisine: あなごめし・牡蠣料理（和食）／ Anago-meshi (conger eel rice) and oyster dishes, Japanese cuisine
neighborhood: 宮島・町家通り（厳島神社近く）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町532
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 広島県廿日市市宮島町532, landing on Miyajima island near Machiya-dori; within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 35席（2階に団体・グループ向けの座敷あり）
closed_days: 月曜日（Mondays）。臨時休業あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly local lunch spot on a quieter Miyajima street where the menu and an English website exist, so a foreign student with a few words of Japanese (and pointing at the anago-meshi set) will get by comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature anago-meshi and oyster dishes are seasoned with Japanese soy sauce / tare, which contain wheat, and fried items (oyster cutlet) use wheat batter/breading. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. The restaurant is built around conger eel (fish) and oysters (shellfish); even the rice sets use fish-based tare/soy seasoning. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: Miyajima Ichiwa sits on Machiya-dori, the old townhouse street a few minutes inland from the Miyajima ferry pier and a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine, away from the busier main shopping lanes. It is a Japanese restaurant devoted to the island's two best-known specialties: anago-meshi (grilled conger eel over rice) and oyster dishes.
bio.specialty: Anago-meshi made with purely domestic, wild-caught conger eel, and dishes featuring Miyajima oysters; signature plates include the anago-meshi set, the asari (clam) and anago rice set, and the 'Ichiwa set' pairing anago rice with a fried oyster cutlet.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Miyajima's local cuisine using regional ingredients, with a spacious second-floor tatami room for groups and the island's catered bento delivery service.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen highlights 'purely domestic natural conger eel,' which it describes as synonymous with Miyajima cuisine, alongside 'pure Miyajima oysters.'
An original dish on the menu is the 'asari anago-meshi' set, combining clams with the conger-eel rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi and the rice sets are seasoned with Japanese soy-sauce-based tare, which standardly contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on conger eel and oysters with fish-based seasonings; no vegan or vegetarian items are listed.

caveats: Tabelog and the official site differ slightly on hours (Tabelog: lunch 11:30-15:00/16:00; official site lists 11:30-16:30); evening service (around 17:00-21:00) appears to run only on certain days (Thu-Sat) and for groups, so hours are treated as irregular. Dietary safety confidence is low because no allergen, GF, or vegan information is published; the no-GF / no-vegan assessment is inferred from the documented anago/oyster/soy-sauce menu.

lead: わたしの食卓 紙屋町店
name_ja: わたしの食卓 紙屋町店
cuisine: 定食・食堂（家庭的な和食定食）/ Home-style Japanese set meals (teishoku)
neighborhood: 中区・紙屋町／本通（原爆ドーム・平和記念公園の東側、徒歩圏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町2丁目2-6
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned first feature coordinates [132.457108, 34.39431], within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50).
seats_text: 28 seats, counter-style casual seating
closed_days: No regular closing day (無休); closes for occasional staff training, Obon, and New Year holidays (announced in advance)
cultural_comfort_note: You buy a meal ticket from a Japanese-only machine at the entrance and then pick up your tray at the counter, so a translation app or a quick look at the photo menu posted outside helps; staff are friendly but communication is mostly in Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a home-style teishoku diner where dishes are seasoned with regular soy sauce and miso (both contain wheat), and fried items like chicken nanban and karaage use wheat-based batter and shared fryers. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is indicated.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on meat and fish set meals (chicken nanban, karaage, mackerel simmered in miso, pork kimchi) and uses fish-based dashi and soy sauce throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: Watashi no Shokutaku is a casual, self-service set-meal diner in central Hiroshima, opened in December 2010, just steps from the Hondori shopping arcade and within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. It is one of a small handful of locations operated by the same family company within the city.
bio.specialty: Affordable, all-made-from-scratch Japanese set meals (teishoku) for under about 1,000 yen, with rice available as either white or mixed grain. The signature is the chicken nanban set, alongside karaage, miso-simmered mackerel, and pork kimchi sets.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach is body-friendly, home-style cooking made entirely in-house, with the simple aim of serving warm, satisfying rice-based meals at a fair price.

anecdotes.text: Diners buy a ticket at the door and collect their tray when it is called, a streamlined self-service style praised for quick turnover that makes it easy to drop in even on a busy lunch break.
Local diners note the chicken breast set is prepared to stay tender with a lightly spicy seasoning, and that the spot is comfortable for solo office workers.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals such as miso-simmered mackerel and pork kimchi rely on regular soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat.

caveats: Self-service ticket-machine diner, cash only. Geocode and core facts (address, phone, 28 seats, 11:00-21:00 daily, opened Dec 2010, signature chicken nanban set under 1,000 yen) confirmed via Tabelog and a May 2026 local food article. No official website found and no allergen/dietary information is published, so GF and vegan suitability is assessed as not accommodated rather than confirmed safe; the safety section is sparse because no specific allergen-handling details are documented.

cuisine: 日本料理・割烹（瀬戸内の魚介、ふぐ、牡蠣、季節の会席）
neighborhood: 中区三川町（本通・八丁堀の中心商店街エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区三川町10-26
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box; location matches central Nakaku Mikawa-cho near the Hondori/Hacchobori shopping arcade, walkable to Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: Approximately 80 seats total: counter, table seating, sunken-hearth tatami rooms, and private rooms accommodating from 2 to 30 guests across multiple floors.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:15); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Open year-round except part of the year-end/New Year period (closed 12/31 through midday 1/4; reopens evening of 1/4).
closed_days: Open year-round; closed only for part of the year-end/New Year holidays (12/31 to midday 1/4).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a long-established, somewhat formal kaiseki restaurant where some Japanese language ability and familiarity with traditional dining etiquette will help; calling ahead to reserve a private room is wise for a comfortable experience.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The cuisine relies heavily on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and ponzu, plus tempura/fried items, with no documented gluten-free menu or accommodations. A celiac diner could not eat here safely without extensive negotiation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around Seto Inland Sea seafood — fugu (pufferfish), oysters, anago (conger eel), sashimi and fish-based broths — and uses fish/dashi and wheat-soy seasonings throughout. No documented vegan or vegetarian set menu.

bio.background: A long-established Japanese kaiseki house in the heart of Hiroshima, tucked into a side street off the bustling central shopping arcade between Hondori and Hacchobori, a short walk from Peace Memorial Park. The restaurant has been welcoming guests for well over half a century, and is set across several floors with counter seating, sunken-hearth tatami rooms, and private rooms sized from intimate pairs to large parties.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Seto Inland Sea seafood — fugu (pufferfish) courses with house-made plum paste and ponzu, year-round oyster courses popular with out-of-prefecture visitors, anago (conger eel), and sashimi — alongside mountain ingredients from the Chugoku region. The kitchen makes its own soy sauce and ponzu.
bio.philosophy: Rather than avant-garde experimentation, the kitchen honors traditional preparation to draw the maximum flavor from premium ingredients, pairing dishes with house-made condiments and serving each at its optimal moment. The house emphasizes omotenashi — attentive, refined hospitality in a calm adult atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: Year-round oyster courses are a particular draw for visitors traveling to Hiroshima from outside the prefecture, served alongside the restaurant's signature Seto Inland Sea fugu.
Fugu and anago are served with the house's own handmade umeboshi paste and ponzu, reflecting a philosophy of enhancing rather than overshadowing the ingredient.

caveats: Address verified as 中区三川町10-26 (Mikawa-cho) on both Tabelog and the official site — slightly different from the "袋町" descriptor in the brief, but the same central Hondori/Hacchobori arcade district. Specific founding year not documented; sources state "over 50/60 years" of operation. Chef/owner name not published on the pages reviewed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on traditional Japanese seasonings including house-made soy sauce and ponzu, which are wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fish, fugu, oysters and dashi-based preparations, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive throughout the menu.

cuisine: 汁なし担々麺（魚介ダレ／ごまだれ選択、辛さ調整可）
neighborhood: 中区・鉄砲町／八丁堀
gf_detail: This is a brothless dan dan noodle (汁なし担々麺) shop. Wheat noodles are the core of every dish, and the dishes are dressed in sesame or seafood-based sauces that conventionally contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The food centers on dan dan noodles with pork mince, a fish/seafood-based sauce option (元祖魚介タレ), and side bowls like roasted-pork-and-egg rice (焼豚玉子飯) and mapo tofu rice. No documented vegan or plant-based options. Not suitable for vegan diners.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町8-24 にしたやビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the building-level address (鉄砲町8-24) via GSI; within the Hiroshima box, in central Naka Ward near the Hatchobori tram area.
seats_text: 13 seats (7 counter, 6 table)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 13-seat counter-and-table noodle shop with a Japanese-only menu and cash-only payment, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the posted menu and have cash on hand.
caveats: Cash only (no cards, e-money, or QR pay). Tiny 13-seat shop with no exterior sign, so it is easy to walk past. Irregular closing days. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: A small, independent brothless dan dan noodle (汁なし担々麺) shop that opened in March 2022 on a main street in Hiroshima's Naka Ward, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop. The storefront is modest and famously carries no exterior sign, giving it a hidden-gem feel even though it sits right on a busy street; the menu is posted outside so diners can choose before stepping in.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodles, where you choose between an original seafood-based sauce (元祖魚介タレ) and a rich sesame sauce (ごまだれ), with adjustable spice and Sichuan-pepper (山椒) numbing levels. The kitchen also turns out generous, budget-friendly set meals pairing the noodles with bowls like roasted-pork-and-egg rice (焼豚玉子飯) and mapo tofu rice for around 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop has no sign out front and even posts a lighthearted apology for it, leaning into its quiet, easy-to-miss charm despite a prime street-facing spot.

A local reviewer ordered the 950-yen set pairing gapao rice with sesame-sauce dan dan noodles at numbing-level 3, finding the creamy sauce mellowed the heat and that spooning the gapao rice in at the end gave a surprising, almost cheese-like richness.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop specializes in wheat-noodle brothless dan dan noodles dressed in sesame or seafood-based sauces; such Japanese sauces conventionally contain wheat-based soy sauce, though no allergen statement is published.

cuisine: 老舗喫茶店（Showa-era kissaten／coffee shop） — homemade bread, morning sets, napolitan, curry, gratin
neighborhood: 中区・大手町／鷹野橋商店街（平和記念公園の南、徒歩圏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町5-6-11
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 広島県広島市中区大手町五丁目6番11号; within Hiroshima box.
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop whose signature offerings are built around wheat: homemade bread, toast, sandwiches, napolitan (wheat pasta), and gratin. No gluten-free items, menu, or accommodation are documented. Not suitable for those requiring gluten-free.
vegan_detail: A retro kissaten centered on egg-based morning sets, dairy (gratin, coffee/tea service), and meat-containing napolitan and curry. No vegan options, labeling, or accommodation are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
seats_text: About 38 seats.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 07:00-19:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 07:00-18:00. Morning service served 07:00-10:30.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day documented.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, old-fashioned local coffee shop where you order at your table; a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at the picture menu and the simple morning sets makes it easy and welcoming for a foreign student.
caveats: Signature dishes are wheat- and egg-based; no gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented. Credit cards are not accepted, though electronic money and QR-code payment are. No reservations.

bio.background: Opened in 1946 in the immediate aftermath of the war, this Otemachi coffee house sits along the Takanosashi shopping arcade, a short walk south of the Peace Memorial Park. It is widely credited as the birthplace of Japan's 'morning service' tradition, having begun offering a morning set of bread, egg, salad and coffee in the post-war years.
bio.specialty: Morning sets and homemade bread baked on-site, alongside enduring kissaten classics like napolitan, gratin and curry. Coffee is roasted by a local roaster.
bio.philosophy: A neighborhood haven of Showa-era calm — soft lighting, jazz, a green inner courtyard and shelves of reading material — where regulars and visitors alike slow down over morning coffee.

anecdotes.text: Customers describe businesspeople and locals settling in with the morning paper before work, choosing from several morning sets while freshly baked bread fills a counter near the entrance.
Regarded as the place where Japan's morning-service custom began, founded in 1946 and serving morning sets in the years that followed.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread is baked on-site and toast, sandwiches, napolitan and gratin are core menu items, so wheat is pervasive in the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on eggs, dairy and meat (egg morning sets, gratin, napolitan, curry); no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: Momiji manju confectionery and Seto Inland Sea dining — anago (conger eel) rice, Hiroshima oysters, regional set meals, plus a tea and sweets rest area
neighborhood: Miyajima / Omotesando shopping arcade
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町461-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町461-1 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Ground-floor tea and sweets rest area with about 40 seats (Japanese garden with koi); separate 2nd-floor dining room with roughly 150 seats (chair-and-table style) for set-meal dining, mainly by reservation.
closed_days: Open daily; occasional irregular temporary closures announced on the shop's calendar.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, tourist-frequented Miyajima shop where you can watch momiji manju being baked and grab sweets or tea easily with a smile and a few words; the upstairs set-meal dining is more formal and works best with a phone reservation, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps there.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature momiji manju is a wheat-flour batter cake, and the set meals (anago rice, oyster dishes) rely on wheat-based soy sauce and breaded/fried items. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes are anago (conger eel), oysters, sashimi and seafood set meals; momiji manju typically contains egg and dairy (cream/ice-cream varieties). No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: だいこん屋 traces its roots to the early Edo period, when it operated as the Ōne-ya (大根屋), a boat-inn lodging house on Miyajima. In 1989 it was reborn as a souvenir and rest shop specializing in momiji manju, and in 1993 the surviving Edo-to-Taisho-era building was fully renovated into the shop that stands today on the island's main approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked momiji manju in classic and original flavors (red bean, cream, chocolate, an original matcha cream, plus pie- and ice-cream-wrapped versions), alongside Seto Inland Sea set meals built around anago rice and Hiroshima oysters.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames its sweets as a way to share 'memories of taste' and hometown flavors with the people you care about, while preserving its historic building as part of Miyajima's streetscape.

anecdotes.text: Inside the shop you can watch the momiji manju being made, with the smell of the cakes baking fresh filling the room, and relax over tea in a small interior Japanese garden with a koi pond.
An original matcha-cream momiji manju is a flavor exclusive to this shop.
The business began in the early Edo period as the Ōne-ya boat-inn and was reinvented as a momiji manju shop and rest stop in 1989, with the historic building fully restored in 1993.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago rice and oyster set meals are seasoned in the regional style, which relies on wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on conger eel, oysters and other seafood, so vegan diners should not assume any shared equipment or stock is free of animal products.

caveats: Tabelog classifies this primarily as a 和菓子/甘味処 (Japanese sweets shop). The official site confirms a 2nd-floor dining room (~150 seats) serving anago/oyster/Seto Inland Sea set meals, mainly geared to reservations and groups, plus a ~40-seat ground-floor tea and sweets rest area. Set-meal prices documented as of April 2025 (¥2,300–¥4,500). Hours 8:30–17:00 from Tabelog; the shop posts occasional irregular closures on its own calendar. Payment: cards not accepted at the sweets counter per Tabelog, but electronic money and QR payment accepted (one secondary source notes credit cards accepted for dining), so payment terms may vary by section — confirm on arrival.

cuisine: 汁なし担担麺・四川料理 (broth-less dandan noodles / Sichuan)
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, Naka-ku)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀2-6
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box for 広島県広島市中区八丁堀2-6.
seats_text: Counter only, around 7 seats; a very small shop that regularly draws a line.
hours_raw: 12:30-15:00 / 18:30-22:00 (per Tabelog). Note: some secondary listings cite lunch starting 11:30; Tabelog shows 12:30 open.
closed_days: Sundays (and some holidays per secondary listings)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only local shop with no English noted and a regular queue; comfortable if you can order simply in Japanese, point at the dandan noodles, and accept cash-only with quick turnover.
gf_detail: The signature dish is wheat ramen-style noodles in a Sichuan dandan sauce; noodles are wheat and the sauce is not gluten-free, so this is not suitable for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Dandan noodles here use minced meat and a savory Sichuan sauce; no documented vegan option, so not suitable for vegan diners.

bio.background: A small, owner-run counter shop in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district devoted to broth-less dandan noodles in the Sichuan style. The shop is named after the rindo (gentian) flower, noted for its solitary bloom and bitter root, a quiet nod to the single-minded focus of the place.
bio.specialty: Broth-less dandan noodles (shiru-nashi tantanmen) with a Sichuan-style sauce, served with adjustable heat. Chili powder and chili oil sit on the counter so guests can dial in their own spice; a soft-boiled-egg topping is a popular add.
bio.philosophy: A focused, no-frills shop: a short menu, short hours, and a few counter seats, letting the noodles and sauce do the talking.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so consistently busy that one reviewer recounts trying four times before finally getting a seat, succeeding only around 1pm.
The heat is built around Sichuan pepper rather than raw chili burn; a reviewer found the mid-level (3) spice gentle enough that the sauce's underlying sweetness stood out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dandan sauce is a savory Sichuan-style seasoning typically built on soy/sesame-based components; no gluten-free preparation is documented, and the wheat noodles share the same kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard dandan noodles include minced meat, so vegan diners cannot rely on the regular dish; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Tabelog lists hours as 12:30-15:00 and 18:30-22:00 closed Sundays; some secondary listings cite an 11:30 lunch open and "Sundays and holidays" closed, so the noon open time and holiday closures should be confirmed on the day. Phone is non-public and reservations are not accepted. No official website found.

lead: 鉄板焼 お好み焼 修竜 薬研堀本店
name_ja: 鉄板焼 お好み焼 修竜 薬研堀本店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀10-11 ハウスアピア1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address via GSI; result 34.390385, 132.465225 is within the Hiroshima box and matches the Yagenbori area of Naka-ku.
seats_text: 45 seats total, including counter, table, tatami and sunken-kotatsu seating; can accommodate groups of up to about 23.
closed_days: Sundays; year-end/New Year holidays (Dec 31 - Jan 3)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively late-night local teppan spot where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a long way, though English support is limited.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which is built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and grilled dishes are typically finished with wheat-based sauces and soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on okonomiyaki with egg, pork and seafood, plus offal (hormone), wagyu steak, oysters and sea-urchin dishes. Sauces and soy sauce are not vegan. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: A family-run, locally rooted teppanyaki and okonomiyaki shop in the Yagenbori nightlife district of central Hiroshima, open late into the early hours for the area's after-work and after-night-out crowd.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki made with distinctive double-yolk eggs and noodles personally selected by the owner, alongside teppan dishes such as house-made offal in a custom sauce, wagyu steak, year-round Hiroshima oysters, and sea-urchin with spinach.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on craftsmanship and carefully chosen ingredients, from the twin-yolk eggs to the hand-picked noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for okonomiyaki using rare double-yolk eggs and noodles the owner selects personally.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, making cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki here are finished with standard sauces and soy sauce, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is shared with pork, seafood, offal and egg, so any item would have animal-product cross-contact.

lead: 広島つけ麺 ひこ 流川店
name_ja: 広島つけ麺 ひこ 流川店
cuisine: 広島つけ麺（魚介・野菜ベースのあっさり系つけ麺）
neighborhood: 中区流川（胡町電停近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町7-28 筒井ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 広島市中区流川町7-28, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: カウンター11席、座敷8席の計19席
closed_days: 年末年始（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual late-night tsukemen counter in the Nagarekawa nightlife district; ordering is simple by size and spice level, but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese words or pointing at the menu helps.
gf_detail: The tsukemen noodles are wheat-based and the dipping sauce is soy-sauce based, so the core dish is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are listed and there is no indication of allergen handling.
vegan_detail: The dipping broth is built on seafood (魚介) stock and a soy-based sauce, and toppings center on pork, egg and chashu. A no-noodle vegetable order (リャンサイ) exists but the broth itself still uses fish stock, so nothing on the menu is reliably vegan.
caveats: Closed days listed as year-end/New Year; late-night hours may vary—confirm by phone. Official website not found; Instagram (@hiko_hiroshima2) and Facebook exist but were not used as authoritative sources here.

bio.background: A tsukemen shop in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, known as a go-to spot for a satisfying late-night finish (shime) after drinking, staying open into the early morning hours.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chewy wheat noodles served with a refreshing, lighter dipping sauce built on vegetables and seafood stock, with adjustable spice levels reaching extreme intensities for chili lovers.
bio.philosophy: Customization and a clean, not-too-heavy finish—offering many toppings and even a no-noodle vegetable bowl for a lighter option.

anecdotes.text: Diners can dial the spice up dramatically (up to roughly 100x), with the chili blend adjusted per heat tier.
Positioned as an ideal late-night shime meal, the shop stays open until the early morning in the Nagarekawa nightlife area.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce is described as soy-based, and standard soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is built on seafood (魚介) stock alongside vegetables, so it is not suitable for vegans/vegetarians.

cuisine: グルテンフリーカフェ（米粉のノンフライから揚げ・スパイスカレー、小麦・卵・乳不使用）
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島神社裏手・紅葉谷公園方面、神社から徒歩約5分）
gf_detail: A dedicated gluten-free cafe: every dish on the menu is made without wheat, egg, or dairy. The signature non-fried rice-flour karaage uses rice flour and gluten-free (wheat-free) soy sauce, and they fry it in separate, dedicated oil to avoid cross-contact. The owner is a former dietician who developed the recipes and pays close attention to seasonings. Staff are knowledgeable about celiac disease and adjusted their practices (switching to gluten-free soy sauce) after feedback from a celiac customer. Widely regarded by celiac travelers as the safest place to eat on Miyajima.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The two signature mains are animal-based: rice-flour karaage (chicken) and a spiced beef/beef-tongue curry. Dishes are free of egg and dairy, so some sides, the lemon squash, and donuts may suit vegans, but the headline dishes contain meat. Vegans should ask about specific items before ordering.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町310
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町310 returned [132.309982, 34.272606], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: Around 20 seats. The 1st floor is the main cafe with a popular tatami lounge area; the 2nd floor is a coworking-only space. (One allergy-blog source cites 26 seats total.)
hours_raw: カフェ 10:00～17:30（L.O. 17:00、カフェ利用はL.O. 16:00頃）／コワーキング 9:30～17:30。営業時間は時期により変動あり。
closed_days: No fixed regular holiday; irregular closures announced on their Google Maps listing.
cultural_comfort_note: A foreign student will feel very welcome: the celiac/allergy-aware owner caters specifically to people who normally can't eat out, and the cafe is well-documented in English gluten-free travel guides, so explaining your dietary needs is easy here.
caveats: Seating count varies by source (20 on Tabelog, 26 cited on an allergy blog). Hours vary seasonally and irregular closures are posted on Google Maps; reservations are not accepted, so arrive expecting possible waits. Vegans should note the signature dishes contain meat.

bio.background: Miyajima Base is a small cafe set in a roughly century-old renovated farmhouse on the path toward Momiji-dani (Maple Valley) Park, about a five-minute walk behind Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in 2021.
bio.origin: The owner spent around ten years working as a dietician in schools, hospitals, and corporate cafeterias. There he repeatedly saw children with food allergies who had very few things they could eat, and adults who couldn't order the same dishes as the people they were dining with. That experience inspired him to create a place where people with allergies could enjoy a memorable meal without worry.
bio.philosophy: Every dish is made completely without wheat, egg, and dairy. The owner says the smiles of people finally able to eat something delicious without anxiety are what make the work worthwhile, and the cafe is built as an inclusive space where families and groups can share an unhurried meal together.
bio.specialty: Crispy, juicy non-fried rice-flour karaage and a spiced beef-tongue curry are the signature dishes, alongside a fresh Hiroshima lemon squash. Meals come with a complimentary donut.

anecdotes.text: The cafe originally used regular soy sauce, but after a customer with celiac disease explained that this wasn't safe for their community, the owner switched to gluten-free soy sauce and tightened their practices.
The tatami-seating lounge has become especially popular as a relaxed spot where families and groups gather for unhurried meals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: They fry the karaage in separate, dedicated oil so there is no cross-contact.
The entire menu is free of wheat, egg, and dairy, so there are no gluten-containing dishes prepared in the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: They use gluten-free (wheat-free) soy sauce; seasonings are also wheat-free.
The owner, a former dietician, developed the recipes and is careful about seasonings being wheat-free.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are very knowledgeable about celiac disease and changed their practices (adopting gluten-free soy sauce) after learning from a celiac customer.
The owner worked about ten years as a dietician across schools, hospitals, and corporate cafeterias before opening the cafe.

positives.text: Described as probably the best place for celiacs to eat anywhere on Miyajima Island, with carefully prepared, excellent karaage.
All meals are wheat-, egg-, and dairy-free and recommended for those seeking a gluten-free meal.

cuisine: 焼き牡蠣・海鮮（醤油バター・味噌バター・レモン醤油バターなどの焼き牡蠣、生牡蠣、穴子、揚げ牡蠣。立ち食い・食べ歩き向けの個人店）
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（厳島神社参道沿い）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町531-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県廿日市市宮島町531-2 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando shopping arcade.
seats_text: Counter seating available; primarily a stand-and-eat / food-walking style stall along the shopping arcade. Exact seat count not documented.
closed_days: No regular closed day documented (listed as unclear/不明); open daily per posted hours.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up oyster stall used to many foreign visitors, so pointing at the charcoal-grilled oysters and a few Japanese words will get you served comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature grilled oysters are finished with soy-butter, miso-butter and lemon-soy-butter sauces, all of which use wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and miso, and other menu items include fried (battered) oysters and croquettes. No gluten-free preparation or labeling is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on oysters (raw, grilled, fried), conger eel (anago) and other seafood, with butter, cheese and bacon among the toppings. No plant-based options are documented.
caveats: Seat count, regular closed days, and any official website are not documented; payment beyond cash and QR is unconfirmed. Hours marked irregular pending confirmation of a closed day.

bio.background: A small family-run shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, on the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. It began in 1970 as a souvenir store and grew into one of the street's go-to spots for grilled oysters, with charcoal grills running in front of the storefront.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled local oysters served in a range of styles -- classic soy-butter and miso-butter, plus playful variations like lemon-soy-butter, cheese-bacon, and garlic butter -- alongside raw oysters, fried oysters, and conger eel (anago) rice bowls.

anecdotes.text: Oysters are grilled over charcoal right at the storefront so you can watch them sizzle, and the shop is known for queues of both international visitors and locals stopping mid-stroll along the arcade.
The shop opened in 1970 as a souvenir store and still sells gifts alongside its grilled-oyster counter, fitting the eat-while-you-walk rhythm of the Omotesando shopping street.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The grilled oysters are finished with soy-butter, miso-butter and lemon-soy-butter sauces, which use standard wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and miso.

lead: 牡蠣ひよっこ商店 広島本店
name_ja: 牡蠣ひよっこ商店 広島本店
cuisine: Oyster-focused izakaya (kaki ryori) — over 20 oyster dishes with an all-you-can-eat oyster course; also fresh market sashimi
neighborhood: Yagenbori / Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima nightlife district, about 1.5 km from the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 〒730-0027 広島県広島市中区薬研堀5-12
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.46521, 34.390343] for 広島県広島市中区薬研堀5-12; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 25 seats; private tatami/room seating available for groups.
hours_raw: Tabelog: daily 17:00–01:00 (L.O. 00:00). Official site lists 18:00–07:00 (L.O. 06:30); hours may vary, so confirm when booking.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local late-night oyster izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the all-you-can-eat oyster course will get you far, though staff may have limited English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu is built around fried oysters, oyster tempura, gratin, peperoncino and pots, and Japanese izakaya seasoning relies on regular soy sauce and ponzu (which contain wheat). No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or GF accommodations are documented; a strictly gluten-free diner should avoid this venue.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Essentially every dish is oyster- or seafood-based (oysters, sashimi, smoked oyster, seafood pots), and sauces use fish/soy-based seasonings. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: An oyster-specialty izakaya in central Hiroshima's Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, built on the idea of serving plump Hiroshima oysters year-round at reasonable prices rather than only in winter. The proprietor has worked in the trade for over a decade and holds a wholesale license at the Hiroshima Kusatsu wholesale market, personally visiting oyster facilities and the market to source.
bio.specialty: Over twenty oyster preparations served all-you-can-eat — including the house vinegared oysters, grilled oysters in the shell, fried oysters, smoked oysters, oyster gratin, oyster tempura and oyster hot pots — alongside fresh market sashimi.
bio.philosophy: To let guests enjoy quality Hiroshima oysters all year at a fishmonger's price by buying directly at wholesale level.

anecdotes.text: The owner buys at wholesale level with a Kusatsu market license (stall 1508) and visits oyster work-sheds and the market personally to compete on both quality and price.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried oysters, oyster-and-root-vegetable tempura and oyster gratin; fried items share oil/breading typical of izakaya and no GF handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as oyster ponzu and pots rely on standard Japanese soy sauce/ponzu, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Virtually all dishes are oyster- or seafood-based, so vegan diners cannot be reliably accommodated.

caveats: Hours conflict between sources (Tabelog 17:00–01:00 vs official site 18:00–07:00); confirm at booking. The brief states Etajima (江田島) oysters, but the official site references Hiroshima oysters sourced via the Kusatsu wholesale market and a named oyster work-shed without confirming Etajima specifically — so Etajima sourcing is unverified. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published; allergy-sensitive diners must contact the shop directly.

name_ja: 汁なし担々麺 きさく
cuisine: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (Hiroshima-style soupless dandan noodles)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core and essentially only dish is shiru-nashi tantanmen built on fresh wheat ramen noodles, and the sauce is seasoned with doubanjiang/fermented-bean (douchi) chili paste that typically contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no GF noodle option, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not eat here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature soupless dandan noodles use ground pork and a concentrated dashi made from dried sardines (niboshi), kombu and chicken bones, topped with minced meat. The flavor base is fundamentally animal-derived, and there is no vegetable/vegan version offered. The single-dish, owner-run format leaves little room for custom modification.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区舟入川口町5-13 佐々木ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-banchi address 広島市中区舟入川口町5-13 via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box. Note: the supplied target area said 舟入幸町 but the confirmed address (and nearest tram stop, Funairi-saiwaicho/舟入幸町) is in adjacent 舟入川口町; the shop is a roughly 1-minute walk from the Funairi-saiwaicho tram stop.
neighborhood: Funairi-kawaguchicho, Naka-ku (near Funairi-saiwaicho tram stop)
seats_text: About 15 counter seats only.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:00; Sun: 11:00-15:00; Holidays: 11:00-14:00. Closed Wednesdays (plus one irregular day per month). Hours may end early when sold out.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus one irregular closing day per month
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only counter shop where ordering is simple (point to the soupless dandan noodles and pick a spice level), but little to no English is spoken, so a foreign student should be ready to order in Japanese or by gesture and expect to share a busy counter.

bio.background: Kisaku began as an ordinary, struggling ramen shop in the Funairi district of central Hiroshima. The owner, Mr. Hattori, was on the verge of closing when he remembered a Chinese exchange student who had once held a cooking class at his shop. Invited by that former student, now back home, he flew to Sichuan Province to taste authentic soupless dandan noodles for himself.
bio.specialty: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles): springy wheat noodles you mix vigorously from the bottom more than ten times, dressed in a secret sauce of chicken-and-niboshi (dried sardine) stock, chili oil and fermented black bean paste, finished with minced pork, scallion, and the tingling numbness of Sichuan pepper (huajiao).
bio.philosophy: Authenticity over adaptation. Hattori deliberately leaves out sesame paste, the ingredient most Japanese versions rely on. As he put it, sesame is something added to suit Japanese tastes, and in all his time in Sichuan he never once saw a soupless dandan noodle shop using it. He chose to reproduce the local Sichuan flavor instead of softening it.

anecdotes.text: In Sichuan, local contacts walked him through dozens of soupless dandan noodle shops, including hole-in-the-wall places frequented only by locals. He sampled so intensively that he fell ill on the flight home and had to be hospitalized after landing.

Back in Hiroshima he converted the failing ramen shop into a specialist counter devoted entirely to the recipe he brought back, and Kisaku is now widely credited as the birthplace of Hiroshima-style soupless dandan noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop serves essentially a single wheat-noodle dish in a small counter kitchen, so any gluten-free preparation is not feasible and cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sauce is built on chili oil and fermented black bean / doubanjiang-style paste rather than a sesame base; such fermented-bean chili pastes commonly contain wheat, and no gluten-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base stock is made from dried sardines, kelp and chicken bones and the dish is topped with minced pork, so the dish is not vegan and shares equipment with animal ingredients.

cuisine: 広島つけ麺 (Hiroshima tsukemen) — spicy cold dipping noodles
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is tsukemen, made with wheat flour noodles, and the spicy dipping sauce is built on a soy-sauce base (which contains wheat). No gluten-free menu, noodle substitution, or allergen accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The spicy dipping broth is a typical Hiroshima tsukemen sauce (soy sauce, chili, sesame) and the menu centers on meat and dashi-based toppings — chashu (roast pork) is the recommended order, with special and natto bowls also built around animal/fish components. No vegan menu or documented plant-based option.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地1-15 NIIビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address (chōme-level, 新天地1丁目); lands in central Naka-ku within the Hiroshima box, consistent with the Shintenchi / Ebisucho area near Hatchobori.
neighborhood: 新天地 (Shintenchi), Naka-ku — Ebisucho area, walking distance from Hondori and Nagarekawa
seats_text: Around 10-11 counter seats only; a tight, single-row late-night counter
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures; some listings note the 3rd Tuesday of the month)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, late-night counter ramen-style spot where ordering in basic Japanese (and pointing at the spice level you want) goes a long way — no English menu is documented, so it suits a student comfortable with a little Japanese.
caveats: Seat count varies slightly between listings (10 vs 11), counter-only. Closed days are irregular (不定休); one source notes the 3rd Tuesday. Cash only and no reservations. Open very late (until 3:30am).

bio.background: A small, counter-only tsukemen shop tucked into the Shintenchi entertainment quarter of central Hiroshima, open since 2001 and serving until the early hours of the morning.
bio.specialty: Spicy Hiroshima-style tsukemen — wheat noodles served with a chili dipping sauce whose heat is fully adjustable, from a gentle 1x up to a fierce 20x, so both spice novices and chili obsessives can dial it in. The chashu (roast pork) bowl is the house recommendation for first-timers.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the spicy tsukemen here as habit-forming, and the cramped late-night counter has made it a favorite stop for a post-drinking meal in the Shintenchi/Nagarekawa nightlife district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish is wheat-noodle tsukemen prepared in a shared noodle kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The spicy dipping sauce is a standard soy-sauce-based tsukemen broth, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around pork chashu and dashi/animal-based broths; no plant-based or vegan preparation is documented.

name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 地蔵通り本店
cuisine: 汁なし担担麺 (soupless / dry tantanmen)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is built on low-hydration wheat noodles, and the soy-sauce version uses regular soy sauce (typically wheat-based). No gluten-free menu, noodle substitute, or allergen accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Every bowl comes loaded with ground pork as a core ingredient, alongside wheat noodles, sesame or soy-based sauce, and green onions. No plant-based or meat-free version is offered.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区富士見町5-12 田ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box (Naka-ku, Fujimicho).
neighborhood: Fujimicho, Naka-ku — central Hiroshima south of Hondori, near the Chuden-mae tram stop
seats_text: 20 seats
hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sun: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-22:00; Fri, Sat & day-before-holiday: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-00:00; Tue: closed
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, fast-paced 20-seat noodle counter where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the two main bowls will carry you through; cash only, so bring yen.

bio.background: Musashibo is one of central Hiroshima's standard-bearers for the city's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen — the broth-less, vigorously-mixed dry version of dan-dan noodles. Opened in 2013 on Jizo-dori in the Fujimicho quarter, the shop has grown into a small Hiroshima institution, expanding to a handful of branches and even a mail-order line while keeping this counter as its flagship.
bio.specialty: Soupless tantanmen served in two house styles — a rich sesame (濃厚胡麻) bowl and an aromatic soy-sauce (芳醇醤油) bowl — each topped with ground pork and a mound of green onion.
bio.philosophy: The cooking centers on the ritual of the bowl: low-hydration noodles meant to be mixed thoroughly the moment they arrive, then tuned by the diner using house-made Musashibo sansho (four kinds of Japanese pepper) and kasho vinegar set out on the table, so each person dials in their own heat and aroma.

anecdotes.text: Every bowl is designed to be self-contained, arriving with all five elements together — noodles, sauce, ground meat, green onion, and the shop's own sansho.
Diners customize their bowl tableside with four varieties of sansho and kasho vinegar to adjust heat and fragrance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat noodles; soy-sauce version uses standard soy sauce. A shared kitchen handling wheat throughout means high gluten cross-contact risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The aromatic soy-sauce (芳醇醤油) bowl is built on soy sauce, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All bowls contain ground pork as a standard topping; no meat-free preparation is offered.

caveats: Two sources give slightly different hours; the more detailed schedule (Tuesday closed, with extended Fri/Sat late-night service) is used here and may shift with holidays. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented on the official site or listing — travelers with celiac disease or a vegan diet should treat this as unsuitable.

cuisine: Anago-meshi (charcoal-grilled conger eel rice)
neighborhood: 宮島町 (Miyajima island, Omotesando shopping street)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The anago-meshi is glazed and served with conger eel cooked in a wheat-based soy sauce tare; udon (wheat noodles) is also on the menu. No indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not rely on this shop.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire menu centers on conger eel (anago), a fish, plus rice bowls and udon typically served with fish/dashi-based and wheat-soy tare. No documented plant-based dishes.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町464-1
geocode_note: GSI address search did not resolve to the building or chō level; it returned only the Hatsukaichi City centroid (廿日市市) on the mainland rather than the actual Miyajima island location. The coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box but are NOT the true location of the shop, which sits on Miyajima island's Omotesando shopping street (roughly 10 min from the Miyajima ferry pier, near Itsukushima Shrine). Coordinates should be corrected to the island before use.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Small independent island shop with a casual, counter/table dine-in style and a food-ticket machine; reservations are not accepted.
hours_raw: 11:00 - 16:30 (closed Thursdays / 木曜定休)
closed_days: Thursday (木曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, ticket-machine island eatery where a basic greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus and ordering are simple but staff may have limited English, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing will get you through.
caveats: Seat count is not published anywhere found. The GSI geocode resolved only to the mainland Hatsukaichi city centroid, not the island building — coordinates need manual correction to the Miyajima Omotesando location. Hours and closed day are from the listing; payment accepts cards and QR but the food-ticket machine flow suggests confirming cash availability on arrival.

bio.background: A small independent conger-eel shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, about a ten-minute walk from the ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine. The shop operated as a maker of traditional simmered-conger anago-meshi until early 2021, when the kitchen was renovated and reopened on February 10, 2021. The family is documented as connected to the long-standing Iwamura/Iwamura-momiji-ya lineage on the island.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled conger-eel rice (anago-meshi). The shop is described as Miyajima's only place serving anago-meshi made with charcoal-grilled conger rather than the more common simmered style, grilled over high-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal. The conger comes from the Tsushima archipelago off Nagasaki, an origin prized by top sushi houses, and is offered in a standard grade and a premium grade (特上) available only when in stock.
bio.philosophy: A focus on a single dish done one way: premium Tsushima conger, charcoal-grilled to order, served as a rice bowl in an unfussy island setting.

anecdotes.text: Until January 2021 the shop served simmered conger; after a kitchen renovation it reopened on February 10, 2021 as the island's sole charcoal-grilled anago-meshi specialist, grilling over high-grade Tosa binchotan.
The conger is sourced from the Tsushima archipelago off Nagasaki, the same premium origin sought by leading sushi restaurants, and is offered in standard and premium (特上) grades.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi is served with a glaze/tare conventionally made from wheat-containing soy sauce, and the menu also includes udon (wheat noodles); no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built entirely around conger eel and fish/dashi-based rice bowls and udon; no plant-only preparation is documented and cross-contact with fish is effectively unavoidable.

cuisine: Anago-meshi (conger eel rice) and oysters — Japanese island eatery
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Hatsukaichi
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町16
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町16 lands on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine / the aquarium area, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Roughly 20-30 seats reported across sources; mix of table seating and a spacious traditional tatami area for groups, no counter or private rooms.
hours_raw: Sources disagree: ~10:30–16:00 daily (closes when ingredients run out) per one listing; 10:30–15:30 with closures mainly on Tuesdays per a visit report; another lists weekend/holiday-only 11:30–16:00. Treat hours as irregular and confirm by phone.
closed_days: Irregular closures, primarily Tuesdays; also closes early when the day's ingredients run out. Confirm by phone before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run island spot with no reservations and cash only; a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, and expect a wait since each anago-meshi is cooked to order.
gf_detail: The core dishes — anago-meshi, oyster cutlet, meat udon — rely on a soy-based glaze (wheat) and breaded/wheat components, so there is no reliable gluten-free option here.
vegan_detail: This is a conger-eel and oyster specialist; the signature dishes are built on fish, shellfish, meat udon, and a fish/soy-based sauce, so there are no vegan options.
caveats: Business hours conflict across sources (daily ~10:30–16:00 vs. 10:30–15:30 vs. weekend-only 11:30–16:00); the kitchen also closes early once ingredients sell out. No official website found. Hours_week reflects the most commonly reported daily-with-Tuesday-closure pattern but should be confirmed by phone (0829-44-2353).

bio.background: A casual, family-run eatery on Miyajima island, set in a building with the feel of a traditional old farmhouse (kominka), with a spacious tatami area for groups. It sits a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the island aquarium, slightly off the busiest stretch of the approach, which makes it a quieter alternative when the famous anago-meshi shops near the pier have long lines or require advance booking.
bio.specialty: Anago-meshi — a bowl of rice topped with densely packed, locally favored conger eel that regulars describe as light and delicate compared with freshwater eel, finished with an aromatic sauce. Oyster cutlet and meat udon round out the menu, and takeout anago bento are available.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is food that is unforgettable once tasted, with the eel baked until soft and fluffy. The house sauce is made with local Miyajima honey, giving it a distinctive sweet note.

anecdotes.text: Customers report walls covered with autographed photos of celebrities who have stopped in, and staff who proactively warn that each order takes about 15-20 minutes because the eel is prepared fresh.

One visiting couple found it as a calm hidden gem after being turned away from busier anago shops that required reservations, enjoying their meal with only a couple of other groups in the room before noon.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-meshi and other dishes are finished with a soy-based sauce, which in Japan is normally brewed with wheat and is not gluten-free.

cuisine: あなごめし (anago-meshi / conger eel rice bowl); Japanese teishoku
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Hatsukaichi — Omotesando shopping street near Itsukushima Shrine
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町785
geocode_note: Geocoder returned a chō-level point (広島県廿日市市) on Miyajima island, within the Hiroshima box. Address 宮島町785 confirmed on the island.
seats_text: 14 seats (6 counter, 8 table) per Tabelog; some sources cite 12 seats. Small local shop.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 12:00-14:30 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:30); Thu & Sun 12:00-14:30 (L.O. 13:30) lunch only; bento sold from 11:00 until sold out. Closed Mondays and 1st & 3rd Sundays.
closed_days: Mondays; 1st and 3rd Sundays. Evening service only Tue/Wed/Fri/Sat; Thursday and Sunday are lunch only.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, mostly Japanese-language local shop where island residents eat; pointing at the photo menu works, but little English is documented, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature dishes use wheat-based soy sauce and tare on the grilled anago, and the dashimaki egg omelette typically contains dashi (fish-based); no dedicated GF preparation is mentioned.
vegan_detail: This is an anago (conger eel) specialist; the core dishes are eel, fish dashi, and egg, with no documented vegan options.
caveats: Hours are irregular (lunch-only on Thursday and Sunday; closed Mondays and 1st/3rd Sundays). Cash only. Seat count varies by source (12-14). Anago is a seasonal/limited item and bento can sell out, so the kitchen may run out.

bio.background: A small, family-style shop tucked into Miyajima's Omotesando area near Itsukushima Shrine, Mikotoya is the kind of place island residents themselves return to rather than a tour-bus stop. The dining room seats only around a dozen guests across a short counter and a few tables, with cheerful rice-bowl illustrations on the signage.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the Golden Conger Rice Bowl (黄金穴子重), about 3,600 yen: grilled anago laid densely over rice and crowned with a fluffy dashimaki (dashi egg omelette), served with broth. The eel is first slow-grilled, then brushed with house tare and grilled again to a golden finish, giving a tender texture and gentle, not-too-rich fat. Other offerings include a roast-beef-and-anago version using Hiroshima beef.

anecdotes.text: Locals on Miyajima eat here regularly, with repeat customers often ordering the rotating daily specials rather than always the same dish.
The anago is double-grilled — slow-grilled first, then finished with a special sauce — producing golden-colored eel that is tender with a mild fat content.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The grilled anago is glazed with a house tare/sauce that, like standard Japanese eel sauces, is based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free version is documented.
