vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on conger eel, egg, and fish-based dashi, so animal products are present throughout the menu.

cuisine: もみじまんじゅう・揚げもみじ (momiji-manju & fried momiji; wagashi)
neighborhood: 宮島町 (Miyajima island) — Omotesando shopping street, near Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core product is momiji-manju, a castella sponge cake made from wheat-flour batter, and the fried version (揚げもみじ) coats that same wheat cake in additional batter before frying. The fry bar also sells gansu (a battered fish cake) and other wheat-containing items. There is no gluten-free option here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The castella batter used for momiji-manju is a standard sponge that contains egg (and the shop states no dietary/allergen accommodation). While the red-bean (koshian) filling is itself typically plant-based, the cake around it is not, and the adjacent fry bar fries fish-cake gansu and oyster-based items, so shared frying and egg-containing batter make this unsuitable for vegans.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町459
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町459 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 15 seats; includes an eat-in / rest area with views toward the sea and the great torii.
hours_raw: 9:00-17:00, 年中無休 (open daily, year-round, including holidays)
closed_days: None — open every day, year-round (年中無休).
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual walk-up sweets shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where you can point at the freshly fried momiji and pay with a smile and a few words of Japanese; English signage is limited but ordering is simple.
caveats: The official site states no allergen/dietary accommodation; egg in the castella batter and shared frying with fish-cake items are inferred from product descriptions rather than an explicit allergen statement.

bio.background: Hakataya is a family-run sweets shop on Miyajima that traces its roots to the early Meiji period and has been in business for around 140 years. It began not as a confectioner but as a dealer in Miyajima's traditional craft of shakushi (the carved wooden rice scoops the island is famous for), and over the generations it grew alongside the island's history into a maker of momiji-manju, the maple-leaf-shaped cakes that are Miyajima's signature treat.
bio.origin: 宮島町 (Miyajima island), Hiroshima — on the Omotesando shopping street that leads toward Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Momiji-manju made fresh on site every day — both the classic baked version and the fried 'agemomiji,' coated and fried to order so the outside is crisp and the inside fluffy and warm. They keep about seven varieties, from traditional smooth red-bean to chocolate, peach and matcha, and run a 'Fry Bar' where the momiji and local Hiroshima specialties are fried after you order.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its aim as taking the natural character of this place and passing it on to the future in new forms, supporting Miyajima's local traditions and cultural heritage while making sweets by hand. They prepare their own castella batter and a homemade smooth koshian bean paste, soaking the beans repeatedly to draw out bitterness.

anecdotes.text: The business started as a wholesaler of Miyajima's wooden rice scoops (宮島杓子) before turning to confectionery, and it has grown together with the island's history over roughly 140 years.

Hakataya is one of the few shops on the island making momiji-manju by hand and selling it the same day, with a 'Fry Bar' that fries momiji and local specialties like gansu fresh to order for the best texture and temperature.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The fried items (agemomiji and battered fish-cake gansu) are all wheat-battered and appear to be fried in the same fry bar, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively guaranteed; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The fry bar fries fish-cake gansu and oyster-based items alongside the sweets, so fried sweets share equipment with animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site provides no allergen or dietary-restriction information for its products.

lead: かき小屋袋町 海平商店
name_ja: かき小屋袋町 海平商店
cuisine: Oyster specialist / kaki-goya (grilled and steamed Hiroshima oysters) and Seto Inland Sea seafood izakaya
gf_detail: This is an oyster and seafood izakaya with no documented gluten-free program. Core dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and there is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation, separate fryers, or staff allergen handling. Travelers with celiac disease should treat it as not safe.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood, served grilled, steamed, and as sashimi, typically with wheat-based soy sauce. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町8-11 森田ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区袋町8-11 via GSI; result lng 132.459518, lat 34.391151 falls within the central Hiroshima box, near the Peace Memorial Park / Hondori area.
neighborhood: 中区袋町 (Fukuromachi), central Hiroshima — a short walk from Hondori shopping arcade and the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome, near Fukuromachi tram stop
seats_text: Listed at 60 seats, including a large counter where dishes are prepared in front of guests and tatami seating that accommodates up to about 24 for groups.
hours_raw: 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), open Mon-Sat; closed Sundays (when Monday is a public holiday, open Sunday and closed Monday instead); also closed around New Year (approx. 12/31-1/2) plus occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Sundays (with holiday-Monday swap), year-end/New Year holidays, and occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local oyster izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the counter or menu will go a long way; English support is not documented, so come with basic phrases and a relaxed attitude.
caveats: Hours and closed days are somewhat irregular (Sunday closures can shift around public holidays), so confirm by phone before visiting. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations are documented, and the menu centers on oysters and seafood, making it unsuitable for strict celiac, vegan, or vegetarian diners.

bio.background: An oyster shack (kaki-goya) in the Fukuromachi quarter of central Hiroshima, serving Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood year-round rather than only in winter. It presents itself as a direct affiliate of the well-known Ujina kaki-goya lineage, bringing that grill-shack style into the heart of the city within easy reach of Hondori and the Peace Memorial Park.
bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Fukuromachi, Naka-ku); positioned as part of the original Ujina oyster-shack family of shops.
bio.specialty: Charcoal grilling and steaming of in-shell Hiroshima oysters at a long counter where guests watch their food cooked in front of them; signature gangan-yaki (steamed oysters) and an oyster-focused course paired with sake.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its priorities as delicious food, good sake, and customers' smiles, with an emphasis on freshness and sourcing.

anecdotes.text: The house describes itself as a direct-line shop of the original Ujina oyster shacks, bringing the kaki-goya grilling style into central Hiroshima and serving Hiroshima oysters all year rather than only in the winter season.
A signature offering is the steamed gangan-yaki, meant to show off the pure umami of the oysters, alongside an oyster-focused course built around Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea fish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on oysters and seafood served grilled, steamed, and as sashimi, conventionally accompanied by Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared on shared oyster grills and seafood stations, so any non-seafood item would carry a high risk of contact with shellfish and fish.

name_ja: 牡蠣ひよっこ商店 広島本店
cuisine: Hiroshima oyster specialist izakaya
neighborhood: 中区 薬研堀 (Yagenbori, central Hiroshima nightlife district)
gf_detail: This is an oyster izakaya with no documented gluten-free accommodation. Fried oysters (kaki-fry) are wheat-battered and deep-fried, simmered and grilled dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce and ponzu, and there is no indication of gluten-free menu items or staff allergy handling. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot eat safely here without major risk; treat it as not suitable.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built around Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea fish, served steamed, grilled, fried, and raw. Sauces use wheat-soy-sauce and dashi. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and the all-you-can-eat format centers on shellfish and seafood. Not a viable option for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀5-12
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 132.46521, lat 34.390343, within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Yagenbori area of central Naka-ku.
seats_text: Roughly 25 seats with a mix of counter and sunken-tatami (horigotatsu) seating; can accommodate larger groups of about 20-50 with arrangement.
hours_raw: 本店 (Yagenbori): 18:00–翌7:00 (L.O. 6:30) per official chain info; Tabelog lists 17:00–01:00 (L.O. 00:00). Late-night oyster izakaya hours; irregular closures possible.
closed_days: Open year-round (年中無休), though irregular closures are noted as possible.
cultural_comfort_note: A lively late-night Japanese oyster izakaya in the Yagenbori nightlife district; ordering is in Japanese and the all-you-can-eat oyster format is geared to local groups, so a translation app and some basic Japanese will help a foreign student feel comfortable.
caveats: The official chain website (kakihiyokko.com) primarily presents the 胡町店 branch; the target listing is the 薬研堀本店 (Honten). Hours differ between the official chain info (18:00 to early morning) and the Tabelog listing (17:00 to 01:00), so confirm directly before a late visit. Address geocoded to the Yagenbori block; the building number is approximate.

bio.background: A Hiroshima oyster specialist izakaya in the Yagenbori entertainment quarter of central Naka-ku, part of a small locally-run group of oyster houses. The operators hold market purchasing rights (買参権), which lets them buy Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood directly at market rather than through wholesalers.
bio.specialty: Large Hiroshima oysters served kaki-goya style and as all-you-can-eat, alongside fresh local fish; preparations include steamed, grilled, fried, and raw oysters with seasonal creative dishes.
bio.philosophy: Deliver high-quality Hiroshima oysters at an accessible price by sourcing directly from the market, summed up by the shop's own framing of 'this deliciousness, this affordability.'

anecdotes.text: The shop secured market purchasing rights so it can buy carefully selected Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea fish straight from the market, cutting out middlemen to keep prices down.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kaki-fry (fried oysters) are wheat-battered and deep-fried; with no documented dedicated fryer, shared fryer oil should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese izakaya seasoning relies on wheat-based soy sauce and ponzu for oyster and seafood dishes; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on oysters and seafood with no separate vegan preparation area documented; cross-contact with shellfish and fish should be assumed throughout.

lead: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き そり家
name_ja: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き そり家
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き（Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki）
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀（八丁堀駅徒歩2〜3分、幟町寄り）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀13-17 ソラリスビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [132.462814, 34.3951] for 広島県広島市中区八丁堀13-17, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 32 seats (counter, table, and tatami/private room; up to about 40 standing). Owner-plus-one-staff operation.
closed_days: 日曜日（要相談で営業の場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, family-run neighborhood okonomiyaki counter where a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way; menus and staff are Japanese-oriented, so a foreign student should come ready to point and smile.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe base plus wheat noodles (soba), and the finishing sauce is a wheat/soy-based okonomiyaki sauce. Teppanyaki and izakaya sides also commonly use wheat-soy seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes use egg, pork, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, cooked on a shared griddle, and the okonomiyaki sauce is animal/fish-influenced. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: Owner Sori Osamu was born in Hiroshima in 1974 and grew up eating the city's signature Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. He spent years running a yakiniku and teppanyaki restaurant before stepping away into another industry, and once his children had grown more independent his desire to cook for people returned. He opened Soriya in December 2018, bringing roughly 18 years of professional griddle experience.
bio.philosophy: He named the place 'そり家' using the character 家 (home) rather than 屋 (shop) so guests would feel they were coming to someone's home. The house creed is hospitality rooted in gratitude and humility: gratitude toward customers, staff, suppliers, and the ingredients themselves.
bio.specialty: Standard Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (niku-tama-soba) made with hand-cut cabbage griddled with no added water so it cooks down sweet and fluffy, layered with fresh noodles delivered daily.

anecdotes.text: The cabbage is hand-shredded and cooked using only its own moisture, with no added water, so each okonomiyaki turns out plump and full-volume.
He says 'I can keep going because I have my family,' and the shop is run as a tight two-person team of the owner and one staff member.
He chose 家 ('home') over 屋 ('shop') in the name to make the restaurant feel welcoming, and describes himself as caring most about putting heart into the food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is layered on a wheat-flour crepe with wheat noodles and finished with wheat/soy-based sauce, all cooked on one shared teppan griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki/teppanyaki seasoning relies on wheat-containing okonomiyaki and soy sauces typical of Hiroshima-style cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg, pork, and fish-based ingredients are cooked on the same shared griddle as all dishes.

caveats: Hours and seat count are from listing pages and may change; Sunday is the regular closed day but the shop notes it may open by prior arrangement. No GF or vegan accommodation is documented, and staff allergy handling could not be confirmed — travelers with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should not rely on this spot.

lead: あなごめし 岩むら
name_ja: あなごめし 岩むら
cuisine: あなごめし・宮島郷土料理
gf_detail: This is an anago (conger eel) rice specialist. The signature dishes are grilled and stewed anago served over rice with a sweet-savory tare that is made from soy sauce (wheat-based) and mirin, and side dishes include items such as udon (wheat noodles) and breaded oyster fries (wheat). There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free tamari soy sauce. Not a suitable destination for celiac or strict gluten-free travelers.
vegan_detail: The entire concept is built around conger eel (anago) caught locally, plus oysters and meat udon. Dishes use fish, fish-based dashi, and wheat-soy-sauce tare. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian set meals. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町464-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chōme-level point for 宮島町464; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50). Accurate to the island block.
seats_text: Eight four-person tables in a row (approximately 32 seats).
hours_raw: 11:00-16:30 (Tabelog Japanese listing; English listing shows 11:00-16:00). Closed Thursdays. Hours and last orders may vary seasonally; confirm directly.
closed_days: Thursday (木曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual ticket-style tourist eatery on Miyajima's main approach street where pointing at the picture menu and a simple greeting will carry you through, though deeper English help is not guaranteed.
neighborhood: 宮島（表参道商店街、厳島神社方面）
caveats: Hours differ slightly between the Japanese (11:00-16:30) and English (11:00-16:00) listings, and may shift seasonally; treat closing time as approximate. Located on Miyajima island, reached by ferry from Miyajimaguchi. Reservations are not taken; expect queues at peak lunch hours.

bio.background: Iwamura sits along Miyajima's bustling Omotesando approach street, on the route walkers take from the pier toward Itsukushima Shrine, just past the local credit union. It is part of the Iwamura family of Miyajima shops and is run as a sit-down spot serving the island's beloved anago (conger eel) over rice alongside oysters, udon and donburi.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled anago rice. After kitchen renovations completed around February 2021, the shop began billing itself as the only place on Miyajima serving 'sumibi-yaki' (charcoal-grilled) anago rice, finishing each portion over premium Tosa binchotan charcoal in a process that takes roughly ten minutes per serving.
bio.philosophy: A focus on thick, generously sized anago with a firm bite, grilled over high-grade charcoal rather than simply steamed or pan-finished, to set the dish apart from the island's many long-established anago houses.

anecdotes.text: The shop promotes itself as Miyajima's only charcoal-grilled ('sumibi-yaki') anago rice, using top-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal, with each serving grilled to order over about ten minutes.
It is laid out simply with eight four-person tables in a row, and its oversized anago signage on the Omotesando street is a recognizable landmark.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares wheat-based items (udon, breaded oyster fries) alongside anago dishes; no separation for gluten is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago rice is finished with a sweet-savory tare made from soy sauce, which in Japan is standardly brewed with wheat; no wheat-free tamari is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every documented dish centers on conger eel, oysters, meat, or fish-based dashi; there are no plant-only preparations noted.

cuisine: Anago (conger eel) rice bowls, oysters, and Miyajima local seafood
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Omotesando shopping street and Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: This is a traditional seafood eatery built around anago (conger eel) rice bowls, oyster dishes, and udon. The anago is glazed with a house sweet-savory sauce that is soy-sauce based (contains wheat), udon is wheat noodles, and fried oysters use a wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on conger eel, oysters, and other seafood, with soy-sauce-based and dashi-based seasonings throughout. There are no documented vegan dishes. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町浜ノ町844
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full street address landed on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.348637, lng 132.331451), near the Omotesando area as expected.
seats_text: 56 seats total: 38 on raised tatami (zashiki) seating and 18 at tables.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00, closed Thursdays (定休日 木曜日)
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, tourist-friendly local seafood hall on Miyajima where pointing at the picture menu and a few polite Japanese words will get you through comfortably, though detailed dietary questions in English may be hard.
caveats: Daytime-only spot (lunch through late afternoon); reservations are not taken, so arrive early to avoid waits at peak times. Built entirely around seafood with wheat-soy-sauce seasonings, so not workable for gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian diners.

bio.background: A long-standing family seafood dining hall on Miyajima that has been welcoming visitors for more than a hundred years and is now run by the third generation. It sits a short walk from the ferry terminal and only a few minutes from Itsukushima Shrine, making it a classic stop for travelers exploring the island.
bio.specialty: Anago-don (conger eel rice bowl) made with Seto Inland Sea conger eel that is grilled without steaming for a thick, tender texture and finished with a house-made sweet-savory glaze; also oyster rice bowls with mountain vegetables, fried and grilled oysters, and trumpet-shell tsuboyaki.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing fresh local Seto Inland Sea ingredients, with oysters bought in daily, served in a relaxed setting suited to a leisurely break during sightseeing.

anecdotes.text: Said to have over a century of history and to be carried on by the third generation of the family.
The trumpet-shell tsuboyaki is a favorite that brings repeat customers back, alongside the signature anago and oyster dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-don is finished with a house sweet-savory sauce that is soy-sauce based, which in Japan standardly contains wheat; oyster udon uses wheat noodles and fried oysters use wheat batter.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is entirely seafood-based (conger eel, oysters, shellfish) with dashi and soy seasonings; no plant-only preparations are documented.

lead: キング軒 大手町本店
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 大手町本店
name_en: Shirunashi Tantanmen Senmon Kingken Otemachi Honten
cuisine: 広島式汁なし担担麺専門（地元密着の個人店）
neighborhood: 中区大手町（平和記念公園東側・市役所前/本通方面）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町3-3-14 武本ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町3-3-14 returned [132.453842, 34.388691], within the Hiroshima box (just east of Peace Memorial Park, near City Hall / Hondori).
seats_text: About 20 seats (counter-focused small shop).
hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays 11:00-20:00; Sun closed. (Hours have historically varied; some listings showed split weekday/weekend hours — confirm on the day of visit.)
closed_days: Sundays (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, fast-moving counter noodle shop where you order a single dish and slurp it down; a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will get you through comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The single signature dish is wheat noodles (from a Hatsukaichi noodle maker), and the seasoning tare is made with a custom soy sauce (from a local Hiroshima brewery), which contains wheat. There is no gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Hiroshima brothless tantanmen here is built on ground pork, wheat noodles, and a soy-sauce-based tare; the popular finishing course mixes leftover sauce with rice. No vegan version is offered.
caveats: The Tabelog URL provided in the task (A340113/34010135) returns HTTP 404; the live record is at A340116/34015896. Reported hours vary between sources (one listing showed weekday 17:00-20:00 / weekend-holiday 11:00-15:00, another showed 11:00-20:00 daily except Sunday) — treat hours as irregular and confirm before visiting.

bio.background: Opened in November 2011 in Otemachi, central Hiroshima, this was the neighborhood's first shop dedicated entirely to Hiroshima-style brothless tantan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen). Around the time of opening, the founder traveled to Chengdu in China and, encountering fresh Sichuan pepper at a local market, built the shop's identity around that fragrant, tingling spice.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (Otemachi, Naka-ku), with roots traced to a trip to Chengdu, China.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless tantan noodles: chewy wheat noodles tossed with a savory soy-based tare, ground pork, chili heat, and a vivid blast of Sichuan pepper. Diners mix the bowl about 30 times before eating, add more pepper from the table, and finish by stirring the leftover sauce into a small bowl of rice (tantan rice).
bio.philosophy: An uncompromising devotion to aroma: even as the dish went mainstream and rivals softened their flavors, this shop keeps its boldly assertive Sichuan-pepper profile. A custom red-and-blue Sichuan pepper blend is hand-ground fresh every single morning at each shop because the fragrance fades over time, and the same fresh blend is used at both counter and kitchen. Ingredients are sourced from trusted local partners.
roles: originator of Otemachi's dedicated brothless tantanmen counter
helped bring Hiroshima brothless tantanmen to nationwide attention

anecdotes.text: The founder's encounter with fresh Sichuan pepper at a market in Chengdu around 2011 became the inspiration for the shop's signature explosively aromatic pepper.

Every morning, without fail, staff hand-grind the day's Sichuan pepper because its aroma fades with time; the noodles come from a Hatsukaichi noodle maker and the tare from a custom soy sauce made with a local brewery in Asa-Minami.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around a single wheat-noodle dish, so flour and gluten are pervasive; there is no gluten-free preparation or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The seasoning tare uses a custom soy sauce produced with a local Hiroshima brewery (Kawanaka Shoyu), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core dish contains ground pork and a soy-sauce-based tare; no vegan option is documented.

lead: 中華そば 寿々女（すずめ）
cuisine: 広島ラーメン（中華そば）・汁なし担々麺
neighborhood: 広島市中区舟入幸町（舟入幸町電停そば）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区舟入幸町18-10 メゾン幸 102
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 舟入幸町18-10 returned [132.440491, 34.383404], within the Hiroshima box. Note: official site lists building no. 18-8 while Tabelog lists 18-10; same block, geocode used the 18-10 value.
seats_text: 20席（カウンター8席・小上がり/座敷12席）
hours_raw: 月〜土 11:30-15:00（スープ売切れ次第終了）。木曜のみ夜 19:30-22:00、土曜のみ夜 19:00-22:00 も営業。定休：日曜・毎月14日（official site）。Tabelogでは昼 11:30-15:00、日曜定休と記載。
closed_days: 日曜・毎月14日（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional cash-only lunch counter with a ticket-machine ordering system and little to no English, so it helps to arrive knowing what you want to order and to have cash ready.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style chuka soba (ramen) shop. The noodles are wheat and the broth is a soy-sauce pork-bone base; Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented, so it is not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
vegan_detail: A pork-bone-and-soy-sauce ramen shop whose core dishes (chuka soba, braised-pork rice, tantanmen) center on pork and animal-based broth. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: Suzume continues the legacy of a beloved Hiroshima ramen shop of the same name that operated for some sixty years before closing in 2015, to the regret of many regulars. It reopened in February 2016 at this Funairi location, carrying forward the original 'traditional flavor' and the so-called 'small bird' (suzume) lineage of Hiroshima ramen, whose postwar identity grew out of the city's street-vendor noodle culture.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (continuing a local lineage dating to the mid-20th century)
bio.specialty: Traditional Hiroshima-style chuka soba in a soy-sauce pork-bone broth, served with a mild house-made chili oil; also known for a mini braised-pork rice bowl and newer Sichuan-influenced dishes such as beni-kara soba and brothless tantanmen.
bio.philosophy: Preserving the nostalgic traditional taste of the original shop while gradually evolving the menu, embodying a 'tradition and innovation in tandem' approach.

anecdotes.text: The current proprietor came from a corporate career drawing air-conditioning blueprints, and put that technical background to use by building custom partitions and fabricating the ceiling ductwork himself at the new shop.
Long associated only with a single 'chuka soba' dish, the revived shop surprised longtime customers by adding Sichuan-influenced items like spicy red noodles and brothless tantanmen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as a soy-sauce pork-bone (醤油豚骨) base characteristic of Hiroshima ramen; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are pork-based ramen and braised-pork rice in animal broth; no vegetarian or vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog lists lunch-only 11:30-15:00 Mon-Sat with Sunday closed, while the official site adds Thursday (19:30-22:00) and Saturday (19:00-22:00) dinner service and lists closures on Sundays and the 14th of each month. Lunch closes when the soup sells out. Address building number differs slightly between sources (18-10 on Tabelog, 18-8 on official site) but same block.

lead: 中華そば 陽気 大手町店
name_ja: 中華そば 陽気 大手町店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style chuka soba (ramen) — soy-sauce tonkotsu/chicken broth, single signature bowl
neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku — by the Chuden-mae tram stop, a short walk east of Peace Memorial Park
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町3丁目1-6
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 広島市中区大手町三丁目１番６号; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Around 19 seats (counter and tables); the official site lists 19, Tabelog lists 24.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 daily; closed the 6th of each month and irregular Sundays.
closed_days: The 6th of each month, plus irregular Sunday closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local ramen counter with a ticket-vending machine and a Japanese-only, one-dish menu, so a foreign student should be ready to point and order in Japanese.
gf_detail: The single dish is a wheat-noodle ramen in a soy-sauce-based broth (soy sauce contains wheat), so there is no gluten-free option here.
vegan_detail: The broth is simmered from pork bone and chicken with a soy-sauce tare, and the bowl is topped with char siu pork, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

bio.background: Yoki traces back to February 1958 (Showa 33), when Hara Hironori took over the noren of a same-named ramen stall in Hiroshima and began serving in the Eba district. It became one of the founding names of 'Hiroshima ramen,' and the shop expanded over the decades while keeping the recipe within the family. The Otemachi branch opened in February 2006.
bio.specialty: A single signature bowl of chuka soba: medium-thin noodles in a broth of pork bone and chicken simmered with vegetables, finished with a special soy-sauce tare, and topped with char siu, green onion, and thin bean sprouts.
bio.philosophy: The family keeps the taste deliberately unchanged across generations. Matriarch Koto's guidance was that every bowl, however routine for the cooks, is a singular moment for the customer and must be prepared with sincerity so guests 'eat the noodles happily and go home pleased.'

anecdotes.text: The shop was born when Hironori, who came to Hiroshima for work and church-construction service tied to his faith, was handed the noren of a stall already named Yoki and carried it on.

The recipe passed from Hironori and his wife Koto to their son Tsutomu and his wife Yumi; after Tsutomu died of liver cancer at 55, Yumi carried the shop forward with her family while preserving the original flavor.
Founded in February 1958, Yoki grew from the Eba stall into a small family of branches across Hiroshima and Fukuyama, with the Otemachi shop opening in February 2006.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only dish is wheat-noodle ramen, so wheat is central to the kitchen and there is no gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a special soy-sauce tare; ordinary soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork bone and chicken simmered with vegetables, so even the broth is animal-based with no vegan version.

caveats: Seat count differs between sources (official site 19 vs Tabelog 24); used 19. No official allergy/gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented, and the menu is effectively a single wheat-noodle, pork-and-chicken-broth ramen. Hours can be affected by the monthly 6th closure and irregular Sundays, so confirm by phone before a special trip.

lead: 汁なし担々麺 きさく
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 きさく
cuisine: 汁なし担担麺
gf_detail: The single signature dish is soup-less tantanmen built on wheat ramen noodles, so it is not gluten-free. The savory sauce blends chicken and dried-sardine broth with Sichuan peppercorn and almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce or seasonings. There is no gluten-free menu or noodle option, and the kitchen is a tiny single-dish noodle counter not set up for gluten avoidance. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The house tantanmen is topped with ground meat and dressed in a sauce made from chicken and dried-sardine (niboshi) broth, so the core dish is not vegan or vegetarian. The optional soft-boiled egg topping and the wheat noodles add further animal/non-vegan elements. There is no documented plant-based version. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区舟入川口町5-13 佐々木ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the GSI address-search result for 舟入川口町5-13; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima bounding box. Note the target listed the neighborhood as 舟入幸町 (the name of the nearby Hiroden tram stop, about 1 minute on foot), but the verified street address is 舟入川口町.
neighborhood: 舟入川口町（中区・舟入幸町電停すぐ）
seats_text: カウンター15席
closed_days: 水曜日（加えて月に1日の不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, cash-only 15-seat counter run by locals where Japanese is the working language and ordering is quick and simple; point at the one signature dish and you'll be fine, but don't expect English menus or staff.

bio.background: Kisaku opened in central Hiroshima in 2001 as an ordinary ramen shop, but the owner struggled to draw customers. Determined to turn things around, he traveled to Sichuan Province in China to study tantanmen at the source, then spent months experimenting back home to adapt what he had learned. The result was a soup-less tantanmen that went on to become a Hiroshima staple, with Kisaku widely regarded as the original shop that started the city's now-famous shirunashi tantanmen.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (founded here in central Funairi, Naka Ward)
bio.specialty: Soup-less tantanmen kept deliberately minimal: just noodles, green onion and ground meat, dressed in a secret sauce that marries chicken and dried-sardine broth with the tingling numbness of Sichuan peppercorn (sansho/huajiao). Diners are told to lift and toss the noodles from the bottom at least ten times before eating so the sauce coats everything.
bio.philosophy: A generous, no-frills counter ethos: a single dish done well, adjustable spice levels (including half and quarter heat), and famously cheap white rice (about 50 yen) offered all-you-can-eat to pair with the noodles.

anecdotes.text: The founder went all the way to Sichuan to apprentice in tantanmen and reworked the recipe at home until he landed on the soup-less version that locals now treat as the origin of the genre in Hiroshima.
House guidance: mix the noodles by lifting from the bottom more than ten times before the first bite so the sauce and peppercorn distribute evenly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a single-dish wheat-noodle counter; noodles are boiled in shared water and the sauce contains gluten-bearing seasonings, so gluten contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature sauce is a chicken-and-dried-sardine broth blend; no gluten-free soy sauce is indicated and standard wheat-based seasoning should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core dish uses ground meat and a fish/chicken broth sauce, and is prepared on a single shared counter line, so animal ingredients are integral to the only menu item.

caveats: The neighborhood given in the target (舟入幸町) is the name of the nearby tram stop; the verified street address is 舟入川口町5-13. The shop closes Wednesdays plus one irregular day per month, is cash only, takes no reservations, and seats only 15 at a counter, so expect queues at peak times. Hours marked irregular due to the monthly floating closure.

lead: 汁なし担々麺 麻沙羅（まさら）
name_ja: 汁なし担々麺 麻沙羅
cuisine: Soupless dan dan noodles (汁なし担々麺) specialist — Hiroshima-style brothless tantanmen with 5 spice levels
neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, near Hondori/Hatchobori shopping arcades)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-20 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [132.459747, 34.393978] fall within the central Hiroshima box, consistent with a Tatemachi location.
seats_text: 12 seats total (8 counter, 4 table); counter has lower chairs designed to be comfortable for female customers
hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon–Sat 11:00–15:00, 17:30–21:00; Sun closed. Other listings give 11:30–15:00 / 17:30–21:00 with a Monday close and no dinner on Sundays/holidays. Hours vary by source and irregular closures occur — check the shop's SNS.
closed_days: Sunday (per Tabelog); some listings cite Monday closed and no dinner on Sundays/holidays. Irregular closures occur — confirm on the shop's social media.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat noodle counter where you order by pointing at a spice level (0–4) and a sansho-pepper level; a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, though the menu is simple enough to manage with gestures.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is wheat noodles (thick flat noodles made from Japanese wheat), and tantanmen sauces typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Dan dan noodles here are built on a meat-and-sesame base and the sauces commonly include animal-derived stock; no vegan version is documented. The only meat-free finishing step (mixing leftover sauce with rice) still uses the non-vegan sauce.

bio.background: Opened in 2013 in the Tatemachi district of central Hiroshima, Masara is a small counter shop devoted entirely to shirunashi tantanmen, the city's beloved brothless dan dan noodles. The name Masara is taken from masala, the Indian spice blend used in curry, signaling the shop's fixation on aromatics.
bio.specialty: Brothless dan dan noodles served with thick, flat house noodles made from Japanese wheat using a slow-hydration fermentation method so they soak up the sauce; diners choose a spice level and a separate Sichuan-pepper numbing level, and finish by stirring the leftover sauce into rice.
bio.philosophy: A spice-forward approach: around ten spices including cloves, star anise and cinnamon are blended differently for each menu item, layering sesame richness with Sichuan numbing heat.

anecdotes.text: The Sichuan tantanmen began as a limited offering for the shop's third anniversary and proved so popular it became a permanent menu fixture.
The counter chairs are set lower than usual so that female customers can eat comfortably.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dishes are wheat-noodle dan dan noodles whose sauces typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce and sesame paste; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dan dan noodles here are prepared with a meat-and-sesame sauce base; no vegan option or separate preparation is documented.

caveats: Hours differ across listings and irregular closures occur, so confirm same-day via the shop's social media. The official site (spicymasara.com) is live but could not be fetched here due to a TLS certificate name mismatch; address, hours and seats were cross-confirmed via Tabelog and Gnavi.

lead: 五エ門 胡町本店（ごえもん）
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・創作鉄板焼き
neighborhood: 広島市中区胡町（広電・胡町／八丁堀電停すぐ）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町3-14 アーバン胡館 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (広島市中区胡町3-14); within Hiroshima box, lands in central Naka-ku near Hatchobori/Ebisucho.
seats_text: 36席（カウンター12席、テーブル6卓）。一部資料では41席との記載もあり。
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, welcoming local okonomiyaki house where solo diners and newcomers are at ease, but staff speak mostly Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help you order comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles (described as 100% domestic wheat), and the kitchen is a shared teppan, so there is no safe gluten-free path here for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki and teppanyaki rely on pork, egg, seafood (oysters, anago/conger eel), and fish-derived sauces; no documented vegan offering.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly between sources (36 vs 41). Closures are irregular (不定休), so confirm before a special trip. Late-night close (around 01:00) on weekdays/Saturday.

bio.background: A locally founded, independent okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house in the heart of Hiroshima's downtown, a short walk from the Ebisucho/Hatchobori streetcar stops. Built-in grills at every table let guests enjoy everything piping hot, and the room is known for an energetic, casual atmosphere where staff calls and customer laughter fill the space.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki made with noodles of 100% domestic wheat that are freshly boiled then grilled crisp, plus over 60 teppanyaki dishes. The house signature is the 'Goemon-maki' (五ェ門巻き), born from staff meals and refined over time, combining yakisoba, pork, egg, and green onion with a spicy kimchi accent. Seasonal Hiroshima specialties like winter oyster butter-grill and anago also feature, paired with local sake and house nigori.
bio.philosophy: Self-described as an okonomiyaki shop that 'competes with flavor and energy' (味と元気で勝負するお好み焼き屋) — firmly seasoned, crisp-textured food served in a warm, high-spirited room.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'Goemon-maki' roll started as a staff meal and was perfected through repeated trial and error before joining the menu.
The shop carries the cheerful Hiroshima-dialect catchphrase 'まあいっぺん食うてみんさい、うまいけぇ！' (just give it a try, it's delicious!).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan grill alongside wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, so gluten contact is unavoidable across the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce typically contains wheat; no gluten-free sauce is documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are grilled on a communal teppan shared with pork, egg, and seafood (oysters, anago), making vegan separation impractical.

lead: クロワッサンマルシェ（Croissant Marche）
name_ja: クロワッサンマルシェ
cuisine: Macrobiotic / organic natural-food shop with daily vegan lunch and bento
vegan_detail: The two daily lunch sets (a macrobiotic plate and a macrobiotic curry, roughly 1,000 yen plus tax) are completely vegan — built on enzyme/germinated brown rice, local pesticide-free vegetables, tofu, hijiki and house-made dressings, with no animal products. The owners cook to the principle of "handmade food without animal products." Note: some of the prepared take-home bento occasionally include animal products following macrobiotic yin-yang balance, so confirm the specific bento before buying. The sit-down lunches themselves are reliably plant-based.
gf_detail: The shop sells a gluten-free sandwich loaf made with rice flour (米粉) instead of wheat, sold as a grocery item. The hot vegan lunches, however, are macrobiotic cooking that typically relies on soy sauce and miso — both usually made with wheat — so the lunch plates should not be assumed gluten-free. A celiac diner can buy the rice-flour bread but should ask, dish by dish, about seasonings before eating the lunch.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堺町1-3-4 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区堺町1-3-4 to [132.448456, 34.394314], within the Hiroshima box. The target brief listed 栄町, but every source gives the address as 中区堺町 1-3-4 — same district, a few minutes' walk west of Peace Memorial Park across the Honkawa, near the Tsuchibashi tram stop, consistent with the brief's location description.
seats_text: About 6 counter seats; the place is primarily a natural-food shop with a small eat-in lunch counter.
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (shop); lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00). Closed Sundays and public holidays.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run natural-food shop where English is unlikely, so come with your dietary words written in Japanese or a translation app, and pointing at the day's lunch works fine.
neighborhood: Sakaimachi, Naka-ku (a few minutes west of Peace Memorial Park, near the Tsuchibashi tram stop)
caveats: Address is 中区堺町1-3-4, not 栄町 as in the brief (same area). The hot vegan lunches use macrobiotic seasonings (soy sauce/miso) that typically contain wheat, so they are not gluten-free even though the shop sells a separate rice-flour GF bread. Take-home bento may occasionally contain animal products — confirm per item.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife shop that grew out of a general store the couple, Katsuji and Yuka Shinano, had run since 1982. Through years of talking with customers they came to want a place where people could both buy and sit down to eat genuinely healthful food, and they opened the natural-food shop in 2006, relocating to its current spot a short walk from Peace Memorial Park in 2014.
bio.specialty: Macrobiotic vegan lunches and bento built on enzyme/germinated brown rice and local pesticide-free vegetables, alongside a shop of organic groceries, condiments, sweets, and rice-flour bread.
bio.philosophy: Their guiding idea is that you don't need to force yourself to eat animal products — everything is handmade without them, leaning on whole grains, vegetables and seaweed in the macrobiotic tradition. Yuka leaned toward plant-based eating from childhood, and Katsuji came to it through brown rice and macrobiotics.

anecdotes.text: The shop is regarded as one of the pioneers of vegetarian dining in Hiroshima, having moved beside Peace Memorial Park in 2014 to make health-conscious food easy to find downtown.
Now in his eighties, founder Katsuji still works at the shop daily, crediting the couple's vitality to a diet built on brown rice with very little animal food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The hot macrobiotic lunches use soy sauce and miso, which are normally wheat-based, so the lunch plates are not gluten-free; only the separately sold rice-flour bread is a GF item.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Macrobiotic seasoning (soy sauce/miso) is used in the cooking and should be assumed to contain wheat unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Some prepared take-home bento occasionally include animal products following macrobiotic yin-yang balance, so a vegan should confirm the specific bento before buying; the dine-in lunch sets are fully vegan.

positives.text: The daily lunch sets are entirely plant-based and made by hand without animal products, using local pesticide-free vegetables and germinated brown rice.
A gluten-free bread made from rice flour instead of wheat is sold in the shop for diners who avoid gluten.

cuisine: Oyster cafe (Hiroshima oyster oil-zuke, local lemon sweets) in a renovated traditional house
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), on a hilltop near the five-story pagoda within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine and the Omotesando shopping street
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町422
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県廿日市市宮島町422; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Described as a wood-centered interior with spacious seating and generous spacing between tables, plus an outdoor terrace deck. Children under 10 are not permitted.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-17:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00-17:30 (Tabelog). One source lists 12:00-16:30. Irregular closures (不定休).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, design-conscious local cafe tucked away from the tourist crowds; a few words of Japanese and a calm, respectful manner go a long way, and note it's cash-only with no children under 10.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature lemon rare cheesecake and other cakes typically contain wheat, and the oyster oil-zuke is not a GF-certified product. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: This is an oyster-focused cafe. The signature items are oyster oil preserves (shellfish) and dairy-based cheesecake, so the core offering is neither vegan nor vegetarian. No vegan menu is documented; at most a black coffee or plain drink might be plant-based, but nothing is confirmed.
caveats: Cash only. Children under 10 not permitted. Irregular closing days — verify before visiting. Hours differ between sources (11:00-17:00/17:30 vs 12:00-16:30); arrive midday to be safe. About a 7-15 minute uphill walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, on a quiet back lane.

bio.background: Kakiwai is the second venture from Kakiya, a well-known oyster specialty shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street. It occupies a renovated traditional wooden house set on a quiet hill above the tourist bustle, near the five-story pagoda and within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Kakiya's oyster oil-zuke (oysters preserved in oil) made by hand from high-quality Hiroshima oysters that rarely reach the wider market, alongside a Hiroshima/Setouchi lemon rare cheesecake.
bio.philosophy: The space is built around silence and whitespace, with carefully chosen art, craft and tableware, aiming to offer the island's original, everyday sense of time rather than a commercialized tourist experience.

anecdotes.text: The hilltop terrace looks out over Miyajima's rooftops, the five-story pagoda and Senjokaku, and the Ono Strait with its oyster rafts on the Seto Inland Sea, making it one of the island's quietly celebrated viewpoints.
The signature oyster oil-zuke is described as having a cheese-like richness yet finishing light and not greasy, made in the Miyajima tradition from prized local oysters.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on oysters (shellfish) and dairy-based cakes, so shared surfaces and utensils make this unsuitable as a vegan-safe environment; no vegan handling is documented.

cuisine: 自家焙煎コーヒー・カフェ（軽食・スイーツ）
neighborhood: 宮島・厳島（表参道商店街／清盛通り）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町464-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.331451, 34.348637] for 広島県廿日市市宮島町464-3; within the Hiroshima box and consistent with Miyajima island's main approach street.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, tourist-friendly coffee shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will be perfectly fine, though detailed allergy questions in English may be hard to answer.
vegan_detail: 焙煎コーヒーが主役のカフェで、フードはひろしま牡蠣を使った丼や和風パスタなどの軽食、ケーキやソフトクリーム・パフェなどのスイーツが中心。乳製品・小麦・魚介を用いた品が多く、ヴィーガン専用メニューは確認できませんでした。ブラックコーヒー自体はプラントベースですが、植物性ミルクの有無は要確認です。
gf_detail: グルテンフリー専用の表示は確認できませんでした。ケーキ類は小麦、和風パスタも小麦麺で、グルテンを含むフードが中心です。コーヒーそのものはグルテンを含みませんが、フードでのGF対応は来店時に直接確認が必要です。
caveats: TARGET記載の「築100年超の町家を改装」「宮島桟橋から徒歩約7分」「個人の小規模店」は公式サイト・食べログでは裏付けが取れませんでした。公式情報では2024年に改装した木調内装の2フロア・全60席の店で、海岸通りに2号店もあり、徒歩約10分（公式）です。これらは未確認のため記録に反映していません。

bio.background: 宮島・厳島神社へ続く表参道商店街（清盛通り）沿いにある自家焙煎のコーヒー店。世界各地から訪れる観光客や参拝客に向けて、毎日たくさんの人が立ち寄る島のひと休みの場として営まれています。
bio.specialty: 「宮島ロースト」と名付けた自家焙煎にこだわり、世界各国の豆を選び抜いて焙煎した鮮度の高いコーヒーを日々提供。店で味わった豆は持ち帰りやオンラインでも購入でき、自宅でも焼きたての一杯を楽しめます。フードはひろしま牡蠣を使った丼や和風パスタ、自家製ケーキ、ソフトクリームやわらびもちをのせたパフェなど。
bio.philosophy: 文化や価値観、味の好みもさまざまな国内外の来島者みんなに美味しいコーヒーを届けたい、という思いを掲げています。

anecdotes.text: 2024年に店内を改装し、木のぬくもりを感じられるウッド調の2階席を備えた2フロア構成に。海岸通り沿いには2号店も構えています。

cuisine: ラーメン・つけ麺（魚介出汁、のどぐろ・鰹・煮干し／個人店）
neighborhood: 中区胡町（流川・薬研堀の繁華街エリア、平和記念公園から東へ約1.5km）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町2-25 アクロスビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区胡町2-25 returned [132.466568, 34.392574], within the Hiroshima box. Note: Tabelog lists the address as 胡町 (in the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife district), not literally 薬研堀.
seats_text: Counter seating only; sources list 12–13 counter seats.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open year-round per Tabelog); irregular closures possible — check Instagram/X before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only ramen bar where ordering is quick and Japanese-only; point at the menu, sit at the counter, and be ready to eat promptly since seats turn over fast.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop; the noodles are wheat-based and the soy-sauce (shoyu) broths typically contain wheat, so dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: The shop's identity is built on fish/seafood dashi — nodoguro (blackthroat seabream), katsuo (bonito), and niboshi (anchovy) broths — plus chashu pork and seasoned eggs. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: The brief's area label (薬研堀) is approximate; the documented address is 中区胡町, within the same Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife district about 1.5 km east of the Peace Memorial Park. Seat count varies by source (12 vs 13). Hours are from Tabelog; confirm via the shop's Instagram/X as a small independent shop may close irregularly.

bio.background: The owner, who goes by 'nao' on social media, is a Hiroshima native who trained within the acclaimed Osaka ramen lineage of Jinrui Mina Menrui (世界が麺で満ちる時) before opening his own shop here on July 21, 2019.
bio.specialty: Seafood-forward shoyu ramen and abura-soba built on delicate fish dashi — the menu runs several styles of soy-sauce ramen and oil ramen, with prized daily-limited bowls such as the nodoguro (blackthroat seabream) shoyu ramen, alongside katsuo (bonito) and citrus-and-shellfish variations.
bio.philosophy: A focus on refined, aromatic seafood broths in the soba-like tradition of his Osaka training, served at a small counter with frequent limited-edition bowls.

anecdotes.text: The name plays on a proverb of perseverance — and the kitchen lives it through ever-changing limited bowls, including a celebrated nodoguro (blackthroat seabream) soy-sauce ramen and an 'orange and shellfish' shoyu creation that regulars seek out.
Diners describe the broth as carrying a fragrant bonito aroma reminiscent of fine soba dashi, paired with thin, rare-cut chashu and a precisely cooked seasoned egg.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop's signature ramen are soy-sauce (shoyu) based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the ramen noodles themselves are wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are fish/seafood dashi (nodoguro, bonito, niboshi) cooked in shared pots, with pork chashu and egg as standard toppings, so no bowl is free of animal products.

name_ja: 間借麺業 あらや識
cuisine: 煮干し醤油らぁ麺・昆布水つけ麺 (niboshi-shoyu ramen and kombu-water tsukemen)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dishes are ramen and tsukemen built on wheat noodles (hand-kneaded medium-thick noodles), and the soy-sauce-based broth contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is animal-based, made from Shiga-raised black chicken (Omi kurodori) combined with several dried fish ingredients including Ibuki Island niboshi (dried anchovy). Toppings include marinated egg, duck, and pork. No plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町6-15
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区銀山町6-15 via GSI; first feature coordinates [132.467316, 34.391922], within the Hiroshima box. Located in the Ginzan-machi / Nagarekawa area of central Hiroshima.
seats_text: Counter seats only; a small shop that fills to capacity around lunchtime (exact seat count not published).
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (lunch only); closed Sunday
closed_days: Sunday (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only lunch spot run by one cook; ordering is in Japanese and it is cash only, so a foreign student should be ready with basic Japanese and come prepared with yen.
neighborhood: 中区銀山町（流川・薬研堀エリア）
caveats: Lunch only (11:00-14:30) and closed Sundays; cash only and no reservations. Operates as a "borrowed-space" (間借り) lunch business inside a charcoal-grill izakaya (炭火焼 西尾), so the daytime ramen shop and the evening yakitori shop share the same premises. Small counter that fills up at midday, so expect a wait or arrive early.

bio.background: Araya Shiki is a 'magari mengyo' (borrowed-space noodle business): a lunch-only ramen operation that sets up inside the premises of a charcoal-grill izakaya, serving from late morning until early afternoon before the space returns to its evening life. It is a small, counter-only place tucked into central Hiroshima's lively Nagarekawa nightlife district.
bio.specialty: A delicate light soy-sauce ramen (tanrei shoyu ra-men) and a kombu-water tsukemen that is still uncommon in Hiroshima. The clear broth is built from Shiga-raised black chicken (Omi kurodori) blended with about four kinds of dried seafood, including niboshi (dried anchovy) from Ibuki Island, and paired with smooth, medium-thick hand-kneaded noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a lunch-only 'borrowed-space' venture, cooking inside a charcoal-grill izakaya during the day and serving a refined chicken-and-niboshi shoyu bowl rather than a heavy tonkotsu style.
Offering a kombu-water tsukemen as a standing menu item is noted as a rarity for Hiroshima.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles and wheat-containing soy-sauce broth are the core of every dish, so gluten is present throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in standard Japanese ramen contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from chicken and multiple dried fish (niboshi), and the shop is a daytime tenant inside a charcoal-grill (meat/fish) izakaya, so animal ingredients are pervasive.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Hatchobori (Naka-ku, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀8-7
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [132.461777, 34.39584] for 広島県広島市中区八丁堀8-7; within the Hiroshima bounding box, near Hatchobori in central Hiroshima.
seats_text: 18 seats total: a 2-seat table-counter (no griddle), a 6-seat counter, and a sunken-floor area seating up to 12.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 daily (last order food 21:00, drinks 21:30). Counter seating only 13:00-17:30. Closed year-end/New Year.
closed_days: Year-end / New Year holidays (年末年始); otherwise open daily.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood okonomiyaki counter popular with local office workers; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English is unlikely.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with wheat-flour batter and wheat soba/ramen noodles, finished with a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce. Teppanyaki and izakaya dishes also rely on wheat-containing sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature dishes center on pork, egg, and noodles, with Hiroshima oysters and beef offal among the teppanyaki options; the okonomiyaki sauce and batter are not plant-based, and no vegan items are documented.
caveats: Family/owner-specific biographical details are not documented on the official site or listings; the "family-run individual shop" framing comes from the target brief and the shop's small, independent local character rather than a sourced owner profile. Hours note a counter-seating-only window 13:00-17:30.

bio.background: A small, long-running okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter tucked into Hiroshima's Hatchobori business district, just south of the Hiroshima YMCA. It has become a go-to for local office workers seeking an unfussy, satisfying plate.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style 肉玉そば (pork-and-egg okonomiyaki layered with noodles), built since opening around ultra-thin fresh noodles. A house variation, the spicy '悟空焼き' (Goku-yaki) loaded with chili, is named for the shop itself; aburi-cheese okonomiyaki is another recommendation.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen presses each okonomiyaki by hand so the ingredients meld together, staying focused on doing one classic dish well.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature spicy 'Goku-yaki' uses extremely thin noodles spiked with habanero and topped with a heap of chili powder, named after the restaurant.
Sits on the south side of the Hiroshima YMCA professional school with a clean, black-toned interior, and is a regular haunt of local office workers.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is finished with a standard okonomiyaki/soy-based sauce, which contains wheat; the batter and noodles are also wheat.

lead: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 じぞう通り本店
cuisine: 汁なし担々麺 (Hiroshima soupless dandan noodles)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is wheat-based ramen-style noodles (low-hydration wheat noodles), and the savory sauce and toppings typically include soy-sauce-based seasoning. No gluten-free menu or noodle alternative is documented. Celiac/gluten-sensitive travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The core dish is built around ground pork (挽き肉) mixed into wheat noodles, plus chili/Sichuan-pepper sauce that is animal- and soy-based. No vegan or vegetarian version is documented on the official site or listings.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区富士見町5-12 田ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.387585, lng 132.46022) in central Naka-ku, Tatemachi/Fujimi-cho area, walkable from Hondori and the A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 中区富士見町・立町 (Fujimi-cho / Tatemachi, central Hiroshima, near Hondori and the A-Bomb Dome)
seats_text: About 20 seats; small counter/table noodle shop, all non-smoking.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, fast-paced local noodle shop where ordering is simple (pick a spice level) and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will get you through comfortably.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations and it can have a queue at peak times. Wheat noodles and pork-based sauce make it unsuitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. The brand now has several Hiroshima locations; this record is the Jizo-dori main shop in Fujimi-cho.

bio.background: Musashibo is a specialist in Hiroshima's beloved shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles), the brothless, mix-it-yourself style of spicy Sichuan-influenced noodles that became a citywide craze. The shop is part of the wave of dedicated tantanmen houses that grew out of Hiroshima's noodle revival, and it has since become one of the city's go-to names for the dish.
bio.specialty: Shiru-nashi tantanmen: low-hydration wheat noodles tossed with a spicy savory sauce, ground pork, scallions and the shop's own blend of Sichuan pepper. Diners are encouraged to first taste it as-is, then dial it in with four kinds of Sichuan pepper, a splash of vinegar, and house sauce.
bio.philosophy: Bring the specialty-shop flavor straight to the bowl (and to the home kitchen via their mail-order sets), emphasizing the interplay of fragrant Sichuan pepper, chili, and freshly mixed noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop is closely tied to Hiroshima's tantanmen revival led by the pioneering shop Kunimatsu, and the two have even joined forces in a collaboration counter at Hiroshima Station's ekie complex.
Customers are guided to eat it in stages, first plain, then adding four varieties of Sichuan pepper, vinegar, and the house sauce to adjust the heat and aroma to taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The dish is built on wheat noodles, so the entire menu centers on gluten; there is no documented gluten-free preparation or separate handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The savory sauce for shiru-nashi tantanmen is typically soy-sauce based, which in Japan generally contains wheat; no gluten-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ground pork is mixed directly into the noodles as a core component, so all bowls are prepared with meat and there is no documented vegan separation.

lead: 汁なし担々麺 麻沙羅
name_ja: 汁なし担々麺 麻沙羅
cuisine: 汁なし担々麺 (brothless / soupless tantanmen, Sichuan-style dandan noodles)
neighborhood: 中区立町（国際通り沿い、袋町電停・原爆ドーム近く / Tatemachi, Naka-ku, near A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-20 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市中区立町3-20 to [132.459747, 34.393978], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with central Naka-ku near the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 12 seats (8 counter, 4 table); counter chairs set low for comfort
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:30-21:00 (per current Tabelog listing). Some sources list lunch starting 11:30 and note no dinner / earlier close on Sundays & holidays. Irregular closures announced on SNS.
closed_days: Sunday (per current post-relocation listing). Sources vary: one lists Monday closed with no Sunday/holiday dinner; confirm via SNS/phone as irregular closures occur.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat counter ramen-style shop where ordering is simple (pick your dish, then dial chili heat and Sichuan numbing 0-4); a few words of Japanese and pointing go a long way, but no English menu is documented.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle shop: shiru-nashi tantanmen is built on Chinese-style wheat noodles, and the spicy meat sauce relies on soy- and wheat-based seasonings. No gluten-free noodles or menu are documented, and there is no indication of allergy accommodation.
vegan_detail: Standard tantanmen here uses minced pork and animal/soy-based seasonings, so it is not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian version is documented. The spice blend itself (cloves, star anise, cinnamon and others) is plant-based, but the dishes are not.
caveats: Listed hours and closed day differ between sources (current Tabelog listing: open Mon-Sat 11:00-15:00 & 17:30-21:00, closed Sun; gnavi/rurubu describe Monday closed and shortened/no Sunday-holiday dinner). The shop relocated from 大手町 to 立町3-20 and reopened on 11 Nov 2025; the originally supplied Tabelog URL (A340116/34018740) is the old, relocated listing. Irregular closures are posted on the shop's X/SNS, so confirm before visiting. Official site is served over HTTP only (HTTPS has an invalid certificate).

bio.background: An independent soupless-tantanmen specialty shop in central Hiroshima. It first opened in 2013 and built a following before relocating to a new home on the Kokusai-dori shopping street in Tatemachi, reopening there in November 2025.
bio.specialty: Brothless (shiru-nashi) tantanmen finished with a house blend of about ten spices tuned to each dish, with variations such as Sichuan-style, chili-tomato, and spicy curry; diners set chili heat and Sichuan numbing separately on a 0-4 scale.
bio.philosophy: A spice-forward, made-your-way approach: the name 'Masara' comes from the Indian word for a mixed-spice blend (masala), reflecting the shop's focus on layering aromatic spices like cloves, star anise and cinnamon. Small touches such as lowered counter chairs show attention to guest comfort.

anecdotes.text: The signature Sichuan-style soupless tantanmen began as a limited 3rd-anniversary special, but proved so popular with regulars that it became a permanent menu item.
The shop name 'Masara' is taken from the Indian term for mixed spices (masala), and the kitchen blends around ten spices to match each dish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the base of every dish in this shiru-nashi tantanmen shop, so the kitchen and water are inherently wheat-heavy; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The spicy tantanmen sauces rely on standard soy- and wheat-based Chinese-style seasonings; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes use minced pork and animal-based seasoning; no separate vegan preparation or station is documented.

positives.text: Heat and Sichuan-pepper numbing levels are adjustable 0-4, so spice-sensitive diners can order a mild bowl.

lead: 鉄板 お好み焼 くらはち
name_ja: 鉄板 お好み焼 くらはち
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼（Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki & teppanyaki izakaya）
neighborhood: 中区流川（銀山町電停近く、流川の路地裏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町5-3 白馬ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町5-3 returned [132.46489, 34.389694], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in the Nagarekawa nightlife district of central Naka-ku, consistent with the listed address.
seats_text: 20 seats total: 6 counter, 8 table, 6 tatami (zashiki). Small private floor rental available for groups of roughly 12-20.
hours_raw: Official site: 18:00-03:00 (L.O. 02:30), closed Tuesday and Wednesday. Aggregator listings vary: HotPepper shows 18:00-02:00 daily with no regular closed day; Tabelog shows 18:00-01:00. Dinner-only.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (per official site; some aggregator listings show no regular closing day, so confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, friendly local teppan izakaya that welcomes solo diners and groups alike, but it is a small Japanese-language neighborhood spot, so basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and teppan dishes are typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians as a destination. The signature okonomiyaki includes egg, pork, and seafood, and the menu centers on Hiroshima oysters, teppan meat/seafood dishes, and izakaya fare cooked on a shared griddle. Sauces use fish-derived and wheat-based ingredients. No vegan options are documented; the pesticide-free vegetable side dishes may be the only plant-forward items, but preparation is not confirmed vegan.

bio.background: Tucked into a back alley off the main Nagarekawa strip, a short walk from the Ginzan-cho streetcar stop, Kurahachi is a small, relaxed teppan-and-okonomiyaki izakaya in the heart of central Hiroshima's nightlife district. It is the kind of unpretentious neighborhood spot where you can drop in for a quiet solo drink at the counter or take over the floor with a group.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'Kurahachi-yaki,' built on crisp griddled noodles made by a dedicated noodle maker. The kitchen deliberately tops the okonomiyaki with a raw egg so guests can break and fold it in to cook it to their own taste, and offers generous toppings like Hiroshima oysters, kimchi, and avocado for customizing. Alongside the okonomiyaki are teppan dishes, izakaya plates, and seasonal one-plate dishes made with pesticide-free vegetables from Sakishima (Saki Island) in Mihara.
bio.philosophy: The owners aim for a warm, easygoing atmosphere where anyone, from solo drinkers to large parties, can relax without standing on ceremony.

anecdotes.text: The crisp noodles in the signature Kurahachi-yaki come from a dedicated noodle maker, and the egg is served raw on top so guests cook and mix it to their own preference.
Seasonal one-plate dishes are made with pesticide-free vegetables grown on Saki Island in Mihara.

caveats: Operating hours and closed days differ between the official website (Tue/Wed closed, 18:00-03:00) and aggregator listings (no regular closing day, 18:00-01:00 or 18:00-02:00). The hours_week reflects the official site; confirm directly before visiting. This is a meat/seafood/wheat-centric okonomiyaki izakaya with no documented gluten-free or vegan accommodation.

cuisine: Momiji-manju (freshly baked maple-leaf cakes), Japanese sweets / wagashi
neighborhood: Miyajima, near Itsukushima Shrine (on the route toward Daisho-in)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町中江町304-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI address search; first feature coordinates [132.331451, 34.348637]. Lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box, consistent with the 宮島町 address.
seats_text: About 10 seats; there is a small eat-in / rest space (a seating area across the street) where freshly-baked momiji manju can be enjoyed hot.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (Tabelog). Official/secondary source notes 9:00-17:00, closes when sold out. 不定休 (irregular holidays); some sources note Wednesday closures (subject to variation). May close early once sold out.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休); may close early when sold out. Some sources cite Wednesday as a typical closing day, but this varies — check the official site.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional family sweet shop where a smile, a point, and a few words of Japanese go a long way; English is unlikely, but ordering momiji manju is simple and friendly.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Momiji manju are baked from a wheat-flour batter (a castella-style sponge), so the cakes contain gluten. There is no gluten-free option here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Momiji manju batter is conventionally made with eggs (and often honey) in addition to wheat flour and sugar; the filling is sweet azuki bean paste (Hokkaido azuki + white sugar), which is plant-based, but the cake itself is not vegan. No vegan version is documented.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (Tabelog lists 10:00-17:00; the shop/secondary sources cite 9:00-17:00). Closing days are irregular and the shop may sell out and close early. Ingredient details for the batter (egg/honey) follow the standard momiji-manju recipe; the shop documents only the bean paste ingredients (Hokkaido azuki and white sugar), so confirm batter allergens directly if needed.

bio.background: Iwamura Momijiya is one of the oldest momiji-manju makers on Miyajima, founded at the end of the Meiji era (early 1900s) and run by the Iwamura family for generations. It sits a little off the main shopping arcade, along the route between Itsukushima Shrine and Daisho-in temple, and is known to locals as a quiet, understated gem.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked momiji manju — both the classic smooth koshian and the shop's signature tsubu-an version ('tsubu-momiji') — made with custom house-cooked bean paste from carefully selected Hokkaido azuki and white sugar, the paste simmered for six to eight hours. Many visitors single out the aroma of the bean paste and the pleasure of eating the cakes warm, fresh off the iron.
bio.philosophy: A dedication to handcrafted bean paste: using the best azuki of the year and slow-cooking it for hours, and baking the cakes to order so guests can eat them hot.

anecdotes.text: The shop is credited with originating the tsubu-an (whole-bean) momiji manju: when Prince Takamatsu visited Itsukushima on May 10, 1934, he asked the then-owner Iwamura Eikichi, 'Don't you have one with tsubu-an?' — and that question led to the creation of 'tsubu-momiji'.
Its tsubu-momiji was honored with the Chairman's Award at the 21st National Confectionery Exposition in 1988.

A visitor recounts arriving to find the shop had sold out for the day, yet a young staff member kindly offered them a sample cake that hadn't met the selling weight — and notes the wonderful aroma of the bean paste outside the shop.

positives.text: The bean paste filling is made simply from carefully selected Hokkaido azuki beans and white granulated sugar, with no documented additives.

cuisine: Cafe / specialty coffee (independent coffee shop)
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando Shopping Street
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町464-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode resolved to Miyajima-cho, Hatsukaichi (the island); within the Hiroshima box. Coordinates are town-level for 宮島町 area, near the Omotesando shopping street between the pier and Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: 60 seats total (1F: 24, 2F: 36)
hours_raw: Mon-Fri & Sun 9:00-18:00; Sat 9:00-19:00; open year-round (無休)
closed_days: None (open year-round / 無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, walk-in coffee shop on Miyajima's main tourist street that is used to serving international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably without much Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The coffee drinks and plain soft serve are naturally wheat-free, but food items (hot sandwiches, curry, momiji manju) contain wheat, and there is no stated allergen handling or dedicated preparation. Travelers needing strict GF should treat this as a drinks-only stop and confirm in person.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature items (affogato, soft serve, cafe latte) contain dairy; hot sandwiches and curry are not stated to be plant-based. A black drip coffee or espresso would be the only reliably plant-based option, but no vegan claims are made by the shop.
caveats: Coffee-focused cafe, not a dietary-destination restaurant. No documented gluten-free or vegan program; allergen/cross-contact details are not published, so anyone with celiac disease or a strict plant-based diet should confirm at the counter. Geocode is town-level for Miyajima island.

bio.background: A specialty coffee shop set in a renovated merchant house more than 100 years old, standing on the Omotesando shopping street that visitors walk between the ferry pier and Itsukushima Shrine. The interior keeps the old-house character, with a dirt-floor-style entry, counter and single seats downstairs, and a more spacious wood-accented second floor added in a recent renovation as a calm rest stop amid the busy street.
bio.specialty: House-roasted 'Miyajima Roast' coffee — drip, espresso and cafe latte — alongside affogato (espresso, matcha or chocolate), soft serve, hot sandwiches and curry.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its aim as serving fresh, high-quality coffee roasted on Miyajima so that visitors from many countries, cultures and tastes can all enjoy a good cup.

anecdotes.text: Opening at 9 a.m., it is one of the rare spots on Miyajima where you can get a proper coffee and a light breakfast early, before the shrine crowds build.
The building is a renovation of a 100-year-old merchant house, blending a rustic old-folk-house feel with a casual, walk-in cafe atmosphere.

positives.text: Plain black drip coffee and espresso are naturally free of gluten and animal products, giving GF and vegan travelers a reliable basic drink option.

lead: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 上八丁堀店
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 上八丁堀店
cuisine: 汁なし担々麺（ブロスなし担々麺）の専門店
neighborhood: 広島市中区上八丁堀（八丁堀・縮景園前）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区上八丁堀5-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区上八丁堀5-1 returned [132.464279, 34.400028], within the Hiroshima box; lands in the Kamihatchobori district near Shukkeien, consistent with the listed address.
seats_text: 22席（カウンター14席、テーブル8席）
closed_days: 日曜日（土曜は昼のみ営業。営業時間は情報源により夜の終了時刻に差があり要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local noodle counter where ordering is quick and menu Japanese-only — knowing the dish name and a few words of Japanese helps, and bring cash since cards are not accepted.
gf_detail: The single signature dish is shirunashi tantanmen built on wheat noodles, dressed with a soy-sauce-based or sesame-based tare and topped with ground pork. Wheat noodles and soy sauce mean it is not gluten-free, and there is no documented gluten-free option or dedicated preparation.
vegan_detail: The dish is centered on ground pork (挽き肉) over wheat noodles with a soy-sauce/sesame tare, so it is not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based version is documented.

bio.background: A specialist devoted almost entirely to one dish: Hiroshima-style shirunashi tantanmen, the city's beloved brothless dan dan noodle. The shop grew into multiple locations around Hiroshima (with a branch reaching as far as Yokohama) on the strength of that single focus, and is reported to share ownership lineage with a well-known local yakitori and Hiroshima tsukemen establishment.
bio.origin: 広島市中区上八丁堀（八丁堀・縮景園前エリア）
bio.specialty: 汁なし担々麺。低加水のバツバツ麺に挽き肉・ネギ・自家製タレを絡め、四種の山椒・高麗人参酢・自家製タレで味変しながら食べるスタイル。芳醇醤油と濃厚ゴマの2系統がある。
bio.philosophy: まずはそのまま麺と具をよく混ぜて味わい、その後に四種の山椒や高麗人参酢で自分好みに変化させて楽しむという食べ方を勧めている。

anecdotes.text: 麺はバツバツの低加水で、すべての具材を絡めて食べるのが身上。卓上の四種の山椒・高麗人参酢・自家製タレで途中から味を変えていくのが店の薦める食べ方。
看板の汁なし担々麺は『芳醇醤油』と『濃厚ゴマ』の2系統で、香り高さが評判。サイドにシンガポールナスカレーなどもある。
