gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish uses low-hydration wheat noodles, so wheat is central to the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: One of the two signature styles is a soy-sauce (醤油) based tantanmen; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dish is topped with ground pork (挽き肉) as a standard component, mixed directly into the noodles.

caveats: Operating, cash-only, no reservations. Evening closing time differs between sources (Tabelog 21:00 vs a search snippet showing 21:30) and hours/holidays at small Hiroshima tantanmen shops vary, so hours_status is irregular and the dinner end time should be confirmed. No gluten-free or vegan options; the dish is wheat noodles with pork and soy/sesame tare. Bio confidence medium: founder name and explicit philosophy are not formally published, though the food style is well documented on the official site.

lead: お好み焼き・鉄板焼 福ちゃん
name_ja: お好み焼き・鉄板焼 福ちゃん
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼（Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki）
neighborhood: 広島市中区十日市町（寺町電停すぐ、平和記念公園の西側）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区十日市町2-2-17
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区十日市町2-2-17 returned [132.44838, 34.39959], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 23 seats total: 9 counter seats, plus tables (one 6-seat, two 4-seat). Counter seating suits solo diners; can accommodate groups up to ~20.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 12:00–22:00; Tue 14:30–22:00 (per Tabelog). Note: another local listing reported 11:00–20:00 with Monday closed; hours appear to vary, confirm directly.
closed_days: No fixed closing day clearly listed; verify directly with the shop.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly counter-style neighborhood okonomiyaki shop where a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way; English menus are not documented, so a foreign student should be comfortable pointing and ordering simple dishes.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the savory sauce is wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Dishes center on egg, pork, seafood and a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce; the iron plate is oiled with lard (ラード), and the house sauce is made with Hiroshima oysters. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Reported hours differ between sources (Tabelog lists 12:00–22:00 with a later Tuesday start; another local listing shows 11:00–20:00 closed Mondays), so hours are treated as irregular — confirm directly. No allergy/dietary accommodation information is documented; gluten-free and vegan diners are not served by the menu.

bio.background: Opened in March 2020 by a young chef surnamed Kato, who honed his okonomiyaki craft at Hiroshima station-area shops and ran a location in Tokyo's Harajuku before returning to open his own place in Hiroshima. By a fitting coincidence, a long-running 福ちゃん had operated at this very spot for years until its elderly proprietor retired; the current shop took over the name and address but is an entirely fresh start with a new owner, new menu, and new flavors. It is run as a small husband-and-wife operation.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with signature creations like a scallion-loaded 山王焼き and a seared-cheese-with-perilla okonomiyaki, plus teppanyaki sides such as shiitake butter.
bio.philosophy: Kato's approach emphasizes meticulous technique and a gentle touch — careful cabbage preparation for ideal texture, and a distinctive house sauce (described as 広島わしらの結晶ソース) made with Hiroshima oysters and lemon for a bright, appetite-whetting acidity. The plate is finished with lard for added savoriness.

anecdotes.text: The shop carries the name and exact location of a beloved older 福ちゃん whose elderly owner retired; the current chef revived the spot in 2020 as a completely reimagined okonomiyaki shop while keeping the cherished name.
Before opening here, the chef worked at okonomiyaki spots near Hiroshima's stations and managed a branch in Tokyo's Harajuku.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is assembled on a shared griddle from a wheat-flour batter and includes wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact on the cook surface is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki/teppanyaki style relies on a wheat-based savory sauce (the house sauce is described as oyster-and-lemon based); assume the sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is oiled with pork lard and the house sauce uses Hiroshima oysters, so vegan diners face animal-product contact across the cooking surface and seasonings.

cuisine: Old-style pure kissaten (coffee house): self-roasted coffee, buttered toast, soft-boiled egg, sandwiches
neighborhood: Tokaichi-machi, Naka-ku (west of Peace Memorial Park, walking distance from the A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区十日市町1-3-38
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned [132.447739, 34.397125] for the chō-level address, inside the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Tokaichi-machi intersection.
seats_text: Counter and table seating; interior is more spacious than it appears from outside, with tables near the entrance and deeper inside. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 08:00-14:00; closed Monday and Sunday
closed_days: Monday and Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood coffee house with a loyal older local crowd and smoking allowed indoors; little to no English, so basic Japanese and a relaxed, respectful manner go a long way.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature food items are buttered toast and sandwiches, both made from wheat bread, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu or handling.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The core food is the "Tokaichi Service" set of coffee with buttered toast and a soft-boiled egg (both egg and butter are animal products), plus sandwiches. Coffee itself is plain, but there is no documented plant-milk or vegan food option.

bio.background: Tokaichi Sabou opened in October 1946, the year after the atomic bombing, started by the current proprietor's father after he was repatriated from China. It has served the Tokaichi neighborhood for well over seven decades from its corner spot directly in front of the Tokaichi-machi streetcar stop, keeping the warm, retro feel of an authentic old-school pure kissaten.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee and an all-day breakfast set. The house blend 'Tokaichi Mix' is bright and clean and easy to drink, while the darker, heavier 'Moon Blend' is favored by enthusiasts. The all-day 'Tokaichi Service' pairs a cup of coffee with buttered toast and a soft-boiled egg.
bio.philosophy: A neighborhood constant: a place where regulars of all ages come for their daily cup, and where the staff notice and worry when a familiar face stops appearing.

anecdotes.text: The 'Moon Blend' was inherited from a now-closed Hiroshima Station kissaten called 'Moon,' preserving that shop's beloved dark-roast flavor for its former customers.
Regulars range in age from about 3 to 94, and many have come for years; the staff become concerned when a long-time face disappears, wondering if they have fallen ill.
Founded in October 1946 by the proprietor's father after he returned from China, it is one of Hiroshima's long-surviving postwar coffee houses.

caveats: Smoking is allowed throughout the interior, which GF/vegan or smoke-sensitive travelers may want to note. Seat count is not published. Hours are short (morning to early afternoon) and the shop is closed Sundays and Mondays. Payment is assumed cash-based as no card/QR payment is listed; confirm on arrival.

lead: 自然食cafe&bar ゆらり（夢楽輪）
name_ja: 自然食cafe&bar ゆらり（夢楽輪）
cuisine: 自然食・オーガニックカフェ＆バー（ヴィーガン／ベジタリアン／グルテンフリー対応）
neighborhood: 広島市中区大手町（鷹野橋商店街／鷹野橋駅）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町5-10-19
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町5-10-19 returned [132.451645, 34.383789], within the Hiroshima box; lands in the Takanobashi shopping street area of Otemachi, central Hiroshima.
seats_text: 31席（ソファ4、カウンター5、テーブル2人席×3、テーブル4人席×3、座敷1組）
closed_days: 定休日なし（お盆・年末年始に3～5日休業）
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, casual organic cafe that explicitly aims to accommodate allergies, gluten-free and vegan diets, so a foreign student should feel comfortable asking about ingredients, though it helps to have your dietary needs written in Japanese or to call ahead.
vegan_detail: The cafe explicitly offers vegan and vegetarian menu items alongside its omnivore dishes (lunch options include a hamburger, fried chicken, curry, fish and gapao rice), so it is not fully vegan but accommodates vegans, ideally with advance notice. Organic, additive-free ingredients are used throughout.
gf_detail: Gluten-free menu items are explicitly available and the owners state that people avoiding gluten and those with allergies can dine comfortably; accommodations are best arranged with advance notice. It is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, so confirm specific dishes when ordering.
caveats: Hours and closed days have been changing: older listings (e.g., Hitosara/Gnavi) show "closed Mondays" with Tue-Sat 11:00-21:30, but the official site and Tabelog reflect a 2026 update with no regular closed day and a shorter Sun-Thu schedule. The hours_week reflects the current Tabelog/official schedule; confirm before visiting. Card and PayPay accepted (not cash-only).

bio.background: A natural-food cafe and bar tucked into the Takanobashi shopping street in central Hiroshima, run as an independent organic kitchen that sources its vegetables from the affiliated "Organic Kitchen" (オーガニックの台所).
bio.origin: The owner started the venture out of a wish to protect future generations (未来の若者を守りたい), responding to concerns about rising health problems linked to pesticides, chemical fertilizers and food additives.
bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is that the secret to deliciousness is subtraction (おいしさの秘訣は引き算): zero chemical pesticides, zero synthetic fertilizers, zero antibiotics and zero additives, letting ingredients keep their natural character. Their motto is "あなたの食べられるがここにある" — "what you can eat is here" — so that people with allergies, gluten-free or vegan needs can share a table comfortably.
bio.specialty: Organic, additive-free lunches with several rotating daily choices plus standards like a hamburger, fried chicken, curry, fish set, gapao rice and rice-ball sets; handmade organic sweets; and an evening menu of additive-free salads, small plates and rare organic craft beer, sake, shochu, gin and whiskey. House-made shio-koji (salt koji) and soy koji are made with naturally cultivated rice and sun-dried salt and also sold.

anecdotes.text: Prices are kept deliberately affordable to draw in younger customers and encourage a return to traditional, healthy Japanese eating habits.
The cafe hosts a bimonthly market called "ゆらり円日 (ゆらりえんにち)" on the second Sunday of even-numbered months, selling organic produce and additive-free foods, along with cooking classes and events.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owners state that people with allergies and those who are gluten-free can dine without worry (アレルギーの方も、グルテンフリーの方も気兼ねなく), and dietary restrictions are accommodated, ideally with advance notice.

positives.text: Explicit vegan, vegetarian and gluten-free menu items are offered.

All ingredients and seasonings are organic and additive-free, with no chemical pesticides, synthetic fertilizers, antibiotics or additives; seasonings like shio-koji are house-made from naturally cultivated rice and sun-dried salt.
The kitchen explicitly aims to let people with allergies, gluten-free and vegan needs all eat together at the same table.

cuisine: Organic plant-based cafe (vegan soft serve, fruit drinks)
gf_detail: The signature "白米の絹ソフト" (silk rice soft serve), made from organic Yamada Nishiki rice with rice milk, soy milk and coconut milk, is documented as egg-, dairy- and wheat-free, and the mochi/rice crackers served with it are also labeled egg/dairy/wheat-free. An allergy-focused review tags the soft serve as gluten-free. The cafe posts clear allergen labeling, but it is not a fully dedicated gluten-free kitchen, so confirm individual items at the counter.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based cafe: no animal-derived ingredients. The rice soft serve uses rice/soy/coconut milk with organic cane sugar and agave (no dairy, egg, or refined white sugar). Menu also includes organic fruit sodas with house-made spiced syrups, organic coffee and spiced hot drinks. Concept is "wholesome seasonal flavors for everyone."
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-2-1 おりづるタワー 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町1-2-1 returned [132.454941, 34.395702], inside the Hiroshima box and matching Oriduru Tower beside Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 大手町・おりづるタワー（原爆ドーム前、徒歩1分）
seats_text: Ground-floor (1F) counter near Oriduru Tower's main entrance and souvenir shop; primarily a takeout/counter format (no observation-deck ticket needed to use it). Seat count not published.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed; check the official site calendar for irregular closures and tower holidays.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual counter in a tourist tower right by the A-Bomb Dome with clear illustrated allergen/vegan labeling and an online menu, so a foreign student can order easily with minimal Japanese.
caveats: It is a small ground-floor counter focused on soft serve and drinks rather than full meals; it sits beside (but is unrelated to) the okonomiyaki shop in the same tower, which is not vegan.

roles: operator: 株式会社握手カフェ
bio.background: Opened March 23, 2024 as a renewal of the former Akushu Cafe inside Oriduru Tower, the observation tower standing right beside Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. The 1F counter was relaunched as an organic, plant-based cafe.
bio.specialty: Plant-based 'silk' rice soft serve made from organic, pesticide-free Yamada Nishiki rice blended with rice, soy and coconut milk; organic fruit sodas with house-made spiced syrups.
bio.philosophy: 'からだにやさしい旬をすべての人へ' — wholesome seasonal flavors for everyone. The cafe sources fruit directly from Hiroshima farmers, deliberately using weather-blemished produce to celebrate regional character and reduce waste, and avoids animal products, additives and refined sugar.

anecdotes.text: The shop deliberately uses Hiroshima fruit with minor weather blemishes, framing the surface marks as proof of hardy, honest growth rather than a defect.

The rice soft serve and even the mochi crackers served alongside it are made without egg, dairy or wheat, and an allergy-focused visitor with a soy aversion found the rice-based flavor pleasant with no beany taste.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe displays clear, illustrated allergen and vegan labeling, and the full menu with ingredient information is published online so guests can verify before visiting.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based menu using organic, pesticide- and additive-free ingredients with no animal products, eggs or refined white sugar.
The silk rice soft serve and its accompanying rice/mochi crackers are documented as egg-, dairy- and wheat-free, and described as gluten-free in an allergy-focused review.

cuisine: Gallery café and sweets shop in a renovated 200-year-old machiya: house-made natural-yeast bread, Pakistani-style curry, chiffon cake, ozenzai, and hand-dripped coffee.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature foods are wheat-based: natural-yeast bread, pizza toast and cheese toast, plus chiffon cake (wheat flour). The savory option is a curry served with bread/rice. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation, and no dedicated preparation surfaces. A celiac traveler should not rely on this café for safe food; at most a plain coffee or tea would be advisable.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented café. The main savory dish (Pakistani curry) contains chicken; pizza/cheese toast use dairy cheese; chiffon cake and other Western sweets typically contain eggs and dairy; ozenzai (sweet red-bean soup) is plant-based in ingredients but may include mochi. No labeled vegan items are documented, so a vegan would need to ask in person. Realistically only black coffee/tea or possibly the red-bean ozenzai (confirm) would suit a strict vegan.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町幸町東表476 町家通り
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 宮島町幸町東表476 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Machiya-dori / Itsukushima Shrine. Accepted.
neighborhood: 宮島・町家通り (Miyajima, Machiya-dori), a short walk inland from the Itsukushima Shrine omotesando shopping street and about 10 minutes on foot from the ferry pier.
seats_text: 30 seats total (4 counter, 26 table).
hours_raw: Tabelog: 10:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30), closed Wednesdays. Miyajima Tourism Association: 10:00-18:30 (winter 10:00-18:00), closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays (closing time may extend to 18:30 outside winter).
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, welcoming gallery café where pointing at the menu and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; English support is not guaranteed, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.

bio.background: Gallery Miyazato occupies a wooden machiya more than 200 years old that once operated as a shakushi (rice-scoop/ladle) wholesaler — fitting, since Miyajima is the birthplace of the carved wooden shakushi. The family carefully restored the house and reopened it on April 6, 2003 as a gallery tea room, keeping the building's history visible throughout.
bio.origin: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Hatsukaichi, Hiroshima — on the quiet Machiya-dori lane behind the main shrine approach.
bio.specialty: House-made natural-yeast bread (sourced from La Pain Juju in Hatsukaichi) turned into pizza toast and cheese toast, a slow-simmered Pakistani-style chicken-and-vegetable curry, hand-dripped original-blend coffee, and homemade chiffon-cake sets and ozenzai enjoyed while looking onto the garden.
bio.philosophy: A cultural space as much as a café: the rebuilt interior shows off hand-drawn glass windows, a century-old clock, reclaimed beams, carved transom (ranma) work, and a stair handrail and counter densely decorated with ladles, doubling as a rental gallery for local art.

anecdotes.text: The staircase handrail and counter are lined with many wooden ladles, and the renovation preserved antique pottery and ornamental kumiko/osa transom panels from the building's life as a shakushi wholesaler.
The Pakistani curry is chicken and vegetables slow-cooked in olive oil with spices, described as spicy but mild in heat; the pizza toast uses generous Hokkaido Yotsuba cheese.
The tourism association describes it as a gallery tea room in a 200-plus-year-old house where you can rest over a handmade chiffon-cake set or ozenzai while gazing at the garden.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat bread (pizza toast, cheese toast) and wheat-flour chiffon cake are core menu items prepared in a small café kitchen, so flour cross-contact is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy cheese (pizza/cheese toast) and chicken (Pakistani curry) are prepared in the same kitchen; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Hours vary slightly by source/season (Tabelog lists 10:00-18:00 with L.O. 17:30; the tourism association lists 10:00-18:30, winter to 18:00); both agree on Wednesday closure. Seat count is exactly 30. Dietary details (vegan ozenzai, allergy handling) are inferred from menu descriptions and not explicitly confirmed by the venue; confirm in person.

cuisine: 老舗純喫茶・ベーカリー（手作りパン・焼き菓子・珈琲、サンドイッチ、オムライス、カレー／家族経営）
neighborhood: 宮島・北之町浜（宮島表参道商店街エリア、厳島神社近く）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町北之町浜588
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.299, lng 132.322).
seats_text: 14 seats per Tabelog; the official site states 16. Small counter/table café.
hours_raw: 8:30-17:00, closed Wednesdays. Morning toast sets served from opening until 11:00.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed family-run café in a major tourist area used to international visitors; a friendly greeting and pointing at the counter pastries will get you far even with little Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a bakery and café built around handmade bread and baked goods made with Hokkaido wheat flour, plus toast sets, sandwiches, pizza toast and curry over rice. The signature offerings are wheat-based and there is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on butter, milk, Hiroshima eggs and honey in its bread and baked goods, and savory items include ham, cheese, teriyaki chicken, BLT and beef curry. No vegan items or plant-based options are documented, so it is not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: Founded in 1966 by Funatsuki Masaru, a former railway worker who fell in love with carefully brewing coffee, the shop has stood on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street for roughly half a century. After a renovation it reopened run by his granddaughter Yamauchi Akiko, supported by her mother Matsuo Michiko, keeping the family line and the original shop name out of respect for longtime local regulars.
bio.specialty: Hand-baked breads (about ten kinds) and baked sweets (around fifteen kinds), the signature 'Miyajima Weekend Citron' lemon cake made with Miyajima honey and Seto Inland Sea lemon, and the house 'Grandfather's Deep-Roast Coffee' carried over from the founder. Morning toast sets, sandwiches, omelette rice, beef curry and pizza toast round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: Aiming every day for bread and sweets that comfort rather than tire the palate, while preserving the previous generation's approach to coffee.

anecdotes.text: The house coffee is named 'Grandfather's Deep-Roast Coffee' in honor of the founder, a former railway worker who started the shop in 1966 out of a passion for brewing coffee.
Owner Yamauchi Akiko spent about a decade working at a Western pastry shop before returning to bake the bread and sweets now sold at the counter.
The signature 'Miyajima Weekend Citron' cake is handmade with honey harvested on Miyajima and lemon from the Seto Inland Sea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a working bakery producing about ten kinds of bread and fifteen kinds of baked goods on site with Hokkaido wheat flour, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination very likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods use butter, milk, eggs and honey, and savory dishes use meat and cheese; no separation from animal products is documented.

caveats: Tabelog lists 14 seats while the official site lists 16; treat seating as roughly 14-16. Founder/lineage details (1966, former railway worker, granddaughter now running it) come from the Hiroshima Keizai Shimbun reopening article. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan handling information is published, so dietary-restricted diners should confirm directly.

lead: お好み焼き・鉄板焼 福ちゃん エキエ
name_ja: お好み焼き・鉄板焼 福ちゃん エキエ
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼（牡蠣料理）
neighborhood: 広島駅 ekie（南区松原町）
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町1-2 エキエ1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市南区松原町1-2 returned [132.475952, 34.398319], within the Hiroshima box; matches the ekie building location at JR Hiroshima Station.
seats_text: 37 seats: 5 counter seats, plus tables (5 tables for 4, 6 tables for 2). One source lists 38 total including counter.
hours_raw: 11:00 - 22:00（L.O. 21:30）, open daily, no regular closed days
closed_days: None (open year-round; follows ekie facility schedule)
cultural_comfort_note: Inside the ekie shopping complex right at JR Hiroshima Station with English and simplified-Chinese menus, this is an easy, welcoming stop for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and yakisoba/udon noodles, and the okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat, so the signature dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki uses egg and is typically topped with pork and bonito/fish-based dashi and sauce, and the teppanyaki menu centers on Hiroshima oysters, wagyu, and other seafood. No vegan dishes or accommodation are documented.
caveats: Owner/chef names and a founding date were not documented in the sources reviewed. Seat count varies slightly by source (37 vs 38).

bio.background: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter tucked inside the ekie food area at JR Hiroshima Station, making it a convenient first or last taste of the city's signature dish for travelers passing through.
bio.specialty: The kitchen leans on slowly steam-grilled cabbage layered with its own noodles to draw out the vegetables' natural sweetness, in classic Hiroshima style. Beyond the namesake Fukuchan-yaki, the menu showcases local seafood and meat on the griddle, including Hiroshima oyster okonomiyaki, oysters in butter-soy sauce, sea urchin and spinach, and wagyu beef.
bio.philosophy: The cooking emphasizes the natural sweetness of well-steamed cabbage and locally sourced Hiroshima ingredients, especially the region's prized oysters.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature plate is the Fukuchan-yaki, and it is known for the oyster dishes that make the most of Hiroshima's local catch, such as Hiroshima oyster okonomiyaki and oysters grilled in butter-soy sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter, noodles, and the finishing sauce all contain wheat, and the griddle is shared across wheat-based dishes, so heavy cross-contamination should be assumed for anyone avoiding gluten.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce and the butter-soy-sauce oyster dish use standard Japanese soy/okonomi sauces, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle cooks pork, oysters, wagyu and other seafood alongside any vegetable items, and okonomiyaki uses egg and fish-based dashi, so animal-product cross-contact is unavoidable.

positives.text: English and simplified-Chinese menus are available, which makes it easier to ask questions about ingredients.

cuisine: Grilled oysters and seafood (producer-direct oyster stall)
gf_detail: Freshly grilled oysters served plain are naturally free of wheat, and the shop recommends eating them with no condiments, so a simple order of yaki-gaki can work. However, fried oysters (kaki-fry) use a wheat batter and vinegar/ponzu-dressed oysters may use soy-sauce-based seasonings that contain wheat. This is a tiny producer-direct stall with no documented gluten-free protocol, so a celiac traveler should point to the grilled oysters and ask for them with nothing added.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around oysters (grilled, fried, and in ponzu/vinegar preparations), which are animal products. There are no vegan options here.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町553-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [132.331451, 34.348637] for 広島県廿日市市宮島町553-1, landing on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: About 4 counter seats; primarily a stand-and-eat / takeout stall on the Omotesando shopping street.
hours_raw: 09:00 - 18:00 (open daily)
closed_days: No regular closing day listed; open 7 days a week. Hours can vary seasonally for a small family stall.
cultural_comfort_note: A quick, friendly oyster stall where you can point at the grill and order with a few words of Japanese; English is not guaranteed, so know that "yaki-gaki" means grilled oyster and have cash ready.

bio.background: Okino Suisan is the only shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street run directly by a local oyster producer, sitting near the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. The family farms Miyajima oysters in the mineral-rich waters around the island and sells them straight from the source, which keeps prices comparatively modest for the area.
bio.specialty: Grill-fired oysters cooked fresh at the storefront, served hot and plump, alongside fried oysters and ponzu/vinegar-dressed oysters; the shop also sells and ships whole oysters.
bio.philosophy: As producers, they grill the oysters with careful heat control to bring out a plump, fresh texture and encourage eating them plain so the creamy, gently briny flavor of the oyster speaks for itself.

anecdotes.text: As the only producer-direct oyster shop on the Omotesando shopping street, the oysters are sold relatively cheaply and grilled fresh right at the storefront, hot and springy off the grill.
The shop suggests eating a whole grilled oyster on a toothpick with no condiments, letting the creamy taste and moderate saltiness spread across the palate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The same small stall prepares wheat-battered fried oysters alongside the grilled oysters, so cross-contact is plausible; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ponzu/vinegar-dressed oyster items may use soy-sauce-based seasonings, which typically contain wheat; ingredients are not documented.

positives.text: Grilled oysters are recommended to be eaten plain with no condiments, so a simple grilled-oyster order avoids added sauces.

lead: お好み焼き ももちゃん
name_ja: お好み焼き ももちゃん（百々商店）
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（地元の小さな個人店）
neighborhood: 宮島・厳島神社／表参道周辺
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町北之町浜553
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address landed on Miyajima (Itsukushima island) within the Hiroshima bounding box; consistent with the shop's location on the Omotesando arcade near Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: Not published as a specific count. Listings describe multiple seating options including tatami (zashiki) and table seats; in person it is a casual, cluttered old-style shop with simple tables and pipe chairs.
closed_days: No fixed closing day; generally open year-round (irregular closures possible).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, welcoming local okonomiyaki shop on Miyajima's tourist street where pointing at the picture menu and a few friendly words of Japanese will get you served comfortably.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop and the dishes are built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and wheat noodles (soba), plus a sweet okonomiyaki sauce that typically contains wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation. Not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki uses egg, and standard fillings are pork, oyster (kaki), and anago/chikuwa (fish), with a wheat-soy sauce. Bonito/fish-based elements are typical of Hiroshima okonomiyaki. No vegan preparation is advertised, so this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
caveats: Seat count is not published; described variously as having tatami and table seating yet also as a small, simple old-style shop, so capacity is uncertain. Hours are listed as generally open daily but with irregular closures, so confirm before a special trip. Geocode lands on Miyajima island, which requires a ferry from Miyajimaguchi.

bio.background: Momochan is a long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked along the Omotesando shopping street on Miyajima, roughly a five-to-eight-minute walk from the ferry pier and a short stroll from Itsukushima Shrine. It has the feel of a neighborhood institution rather than a polished tourist restaurant, with simple tables and pipe chairs and an unpretentious, lived-in atmosphere that regulars and visitors alike appreciate.
bio.specialty: Layered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with plenty of cabbage and noodles, finished with egg. House specialties play to the island: large, prized oysters (kaki) folded in, and anago-chikuwa that nods to Miyajima's famous conger eel. The sweet sauce paired with the noodles is a noted highlight.
bio.philosophy: Cooking okonomiyaki the traditional Hiroshima way, layering ingredients and griddling them rather than mixing, so each component stays distinct even when eaten together.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe it as a charmingly old-fashioned spot whose cluttered, casual setting is part of the appeal, with the oysters singled out for their size and quality.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All okonomiyaki here are built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles cooked on a shared teppan griddle, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a sweet okonomiyaki sauce that typically contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared griddle alongside pork, egg, oysters, and fish (anago/chikuwa), so vegan-safe preparation is not possible here.

cuisine: Oysters and seafood (grilled oysters, oyster set meals, seafood small plates)
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando Shopping Street
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes are grilled and fried oysters; fried oysters are coated in wheat batter, and seasoning relies on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and miso. No gluten-free menu, no documented allergen handling, and no dedicated fryer.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is an oyster and seafood specialist; nearly every dish centers on oysters or other shellfish/fish, and sauces use wheat soy sauce and likely fish-based dashi. No documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町幸町西浜459
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima bounding box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando shopping street.
seats_text: Seat count not published. A casual oyster shop grilling oysters on the ground floor with a calmer cafe-style space and terrace seating upstairs; takeout and stand-and-eat style also available.
hours_raw: 09:00 - 19:00 (Mon-Sun); hours may vary, confirm before visiting
closed_days: No regular closed day listed
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed walk-up oyster stand on the main tourist street where a friendly greeting and pointing at the oysters is enough; English is not guaranteed but the casual order-and-eat format makes it easy for a foreign student.
caveats: Seat count is unknown. Hours are listed as possibly variable, so confirm on the day of a visit. The "no regular closed day" status is the published listing and may change seasonally.

bio.background: An oyster and seafood shop set along Miyajima's Omotesando approach, about a seven-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry pier and close to Hokoku Shrine. A white paper lantern marks the entrance. Oysters are grilled at street level on the ground floor, while a calmer, cafe-like space upstairs opens onto a terrace with sweeping views.
bio.specialty: Grilled oysters orderable from a single piece, plus oyster-focused set meals at lunch and an oyster-everything spread for daytime drinking with sake and beer.

anecdotes.text: Oysters can be ordered one at a time at the ground-floor grill, and the upstairs floor is a relaxed cafe-like room whose terrace is praised for its scenic views.
Beyond grilled oysters, the shop is known for a lunch set and a daytime-drinking spread that pairs alcohol with an array of oyster dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oyster dishes and grilled-oyster seasoning rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around oysters and seafood, so plant-based diners face unavoidable shellfish/fish content and likely fish dashi in shared cooking.

lead: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 本通店
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺専門 キング軒 本通店
cuisine: 汁なし担担麺（広島式）/ Hiroshima-style broth-less dandan noodles
neighborhood: 広島市中区 本通（本通商店街、原爆ドーム・平和記念公園から徒歩圏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-7 しげとみビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded at the chō level (本通8-7) to lng 132.45961, lat 34.392445, inside the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26–34.42, lng 132.28–132.50). Within the central Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: 10 counter seats only
closed_days: 元日のみ (New Year's Day only)
cultural_comfort_note: A fast, casual counter-only noodle bar where you can point at the simple menu and eat quickly; basic Japanese greetings help but it is very approachable for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is shiru-nashi tantanmen built on wheat noodles (custom medium-thin Chinese-style noodles), and the tare is made with a wheat-based soy sauce specially produced by Kawanaka Shoyu (川中醤油). There is no documented gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth-less dandan noodles use a minced-pork (meat) topping and a sesame/chili-oil sauce over wheat noodles, with a wheat-soy-sauce tare; no documented vegan or vegetarian version.
caveats: Counter-only with about 10 seats and a single specialty (broth-less dandan noodles), so lines are common at peak times; spice and Sichuan-pepper numbing levels are adjustable. No gluten-free or vegan options are documented.

bio.background: King-ken opened in Hiroshima in November 2011 and quickly became a destination for the city's distinctive broth-less dandan noodles, drawing daily lines; it later expanded to Tokyo in 2015 and to other cities, and its style was even turned into a commercial instant noodle. The Hondori branch sits in the heart of the covered Hondori shopping arcade, within walking distance of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style shiru-nashi tantanmen (broth-less dandan noodles): firm, custom medium-thin noodles boiled briefly, tossed with a sesame-and-chili-oil sauce, minced pork, and green onion, with adjustable spice and a numbing Sichuan-pepper kick. The classic finish is to mix everything thoroughly and then add rice to soak up the remaining sauce.
bio.philosophy: Obsessive sourcing of every component: a house five-spice chili oil aged at least a month and a half to round out the heat; a soy tare hand-made with a 'King-ken' soy sauce specially commissioned from Kawanaka Shoyu (founded 1906); and a proprietary Sichuan-pepper blend ground fresh in-shop each morning in the exact daily quantity.

anecdotes.text: Custom medium-thin noodles were developed with the long-established Kuchii noodle factory in Hatsukaichi, near the World Heritage island of Miyajima, and the green onions are fragrant 'Takarajima' negi grown in Kurahashi.
The shop grinds its Sichuan pepper (kaショウ/huājiāo) in-house each morning, using only the amount it will serve that day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy tare, so any dish risks gluten contact; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy tare is hand-made using a 'King-ken' soy sauce specially commissioned from Kawanaka Shoyu (founded 1906), a conventional wheat-fermented Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dish includes a minced-pork topping and is prepared in a kitchen dedicated to meat-based dandan noodles, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

lead: お好み焼き 鉄板焼き こひなた
name_ja: お好み焼き 鉄板焼き こひなた
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き（隠れ家的な個人店）
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. Okonomiyaki here is built on a wheat-flour batter and crispy wheat noodles, and dishes are finished with Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce (which contains wheat). There is no indication of gluten-free menu items, separate prep, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood okonomiyaki/teppanyaki izakaya. The signature こひなた焼き contains pork, egg, and squid tempura, and dishes broadly use egg, fish-based dashi/sauces, and seafood. No documented vegan or vegetarian options. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町3-3-6 サンピア大手町 102
neighborhood: 大手町（中電前駅近く、平和記念公園エリア）
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box (near Chūden-mae, Peace Memorial Park area).
seats_text: About 20 seats total: a counter seating roughly 10-11 plus 2 tables (each seating around 5-8).
closed_days: Closed the first Monday of each month.
cultural_comfort_note: A homey, locals-favored counter shop where a little Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way; English is not advertised, so a translation app and pointing at the menu will help.
caveats: Cash and QR payment (PayPay, d-barai) only; no credit cards. Lunch is okonomiyaki-focused while evenings run as a teppanyaki izakaya. Closed the first Monday of each month.

bio.background: A small, homey okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop in central Hiroshima's Otemachi district, opened in 2009. By day it is an okonomiyaki spot; in the evening it turns into a relaxed teppanyaki izakaya where regulars and visitors sit at the counter and watch the dishes come together on the griddle.
bio.origin: The owner trained at a Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop called Hinata, and named this place Kohinata in tribute to that mentorship.
bio.specialty: The house dish, Kohinata-yaki, layers cabbage, pork, egg, squid tempura, and green onion, finished with crisp griddle-fried noodles and a runny egg under sweet-savory okonomiyaki sauce. A grilled potato salad prepared and mashed tableside on the teppan is another favorite.
bio.philosophy: An at-home, welcoming atmosphere built around the counter, where the cooking is part of the experience and conversation flows between guests.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from Hinata, the Hiroshima okonomiyaki restaurant where the owner trained before opening their own place in 2009.
For the signature okonomiyaki, oil is worked into the noodles so they stay crisp without drying out even when cooked gently, a small technique the shop is known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made on a shared teppan griddle using wheat batter and wheat noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent across the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with sweet okonomiyaki sauce (Otafuku-style), which is made with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on one shared griddle alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so vegan separation is not feasible.

cuisine: Local Japanese & Western diner (oyster dishes, anago-meshi, set meals, cafe items)
neighborhood: Miyajima (Hamanocho, seafront near the pier on the approach toward Itsukushima Shrine)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町浜之町855-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町浜之町855-1 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Approx. 64 seats, table seating only; seats near the entrance have sea views and others overlook an inner garden. (One source lists seat count; Tabelog only notes table-only seating.)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, walk-in family diner where a smile and a little Japanese go a long way; picture-style set menus make ordering manageable for a foreign student.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on dishes that rely on wheat: anago-meshi and oyster bowls seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, plus breaded/fried items such as oyster fry, shrimp fry and tonkatsu. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or awareness.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused diner; its identity dishes are oysters and conger eel (anago), and Western set meals use meat and fish. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented, and standard seasonings here use fish/dashi and wheat soy sauce.
caveats: Closed days are irregular (不定休), so confirm before visiting. Payment info conflicts between sources: one listing states cash only while Tabelog suggests some card/QR acceptance, so carry cash to be safe. Address appears as both 宮島町浜之町855-1 and 宮島町855-1 across listings; phone numbers also differ between sources.

bio.background: A roomy, Showa-era retro diner sitting right on the Miyajima seafront, a short walk from the ferry pier as you head toward the great torii and Itsukushima Shrine. Tables near the entrance look out over the water, while inner seats face a quiet garden that leads back toward the main building, giving the place an old-fashioned, unhurried feel.
bio.specialty: Anago-meshi (conger eel over rice) and local oyster dishes — raw with lemon, vinegared, grilled, and as oyster fry — alongside homey Japanese and Western set meals and cafe plates.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Miyajima's two signature ingredients, oysters and conger eel, and rounds the menu out with comforting Western-style sets like tonkatsu, hamburg and fried shrimp, plus simple cafe fare.
Its draw is as much the setting as the food: sea-view seats by the door and a tranquil courtyard view inside make it a relaxed stop on the walk from the pier toward the shrine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Breaded/fried items (oyster fry, shrimp fry, tonkatsu) and wheat-based soy seasoning are central to the menu, so gluten exposure is pervasive; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-meshi and oyster bowls are seasoned in the standard Japanese style with wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around oysters, conger eel and meat dishes, with fish/dashi-based seasoning throughout; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

anecdotes.text: Credited as the originator of the cheese-filled momiji manju, using solid processed cheese against sweet batter for a sweet-and-salty contrast; the method is protected by a manufacturing patent.

bio.origin: Miyajima, Hatsukaichi, Hiroshima, along the approach to Itsukushima Shrine
bio.background: A long-running Miyajima sweets shop described as the only maker on the island still committed to old-style hand-baking of momiji manju, each cake baked individually for its own fragrance and character.
bio.specialty: Hand-baked momiji manju, the famous cheese momiji, and an on-site-only ice momiji.
bio.philosophy: Devotion to traditional hand-baking carried on since the shop's founding.

cuisine: Plant-forward vegetable café — egg- and dairy-free home cooking, daily-changing vegetable plate lunches, and vegan sweets
vegan_detail: A vegetable-centered café that cooks without eggs or dairy. Lunch lets you choose a vegetable (plant-based) main or a meat/fish main; on the vegetable plate everything except the main is plant-based, including the soup stock ("メイン以外はダシからすべて植物性"). The sweets are all egg- and dairy-free and there are vegan cakes and takeout sweets. So a fully vegan meal is readily available if you choose the vegetable main, but the kitchen is not exclusively vegan (meat/fish mains are also served).
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Lunch sets come with a choice of bread or brown rice (the brown rice option could suit a gluten-avoider), but there is no stated GF handling and sauces/seasonings may contain wheat (e.g. soy sauce, ponzu). A celiac traveler should call ahead (082-962-5114) and ask directly.
address_ja: 広島県広島市安佐南区川内1-7-12
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市安佐南区川内1-7-12 to [lng 132.484344, lat 34.459885]. The latitude (34.46) falls north of the curated Hiroshima box (lat 34.26–34.42), so coordinates are set null. This is correct: the café is in Asa-Minami Ward (Furuichi/Kawauchi area), roughly a 15–20 min walk from Furuichi Station, NOT in the central A-Bomb Dome area or on Miyajima.
neighborhood: 安佐南区川内（古市エリア） / Kawauchi, Asa-Minami Ward (Furuichi area)
seats_text: Small café; sources describe roughly two tables plus a counter and note seats are often full. Exact seat count not published. 4 parking spaces (1 in front, 3 across the street).
hours_raw: Per the café's own listing and a local reporter: Mon–Sat 9:00–16:00, with morning 9:00–11:00, lunch 11:30–14:30 (last order ~14:00), café 14:00–16:00. Tabelog lists lunch hours 11:30–14:00. Closed Sundays and holidays.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日 (Sundays and public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, homey neighborhood café run by family; menus and likely conversation are in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help, and calling ahead (it fills up) is wise.
caveats: Located in Asa-Minami Ward (Furuichi/Kawauchi), not central Hiroshima or Miyajima — about a 15–20 min walk from Furuichi Station on the Astram Line. Hours vary by source (the café's own listing shows a fuller 9:00–16:00 day with morning/lunch/café segments, while Tabelog shows only the lunch window 11:30–14:00); confirm before visiting. It is a vegetable café with both plant-based and meat/fish main options, not a 100% vegan kitchen.

roles: owner-operators (run as a family / parent-and-child café)
bio.background: A homey little café whose French name, un peu de repos, means 'a little rest.' The owners set out to make a time and space where guests can take a relaxed breath and let their hearts and bodies recharge, in rooms with a nostalgic, Showa-era coffee-shop feel.
bio.origin: Hiroshima — the Kawauchi / Furuichi neighborhood of Asa-Minami Ward.
bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward, body-friendly home cooking made without eggs or dairy: a daily-changing main (you can pick a vegetable plate or a meat/fish plate) with several seasonal side dishes, plus egg- and dairy-free sweets and cakes.
bio.philosophy: Cooking that is gentle on the body, built around seasonal vegetables and welcoming to people who are health-conscious or have allergies; on the vegetable plate even the soup stock is plant-based.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen never repeats a main dish — the chef dreams up a brand-new main every single day, so no two days share the same lunch.
The egg- and dairy-free sweets are designed to still feel rich and satisfying, and are also sold for takeout.
It is a well-loved spot where the seats are often full and the reservation phone keeps ringing, so booking ahead is recommended.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The café cooks both plant-based and meat/fish mains in the same kitchen; on the vegetable plate everything except the main is plant-based (including the dashi stock), but no separate-prep or cross-contact protocol is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The café is described as accommodating health-conscious diners and people with allergies, and all sweets are made egg- and dairy-free; specific allergen-handling procedures are not detailed, so confirm your needs when booking.

positives.text: All baked sweets and cakes are made without eggs or dairy.
The vegetable lunch plate is plant-based right down to the soup stock, so a fully vegan meal is achievable.

lead: Oyster Bar MABUI 袋町店 （オイスターバー マブイ）
name_ja: オイスターバー MABUI 袋町店
cuisine: オイスターバー（広島産・国内外の生牡蠣常時7〜8種＋牡蠣のグラタン、ガーリックバター焼き、アヒージョ、カキフライ、カルパッチョなど牡蠣料理／シーフード・ダイニングバー）
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. The menu centers on oysters served raw and in cooked preparations, but typical dishes such as fried oysters (panko/wheat breading), oyster gratin, carpaccio dressings and the use of wheat-containing soy sauce mean most items are not gluten-free. Raw oyster tastings could in principle be eaten plain, but there is no documented allergen handling, so cross-contamination cannot be confirmed. Travelers requiring strict gluten-free should ask directly.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated oyster bar; the menu is built around raw and cooked oysters and other seafood, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町2-26 カッパーハウス 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区袋町2-26 via GSI; coordinates [132.45929, 34.392467] fall within the Hiroshima box. Located in central Hiroshima near Hondori and Tachimachi tram stops, in the Hondori/Fukuromachi area.
seats_text: 約40席。カウンター席、テーブル席（最大20名）、ロフト席（1組6名まで）。一部資料では50席との記載もあり。
closed_days: 不定休（基本的に年中無休との情報あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed central-Hiroshima oyster bar a few minutes from the Hondori arcade; a foreign student can comfortably order with basic Japanese or by pointing at the oyster varieties, though menus and staff are mostly Japanese.
caveats: Hours and seat counts vary across listings (lunch start reported as 11:30 on most sources but 13:00 on one; seats reported as 40 or 50); weekend hours may run straight through. No official allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, so dietary travelers should confirm directly. Course cancellations are charged 100%.

bio.background: Opened on January 20, 2014, this oyster bar in central Hiroshima's Fukuromachi district keeps seven to eight or more varieties of fresh oysters on hand at all times, sourced from Hiroshima and from producers across Japan and abroad. Oysters are shucked to order so guests can taste them at their freshest, and a comparison tasting set lets diners sample varieties side by side.
bio.origin: Hiroshima City, in the Fukuromachi area near the Hondori shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: Year-round fresh raw oysters with a rotating lineup of 7–8+ varieties, alongside cooked dishes such as oyster gratin, garlic-butter sauteed oysters, ajillo, fried oysters and oyster carpaccio.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing oysters at their freshest while championing Hiroshima-region ingredients, presented in a space described as a fusion of European and North African aesthetics.

anecdotes.text: The interior is described as blending European and North African design, with a snug attic-style loft seat tucked above the main floor.
Oysters are shucked fresh to order, and a tasting comparison set is offered so guests can sample the rotating varieties.

cuisine: Lunch: original spice curry set meal (served with brown rice); Evening: izakaya focused on Hiroshima local sake with creative small plates and Seto Inland Sea seafood
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free offering documented. Lunch is a spice curry set; curry roux and spice blends commonly contain wheat flour, and the evening izakaya menu relies on Japanese sake-bar small plates that typically use wheat-based soy sauce. A celiac diner would need to ask the proprietor directly about each dish. No gluten-free preparations are confirmed.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The lunch spice curry can include ingredients such as smoked mackerel (e.g., a smoked-saba curry), and the evening izakaya centers on sashimi, Seto Inland Sea seafood and sake-bar plates, most using fish or dashi. The brown-rice spice curry might be adaptable, but no vegan options are documented; a vegan diner would need to ask in advance.
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区大須賀町11-10
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to the building-level address 大須賀町11-10; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box, a few minutes' walk from Hiroshima Station.
neighborhood: 広島駅周辺・大須賀町（広島駅北口から徒歩約4分）
seats_text: 8 counter seats, with additional table seating on the second floor (private room for up to about 8).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-13:30 (sources also list 11:30-14:00); Dinner 18:00-22:00; Sunday 12:00-18:00. Closed Mondays and holidays.
closed_days: 月曜日・祝日 (Mondays and public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter shokudo run by the owner; a little basic Japanese helps for ordering and chatting about the sake, though a curious foreign student would be welcomed at the counter.
caveats: Lunch closing time and weekend hours vary between sources (lunch listed as both 11:30-13:30 and 11:30-14:00; some listings show a Sunday afternoon service while marking Monday closed). Cash only despite QR-code payment being mentioned on one listing. Confirm current hours by phone before visiting.

bio.background: A small retro neighborhood shokudo within a few minutes' walk of Hiroshima Station, operating since around 2012. It wears two faces: by day a counter serving an original brown-rice spice curry set, and by evening an intimate izakaya built around hard-to-find Hiroshima local sake paired with creative small plates.
bio.specialty: Original spice curry over brown rice at lunch (including offbeat versions such as a smoked-mackerel curry); in the evening, rare Hiroshima regional sake with creative otsumami and fresh Seto Inland Sea seafood.
bio.philosophy: A focus on prefecture-grown and local ingredients and a personal, hands-on approach: the owner picks sake to match each guest's taste and explains it in a way that's friendly even to first-timers.

anecdotes.text: The owner selects sake to match each customer's preference and explains it clearly, making it approachable even for sake beginners.
Bottles of Hiroshima sake line the shelves above the counter, and the kitchen leans on prefecture-sourced ingredients with first-rate sashimi and dishes like cheese tofu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Evening service centers on Japanese sake-bar small plates and sashimi, which conventionally use wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Lunch curry can contain fish (e.g., a smoked-mackerel version) and the evening menu features sashimi and seafood, so shared preparation with animal products is likely.

positives.text: Small owner-run counter where the proprietor personally explains the menu and sake to guests, so dietary questions can be discussed directly.

cuisine: Heritage Miyajima confectionery-turned-cafe — Western sweets, Japanese tea, plus light savory dishes using Hiroshima oysters
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment, but it offers a notable rice-flour (米粉) Basque cheesecake described as made without wheat — a genuine wheat-free dessert option. However, the shop's heritage products (momiji manju, the みやじまオムレット sponge cake, breads such as かきパン) are wheat-based, and the savory oyster chowder (かきチャウダー) is not confirmed gluten-free. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or celiac-level cross-contamination controls are documented. A gluten-sensitive visitor should confirm the rice-flour cheesecake's full ingredients and ask directly about shared baking surfaces.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. As a Western confectionery, core items (cream-filled みやじまオムレット, cheesecakes, momiji manju) use dairy and eggs, and the savory menu features Hiroshima oysters and seafood. Plant-based diners would need to ask whether any item (e.g., a fruit-based shaved ice or jam drink) can be served without animal products; nothing is confirmed vegan.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町592
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町592 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando shopping street / Itsukushima Shrine approach. Address-level (chōme 592) resolution.
seats_text: Small cafe seating; reported as counter plus tables (around 12-13 seats: roughly 5 counter / 8 table).
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 daily; additionally 19:00-22:00 on Fri-Sat (evening service). Closed Thursdays. (Sources differ slightly on evening hours; daytime 10:00-17:00 is consistent.)
closed_days: Thursdays (木曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly local sweets cafe right on Miyajima's busy shrine-approach shopping street, used to tourists — a few words of Japanese and pointing at the display case will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English menu support is not guaranteed.
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（清盛通り近く、厳島神社参道）/ Miyajima Omotesando shopping street, near Itsukushima Shrine

bio.background: KIMURA grew out of 菓子処きむら (Kashidokoro Kimura), a long-loved Miyajima confectionery best known for making momiji manju from a chiffon-sponge batter rather than the usual castella-style cake — a distinctive twist that built a devoted local and visitor following over the decades. The shop sits on the bustling Omotesando shopping street that leads pilgrims and travelers toward Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: The みやじまオムレット (Miyajima Omelette) — a folded fluffy sponge cake with lightly sweetened cream that has been a signature since 1970, its chiffon texture unchanged from the original recipe — alongside the shop's chiffon-based momiji manju.
bio.philosophy: The makers describe working with carefully selected ingredients and blending Western pastry technique with modern equipment to make sweets that bring joy to customers, while preserving the flavors longtime fans grew up with.

anecdotes.text: In May 2025 the heritage confectionery was reborn as a stylish cafe pairing Western sweets with Japanese (wakōcha) tea, while deliberately keeping the elements old fans loved — the chiffon momiji manju and the みやじまオムレット remain on the menu.
The みやじまオムレット has been a long-seller since its 1970 debut; its airy chiffon sponge is kept exactly as it was at launch, now filled with a less-sweet cream that appeals across generations.

The cafe leans into its location, offering savory items made with Hiroshima-grown oysters — including an oyster chowder (かきチャウダー) and an oyster bread (かきパン) — plus a MASU tiramisu whose matcha swirl evokes the Seto Inland Sea.

positives.text: Offers a rice-flour (米粉) Basque cheesecake described as made without wheat, giving a genuine wheat-free dessert choice in a heritage sweets shop.

caveats: The savory cafe menu (oyster chowder, oyster bread) and signature sweets (momiji manju, みやじまオムレット, cream cakes) use wheat, dairy, eggs and seafood, so this is not a dietary-destination cafe — the standout for restricted diners is the single rice-flour wheat-free cheesecake. Evening (19:00-22:00) service is reported only for Fri-Sat and sources vary, so confirm dinner hours before visiting. Founding year of the original confectionery and the family names are not documented in the sources reviewed.

lead: お食事処 よさこい
name_ja: お食事処 よさこい
cuisine: Miyajima specialty diner — wild anago (conger eel) rice bowls, local Hiroshima oysters, okonomiyaki, set meals, donburi and udon
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Core dishes are not gluten-free: okonomiyaki is made with a wheat-flour batter, anago and oyster dishes are typically prepared with wheat-based soy sauce and tare, and udon is wheat noodles. No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on fish (anago/conger eel), shellfish (oysters), pork (black pork), chicken (jidori), and egg-and-seafood okonomiyaki. Dishes rely on wheat-soy-based seasonings and dashi. No documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町590（みやじま薬局2F）
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県廿日市市宮島町590 via GSI. Lands on Miyajima island within the accepted Hiroshima box (lat 34.26–34.42, lng 132.28–132.50).
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Hatsukaichi — near the Omotesando shopping street and Itsukushima Shrine, with views of the great torii and Seto Inland Sea
seats_text: 50 seats; can accommodate private events up to 50 guests
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:15); Dinner 17:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:15), evening service Thursday–Saturday only. Closed Wednesdays. Hours vary seasonally and by reservation.
closed_days: Wednesdays (closure may shift week-to-week by season; open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the place is used to international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably without Japanese.
caveats: Evening (dinner) service runs only Thursday through Saturday; the rest of the week is lunch-only. Closed Wednesdays, but the exact Wednesday closures shift by season, so confirm before a special trip. Anago hot-pot (anago shabu-shabu) requires advance reservation. Hours vary seasonally.

bio.background: A casual sit-down eatery on the second floor of a building along Miyajima's approach to Itsukushima Shrine, a short walk from the ferry terminal and the Omotesando shopping street.
bio.specialty: Wild conger eel (anago) rice bowls and an anago shabu-shabu hot pot, alongside local Hiroshima oysters and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. The kitchen also turns out set meals, rice bowls, and udon, so a group can mix island specialties with simpler fare.

anecdotes.text: The dining room looks out over the great torii gate and the Seto Inland Sea, so diners can take in the island's signature view while they eat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago and oyster dishes and okonomiyaki are standard Hiroshima preparations that use wheat-based soy sauce/tare and wheat-flour batter; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers identify ingredients before ordering.

lead: 広島名物 牡蠣一番
name_ja: 広島名物 牡蠣一番
cuisine: 牡蠣居酒屋 — oyster-focused izakaya serving 15 varieties of raw and grilled oysters plus other Hiroshima specialties, paired with local sake/shochu/wine
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Most dishes rely on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce, and signature items such as fried oysters (カキフライ), okonomiyaki-style grilled oysters, and oyster gratin all use wheat flour/breading. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. A celiac traveler would have very few safe options here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire concept centers on oysters (raw, grilled, fried, in rice, in hot pot), and the kitchen leans heavily on seafood, fish-based dashi, and wheat-soy sauce. No vegan dishes are documented.
address_ja: 〒730-0034 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 returned [132.461609, 34.391186], within the Hiroshima box; lands in central Nakaku near the Hatchobori/Nagarekawa entertainment district, about 5 min walk from Hatchobori Station.
neighborhood: 広島市中区新天地（八丁堀・流川エリア）
seats_text: 28 seats total (6 counter seats, 22 table/sofa seats)
closed_days: 年中無休（年末年始は休む場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the central, casual setting makes it easy for a foreign student to walk in and order without speaking much Japanese.
caveats: HotPepper lists this as cash only (credit cards, e-money and QR payments not accepted), while a secondary read of the English Tabelog page mentioned cards accepted; treat as cash only to be safe and confirm at the door. Target brief listed the area as 流川; the registered address is the adjacent 新天地 block within the same Hatchobori/Nagarekawa entertainment district. Hours from open-now sources; year-end/New Year closures possible.

bio.background: A compact, modern oyster izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's Shintenchi block, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop, opened in January 2023. The kitchen revolves entirely around Hiroshima's signature oyster, offering around fifteen styles between raw and grilled.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served raw or grilled in a wide range of preparations — plain, with lemon, with green onion and grated radish, a Yamagata-dashi version, topped with salmon roe, and an okonomiyaki-inspired style — alongside fried oysters, oyster rice, oyster hot pot and oyster gratin.
bio.philosophy: The owner sources oysters directly so guests can taste a broad spread of preparations in one sitting, pairing them with a deep list of local sake, shochu and wine.

anecdotes.text: All fifteen raw and grilled oyster varieties are bought in directly by the owner, and a popular all-oyster lunch set brings together grilled oysters, fried oysters, oyster miso soup and oyster rice at a modest price.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters, oyster gratin and okonomiyaki-style oysters use wheat flour/breading, and the fryer is also used for wheat-coated items, so cross-contact with gluten is likely throughout the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya style with wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Effectively every dish involves oysters, other seafood or fish-based dashi, so a fully plant-based meal is not realistic here.

positives.text: An English-language menu is available, which makes it easier for travelers to identify ingredients before ordering.

lead: 野菜to発芽酵素玄米弁当 YOU&I TATEMACHI
name_ja: 野菜to発芽酵素玄米弁当 YOU&I
name_en: Yasai to Hatsuga Koso Genmai Bento YOU&I (TATEMACHI)
cuisine: Gluten-free takeout bento and deli (sprouted enzyme brown rice base); full vegan/shojin-style bento available in limited daily quantity
gf_detail: Fully dedicated gluten-free shop. Every menu item is gluten-free: no wheat flour or gluten-containing ingredients are used, and wheat is avoided even in seasonings such as soy sauce. Rice flour replaces breadcrumbs in fried dishes (e.g. the salt-koji karaage). All rice is sprouted enzyme brown rice (発芽酵素玄米). This is a genuine GF destination, not a place with incidental options.
vegan_detail: A fully plant-based vegan bento (1,260 yen, tax incl.) is offered, built on traditional Japanese Buddhist (shojin) cooking with no animal-derived ingredients. It is limited to 10 per day and sells out, so come early. The rest of the menu includes meat and fish bento (karaage, sea-bream-dashi curry, daily meat/fish bento), so the shop is not fully vegan — but the dedicated vegan bento is a real, well-documented offering.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町6-7 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区立町6-7 returned [132.461227, 34.393089], within the Hiroshima box; central Naka-ku Tatemachi, a short walk from Hondori/Kamiyacho and within the Peace Park district.
neighborhood: 中区立町（中の棚商店街内・本通り／紙屋町すぐ、平和公園圏内）
seats_text: Takeout-only specialist; no dining seats.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00（売切れ次第終了）。定休日：水曜・日曜・祝日
closed_days: Wednesdays, Sundays, and public holidays (also closes early when items sell out)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a Japanese-language takeout counter where you point, pay, and go, so a foreign student can manage with a few Japanese words or a translation app — but for the limited vegan bento it helps to arrive early or call ahead, and don't expect English service.
caveats: Takeout only — there is nowhere to eat on site. The vegan bento is capped at 10 per day and sells out, and the whole shop closes once stock runs out, so don't count on late-afternoon availability. Closed Wednesdays, Sundays, and holidays.

bio.background: YOU&I was created by Suishin, a long-established Hiroshima Japanese-cuisine company, as a new takeout-only format opened in October 2021 in the Nakanotana arcade, right beside the Suishin main building in central Tatemachi. The idea grew from the company's own female staff, who wanted to eat something healthy yet genuinely delicious even on their busiest days.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (Tatemachi, Naka-ku), launched by the Hiroshima Japanese-restaurant company Suishin.
bio.specialty: Gluten-free bento built on sprouted enzyme brown rice (発芽酵素玄米) — fermented brown rice aged with adzuki beans and salt for a nutty, chewy texture — including a daily-limited fully plant-based vegan bento rooted in shojin (Buddhist) cooking.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated concept is to support women's beauty and wellbeing through food (女性の美を食から支える), sharing the joy that delicious food and good health can go together. Because the parent company already had deep experience cooking shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) cuisine, extending into gluten-free and vegan cooking was a natural step; as an executive of the company put it, the Japanese technique of drawing out ingredients' natural flavors matched what health-minded diners were looking for.
roles: Operated by Suishin (long-established Hiroshima Japanese-cuisine company)

anecdotes.text: The shop was born from a wish voiced by the company's own female employees: that even on busy days they wanted at least one meal that was both healthy and truly tasty.
Everything is gluten-free down to the seasonings — wheat is avoided even in the soy sauce — and rice flour stands in for breadcrumbs in the fried dishes.

The signature sprouted enzyme brown rice is made by fermenting brown rice, mixing it with adzuki beans and salt, then keeping it warm and aging it for a chewier, more nutritious result than plain brown rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop states that wheat is completely avoided even in seasonings such as soy sauce, and no wheat flour or gluten-containing ingredients are used in any item.

positives.text: Entirely dedicated gluten-free kitchen: all menu items are gluten-free, with rice flour replacing breadcrumbs in fried dishes and wheat excluded even from seasonings.
A genuinely vegan bento with no animal-derived ingredients, based on traditional Japanese shojin (Buddhist) cuisine, is offered daily (limited to 10).

lead: 汁なし担担麺 きさく
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 きさく
cuisine: 広島汁なし担々麺（四川式・ごまペースト不使用、鶏ガラと煮干し出汁の合わせダレに花椒の痺れ）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle shop. The signature soupless dandan noodles are made with wheat ramen noodles, and the sauce is built on a fermented soybean (douchi) and broth base typical of Chinese cooking, which commonly involves soy/wheat ingredients. There is no documented gluten-free option, no gluten-free menu, and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The dish is fundamentally meat-and-fish based: the standard toppings are ground pork and green onion, and the defining sauce is a blend of chicken-bone and dried-sardine (niboshi) stock. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian versions of the dish.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区舟入川口町5-13 佐々木ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 132.440903, lat 34.382229 (within Hiroshima box). Location is in Funairi-Kawaguchicho, Naka-ku, just west of Peace Memorial Park, near the Funairi-Saiwaicho tram stop.
neighborhood: 中区舟入川口町（舟入幸町電停すぐ／平和公園の西）
seats_text: 15 counter seats only (no tables)
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 11:00-14:00 / 18:00-20:00; Sun 11:00-15:00; closed Wednesdays plus one additional day per month. (Some sources note shortened evening service e.g. until ~18:50; hours can vary.)
closed_days: Wednesdays; plus one additional irregular closing day each month. Sunday/holiday evenings also closed.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, no-frills 15-seat counter shop run by locals where you buy a meal ticket from a machine and choose your spice level before sitting down; little to no English, so come ready to point and use basic Japanese, and remember to mix the noodles thoroughly before eating.
caveats: Wheat-noodle shop with no gluten-free or vegan options; the sauce uses chicken and dried-sardine stock and the topping is ground pork. Cash only. Hours are somewhat irregular (closed Wednesdays plus an extra day each month, and evening service may end early), so go earlier rather than later. Tiny counter, so expect a queue at peak times.

bio.background: Kisaku began as an ordinary, struggling ramen shop in the Funairi district of Hiroshima. Its fortunes changed thanks to a Chinese exchange student living nearby who held cooking classes; after the owner shared noodles with the student and was treated in return to authentic Sichuan dandan noodles, he became determined to learn the dish at its source. He traveled to Sichuan Province, where contacts of the former exchange student guided him through dozens of local noodle shops. He brought the technique home and, after much trial and error, created what is now widely regarded as the original Hiroshima-style soupless dandan noodle, which the shop began serving around 2001.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima soupless dandan noodles (shirunashi tantanmen): springy wheat noodles tossed with a secret sauce of chicken and dried-sardine stock, ground pork and green onion, and the tongue-numbing tingle of Sichuan pepper. The classic order adds a soft-cooked egg, and diners pick their spice level when buying a ticket. The menu also includes a chilled 'pin dandan' version and a tomato-based cold dandan.
bio.philosophy: Authenticity over Japanese convention, and deliberate simplicity. The owner is emphatic that he uses no sesame paste, saying that sesame is an addition made to suit Japanese palates and that in all his visits to Sichuan he never once saw a shop using it; instead the depth comes from fish and chicken stock with fermented soybeans (douchi). The toppings are kept to just green onion and ground meat as a point of pride, letting the sauce and the Sichuan pepper carry the dish.

anecdotes.text: The owner credits a neighborhood Chinese exchange student with sparking the whole idea: after he gave the student noodles, he was treated to real Sichuan dandan noodles and was so struck that he resolved to go to China to learn it properly.

On his training trip he sampled dandan noodles at dozens of restaurants across Sichuan Province, an immersive research tour that shaped the final recipe (one account even mentions an airplane mishap that landed him needing medical attention along the way).
He insists on using no sesame paste at all: 'Sesame is an addition for Japanese palates. In all my visits to Sichuan, I never found a single shop using it.'

The shop is regarded locally as the birthplace of Hiroshima's soupless dandan noodles and is described as a regional soul food; diners are coached to mix the noodles thoroughly (lifting from the bottom dozens of times) before eating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core product is wheat ramen noodles, so the kitchen is a wheat-heavy environment; there is no documented gluten-free preparation or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sauce is built on a Chinese-style base including fermented soybeans (douchi) plus chicken and dried-sardine stock; standard Chinese seasonings of this type typically involve soy/wheat. No allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every version of the signature dish contains animal products (ground pork, plus chicken-bone and dried-sardine stock), so vegan diners cannot be accommodated.

cuisine: Kominka (century-old traditional house) teahouse and sweet shop serving anago-meshi (grilled conger eel over rice), handmade warabi-mochi, and cha-soba (green tea buckwheat noodles)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. Core dishes are not gluten-free: anago-meshi is glazed/seasoned with wheat-based soy-sauce tare, cha-soba is buckwheat noodles typically blended with wheat flour, and warabi-mochi is served with kinako (roasted soybean powder) and kuromitsu. No allergen or gluten information is published.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The signature anago-meshi and the anago cha-soba variant use fish, and savory dishes rely on wheat-soy-sauce-based seasoning, so they are not vegan. Some sweets (e.g. warabi-mochi with kuromitsu and kinako, shiratama zenzai) may be plant-based, but ingredients such as dairy in the assorted sweets platter and broth bases are not confirmed, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町319-4
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature coordinates land on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Takishoji alley near Fujidana Park, about 7 minutes on foot from Itsukushima Shrine's exit and ~15 minutes from the ferry terminal
seats_text: 18 seats (table seating only: three 4-seat tables and one 6-seat table)
hours_raw: 10:00–15:30 (last order 15:15); may close earlier if sold out. Closed Tuesdays (may stay open through peak seasons). Sources vary: official site lists 10:00–15:00, guide lists 10:00–15:30.
closed_days: Tuesdays (may remain open during busy seasons; may close early when sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, family-run traditional teahouse off the main tourist strip where English is unlikely, so a foreign student should be ready to order by pointing at the menu, pay in cash, and not expect allergen guidance.
caveats: Cash only and no card or e-money accepted. Hours differ slightly between the official site (10:00–15:00) and a local guide (10:00–15:30); kitchen may close early when sold out. Official site says reservations are not accepted, while one listing notes reservations possible — treat as walk-in. No published allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information.

bio.background: A small family-run teahouse set inside a renovated traditional wooden house more than a century old, tucked along the quiet Takishoji slope near Fujidana Park, a short walk uphill from Itsukushima Shrine.
