The building is recognized as a registered tangible cultural property, and its timber beams and old fixtures give the room a hushed, time-slipped calm that contrasts with the bustle of Miyajima's main streets.
bio.specialty: Anago-meshi (conger eel simmered until tender, then grilled and laid over rice), handmade warabi-mochi served with kuromitsu black syrup and kinako, and fragrant cha-soba. A popular set pairs a half portion of anago-meshi with the warabi-mochi.
bio.philosophy: Preserving the original character of the historic house while keeping cooking handmade and unhurried, so a visit feels like a gentle pause after visiting the shrine.

anecdotes.text: The anago is slow-simmered until soft and then finished over the grill, so it carries deep savory seasoning and melts in the mouth; the house's handmade warabi-mochi is its most beloved item, served with black syrup and roasted soybean powder.
Seasonal flowers around nearby Fujidana Park can be seen from the windows, adding to the quiet, withdrawn atmosphere of the old house.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago-meshi is seasoned with a savory soy-sauce-based glaze; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no gluten-free alternative is documented.

lead: 汐まち寿司 つるみ
name_ja: 汐まち寿司 つるみ
cuisine: Sushi and anago-meshi (grilled conger eel over rice)
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. As a traditional sushi and anago-meshi restaurant, dishes rely on soy sauce and sweet anago tare (eel glaze), which are wheat-based, and items like California rolls and tamago contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not rely on this restaurant; contact them directly and in detail before visiting.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The menu centers on raw fish nigiri, oysters, and live-killed anago (conger eel), and even the rice is cooked in a two-day kombu-and-bonito dashi, with bonito flakes used in dishes. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町73-2 汐まち庵 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町73-2 via GSI; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 13 seats total: a small tatami room, a counter, and additional table seating.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Wednesday (per Tabelog). The restaurant's own site lists lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), dinner 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:00), closed Tuesday and Wednesday — confirm current hours by phone.
closed_days: Wednesday (Tabelog); the official site indicates Tuesday and Wednesday — verify directly.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 13-seat sushi counter run by the chef in a century-old townhouse; some Japanese or a translation app will help, and a phone reservation is wise since seating is very limited.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ between sources: Tabelog lists closed Wednesday with dinner until 21:00, while the restaurant's own site lists closed Tuesday and Wednesday with dinner until 20:30 (L.O. 19:00). Opening year is also uncertain — the target brief says 2017 while one local article states July 2020. Confirm hours and reservations by phone (0829-44-0008) before visiting.

bio.background: A small sushi counter on the ground floor of Shiomachi-an, a townhouse with roughly a century of history standing just a short walk from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine toward the Miyajima aquarium. The dining room was created so guests of the historic lodging upstairs, and walk-in visitors, could enjoy proper Edomae-style sushi and a local specialty on the island itself.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri and the house anago-meshi made with live-killed conger eel, alongside Hiroshima oysters and local sake.
bio.philosophy: Careful attention to fundamentals: the sushi rice is served at gentle body temperature because the chef believes that warmth changes how the flavor reads, and the rice itself is built on a kombu dashi simmered over two days. The kitchen prizes ingredient quality and temperature control above flash.

anecdotes.text: The anago dish layers simmered conger eel, kamaage whitebait, local Hiroshima-na mustard greens and bonito flakes between sweet tare-seasoned rice and vinegared rice, so a single bite carries several textures of the island's flavors.
The shop occupies the first floor of Shiomachi-an, a converted townhouse with about a century of history near Itsukushima Shrine, and grew out of wanting to give overnight guests a place to eat well.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi service and the sweet anago tare glaze rely on standard soy-sauce-based seasonings, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even the rice is cooked in a kombu-and-bonito dashi and bonito flakes are used within dishes, so fish products are pervasive throughout the kitchen.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（家族経営の個人店）
neighborhood: 中区 流川（八丁堀・流川エリア）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The batter, the layered noodles (yakisoba), and the okonomiyaki sauce are all wheat-based, and the signature 肉玉そば (pork, egg, and noodle okonomiyaki) is not gluten-free. There is no indication of gluten-free flour, dedicated equipment, or any allergen accommodation. Travelers who must avoid gluten should not eat here.
vegan_detail: A standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki griddle shop. The dishes are built around egg, pork, and noodles, and the sauce contains animal-derived ingredients; okonomiyaki here is also typically finished with bonito (fish) flakes. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町5-32
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町5-32 returned [132.464859, 34.389927], inside the Hiroshima box; lands in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Naka Ward, consistent with the listed address.
seats_text: 10 counter seats only
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular / no fixed closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny 10-seat late-night counter shop in the nightlife district where the cook works the griddle right in front of you, so a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude help; pointing at the popular 肉玉そば works fine, but bring cash and don't expect English.
caveats: Open only late at night (roughly 9pm to 3am) and closes on an irregular schedule, so confirm before going. Cash only. There is at least one other okonomiyaki shop also called "がんちゃん" nearby in the same district, so use the address (流川町5-32) and the yellow sign near Yagenbori-dori to find the right one.

bio.background: A small, owner-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, recognizable by its yellow sign near Yagenbori-dori. With just a ten-seat counter and doors that open only late in the evening, it has the feel of a neighborhood late-night spot where regulars and night owls drift in for a hot plate off the griddle.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the 肉玉そば (pork, egg, and soba-noodle okonomiyaki) as the house standard, optionally topped with green onion.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the shop by its yellow sign near Yagenbori-dori and praise the nicely judged cooking of the cabbage and noodles on the griddle.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・もんじゃ
neighborhood: 中区 胡町・銀山町（てっぱん横丁内）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町2-12 広栄ビルB1F てっぱん横丁
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島市中区胡町2-12 via GSI; within Hiroshima box. Building basement (広栄ビルB1F) inside Teppan Yokocho food alley near Ginzancho.
seats_text: 座席数の記載なし。てっぱん横丁内の鉄板カウンター中心の小さな個人店。
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny late-night counter spot inside a teppan food alley with mostly Japanese-speaking regulars, so a few words of Japanese and a friendly, relaxed attitude will help a foreign student feel welcome.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and monja are built on wheat batter and wheat noodles, and the finishing sauce (Otafuku-style) is wheat-based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on pork-belly okonomiyaki, hormone (offal) grills and egg, with bonito/fish-based sauces and toppings, so dishes are not vegan. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Seating capacity, payment methods, and reservation policy are not documented online; cash_only and no-reservation are inferred from it being a tiny late-night alley stand and should be confirmed on arrival. Hours run past midnight (listed as 18:00-05:00); represented as 18:00-29:00.

bio.background: A small independent okonomiyaki and monja stand tucked into Teppan Yokocho, a basement teppan food alley in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa-Ginzancho nightlife district. It opens in the evening and runs deep into the early morning, the kind of late-night griddle spot that fills up with people winding down after a night out.
bio.specialty: Layered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built up in stages, with favorites like pork-and-egg soba okonomiyaki, grilled hormone (offal), and potato-cheese okonomiyaki finished with a rich sweet-savory sauce.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the okonomiyaki as built up in many layers and praise the rich, sweet-savory finishing sauce, alongside hormone (offal) grills and a potato-cheese variation.

cuisine: Anagomeshi (conger eel rice), udon, grilled oysters — Miyajima local fare
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (near Itsukushima Shrine)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町536
geocode_note: GSI geocoder resolved to chō-level (廿日市市宮島町); coordinates land on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box, as expected for a Miyajima address.
seats_text: 20 seats; the interior is small and narrow, suited to a quick meal rather than a leisurely sit-down.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:00（火曜定休、火曜が祝日の場合は営業）
closed_days: Tuesday (open if Tuesday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual local eatery on the busy Omotesando shopping street where ordering is straightforward by pointing at the picture menu, though staff likely speak limited English, so a foreign student should be ready to use a phrase or two of Japanese or a translation app.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core dishes — anagomeshi and rice bowls glazed with wheat-based soy/tare sauce, udon noodles (wheat), tempura, and breaded fried oysters — all contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, ingredients, or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The menu is built around conger eel (anago), oysters, and fish-based dishes, with sauces and broths that typically use dashi and wheat-containing soy sauce. No plant-based options are documented.
caveats: Lunch-only (11:30-16:00) and closed Tuesdays; the dining room is small and cramped, better for a fast meal than a relaxed one. Dietary documentation is absent, so travelers with allergies or plant-based needs should treat all dishes as containing fish, wheat, and seafood unless they confirm otherwise in person.

bio.background: A small, family-run eatery tucked into Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, the lane that leads visitors toward Itsukushima Shrine. It serves the island's signature anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) alongside udon and Hiroshima's famous oysters, with a streetside counter where grilled oysters are cooked and handed over fresh to passers-by.
bio.specialty: Anagomeshi — conger eel laid over rice, described as fluffy eel with a light, restrained seasoning — plus a broad udon menu and freshly grilled oysters.

anecdotes.text: Regulars note the eel dishes are a hit with children, making it an easy stop for families who want to share a Miyajima eel meal together.
Set on the main approach to Itsukushima Shrine, it is valued as an affordable, quick lunch spot among the souvenir shops, with a grilled-oyster counter at the storefront for eating on the move.

cuisine: 焼き牡蠣・牡蠣料理（ろばた焼き）
vegan_detail: This is an oyster specialist; the entire menu is built around shellfish (grilled and fried oysters), with sides like beef croquette and oyster combination sets. There are no vegan dishes, and seasonings such as miso, butter, mayonnaise, and cheese appear across the menu. Drinks (beer, soft drinks) are the only animal-free items.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is indicated. Fried oysters and the grilled-and-fried sets use wheat flour and breadcrumbs, and flavored grilled oysters include miso and other sauces that typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. The kitchen is small and shared, and no allergen handling is documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町553-2
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県廿日市市宮島町553-2 via GSI; coordinates land on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box, near the Omotesando shopping street.
neighborhood: 宮島 表参道商店街
seats_text: Around 12 seats; primarily a stand-and-eat / takeout oyster counter on the shopping street.
closed_days: Thursdays (with occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up oyster counter on Miyajima's main shopping street where you order by pointing at the picture menu, so a foreign student can get by with a smile and a few words of Japanese.

bio.background: Kakifukumaru is the directly operated shop of Ogoshi Suisan, an oyster-farming company based in Miyajima. Sitting just inside the Omotesando shopping street a short walk from the pier, it is marked by blue noren curtains and banners and grew out of the farm's own harvest in the surrounding waters.
bio.origin: Operated by Ogoshi Suisan, an oyster aquaculture business in Miyajima, Hiroshima.
bio.specialty: Robatayaki-style grilled oysters offered in around six flavors (plain, garlic butter, cheese mayonnaise, gratin-style, miso and more), alongside freshly fried oysters and grilled-and-fried combination sets.
bio.philosophy: As a producer-direct shop, it uses only oysters harvested that same day and cooks everything to order rather than in advance, so each oyster is served freshly grilled or fried at its peak.

anecdotes.text: Because the farm runs the shop itself, the oysters are landed that day and prepared the moment you order, letting visitors eat farm-fresh oysters at a reasonable price right on the shopping street.
The grilled oysters come in roughly six flavor variations, with garlic butter being the most popular among visitors.

caveats: Seat count (12) and exact hours come from secondary listings and the official site; hours vary seasonally and Thursday closures can be irregular, so confirm before a special trip. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan handling information is published, and the menu is entirely oyster/shellfish based.

cuisine: Anago-meshi (charcoal-grilled conger eel rice) and local Miyajima oyster dishes; casual local eatery on the Omotesando shopping street
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation documented. The signature dishes rely on a house-made sweet sauce for the anago rice bowl (anago/eel-style tare is typically made with wheat-based soy sauce and mirin), and the popular fried items (oyster fry, nigiri-ten fish-cake tempura) are battered/breaded with wheat flour. No dedicated fryer or GF menu is indicated on any source. Travelers with celiac disease should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm ingredients directly.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around conger eel (anago) and oysters, both animal products, and the rice dishes use fish-derived sweet tare. Even the side items (nigiri-ten) are made from fish cake (kamaboko). No documented plant-based or vegetable-only options.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町536-9
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町536-9 returned [132.32135, 34.298687], which lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box. Address confirmed by Tabelog, official site, and tabiiro.
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (表参道商店街), near Itsukushima Shrine; ~6 min walk from the Miyajima ferry terminal
seats_text: Around 20-35 seats across two floors; one source lists 20 seats, another lists 35 (1F 15 + 2F 20). Small casual eatery.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily; open year-round (無休)
closed_days: None (open year-round / 無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where pointing at the picture menu and a few polite words in Japanese will get you a budget anago rice bowl with no fuss; takeout is also available if you'd rather eat on the ferry.
caveats: Seat count differs between sources (20 vs 35); reservations are described as accepted by some listings but generally walk-in, so arrive early at peak tourist times. No documented allergy, gluten-free, or vegan handling.

bio.background: A small, family-style eatery tucked into the bustling Omotesando shopping arcade on Miyajima, just a short walk from the great torii gate and Itsukushima Shrine. It serves Miyajima's two signature local foods, charcoal-grilled conger eel and freshly harvested oysters, at prices friendly to everyday travelers rather than the splurge level of the island's famous specialist houses.
bio.specialty: Anago-don: fluffy charcoal-grilled conger eel over rice with a house-made sweet tare. The kitchen also turns out a Kaishin'yu set pairing the eel bowl with locally caught oyster fry, oyster rice, and nigiri-ten (fish-cake tempura on a skewer) for eating as you stroll.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Miyajima's local ingredients, especially its prized conger eel and oysters, in an approachable, good-value form, with many items offered as takeout for the ferry or as a souvenir.

anecdotes.text: The shop was featured on the nationally broadcast TV program Kaere Monday in June 2025, where musician Koji Kikkawa highlighted its anago dish, drawing fresh attention to this modest island spot.

Its location right on the main Omotesando approach to Itsukushima Shrine, about six minutes on foot from the ferry pier, makes it a convenient stop for sightseers, and its takeout anago bento is popular to carry onto the boat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-don uses a house-made sweet tare (sauce); eel-style tare is conventionally based on wheat-containing soy sauce and mirin. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on conger eel and oysters with fish-cake (kamaboko) sides; no plant-based options are documented, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cuisine: 精進料理・ヴィーガン／ベジタリアン創作料理（Plant-based Buddhist-style / vegetarian cuisine）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen's signature meat substitutes are wheat gluten (seitan/麸) and soy meat, and dishes include ramen, pasta, katsu curry and breaded cutlets — all wheat-based. Okara-konjac is also used but the core protein swaps rely on wheat, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
vegan_detail: A fully meat- and seafood-free vegetarian kitchen with many vegan dishes, but not 100% vegan: the restaurant states it primarily uses plant-based ingredients while some menu items contain egg or dairy. Standout plant-based items include a vegan mapo rice bowl made without the five pungent vegetables (no garlic or onion), an Oriental vegetarian katsu curry, and lunch sets where you choose a rice bowl, pasta, or ramen. Vegan diners should confirm egg/dairy on a given dish when ordering.
address_ja: 広島県広島市西区己斐上4丁目32-2
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島市西区己斐上4丁目32-2 at lat 34.4101, lng 132.4203 — within the Hiroshima box. Note this is in Koegami-ue, western Hiroshima near Nishi-Hiroshima (西広島) station, in the hills above the city — it is NOT in central Hiroshima near the A-Bomb Dome, nor on Miyajima.
neighborhood: 己斐上（西広島駅方面／広島市西部の高台）
seats_text: 24 seats (per restaurant listing)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); lunch service 11:00-14:30. Closed Tuesdays, with occasional temporary closures.
closed_days: 火曜日（Tuesdays）、加えて不定期の臨時休業あり
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, lunch-focused plant-based spot where ordering is straightforward via set menus, but staff communication is likely mostly in Japanese, so a translation app or a few key phrases will help a foreign student.
caveats: Hours differ slightly across listings (one shows service to 16:00); the official site states 11:00-15:00, L.O. 14:30, so this is a lunch-only venue. Closed Tuesdays plus occasional irregular closures — check before going. Located in the western hills of Hiroshima (己斐上), about a station out from the city center, not in the central A-Bomb Dome area or on Miyajima.

bio.background: Saishoku Kenbi is the restaurant arm of a small group that operates plant-based eateries in a handful of Japanese cities, with the Hiroshima location set in the quiet western hills of the city. The kitchen builds satisfying, meat-style dishes entirely from plants, leaning on wheat gluten, soy protein, and okara-konjac to recreate hearty textures.
bio.origin: Part of a multi-city plant-based group (locations also in Tokyo, Nagoya, and Nakatsugawa).
bio.philosophy: The group's guiding idea is that the foundation of all of life is what we eat — '生活すべての基本は食生活から' — and that the most delicious food is also 'kind and joyful to both body and mind.' They frame their cooking as 健美食 (health-and-beauty food): vegetarian cuisine meant to nourish wellbeing, using no meat or seafood and avoiding the five pungent vegetables in signature dishes like the mapo rice bowl.
bio.specialty: Plant-based comfort dishes — vegan mapo rice bowl (no garlic/onion), Oriental vegetarian katsu curry, and lunch sets with a choice of rice bowl, pasta, or ramen — built from wheat gluten, soy meat, and okara-konjac, with organic vegetables.

anecdotes.text: Signature dishes are designed around the Buddhist-style avoidance of the five pungent vegetables (no garlic or onion), as seen in the vegan mapo rice bowl.
The kitchen recreates hearty, meaty textures from plants using okara-konjac, wheat gluten, and soy protein, with organic vegetables sourced from Hokkaido.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core protein substitutes are wheat gluten and soy meat, and the menu spans ramen, pasta, breaded cutlets and curry — wheat is central throughout the kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is entirely free of meat and seafood, but states that some menu items use egg or dairy, so vegan diners should confirm egg/dairy on each specific dish.

positives.text: No meat or seafood is used anywhere in the kitchen, and signature dishes omit the five pungent vegetables (garlic/onion).

lead: 広島牡蠣処 大衆酒場バケツ 立町店
name_ja: 広島牡蠣処 大衆酒場バケツ 立町店
name_en: Hiroshima Kakidokoro Taishu Sakaba Baketsu Tatemachiten
cuisine: 牡蠣料理・海鮮居酒屋 (oyster and Seto Inland Sea seafood izakaya)
neighborhood: 中区立町
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町5-7 立町Gハウス 2F AB室
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (立町5-7) via GSI; coordinates 34.393448, 132.461365 fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 36 seats total, including a 6-seat counter; private and semi-private rooms available for small to large parties.
hours_raw: 月～水・金～日・祝日・祝前日 11:30-14:00, 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00); 木曜 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Lunch may occasionally be closed; lunch is cash only.
closed_days: No fixed regular holiday listed; lunch service is occasionally closed, so hours can vary.
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local oyster izakaya with a Japanese-language menu and atmosphere; a foreign student should be comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app, and dinner accepts cards while lunch is cash only.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated oyster and seafood izakaya; the menu centers on oysters, anago (conger eel), small sardines (小鰯) and other seafood, with sauces and dressings that typically rely on fish and wheat-based soy sauce. There are no documented vegan dishes.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Signature items such as fried oysters and oyster gratin use wheat, and seafood dishes use standard (wheat-based) soy sauce; there is no indication of gluten-free options or handling.

bio.background: A back-alley oyster izakaya near the Tatemachi tram stop in central Hiroshima, with the dining room on the second floor. The house specializes in Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood, built around its signature 'bucket platter' of steamed and raw oysters served in a metal bucket.
bio.specialty: Oysters in roughly 50 preparations, anchored by the bucket platter of steamed and raw oysters, with fried oysters, butter-grilled oysters, oyster gratin, and oyster ajillo; also anago and small sardines.
bio.philosophy: Each dish is prepared to order after it is requested, emphasizing freshly cooked seafood.

anecdotes.text: The oysters are sourced from Miyamoto Kaisan in Etajima, and the menu also features Hiroshima specialties such as kone (whale skin/cone).
The signature raw-oyster bucket platter (生食用カキ 鬼 バケツ盛り, 6 pieces) is a centerpiece of the menu, with a wide range of oyster dishes made one by one to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters and oyster gratin are on the menu, indicating wheat (breading/flour) is used in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese seafood izakaya dishes here rely on conventional wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is almost entirely oysters and other seafood, so vegan diners face pervasive seafood contact with no documented plant-based options.

caveats: No fixed regular holiday is published and lunch is sometimes closed, so hours are irregular; confirm by phone. Oyster sourcing (Etajima / Miyamoto Kaisan) and the ~50 oyster dishes come from aggregated search descriptions rather than a primary official page. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented.

lead: お好み焼き じゅにあ
name_ja: お好み焼き じゅにあ
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・府中焼き
neighborhood: 広島市中区立町（立町電停すぐ／本通・八丁堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-21
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区立町3-21 returned [132.459778, 34.394054], within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Counter seating on the 1st floor where the chef cooks on the teppan; sofa private rooms on the 2nd floor. Exact seat count not documented.
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional counter-style okonomiyaki shop where the chef cooks in front of you; little to no English is documented, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, but pointing at the menu works fine for a relaxed casual meal.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are okonomiyaki and Fuchu-yaki built on a wheat-flour batter, wheat noodles, and a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The specialty Fuchu-yaki uses beef-fat mince, and okonomiyaki here typically includes egg, pork, and fish-based dashi/bonito plus a wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Cash and PayPay only (no credit cards). Lines form regularly at peak times. Closed Mondays. Hours may change; confirm before visiting.

bio.background: A long-standing, individually run okonomiyaki shop in central Hiroshima, tucked near the Tatemachi tram stop behind the Nomura Securities building. The owner-chef cooks every order himself on the first-floor teppan counter, with sofa private rooms upstairs.
bio.specialty: Fuchu-yaki, a regional style that uses beef-fat mince rather than the usual pork; the result is crisp on the outside and fluffy inside, with the sweetness of the cabbage playing against the sauce. The shop also serves classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, teppan dishes, and even hamburgers.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its approach as keeping to old-fashioned cooking methods and ways of eating, and takes particular pride in its premium hand-pulled noodles, which it says even the noodle maker has full confidence in.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the owner as extremely shy and quiet, soft-spoken rather than gruff, and ultimately a very kind gentleman behind the griddle.
The shop is known for being crowded with lines forming regularly, but locals say the wait is worth it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki and Fuchu-yaki are finished with a standard sweet okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-and-soy based; the dishes are also built on wheat batter and wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature Fuchu-yaki is made with beef-fat mince, and a shared teppan is used for all dishes including meat, egg, and noodles, so vegan separation is not feasible.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（肉玉そば・肉玉うどん中心の鉄板焼き）
neighborhood: 広島市中区加古町（舟入町電停付近／平和記念公園の西側徒歩圏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区加古町8-13
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 広島市中区加古町8-13, within the Hiroshima validation box.
seats_text: 12席（カウンター6席、テーブル6席）
closed_days: 月曜日
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny family-run okonomiyaki counter where ordering is simple (point to 肉玉そば or 肉玉うどん) but little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (そば) or udon, then finished with a wheat-soy-based okonomi sauce. The core dishes here are inherently not gluten-free, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation.
vegan_detail: The signature 肉玉そば and 肉玉うどん contain pork, egg, and a fish/bonito-derived savory base plus wheat-soy okonomi sauce, so the menu is not suitable for vegans. No plant-based version is documented.

bio.background: A small, owner-run okonomiyaki shop tucked into a quiet residential pocket of central Hiroshima, a short walk west of the Peace Memorial Park area. It keeps a deliberately narrow menu, focusing on doing the classic meat-and-egg okonomiyaki well rather than offering a long list of variations.
bio.specialty: The house standard is 肉玉そば (meat, egg, and noodles) with an udon version also available; cheese and double-egg are among the few simple add-ons.
bio.philosophy: A traditional Hiroshima technique of pressing the okonomiyaki down with a weight on the griddle, using fine boiled noodles griddled to a crisp, fragrant finish.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen presses the okonomiyaki with a weight while it cooks and uses fine-textured boiled noodles griddled until crisp, giving a noticeable toasted-wheat aroma that regulars single out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is finished with a standard okonomi/Worcester-type sauce that is wheat- and soy-based.

caveats: Address is 広島市中区加古町8-13 (Kakomachi), which is the same district as 舟入町 and a short walk from the Funairicho tram stop, consistent with the target. Seat count, hours, and menu are from listing pages; no official website found and no allergen/dietary handling information is documented, so GF/vegan travelers should treat all dishes as containing wheat, egg, pork, and fish-derived ingredients.

lead: 太閤うどん 中町店
name_ja: 太閤うどん 中町店
cuisine: 手打ちうどん（夜は居酒屋「饂飩前太閤」）。ヴィーガン・ベジタリアン対応メニューあり、無化調
gf_detail: The noodles are handmade wheat udon (a blend of three domestic wheat flours), so the core dish is not gluten-free. Tempura batter is also wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or wheat-free dashi/soy-sauce alternative, so this is not a suitable destination for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The Nakamachi branch has a dedicated vegan menu that uses no animal ingredients at all — notably soy-milk tantan udon and a no-soup (dry) tantan udon, available only at this location. The vegan dashi is made 100% from plant materials (kombu, vegetables, fruit) after extensive in-house research, and plant-based tempura is fried in a separate pot and oil kept apart from other orders. Note the standard (non-vegan) dashi uses bonito/mackerel/anchovy and wheat-containing soy sauce, so you must order from the vegan menu specifically. Foreign-language menus are available, which helps when specifying vegan needs.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町6-30 広テレプラザ 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区中町6-30 returned [132.458588, 34.39077], within the Hiroshima box. Central Naka-ku, walking distance from Peace Memorial Park / Hondori; nearest tram stop Fukuromachi (袋町), about 2 min on foot.
neighborhood: 中区中町（袋町・本通エリア、平和記念公園から徒歩圏）
seats_text: About 40 seats: roughly 10 counter and 30 table seats, with private rooms accommodating 6-10 people. Fully non-smoking, free Wi-Fi, and power outlets at the counter.
closed_days: 月曜定休（Closed Mondays）
cultural_comfort_note: Foreign-language menus, free Wi-Fi, and counter power outlets make this an easy, welcoming stop for a foreign student, and staff can point vegan diners to the dedicated plant-based menu.
caveats: Lunch and dinner only with a mid-afternoon break (closed 15:00-17:30); closed Mondays. Vegan items are limited to a specific menu (mainly soy-milk tantan udon) — be sure to order from that, as the regular broth contains fish and wheat soy sauce. Udon and tempura are wheat-based, so not suitable for gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Taiko Udon opened this central Hiroshima location in 2016 as its second shop, set in the city's downtown business and tourism district within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and Hondori shopping street. By day it is a handmade-udon restaurant; in the evening it shifts into an izakaya-style spot known as 'Udon-mae Taiko' (饂飩前太閤), serving small plates built around local seasonal ingredients alongside the noodles.
bio.specialty: Handmade udon made from a blend of three domestic wheat flours and mineral-rich salt water, given a two-stage rest and made over a full day so it can be served freshly cut and freshly boiled. Signature dishes include ojiya udon and a creative carbonara udon.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen uses no chemical seasonings (mukacho) and emphasizes carefully selected, safe, local seasonal ingredients. The shop went to unusual lengths for plant-based diners, developing a 100% plant dashi from kombu, vegetables and fruit and keeping a separate fryer and oil for plant-based tempura.

anecdotes.text: The shop offers a free 'baby udon' made only with white dashi for families dining with infants.
On the last day of each month the shop reportedly runs a daytime-only ramen service ('Taiko Ramen').

gf_cross_contamination.text: The udon noodles are made from a blend of three domestic wheat flours, and tempura uses wheat batter; no gluten-free option is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The standard dashi uses Marushima soy sauce from Shodoshima (a conventional wheat-based shoyu) along with bonito, mackerel and anchovy, so the regular broth is neither vegan nor wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: For plant-based tempura and fried items, the shop prepares a dedicated oil and pot used only for plant-based items, kept separate from other customers' orders.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Foreign-language menus and a clearly marked vegan menu are available, and the vegan dishes are explicitly stated to contain no animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: The vegan dashi is made 100% from plant materials (kombu, vegetables and fruit) after extensive in-house research.
Dedicated separate oil and pot are used for plant-based tempura to avoid cross-contact with animal products.

lead: 野菜to発芽酵素玄米弁当 YOU&I
name_ja: 野菜to発芽酵素玄米弁当 YOU&I
name_en: Yasai to Hatsuga Koso Genmai Bento YOU&I
cuisine: グルテンフリーのテイクアウト弁当・惣菜（発芽酵素玄米・米粉使用、ヴィーガン弁当あり）
gf_detail: The entire shop is run gluten-free. They avoid wheat flour throughout the menu, and even fried items (e.g. the salt-koji karaage) are coated in rice flour (米粉) instead of wheat breadcrumbs. The signature base for nearly all dishes is sprouted enzyme brown rice (発芽酵素玄米), which is naturally gluten-free. Note: standard Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce may still contain wheat — no specific wheat-free soy sauce claim was documented, so a strictly gluten-intolerant traveler should confirm sauces directly.
vegan_detail: A dedicated vegan bento (¥1,260), built on a Buddhist temple-cuisine (精進) base, is offered with zero animal-derived ingredients. It is limited to roughly 10 units per day and sells out, so arrive early or order ahead. The rest of the menu includes meat and fish items (e.g. karaage and daily meat/fish bento), so the shop is not fully vegan — vegans should specifically request the vegan bento.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町6-7 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区立町6-7; result lands in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district, within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 中区立町（中の棚商店街・本通近く）
seats_text: Takeout / delivery only — no in-store seating. Bento and side dishes are sold to go.
hours_raw: 10:00～18:00（売り切れ次第終了）／定休日：水曜・日曜・祝日
closed_days: Wednesdays, Sundays, and public holidays (also closes early when sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly takeout counter where you simply point at the bento you want; little conversation is needed, but staff likely speak limited English, so knowing the words "vegan" (ヴィーガン) and "gluten-free" (グルテンフリー) helps you grab the right box.
caveats: Takeout only with no seating — you buy a bento to eat elsewhere. The vegan bento is capped at about 10 per day and sells out, and the whole shop may close early once stock runs out. No documented dedicated-fryer or detailed cross-contamination protocol, so confirm seasonings (e.g. soy sauce) if you are strictly gluten-intolerant.

bio.background: YOU&I is a gluten-free takeout bento and side-dish shop in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district, on the first floor of a long-established local Japanese-restaurant company's main building. It opened on October 18, 2021 as a new wellness-focused concept, just a short walk from the Hondori shopping street and the Nakanotana arcade.
bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Naka Ward, Tatemachi), launched by an established Hiroshima Japanese-cuisine company.
bio.specialty: Bento built on 発芽酵素玄米 (sprouted enzyme brown rice) — brown rice sprouted, fermented with adzuki beans and salt, then gently warmed for a nutrient-dense, mochi-like chewy texture. Everything is gluten-free, with rice flour used in place of wheat for fried items, plus a daily-limited vegan bento on a temple-cuisine base.
bio.philosophy: "お米でご縁を結ぶ。毎日の健康は、毎日のごはんから" — building connections through rice, with daily health beginning at the daily meal. The shop frames its cooking around supporting beauty and wellness through food.

anecdotes.text: The shop bases its whole menu on sprouted enzyme brown rice, which it describes as a modern superfood: brown rice that is sprouted, fermented with adzuki beans and salt, then kept warm to develop a chewy, mochi-like texture and high nutritional density.

The vegan bento, built on a Buddhist temple-cuisine (精進) base and free of any animal-derived ingredients, is made in a strictly limited run of about 10 per day and became an immediate hit after opening.
Even the fried dishes skip wheat: the salt-koji karaage is coated in rice flour rather than wheat breadcrumbs, keeping the whole shop gluten-free.

positives.text: The entire shop is operated gluten-free, with wheat flour avoided across the menu and rice flour used instead of wheat breadcrumbs for fried items.
All dishes are built on naturally gluten-free sprouted enzyme brown rice rather than wheat-based grains.
A fully animal-product-free vegan bento on a temple-cuisine base is available (limited daily quantity).

lead: 伊都岐珈琲 宮島店
name_ja: 伊都岐珈琲 宮島店
cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー専門カフェ（コーヒーソフトクリーム、モーニング、自家焙煎豆販売）
neighborhood: 宮島・厳島神社近く（表参道商店街エリア）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町420
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町420 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box; lands on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: 約20席（カウンター、テーブル、ソファ席）
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and ordering is casual cafe-style, so a foreign student can visit comfortably; note that credit cards are not accepted (electronic money and QR payments only).
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labeling is documented. Drip coffee, espresso and the specialty-coffee soft serve are not gluten-containing by nature, but food items like the morning hot sandwich and cream pan use wheat bread. No allergy or gluten-free information is published, so a GF traveler must ask directly.
vegan_detail: Primarily a coffee shop. Black drip coffee and espresso are plant-based, but the signature soft serve uses fresh dairy cream, the latte/mocha use milk, and the morning set uses ham, cheese and bread. No vegan menu or plant-milk option is documented, so a vegan should ask about dairy-free milk before ordering.

bio.background: Founder Keisuke Sasaki grew up in Hiroshima City. As a university student he and two friends launched a venture pulling rickshaws on Miyajima, which led him to study the island's history and culture to answer tourists' questions. While doing this he also worked part-time at a coffee shop, and in 2006 he opened his first self-roasted-coffee cafe at age 23, working until midnight in a back-alley spot with few customers. By 2009 he had opened a second cafe, sarasvati, as Japan's appreciation for specialty coffee grew.
bio.specialty: Specialty coffee that he roasts himself every morning on the island, using only the roughly 5% of beans in the market that qualify as 'specialty', alongside a popular specialty-coffee soft serve.
bio.philosophy: He aims for an 'unnoticed specialty' (意識されないスペシャルティ): he is happiest when people enjoy his coffee without consciously thinking about how special it is, letting it blend naturally into a quiet moment of their day.

anecdotes.text: He describes the early years bluntly: 'barely surviving, starting in an alley where almost no one came, so we did not get many customers' (生きていくのに精一杯。路地裏の、誰も来ないような場所で始めたので、お客さんもそんなに来なくて).
The shop is the only place self-roasting specialty coffee on Miyajima, a UNESCO World Heritage island, set in a white old-house-style (古民家風) renovated building near the shrine approach.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which helps travelers communicate basic preferences when ordering.

caveats: Hours are 09:00-18:00 daily per Tabelog but closures are irregular (不定休); confirm the day of visit. The Tabelog address (宮島町420) and the official-site listing (宮島町413) differ slightly between the brand's nearby shops — both place it in the central Miyajima shrine-approach area. Momiji manju is mentioned in the brand profile but not confirmed on this shop's current menu, where the signature item is the specialty-coffee soft serve.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (cold dipping noodles in a spicy soy-chili sauce) and soup-less tantanmen
neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima nightlife district); ~5 min walk from Ebisucho tram stop
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町7-31
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 15 seats (8 counter seats, 2 tables for 4)
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 / 19:00-07:00 (open daily, year-round)
closed_days: None (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, no-frills local noodle counter in a nightlife alley with no reservations and a mostly Japanese-speaking, late-night crowd; pointing at the menu and choosing your noodle size and spice level works fine, but expect little to no English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat-based: tsukemen uses wheat noodles dipped in a soy-sauce-and-chili sauce (Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat), and the tantanmen is also a wheat-noodle dish. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature tsukemen is topped with char siu (pork) and served with a soy-chili dipping sauce, and the tantanmen is meat-and-sesame based. No vegan option is documented.
caveats: Story details (founder, exact founding year) are not documented on the pages reviewed; described only as a long-loved Hiroshima tsukemen veteran shop of 10+ years. Dipping-sauce ingredient breakdown (e.g., whether fish broth is used) is not published, so GF/vegan cross-contact details are inferred from the dish type rather than stated by the shop.

bio.background: A veteran Hiroshima tsukemen shop that has been a fixture of the Nagarekawa nightlife street for more than ten years, marked by a red noren curtain near the Billy the Kid bar.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chewy noodles served cold with crisp shredded cabbage, julienned daikon, and char siu, dipped in a bright, refreshingly tangy and spicy sauce; also a Hiroshima specialty soup-less tantanmen.
bio.philosophy: Make-it-your-own bowls: diners choose their noodle portion and toppings and dial the spice level up or down to taste, with a squeeze of lemon to brighten the dip.

anecdotes.text: Open from late night into the early morning in the heart of the drinking district, it is a favorite spot to round off a night out with a bowl of noodles.
Marked by a red noren in front of the Billy the Kid bar on Hiroshima's Nagarekawa street, it is described as a long-established tsukemen shop loved for over ten years.
The tsukemen has a refreshing sour edge, comes piled with cabbage, shredded daikon and char siu, and lets you adjust spice and portion; adding lemon makes it even more refreshing.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat-based (wheat tsukemen/ramen noodles and a soy-sauce-based dip), so a shared kitchen makes gluten avoidance impractical.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima tsukemen is dipped in a soy-sauce-and-chili sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on pork char siu and meat/sesame-based tantanmen, so animal products are central and shared.

cuisine: Local Hiroshima/Miyajima dining hall (shokudo) specializing in fried oysters, anago (conger eel), and front-of-shop grilled oysters, with seafood set meals, donburi, and noodles.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dishes — fried oysters (wheat-flour batter and breadcrumbs), tempura-style items, udon/soba/ramen noodles, and grilled/simmered seafood glazed with standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) — all rely on gluten. There is no documented dedicated fryer or gluten-free menu, so cross-contamination from the shared fry oil and soy-based sauces is likely. The parent company notes it can discuss food allergies in general, but no celiac-safe protocol is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around oysters, anago (conger eel), and other Setouchi seafood; set meals include chawanmushi (egg custard) and dashi-based soups, and dishes are seasoned with fish stock and soy sauce. No documented plant-based or vegan options.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町1144
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町1144 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine / Senjokaku.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine and Senjokaku Hall
seats_text: 1st floor 50 seats (tables, chairs, box seats); 2nd floor 65 seats (tables, tatami zashiki, window counter). Approx. 115 total across two floors.
hours_raw: 10:30–16:00 (per official site). Note: Tabelog lists 11:00–16:00.
closed_days: No regular closing day documented (open daily).
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, tourist-friendly seafood dining hall right by the shrine where pointing at picture menus works fine, but staff may have limited English and there is no vegan or gluten-free provision, so a guide or translation help is useful for anyone with strict dietary needs.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations. Hours differ slightly between the official site (10:30–16:00) and Tabelog (11:00–16:00). The name "とりい" is also used by the larger affiliated 宮島観光センター鳥居屋 group/tour facility nearby; this record is the individual diners' restaurant (お食事処とりい) at 宮島町大町1144.

bio.background: A seafood dining hall on the island of Miyajima, set among the shops on the approach near Itsukushima Shrine and Senjokaku Hall, where oysters are grilled out front to draw in passers-by.
bio.specialty: Fried-oyster set meals, anago (conger eel) dishes, and front-of-shop charcoal-grilled oysters, alongside Setouchi seafood, donburi, and noodles.
bio.philosophy: The house aims to be a place to enjoy the flavors of the Seto Inland Sea and to serve a taste that stays in your memory, choosing ingredients and cooking with care.

anecdotes.text: Oysters are grilled at the storefront, a classic Miyajima sight, with the seasonal Setouchi lemon soft serve and strawberry shaved ice (spring–autumn) offered alongside the seafood.
Seating spans two floors, including window-side counter seats and tatami zashiki rooms upstairs, making it comfortable for families and groups visiting the shrine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature fried oysters use wheat-flour batter and breadcrumbs, and the kitchen also serves udon, soba, and ramen, so the shared fry oil and prep surfaces present a high gluten cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Grilled and simmered seafood and donburi are glazed/seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is seafood-centered (oysters, anago) with egg-based chawanmushi and dashi/soy seasoning throughout, so there is no separation from animal ingredients.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The affiliated company's site states food-allergy inquiries can be accommodated, but no celiac- or vegan-specific protocol is documented for the dining hall.

lead: 貴家。 地蔵通り本店
name_ja: 貴家。 地蔵通り本店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (teppanyaki)
neighborhood: Fujimi-cho, Naka-ku (Jizo-dori), near Chuden-mae
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区富士見町5-11 藤井ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō/banchi-level address (富士見町5-11); within Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 12 counter seats only; food is grilled directly in front of you. One source describes it as roughly 15 counter seats — in either case it is a small, intimate counter-only space.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 17:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Tuesdays; also closed 1st and 3rd Mondays (holiday hours may vary).
closed_days: Tuesdays; also 1st and 3rd Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only local spot where the owner grills in front of you and you eat straight off the hot griddle with a metal spatula (no chopsticks) — charming but with its own etiquette, so some basic Japanese or a willingness to follow along helps.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The okonomiyaki itself is built on a wheat-flour batter (crepe layer), is layered with udon or soba noodles, and is finished with okonomiyaki sauce — all of which contain wheat/gluten. There is no indication of any gluten-free menu or accommodation, so it is not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
vegan_detail: The core dish is a "meat and egg" (肉玉) okonomiyaki containing pork, egg, and noodles, and the standard sauce/preparation are not plant-based. No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Very small counter (about 12 seats), so seating is restricted by day/time/party size and there can be waits, especially when takeout orders are busy. Cash is the primary payment method (PayPay QR also accepted). Hours are somewhat irregular due to the extra Monday closures.

bio.background: A small, owner-run okonomiyaki counter tucked along Hiroshima's historic Jizo-dori in Fujimi-cho. The proprietor has spent roughly three decades devoted to okonomiyaki, grilling each one personally and one at a time right in front of guests at the 12-seat counter.
bio.philosophy: The shop is proud of being a 'seitoha' (orthodox) Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — authentic, traditional preparation done meticulously. The owner changes the cabbage variety with the seasons and pays close attention to how the vegetables are cut and grilled so their natural sweetness comes through, finished with a house sauce made with plenty of vegetables that is clean and not overly sweet.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with a meat-and-egg base, customized with your choice of udon or soba; the noodle layer is grilled until distinctively crisp, and the seasonal cabbage and house vegetable-rich sauce are the signatures.

anecdotes.text: You eat straight off the teppan griddle using only a metal spatula — no chopsticks — which feels intimidating to newcomers but becomes part of the charm once you get the hang of it.
The cabbage is swapped by season and the vegetables are cut and grilled with care to draw out their sweetness, paired with a clean, not-sweet house sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle where wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-containing sauce are all prepared, making cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard okonomiyaki contains pork, egg, and noodles cooked on a shared griddle alongside other meat dishes; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

lead: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 地蔵通り本店
name_ja: 汁なし担担麺 武蔵坊 地蔵通り本店
cuisine: Hiroshima shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodles)
neighborhood: Fujimi-cho, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (near Chuden-mae tram stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区富士見町5-12 田ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [132.46022, 34.387585] fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: About 20 seats, counter-style small specialty shop.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sun: 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-22:00; Fri, Sat & day before holidays: 11:00-15:00 / 18:00-00:00; closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local noodle counter where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you through; ordering and the self-seasoning ritual are simple once you sit down.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire menu is built on wheat-flour Chinese-style noodles, and the sauces include soy-sauce-based variants; there are no gluten-free options or substitutions documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The signature soupless tantanmen is topped with ground pork (挽き肉) and the base broth/sauce is built on chicken stock; sesame-paste, soy-sauce, cheese and curry variants are all animal-based. No meat-free option is documented.

bio.background: Musashibo opened in April 2013 in Hiroshima's Fujimi-cho and built a following as a dedicated shiru-nashi tantanmen (soupless dandan noodle) house, one of more than seventy shops keeping alive a dish that has become a Hiroshima specialty. After roughly a decade it had grown into a name locals line up for daily and now sends its noodles nationwide through a mail-order shop and additional branches.
bio.specialty: Soupless dandan noodles built on low-hydration wheat noodles, a generous house-made white-sesame paste with a mild sweetness, ground pork, green onion, and the shop's own Musashibo sansho (Sichuan pepper). Diners are encouraged to eat a first bite plain, then tune the bowl with four kinds of Sichuan pepper, ginseng vinegar, and house sauce. Variations run from rich sesame and aromatic soy sauce to curry, cheese, and the fiery Shoten (昇天) and DEATH bowls.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames its noodles as 'tantanmen you could eat every day,' aiming for an approachable, customizable bowl through carefully chosen ingredients rather than one-note heat.

anecdotes.text: The base broth is built from chicken stock simmered with onion, carrot and ginger for about four hours, then paired with a homemade white-sesame paste for a mild, sweet richness.
Each set sold for home cooking includes noodles, sauce, ground meat, green onion and the shop's signature Musashibo sansho so customers can recreate the in-store flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core product is wheat-flour noodles cooked in shared boiling water; no gluten-free preparation is described.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu includes an 'aromatic soy sauce' (芜醒醤油) variant, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are topped with ground pork and the broth is chicken-stock based, so all dishes share animal-product preparation surfaces.

caveats: No published allergen labeling or staff allergy-handling information was found; the shop is cash-only and does not take reservations. Travelers with celiac disease or strict vegan needs cannot be accommodated. Allergen specifics should be confirmed directly with the shop.

cuisine: Seafood — grilled and raw oysters, anago (conger eel), seafood rice bowls, sashimi, and local-style ramen
neighborhood: Miyajima island, Minatomachi (by the ferry terminal)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町港町1162-6
geocode_note: Geocoded from 宮島町港町1162-6; lands near the Miyajima ferry terminal on the island, within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Not published; a small casual spot near the ferry terminal.
hours_raw: 10:30-20:00 (per Tabelog and Hitosara, listed daily). Note: one listing (Retty) indicates closed Tuesdays — confirm with the shop.
closed_days: None listed on the main listing; one source notes a possible Tuesday closure.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly seafood counter right by the ferry where pointing at the photo menu works fine, but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese phrases help.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Core dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce and tare (oysters, anago, seafood bowls), and the menu includes ramen and other wheat items, so this is not a safe choice for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood specialist — oysters, conger eel, sashimi and seafood rice bowls are the focus, prepared with fish and wheat-soy-based seasonings. There are no documented vegan options.
caveats: Hours conflict between sources: the main listing and Hitosara show 10:30-20:00 daily with no fixed closing day, while Retty lists a Tuesday closure. Seat count is not published. No official website was found.

bio.background: A small, locally-run seafood spot sitting right by the Miyajima ferry terminal, opened in 2017 as one of the first things visitors see when they step onto the island. It is run by a small local operator dedicated to this shop.
bio.specialty: Miyajima oysters served grilled and raw, alongside anago (conger eel), sashimi, seafood rice bowls, and a local lemon-salt ramen.

anecdotes.text: Positioned directly in front of the ferry terminal, it functions as a quick, casual first taste of Miyajima's signature oysters for travelers arriving on the island.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oysters, anago and seafood bowls are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and tare, which are wheat-based.

cuisine: 100% plant-based Japanese vegan: vegan sushi, okonomiyaki, ramen, curry, tendon, katsudon, karaage, tempura, agedashi tofu, parfaits and lemon cake
neighborhood: 中区大手町（平和記念公園・原爆ドーム近く、広電「袋町」電停 徒歩約2〜5分）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町2-7-1 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町2-7-1 returned [132.456467, 34.39249], within the Hiroshima box, consistent with the Otemachi / Peace Memorial Park area.
seats_text: Total 29 seats per the restaurant's listing (another listing states 30); a compact single-floor dining room.
closed_days: 不定休 (Irregular holidays; closures announced on Instagram)
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant's own website is offered in English, Japanese, and Chinese with an English contact option, so a foreign student should feel comfortable communicating here.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan. 100% plant-based and alcohol-free across all dishes and desserts, with no animal-derived ingredients used anywhere in the kitchen.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free venue. Although the menu is allergy-conscious, no gluten-free certification, dedicated GF preparation, or GF menu is documented. Several signature items are inherently wheat-based: okonomiyaki batter, ramen noodles, tempura/karaage batter, katsu breading; and standard soy sauce typically contains wheat. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask directly about specific dishes and cross-contamination before ordering.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly between listings (29 vs 30). No published gluten-free certification or cross-contamination protocol; GF diners should confirm per dish. Closures are irregular and announced on Instagram, so check before visiting.

bio.origin: Otemachi, central Hiroshima, near Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome
bio.background: JoGeSaYu opened in May 2025 in central Hiroshima as a fully plant-based restaurant. Its food is overseen by Katsumi Kusumoto, the chef behind Saido, which the international vegan platform HappyCow named the world's best vegan restaurant in 2019, while the desserts are guided by award-winning pastry chef Hironobu Tsujiguchi. The kitchen turns Japanese comfort food such as sushi, okonomiyaki, ramen, curry and katsu into entirely plant-based versions.
bio.specialty: Plant-based renditions of Japanese classics: vegan sushi, okonomiyaki, ramen and curry, alongside refined vegan desserts such as parfaits and lemon cake.
bio.philosophy: The name (up, down, left, right) expresses acceptance of every direction, value, and form of diversity. The team set out to create a single table where people of any background can share a meal together, and to prove that plant-based food need not lack flavor.

anecdotes.text: The name was chosen to symbolize openness to all directions and forms of diversity, reflecting its goal of a table where everyone can eat together regardless of dietary restriction.
Culinary direction comes from Katsumi Kusumoto of Saido, named the world's best vegan restaurant by HappyCow in 2019, with desserts by pastry chef Hironobu Tsujiguchi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information published on whether the soy sauce or seasonings used are gluten-free or wheat-free; standard soy sauce typically contains wheat. Confirm directly if avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is 100% plant-based with no animal products or alcohol used anywhere, so there is no animal-ingredient cross-contact risk.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu is described as built to accommodate diverse backgrounds including religious dietary rules and allergies, so diners with and without restrictions can eat together.
The restaurant states it welcomes all guests regardless of religious, health, or lifestyle restrictions.

positives.text: 100% plant-based and alcohol-free across the whole menu, making it a fully safe choice for vegans and vegetarians.
Vegan versions of Japanese favorites such as sushi, okonomiyaki, ramen, curry, tempura and karaage, plus vegan parfaits and lemon cake.
Official website available in English, Japanese, and Chinese, easing communication for international diners.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki
neighborhood: Nakaku (Nekoyacho), near Tsuchihashi / Tokaichimachi tram stops, west of the Hondori area
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区猫屋町2-16
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区猫屋町2-16 returned [132.44754, 34.39555], within the Hiroshima box; consistent with the Tsuchihashi/Tokaichimachi tram-stop location.
seats_text: 14 seats per the listing (8 counter + 6 table); other write-ups describe roughly 7 counter seats and 2 tables, so figures vary slightly but all point to a very small shop.
closed_days: Monday and Friday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny owner-run counter shop where ordering and chatting happen mostly in Japanese, though the cook is used to visitors and will plate your okonomiyaki rather than serve it straight off the griddle if you prefer.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made with egg, pork, and a fish/bonito-derived sauce on a shared griddle, and there is no documented vegan or vegetarian option. The shop's signature is cabbage-forward ('倍ヤサイ' double-vegetable is offered) but the dishes themselves are not vegan.
caveats: Seat count varies across sources (13–15); listed as 14. Phone or Instagram reservation only holds a place in line, with orders taken in arrival order, and the shop may decline reservations in busy periods. Takeout orders are prioritized. Cash only.

bio.background: A small, tucked-away okonomiyaki shop in a back alley near the Tsuchihashi/Tokaichimachi trams, only about an eight-minute walk from the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. Despite being off the main tourist routes, it has been a neighborhood favorite for many years, run intimately by the cook and his wife.
bio.specialty: Cabbage-forward Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki. The name plays on 甘藍 (kanran, classical Japanese for cabbage), signaling that cabbage is the true star; the shop even offers a double-vegetable ('倍ヤサイ') option.
bio.philosophy: Let the vegetable lead and finish each okonomiyaki with care. The cook works the griddle by reading its different temperature zones to cook precisely, a hands-on craft at a counter that seats only a handful of people.

anecdotes.text: The cook is described as a true craftsman, deftly using the different heat zones across the iron griddle to finish each okonomiyaki carefully.
Though traditionally eaten straight off the hot griddle, the shop will serve okonomiyaki on a plate for visitors who prefer it, a small kindness toward travelers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared iron griddle (teppan) used for wheat batter, wheat noodles, pork, and egg, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-and-soy based; standard for Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on one griddle shared with pork and egg, so no dish can be considered free of animal-product cross-contact.

cuisine: Anago (conger eel) kaiseki / Japanese course cuisine
neighborhood: Miyajimaguchi
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島口1-5-11 あなごめしうえの宮島口本店 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; lands at Miyajimaguchi within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 32 seats: five tables of 4, two semi-private rooms for 4, and one fully private room.
hours_raw: Mon, Thu 11:00-15:00; Fri-Sun 11:00-15:00, 17:00-22:00; closed Tue, Wed. (Dinner service Fri-Sun only.)
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A calm sit-down course restaurant where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed pace go a long way; staff are used to visitors arriving from the nearby Miyajima ferry but full English support is not guaranteed.
gf_detail: This is an anago (conger eel) course restaurant. The signature anagomeshi is cooked in a wheat-based soy-sauce broth, and the courses include grilled, vinegared, simmered, and Saikyo-miso preparations that rely on soy sauce and miso (wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented; treat it as not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The entire concept centers on conger eel (anago) and Seto Inland Sea seafood, served in multi-course form. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes; the rice itself is cooked with anago bones and soy sauce. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Dinner service runs Friday through Sunday only; lunch is offered Monday and Thursday through Sunday. Mondays and Thursdays are limited to the anagomeshi set-meal menu, while course menus are served Friday to Sunday. Hours and seat configuration drawn from listing pages and may change seasonally.

bio.background: Tanikichi sits on the second floor of Anagomeshi Ueno, the Miyajimaguchi institution credited with inventing anagomeshi (conger-eel rice). The dish traces back to founder Ueno Tanikichi, who began selling anagomeshi as a station boxed lunch around 1901 at Miyajima Station; the restaurant upstairs carries his name. Where the ground floor serves the famous boxed and bowled anagomeshi, Tanikichi presents the same beloved eel in a fuller course format in a quiet, traditional Japanese setting just a couple of minutes from the Miyajima ferry pier.
bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) served as multi-course cuisine: the signature anagomeshi alongside grilled (white-grilled and Saikyo-miso), vinegared, and simmered eel preparations, built around Seto Inland Sea ingredients.
bio.philosophy: The fourth-generation owner, Ueno Junichi, speaks of being deeply mindful that anagomeshi has been loved for generations, and of his wish to protect and pass on that flavor together with the customers who know its taste. Chef Yasui, a local of the area, is described as cooking gentle, refined flavors.

anecdotes.text: Anagomeshi was born here: the founder reworked the plain rice of the local eel bowl into a soy-seasoned rice cooked with conger-eel bones, then layered the eel densely on top, and sold it along the Sanyo Main Line, where it has been served for more than 110 years.
The upstairs restaurant takes the founder's own name, Tanikichi, continuing the family line that began as rice merchants on Miyajima before opening a teahouse near the Miyajimaguchi station approach.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anagomeshi rice is cooked in a soy-sauce-based seasoning, and the course includes Saikyo-miso-grilled and simmered eel, all of which rely on standard wheat-containing soy sauce and miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every documented dish is built on conger eel and seafood; the rice itself is cooked with anago bones, so there is no vegan-safe base.

cuisine: Anago (conger eel) and seafood eatery — anago-ju (eel over rice), fried oyster sets, nigiri-ten skewers, oyster rice
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Omotesando shopping street near Itsukushima Shrine approach, ~6 min walk from the ferry pier
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町536-9
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町536-9 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando area as expected.
seats_text: Around 20 seats over two floors (one source lists 35); small casual local eatery.
closed_days: No regular closing days (無休); open year-round
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-in eatery on the busy Miyajima approach used to tourists; you can point at the photo menu and a few words of Japanese plus pointing will get you through ordering comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature anago-ju is glazed with a house-made sweet tare that is built on Japanese soy sauce and mirin (wheat-containing), and the headline fried items — nigiri-ten skewers and kaki-fry (fried oysters) — are breaded/battered with wheat flour. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built around conger eel, oysters, and other seafood, with fish-based fishcake (nigiri-ten) and dashi/soy-based sauces. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
caveats: Seat count varies by source (Tabelog ~20, another listing 35). Official website kaishinyuu.jp is referenced across listings but the /en and root pages returned errors at time of verification; phone is 0829-44-1950. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published anywhere found, so celiac/vegan travelers should confirm directly before visiting.

bio.background: A casual sit-down eatery on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, just a few minutes' walk from the ferry pier on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. It serves the island's signature conger eel over rice alongside fried oysters and other local seafood, with many items also sold for takeout so visitors can eat along the approach or on the ferry.
bio.specialty: Anago-ju — conger eel laid over rice, described as having a fluffy texture matched with a house-made sweet glaze. The 'Kaishinyu Set' pairs the eel bowl with fried oysters so diners can sample two Miyajima specialties at once. Nigiri-ten skewers (grilled/fried fishcake in flavors like negi-dako and cheese-bacon) and oyster rice round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself on value and flavor, aiming to be a convenient, affordable stop for shrine visitors who want to try Miyajima's famous eel and oysters.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant was featured on the TV variety program 'Kaere Monday' broadcast on June 23, 2025, with Sandwich Man and guests Tamio Okuda and Koji Kikkawa, highlighting its anago-ju.

Because much of the menu travels well, the anago bento is pitched as something to enjoy on the ferry or take home — though the listing jokes that diners eating outside near the shrine should watch out for the local deer trying to steal their food.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago-ju is finished with a house-made sweet sauce (tare), which in Japanese eel cuisine is standard soy-sauce-and-mirin based and therefore contains wheat.

lead: VEGAN FRUITSCAFE TAMARU（フルーツカフェ タマル 上幟町店）
name_ja: ビーガン フルーツカフェ タマル 紙屋町店
cuisine: All-vegan fruit cafe: fruit parfaits, vegan sweets and cakes, fruit sandwiches and toast, fruit oatmeal with almond milk, smoothies and fresh juices. No eggs, dairy, or white sugar used.
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is documented on any available source. The menu centers on fruit-forward vegan items (parfaits, oatmeal, smoothies) that may suit gluten-avoidant diners, but sandwiches and toast use bread, and there is no stated GF preparation or cross-contamination control. A traveler with celiac disease should ask in person.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan since an April 2022 renovation made the entire menu plant-based. No animal products (eggs, dairy) and no white sugar are used. Signature items include fruit parfaits with whole bunches of seasonal fruit (e.g. Shine Muscat), vegan sandwiches, and fruit oatmeal served with almond milk.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町2-2-2 源吉兆庵 2階
geocode_note: Geocoded from the chō-level address 広島県広島市中区紙屋町2-2-2 via GSI; result lands in central Naka-ku within the Hiroshima box, near the A-Bomb Dome and Hondori arcade.
neighborhood: Kamiyachō / Hondori, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (near A-Bomb Dome)
seats_text: Seat count not documented in available sources.
closed_days: Mondays (open on national holidays, closed the following day instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, welcoming fruit cafe in a busy downtown shopping area; menus are mostly in Japanese with lots of photos, so a few basic phrases and pointing will get you by comfortably.
caveats: The prompt-supplied branch name 上幟町店 appears to be incorrect: the dedicated all-vegan TAMARU branch is the 紙屋町 (Kamiyachō) location inside the Minamoto Kitchoan building 2F. The Tabelog page for this branch currently shows 掲載保留 (listing on hold, meaning the listing is unverified and the shop may have moved or closed), but a dedicated Hiroshima vegan restaurant guide, an active Wolt delivery listing, and the cafe's own Instagram (@veganfruitscafetamaru_kamiya) all indicate it operates. Hours and Monday-closed status are from the vegan guide; confirm current hours via Instagram before visiting. TAMARU is a multi-branch Hiroshima fruit-cafe brand, so this is not an independent single shop.

bio.background: Vegan Fruits Cafe Tamaru is part of TAMARU, a Hiroshima fruit business that grew out of a small fruit vendor near Hiroshima Station with roughly 70 years of history. The downtown Kamiyacho location reinvented itself in April 2022, turning its entire menu plant-based to introduce locals and visitors to vegan food.
bio.specialty: Fruit-forward vegan parfaits and sweets made without eggs, dairy, or white sugar; signature parfaits pile on whole bunches of seasonal fruit such as Shine Muscat, alongside vegan sandwiches and fruit oatmeal with almond milk.
bio.philosophy: To show that vegan dessert can be genuinely delicious by building everything around high-quality fruit and grains rather than animal products or refined white sugar.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is an offshoot of a long-running family fruit vendor near Hiroshima Station that has been selling fruit for about 70 years, so the parfaits are built on a deep fruit-sourcing pedigree.
In April 2022 the Kamiyacho branch renovated and switched its entire menu to vegan, eliminating eggs, dairy, and white sugar.

positives.text: The entire menu is plant-based, made without eggs, dairy, or white sugar, so vegan diners do not need to scrutinize individual dishes.

lead: 牡蠣と海鮮酒場 うみいち 流川店
name_ja: 牡蠣と海鮮酒場 うみいち 流川店
name_en: Kaki to Kaisen Sakaba Umiichi Nagarekawa-ten
cuisine: Hiroshima oyster & seafood izakaya
neighborhood: Naka-ku, Ebisu-chō / Nagarekawa entertainment district, ~1 min from Ebisuchō tram stop, near Hatchōbori and walkable to Hondōri
address_ja: 〒730-0021 広島県広島市中区胡町3-15 WAOビル5F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区胡町3-15; result lands in central Naka-ku, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 50 seats; all private-room layout with sunken-floor (horigotatsu) seating
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:00-翌1:00, Fri 17:00-翌2:00, Sat 16:00-翌2:00, Sun 17:00-翌1:00 (official site). Note: reservation aggregators list earlier midday openings on some days; official site hours used here.
closed_days: None (open year-round, 無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local izakaya in the Nagarekawa nightlife district where some basic Japanese or a translation app will help, as menus and staff are oriented to Japanese guests.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. As an izakaya, fried/tempura oysters use wheat batter and most dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce; no dedicated GF preparation indicated.
vegan_detail: An oyster and seafood izakaya — the menu centers on oysters, sashimi and fish, with wheat-soy-sauce seasonings. No vegan offerings indicated.
caveats: Official-site hours differ from some reservation-platform listings (which show midday openings on certain days); confirm before a daytime visit. The address is given as both 胡町3-15 WAOビル5F (official site) and is part of the Nagarekawa GRIT/WAO building.

bio.background: An oyster-and-seafood izakaya in the heart of Nagarekawa, central Hiroshima's largest nightlife quarter, serving oysters and seafood bought from local Hiroshima suppliers.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served many ways — creamy raw oysters by the piece, fragrant grilled oysters, oyster tempura, fried oysters and sake-steamed oysters with butter — alongside sashimi platters and a deep lineup of Hiroshima local sake such as Kamotsuru, Ugo no Tsuki and Kirei.

anecdotes.text: Set in a modern, black-toned interior with atmospheric lighting and fully private rooms with sunken-floor seating, it pairs market-fresh oysters with regional sake.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (teppanyaki)
neighborhood: Yagenbori, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori, walkable to Hondori)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe layered with cabbage, pork, wheat noodles (soba/udon), and egg, then finished with a wheat-containing okonomiyaki sauce. Effectively every dish here contains gluten, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki centers on pork, egg, and a single shared griddle, with bonito/fish-based seasonings and oysters added in winter. No vegan version is documented and the cooking is fundamentally animal-based.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀10-6
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned a single feature at the building address; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: About 47 seats total, a mix of teppan counter and tatami seating, with a private tatami room that can hold roughly 20-24 people.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 16:00-22:30 (L.O. 22:30); Sun & holidays 16:00-21:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Mondays, and the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month.
closed_days: Mondays, plus the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the counter is welcoming, but it is a small, busy, cash-only neighborhood shop, so a friendly greeting and patience during the long cook time go a long way.
