caveats: Cash only. Cooking each okonomiyaki takes 20-30 minutes, so expect a wait and possibly a line at this popular original shop. Reservations are only taken for groups of about four or more. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Yagenbori Hassho is the original, owner-run shop tucked into the Yagenbori district of central Hiroshima, around the corner from the Hatchobori entertainment area and within walking distance of Hondori. It has been grilling Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki for roughly four decades and is regarded as one of the city's defining okonomiyaki houses.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the soba-niku-tama (noodle, pork and egg) okonomiyaki as the signature; oysters appear on the griddle in winter.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen takes its time, cooking each okonomiyaki slowly over about 20 to 30 minutes so the cabbage steams down sweet and the layers meld, prizing careful technique and good ingredients over speed.

anecdotes.text: Each okonomiyaki is grilled patiently for about 20 to 30 minutes, a deliberately slow approach that is part of the shop's reputation.
The house favorite is the soba-niku-tama okonomiyaki, layering wheat noodles, cabbage, pork and egg.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is a wheat-containing sauce, and the noodle versions use wheat soba/udon.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on one griddle used for pork, egg and fish-based seasonings, with no separate vegan preparation.

lead: お好み焼き さらしな
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (広島風お好み焼き)
neighborhood: 新天地・お好み村 (Shintenchi / Okonomimura), Naka-ku, near Hatchobori
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザ 2F お好み村内
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-banchi address 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 via GSI; coordinates (34.391, 132.461) fall within the central Hiroshima box, landing on the Okonomimura building in the Shintenchi entertainment district, a short walk east of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: 17 seats (counter/teppan seating inside the Okonomimura food complex)
hours_raw: 11:00-21:30 (Tue-Sun); closed Mondays (open the following day if Monday is a public holiday)
closed_days: Mondays (if Monday is a public holiday, closed the following day instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional teppan counter run mostly by one cook with no English menu, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the popular nikutama-soba will carry you through; come with cash since cards are not accepted.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; there is no documented gluten-free option here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The traditional batter and broth lean on bonito (katsuo) and tororo-kombu dashi flavor, the signature dish includes pork and egg, and dishes are cooked on a shared teppan with meat and seafood. No vegan version is documented.
caveats: Owner/generation details ("2nd and 3rd generation female owners") appear in a search-result summary but could not be confirmed on a primary page fetch; treat the multi-generation framing as likely but not firmly verified. The official okonomimura.jp page refused a direct fetch (HTTP), so its content here is drawn from the search snippet and the gnavi listing.

bio.background: Sarashina has been making Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki since 1951, having started in the post-war years and grown into one of the long-running stalls that became part of Okonomimura, the multi-floor okonomiyaki complex in the Shintenchi district just east of the Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: Traditional griddle-cooked okonomiyaki layered with cabbage, pork, egg and noodles; the kitchen is known for generously folding in bonito flakes and tororo-kombu (shaved kelp) for a deep, gentle umami that defines its old-school flavor. The most-ordered plate is the classic nikutama-soba (pork, egg and noodles).
bio.philosophy: An unhurried, careful approach: the cook grills each okonomiyaki slowly and asks each guest about preferences such as whether to add red pickled ginger, tailoring the dish to the person in front of the counter.

anecdotes.text: The shop dates to 1951 and is part of Okonomimura, the okonomiyaki food theme park near Hatchobori; it has kept the same tororo-kombu-and-bonito tradition for decades.
Staff take the time to ask guests about toppings like benishoga (red pickled ginger) so each okonomiyaki matches the customer's taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared flat-top griddle where wheat batter and wheat noodles are prepared together, so gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cooking surface.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dish is finished with traditional okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are griddled alongside pork, egg and seafood-derived dashi/bonito on a shared teppan, so animal-product cross-contact is unavoidable.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (and negiyaki / teppanyaki)
neighborhood: Shintenchi / Hatchobori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザ2F お好み村内
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 returned [132.461609, 34.391186], within the Hiroshima box; corresponds to the Okonomi-mura building in central Hiroshima.
seats_text: All counter seating around the teppan (griddle); roughly 18-20 seats. Tabelog lists 20; the official-side description lists 18 counter seats.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri 11:30-21:00; Sat-Sun 11:00-21:00; closed Tuesdays. (One source lists opening from 11:00 with variable close.)
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, family-run counter griddle shop inside the tourist-familiar Okonomi-mura; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way, and staff are used to visitors.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing sauce is a wheat-based okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The house specialties center on egg (signature soft half-cooked egg negiyaki), pork, seafood and winter Hiroshima oysters, cooked on a shared griddle, with bonito/fish-based and egg-containing toppings and wheat-soy sauce. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Official website (chicyan.com) and the Okonomi-mura page could not be fetched directly (self-signed certificate / connection refused), so history, hours and seat details were corroborated via Tabelog and web search summaries of those sites. Seat count and exact daily hours vary slightly between sources.

bio.chef_name: Furuta family (founded by Shozaburo Furuta, the first head of Okonomi-mura)
bio.background: Chiichan is one of the oldest shops in Hiroshima's Okonomi-mura, the famous building stacked with okonomiyaki stalls. It was opened by Shozaburo Furuta, who became the very first 'village head' of Okonomi-mura when the collective of food stalls was organized in 1965, and it has been passed down within the family. Today it is run as a small two-person operation by the second generation alongside the founder's descendants, keeping a single griddle counter going for over half a century.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style negiyaki piled with sweet locally grown green onion (negi) and a trademark soft, runny half-cooked egg; seafood-and-oyster negiyaki in winter is a draw, alongside classic Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki.
bio.philosophy: Cooking centered on local Hiroshima ingredients while keeping salt and oil to a minimum, prizing the soft-runny egg and the fragrant crispness of the griddled noodles.

anecdotes.text: Chiichan's founder, Shozaburo Furuta, was the first 'village head' (shochou) of Okonomi-mura when the stall collective was formed in 1965, and the shop has operated continuously for over 50 years, carried on by the next generation.
The shop is known for negiyaki using sweet Hiroshima-grown green onion and a signature runny half-cooked egg, with winter Hiroshima oysters among its seasonal highlights.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki and negiyaki are cooked on a single shared teppan griddle and built from wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with a wheat-based okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle is used for pork, egg, seafood and oysters; specialties prominently feature egg, so vegan separation is not feasible.

positives.text: Small two-person family operation cooking at the counter, so you can watch your dish made and speak directly with the cook about ingredients.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki
neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (Okonomimura), central Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-23 お好み共和国ひろしま村 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from street address 新天地5-23; lands in central Naka-ku within the Hiroshima box. The shop is on the 2nd floor of the Okonomimura building.
seats_text: 26 seats (counter and tables around the teppan). Note: directory listings vary (one source lists 15); the verified count is 26.
hours_raw: 11:00-23:00; closed Tuesdays (one directory listing notes Wednesday instead)
closed_days: Tuesday (some listings indicate Wednesday; confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run teppan stall inside the bustling Okonomimura food building; staff are used to tourists but English is limited, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce, plus typically yakisoba/udon noodles. This is not a gluten-free-friendly dish or venue and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation.
vegan_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki here uses egg, pork belly, and a bonito/dashi-influenced wheat sauce, and noodles are added on the griddle shared with meat and seafood. There is no documented vegan option.

bio.background: Susumu is one of the small, individually run okonomiyaki stalls packed inside Okonomimura, the iconic multi-floor okonomiyaki building in central Hiroshima's Shintenchi district. Like its neighbors, it serves classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on a hot teppan right in front of guests seated at the counter.
bio.origin: Part of the Okonomimura complex, a Hiroshima institution that gathers dozens of independent okonomiyaki shops under one roof in the entertainment quarter near Hatchobori.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (cabbage, batter crepe, noodles, pork, egg, and okonomiyaki sauce griddled to order).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter, wheat-based sauce, and noodles; high gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cuisine.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared griddle used for pork, egg, and seafood; not suitable for strict vegans.

caveats: Susumu is confirmed as a currently-operating okonomiyaki stall inside Okonomimura. Source details conflict on a few logistics: seats (Tabelog 26 vs a directory listing 15), closed day (Tuesday vs Wednesday), and phone number, so visitors should confirm hours/closed day before going. No documented owner story or culinary philosophy was found, so the bio is low-confidence. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diets.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki
neighborhood: Shintenchi / Okonomimura (Naka-ku, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザ 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Hatchobori / Okonomimura.
seats_text: 15 counter seats (one source lists 16); a small, single-counter shop inside the Okonomimura building.
hours_raw: 11:00–22:30 (Tabelog). Some listings show 11:00–23:00.
closed_days: Tuesdays, plus the third Monday of the month (per Tabelog). Note: one other listing states Wednesdays instead — see caveats.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only counter shop inside the bustling Okonomimura okonomiyaki building; a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way, and you can watch your okonomiyaki cooked on the griddle right in front of you.
gf_detail: This is a traditional Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop. The okonomiyaki crepe base, the savory sauce, and the noodles all contain wheat, and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
vegan_detail: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built around egg, pork, and a layer of fried noodles, finished with a savory sauce and bonito; the menu centers on these. There is no documented vegan option, so this is not a suitable destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
caveats: Sources disagree on the closed day: Tabelog lists Tuesdays (plus third Mondays) while another listing lists Wednesdays. Closing times also vary by source (22:30 vs 23:00). Confirm the current schedule by phone (082-248-2758) before visiting. The official Okonomimura page for this shop was unreachable at verification time.

bio.background: A family-run okonomiyaki counter tucked on the second floor of Okonomimura, the famous Hiroshima building that gathers dozens of okonomiyaki stalls under one roof. The shop traces its roots back to the postwar street-stall (yatai) era and has been cooking okonomiyaki here for decades, now in the hands of a second-generation owner.
bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, layered on a thin crepe with cabbage and freshly boiled noodles cooked daily, griddled until the base is crisp and fragrant.
bio.philosophy: A homey, no-frills counter where the okonomiyaki is kept light and easy to finish — a style locals reach for as a satisfying close to an evening out.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for a lighter, crispier okonomiyaki that diners describe as easy to finish even after drinking, making it a popular end-of-night spot.
It has been serving okonomiyaki inside Okonomimura for over sixty years, with a history reaching back to the era of postwar food stalls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat-based noodles; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based, and standard soy sauce contains wheat; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single griddle is used for pork, egg, and bonito-finished dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

lead: 廣島辛つけ麺 くじら屋
name_ja: 廣島辛つけ麺 くじら屋
cuisine: 広島辛つけ麺・油そば（ラーメン）
neighborhood: 広島市中区薬研堀（流川・八丁堀の南、中央広島）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀5-18 パールビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 広島県広島市中区薬研堀5番18号; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 14 seats (6 counter, 8 table).
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A small late-night local counter where ordering is in Japanese and the vibe is a post-drinking noodle stop; a foreign student comfortable pointing at the menu or with basic Japanese will do fine.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are tsukemen and abura soba built on wheat noodles, and the dipping broth uses standard (wheat-based) soy-style seasoning. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature spicy dipping broth is built on seafood (oyster and scallop adductor), and toppings include squid tempura and other animal-based items. No vegan option is documented.
caveats: Hours run past midnight (closes 03:00); kitchen last order around 02:30. Closed Sundays. Address building/unit is variously listed as パールビル1F / 1A.

bio.background: A small late-night noodle counter in Hiroshima's Yagenbori district that opened in April 2022, sitting just south of the Nagarekawa and Hatchobori nightlife and drawing diners looking for a satisfying bowl to cap off an evening out.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style spicy tsukemen (dipping noodles) with a seafood-forward broth made using oyster and scallop, served with curly dry noodles and distinctive toppings like lemon-scented squid tempura and mushroom duxelles; oil-based abura soba is also on the menu.
bio.philosophy: Standing out from the crowd: the shop leans into unusual, original ingredients rather than copying the standard local tsukemen template.

anecdotes.text: A local food writer remarked he had never seen Hiroshima tsukemen differentiated this far, calling out ingredients not found at other shops.
The shop is open only at night and is widely treated as a go-to spot for a finishing bowl after drinking in the nearby entertainment district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the base of both the tsukemen and abura soba, so the kitchen handles wheat throughout; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth uses a soy-style seasoning typical of Hiroshima tsukemen, which is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth itself is seafood-based (oyster and scallop), so it is not separable from animal ingredients.

cuisine: 中華そば・ラーメン（博多長浜系豚骨ラーメン）
neighborhood: 広島市中区薬研堀（流川エリア、中央広島）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀10-10
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 広島市中区薬研堀10-10; result 34.390385, 132.465225 falls within the central Hiroshima box, in the Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district near Ginzancho.
seats_text: Counter-only, roughly 12-13 seats (sources vary: Tabelog lists 12 counter seats, Gnavi lists 13).
hours_raw: 19:00–07:00 daily (Tabelog). Other listings give 20:00–翌5:00. Late-night ramen shop; hours can vary, confirm before visiting.
closed_days: No regular closing day (open daily).
cultural_comfort_note: A small late-night counter ramen shop in a nightlife district where ordering is in Japanese and there is no English menu, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help; pointing at the menu works fine for a simple bowl.
gf_detail: This is a Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen shop. The noodles are wheat-based and the broth and tare contain wheat-derived soy sauce, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are documented and there is no indication of allergen accommodation.
vegan_detail: The signature ramen is built on a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with chashu pork, egg, and back-fat. The broth itself is animal-based, so the menu is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians and no plant-based bowl is documented.

bio.background: A tiny late-night ramen counter tucked into Hiroshima's Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife quarter in Naka Ward, Shoryuken caters to the after-drinking crowd, staying open through the small hours when most of the city has closed. It operates as a sister shop of the Hakata ramen house Nagahama No. 1 (長浜ナンバーワン).
bio.specialty: Hakata Nagahama-style tonkotsu ramen: a rich pork-bone broth served with thin, firm noodles you can order to your preferred firmness, soft chashu, plenty of green onion, and a half boiled egg, finished with a touch of back-fat. Free-to-add toppings like takana (pickled mustard greens) and red pickled ginger are on the counter, and tsukemen and spicy versions are also offered.

anecdotes.text: Shoryuken is run as a sister shop of the Hakata ramen house Nagahama No. 1, reflected in its Nagahama-style thin-noodle tonkotsu bowls.
It is known locally as a go-to spot for a satisfying late-night bowl after a night out in the Nagarekawa district.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a tonkotsu ramen shop, the broth tare uses standard wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and the noodles are wheat; gluten is unavoidable in the core menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu); there is no animal-free broth, so even noodles-only orders are cooked in and around animal-based products.

caveats: Hours differ across sources (Tabelog 19:00–07:00 vs. Gnavi/HotPepper 20:00–05:00); treat as a late-night shop and confirm before a late visit. Seat count varies (12–13). It is a sister shop of 長浜ナンバーワン serving Hakata Nagahama tonkotsu ramen. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

cuisine: Garden cafe in a traditional Miyajima townhouse
neighborhood: Miyajima Machiya-dori (a quiet lane just off the Omotesando shopping arcade, near Itsukushima Shrine)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町527-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chome-level point for 宮島町527; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; described as a small, quiet cafe set in a traditional townhouse with a garden, so capacity is limited.
hours_raw: Sources conflict: an earlier listing shows Lunch 11:00-14:00 / Cafe 14:00-17:00 / Dinner 17:00-20:30 with Tuesday closed; a more recent listing shows Mon/Fri/Sun 17:00-20:30, Sat 11:00-20:30, and Tue/Wed/Thu closed. Confirm directly before visiting.
closed_days: Tuesday (older listing); more recent listing also closed Wednesday and Thursday — verify before visiting
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally run Japanese cafe off the tourist path; some basic Japanese (or a translation app) will help, though the relaxed garden setting is welcoming to visitors.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on home-style set meals, sweets/coffee, and a wagyu beef steak set; sauces and seasonings typically contain wheat-based soy sauce, and no dedicated gluten-free preparation is advertised. A celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume safe options.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian establishment. The set lunch includes home-style side dishes (obanzai) and miso soup, and a signature dish is Hiroshima wagyu beef; miso soup and dashi-based dishes commonly contain fish stock. A few vegetable sides may be available, but there is no documented vegan menu, so ask in advance.
caveats: Operating hours and closed days appear to have changed over time and differ between sources; this is a small owner-run cafe, so hours can be irregular. Confirm by phone (0829-44-2777) before visiting. Opened April 2020.
sources: https://hg-nishichugoku.co.jp/グランドオープン　庭カフェ御山/

bio.background: Niwa Cafe Miyama opened in April 2020 on Machiya-dori, a quiet lane that runs parallel to Miyajima's busy Omotesando shopping arcade, away from the crowds that fill the island's main street. It occupies a traditional townhouse, and visitors reach it down a narrow alley and through a garden courtyard before stepping inside.
bio.specialty: Home-style daily set lunch with seasonal vegetable sides, miso soup and pickles, plus a Hiroshima wagyu beef steak set; coffee and sweets in the afternoon.
bio.philosophy: The cafe is built around its rare garden setting, offering a calm, unhurried place to eat at your own pace rather than the rushed, crowded experience common at tourist spots on the island.

anecdotes.text: Having a cafe with its own garden is unusual on Miyajima, and the spot is known as a hidden retreat reached through a quiet courtyard off the main lane, with views of the garden from inside.
It was announced as a newly opened dining spot on Miyajima's machiya street in April 2020.
anecdotes.source: https://hg-nishichugoku.co.jp/グランドオープン　庭カフェ御山/

sources: https://hg-nishichugoku.co.jp/グランドオープン　庭カフェ御山/

cuisine: 汁なし担担麺（広島名物の汁なし担々麺）・台湾まぜそば・油そば
neighborhood: 中区三川町（本通・八丁堀エリア、広島PARCO裏手）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区三川町3-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区三川町3-1 returned [132.46196, 34.390991], within the Hiroshima box. Address is Mikawachō, in the central Hondori/Hatchobori shopping district behind Hiroshima PARCO, roughly a 5-minute walk from Hatchobori and within walking distance of the A-Bomb Dome area.
seats_text: Counter seating is the main layout with an open kitchen; a private room seating up to 4 is also available. Exact seat count not published, but it is a small counter-focused shop welcoming to solo diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00（なくなり次第終了）。一部資料では11:00〜20:30。定休日は水曜（公式・食べログ）／木曜（記事により記載差あり）。
closed_days: 水曜定休（資料により木曜とする記載もあり）。麺がなくなり次第終了。
cultural_comfort_note: A small, stylish counter noodle shop with a PayPay-enabled ticket machine that is comfortable for solo diners and described as welcoming to international visitors, though ordering is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat-noodle ramen (汁なし担々麺, 台湾まぜそば, 油そば) and the sauce/tare is soy-sauce based, so wheat is present in both the noodles and the seasoning. No gluten-free menu, noodle, or preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Documented ingredients include pork mince (豚ミンチ) in most dan dan noodles, raw egg (生卵) on the Taiwan mazesoba, a chicken-bone broth, and bonito/tuna katsuobushi (マグロ削り節) used in the broth and tare. Peanuts appear in one oil-noodle dish. No vegan options are documented.

bio.origin: Higashi-Hiroshima (Saijō); opened this central Hiroshima branch behind Hiroshima PARCO
bio.background: This is the second shop ('Bettei', meaning annex) of Menya Sassou, which owner Masanobu Miyafuji opened in Saijō, Higashi-Hiroshima in 2016 and built into a local favorite for brothless dan dan noodles. He opened this central Hiroshima location on September 2, 2022, in a monotone, café-like modern space with a counter and open kitchen, deliberately designed to be easy to enter for women dining alone and welcoming to international visitors.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style brothless dan dan noodles (汁なし担々麺), alongside Taiwan mazesoba and abura soba
bio.philosophy: Miyafuji emphasizes balance over heat — prioritizing aroma rather than spiciness. He makes his own chili oil tuned for fragrance, lets diners adjust heat themselves with a five-spice chili oil, fruit vinegar, and sansho pepper on the table, and recommends level 1 heat for newcomers. The tare is a soy-sauce base with sesame paste (芝麻醬) and vinegar; the pork mince is salt-stir-fried then simmered in pure-rice sake to render out oil and water; the chicken broth is built up with plenty of aromatic vegetables and sweetened with tuna katsuobushi; the noodles come from a maker in Higashi-Hiroshima.

anecdotes.text: After opening during the COVID period the central Hiroshima shop was initially quiet, but word of mouth grew from about three months in and it now has a loyal regular following.
Miyafuji describes his approach as balance-focused, prioritizing fragrance over heat, with a house-made chili oil and a tare built on soy sauce, sesame paste and vinegar.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All main dishes are wheat noodles prepared in a shared noodle kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tare (sauce) is described as soy-sauce based with sesame paste and vinegar; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes use pork mince, raw egg, chicken broth and tuna/bonito katsuobushi; animal products are central to the menu with no vegan items documented.
The chicken-bone broth is sweetened with tuna katsuobushi and the pork mince is simmered in sake, so fish and meat are integral even to the base components.

positives.text: The official menu lists specific allergen notes per dish (pork mince in most dan dan noodles, raw egg in Taiwan mazesoba, peanuts in the junk oil noodles), which helps diners identify what to avoid.

caveats: Source discrepancies: the closed day is listed as Wednesday on the official menu site and Tabelog but as Thursday in some write-ups; menu prices also differ between sources (汁なし担々麺 listed at both ¥680 and ¥850), suggesting prices have risen over time. The registered address is 三川町 (Mikawachō), in the central Hondori/Hatchobori shopping district behind Hiroshima PARCO, rather than literally on 八丁堀. Confirm hours and the day off before visiting.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (cold dipping noodles) and soup-less dandan noodles
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀（八丁堀・流川の繁華街エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀9-10 横田ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区薬研堀9-10 returned [132.465454, 34.389671], within the central Hiroshima box. Shop relocated here from its former 胡町/銀山町 address on 2026-03-01.
seats_text: L-shaped counter seating only; small (former location had 19 seats). Exact seat count at the new location not published.
hours_raw: New 薬研堀 location (reopened 2026-03-01) does not publish exact hours on its listing; mobile-order system. Prior operating pattern: Mon–Sat 18:00–03:00 (some listings 17:30–03:30), closed Sundays and holidays (also Obon and New Year).
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (also closed during Obon and New Year). Confirm before visiting as new-location hours are not officially published.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny late-night counter ramen shop with a mobile-order system and a Japanese-only menu, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps; the spice level is fully customizable, so it is easy to order something mild.
gf_detail: The core dishes are tsukemen and dandan noodles made with wheat flour noodles, plus char siu pork and a chili-based dipping sauce that typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergen accommodation.
vegan_detail: Dishes are built around wheat noodles topped with char siu pork, and the dipping/seasoning sauce is meat- and likely fish/soy-based. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: The original 胡町/銀山町 Tabelog page is marked 移転 (relocated); the shop reopened at 中区薬研堀9-10 横田ビル 1F on 2026-03-01. Exact business hours and seat count for the new location are not officially published — the hours shown follow the long-standing prior pattern and should be confirmed by phone (090-3263-8165) before visiting. Cash only.

bio.background: A small, independent late-night noodle shop in central Hiroshima's nightlife district, specializing in Hiroshima-style tsukemen (cold dipping noodles) and chili-driven dandan noodles. After years near Kochi-cho/Ginzan-cho, the shop relocated a short distance to a counter-only space in Yagenbori, reopening in March 2026.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: firm, springy wheat noodles served cold with cucumber, green onion, boiled cabbage and soft char siu, dipped into a chili-based sauce with spice levels customizable up to roughly 10-20x heat at no extra charge.
bio.philosophy: An unfussy, noodle-first approach: well-textured noodles and a clean dipping sauce that lets the chili and the noodles speak rather than heavy seasoning.

anecdotes.text: A regular described the dipping sauce as pleasantly simple and not over-seasoned, with heat that starts mild and builds gradually as you eat; staff steady customers by suggesting a starting spice level (around 3x for chili fans).
The noodle prep is something of a spectacle: after boiling, the cook drains and flips the noodles in a large colander dozens of times to firm up their texture before plating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are boiled in shared water and the menu is entirely noodle-based, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chili dipping/seasoning sauce for tsukemen and dandan noodles typically relies on wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are topped with char siu pork and the sauces are meat/fish-based, so all menu items involve animal products.

cuisine: 汁なし担々麺・弁当・居酒屋 (soupless dan dan noodles by day; bento / izakaya in the evening)
neighborhood: 中区大手町（中電病院裏、紙屋町・平和公園近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町3-5-21
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Kamiyacho and the Peace Park.
seats_text: Small shop with counter seating and a tatami (zashiki) area; exact seat count not published, but it is a tiny owner-run space.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–13:30, 18:00–22:00; closed Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. (Lunch noodle service runs only the two midday hours.)
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日 (Saturday, Sunday, holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, cash-only neighborhood spot run by the owner with ordering via ticket machine and little to no English, so basic Japanese and a relaxed attitude will help a foreign student feel at home.
gf_detail: The signature dish is a wheat-noodle soupless dan dan men, and the seasoning is built on a miso-and-soy-sauce base (soy sauce normally contains wheat). There is no indication of gluten-free menu items or preparation, so this is not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The dan dan noodles are topped with a miso-seasoned minced-meat base, and the evening menu is bento/izakaya fare; the broth/seasoning uses soy sauce and animal ingredients. No vegan dishes are documented.
caveats: Lunch noodle service is famously short — only 11:30 to 13:30 — and the place draws a steady local crowd, so arrive early. Cash only via a ticket machine. The shop is by night an izakaya, so the menu differs sharply between lunch and dinner.

bio.background: Delica Celery started life as a bento and prepared-foods shop in Nakamachi, where the owner, Yamamoto Hiroyuki, set out roughly 50 large trays of dishes every day under a punishing by-the-portion sales grind. The relentless workload eventually wore down his health, prompting a move to the present Otemachi location behind Chuden Hospital and a complete reinvention of the business about a dozen years ago.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (originally in the Nakamachi district before relocating to Otemachi)
bio.specialty: Soupless dan dan noodles (汁なし担々麺) at lunch, with the shop turning into an izakaya in the evening
bio.philosophy: After burning out on the old high-volume deli, Yamamoto rebuilt the shop around a focused, sustainable concept: a short, two-hour lunch service centered on one dish done well. His seasoning is deliberately bold so it doubles as a sauce for rice.

anecdotes.text: The miso seasoning is made strong on purpose so it pairs with rice — in the owner's words, 'みその強い味付けにしているのでご飯との相性がいいんです.' The base layers slow-simmered root vegetables, ginger, garlic and star anise into a soy-sauce broth, then piles on miso minced meat, sesame, crunchy Korean chili and a mountain of green onion, leaving plenty of sauce to finish over self-serve rice (50 yen a bowl, all-you-can-refill).

The rice habit borders on legend: on one midweek lunch the shop blew through 4.5 measures of cooked rice in two hours and had to cook two more, thanks to neighborhood office workers ordering refill after refill to soak up the leftover sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dish is wheat noodles seasoned with a miso and soy-sauce base, so gluten is integral and there is no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth/seasoning is built on soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) layered with miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature noodles are topped with miso-seasoned minced meat; no vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: もみじ饅頭・和菓子（昔ながらの製法、イートインあり）
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街
gf_detail: Momiji manju are sponge cakes made from wheat flour batter, so the core product is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are indicated. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: Momiji manju batter is a castella-style sponge typically made with eggs and dairy, and flavors include milk chocolate and others with dairy. The seasonal oyster gratin bun (kaki gra-man) contains shellfish and dairy. No vegan offerings are documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町536-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode landed on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box, as expected for a 宮島町 address.
seats_text: Small in-shop eat-in space with seating for enjoying freshly baked manju, shaved ice, soft serve, and coffee; exact seat count not published.
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures; no fixed closing day）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed walk-up confectionery counter on the main Miyajima arcade; you can point at the maple-leaf cakes and pay easily, though staff likely speak little English so a few words of Japanese or pointing helps.
caveats: Closes on an irregular schedule (no fixed day off), so it may be shut without notice; reservations are not taken. Core products contain wheat, eggs and dairy, and the seasonal oyster bun contains shellfish.

bio.background: A long-established maple-leaf bun (momiji manju) shop on Miyajima founded in 1908 (Meiji 41) and now run by the third generation of the same family. It sits in the middle of the Omotesando shopping arcade leading toward Itsukushima Shrine, in a nostalgic retro storefront.
bio.specialty: Traditional koshi-an (smooth red bean) momiji manju baked in the classic style, with varieties such as red bean, milk chocolate, blueberry, and black sesame; in autumn and winter it also sells a distinctive oyster gratin steamed bun (kaki gra-man) and offers shaved ice and soft serve in warmer months.
bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing novelty, the shop values doing the ordinary things well and keeping the old-fashioned method consistent across generations.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been baking momiji manju in the classic style since 1908 and is now in its third family generation, and invites visitors to sit in its small rest area to enjoy a freshly baked manju with tea.
A beautiful calico cat acts as the shop's signboard mascot and is a popular photo subject for visitors, often appearing on the shop's Instagram.
Its in-shop pamphlet recounts the playful origin legend of momiji manju tied to a remark attributed to Ito Hirobumi about maple-leaf-shaped hands.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's entire core product line is wheat-flour sponge cake baked on the premises, so the environment is flour-heavy with no gluten-free preparation noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Manju are baked with batter and offered alongside milk chocolate and dairy items, with the seasonal oyster gratin bun containing shellfish and cream sauce, indicating shared handling of animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Momiji manju are hand-made and baked in-shop, and can be eaten fresh in a small seating area.

lead: 和奏 ぺったらぽったら本舗
name_ja: 和奏 ぺったらぽったら本舗
cuisine: 宮島名物 ぺったらぽったら（牡蠣・穴子の焼きおにぎり風）／個人店の食べ歩きスタンド
neighborhood: 宮島・海沿い（厳島神社エリア、北之浜）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町北之浜1183-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of full street address landed on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.299492, lng 132.32164), near the seaside path leading to Itsukushima Shrine. Accepted per Miyajima 宮島町 guidance.
seats_text: 3席。基本は食べ歩き／テイクアウト中心の小さなスタンド。
hours_raw: 10:00 - 17:30（おおむね10時〜17時頃、天候等により変動）
closed_days: 不定休（天候・状況により変動）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny walk-up stall on Miyajima's seaside path; you simply point to the oyster or conger-eel version, pay cash, and eat standing, so very little Japanese is needed but no English menu or service should be expected.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The mochi/rice base is gluten-free in itself, but each piece is glazed and finished with a sweet soy sauce, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation or separate handling is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The only items are grilled mochi-rice topped with oyster (牡蠣) or filled with conger eel (穴子), both finished with a soy-based glaze. There is no plant-based option on the menu.
caveats: Hours are irregular and weather-dependent (roughly 10:00–17:30); the listed 10:00-17:30 applies loosely to every day but closures can happen on any day. Seat count is minimal (3) and this is primarily a stand-and-eat or takeaway stall. Cash only. The Instagram handle is used as the closest thing to an official page; no standalone website was confirmed.

bio.background: 和奏 ぺったらぽったら本舗 is a small independent stall on Miyajima, set along the seaside walking path that leads toward Itsukushima Shrine. Its signature is 'pettara pottara' — a Miyajima specialty of mochi-rice (a blend of glutinous and regular rice) molded into a small oval onigiri-like cake, brushed with a sweet-savory soy glaze and grilled until the outside is crisp and caramelized while the inside stays chewy ('crispy outside, chewy inside'). It comes two ways: topped with a plump local oyster, or with conger eel (anago) tucked inside.
bio.specialty: Grilled mochi-rice 'pettara pottara' with either oyster on top or conger eel inside, finished tableside with a torch and a brush of sweet soy sauce

anecdotes.text: The name 'pettara pottara' is the shop's own registered trademark and evokes the rhythmic sounds of pressing and shaping the rice; a little in-shop jingle sings the words, and customers say it lodges in your head long after you leave.

Each piece is finished to order with an extra brush of soy and a quick torching, sending up a fragrant aroma of caramelized soy that visitors say you can smell from along the path; one customer warns the glaze drips easily, so mind your clothes when you tilt the wrapper.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Each piece is glazed and finished with a sweet soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so these items are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The only menu items contain oyster or conger eel plus a soy-based glaze, so there is no vegan-safe item to protect from cross-contact.

cuisine: ヴィーガンインドカレー・カフェ（豆のスパイスカレー、ファラフェル、ヴィーガンスイーツ）
vegan_detail: 100% plant-based cafe. The entire menu is vegan: a rotating daily curry built around legumes (soybeans, azuki, kidney beans, lentils, chickpeas) with an 11-spice blend, served with organic rice and vegetable sides; plus falafel, vegetable momos, tempeh fries, and a soy-meat "luorou" rice. All desserts (chocolate cake, homemade soy "cheese" cake, pound cakes) use no animal products. Drinks include organic coffee and chai.
gf_detail: Listed as offering a gluten-free menu, and the desserts (chocolate cake, soy cheesecake, pound cakes) are described by the owner as gluten-free as well as animal-free. However, savory items such as the falafel, momos, and fried tempeh may involve wheat or shared frying, and no dedicated-fryer or strict cross-contamination protocol is documented. Gluten-sensitive diners should confirm specifics directly (via the cafe's LINE account) before ordering.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市大野1886-3
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県廿日市市大野1886-3, on the mainland near the Miyajima-guchi ferry terminal (the "対岸"/opposite shore from Miyajima island). Within the Hiroshima bounding box. It is not on Miyajima island itself nor in central Hiroshima, but in the Miyajima-guchi vicinity (about 5 min by taxi / ~28 min walk from Miyajima-guchi Station).
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00（早じまいの場合あり）。定休日不定休。
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures; may also close earlier than 17:00）
neighborhood: 宮島口（廿日市市大野、宮島フェリー乗り場側の対岸）
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff are available and the casual, pet-friendly atmosphere makes this an easy, welcoming stop for a foreign student.
caveats: Opening hours are irregular and the cafe may close earlier than the listed 17:00, so check the LINE account or Instagram before visiting. There is no phone line; contact is via the LINE official account (@236pciwg). The location is on the mainland near the Miyajima-guchi ferry terminal, not on Miyajima island itself.

bio.chef_name: Maiko (グル・マイコ / Guru Maiko)
bio.origin: Japan (owner); her husband and co-founder is from India
bio.background: The owner, Maiko, discovered plant-based eating over more than a decade of yoga-centered travels to India, visiting the country four times. There she found that food could be deeply flavorful even without meat, an experience that reshaped how she wanted to cook and eat. She went on to marry her Indian husband, and together the couple opened this relaxed neighborhood cafe in spring 2023 in the Ono district of Hatsukaichi, near the Miyajima-guchi ferry crossing.
bio.specialty: A rotating daily legume curry: each day features a different bean (soybean, azuki, kidney bean, lentil, chickpea) paired with a custom blend of eleven spices chosen to suit that day's bean, served with organic rice, Indian pickles, sautéed vegetables, fried tempeh, and a salad. Also known for falafel, vegetable momos, tempeh fries, and entirely plant-based, gluten-free cakes, plus chai and Indian sweets (laddu).
bio.philosophy: An entirely plant-based kitchen rooted in authentic Indian home cooking, built on the conviction that food can be rich and satisfying without any animal products. The cafe is casual and pet-friendly, and the couple also takes bento and catering orders.

anecdotes.text: Maiko's path to opening the cafe began with yoga trips to India spanning more than ten years; tasting how flavorful vegetarian Indian cooking could be inspired her to build a fully plant-based menu of her own.
She and her Indian husband run the cafe together, and his roots inform the from-scratch spice blending behind the curries.

staff_allergy_handling.text: English-speaking staff are available, which makes it easier for travelers to ask questions about ingredients and dietary needs.

positives.text: The kitchen is 100% plant-based, so there is no meat, fish, dairy, or egg used anywhere on the premises, removing the main cross-contact risks for vegan diners.
All desserts are made without animal products and are described as gluten-free.
A gluten-free menu is available.

lead: gelateria BACCANO（ジェラテリア バッカーノ）
name_ja: ジェラテリア バッカーノ
cuisine: 無添加・地産地消の自家製ジェラート専門店（広島レモン等）
gf_detail: A dedicated gelato shop. The gelato itself (a frozen dairy custard) does not inherently contain wheat, and many fruit and milk-based flavors are naturally gluten-free, but this is not a gluten-free establishment and no gluten-free protocols are documented. Served in a cup avoids the wheat cone. Flavors such as genmaicha (brown rice) are fine, but verify nothing contains barley/wheat-derived additives, and note possible cross-contact from shared scoops/equipment. Ask staff directly about each flavor.
vegan_detail: The core product is dairy gelato made with local Chichiyasu 'Hiroshima delicious milk', so standard flavors are not vegan. Fruit-based sorbet-style options (e.g. strawberry, pineapple, and the signature Hiroshima/Setoda lemon) appear seasonally and are typically water-and-fruit based, which can be dairy-free — but the shop does not explicitly advertise vegan items, and recipes/equipment may share dairy. A vegan visitor should confirm with staff which sorbets are made without milk.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町幸町西浜435-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address landed on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街と町家通りの間（厳島神社方面）
seats_text: 1F counter 4 seats; 2F shoes-off tatami (Japanese-modern) room 18 seats; total 22. 2F use requires one order per person (preschoolers excluded).
closed_days: 不定休（severe weather may force temporary closure; check social media）
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly family-run gelato counter where pointing at the colorful case and a few words of Japanese go a long way; the upstairs tatami room is shoes-off and asks each person to order something.
caveats: Hours vary by source (official site lists 10:00-18:00; visitor guides list 11:00-17:00 with 2F closing 16:30) and closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. Vegan/sorbet availability is seasonal and not officially documented as dairy-free — confirm in person.

bio.background: A mother-and-son shop that opened in August 2014 on Miyajima, tucked between the Omotesando shopping arcade and the quieter Machiya-dori lane on the way to Itsukushima Shrine. Etsuko Doi had long dreamed of running her own business; when her son, who had worked in Italian dining, proposed opening a gelateria, they made the dream real together.
bio.specialty: 無添加の自家製ジェラート。看板はストレート果汁を使った広島・瀬戸田レモン。黒ごま・ピスタチオ・抹茶・ほうじ茶・玄米茶なども人気。
bio.philosophy: All gelato is made with no artificial coloring, no artificial flavoring, and no preservatives, so every vivid color in the case comes from the ingredient itself (the pink apple gelato, for instance, gets its hue from red apples cooked with their skins). The shop champions local sourcing, building the base from Chichiyasu's 'Hiroshima delicious milk' and featuring seasonal produce such as strawberries from contracted Miyajima farms.

anecdotes.text: The name 'BACCANO' means 'revelry' or 'a lively din' in Italian, chosen to express the family's wish that the shop always be bustling with happy customers.
The pink color of the apple gelato is entirely natural, achieved by cooking red apples together with their skins rather than using any coloring.
Around 18 flavors are available daily, with seasonal fruit such as strawberry and pineapple rotating in during the early-summer rainy season.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free protocol is documented; shared scoops and a common gelato case mean cross-contact between flavors and with any wheat cones is possible. Request a cup and ask staff per flavor.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The gelato base is dairy (Chichiyasu Hiroshima milk); fruit sorbet-style flavors may be dairy-free but are produced in the same kitchen, so cross-contact with milk is possible. Confirm specific flavors with staff.

positives.text: Gelato is made in-house with no artificial coloring, flavoring, or preservatives, and the milk base and many ingredients are locally sourced, which gives clear visibility into what is in each flavor.

lead: 広島大衆居酒屋 十升 流川通り店
name_ja: 広島大衆居酒屋 十升 流川通り店
cuisine: 大衆居酒屋・広島名物（自家製がんす、せんじがら、瀬戸内の牡蠣・穴子、地酒、高宮鶏）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on breaded-and-fried items (gansu is white fish paste breaded and deep-fried; lever katsu is breaded fried liver) and izakaya dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, ponzu, and wasabi-soy. Fried foods share fryers with battered items. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diner.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and meat-forward izakaya. Signature dishes are Hiroshima Bay oysters, conger eel (anago) sashimi and grilled, sashimi platters, senjigara (fried pork offal), lever katsu (liver), and Takamiya brand chicken. Dishes rely on fish, shellfish, meat, and dashi/soy seasonings. No vegan menu or documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町8-13 第3ハワイヤビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町8番13号 returned [132.463562, 34.389133], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife district of Naka-ku, walking distance from the Ebisucho tram stop and toward the A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 中区流川町（流川・薬研堀）
seats_text: 45 seats (Tabelog); the official site lists 44. A medium-sized izakaya, not a tiny counter spot.
hours_raw: 月・火・木〜日・祝：11:00〜翌09:00（ほぼ24時間営業）／水曜定休
closed_days: 水曜日 (Wednesday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a lively local izakaya in the Nagarekawa nightlife area where Japanese is the working language and no English menu is documented, so a foreign student should be comfortable ordering in basic Japanese and bring cash.
caveats: Cash only and no parking. Open through the night (closes around 9:00 the next morning) except Wednesdays. Hours and seat count differ slightly between sources (Tabelog 45 seats vs official site 44). Heavily fried and seafood/meat-based menu; not suitable for strict vegan or gluten-free diners.

bio.origin: Hiroshima, in the lively Nagarekawa drinking quarter of Naka-ku
bio.background: A casual, around-the-clock neighborhood izakaya in central Hiroshima built around fresh Seto Inland Sea seafood and Hiroshima regional dishes, with locally sourced ingredients including Takamiya brand chicken.
bio.philosophy: The house motto is to be a warm, approachable shop 'loved by the local oyaji (regulars),' the kind of place friends say 'let's meet at Jissho again tonight.' The kitchen emphasizes heartfelt, homemade cooking and seasonal local ingredients at friendly prices.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima Bay oysters (raw, fried, butter-sauteed, sake-steamed, tempura), conger eel/anago prepared as seared sashimi with wasabi-soy or thin-sliced with ponzu, homemade gansu (fish-paste fritter), and homemade senjigara (fried pork offal).

anecdotes.text: The signature anago (conger eel) is offered as seared sashimi dressed with wasabi-soy, or thin-sliced with ponzu, prized for its fatty sweetness and texture.

Gansu, a Kure-area specialty, is made here from white fish paste mixed with vegetables and seasoning, breaded and deep-fried to order for a crisp outside and soft center; senjigara (fried dried pork offal) is also made in-house.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu features multiple breaded deep-fried items (homemade gansu fish-paste fritters, lever katsu breaded liver, fried oysters, anago tempura) that share fryer oil, presenting wheat cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and soy-based dressings (e.g., anago sashimi with wasabi-soy or ponzu), which are wheat-containing; no gluten-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A seafood- and meat-centric izakaya (oysters, conger eel, pork offal, liver, chicken, sashimi) using fish-based dashi and seasonings; no documented vegan dishes or separate preparation.

cuisine: 和食居酒屋 — Setouchi-style Japanese izakaya: fresh Inland Sea seafood (sashimi platters), Etajima winter oysters, local Takamiya chicken cooked low-temperature, with around 40 sake varieties
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a Japanese izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, ponzu, tempura/fried items and marinades. Sashimi and grilled items could potentially be adapted, but there is no documented GF handling, so a celiac diner would need to communicate carefully and confirm each dish; cross-contamination is likely given the menu.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu is built around fish, oysters, anago, chicken, horse-meat sashimi and grilled beef, and standard Japanese seasonings use bonito dashi and wheat-soy-sauce. No documented vegan dishes; a vegan would find very little here beyond possibly a plain vegetable side, which would still need to be confirmed dashi-free.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀3-8 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区薬研堀3-8 via GSI; result lat 34.390827, lng 132.464767 falls within the Hiroshima box and matches the Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district in central Naka-ku.
neighborhood: 薬研堀・流川 (Yagenbori / Nagarekawa), Naka-ku — central Hiroshima nightlife district, about a 7-minute walk from Kanayamacho tram stop and short walk from Ebisucho
seats_text: 64 seats total: counter, table, and dug-in horigotatsu private rooms accommodating parties of 2 to 50
hours_raw: 月～土・祝・祝前日 17:00～翌0:00（料理L.O. 23:00、ドリンクL.O. 23:00）／日曜定休
closed_days: Sundays (and per official site, national holidays may vary; open on days before holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: This is an evening-only adult izakaya in Hiroshima's nightlife quarter with private tatami-style rooms; basic Japanese helps, reservations are smart for the private rooms, and note it is a smoking venue with no entry for under-18s.
caveats: Dinner only (opens 17:00, closed Sundays). Smoking is permitted indoors and guests under 18 are not admitted. Budget runs roughly 4,000-9,000 yen with drinks. Not suitable as a vegan or strict gluten-free destination.

bio.background: 肆一饗 (Hoshiimama Ikkyo) is a Japanese izakaya tucked into the Nagarekawa-Yagenbori nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, opened in December 2019. It is designed as an 'adult hideaway' — a calm, traditionally styled space of dug-in horigotatsu private rooms that can seat anywhere from a couple to a party of fifty.
bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Naka-ku), Setouchi Inland Sea region
bio.specialty: Seasonal Inland Sea seafood and local Hiroshima ingredients: sashimi platters of Setouchi fish, oyster dishes made with Etajima winter-bloom oysters, and the local Takamiya-area chicken served as a low-temperature steamed chicken, alongside roughly 40 kinds of sake.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen works directly with producers to source seasonal regional ingredients, pairing local Hiroshima sake and premium nationwide labels with seafood-forward Japanese cooking in an intimate private-room setting.

anecdotes.text: The house highlights Etajima winter-bloom oysters and locally raised Takamiya chicken prepared by low-temperature cooking, sourced through direct relationships with producers.
Signature plates include a tuna rare-cutlet, horse-meat sashimi, and grilled beef 'koune', a Hiroshima specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling is documented; the menu includes fried items (tuna rare-cutlet), and Japanese izakaya cooking relies on wheat-based soy sauce, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese izakaya, standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used across sashimi, simmered and grilled dishes; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on fish, oysters, anago, chicken, horse and beef, with bonito dashi typical of the cuisine; no vegan preparations are documented.

lead: Kiyo courage（キヨ コラージュ）
cuisine: 自然派フレンチ（地元・無農薬有機野菜中心のフランス料理）
neighborhood: 中区南千田西町（移転前は幟町）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区南千田西町9-20-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from current (post-May-2025) address 広島市中区南千田西町9-20-1 via GSI; coordinates [132.454941, 34.37302] fall within the Hiroshima box. Note: this relocated site is in southern Naka-ku, roughly 2km south of the A-Bomb Dome, not in the 幟町/八丁堀 area cited in the target.
seats_text: Counter 6 + table 12 (18 total) per the restaurant's listings from the prior 幟町 premises; a separate listing cited 22 total. Seat count at the May 2025 relocation site is not independently published, so treat as approximate.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00–15:00 (L.O. 13:30) / Dinner 18:00–23:00 (L.O. 20:00); 定休日 水曜（夏季・年末年始休業あり）
closed_days: 水曜定休（加えて夏季休業・年末年始休業）
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu and English-speaking staff have been reported, and the kitchen will prepare a vegetarian/vegan course if you tell them when you book, so a foreign student can dine comfortably as long as they reserve ahead and explain their needs.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a course-based French kitchen that bakes its own bread and relies on flour in sauces and pastry, so gluten is present throughout the menu. There is no published information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination control. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner must call ahead (082-221-2077) to ask whether a wheat-free course can be arranged; do not assume any dish is safe.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around pesticide-free organic vegetables delivered twice weekly from a long-standing grower, and listings report it offers vegetarian and partial vegan options. There is no fixed vegan menu: a meat-free or fully plant-based course is made to order, and reservations are required so the kitchen can prepare it. A classic French kitchen uses butter, cream, eggs and stock, so confirm at booking exactly how strict (vegetarian vs. vegan, dairy/egg-free) your needs are.
caveats: The restaurant relocated in May 2025 from 幟町 (八丁堀/原爆ドーム方面) to 広島市中区南千田西町9-20-1, in southern Naka-ku. The provided Tabelog URL points to the former premises and is flagged 【移転】(relocated); the official site (kiyocourage.jp) carries the current address. Operating hours and seat count shown here are carried over from the prior location's listings, as the new-site details are not separately published — verify by phone (082-221-2077) before visiting. Access: ~2 min walk from the 南千田西町 bus stop (Hiroshima Bus 21-1/21-2 from Hiroshima Station).

bio.background: Chef Sumida Kiyokazu was born in Hiroshima in 1979 and entered the kitchen at nineteen. He spent roughly a decade training between Japan and France before opening his own restaurant at twenty-eight in 2008. After more than sixteen years in the city's center, he moved the restaurant to a new home in southern Hiroshima in May 2025 to pursue his ideal more fully.
bio.specialty: Natural-style French cooking that lets local ingredients speak for themselves, anchored by pesticide-free organic vegetables and the seafood and mountain produce of the Seto Inland Sea region.
bio.philosophy: The aim is to draw out the full, honest character of regional ingredients rather than mask them with technique. Vegetables come from a grower he has worked with for over ten years, delivered twice a week, and dishes are kept deliberately simple so that the produce's natural minerality and freshness carry the plate.

anecdotes.text: A signature spinach purée is described by the chef as quite literally just spinach turned into purée, yet it lands as vividly mineral and alive thanks to the quality of the vegetable.

The vegetables are grown without pesticides using organic fertilizer by a farmer in Yamaguchi Prefecture with whom the restaurant has kept a relationship for more than ten years, with deliveries twice a week.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests are advised to inform staff of vegetarian or vegan preferences before ordering and to reserve in advance so the kitchen can accommodate; specific dairy, egg, and cross-contact details are not published and should be confirmed directly.

positives.text: Vegetarian and partial vegan options can be prepared, and an English menu plus English-speaking staff have been reported, making it possible to discuss dietary needs.
The cooking centers on pesticide-free, organically grown vegetables delivered twice weekly from a trusted long-term grower.

cuisine: 中華そば・ラーメン（広島の昔ながらの豚骨醤油ラーメン）
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀（女学院前電停すぐ）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀4-14
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市中区八丁堀4-14 to [132.463455, 34.397026], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in central Hiroshima near Jogakuin-mae tram stop, within A-Bomb Dome area.
seats_text: 35 seats total: 11 counter, 24 table
closed_days: 日曜・祝日、盆・GW・年末年始
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, no-frills neighborhood ramen counter where ordering a bowl in basic Japanese (or by pointing) is enough; no English menu is documented, so a translation app helps but the experience is friendly and approachable.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce (shoyu) broth; the soy tare is wheat-based. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a tonkotsu (pork-bone) soy base, dishes feature char siu pork, wontons, and meat, and the soy tare contains wheat. No plant-based options are documented.

bio.background: A family ramen shop with roots going back more than 60 years. The lineage began with the founder's grandfather, who started a noodle-making business after returning from Manchuria; that led the founder's father to open the original shop, named simply 上海 (Shanhai), in the Nobori-cho area of central Hiroshima. The business later moved to its current Hatchobori location and became 上海総本店. It has been run by the Fujimoto family across generations, with younger family members now working in the shop.
bio.specialty: Old-style Hiroshima chuka-soba (ramen): a rich pork-bone and soy-sauce broth with medium-thick straight noodles, served with generous toppings of bean sprouts, green onion and menma; the wonton ramen and char siu are longtime favorites.
bio.philosophy: Preserving an unbroken flavor across generations. The soy-based tare has been continuously topped up since the shop opened, and the family carried that precious sauce with them when they relocated to keep the taste alive.

anecdotes.text: Owner Toshiaki Fujimoto says the flavor hasn't changed since his father's time, and the soy tare has been continuously replenished for over 60 years since founding.
Fujimoto ran the shop alongside his twin brother Eiji as an inseparable pair for about 40 years, sharing shifts and deliveries, until Eiji's recent retirement.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the core of nearly every dish, so a shared noodle-boiling pot and wheat-heavy kitchen make this unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare that has been continuously replenished for decades; Japanese soy sauce is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) and dishes include char siu pork and wontons, so animal products are integral and unavoidable.

caveats: Cash and PayPay QR accepted; credit cards and electronic money are not. No reservations. Hours and closed periods (Obon, Golden Week, year-end/New Year) per listings; confirm before visiting holidays. Official web presence is the shop's Instagram (@shanhai957); no standalone website found.

lead: 鉄板焼･お好み焼 花子 本店
name_ja: 鉄板焼･お好み焼 花子 本店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Kokutaijimachi, Naka-ku (near Chuden-mae tram stop; Otemachi / Peace Memorial Park area)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区国泰寺町1-3-14 OS2ビル 2F～3F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区国泰寺町1-3-14 via GSI address-search; first feature coordinates [132.455597, 34.387215], within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 95 seats total across two floors (2F counter and table seating ~35; 3F sunken tatami seating ~32 plus a karaoke room ~28). Private rooms available; a relatively large okonomiyaki house rather than a tiny counter shop.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (holiday operation may vary; calling ahead is advised)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, locally popular griddle house where pointing at the okonomiyaki menu and a few words of Japanese will get you by, though staff are not specifically set up for detailed English allergy discussions.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered noodles (typically wheat ramen-style noodles), and the okonomiyaki sauce and teppanyaki seasonings generally contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The cooking is centered on pork (the signature 国泰寺焼き uses pork jowl/tontoro), egg, bonito (katsuobushi) powder, and the griddle is shared with meat and seafood. No vegan options are documented.

roles: family-run independent okonomiyaki and teppanyaki restaurant
bio.background: Hanako is a long-loved Hiroshima okonomiyaki house in the Kokutaijimachi district near the Chuden-mae tram stop, within walking distance of Otemachi and Peace Memorial Park. It grew from its main shop into several branches around Hiroshima and even a Tokyo location, but the original honten remains the heart of the operation, embodying the postwar Hiroshima tradition of neighborhood okonomiyaki shops that often took on affectionate women's names like 'Hanako.'
bio.specialty: Its signature dish is the original 国泰寺焼き (Kokutaiji-yaki): instead of the usual pork belly it uses tontoro (pork jowl), cracks a whole egg into the center, dusts the batter with bonito powder, crisps the thin noodles by drizzling oil around the edges, and finishes with ginger and garlic for a flavor described as rich yet refreshingly light.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for a savory but not heavy okonomiyaki, leaning on crisped noodles and aromatic ginger and garlic rather than weight, which has earned a loyal local following.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature 国泰寺焼き breaks from Hiroshima convention by using pork jowl and a whole egg cracked into the center, with the noodles deliberately crisped at the edges via an oil-drizzling technique and bonito powder sprinkled on the batter.
The crispy thin-noodle style of the 国泰寺焼き, fried with fragrant pork and finished with ginger and garlic, is the dish the restaurant is best known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-soy-sauce, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent to the cooking surface.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce and teppanyaki seasonings used in Hiroshima-style cooking are generally based on wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is used continuously for pork, egg, and bonito-topped okonomiyaki, so any dish would share a surface with animal products.

caveats: Hours and seat configuration are drawn from a restaurant listing and may change; holiday opening varies, so call 082-240-0875 to confirm. This is a meat- and seafood-centered okonomiyaki/teppanyaki house with no documented gluten-free or vegan accommodations.

cuisine: Cafe — specialty hand-dripped coffee, tea, and cakes on a hilltop terrace overlooking the Five-Story Pagoda and Senjokaku
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), elevated terrace beside the Five-Story Pagoda, near Itsukushima Shrine and the Omotesando shopping street
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町413
geocode_note: GSI address search resolved 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町413 to [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island and within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Roughly 50 seats: about 30 indoors and 20 on the open wooden terrace.
hours_raw: 13:00-17:00 (official site states open year-round / 無休; last order around 17:00). Hours and closed days may change seasonally — one owner interview noted Wednesday/Thursday closures.
closed_days: Officially open year-round (無休), though hours and days can vary seasonally; an older owner interview cited Wednesday/Thursday closures, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed tourist-area cafe where you order simple drinks and cakes, so basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu are enough — just be ready to climb about 70 stone steps to reach it.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on coffee, tea, lemon soda, cheesecake and lemon cake; the cakes are conventional wheat-flour bakes, so a celiac would have essentially no safe food option here. Drinks (coffee, tea, Hiroshima lemon soda) are naturally gluten-free, but cross-contact is not addressed. Ask staff directly if you have a strict gluten need.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The food is cheesecake and lemon cake, which normally contain dairy and eggs, so they are not vegan. Black coffee and tea are vegan; plant milk availability is not stated. Best to ask whether any cake or milk alternative is plant-based.

bio.background: Tenshinkaku is the hilltop coffee salon of the Itsuki Coffee (伊都岐珈琲) group, founded by Hiroshima native Sasaki Keisuke, a Hiroshima Shudo University graduate who started out working in cafes before building his own specialty-coffee company. He opened Tenshinkaku to bring a serious, authentic coffee experience to Miyajima.
bio.origin: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Hiroshima — on a historic hilltop once tied to the Itsuki Shrine and later used as the town mayor's residence.
bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin specialty coffee, hand-dripped, with beans rotated seasonally (often Guatemala and Ethiopia); also homemade cheesecake and Hiroshima lemon cake, plus a Hiroshima lemon soda.
bio.philosophy: Sasaki stresses consistency and transparency — 'providing the same quality continuously matters' — sourcing traceable specialty beans. The shop name 天心閣 comes from the idea of a view so sublime it cleanses the spirit, and he chose to share this restored hilltop with visitors rather than keep it as a private home.

anecdotes.text: The hilltop site had decayed into what was described as a jungle before Sasaki rebuilt the stone walls and retaining walls and, in a 2022 renovation, extended the cypress-wood deck terrace so guests can view the Five-Story Pagoda and Senjokaku at eye level.

Reaching the cafe means climbing roughly 70 stone steps up a narrow alley off the Omotesando street, a flight locals jokingly call the 'stairway to heaven,' rewarded by a terrace overlooking the pagoda, Senjokaku and the Seto Inland Sea.

positives.text: Drinks are simple and many are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, such as black drip coffee, tea, and the Hiroshima lemon soda.

caveats: This is a view-focused coffee cafe, not a meal destination; the only food is cheesecake and lemon cake (both contain wheat/dairy/eggs), so it suits drinks and dessert rather than a full GF or vegan meal. There is a small seat charge that is waived with any order. Hours and closed days vary seasonally — sources disagree (official site says open year-round; an older interview cited Wed/Thu closed) — so confirm before going. Reaching it requires climbing about 70 stone steps.

name_ja: VEGAN CAFE -精進-（シャンティヨガ）
cuisine: ヴィーガン精進料理・精進カレー（完全菜食）
gf_detail: No source confirms dedicated gluten-free preparation. The menu centers on 精進カレー (shojin curry) and a 精進料理 set; curry roux and any bread/bagel items typically contain wheat, so gluten-free cannot be assumed. A diner needing gluten-free should call ahead (082-247-8529) and ask directly. The "グルテンフリー対応" label in the brief is not corroborated by any fetched page.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. The cafe is run by the Shanti Yoga studio and explicitly excludes meat, fish, eggs, dairy and all animal-derived foods (「肉や魚、卵などの乳製品、動物性食品を使わない」), grounded in the yoga principle of ahimsa (non-violence). Documented dishes include a 精進料理 set with dessert and matcha (1,500 yen) and 精進カレー with side dish and drink (1,000 yen). Kitchen staff historically made items from scratch, including tofu-based mayonnaise.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区三川町2-20 mandano BLD. 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on 広島市中区三川町2-20; result 34.390442, 132.461884 falls within the central Hiroshima box (Nagarekawa/Hondori entertainment district).
neighborhood: 中区三川町（流川・本通エリア、繁華街内）
seats_text: Seat count not documented; a small studio-attached cafe on the 2nd floor of a building in the entertainment district.
hours_raw: 営業時間 12:00-17:00 / 定休日 日曜日・月曜日 / ※ご予約をおすすめします
closed_days: 日曜・月曜（Sundays and Mondays）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, welcoming yoga-studio cafe used to health-conscious and foreign visitors; a few words of Japanese and a phone call to reserve will smooth your visit since menus and signage are primarily Japanese.
caveats: Operating status is uncertain: the official Shanti Yoga site still publishes a current cafe page with the 精進 menu and 12:00-17:00 hours, but a third-party listing marks it as closed and the studio's main homepage no longer foregrounds the cafe. Strongly recommend calling 082-247-8529 to confirm it is open before visiting. Hours, seating and the gluten-free claim should also be verified by phone.

bio.background: The cafe grew out of the Shanti Yoga studio in central Hiroshima's Mikawacho, which wanted to extend its wellness practice from the mat to the table. Studio leaders said they 'wanted to propose food as well' (「食事も提案していきたい」), and the kitchen was originally run by two young pastry-school graduates who made dishes from scratch, including a tofu-based mayonnaise.
bio.specialty: Plant-based 精進 (shojin) cooking — a 精進料理 set served with dessert and matcha, and a hearty 精進カレー with a side dish and drink — built around seasonal organic vegetables, much of it from Hiroshima.
bio.philosophy: Eating is treated as an extension of yoga practice and the principle of ahimsa (non-violence): no meat, fish, eggs, dairy or other animal products, offered in a relaxed, casual setting. As one founder put it, it is 'a specialized food style, but we want to propose cuisine with creative freedom' (「特殊なフードスタイルだが、自由な発想の中で料理を提案していきたい」).

anecdotes.text: The cafe began as an offshoot of the yoga studio so members and visitors could practice mindful, plant-based eating; over time it drew health-conscious diners, foreign visitors and parents of children with food allergies.
A later refresh added prepared deli dishes and takeout, with a showcase displaying about ten dishes organized by cooking method so guests could eat casually.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is plant-based, excluding meat, fish, eggs, dairy and all animal products, so there is no animal-product cross-contact in the cafe.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe historically attracted parents of children with food allergies, suggesting staff are accustomed to allergy-aware requests, though no specific handling protocol is documented.

positives.text: Fully vegan menu of shojin cuisine and curry made from seasonal organic vegetables, prepared from scratch.

cuisine: 無添加グリークヨーグルト＆アサイーボウル専門店（スイーツ）
gf_detail: The chain advertises a "gluten-free" menu at some of its other locations, and the strained yogurt itself (milk and cultures only) is naturally gluten-free, as are acai, fresh fruit, and honey toppings. However, the house granola is a signature topping and granola commonly contains oats/wheat. No gluten-free or cross-contamination details are documented for the Hiroshima shop specifically, so a gluten-sensitive guest should ask in person and request bowls without granola.
vegan_detail: This is fundamentally a Greek yogurt shop, and the yogurt is dairy (produced at a Hokkaido facility from milk and lactic-acid cultures), so the signature yogurt plates are NOT vegan. The acai is described as fully organic with no additives or artificial sweeteners, and the chain promotes a build-your-own, high-freedom lineup, so a dairy-free acai-and-fruit bowl may be possible if ordered without yogurt and without honey. There is no documented dedicated vegan menu, plant-based milk option, or vegan certification for the Hiroshima location, so vegans must ask staff to confirm each component.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-2 本通ビル201
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区本通8-2 via GSI AddressSearch; result (lat 34.392651, lng 132.459961) falls within the Hiroshima box, in the Hondori shopping arcade in central Naka-ku near the A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 中区本通（本通商店街）
seats_text: Seat count not documented. Small specialty sweets shop on the second floor (201) of a building in the Hondori arcade; takeout-oriented. Reported wait times of roughly 30 minutes at peak shortly after opening.
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30), open daily (per Tabelog listing)
closed_days: No regular closed day listed (open 7 days/week per current listing); hours may change as this is a newly opened shop.
cultural_comfort_note: A bright, trendy build-your-own yogurt and acai bowl counter where pointing at the display and a few words of Japanese will get you served easily, though there is no documented English menu.
caveats: Newly opened (April 1, 2026), so hours, seating, and menu details are not yet fully documented and may change; phone number is not publicly listed. This is one branch of a national franchise chain, not an independent local business. The acai bowls' vegan suitability and any gluten-free options were not confirmed for this specific location and must be verified with staff.

bio.background: YOGI is billed as Japan's first specialty shop dedicated to additive-free Greek yogurt and acai bowls, and the Hiroshima branch opened on April 1, 2026, in the Hondori covered shopping arcade in central Naka-ku. The Greek yogurt is produced at a dedicated Hokkaido facility from carefully selected lactic-acid cultures and slowly drained over three days (about 72 hours) with no sugar or additives, giving it a rich, low-acidity, cream-cheese-like texture.
bio.origin: Hokkaido-made yogurt; shop located in Hiroshima's Hondori arcade
bio.specialty: Thick additive-free Greek yogurt plates and organic acai bowls, finished with fresh fruit, house-made granola, and comb honey; flavored yogurts include plain, blueberry, strawberry, Earl Grey, and cookie.
