bio.philosophy: The brand's stated mission is to deliver beauty and health to everyday life through treats that delight both heart and body, aiming to make eating that is 'healthy yet indulgent' an everyday norm.

anecdotes.text: On opening, the shop was popular enough that visitors reported waiting around 30 minutes to be served.
The yogurt is strained over roughly 72 hours without sugar or additives, producing a thick, low-acid, cream-cheese-like texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free or cross-contamination information is documented for the Hiroshima shop; the signature house granola topping may contain gluten and should be omitted by gluten-sensitive guests.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The core product is dairy Greek yogurt, so vegan guests should ask staff to build an acai-and-fruit bowl with no yogurt and no honey; no plant-milk option or dedicated vegan handling is documented.

positives.text: Yogurt and acai are advertised as additive-free, with the acai described as fully organic with no artificial sweeteners.

cuisine: カフェ・喫茶（個人経営の小規模カフェ、コーヒー・ラテアート中心）
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道〜厳島神社エリア
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町98
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県廿日市市宮島町98 on Miyajima island; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima validity box and land on the island as expected for a 宮島町 address.
seats_text: 小さなカフェでカウンター席あり。総席数は明記されていないが、小規模な個人店。
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00（月〜日）。営業時間は変更になる場合があるため事前確認推奨。
closed_days: 定休日は明記されていない（不定休の可能性あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, welcoming counter cafe where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; bring cash, as cards and electronic payments are not accepted.
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is documented for this cafe. As a small coffee-focused spot, plain espresso-based drinks (without baked goods) may suit those avoiding gluten, but there is no stated GF menu or cross-contamination handling, so ask directly.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is documented. The cafe centers on coffee and latte art, which typically uses dairy milk; whether plant-based milk is available is not stated, so ask in advance.
caveats: Cash only. Hours may change and no clear regular closing day is published, so confirm before visiting. Seat count is not documented but the space is small.

bio.background: A small, independently run cafe tucked into the Miyajima streets near Itsukushima Shrine, a short stroll from the busy Omotesando shopping approach. It is the kind of quiet, cozy spot that locals and travelers describe as a hidden gem amid the island's tourist bustle.
bio.specialty: Coffee with carefully poured latte art is the cafe's signature; visitors mention designs in their cappuccinos and lattes.

anecdotes.text: Described by visitors as a cozy, charming cafe with a welcoming feel for solo guests, known for its latte art.

cuisine: Seafood izakaya specializing in Hiroshima oysters and anago (conger eel)
neighborhood: Hikarimachi, Higashi Ward (near JR Hiroshima Station / Shinkansen exit)
address_ja: 広島県広島市東区光町2-8-24 ダイケンビル1階
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address; lands in Higashi Ward, Hiroshima, within the Hiroshima box. Note: this is near Hiroshima Station, not the A-Bomb Dome or Miyajima.
seats_text: 50 seats total: roughly 32 at floor tables and about 16-18 in tatami (sunken kotatsu) private rooms
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays. Monday lunch is closed except when Monday is a public holiday.
closed_days: Sundays (and Monday lunch, except public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Japanese seafood izakaya with private tatami rooms; a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English support is not advertised.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. The menu centers on dishes prepared with Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and items such as anago tempura that are wheat-battered. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would find very little that is safe and should not assume any dish is gluten-free.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya built around oysters and conger eel; nearly every dish involves fish, shellfish, or dashi/soy-based seasonings. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, so vegans should not plan to eat here.
caveats: Located near Hiroshima Station, not in the A-Bomb Dome area or on Miyajima. Not suitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Opened in 1980, this is a long-running seafood izakaya in the Hikarimachi district a short walk from Hiroshima Station. It serves regional Setouchi cuisine and local sake in a setting that includes private tatami rooms with sunken-kotatsu seating, making it a popular choice for both travelers stopping near the station and locals gathering for celebrations.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served raw, grilled, or steamed, and anago (conger eel) prepared as white-grilled, sashimi, and tempura; the anago rice bowl is a signature dish offered at lunch.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the seasonal seafood of Hiroshima and the Seto Inland Sea paired with regional sake.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen makes its own original items such as smoked mackerel and potato buns alongside the classic seafood lineup, and the long-standing anago rice dish is a favorite of both tourists and regulars.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese style with soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

cuisine: Seafood izakaya specializing in Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea fish, with sashimi and seasonal hot pots (oyster miso nabe, crab, pufferfish, monkfish)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on sashimi, simmered and grilled seafood, and miso-based hot pots, which rely on Japanese soy sauce, miso, and fish dashi — all typically containing wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. This is a seafood-focused izakaya whose core offerings are oysters, sashimi, and fish/crab/pufferfish hot pots, all built on seafood and fish-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町12-10 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区幟町12-10 via GSI; result (lat 34.394009, lng 132.46524) falls within the central Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 幟町 (Noboricho), Naka Ward — about a 3-minute walk from the Hatchobori tram stop
seats_text: Roughly 17 seats — counter seating plus table seating; the official site notes tables can hold up to 20 and the counter up to 8 for larger configurations.
hours_raw: 17:00 - until sold out (Tabelog lists 17:00-22:00). Lunch by reservation only on weekdays/holidays (minimum 4 guests, 3 days advance notice). Sundays and public holidays operate irregularly — call ahead.
closed_days: No fixed closing day; Sundays and public holidays are irregular, so phone confirmation is advised.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, traditional owner-run Japanese seafood izakaya with no documented English support, so a foreign student would be most comfortable with some basic Japanese and an open mind toward an intimate local-dining setting.
caveats: Hours are irregular: dinner starts at 17:00 and runs until dishes sell out, and Sundays/holidays vary — phoning ahead is recommended. Lunch is offered only by advance reservation (minimum 4 people, 3 days notice). Not appropriate for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Shikisai is a small, refined seafood izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in Hiroshima's Noboricho district, a short walk from the Hatchobori tram stop. It works closely with local suppliers, sourcing fish and oysters directly from the Seto Inland Sea so that each plate arrives at the peak of freshness.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood: sashimi, grilled and steamed preparations, and a rotating line of seasonal hot pots — oyster-miso nabe, snow/matsuba crab, pufferfish (torafugu), and monkfish — paired with Japanese sake.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen prizes quality, freshness, and direct sourcing, preparing each dish with care so that every plate earns genuine appreciation.

anecdotes.text: The house presents itself as serving kappo-quality cooking in the relaxed setting of an izakaya, with an intimate counter-and-table room seating only around 17 guests.
Seasonal hot pots rotate through the year — from oyster-miso nabe to crab, pufferfish, and monkfish — reflecting the shop's name, which evokes a 'celebration of the four seasons.'

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Core dishes (sashimi, simmered seafood, miso-based hot pots) rely on Japanese soy sauce and miso, which typically contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes are seafood- and fish-dashi-based (oysters, sashimi, fish/crab hot pots); no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

lead: かきと水晶焼き 空
name_ja: かきと水晶焼き 空
cuisine: Izakaya specializing in Hiroshima oysters, "crystal grilling" (suishou-yaki) of local beef and pork, and Hiroshima regional dishes
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀 / Yagenbori, Naka Ward
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町1-2 船屋ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (中区堀川町1-2) via GSI; falls within the Hiroshima box, in the Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district near Ginzanchō and Ebisuchō tram stops.
seats_text: 40 seats total, including counter seating and private rooms for groups of roughly 3 to 20.
hours_raw: 17:00-02:00 (food L.O. around 01:30, drink later). Open daily including weekends and holidays.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); generally open daily, opens Sundays when reservations exist or when Monday is a holiday.
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language menu and other foreign-language menus are available, and the venue takes online reservations, so a foreign student should be comfortable here; note it is a smoking-permitted izakaya open late into the night.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. As a Hiroshima izakaya the menu relies heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, ponzu, and grill sauces, plus breaded/fried and flambeed dishes, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and meat-centric izakaya built around Hiroshima oysters, conger eel, and crystal-grilled local beef and pork. No vegan dishes are advertised, and standard seasonings use fish and wheat-soy-sauce, so it is not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: An izakaya in Hiroshima's Yagenbori nightlife quarter built around two pillars: 100% Hiroshima-grown oysters and a theatrical cooking style called suishou-yaki, or crystal grilling. The owners source oysters directly from local producers rather than through wholesale markets, aiming for daily premium freshness, with raw oysters offered into spring and grilled oyster preparations served year-round.
bio.specialty: Suishou-yaki crystal grilling, where meat and vegetables are cooked on a crystal-stone plate said to use far-infrared heat to keep the meat juicy, finished tableside with a dramatic flambe to seal in the umami. Signature ingredients include Sera Minori beef and local pork, alongside Hiroshima oysters and conger eel.
bio.philosophy: A focus on local Hiroshima ingredients sourced as freshly and directly as possible, presented through showy tableside cooking.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen sources 100% Hiroshima-grown oysters delivered daily, buying direct from producers rather than through markets to maximize freshness.
The crystal-grilling course is finished with a bold tableside flambe described as sealing in the meat's umami.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Hiroshima izakaya the menu relies on standard Japanese seasonings, which are wheat-based soy sauce and ponzu; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking surfaces and grills are shared across oysters, conger eel, beef, and pork, so plant-only items cannot be guaranteed free of animal contact.

caveats: Smoking-permitted venue; entry of children is restricted for that reason. Hours are irregular (不定休). Address geocoded at chō-level (block 1-2), not exact building. Phone numbers vary across listings (082-246-7999 / 050 booking lines).

cuisine: Oyster-focused izakaya serving Hiroshima oysters and local sake
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision is documented. The menu centers on oysters and local Hiroshima izakaya fare (gansu and senjigara are wheat-flour-battered fried items), and Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is standard for seasoning and dipping. Travelers requiring strict gluten-free should not assume any dish is safe here.
vegan_detail: This is an oyster and seafood izakaya; the signature offerings are oysters, octopus sashimi, and fried fish-paste snacks (gansu, senjigara), with sake and beer to pair. Dishes rely on seafood and wheat-containing soy sauce, so there is no documented vegan or vegetarian option.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-27 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-27; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 中区大手町（紙屋町西電停から徒歩約2分、平和記念公園至近）
seats_text: 40 seats
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-13:30 (lunch), 17:00-22:30 (dinner); Sat 17:00-22:00; Sun closed
closed_days: Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, unpretentious local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at dishes will get you a warm welcome, though an English menu is not guaranteed.
caveats: Hours and closed days may change; phoning ahead is wise. Strict gluten-free and vegan/vegetarian diners are not well served here, as the menu is built around oysters, seafood, and wheat-based seasonings.

bio.background: An oyster-specialist izakaya that opened in central Hiroshima within easy walking distance of the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome. The proprietor's idea is simple and generous: bring the island's best oysters into the heart of the city so visitors can enjoy fresh, high-grade shellfish without having to travel out to the producing islands.
bio.specialty: Salt-field-aged oysters from Farm Suzuki, alongside Hiroshima local dishes such as gansu (fried fish-paste cake), senjigara, octopus sashimi, and a lineup of Hiroshima sake and ice-cold draft beer.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing premium local oysters and Hiroshima flavors in an easygoing, welcoming setting close to the city's most visited sites.

anecdotes.text: The owner is proud that you can eat fresh, top-quality Farm Suzuki oysters here without having to make the trip out to the island.
Small touches set the tone — boiled peanuts served as an opening nibble before the oysters, beer, and sake arrive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya manner with soy sauce, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around oysters and seafood, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable for vegan diners.

lead: ひろしま丸かじり 中ちゃん
name_ja: ひろしま丸かじり 中ちゃん
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, teppanyaki and izakaya fare, with seasonal oyster dishes and the house-original uni-watercress (uni-cresson)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, whose batter and the noodles layered inside (soba/udon) are wheat-based. Teppanyaki and izakaya dishes are seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce, and signature plates like uni-cresson use soy sauce and butter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on seafood and meat: seasonal oysters, sea urchin (uni-cresson), fish dishes, and okonomiyaki containing egg, pork and bonito/seafood. Dishes use butter and wheat soy sauce. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区弥生町6-8
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 広島県広島市中区弥生町6-8 at lat 34.389801, lng 132.466019, within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: Yayoi-cho, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (about a 5-minute walk from the Kanayama-cho tram stop)
seats_text: Roughly 28-35 seats reported (teppan counter plus tables, with a private room in the back); counts vary by source. A small, busy local spot.
hours_raw: 17:00～売り切れまで（定休日：日曜日）
closed_days: Sundays. Closing time is not fixed — open from 17:00 until they sell out, so an early arrival is wise.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a lively, locals-favorite teppanyaki/okonomiyaki spot where a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) goes a long way; come early since they close once dishes sell out.
caveats: Open hours end whenever the food sells out, not at a fixed time, so arrive early. Reservations are accepted (reported as parties from about 8 people, or for arrivals before 19:00 depending on source) but are not required. Closed Sundays.

bio.background: A Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki institution founded in 1970 by Mamoru Nakasako. The shop's familiar name, 'Naka-chan,' comes from the founder's own affectionate nickname. Decades on, it remains a small, bustling counter-and-table spot beloved by locals and visitors alike, filling up the moment its doors open.
bio.specialty: The house signature is 'uni-cresson' (sea urchin and watercress on the teppan with butter, soy sauce and lemon) — a dish the original owner invented after drawing inspiration from Western cooking, and one credited here as its birthplace. Alongside it are classic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, a wide spread of teppanyaki and izakaya plates (dozens of menu items in all), and prized local oyster dishes in winter.
bio.philosophy: Keep serving the same beloved, generously prepared Hiroshima comfort food that has drawn regulars for half a century, cooking until the day's ingredients run out.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is credited as the birthplace of 'uni-cresson,' a dish founder Mamoru Nakasako created by taking inspiration from Western cuisine; it has been a favorite from the day it was first served.
The shop name 'Naka-chan' is taken from the affectionate nickname of the first-generation owner, Mamoru Nakasako.
Founded in 1970, the counter packs with a steady stream of customers from the moment it opens each evening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A teppan (flat-grill) okonomiyaki and teppanyaki kitchen where wheat batter, wheat noodles and wheat-containing soy sauce are cooked on shared surfaces, making cross-contamination essentially unavoidable for gluten-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature and everyday dishes (e.g. uni-cresson and teppanyaki items) are seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan is standardly wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on seafood and meat (oysters, sea urchin, fish, pork, egg) cooked on the same teppan with butter, so animal-product contact is pervasive.

lead: オイスター&スモークドバー サンゴ
name_ja: オイスター&スモークドバー サンゴ
cuisine: オイスターバー / ワインバー（旬の生牡蠣・牡蠣のアヒージョ・自家製スモーク料理）
neighborhood: エキニシ（広島駅西エリア）・大須賀町
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区大須賀町13-27-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoded the registered address 広島県広島市南区大須賀町13-27-1 to lat 34.397984, lng 132.472687, inside the Hiroshima box. Note: the establishment is in 南区 (Minami Ward), not 東区 as listed in the target brief; it sits just south/west of JR Hiroshima Station in the Ekinishi district.
seats_text: About 24-27 seats across two floors: a roughly 12-seat open-kitchen counter on the 1st floor and table seating (~12-15) on the 2nd floor. Source figures vary (Tabelog lists 24; the shop's reservation page lists 27).
hours_raw: 月・水〜日・祝・祝前日 18:00〜00:00（L.O. フード/ドリンク 23:30）／火曜定休
closed_days: 火曜日（Tuesdays）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a lively local izakaya-style oyster bar where the menu and conversation are mainly in Japanese, so a phrase or two of Japanese (or a translation app) will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes that typically use wheat-based seasonings and breading: fried oysters (karaage), smoked items, pasta, and izakaya plates seasoned with soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or cross-contamination controls are mentioned on any page reviewed.
vegan_detail: This is an oyster and smoked-seafood bar; the entire concept is built around raw, grilled, and fried oysters plus house-smoked seafood, bacon, and cheese. Dishes are seasoned with fish- and wheat-soy-based sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and it is not a suitable destination for plant-based diners.
caveats: The target brief listed the area as 東区大須賀町, but the registered address and all sources place it in 南区大須賀町 (Minami Ward), in the Ekinishi area just west of JR Hiroshima Station's south side. Seat count and exact hours differ slightly between sources. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan information is published; plant-based and celiac travelers should look elsewhere.
sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=広島県広島市南区大須賀町13-27-1

bio.background: A casual oyster-and-smoke bar in Hiroshima's buzzing Ekinishi district, a short walk west of the station. It pairs fresh oysters with a wine-bar sensibility and a lineup of house-smoked dishes.
bio.specialty: Direct-sourced seasonal oysters served raw, grilled, and fried, plus a signature raw oyster crowned with uni and ikura, oyster ajillo, and a wide range of house-smoked items including oysters, bacon, chicken wings, eggs, cheese, salmon, and takuan pickles.
bio.philosophy: Oysters are bought directly from producers, with the varieties rotating by season and region; the kitchen highlights notably large, plump, sweet-and-savory oysters.

anecdotes.text: The first-floor open-kitchen counter lets guests watch oysters being shucked and smoked dishes prepared, while a quieter second floor offers table seating for groups.

A house signature is raw oysters topped with sea urchin and salmon roe, and the kitchen runs an in-house smoker turning out everything from smoked oysters and bacon to smoked cheese and pickled radish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Izakaya-style oyster and smoked dishes here are typically seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen smokes and fries seafood, bacon, and cheese on shared equipment, so any vegetable item would share surfaces and oil with animal products.

cuisine: Shellfish and seafood izakaya (oysters, shellfish, sashimi)
neighborhood: Tachimachi, Naka-ku (about 1 min from Tachimachi tram stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-23
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区立町3-23 returned [132.4599, 34.394085], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Approximately 24 seats: ground-floor counter plus three second-floor semi-private rooms seating about 6 each. (The owner's site describes an 8-seat counter and three 6-person rooms.)
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:30–23:00 (Food L.O. 22:00, Drink L.O. 22:30). Closed Sundays; if a Monday is a public holiday the shop opens that Monday and closes the following day instead.
closed_days: Sundays (irregular adjustments around public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, locally-run shellfish specialist where reservations are taken by phone or LINE and the menu is built almost entirely around shellfish, so a foreign student should be ready to communicate dietary needs in Japanese and expect a dinner-only, course-style experience.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. As a shellfish izakaya, dishes rely on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and typical izakaya seasonings; no dedicated gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The restaurant is a dedicated shellfish specialist and its own site states plainly that people who dislike shellfish will find nothing to eat here; the menu centers on oysters, shellfish and seafood.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly between sources (around 24 total). Hours follow a special holiday-Monday rule. Reservations are by phone or LINE only, not via web forms.

bio.background: The shop is an authorized offshoot (noren-wake) of the Hiroshima establishment 笹組 (Sasagumi), opened around 2014 in the Tachimachi quarter of central Hiroshima.
bio.specialty: A specialist in shellfish cuisine, best known for oysters served several ways, a comparison plate of three kinds of raw oysters, shell-roasted oysters, vinegar-dressed oysters, and assorted shellfish sashimi.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on quality fats such as extra-virgin olive oil and plant-based safflower oil and avoids chemical seasonings and preservatives, and the house is unapologetically focused: it states outright that this is a shellfish specialty house and that those who do not enjoy shellfish will find little to eat.

anecdotes.text: The owner's site warns guests directly that this is a shellfish specialty house and that people who dislike shellfish will find nothing to eat, signalling a single-minded devotion to the ingredient.
It carries on the lineage of 笹組 as an authorized noren-wake spinoff.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese shellfish izakaya, dishes are expected to use wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and standard seasonings; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around shellfish and seafood; the house states that those who dislike shellfish will find nothing to eat, so no plant-only meal is offered.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner's site asks guests to review allergy and disliked-ingredient information before booking, indicating allergies can be discussed at reservation by phone or LINE.

positives.text: Cooking fats are limited to extra-virgin olive oil and plant-based safflower oil with low trans-fat content, and the kitchen avoids chemical seasonings and preservatives.

cuisine: Grilled oysters / oyster hut (焼き牡蠣・かき小屋)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on oysters served with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and ponzu, plus deep-fried oysters (kaki-fry, breaded in wheat flour/panko) and miso soup. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented. A diner avoiding gluten could in theory order plain grilled oysters with no sauce, but cross-contamination on shared grills and the absence of any stated allergen handling mean it cannot be recommended as safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a single-product oyster hut: the entire concept is grilling and serving fresh Hiroshima oysters (shellfish), supplemented by fried oysters and oyster miso soup. There are no documented plant-based dishes, and sauces use wheat-soy and fish-based seasonings.
address_ja: 広島県広島市西区草津港1-13
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [132.403564, 34.36375] for 広島県広島市西区草津港1-13, within the Hiroshima box; lands at Kusatsu Port in Nishi Ward, consistent with the address.
neighborhood: 広島市西区 草津港（草津漁港）
seats_text: About 104 seats: roughly 2 counter seats plus 17 six-person tables, in a large open hut-style space.
hours_raw: Seasonal operation (approx. early/mid November - late April). Mon-Fri 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat & day before holidays 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sun & holidays 11:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30). Closed Dec 31-Jan 3 and during severe weather (snow, typhoons). Season-dependent — confirm by phone before visiting.
closed_days: Operates seasonally (closed roughly May through early/mid-November in the off-season). Also closed Dec 31-Jan 3 and during severe weather (heavy snow, typhoons).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, rustic seasonal oyster shack run by the local fishery; you grill your own oysters at the table, it is cash-only with no English menu, so a foreign student would do best coming with a Japanese-speaking friend or a guide.
caveats: Open only during the winter oyster season (roughly November to late April) and cash-only. Hours and the exact season open/close dates change year to year and are not finalized far in advance, so call ahead. The geocode and address point to Kusatsu Port in Nishi Ward, west of the city center, not within walking distance of the A-Bomb Dome.

bio.background: A seasonal oyster hut sitting right at Kusatsu fishing port on the western edge of Hiroshima City, operated by the local oyster producer Oosato Kaisan. Because it stands at the working port, the oysters travel only the shortest distance from raft to grill.
bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat grilled oysters and other oyster preparations using 'Kusatsu kaki,' a Hiroshima brand-certified oyster, with diners cooking the shellfish themselves over tabletop grills.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing locally farmed, brand-certified Kusatsu oysters at peak winter season in a simple, hands-on hut setting rather than a polished restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The hut is run by Oosato Kaisan and serves 'Kusatsu kaki,' an oyster recognized as a certified Hiroshima brand.
It operates only in the cold-weather oyster season, opening around mid-November and closing in spring, when Hiroshima oysters are at their best.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Self-grilling at shared tabletop grills and fried oysters breaded in wheat flour mean a high likelihood of gluten cross-contact; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oysters are typically eaten with standard Japanese soy sauce and ponzu, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The venue is dedicated to oysters and other shellfish/seafood with no plant-based menu, so it is unsuitable for vegans.

lead: 中華そば 陽気 （ようき）
name_ja: 中華そば 陽気 江波本店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style chuka soba (shoyu-tonkotsu ramen)
neighborhood: Eba, Naka Ward (central Hiroshima, ~3.5km SW of the A-Bomb Dome)
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The sole offering is wheat ramen noodles (specially made by Harada noodle company) in a soy-sauce-and-pork-bone broth. Soy sauce is the defining seasoning of the soup and is virtually certain to contain wheat. There is no indication of GF accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The single signature dish is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and soy-sauce broth topped with char siu (roast pork), green onion, and bean sprouts. The broth itself is animal-based, so even a topping-free bowl would not be vegan.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区江波南3-4-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 広島市中区江波南3-4-1 within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.3654, lng 132.4305), located in the Eba district of Naka Ward.
seats_text: 20 seats (8 counter, 10 table); 8 parking spaces available
hours_raw: 11:30-13:30, 18:00-23:00. Closed days reported as the 1st, 12th, 13th and 26th of each month (official site); a listing also reports Tuesday/Wednesday closed — confirm locally before visiting.
closed_days: Closed days reported differently by sources: the official site lists fixed calendar dates (1st, 12th, 13th, 26th of each month), while another listing reports Tuesday and Wednesday. Treat closures as irregular and confirm by phone.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, no-frills local ramen counter where ordering is simple but staff and menu are Japanese-only, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and cash) will make the visit smooth.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations. Closing days conflict between sources — verify before traveling. The single dish contains wheat noodles, pork-bone/soy broth and pork, so it is unsuitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Founded in 1958 in the Eba neighborhood of central Hiroshima, this shop began as a humble stall and grew into one of the city's defining ramen institutions. The founder learned his craft in the lineage that traces back to the earliest Hiroshima ramen, and the original Eba shop has served the same nostalgic bowl for generations.
bio.specialty: A single, focused offering: chuka soba with thin straight noodles in a soy-sauce-and-pork-bone broth, topped with char siu, green onion and bean sprouts. Soy sauce is treated as the soul of the soup.
bio.philosophy: A nostalgic, everyday taste meant to be loved by everyone — simplicity and consistency over novelty, with house-spec noodles made to order by a local noodle maker.

anecdotes.text: The founder is said to have learned ramen-making within the lineage regarded as the origin of Hiroshima ramen, and the shop serves essentially one dish — chuka soba — to this day.
The Eba honten is the original location of what has since grown into several shops across Hiroshima and Fukuyama, and it still sells its broth and char siu for home cooking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares wheat ramen noodles as its only product, so any gluten-free request would face heavy cross-contact in a shared noodle-cooking environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is described as the key ingredient of the soup, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a pork-bone and soy-sauce base and the dish is topped with char siu pork, so animal products are central to every bowl.

cuisine: Anago (conger eel) rice bowls, oysters, udon and soba in a dried-sardine broth, beef curry, and Japanese sweets at a seaside family teahouse
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house anago donburi uses a soy-sauce-based "secret sauce" (Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the menu centers on udon and soba (wheat/buckwheat noodles) plus dishes like beef curry rice. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or GF accommodation is documented. Travelers requiring strict gluten avoidance should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Core dishes are anago (conger eel) and oysters, and the prized udon/soba broth is made abundantly with Seto Inland Sea dried sardines (niboshi), so even the noodle dishes contain fish. No plant-based menu or vegan accommodation is documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町
geocode_note: GSI geocoder resolved the Miyajima-machi (宮島町) chō-level address to lng 132.331451 / lat 34.348637, which lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box. This is a town-level centroid, not a rooftop pin; the teahouse sits along the seaside pine grove beyond Itsukushima Shrine near Kiyomori Shrine.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), seaside pine grove beyond Itsukushima Shrine near Kiyomori Shrine, en route to the aquarium
seats_text: 60 seats total (about 40 indoors, 20 on the terrace)
hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (Tabelog); 10:30–17:00 (official site). Evening service possible by advance phone reservation.
closed_days: Irregular (no fixed closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed family-run seaside teahouse with terrace seating where you can point at the photo menu and order simple set meals; no English service is documented, so a translation app and a little patience will help.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (official site lists 10:30 open, Tabelog lists 11:00); closures are irregular so confirm by phone before a special trip. Evening dining is only available with an advance reservation. The seating split (40 indoor / 20 terrace) and the 60-seat total come from listing data and may vary seasonally.

bio.background: A seaside teahouse on Miyajima that has been run by the same family across three generations since 1955 (Showa 30). It sits in a quiet pine grove along the shore past Itsukushima Shrine, near the small Kiyomori Shrine and on the way toward the aquarium — a calm retreat away from the crowded main shopping street.
bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) donburi finished with a long-guarded house sauce, alongside udon and soba in a broth made generously from Seto Inland Sea dried sardines. The signature Kiyomori Set lets you enjoy a half-portion anago bowl together with your choice of noodles. Grilled small sardines (iwashi shioyaki) are a house exclusive.
bio.philosophy: Their stated spirit is to let visitors pause and rest while listening to the sound of the tide — 潮の音を聴きながら、のどかな場所で一休み — offering a peaceful seaside breather from the bustle.

anecdotes.text: The teahouse has welcomed visitors since 1955 and is still run by the founding family into a third generation, tucked into the seaside pine grove beyond Itsukushima Shrine near Kiyomori Shrine.
In winter a wood-burning stove warms the room, and the spot is welcoming to families with children, with high chairs, terrace seating, and ocean views; pets are also welcome.
Its prized noodle broth is made abundantly with dried sardines (niboshi) from the Seto Inland Sea, and the anago donburi is finished with a secret house sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Udon and soba are prepared on-site and the kitchen handles wheat-based dishes throughout, so cross-contact with gluten is likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago donburi is finished with a soy-sauce-based house sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature udon/soba broth is made with Seto Inland Sea dried sardines (niboshi), so noodle dishes are not vegetarian or vegan; the menu centers on conger eel and oysters.

cuisine: Oyster bar / Italian-style creative oyster dishes (牡蠣・イタリアン創作)
neighborhood: Miyajima island (between Machiya-dori and Omotesando shopping street, near Itsukushima Shrine)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町字幸町435-4
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 幸町西浜 on Miyajima island at lng 132.32074, lat 34.297993 — within the Hiroshima box and correctly on the island.
seats_text: 10 seats total: 6 counter seats and 1 table for 4.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:00; closed Tuesdays
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual walk-in oyster counter where you can order a single oyster, point at the menu, and enjoy a quick bite — friendly for a foreign student, though a few basic Japanese words and cash on hand will smooth things along.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. Signature creative dishes such as oyster gratin and oyster tomato sauce typically use wheat flour and dairy, and oyster dishes generally involve wheat-based seasonings, so this is not a gluten-free destination. Confirm directly with staff if you have celiac disease.
vegan_detail: An oyster-focused bar — every documented dish centers on Hiroshima oysters (shellfish), so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Beverages such as wine, beer, and Hiroshima lemon soda are available, but there are no documented vegan food options.
caveats: Owner/chef names and detailed personal background are not documented in available sources; cash-only and no reservations confirmed via Tabelog. Phone number is not publicly listed.

bio.background: tuGiico opened on October 1, 2021, in a renovated traditional wooden townhouse tucked along an alley between Miyajima's machiya street and the Omotesando shopping arcade, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine. It is a small, independent oyster bar run by a chef couple who serve Hiroshima oysters paired with wine, beer, and Hiroshima lemon soda.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served by the single piece — from simple grilled oysters that let the ingredient shine to Italian-influenced creative plates like oyster gratin and oyster tomato sauce.
bio.philosophy: The shop's name reflects its guiding idea: because Miyajima has so many wonderful spots and eateries, guests should enjoy a quick bite here and then move on to the next place. To make this possible, oysters are sold one at a time — an unusual practice in Miyajima — so even solo travelers can drop in casually.

anecdotes.text: Selling oysters by the single piece is described as a rare practice in Miyajima, deliberately designed so visitors can sample a little and keep exploring the island.
Set in a stylishly renovated old townhouse on a quiet alley between the machiya street and the main Omotesando arcade, the spot is small with just a six-seat counter and one table for four.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around oysters (shellfish); creative dishes such as gratin also use dairy, so there is no separation from animal products.

cuisine: Western-style (yoshoku) grill — Japanese-style omelette rice, hamburger steak, vegetable-forward creative dishes
neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi (Hondori shopping arcade, central Naka-ku)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature Japanese-style omelette rice, hamburger steak, curry, and Western sauces (roux/demi-glace style) are wheat-based, and no dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Although vegetables are the centerpiece of the cooking, dishes deliberately combine seasonal vegetables with seafood, meat, and dairy to highlight flavors (egg-based omelette rice, hamburger steak, etc.). No vegan menu is documented; a plant-based diner would need to ask the owner directly.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通1-21 ダルニービル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address to the chō-block level via GSI; point falls within the central Hiroshima box, on the Hondori arcade roughly 0.7 km east of the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 16 seats (8 counter, 8 table); can accommodate groups up to about 14
hours_raw: Tue, Thu–Sat: 12:00-14:00 lunch, 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30) dinner; Wed: dinner only 18:00-22:30 (L.O. 21:30); Sun/Holidays: dinner only 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays (Wednesday and Sunday are dinner-only)
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run counter-and-table spot where service is in Japanese; a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese or a translation app will be welcomed warmly, but no English menu is documented.
caveats: Lunch is served only Tue and Thu–Sat (Wed and Sun are dinner-only). Vegetable-forward but not vegetarian/vegan, and not gluten-free.

bio.background: Inspired to cook by a popular television cooking program in his student years, Fukuma trained in the banquet kitchen of the Hiroshima Grand Hotel, where he learned the fundamentals of cooking thoroughly. He went on to work at a well-known Western-cuisine restaurant, rose to become its branch manager, and eventually took the business over to open his own place, Grill Hikoboshi.
bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward creative Western cooking; the house favorite is a gentle Japanese-style omelette rice, alongside hamburger steak and seasonal vegetable dishes that pair produce with seafood, meat, and dairy to bring out each ingredient's flavor.
bio.philosophy: Fukuma approaches cooking and service with sincerity every day, aiming to welcome guests comfortably and to tailor dishes thoughtfully to each customer's preferences rather than imposing a fixed menu.

anecdotes.text: Fukuma runs the intimate 16-seat room largely on his own, creating a warm, homey atmosphere where guests can dine quietly and at their own pace.
The Japanese-style omelette rice is the dish guests most associate with the place and is especially popular.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner-chef states he tailors dishes to customer preferences rather than imposing a set menu, so accommodation requests can be discussed directly; however, no specific allergy or gluten/vegan handling procedures are documented.

positives.text: Vegetables are the centerpiece of the cooking, with many seasonal vegetable dishes available.

cuisine: Retro café / kissaten (coffee, hot dogs, morning sets, sandwiches)
neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通1-3
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市中区本通1-3 via GSI; lands within the central Hiroshima box near the Hondori/Tatemachi shopping arcade, east of the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 47 seats total: 2nd floor counter 11 seats, 3rd floor tables 36 seats.
closed_days: No regular closing day
cultural_comfort_note: A laid-back self-service retro café where you order at the counter and find a seat; little to no English, but pointing at the simple coffee-and-hot-dog menu works fine and prices are very gentle.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core menu is bread-based — ham-and-egg sandwiches, hot dogs on wheat buns, and morning-set toast — so it is not a suitable choice for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan items are documented. The food menu centers on hot dogs, ham-and-egg sandwiches and similar items containing meat, egg and dairy; only plain drinks (coffee, tea, juice) would be incidentally plant-based, and no plant-milk option is noted.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations; self-service ordering. Omelet rice (オムライス) was not confirmed by any source fetched — the documented specialties are hot dogs and morning sets, so omelet rice should not be promised. Located in the Hondori/Tatemachi arcade area (Tabelog lists the address as 本通1-3), a short walk from the Tatemachi tram stop.

bio.background: A long-running, self-service retro coffee house tucked into the arcade between Hatchobori and Tatemachi in central Hiroshima, marked by a Beatles sign next to the Kamakura Pasta shop. The cafe spreads over upper floors with a counter and table seating in a vintage, vaguely British atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Inexpensive cafe fare: hot dogs (including the 'country dog' and chili dog), morning sets with fluffy-bread ham-and-egg sandwiches, and simple, well-made iced coffee.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately simple, affordable menu meant to evoke an old-school neighborhood kissaten where regulars can linger from early morning until late night.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is named after the Beatles song, and the owner is a serious Beatles fan: the interior is packed wall-to-wall with the band's music, photographs and record jackets, and an Abbey Road-inspired sign marks the entrance.

Despite high local ratings it is treated as a hidden gem; drinks run around 250 yen and food items roughly 200-300 yen, and it opens from 8:00 in the morning until midnight with no regular closing day.

cuisine: Japanese wild game and hot pot (wild boar shabu-shabu, botan nabe, grilled game meat)
neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima shopping arcade, ~0.8 km east of the A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-18 HM本通会館5F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (本通8-18) via GSI; coordinates [132.458542, 34.392769] fall within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; layout includes counter, table, and tatami/private-room seating (small establishment).
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-14:00 and 17:00-22:00; public holidays 17:00-22:00; closed Sundays.
closed_days: Sundays (public holidays open dinner only, 17:00-22:00)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional game-meat specialist with mostly Japanese-language menus and no advertised English support, so a translation app and some basic Japanese will help you order and explain any needs.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The cooking relies on wheat-containing seasonings such as ponzu and miso-based shabu-shabu broths, and the meal-closing courses include rice/risotto-style and noodle finishes; no gluten-free accommodations are advertised. A celiac traveler should not rely on this restaurant.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built almost entirely around wild boar and other game meats (boar shabu-shabu, botan miso hot pot, grilled boar and pheasant, boar sashimi). The only plant-leaning items are tofu and seasonal mountain-vegetable dishes, but they are part of a meat-centric kitchen using fish/meat-based broths and soy seasonings.
caveats: Target metadata described this as 洋食 (Western/meat), but the actual menu is Japanese wild-game cuisine (boar shabu-shabu, botan nabe, grilled game). It is a meat-forward specialty restaurant, not a casual diner and not budget-friendly (dinner roughly 8,000-10,000 yen). Seat count is not published.

bio.background: A tucked-away specialist in Hiroshima's Hondori arcade devoted to wild boar and mountain game, the kind of quietly serious place locals seek out rather than stumble into. It is described as having moved from an earlier location while keeping its hidden-gem character.
bio.specialty: Wild boar (inoshishi): boar shabu-shabu and botan miso hot pot, grilled boar and pheasant, and boar sashimi, rounded out with seasonal mountain vegetables and a rice or risotto-style finish.
bio.philosophy: Letting seasonal, naturally raised game speak for itself, with the boar praised for tender meat and clean, sweet fat rather than gaminess.
roles: wild game and hot pot specialist

anecdotes.text: Diners single it out as a definitive Hiroshima destination for wild boar, praising meat that is tender with sweet fat and little of the odor people fear from game.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shabu-shabu and hot pot are served with ponzu and miso-based sauces/broths, which are typically wheat-containing soy seasonings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around boar and game meat with meat/fish-based hot pot broths, so even tofu and vegetable dishes share a thoroughly non-vegan cooking environment.

cuisine: カレー（ビーフベースのスパイスカレー）
neighborhood: 中区本通／立町（本通・八丁堀近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通4-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区本通4-1 returned [132.459747, 34.393356], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in the Hontori/Tatemachi central shopping arcade area, consistent with the documented location.
seats_text: カウンター10席のみ（個室なし）
closed_days: 不定休（Irregular closures）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter curry counter run by locals; ordering is simple (pick a curry and a spice level 0–20) but expect Japanese-only service, cash only, and possibly a queue, so a little Japanese or pointing helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or claim. The curry is a slow-simmered beef roux blended with ~20 spices, which in this style typically includes wheat flour, and no GF accommodation or dedicated preparation is documented. Travelers requiring strict gluten-free should not assume safety here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The base curry is beef, simmered for three days until the beef dissolves into the roux, and signature dishes feature bacon, shrimp, octopus, squid, and cheese. Even the house lassi uses yogurt. No vegan or vegetarian curry option is documented.
caveats: Address on the official site is 中区本通4-1 (postal 730-0035); the listing area name is 立町, and the shop is steps from Tatemachi tram stop in the Hontori arcade district — these refer to the same central location, not separate addresses. Vegan/GF/allergy handling is not documented on any fetched page.

bio.background: ガリバーは1984年（昭和59年）に創業した広島・本通エリアの老舗カレー店で、現在は二代目が初代から受け継いだレシピを守りながら営む、カウンター10席だけの小さな店。広島の中心商店街でカレーの香りを漂わせる名物店として長く親しまれている。
bio.origin: 広島市中区本通（立町・本通アーケード周辺）
bio.specialty: ビーフをベースに約20種のスパイスを調合し、出汁から取って弱火でじっくり煮込み、寝かせて完成まで最低3日かける手間ひまかけたカレー。肉も野菜もすべて国産にこだわる。看板メニューにはエビ・タコ・イカ・ベーコン・きのこ・ナスが入る『ガリバースペシャルカレー』や、ベーコン・きのこ・チーズの『山の幸スペシャル』、ターメリックライス（ご飯大盛り無料）など。
bio.philosophy: 初代の味を忠実に守り、国産素材と時間を惜しまない仕込みで『変わらない味』を提供すること。

anecdotes.text: 辛さは0番（マイルド）から20番まで選べ、16番以上を食べ切った人には『辛党認定ステッカー』が贈られる。0〜3番は無料、4番以降は追加料金。
牛肉が溶けるまで弱火で煮込むため、ルーの完成には最低3日かかると紹介されている。
ヨーグルトを使った自家製ラッシーがスパイスの辛さを和らげる定番のお供。

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared kitchen builds all curries on the same beef-based roux simmered for three days, so any vegetable order would carry meat-based stock; no separate vegan preparation is described.

positives.text: All meat and vegetables are sourced domestically (国産) and roughly 20 spices are blended in-house, reflecting transparency about ingredients.

cuisine: Tempura (counter-style tempura shop; also serves eel/anago, oysters, sushi)
neighborhood: Hondori / Kamiyacho (Hondori shopping arcade, near the A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通5-13 本通ウェーブビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chōme-level address 広島県広島市中区本通5-13 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box, in the Hondori arcade area.
seats_text: 13 seats total (5 counter seats + 8 tatami/zashiki seats)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 / Dinner 17:30-22:30 (sources also list 10:30-15:00 / 17:00-21:00); open daily with irregular closures (不定休)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed day off)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual local tempura counter in the Hondori arcade where ordering is in Japanese and seating is tight, so a few basic Japanese words and patience help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: This is a tempura shop, and tempura batter is made with wheat flour, so the signature fried dishes are not gluten-free. The dipping tsuyu (broth) is also typically soy-sauce based (contains wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on seafood and eel: anago (conger eel) tempura don, oyster tempura, shrimp tempura, and sushi, with a dashi/fish-based dipping broth and wheat-soy sauces. There is no documented vegan offering.
caveats: Two listings give slightly different hours (11:30-15:00/17:30-22:30 vs 10:30-15:00/17:00-21:00); the restaurant has irregular closing days, so confirm by phone before visiting. Cards are not accepted (cash; PayPay QR may be accepted).

bio.background: A small, long-running tempura counter tucked into Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from the A-Bomb Dome. With just a handful of counter and tatami seats, it is the kind of unassuming neighborhood spot regulars return to for an honest, generous fried lunch.
bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) tempura over rice and seafood tempura bowls, served with the dipping sauce on the side so each piece can be eaten with salt or dunked to taste; oyster and shrimp tempura and sushi also feature.
bio.philosophy: Tempura is fried to order, which keeps service unhurried but means each plate arrives fresh and crisp rather than pre-soaked.

anecdotes.text: The anago tempura don is famous for portions so large the eel spills over the rim of the bowl, served with rice, pickles, grated daikon and miso soup, and a separately served broth praised for its rich dashi.
The shop's big-portion lunch was featured on the local TV program Mantenma (満点ママ).

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a tempura specialist, the kitchen fries wheat-battered items throughout; a shared fryer and wheat-based tsuyu make cross-contamination very likely for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes and the dipping broth use a dashi/soy-sauce base, which standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on seafood, eel and fish-based dashi, so vegan diners face pervasive animal-product contact.

cuisine: 中華料理 (local Chinese / town-Chinese, machi-chuka)
neighborhood: 中区本通／立町（本通・八丁堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通4-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区本通4-1 returned [132.459747, 34.393356], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in the central Hondori arcade area near Tatemachi.
seats_text: 11 counter seats only
closed_days: None listed (open daily); small independent shops like this may close irregularly, so calling ahead is wise.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, cash-only counter shop run in Japanese with no reservations or English menu, so a few basic Japanese phrases and patience will make the visit smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is a traditional town-Chinese kitchen built around fried rice, ramen, dumplings and stir-fries; soy sauce, wheat noodles and wheat-flour dumpling wrappers are central, and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat, egg and seafood Chinese dishes (fried rice, dumplings, ramen) and uses standard soy sauce; some vegetable stir-fries exist but are typically cooked with meat-based broth, lard or oyster/soy seasonings, so a vegan would need to ask directly and options are likely very limited.

bio.background: A long-running, family-style Chinese counter shop tucked into central Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, said to have been serving the neighborhood for more than 40 years. With just a row of counter seats and a single-house storefront, it keeps the nostalgic feel of an old-school Showa-era 'town Chinese' diner.
bio.specialty: Wok-fried rice (yakimeshi / chahan) is the signature, alongside everyday Chinese plates and a very affordable set lunch that has included dishes like eight-treasure vegetable stir-fry, pan-fried dumplings, and egg-and-potato.

anecdotes.text: Regulars come for big, cheap, satisfying plates, with most lunches landing under 1,000 yen and a service lunch around 750 yen.
The shop has kept a nostalgic Showa-era town-Chinese atmosphere with counter-only seating in the heart of the Hondori arcade.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a small single-counter wok kitchen serving noodles, dumplings and fried rice, shared woks, soy-based seasonings and wheat ingredients make cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is used throughout the cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes are prepared in the same wok and seasonings as meat, egg and seafood dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

caveats: Cash only and no reservations. Only 11 counter seats, so seating is tight and waits are possible at peak lunch. No documented gluten-free or vegan accommodation; allergy handling is unknown and should be confirmed in person. Closed days were not clearly listed, so call ahead to confirm before visiting.

cuisine: Hiroshima tsukemen and ramen (lunch); premium yakiniku course (evening, by reservation)
neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀12-23
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区八丁堀12-23 returned [132.463684, 34.395565], within the Hiroshima box; about a 3-minute walk from the Hatchobori tram stop.
seats_text: 20 seats (10 counter, 10 table)
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:30-14:00, 17:00-21:00; Sun and holidays closed. Evening service (yakiniku course) is by reservation only.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only neighborhood noodle shop with a retro Showa-era feel and many regulars; little to no English is likely, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, and lunchtime tsukemen is the easy, walk-in-friendly option.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are wheat noodles (tsukemen and ramen), and the dipping broth/seasoning relies on standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or preparation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The tsukemen broth is built on bonito (katsuo) fish dashi, dishes are topped with chashu pork and boiled egg, and the evening service is a beef yakiniku course. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Evening service operates as a reservation-only premium beef yakiniku course, which is a different experience and price point from the casual daytime noodles. Cash only.

bio.background: The owner left a corporate career to move into the restaurant business and opened this tsukemen and ramen shop in May 2001, just as Hiroshima's local-style tsukemen was taking off, making it one of the early standard-bearers of the city's dipping-noodle culture.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: chilled wheat noodles served on a large plate with a colorful spread of toppings (onion, boiled egg, chashu, cucumber, seaweed, lemon, radish sprouts, cabbage, tomato) and a bonito-dashi dipping sauce. By night the same space becomes a reservation-only yakiniku course of high-grade, never-frozen beef.
bio.philosophy: A wear-two-hats neighborhood spot run with care, holding to never-frozen, carefully sourced beef for its evening course and keeping a warm, Showa-retro room full of regulars.

anecdotes.text: The owner left office work to open the shop in May 2001, riding the wave of the Hiroshima tsukemen boom that had begun the year before.
The walls are lined with autographed photos of pro baseball players, including Shohei Ohtani from his Fighters days.
A loyalty card rewards ten visits with a free bowl of your favorite noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat noodles for both tsukemen and ramen, so flour and wheat-based broth are present throughout; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce and seasonings use standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made with bonito (fish) dashi and dishes include chashu pork and egg, so animal products are integral to the kitchen.

lead: 生粉 手打そば 結喜庵
name_ja: 生粉 手打そば 結喜庵
cuisine: Handmade 100% buckwheat soba
gf_detail: The house specialty is kiko juwari soba — 100% buckwheat noodles made with no wheat binder (tsunagi), so the noodles themselves are naturally wheat-free. However, this is a traditional soba shop with no documented gluten-free program: the dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, and tempura is wheat-battered. There is also no information confirming a dedicated buckwheat-only kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. A celiac or highly sensitive diner would need to communicate carefully and could realistically only have the plain noodles without the wheat-based tsuyu, tempura, or soy sauce.
vegan_detail: Soba broth (tsuyu) is traditionally made with bonito/fish dashi, so standard cold and hot soba here should be assumed non-vegan. Some menu items such as the vegetable tempura (maitake mushroom, lotus root, paprika) are plant-based as ingredients, but they are fried in shared oil and the noodles are normally served with fish-based tsuyu. No vegan menu or vegan dashi is documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町8-15
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島市中区幟町8-15 via GSI; result lat 34.394585, lng 132.466644 falls within the central Hiroshima bounding box (Noboricho, near the Hatchobori / Kanayamacho tram stops).
neighborhood: Noboricho, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori / Kanayamacho tram stops, central Hiroshima)
seats_text: About 41 seats: counter (around 5), tables (around 22), and a tatami zashiki area (around 14). Some listings round to 42.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) daily; dinner 17:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00) Monday-Saturday. Sunday is lunch only. Sources vary — one listing shows lunch-only hours — so hours can change; confirm before a dinner visit.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休); closed during the New Year period. No fixed weekly closing day documented.
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, traditional soba shop where ordering is simple by pointing at the soba you want, but it is cash-only with little English, so bring yen and a few Japanese phrases.

bio.background: This is a small, owner-run handmade soba shop tucked into the Noboricho district of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the downtown tram stops and about 1.5 km from the Peace Memorial Park. The family's connection to traditional food runs deep — the owner's roots trace back to a Meiji-era dengaku (skewered tofu) teahouse run by his father. The owner came to soba through his brother, who was devoted to the craft, and the two worked side by side refining their own noodle.
bio.specialty: Kiko juwari soba — 100% buckwheat noodles hand-cut with no wheat binder. The house is known for a signature 'shogen' soba: an unusually pale, almost translucent, glossy noodle prized for its smooth texture and clean finish. Diners can compare several styles, typically shogen, sarashina, and inaka (rustic), each made from buckwheat milled a different way.
bio.philosophy: The shop uses only carefully selected buckwheat flour and adds no binding flour, letting the grain itself carry the aroma, texture, and the satisfying slip down the throat that defines great soba.

anecdotes.text: A popular pairing is the shogen soba served with a vegetable tempura of maitake mushroom, lotus root, and paprika; the bowl looks modest but eats with surprising substance, the noodles described as delicately aromatic with a clean, smooth finish.
The brothers developed their signature pale, translucent 'shogen' noodle together through repeated experimentation, building on the family's older roots in a traditional dengaku teahouse.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional soba shop with no documented gluten-free protocol; tempura is wheat-battered and the kitchen handles wheat, so cross-contact with the buckwheat noodles cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with traditional tsuyu based on Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard soba broth uses fish-based dashi; tempura is fried in shared oil. No vegan dashi or separate preparation is documented.

positives.text: The noodles are genuine kiko juwari (100% buckwheat) made with no wheat binder, so the soba itself contains no wheat in the noodle.

cuisine: 手打ちそば (handmade soba)
neighborhood: 広島市中区袋町（本通・立町エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町8-14 ハイネス国泰ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI at chiban level (袋町8-14); coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box near the Hondori/Tatemachi central district.
seats_text: Small shop with counter seats (suited to solo diners) and table seating; exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 月・火・木・金・土 11:00-14:45 / 17:30-21:30、水 11:00-14:45、日 定休（ランチは現金のみ、ディナーはカード・QR決済可）
closed_days: Sundays (Wednesday is lunch only)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local soba shop where ordering is easiest with a little Japanese or by pointing at the menu; the friendly owner makes solo diners feel welcome.
gf_detail: Standard soba contains wheat flour, and the dipping/broth dashi is made with wheat-based soy sauce, so the menu is not gluten-free. A daily limited "十割" (100% buckwheat) soba is offered, but the broth/tsuyu still uses regular soy sauce containing wheat and there is no documented gluten-free or cross-contamination protocol, so this is not a safe choice for those needing strict gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The broth (tsuyu/dashi) is built on bonito and mackerel flakes plus kombu, so it is not vegan; menu highlights also include duck, kurobuta pork, and tempura. No vegan-specific dishes are documented.
caveats: Owner-run soba shop; lunch is cash-only while dinner accepts cards and QR payment. Often busy at lunch with waits. Wednesday is lunch-only and Sundays are closed. The popular 100% buckwheat soba is limited to about 30 portions per day.

bio.background: A small wa-modern soba shop tucked into the Fukuromachi area of central Hiroshima, near the Hondori shopping arcade. The shop is run by manager Shirakawa, known as approachable and warm, which has made the counter a comfortable spot even for solo diners.
bio.specialty: Handmade soba using flour from a Hokkaido farm (Uehara/Kamihara Farm) that earned an Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries Minister's award, paired with a dashi carefully drawn from selected bonito and mackerel flakes and premium kombu, finished with a specially sourced soy sauce meant to let the buckwheat's flavor stand out.
bio.philosophy: Let the quality of the soba flour and a meticulously built broth speak for themselves, served in a calm, jazz-tinged modern-Japanese room.

anecdotes.text: The room is a sophisticated wa-modern space with jazz playing, drawing a diverse crowd and welcoming solo female diners who enjoy chatting with the owner.
A rare 100% buckwheat soba is offered daily but limited to roughly 30 portions, alongside seasonal dishes like avocado soba and plum-radish soba.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard soba is made with wheat flour and shares the same kitchen and broth as all dishes; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is finished with a specially sourced soy sauce, which in standard soba shops contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is made from bonito and mackerel flakes plus kombu, so broths and dipping sauces are fish-based and not vegan.

cuisine: Handmade soba (buckwheat noodles) with tempura; owner stone-grinds his own buckwheat
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. While the soba is made from stone-ground buckwheat, soba noodles typically include wheat flour as a binder and the dipping broth (tsuyu) is based on wheat-containing soy sauce. The tempura (including the signature anago/conger eel) uses a wheat-flour batter. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The core soba broth is built on katsuo/kombu dashi (fish), the standout dishes feature anago (conger eel) and duck (kamo nanban), and sides such as dashimaki tamago (egg) and agedashi tofu use dashi. Soy sauce-based tsuyu is used throughout. No vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation.
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区段原1-7-18
geocode_note: Geocoded from the full street address via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box, in the Danbara district of Minami Ward, just east of Hiroshima Station. Note: the target brief listed Naka Ward, but the confirmed address (matching the given Tabelog page) is Minami-ku Danbara.
neighborhood: Danbara, Minami Ward (east of Hiroshima Station)
seats_text: Around 12-20 seats: counter seating, two-person tables, and a small private room. Intimate, refined Japanese interior with jazz background music. (Tabelog lists 20; a feature article describes roughly a dozen seats.)
hours_raw: Sources differ: Tabelog lists 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 15:00); a detailed feature article lists 11:30-14:30 and 17:30-20:30, closing earlier when the day's soba runs out. Reservations available on weekdays; dinner courses require reservation.
closed_days: Wednesdays (水曜日). May also close early on any day once the handmade soba sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional owner-run soba shop with no indication of English support and cash-only payment, so basic Japanese phrases and exact-change cash will make a visit much smoother for a foreign student.
caveats: Cash only. Closes when the day's handmade soba sells out, so arriving early is wise. Dinner is course-style and requires a reservation. The target brief's stated area (Naka Ward / near A-Bomb Dome) is inaccurate: the shop is in Danbara, Minami Ward, east of Hiroshima Station.

bio.background: A small, owner-run soba specialist tucked into the Danbara neighborhood just east of Hiroshima Station. It has built a quiet reputation among soba lovers as an 'adults' hideaway' (大人の隠れ家的お店) rather than a flashy destination.
bio.specialty: Handmade soba made from buckwheat the owner stone-grinds in-house. He starts from whole, unhulled buckwheat kernels (玄蕎麦), removes the shells and impurities, then mills the flour himself with a stone mill before cutting each batch by hand. The signature dishes are cold seiro soba and a prized anago (conger eel) tempura; duck nanban and side dishes like dashimaki tamago and agedashi tofu round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to handmade soba crafted from flour the owner stone-grinds himself, beginning with carefully sourced whole buckwheat. Milling everything in-house is described as uncommon among Hiroshima soba shops.
roles: certified sake sommelier (唎き酒師) who curates the sake list

anecdotes.text: The owner is a certified sake sommelier (kikizakeshi) and pairs the soba with a curated selection of Japanese sake; the dining room has a refined Japanese feel with jazz playing in the background.
Grinding one's own buckwheat from whole kernels, with all shells and impurities removed before stone milling, is noted as rare among soba shops in Hiroshima.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles here are stone-ground buckwheat but, as is standard, are not certified gluten-free and the broth uses wheat-based soy sauce; tempura is fried in wheat batter in the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba dipping broth (tsuyu) is based on dashi and Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-based dashi (katsuo/kombu); menu centers on anago (eel), duck, and egg dishes, so plant-based diners cannot avoid animal ingredients in the standard offerings.

lead: 板蕎麦 香り家 京橋店
name_ja: 板蕎麦 香り家 京橋店
cuisine: Hand-style Yamagata country soba (itasoba); also tempura and small Japanese plates
neighborhood: 広島市南区（京橋・広島駅周辺）
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区京橋町11-11
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島市南区京橋町11 returned [132.470306, 34.393555], within the Hiroshima box; matches the Kyobashi River open-cafe location described in sources.
seats_text: 24 seats (Tabelog)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. dishes 21:15, drinks 21:30). Closed: irregular (不定休); some sources list no fixed closing day except New Year period.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); sources also note open daily except the New Year period
cultural_comfort_note: A popular riverside soba spot that often has a queue and a Japanese-language menu, so a little basic Japanese (or a translation app) and patience in line will help a foreign student feel comfortable.
gf_detail: This is a buckwheat soba house, but Japanese soba is normally blended with wheat flour and the dipping sauces (including the signature sesame dare and the soy-based tsuyu) are made with wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is documented. Treat as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: The dipping sauces are dashi/soy-based (typically fish-derived) and the set meals include egg omelet and tempura; no vegan menu is documented. Not a vegan-friendly destination.

bio.background: A soba house on the bank of the Kyobashi River, set in a striking black box-shaped building within the Kyobashi River open-cafe stretch where pastry shops and cafes also gather. Floor-to-ceiling glass frames the riverside scenery, and the shop has become a lunchtime landmark known for the queue that forms outside.
bio.origin: Specializes in Yamagata-style country soba (inaka-soba) served on the rectangular wooden board that gives 'itasoba' its name; the shop takes its presentation cue from the wooden storage boxes traditionally used to keep soba, using the board itself as the serving vessel.
bio.specialty: Stone-milled whole-grain country soba offered in your choice of thin-cut (for a smooth throat-feel that carries the broth's aroma) or thick-cut (for chew and fuller buckwheat flavor), notably paired with a sesame dipping sauce (gomadare) that is uncommon among soba shops. The signature lunch set comes with two dipping sauces, a seasonal small dish, egg omelet, tempura, refillable rice, and soba-cha to finish.
bio.philosophy: Emphasizes hospitality and care in serving whole-grain country soba to guests.

anecdotes.text: Reporters describe a black box-shaped building beside the Kyobashi River with full-height windows overlooking the water, and a sesame dipping sauce that is rare for a soba shop.
Local coverage notes the soba is served on the preserving board itself, in the spirit of hospitality, using stone-milled whole-grain country soba.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauces are soy-sauce and dashi based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

caveats: Lunchtime queues are common and the shop generally does not take reservations for the line; hours are listed as irregular with minor variation between sources (one lists dinner ending 21:00). Operated by FOODGATE Co., Ltd. as one of several Kaoriya branches, so not an independent single-location shop.

cuisine: フレンチ（地元食材・季節野菜を生かしたコース料理）
neighborhood: 広島市中区南千田西町
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区南千田西町9-20-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from current (post-May-2025 relocation) address 広島市中区南千田西町9-20-1 via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box. Note: the linked Tabelog page reflects the former Nobori-cho location, which the restaurant left in May 2025.
seats_text: 小規模なオーナーシェフの店。最大6名まで対応の個室あり（移転前の幟町店は18〜22席程度のカウンター＋テーブルの小箱だった）。現店舗の正確な席数は未確認。
closed_days: 水曜日（夏季・年末年始の休業あり）
cultural_comfort_note: An owner-chef French restaurant with set courses where reservations are essential; the official website has English/Chinese/Korean pages, but in-restaurant service is mostly in Japanese, so a Japanese-speaking friend or some basic phrases will help you communicate dietary needs.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a French restaurant whose signature includes house-made bread (and a bread reportedly infused with soil extract), and French sauces commonly use flour. There is no information on dedicated gluten-free preparation, so a celiac diner must call ahead and discuss the full tasting menu directly with the chef.
vegan_detail: Strongly vegetable-forward French cooking — the chef sources organic seasonal vegetables directly from contracted farmers and is known for restrained, ingredient-led plates (e.g. simple spinach purée). However, the kitchen uses Seto Inland Sea seafood, dairy (house-made sour cream) and likely animal stocks, and no vegan or vegetarian course is advertised. Vegetarian/vegan accommodation may be possible by prior arrangement but must be confirmed by reservation; it is not a vegan destination.

bio.background: Owner-chef Sumita trained in France and at French restaurants in Tokyo before opening his own place in central Hiroshima in November 2008. He spent years working within the tradition of soil-and-terroir French cooking before establishing his own kitchen.
bio.specialty: Seasonal French courses built around organic vegetables sent directly from contracted farmers, paired with seafood from the Seto Inland Sea and produce from the surrounding mountains. The cooking is deliberately restrained, letting single ingredients speak — a famously simple spinach purée, house-made sour cream churned from milk, and an in-house bread that nods to the 'cuisine of the soil' philosophy.
bio.philosophy: To draw out the full natural character of local ingredients with minimal intervention, so that a vegetable tastes intensely of itself rather than of technique.

anecdotes.text: After running the restaurant in Hiroshima's Nobori-cho district for over fifteen years, the chef relocated the restaurant in May 2025 to a quieter spot in Minami-senda Nishi-machi, continuing the same organic-vegetable, terroir-driven French cooking.

Diners describe a seemingly plain spinach purée as 'startlingly flavorful' in its mineral intensity, with a signature house bread made with a soil extract — a hallmark of the chef's soil-and-terroir French training.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward cooking using organic seasonal vegetables delivered directly from contracted farmers, which gives the kitchen flexibility to build plant-centric plates by prior arrangement.
Small owner-run kitchen serving a set tasting course, so the chef personally controls each plate and can discuss dietary needs when you reserve.

caveats: The linked Tabelog page reflects the former Nobori-cho (幟町) location; the restaurant relocated in May 2025 to 広島市中区南千田西町9-20-1, confirmed via the official site. Current exact seat count, payment, and detailed hours for the new location are partly inferred from the official site plus aggregator listings of the prior location; confirm seats, hours, and any vegetarian/gluten-free accommodation directly when reserving. This location is in central Naka-ku but is a bit south of the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Park core.

lead: 広島牡蠣と鉄板焼き 一娯一笑 いちごいちえ
name_ja: 広島牡蠣と鉄板焼き 一娯一笑 いちごいちえ
name_en: Hiroshima Kaki to Teppanyaki Ichigo Ichie
cuisine: Hiroshima oysters, teppanyaki, and okonomiyaki — a small local teppan izakaya
neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima nightlife district)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町7-18 古本ビル
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区流川町7-18 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box (Nagarekawa nightlife district).
seats_text: 28 seats total (22 at tables, 6 at the counter); group capacity around 20
hours_raw: Mon-Fri & day before holidays: 18:00-23:55 (L.O. 23:00); Sat: 17:00-23:55 (L.O. 23:00); Sun & holidays: 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Many Sundays closed but may open the Sunday before a holiday.
closed_days: Irregular holidays; many Sundays closed (may open the Sunday before a public holiday)
