cultural_comfort_note: A cozy locals' teppan izakaya tucked in the Nagarekawa backstreets; no documented English support, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app, and note it is an all-smoking, adults-only venue.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house centers on Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (wheat batter) and teppanyaki seasoned with regular soy-based sauces, so most dishes contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around raw and grilled oysters, conger eel (anago), Hiroshima beef, sea urchin, and teppanyaki/okonomiyaki that use eggs, fish, and wheat-soy sauces. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
caveats: Hours are irregular and Sundays are often closed; same-day reservations are best made by phone. There is a 480 yen per-person seat charge, a 2-hour seating limit with a one-drink-per-person minimum, the venue is all-smoking, and minors are not admitted. The weekly hours listed use the 23:55 closing time noted by the venue; last orders are earlier (23:00 weekdays/Sat, 22:00 Sun/holidays).

bio.background: A small, owner-run teppanyaki spot in the Nagarekawa backstreets of central Hiroshima, open since August 2009. It is run by the owner with a small team, the kind of intimate counter-and-table place where regulars and travelers sit side by side.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima's local bounty cooked on the iron plate — premium local oysters (including the prized Kanawa brand), uni-spinach, Hiroshima beef, conger eel, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.
bio.philosophy: The house motto is 安心・丁寧・笑顔 — reassurance, care, and a smile — with the aim of delivering 'the deliciousness of Hiroshima' in one sitting to locals and visitors alike.

anecdotes.text: The name Ichigo Ichie plays on the Japanese saying ichi-go ichi-e, treasuring each once-in-a-lifetime encounter, fitting for a small place that welcomes both neighborhood regulars and travelers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes use wheat: okonomiyaki batter and teppanyaki sauces are wheat-based, and the iron plate is shared across all dishes, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and oyster dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy-based sauces (e.g. butter-soy, ponzu), which typically contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single teppan grills oysters, eel, beef, and egg-based okonomiyaki, so any vegetable item would share a surface with seafood and meat; no separate preparation is documented.

cuisine: Seafood diner (shokudo) — oysters, fried oyster/shrimp set meals, curry, noodles
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima Island), near Omotesando shopping street and Itsukushima Shrine
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町北之町浜566-1 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full street address returned coordinates within the Hiroshima bounding box, on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: 70 seats, with table and tatami seating
hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sat, Sun 10:30-16:30; Closed Friday
closed_days: Friday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual island diner where you can point at a set-meal photo and pay by card or cash; no English service is documented, so a phrasebook or translation app helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature dishes are breaded fried oysters and fried shrimp (wheat batter/breadcrumbs), curry, and noodles, and Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused diner; its core menu is built around oysters, fried shrimp, and other seafood. No vegan dishes are documented.
caveats: Closed on Fridays. Reservations are not accepted; it is walk-in only. Daytime hours only (closes 16:30).

bio.background: A casual island diner on Miyajima serving Hiroshima's celebrated oysters in homely set-meal form — most notably fried-oyster set meals — alongside fried shrimp, curry, and noodle dishes, just a short walk from the approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Fried oyster set meal (kaki-fry teishoku) and other oyster dishes

cuisine: Miyajima regional cuisine — fried oysters (kaki fry), oyster bowls and oyster udon, grilled and rice-bowl conger eel (anago), plus donburi, curry, udon and soba
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near Itsukushima Shrine and Miyajima Aquarium
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町12
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町12 via GSI; coordinates land on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 85 seats total — 40 tatami (zashiki) seats and 45 table seats
hours_raw: 10:30 - 17:00; 不定休 (irregular closures)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures — no fixed regular closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, family-run Miyajima eatery with plastic food samples displayed out front so you can point to what you want; a little basic Japanese helps but the visual menu makes ordering easy for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature fried oysters use wheat batter/breadcrumbs, anago and donburi are served with wheat-based soy sauce and tare, and the menu leans heavily on udon, soba, curry and katsu — all wheat-containing. No gluten-free preparations or dedicated equipment are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around oysters, conger eel and other seafood, plus egg-and-meat donburi (katsu-don, oyako-don) and dashi/soy-based broths. No vegan dishes are listed and dashi and tare contain fish.
caveats: Hours are 10:30–17:00 daily (lunch/daytime only) with irregular closing days, so confirm by phone before a special trip. Cash only — no cards, e-money or QR payment. Seat count, hours and payment details are as listed on aggregator and the restaurant's official site at time of verification.

bio.background: A long-standing casual eatery on Miyajima island, set a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine in front of Kiyomori Shrine and just about a minute from the Miyajima Aquarium. It serves the island's classic local fare in a traditional, clean dining room with both tatami and table seating.
bio.specialty: Miyajima specialties — fried oysters and other oyster dishes drawn from the nearby waters that have long been one of Hiroshima's great oyster-farming grounds, alongside conger eel (anago) served grilled or over rice with clam miso soup.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on its location: the restaurant notes the seas around Miyajima have long thrived as a Hiroshima oyster-farming region, which is reflected in its oyster-forward menu.
The dining room keeps both tatami (zashiki) and table seating, and the house welcomes group bookings including children's groups, senior citizens' clubs and welfare groups.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago and rice-bowl dishes are served with Japanese soy sauce and tare, which are standard wheat-containing seasonings; no gluten-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on oysters, conger eel and other seafood plus meat and egg donburi, with fish-based dashi broths; there are no documented vegan items or separate vegan preparation.

cuisine: Kissaten / self-roasted coffee house (純喫茶・自家焙煎珈琲), morning sets, sandwiches
neighborhood: Takaramachi, Naka-ku, Hiroshima (near Hijiyama-bashi; about 1.5 km south of the Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区宝町6-15
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区宝町6-15 returned [132.462082, 34.384865], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 22 seats (5 counter, 17 table)
hours_raw: 8:00〜18:30 (morning service 8:00-11:00); closed Sundays and holidays
closed_days: Sundays and national holidays (日曜・祝日)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood coffee house where service is in Japanese and the warm, regulars-focused hospitality is best enjoyed with a few polite Japanese phrases; pointing at the menu and a smile will carry you through.
gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese coffee house whose food centers on house-baked thick-cut bread, sandwiches and toast — all wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and the morning set is built around bread, so it is not suitable for someone requiring gluten-free meals. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free if you simply order a drink.
vegan_detail: Black self-roasted nel-drip coffee and plain tea are vegan, and an oat-milk iced coffee is offered as a plant-milk option. Food, however, is built around bread with butter/jam, salad and sandwiches, and the signature cream coffees use dairy cream, so there is no documented fully vegan meal. A vegan could drink the black coffee or oat-milk coffee but should ask about any food item rather than assume.

bio.background: Teranishi Coffee was opened in 1978 by Takayuki Teranishi after he spent about two years training at the renowned Nishimura Coffee in Kobe, where he learned to brew and absorbed a deep commitment to craft. When a director of the Kobe shop encouraged him to strike out on his own, he returned to Hiroshima and founded his own coffee house, which has now served the city for more than 45 years.
bio.specialty: Beans roasted in-house every day on a Probat roaster and brewed by hand through an original nel (cloth) filter, served alongside house-baked thick-cut morning bread; the simple morning set pairs that bread with salad, and the kitchen also pours an oat-milk iced coffee and dairy-cream coffees in custom-made ceramic cups.
bio.philosophy: Teranishi distinguishes between merely 'getting tasks done' (こなす) and truly 'hosting' a guest (もてなす), guided by the question of how many people one can make happy in a lifetime. He keeps no written service manual, preferring to pass hospitality on person to person, and spends an hour each evening reflecting on the day's service so he can do better; staff learn regulars' preferred seats, newspapers and drinks, and by a third visit a guest is treated as a regular.

anecdotes.text: Bread for the morning set is made with salt rather than sugar, and the accompanying jams (strawberry, blueberry, apricot) were chosen only after extensive tasting.
The owner adjusts milk ratios by the dropper and had custom ceramic cups made to bring out the best in each coffee.
Staff are known for learning a customer's preferred seat, newspaper and order, treating guests as regulars by the third visit.

positives.text: An oat-milk iced coffee is offered, giving a plant-milk option alongside black self-roasted coffee.

caveats: Cash only (no cards, e-money or QR pay) and no reservations. Food is wheat-bread based, so not suitable for gluten-free diners; vegans are limited to black or oat-milk coffee. Service is Japanese-language and oriented to regulars.

cuisine: 昭和レトロな老舗純喫茶
gf_detail: A traditional kissaten serving coffee, sandwiches, pasta (okonomiyaki-style spaghetti), curries and pilafs — wheat is central to the food menu (bread, pasta) and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for those needing strict gluten-free.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu. Most food items contain egg, dairy, meat or fish (morning sets with eggs and sandwiches, grilled-meat lunches, salmon/tarako pasta). Black siphon coffee and possibly plain toast could be ordered, but nothing is documented as plant-based and staff allergy/vegan handling is unknown — a vegan would need to ask in advance.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-30
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 大手町一丁目4番30号, within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 広島市中区大手町（市役所・紙屋町エリア、平和記念公園のすぐ近く）
seats_text: Seat count not documented in available sources; described as a small intimate kissaten.
hours_raw: 7:00～18:00（フードL.O.17:00、ドリンクL.O.17:30）。モーニングは朝7時から。
closed_days: 年中無休（定休日なし）
cultural_comfort_note: A classic local kissaten with little to no English and an all-smoking interior, so a foreign student will be most comfortable visiting with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend who can help order.
caveats: All seats permit smoking (全席喫煙可) — the entire interior allows smoking. Cash only, no reservations. Food revolves around coffee, sandwiches, pasta and lunch plates; nothing is documented as gluten-free or vegan.

bio.background: A Showa-era kissaten in central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, tucked one street back from the streetcar avenue in Otemachi. The original shop opened in 1970, and this second location followed about a decade later; it is now run by at least the third generation of the same family.
bio.specialty: House-roasted coffee brewed cup-by-cup in a siphon (with free refills), morning sets, daily lunches, and a signature okonomiyaki-style spaghetti.
bio.philosophy: Preserving the calm, nostalgic world of the traditional pure coffee house — red velvet sofas, brick interior, classical music, and newspapers and magazines to linger over.

anecdotes.text: The name carries the family's musical heritage: 'Tsubai' is German for 'two' (this being the second shop), and 'G-sen' comes from 'Air on the G String,' a reference to the second-generation owner who played the violin.
The method for cooking the lunchtime spaghetti noodles is a closely guarded family recipe, developed by the previous generation to keep a freshly-cooked texture even during the busy lunch rush.
The shop opens at 7am and fills with regulars from the morning, with classical music playing while siphon coffee is brewed to order with free refills.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around wheat (sandwiches/bread, pasta, lunch plates) with no documented gluten-free handling; high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food items center on egg, dairy, meat and fish (egg sandwiches, grilled-meat lunches, salmon/tarako pasta); no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: 広島名物 牡蠣料理・瀬戸内海鮮（Hiroshima oyster cuisine and Seto Inland Sea seafood)
gf_detail: This is a traditional oyster and seafood restaurant with no documented gluten-free program. Dishes rely heavily on Japanese soy sauce and ponzu (both contain wheat), fried oysters (kaki fry) are breaded in wheat flour, and many courses include tempura or wheat-based seasonings. There is no published information about gluten-free menus, dedicated fryers, or cross-contamination controls. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe without a careful direct conversation, and even then options would be very limited (e.g., plain grilled or raw oysters without sauce).
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built entirely around oysters and Seto Inland Sea seafood; the signature dishes are raw oysters, grilled oysters, fried oysters, and oyster rice. Sauces use fish-based dashi and soy sauce. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and the concept is fundamentally seafood-centric, so it is not a suitable destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1丁目地先
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市中区大手町1丁目 via GSI; lands on the Motoyasu River bank just south of Peace Memorial Park, consistent with the moored oyster-boat location. Within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 大手町 / 平和記念公園・原爆ドーム南側（元安川の川船）
seats_text: 84 seats across two floors: 1F "瀬戸" (Seto, casual) and 2F "和久" (Wakyu, private-room dining), all with river views.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00 (L.O. 14:00 on 2F Wakyu / 14:30 on 1F Seto); Dinner 17:00 (L.O. 20:00). Open daily.
closed_days: 年末年始のみ（Year-end/New Year holidays only)
cultural_comfort_note: A well-known sightseeing-area restaurant with an English website and menu and experience hosting international visitors, so a foreign student can dine comfortably, though it is an upscale sit-down experience rather than a casual budget spot.
caveats: This is an upscale, course-oriented oyster restaurant aboard a moored boat on the Motoyasu River; expect higher prices, especially on the 2F Wakyu private-room side. Lunch last orders are around 14:00-14:30. It is not a budget or quick-bite option and is unsuitable for vegans/vegetarians and unreliable for strict gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Kanawa traces its roots to 1867 (Keio 3), when Hisataro Miho began oyster farming as a family enterprise in Hiroshima. By the postwar years the third generation opened fresh raw-oyster counters in Tokyo and Osaka department stores, introducing Hiroshima oysters to the capital for the first time. In 1963 the family opened this floating restaurant, reviving the Edo-period tradition of the 'kakibune' (oyster boat) that had once been used to sell and serve oysters along the rivers.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served raw, grilled, fried, and as oyster rice, alongside Seto Inland Sea seafood, eaten aboard a traditional moored oyster boat on the Motoyasu River.
bio.philosophy: The house prioritizes safety and quality by sourcing oysters exclusively from the clean, high-salinity waters off Nomijima rather than the inner bay. Moored opposite Peace Memorial Park, it also frames the act of sharing Hiroshima oysters as a small contribution to lasting peace.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant grew directly out of a six-generation oyster-farming family that began in 1867; the 'kakibune' boat format was deliberately chosen as a way to popularize oyster cuisine, reviving an Edo-era sales tradition.
All oysters are farmed off Nomijima (Fukae) in waters chosen for high salinity and clarity, used because they meet the standards for oysters eaten raw.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's oyster and seafood dishes use Japanese soy sauce, ponzu, and dashi-based seasonings, which standardly contain wheat; fried oysters are breaded in wheat flour. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is oyster- and seafood-centered with fish-based dashi; there is no documented vegan or vegetarian preparation.

cuisine: 貝料理専門の居酒屋（牡蠣・全国の貝、海鮮、日本酒）
neighborhood: 薬研堀（中区、胡町・八丁堀近くの繁華街）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀2-11
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県広島市中区薬研堀2-11 returned [132.464676, 34.391315], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Around 31-33 seats: counter (about 11), tables, sunken kotatsu seating, and a small private room (2-5 guests). Sources vary slightly between 31 and 33.
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 18:00-01:30 (L.O. 01:00); Fri-Sat 18:00-02:30 (L.O. 02:00); some sources list 18:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:00). Closed Sundays and holidays (may open on some holidays).
closed_days: 日曜・祝日（祝日は営業の場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A late-night local shellfish izakaya in a busy nightlife district; little English is likely, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, and it is a hands-on spot best enjoyed if you eat oysters and other shellfish.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a shellfish izakaya where most dishes are seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and items like the oyster kamameshi (rice) and various sauces are not gluten-free. No celiac-safe accommodation is documented; cross-contamination from a shared kitchen should be assumed.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is shellfish (oysters, sazae, scallops and other shellfish nationwide) plus seafood, with dishes such as shellfish sashimi, grilled oysters, shellfish-dashi chawanmushi (egg and fish/shellfish stock) and oyster kamameshi. There is no documented plant-based menu.
caveats: Two slightly different hour sets appear across sources (some list closing at 24:00, others at 01:30/02:30); evening start of 18:00 and Sunday closure are consistent. Seat count is reported as 31 in one source and 33 in another. Payment methods (cards, IC, QR) are listed on the Tabelog page but not confirmed on the venue's own listing.

bio.background: A small specialist shellfish izakaya in Hiroshima's Yakkenbori nightlife quarter, devoted to kai (shellfish) from across Japan. The team travels directly to markets from Hokkaido in the north to Fukuoka in the south, building relationships with trusted suppliers to bring in carefully chosen shellfish daily.
bio.philosophy: Showcase seasonal shellfish at their peak with minimal preparation, offering each variety raw, grilled, or simmered so guests can taste its natural aroma and umami; staff suggest pairings such as oysters with white wine, raw shellfish sashimi with sake, and grilled shellfish with wine.
bio.specialty: Shellfish in every form: shellfish sashimi (a signature), grilled oysters, oyster kamameshi (clay-pot rice), and a shellfish-dashi chawanmushi. A standout is the Oki Islands rock oyster called Seikai, which the shop says it handles exclusively.

anecdotes.text: The owners go directly to markets from Hokkaido down to Fukuoka to source from trusted suppliers, bringing in selected shellfish fresh each day.
The shop highlights the Oki Islands rock oyster 'Seikai' as a dish it alone handles, and recommends pairing oysters with white wine and raw shellfish with sake.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shellfish sashimi and grilled shellfish are served and seasoned in the Japanese style, which standardly uses wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely shellfish- and seafood-based (oysters, shellfish sashimi, fish/shellfish dashi), so any plant items would share equipment with seafood.

lead: ひろしまテッパン produced by 餃子家 龍
name_ja: ひろしまテッパン produced by 餃子家 龍
name_en: Hiroshima Teppan produced by Gyozaya Ryu
cuisine: Teppanyaki & Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki izakaya (with a rice-flour gluten-free okonomiyaki option)
gf_detail: There is one dedicated gluten-free dish: a rice-flour okonomiyaki (米粉使用グルテンフリーお好み焼き, around 1,200 yen). It is built on a rice-flour sheet instead of the usual wheat batter, uses thick chewy rice-flour noodles in place of wheat soba, and is finished with a wheat-free sauce so that people with a wheat allergy can eat it. This is unusual and genuinely useful for GF diners. However, the rest of the menu is heavily wheat-based (standard okonomiyaki, gyoza, many teppan items), and the GF okonomiyaki is cooked on the same shared teppan griddle as wheat dishes, so it is a GF-friendly option rather than a dedicated GF kitchen. Strict celiacs should confirm preparation when ordering.
vegan_detail: The menu lists a vegetable gyoza described as vegan (野菜餃子・vegan, around 500 yen). Beyond that, this is a meat-and-seafood izakaya: okonomiyaki contains egg and pork, and most teppan dishes use beef, pork, chicken, seafood, oysters and butter, so vegan choices are minimal and there is no dedicated vegan menu.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町7-12 タナカビル2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区袋町7-12 returned [132.460297, 34.391495], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in central Hiroshima's Fukuro-cho, next to Fukuromachi Park in the Hondori/Tatemachi shopping district, a short walk from the A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 袋町（本通・立町エリア、袋町公園隣）
seats_text: 55 seats including teppan-view counter, table seats, sunken hori-gotatsu seating, and private rooms (groups up to about 45).
hours_raw: 月～日、祝日、祝前日: 17:00～22:00（料理L.O. 21:30、ドリンクL.O. 21:30）／定休日：なし（年中無休）
closed_days: None (open year-round; the official listing notes 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, dinner-only izakaya in the heart of Hiroshima's shopping district; staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the picture menu will go a long way, and you can mention 小麦アレルギー (wheat allergy) when ordering the rice-flour okonomiyaki.
caveats: Reported hours differ slightly across sources: the official site and HotPepper list 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30), while a TV feature and Tabelog mentioned 17:00-24:00 / 23:00; the official 17:00-22:00 is used here. Dinner only. The gluten-free okonomiyaki is a real, documented option but is prepared on a shared teppan with no documented dedicated GF equipment, so it is not safe for strict celiacs without confirming.

bio.background: This central-Hiroshima teppan izakaya is produced by Gyozaya Ryu, a Hiroshima gyoza specialist that runs as the direct outlet of a gyoza-wrapper maker (餃子皮メーカー直営). It opened on June 30, 2023 in a wood-grained, old-school izakaya-style space next to Fukuromachi Park, pairing local teppan favorites and the house gyoza with Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and house gyoza alongside teppan dishes, with a signature rice-flour gluten-free okonomiyaki that the kitchen developed so people with wheat allergies can enjoy the local specialty.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Hiroshima ingredients and the city's okonomiyaki and gyoza culture, while making the dish accessible to wheat-allergic guests through a rice-flour, wheat-free-sauce version.

anecdotes.text: Their gluten-free okonomiyaki is built on a rice-flour sheet rather than wheat batter and layered with thick, chewy white rice-flour noodles, then finished with a wheat-free sauce; cooked slowly without pressing, it comes out juicy with a natural sweetness from the rice flour.
The operator describes itself as a gyoza-wrapper manufacturer's own restaurant serving 'Hiroshima gyoza born and raised in Hiroshima.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: The gluten-free rice-flour okonomiyaki is cooked on the same shared teppan griddle used for wheat-based okonomiyaki, gyoza and other dishes; no dedicated griddle area or separate preparation is documented, so cross-contamination is likely for strict celiacs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen specifically uses a wheat-free sauce for the gluten-free okonomiyaki so that guests with a wheat allergy can eat it (ソースは小麦が入っていないものを使う).

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant created the rice-flour okonomiyaki and wheat-free sauce explicitly so that people with a wheat allergy can eat it safely (小麦アレルギーの人でも安心して食べられる), indicating awareness of wheat-allergy needs, though no formal cross-contact protocol is documented.

positives.text: Offers a genuine rice-flour, wheat-free-sauce gluten-free okonomiyaki using a rice-flour sheet and rice-flour noodles, letting wheat-sensitive diners try the Hiroshima specialty.
A vegetable gyoza is listed as vegan, giving plant-based diners at least one option.

cuisine: Self-roast coffee house and cafe in a renovated Taisho-era warehouse; morning sets, pasta, cakes and specialty coffee
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), in the machiya backstreets near the five-story pagoda and Itsukushima Shrine
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町407
geocode_note: GSI address search on 広島県廿日市市宮島町407 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: Around 50 seats across a two-story interior; spacious and relaxed.
hours_raw: Open roughly 8:30/10:00-18:00/19:00 daily (sources vary slightly); morning service available; open year-round (無休).
closed_days: Open year-round (無休); no regular closing day.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed tourist-area cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; some English signage and a foreign-visitor-friendly setting, but staff English may be limited.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature dishes are pasta (wheat) and cakes/scones (wheat flour), and bread is served with the morning and lunch sets, so the core menu is wheat-based. Assume no gluten-free options unless you confirm directly.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The cafe is coffee- and dessert-focused; signature savory dishes (Miyajima oyster and beef pasta) contain seafood and meat, and cakes typically use dairy/eggs. Black coffee and the Hiroshima lemon-honey syrup drink may suit a relaxed plant-based diner, but ask about specific items.

bio.background: Sarasvati occupies a warehouse surviving from the Taisho era, renovated into a calm two-story cafe tucked into the quiet machiya backstreets just inland from the five-story pagoda and Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in 2009 and is run as the cafe arm of Itsuki Coffee, a Miyajima specialty-coffee roaster.
bio.philosophy: The space is conceived as a special place dedicated to enjoying coffee: beans are roasted in-house on a 3kg direct-fire roaster, with monthly rotating single-origin specialty coffees served in a deliberately serene, unhurried atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee, a morning service, and a signature lunch of Miyajima oyster and braised beef-shin pasta served with bread; cakes such as a rich cheesecake with raspberry sauce.

anecdotes.text: The cafe roasts its own beans on a 3kg direct-fire roaster on site and rotates the featured single-origin specialty coffee each month.
The building is a renovated warehouse remaining from the Taisho period, set in the quiet lanes behind the main approach near the pagoda and shrine.
The standout lunch is a hearty pasta of Miyajima oyster and braised beef shin served with bread.

positives.text: All seats are non-smoking.

caveats: Reported hours differ across sources (Tabelog shows roughly 9:30-19:00, the official Itsuki page shows 10:00-18:00, and one summary cites 8:30-19:00); confirm same-day hours. Two phone numbers appear (0829-44-2266 and 0829-44-0611). Seat count (~50) is from listing data. No vegan or gluten-free program is documented anywhere fetched, so dietary travelers should ask directly.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（鉄板焼き・自家製麺）
neighborhood: 広島駅前（松原町・ひろしまお好み物語 駅前ひろば）
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町10-1 広島フルフォーカスビル6F ひろしまお好み物語 駅前ひろば
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (広島市南区松原町10-1); lands at the Hiroshima Full Focus Building near JR Hiroshima Station, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Counter-focused seating; official complex listing and Retty cite about 28 seats (Tabelog lists 15). Small stall within the Ekimae Hiroba okonomiyaki hall.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly counter-style okonomiyaki stall inside a tourist-oriented food hall by the station; pointing at the menu and a few words of Japanese will carry you, though full English support is not guaranteed.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built on a thin wheat-flour batter and layered with house-made wheat (Chinese-style) noodles. The house "crystal" sauce and standard toppings also make this unsuitable for those avoiding gluten. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Core dishes use egg, pork, oysters, conger eel, beef offal, fish-cake (gansu), bonito/fish-based dashi and savory sauce. No vegan okonomiyaki or plant-based option is documented.
caveats: Seat count differs between sources (official/Retty ~28 counter seats vs Tabelog 15). Owner/chef name not confirmed in source text, so left null. Instagram is the only "official" web presence; the complex page serves as the official listing.

bio.background: A Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked into a counter spot in the station-front food hall, built around an unusually all-in-house approach. The team runs its own dedicated noodle factory, the "Sozo Noodle Factory," pressing fresh okonomiyaki noodles from domestic wheat for a chewy, springy bite you do not get from bought-in noodles.
bio.specialty: Their signature is a tall, fluffy dome-shaped okonomiyaki finished with a free mound of grated daikon on top, which lightens the whole thing and plays against the sauce. Beyond the standard pork-and-noodle build, the kitchen leans into Hiroshima and Seto Inland specialties: oyster butter-yaki, rare beef shoulder (koune), miso-cooked beef lung, white-grilled conger eel, fried fish-cake (gansu), and a slow offal stew of several cuts in an original broth.
bio.philosophy: The cooks describe themselves as iron-plate craftsmen who use no chemical seasonings at all, aiming to let the natural flavor of the ingredients carry the dish. To that end they make their own "crystal" sauce, blending Seto Inland oysters and Hiroshima lemon to pair with okonomiyaki rather than relying on a generic sweet sauce; the lighter, less-sweet sauce is what makes the grated-daikon topping work so well.

anecdotes.text: The shop makes its own okonomiyaki-specific noodles in a dedicated noodle factory using domestic wheat, prizing a chewy, mochi-like texture in the fresh noodle.
Their original 'crystal sauce' is built around Seto Inland oysters and Hiroshima lemon, made less sweet so a free grated-daikon topping can sit on top and keep the okonomiyaki refreshing.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared griddle is used for pork, oysters, eel, beef offal and egg, so any item will share surfaces with animal products.

positives.text: The kitchen states it uses no chemical seasonings and makes its own sauce and noodles in-house, so ingredient sourcing is unusually transparent.

lead: 手作りのパン 河内ベーカリー
name_ja: 手作りのパン 河内ベーカリー
cuisine: 老舗の個人経営ベーカリー（無添加パン・サンドイッチ。シナモントーストが名物）
neighborhood: 中区幟町（八丁堀・原爆ドーム/平和記念公園エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町7-18
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区幟町7-18 returned [132.465378, 34.394993], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: No seating — takeout only.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 08:00–18:00; closed Sat, Sun, and holidays; also closed 2nd and 4th Wednesdays. (Some local listings note hours as roughly 07:00–19:00; the most detailed listing gives 08:00–18:00.)
closed_days: 土日祝日（さらに第2・第4水曜日も休み）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny old-school neighborhood bakery where you just point at what you want and pay in cash (PayPay also works), so almost no Japanese is needed, though an English menu is unlikely.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour bakery; bread, sandwiches and pastries are all made from wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: A classic Japanese bakery whose signature items include egg sandwiches, ham-and-egg mix sandwiches and curry bread, which are not vegan. Plain breads may be vegan-friendly but milk, butter and egg are common in Japanese bakery bread, so a strict vegan would need to ask about each item; no vegan labeling is documented.
caveats: Reported opening hours vary slightly between listings (08:00–18:00 vs. roughly 07:00–19:00); the bakery also takes irregular 2nd/4th Wednesday closures, so confirm before a special trip. No official website was found; an Instagram account (@kouchi.bakary) is referenced.

bio.background: A handmade-bread bakery in the Nobori-cho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, within walking distance of the Hatchobori area and the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome. It has been a fixture of the neighborhood for more than half a century, with listings citing roughly 60 years of operation, and it has kept making bread the old-fashioned way the whole time.
bio.specialty: Its best-known item is cinnamon toast, treated locally almost as a Hiroshima B-grade-gourmet specialty, alongside a hearty mix sandwich (toasted bread with egg on one side and cabbage-mayo, egg and ham in the center), curry bread, egg sandwiches and a wide range of everyday loaves and pastries.
bio.philosophy: The shop is defined by a commitment to additive-free (無添加) baking and to preserving traditional, old-fashioned flavors over the decades.

anecdotes.text: Local food writers describe it as a beloved, retro neighborhood bakery — the kind of long-standing local spot people walk past for years before finally stepping in and discovering just how many kinds of bread it stocks.
The cinnamon toast is singled out as a signature treat and is talked about as a Hiroshima B-grade gourmet item, while the volume-packed mix sandwich is recommended as an ideal lunch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional wheat-flour bakery; flour is handled throughout, so the environment is unsuitable for anyone avoiding gluten and there is no gluten-free offering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items include egg and ham-and-egg sandwiches and curry bread; egg, dairy and meat are used in the kitchen, and plain breads may share equipment, so vegans should ask about specific items.

cuisine: 地元食材のイタリアン・洋食＆ケーキ（邸宅レストラン＆カフェ）
neighborhood: 宮島・厳島神社/桟橋エリア（町家通り、築100年超の邸宅）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町527-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町527-1 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], which lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box. Kept per Miyajima guidance.
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A refined sit-down restaurant in a restored historic mansion; menus and service are mainly in Japanese, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help a foreign student feel comfortable ordering courses.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The course menus center on seasonal pasta (wheat) and Western dishes with sauces, and desserts (tiramisu, chocolate cake) contain wheat/dairy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is mentioned anywhere.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Courses are built around Seto Inland Sea seafood (including the local conger eel/anago) and Japanese beef, with pasta and dairy-rich desserts. No plant-based course or substitution is mentioned.

bio.background: Les Clos occupies a mansion completed in 1912 (Meiji 45), a residence now more than a century old that has been carefully renovated into a restaurant and cafe. Original architectural details such as the latticed shoji screens and carved transom panels were preserved, and the dining rooms look out onto a Japanese garden that changes with the seasons.
bio.origin: 宮島（廿日市市宮島町）, on Miyajima island a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the pier
bio.specialty: Western and Italian cooking built on Seto Inland Sea produce, including seafood such as the local conger eel, alongside Japanese-beef steaks and house-made cakes and desserts
bio.philosophy: To let Japanese tradition and Western modern design coexist in one space, pairing the heritage of the old house with cuisine that showcases seasonal local ingredients of the Seto Inland Sea.

anecdotes.text: The building dates to 1912 and retains period features like its shoji and transom woodwork, giving diners the feel of eating inside a preserved Meiji-era home with a seasonal garden view.
Set along Miyajima's machiya street, a roughly 7-15 minute walk from the Miyajima pier, it pairs the historic setting with seasonal lunch and dinner courses.

caveats: Official site lists lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O.14:00) and dinner 17:00-20:00 (L.O.19:00); Tabelog shows wider windows (11:30-17:00 / 17:30-21:00). hours_week reflects the official-site times. Chef name reading (Isamu Kurokoshi) is from the official site's transcription; the operating company is 株式会社黒きん. Closures are irregular (不定休) — confirm before visiting.

cuisine: Japanese — conger eel (anago) and oyster dishes
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), a back alley off the Omotesando shopping street near Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen centers on anago (conger eel) and oysters served with a wheat-based soy-sauce braising/finishing sauce, plus miso soup and oyster fry (battered/breaded in wheat flour). No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented, and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or allergen handling. Travelers with celiac disease should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu is built around fish and seafood (conger eel, oysters) and pork (koune/pork neck), and sauces rely on fish-derived dashi and soy sauce. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町531-4
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building level on Miyajima island; within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 9 seats total — 6 at the counter and 3 at tables, spaced apart, on the second floor.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00. Closed Tuesdays. (One listing shows dinner as 16:00-18:30; sources vary, so confirm dinner timing in advance.)
closed_days: Tuesdays (sources also note occasionally irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter-and-table spot run by a small local crew with an open kitchen; a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will go a long way, though English support is not documented.
caveats: Dinner hours differ between listings (17:00-21:00 vs 16:00-18:30) — confirm before an evening visit. Closures can be irregular beyond the standard Tuesday holiday.

bio.background: A small, hidden-gem eatery tucked into one of the quiet back alleys that branch off Miyajima's bustling Omotesando shopping street, just a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine. On the second floor behind glass sliding doors, it offers a calm retreat from the crowds, with only six counter seats and three table seats.
bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) and oysters prepared as Miyajima home-style rice bowls. The house signature is an anago toro rice and an anago hitsumabushi — and unusually for Miyajima, the conger eel here is gently stewed rather than grilled, leaving it fluffy and tender in a savory braising sauce, paired with grated yam (tororo) for a mellow finish. Oyster dishes, including oyster fry, round out the seasonal local menu.
bio.philosophy: An open kitchen lets guests watch the dishes being put together, and the room is finished with quiet, modern Japanese touches — naguri-textured flooring patterned like flowing water and bentwood-style rattan chairs — for a refined but unfussy feel.

anecdotes.text: Where most Miyajima anago shops grill the eel, this kitchen stews it instead, so the conger eel comes out soft and fluffy in a flavorful braising sauce.
Lunch here runs roughly half the price of comparable anago specialists in the area, making it an easygoing under-2,000-yen meal.
The space is designed as a bright, refined hideaway with naguri-finished flooring evoking flowing water and an open kitchen you can watch.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature stewed anago is finished with a savory braising/soy-style sauce, which in Japanese cooking is conventionally wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around conger eel, oysters and pork, with fish-dashi sauces, so vegan diners cannot be accommodated and cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

lead: お好み焼き きしべ
name_ja: お好み焼き きしべ
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (savory layered pancake), family-run, with local Miyajima oyster topping available
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba or udon), and the finishing Otafuku-style sauce is made with wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no gluten-free menu or preparation here, so this shop is not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki uses egg in the batter, the popular versions include pork (niku-tama) and the signature topping is local oysters; the sauce contains fish-derived ingredients. There are no documented vegan options on the menu.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町483-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chōme point for 宮島町483-2; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box, consistent with the machiya-dōri location off the Omotesandō shopping street.
seats_text: About 12 seats, mostly counter seating.
closed_days: Thursdays and Fridays (weekday daytime and Monday are also without service per the published schedule)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter shop run by a local okā-san with little to no English and no English menu, so a foreign student will likely want a guide or translation app and some confidence ordering by pointing.
neighborhood: Miyajima, Machiya-dōri (one street off the Omotesandō arcade), near Itsukushima Shrine, Hatsukaichi
caveats: Cash only and no reservations; the counter holds only about a dozen people and fills up fast, especially with the lunch crowd on weekends. Hours are split and unusual — dinner only Mon-Wed and lunch only Sat-Sun — so confirm before going, and note it is closed Thursday and Friday.

bio.background: A long-running neighborhood okonomiyaki shop tucked away on Miyajima's quiet machiya-dōri, a back lane just off the busy Omotesandō shopping arcade that leads to Itsukushima Shrine. It is the kind of nostalgic, old-fashioned street-corner griddle shop that locals have kept coming back to for decades.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki griddled to order one at a time, including hearty versions like niku-tama (pork and egg) soba and udon, with the option of adding Miyajima's famous local oysters as a topping.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run by an okā-san who griddles every pancake herself, and regulars describe each one as full of warmth and old-fashioned comfort.

One visitor recounted being led down a back street to the shop on the recommendation of a sweet older lady, where the cook said she had been making nothing but this okonomiyaki here for decades; they ate it at the counter with a beer and found it genuinely delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle with wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The okonomiyaki is finished with an Otafuku-style sauce, which is made with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The same griddle is used for pork, egg and oysters, so any dish would be cooked alongside animal products.

cuisine: 広東料理を軸にした中国料理（要事前予約でベジタリアン対応可）
neighborhood: 広島駅エリア（中央）
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町1-5 ホテルグランヴィア広島 2階
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市南区松原町1-5 returned [132.476166, 34.397854], within the Hiroshima box. Lands at Hiroshima Station / Hotel Granvia area as expected.
seats_text: 106 seats total (all non-smoking), including private rooms for 4, 6, 8, 10-20, and 20-30 people.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Tuesdays (open on holidays).
closed_days: Tuesdays (open if a holiday falls on Tuesday)
cultural_comfort_note: As the restaurant inside Hotel Granvia Hiroshima (directly connected to Hiroshima Station), staff are used to international hotel guests and can handle English and special dietary requests made in advance, so a foreign student should feel comfortable here.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a Cantonese-style Chinese kitchen, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, oyster sauce, and other wheat-containing seasonings, and there is no mention of gluten-free preparation or a dedicated fryer. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The restaurant offers a dedicated vegetarian Chinese course and à la carte items prepared without meat or fish, built around vegetables, mushrooms, black sticky rice, and tofu. However, the official course page describes it as lacto-ovo vegetarian: it includes dairy (e.g., a milk-based pudding) and eggs, so it is not fully vegan as listed. A strict vegan would need to call ahead (the vegetarian course requires reservation at least 3 days in advance) and explicitly request removal of dairy and eggs, plus confirm seasonings. Also confirm whether vegetable dishes use oyster sauce or chicken/fish stock common in Cantonese cooking.
caveats: The advertised vegetarian course is lacto-ovo (contains dairy and eggs), not vegan. Vegan and gluten-free diners must arrange details by phone in advance; the vegetarian course needs a 3-day-ahead reservation. This is a hotel restaurant inside Hotel Granvia Hiroshima, not an independent vegan/GF destination.

bio.background: 煌蘭苑 is the Chinese restaurant on the second floor of Hotel Granvia Hiroshima, directly connected to Hiroshima Station so guests can reach it without stepping outside even on rainy days. It serves Cantonese-style Chinese cuisine in a setting that ranges from open dining to private rooms of several sizes, geared toward family meals and formal entertaining alike.
bio.specialty: Cantonese cuisine guided by the principle of 医食同源 ("food and medicine share the same origin"), with carefully balanced menus that combine a wide range of ingredients. Hong Kong-style clay-pot (煲仔) dishes are a signature, alongside multi-course menus and à la carte selections.
bio.philosophy: Head chef Daiji Yamamoto cooks around the ancient idea that food and medicine come from the same source, building well-balanced dishes meant to bring diners health and joy suited to the occasion.
roles: Head Chef: Daiji Yamamoto (山本大治)

anecdotes.text: The kitchen prepares a dedicated vegetarian Chinese course without meat or fish, centered on vegetables, mushrooms, black sticky rice, and tofu, so that guests with dietary preferences can still enjoy authentic Chinese cooking when they reserve ahead.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cantonese-style Chinese cooking at this restaurant relies on standard Chinese seasonings such as soy sauce and oyster sauce, which contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegetarian course is lacto-ovo: it explicitly contains no meat or fish but does include dairy (a milk-based pudding) and eggs, so it is not vegan without modification.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A dedicated meat-and-fish-free vegetarian course is offered with a required reservation at least three days in advance, indicating the kitchen accommodates dietary requests arranged ahead of time.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated vegetarian course and à la carte vegetarian items free of meat and fish, built on vegetables, mushrooms, tofu, and black sticky rice.

cuisine: Local independent neighborhood bakery — savory filled and burger-style breads, sandwiches, pastries
neighborhood: 中区堺町（土橋・本通エリア西側）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堺町1-7-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県広島市中区堺町1-7-2 returned [132.449203, 34.393913], inside the Hiroshima box. Note: target brief described the area as 本通・紙屋町 near the A-Bomb Dome, but the confirmed address is in Sakaimachi near Dobashi/Tsuchihashi tram stops, roughly 700m west of the Peace Memorial Park — still central Naka-ku.
seats_text: Small shop with a modest eat-in space where customers can enjoy bread on-site; exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 7:00–19:00, closed Wednesdays (per local blog and news coverage). One platform listing alternatively shows 7:00–18:30 and Tue–Wed closed; confirm directly with the shop.
closed_days: Wednesday (one listing also shows Tuesday closed — verify directly)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed self-service neighborhood bakery where you pick up bread on a tray and pay at the counter, so very little Japanese is needed, though staff likely speak little English.
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific information is documented. This is a conventional Japanese bakery whose breads typically contain egg, milk, and butter, and signature items (cheeseburger bread, snow-crab cream, BLT dog, French toast, cinnamon rolls) clearly include dairy, egg, meat, and seafood. A vegan visitor would need to ask item-by-item and should not assume any product is plant-based.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery, so essentially all products contain gluten. No gluten-free options or dedicated gluten-free handling are documented; not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ between sources: local blog and news coverage say 7:00–19:00 closed Wednesday, while one restaurant-platform listing shows 7:00–18:30 with Tuesday and Wednesday closed. Bread sells out earlier in the day; the owner advises visiting before noon for the full selection. No official website found.

bio.background: A small, owner-run neighborhood bakery in Hiroshima's Sakaimachi district, a short walk west of the downtown Hondori shopping street and the Peace Memorial Park. Open since 2009, it has become a quietly beloved local fixture, filling the shop each morning with the smell of freshly baked bread.
bio.specialty: Known for an unusually wide and playful range of savory filled breads — a Japanese-style cheeseburger bun finished with grated-daikon sauce, a BLT hot dog, karaage (fried chicken) dog, and creative fillings like snow-crab cream, smoked cheese with potato salad, and Sicilian-style caponata — alongside sweet options such as French toast and cinnamon rolls.

anecdotes.text: The owner recommends coming before noon, when the full lineup of breads is on display, reflecting a hands-on, made-fresh-daily approach.
The interior is described as a warm, calming space with bread arranged in the center and along the windows, plus a small eat-in corner so you can enjoy a roll on the spot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour bakery; flour is handled openly throughout, so airborne and surface gluten cross-contact is unavoidable. No gluten-free production documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and fillings include dairy, egg, meat, and seafood; no separation from animal ingredients is documented.

lead: 鉄板焼×府中焼 あっちゃん
name_ja: 鉄板焼×府中焼 あっちゃん
cuisine: Okonomiyaki and Fuchu-yaki (teppanyaki-style; minced-beef "Fuchu" okonomiyaki)
neighborhood: 中区立町 (Tatemachi), central Hiroshima near Hondori/Hatchobori, about 1 km from the Peace Memorial Park
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町5-7 Gハウス 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市中区立町5-7 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Teppan counter (7 seats) plus a couple of tables and tatami seating; a small second-floor independent shop welcoming to solo diners. Seat count is approximate.
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–13:15, 17:00–21:15; closed Monday (per Tabelog). One secondary listing shows 11:00–14:30 / 17:00–22:00 with irregular closures, so confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Monday (sources conflict; one lists irregular closures — confirm ahead)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, lively counter shop where the cook chats with guests; a little conversational Japanese or a translation app will make ordering and the back-and-forth much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is an okonomiyaki/Fuchu-yaki house. The batter and the layered noodles are wheat-based, and the savory sauce typically contains wheat, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The signature Fuchu-yaki is built on crispy minced beef, and other dishes feature pork, squid and egg, plus a sauce that is generally not vegan. No vegan menu or accommodation is documented.
caveats: Business hours and closed days differ between sources (Tabelog vs. another listing); the seat count is approximate and not officially published. No official website was found.

bio.background: A small second-floor okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop that opened in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district in November 2023, a short walk from the Hondori shopping arcade.
bio.origin: Hiroshima, specializing in Fuchu-yaki, the minced-meat style of okonomiyaki that originated in the city of Fuchu.
bio.specialty: Fuchu-yaki: instead of sliced pork, the cook uses 100% beef mince griddled until crisp, layered with noodles fried until crackly-crunchy. A house favorite is the Acchan Special, combining pork, shiso (perilla) leaf, fresh squid and plenty of green onion for a mix of crispy noodles and snappy scallions.

anecdotes.text: The shop's draw is as much the cook as the food: he is known for a big, funny personality, and regulars say you fall for the manager before you even taste the okonomiyaki.
The Fuchu-yaki is made with 100% beef mince griddled crisp, giving the noodles a signature crackly texture.

cuisine: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き・居酒屋 (okonomiyaki, teppanyaki, izakaya)
neighborhood: 中区立町（立町電停・本通アーケード近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-23 曽我部ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区立町3-23 returned [132.4599, 34.394085], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Tachimachi tram-stop / Hondori arcade area, walking distance from the Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: Small spot: roughly 22-30 seats depending on source (counter plus a few tables); counter seating is the highlight. Full-venue rental for up to ~30 people.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (some sources 11:30-13:30); Dinner Mon-Thu 17:30-23:00, Fri & day-before-holidays 17:30-24:00, Sat 17:30-24:00 (L.O. 23:00). Sources disagree on closed day (one lists Wed, others list Sun/holidays, others 不定休/irregular).
closed_days: Closed day is reported inconsistently across listings (some say Sundays/holidays, one says Wednesday, others list it as irregular / 不定休). Best to confirm directly before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly small local teppanyaki-izakaya where counter seats put you right by the grill; little to no English is documented, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, but the casual atmosphere is welcoming to visitors.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is okonomiyaki (wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles), yakisoba, and teppanyaki served with standard Japanese sauces (okonomi/soy sauces typically contain wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on meat (1-pound steak, Hiroshima wagyu, hormone), seafood (local oysters, uni-and-spinach 'uni-horen'), and egg/pork-based okonomiyaki. No vegan dishes are documented.
caveats: Reported hours and the weekly closed day differ noticeably across listings (lunch end 13:30 vs 14:00; closed Sunday vs Wednesday vs irregular). The hours_week here reflects the most common pattern but should be confirmed directly with the shop. Seat count also varies by source (22-30).

bio.background: A small, independently run teppanyaki and okonomiyaki spot tucked in the Tachimachi/Nakanotana shopping street area of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Hondori arcade and the Peace Memorial Park. The counter sits right at the grill, so guests take in the taste, the sizzle, and the aroma of Hiroshima ingredients cooked in front of them.
bio.specialty: House 'Ganga-yaki' okonomiyaki, the hearty 1-pound steak, fried local oysters, and a uni-and-spinach teppan dish (uni-horen); a no-soup dandan noodle is a lunch favorite. Local sake and wine are paired with the grill dishes.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Hiroshima regional ingredients on the teppan in a relaxed, neighborhood setting suited to everything from after-work drinks to small gatherings.

anecdotes.text: The shop bills itself around tasting Hiroshima's local gourmet on the teppan, with the counter described as a place to enjoy taste, sound, and aroma all at once.
Signature items highlighted by the shop include the popular value-priced 1-pound steak, the house 'Ganga-yaki' okonomiyaki, and a uni-and-spinach dish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based okonomiyaki batter and wheat noodles are cooked on a shared teppan grill alongside other dishes; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard okonomiyaki/soy-style sauces, which typically contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on meat and seafood (steak, oysters, uni) cooked on a shared grill; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cuisine: 中華料理（四川・広東）／Sichuan-Cantonese Chinese
neighborhood: 中区銀山町（流川・八丁堀近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町11-23
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature for 広島県広島市中区銀山町11番23号; coordinates within Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 18 seats total — counter seats plus a few tables (sources vary: counter 10 + tables 6 + small table 2, or counter 12 + table 6). Counter seating lets you watch the chef cook.
hours_raw: 18:00-23:00; closed Sundays and holidays (日曜・祝日定休)
closed_days: 日曜・祝日 (Sundays and public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, lively counter-style Chinese izakaya run by one chef; basic Japanese helps for ordering and casual conversation, and there is no indication of an English menu.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu centers on Chinese dishes using soy sauce, XO sauce and other wheat-based seasonings, plus noodles (ramen) and fried rice; mapo tofu and stir-fries rely on soy/wheat-containing sauces. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Signature dishes are meat- and seafood-heavy (chicken, shrimp, squid; mapo tofu and mapo eggplant typically contain minced pork) and rely on animal-based and fermented sauces (XO sauce, soy sauce). No documented plant-based options.
caveats: Seat layout varies between sources (counter 10 + 6 + 2 vs counter 12 + 6); total of 18 seats is consistent. Phone numbers listed include a 050 booking line and an 082 direct line. Budget estimates vary widely by source (one casual blog cites ~¥2,000 evening; the listing's course pricing is ~¥5,000+).

bio.background: The owner-chef, nicknamed the "Tetsujin" (Iron Man), trained at well-regarded Sichuan and Cantonese restaurants before opening his own counter-style place in central Hiroshima.
bio.specialty: Sichuan-Cantonese Chinese cooking, with a mapo tofu served bubbling-hot and heavy on Sichuan pepper that has become a local favorite; other signatures include mapo eggplant, ebi chili (shrimp in chili sauce), and cuttlefish stir-fried in XO sauce.
bio.philosophy: He makes his seasonings by hand from scratch and pursues ingredient quality — domestic meats, fresh seafood, and hand-peeled shrimp — serving authentic, handmade Chinese food in a casual, reasonably priced setting.

anecdotes.text: The counter seats are the recommended spot because diners get a front-row "live" view of the chef working the wok.
When a local food blogger polled their Instagram followers for the best mapo tofu in town, this shop placed third.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use soy sauce, XO sauce and other Chinese seasonings that are typically wheat-containing, and the menu includes ramen and fried rice.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (chicken, shrimp, squid; pork-based mapo dishes), so heavy shared-equipment animal cross-contact is expected.

lead: 焼鳥かけはし 銀山
name_ja: 焼鳥かけはし 銀山
cuisine: 炭火焼鳥・地鶏料理（居酒屋）
neighborhood: 中区銀山町（銀山町電停・流川/八丁堀近く）
gf_detail: This is a charcoal yakitori and chicken-hotpot izakaya. The grilled skewers are basted in tare and seasonings built on regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and fried/marinated items and the hotpot broth also rely on standard soy-based seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer information, and no indication that wheat can be avoided across the menu. Gluten-free travelers should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm specifics directly with the kitchen in Japanese.
vegan_detail: A specialist chicken restaurant with no documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町11-23 西川ビル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 広島県広島市中区銀山町11-23; result [132.465973, 34.392162] falls within the central Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 18 seats total, including counter seating and three semi-private booth areas with curtain dividers; can accommodate up to about 12 guests for a private gathering.
closed_days: Sundays (with occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, intimate local yakitori counter where some basic Japanese will go a long way; a foreign student can enjoy it but should be ready to order in Japanese or with pointing, and ideally reserve ahead.
caveats: Opened in 2024; hours and the chicken varieties offered can vary seasonally. The water hotpot (mizutaki) generally requires an advance reservation. Reservations are recommended for this small venue, especially on weekends and after 21:00 (phone only). Card, e-money, and QR payments are accepted.

bio.background: A compact charcoal-yakitori and chicken-hotpot spot tucked away in Hiroshima's Ginzan-cho nightlife district, a few minutes' walk from the Ginzan-cho tram stop. It opened in 2024 as a sister to a nearby Nagarekawa chicken restaurant under the same Kakehashi name.
bio.specialty: Skewers of carefully selected heritage-breed jidori chicken grilled over Tosa binchotan charcoal, plus a rich chicken water-hotpot (mizutaki) featuring Miyazaki jidori.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on Tosa binchotan charcoal to draw out the true character of each chicken breed, crisping the surface with infrared heat while keeping the inside juicy, and frames itself as an artisanal, sophisticated hideaway.

anecdotes.text: The menu showcases distinct regional breeds chosen for different roles, including Akita's Hinai-jidori, Mie's Kumano-jidori, and Ehime's Himekko-jidori on the grill, with Miyazaki jidori starring in the hotpot.
A local food blogger covered the shop as a newly opened yakitori spot in Ginzan-cho.

gf_cross_contamination.text: As a yakitori and izakaya kitchen, grilled skewers, fried items, and the hotpot share seasonings and surfaces built on wheat-containing soy sauce and tare; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare and dipping/seasoning sauces for the grilled chicken are based on standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built entirely around chicken, with broths and seasonings of animal/soy origin, so plant-based dishes would share equipment and stocks with meat.

lead: 瀬戸内料理 雑草庵瀬戸
name_ja: 瀬戸内料理 雑草庵瀬戸
cuisine: Setouchi (Seto Inland Sea) cuisine — local Hiroshima seafood (oysters, conger eel/anago, sea bream, octopus, small sardines), regional pork, and Hiroshima sake
neighborhood: Gin'yamachi, Naka-ku (near Nagarekawa / Yagenbori nightlife, close to Gin'yamachi tram stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町12-21 Fビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [132.466187, 34.391453] for the chō-level address; within the Hiroshima box and consistent with Gin'yamachi, Naka-ku.
seats_text: Around 30-35 seats including counter and private tatami rooms; private rooms accommodate up to roughly 26 guests, with full-venue buyout available.
hours_raw: 18:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:30); some listings show extended Fri/Sat hours to ~02:00. Closed Sundays.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Hiroshima izakaya where menus and service are primarily in Japanese; a little Japanese or a translation app will make ordering the local seafood dishes much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. As a Setouchi izakaya the cooking relies on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based sauces, plus tempura (octopus tempura) and other battered/fried items, so cross-contact with wheat is pervasive. No documented gluten-free menu or accommodation.
caveats: Owner/chef name is not documented in the sources reviewed. Reported seat count and closing hours vary slightly between listings (30 vs ~35 seats; midnight vs ~02:00 on weekends).
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept centers on local Seto Inland Sea seafood — oysters (including year-round raw oysters), conger eel, sea bream, octopus, small sardines — plus regional pork. Dashi (fish stock) and wheat-soy-based seasonings run through the menu. No documented vegan dishes.

bio.background: Zassouan is a Hiroshima dining group with roughly a quarter-century of history, built on a simple ambition: to make a restaurant that represents Hiroshima using only Hiroshima ingredients and Hiroshima sake. The Seto (銀山町) branch carries that idea into the Nagarekawa nightlife district, serving the bounty of the Seto Inland Sea in an intimate setting.
bio.specialty: Setouchi local cuisine: oysters, conger eel (anago — the conger eel rice, anago-meshi, is the signature favorite), sea bream, octopus, small sardines, and regional pork, paired with Hiroshima local sake.
bio.philosophy: Local production for local consumption (地産地消) — the kitchen set out to champion Hiroshima's own ingredients and rice wines long before the farm-to-table idea became fashionable, so that transplants and out-of-town guests can taste and understand Hiroshima's food culture in one sitting.

anecdotes.text: Through ongoing research and testing with oyster producers, the restaurant secured a supply of sterilized oysters that lets it serve raw oysters all year round — a distinction it shares with only one dedicated oyster specialist in the city.

The house aims to be a place where newcomers to the region can grasp Hiroshima's food culture, presenting marine fare alongside mountain produce such as Momiji pork, Akitsu potatoes, and pickled Hiroshima greens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes tempura (e.g., octopus tempura) and other fried items, indicating shared frying and battering with wheat flour.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasonal Setouchi seafood dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese izakaya manner, relying on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fish, shellfish (oysters), and meat, with fish-based dashi throughout; no separation for plant-only diets is documented.

cuisine: Old-fashioned local diner (shokudo): anagomeshi (conger eel over rice), anago-don, omurice, oyster rice bowls (kaki-don), egg rice bowls (tamago-don), fried rice, udon
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), near Momijidani Park / Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The core dishes rely on wheat: anagomeshi and the various rice bowls are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), omurice is made with wheat-containing seasonings, and udon noodles are wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on animal products: conger eel (anago), oysters, eggs (omurice, tamago-don, kaki-tama), and dishes seasoned with fish-based dashi and soy sauce. No documented plant-based or vegetarian set meals.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町藤の棚公園内
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Fujinotana/Momijidani Park area.
seats_text: Seat count not published; described as a small, cozy local teahouse-style eatery inside a park.
hours_raw: 10:00-14:00 (closed Wednesday and Thursday)
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a relaxed local spot where residents chat casually with the staff, so a little basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help you feel welcome; English support is unlikely.
caveats: Hours are short (lunch only, roughly 10:00-14:00) and the kitchen closes on Wednesdays and Thursdays, so plan around that. Reported on a review platform as a genuinely local, non-touristy place; menu and prices may vary day to day. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diets.

bio.background: Tucked inside a small park on Miyajima island, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine and the Momijidani Park ropeway area, Yamamura Chaya is the kind of unassuming neighborhood eatery that islanders themselves drop into. While most of Miyajima's dining caters to day-trippers, this is a genuine old-fashioned shokudo (everyday diner) serving honest, affordable home-style lunches.
bio.specialty: Conger eel over rice (anagomeshi) alongside everyday comfort plates the locals order most: a fluffy omurice, oyster rice bowls, egg rice bowls, fried rice, and udon.
bio.philosophy: An ordinary, comforting lunch over casual conversation rather than a tourist showpiece; the appeal is the warm, lived-in atmosphere of a real island teahouse.

anecdotes.text: A diner who went looking for somewhere genuinely local rather than tourist-facing described it as the place 'where Miyajima residents come to eat in a cozy atmosphere,' valuing the comfort and the offhand conversation with the staff, and noted the omurice as the most popular item among islanders.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anagomeshi and the rice bowls are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce/tare, which contains wheat; udon is also wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on conger eel, oysters, eggs, and fish-based dashi, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable across the menu.

lead: お好み焼 よっちゃん
name_ja: お好み焼 よっちゃん
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き
neighborhood: 中区基町・紙屋町東（広島県庁横の地下、平和記念公園至近）
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The dish is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and finished with okonomiyaki sauce, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or accommodations are documented, and everything is cooked on a single shared griddle. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Every okonomiyaki here includes pork and egg as standard, and the signature dishes use them plus optional squid, shrimp, and other animal toppings. The sauce and griddle are shared with meat. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区基町11-10 プライム紙屋町 B1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区基町11-10 returned [132.458557, 34.395252], within the Hiroshima box; lands beside the Prefectural Government building as expected.
seats_text: About 40 seats total, including roughly 22 counter seats; counter-centered small shop.
closed_days: 日曜日（祝日は短縮営業）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, cash-only lunchtime counter shop in an office-building basement frequented by local workers, so come ready to order in Japanese (or by pointing) and don't expect English menus or card payment.
caveats: Open lunchtime only (closes early afternoon) and closed Sundays; cash only. Owner's romanized name (Hidenori Yoshiyama) and the 1990 founding year come from a single news article and could not be cross-confirmed elsewhere.

bio.background: A small counter-style Hiroshima-okonomiyaki shop tucked into the basement of an office building right next to the Hiroshima Prefectural Government, founded by owner Hidenori Yoshiyama in 1990 and run as a beloved weekday-lunch spot for local office workers.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the most popular order being the soba nikutama (noodles layered with pork and egg). The owner sources fresh noodles from a local maker (Isono Seimen) and finishes the dish with Mouri Brewing's Carp Sauce from Miyoshi, which uses vegetable-based natural sweetness rather than artificial sweeteners.
bio.philosophy: Yoshiyama believes great okonomiyaki comes from reading the griddle: 'a person who understands the griddle's feelings can make delicious okonomiyaki.' He steams the cabbage gently to coax out its natural sweetness for a light, fluffy texture rather than a dense one, and holds to a simple standard of cheap prices, generous portions, and good taste.

anecdotes.text: The owner carefully controls cooking time and the cabbage's position on the griddle to bring out its natural sweetness, aiming for a fluffy texture instead of a hard one.
He chooses Mouri Brewing's Carp Sauce because it has no artificial sweeteners, so when brushed onto the hot okonomiyaki it gives off an appetizing aroma.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan griddle on which wheat-based okonomiyaki and noodles are cooked together; no separation documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Finished with okonomiyaki/Carp brand sauce (Mouri Brewing), which is wheat-containing and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All okonomiyaki are made with pork and egg as standard and cooked on a shared griddle with other meat and seafood toppings.

lead: 体にやさしい創作おばんざい AGRI（アグリ）
name_ja: 体にやさしい創作おばんざい AGRI（アグリ）
name_en: Karada ni Yasashii Sosaku Obanzai AGRI
cuisine: オーガニック志向の創作おばんざい・玄米（減塩ランチ、ベジタリアン/ヴィーガン・グルテンフリー対応可）
gf_detail: 複数の予約情報サイトによると、地元食材を使ったメニューはリクエストに応じてグルテンフリー対応にカスタマイズ可能とされています。ただし専用調理ライン等の記載はなく、和食店のため通常メニューには小麦を含む調味料（醤油・味噌等）が使われている可能性が高いです。グルテンフリー希望は事前に電話（082-246-7721）で相談・確認が必要です。
vegan_detail: 店は野菜料理を主軸とした「体にやさしい」おばんざいが看板で、ベジタリアンメニューがあると明記されています。予約情報サイトによると、リクエストに応じてヴィーガン対応のカスタマイズも可能とのこと。ただし定番のおばんざいや出汁には魚介・かつお出汁などの動物性食材が使われていることが多いため、完全ヴィーガン希望の場合は事前予約時に相談が必要です。
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町4-18 エスポアールビル1F
geocode_note: GSIで街区レベル（中区中町4-18）をジオコード。平和記念公園・原爆ドームから徒歩圏の広島市中心部、Hiroshima box内。
neighborhood: 中区中町（袋町、平和記念公園から徒歩圏）
seats_text: 約30席（カウンター席あり）。情報源により26席との記載もあり。
closed_days: 日曜のランチは休み（情報源により日曜終日定休との記載もあり）。貸切・大口ケータリング時は不定休。
cultural_comfort_note: 料理研究家のオーナーが営む個人店で、英語メニューや英語対応の記載は確認できないため、基本的な日本語(または翻訳アプリ)があると安心して食事と食事制限の相談ができます。
caveats: 食べログでは「閉店」表示が出ていますが、公式サイト(agri-obanzai.com、メンテナンス中表示)・ホットペッパー・ぐるなび・地元メディア記事はいずれも現役の営業店として掲載しており、現在も営業しているとみられます。来店前に電話(082-246-7721)で営業状況の確認を推奨。日曜の営業可否や席数は情報源により差があります。ヴィーガン/グルテンフリー対応は予約時の事前相談が前提です。

bio.origin: 広島市出身（1956年生まれ）。実家は鮮魚店と仕出し業を営んでいた。
bio.background: 1979年に調理師免許を取得、1980年に広島女学院大学文学部を卒業。結婚後は家事に専念していたが、夫の病気をきっかけに2000年にスープとおばんざいの店『キッチンポットラック』を開業。2010年10月に中区袋町・中町へ移転し、自然派の店『AGRI』をオープンした。スローフード広島の理事を務め、米粉普及の取り組みにも関わるほか、テレビ・ラジオの料理コーナーや母校の広島女学院大学での講義など、多方面で活動している。
bio.specialty: 季節の野菜をたっぷり使い、20種類以上の食材を一皿に盛り込む『おばんざいランチ』。広島県世羅産の米や玄米を用い、塩分2g以下に抑えた減塩メニューも看板。
bio.philosophy: 『野菜の力を創り出す』『食と人をつないで新しい価値を創り出す』をコンセプトに、体にやさしい料理で食卓を豊かにすることを目指す。栄養士・医師と共著で減塩レシピ本も手がけている。

anecdotes.text: 実家が鮮魚店兼仕出し業という食の環境で育ち、夫の病気を機に2000年に自身の店を開いたことが料理研究家としての出発点となった。
広島の減塩サミット(2012年・2014年)に参加し、瀬戸内ブランド認定を受けた『まるごと広島レモンケーキ』など地域食材を生かした商品開発にも取り組んでいる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 和食・おばんざいの店であり、醤油・味噌など小麦を含む調味料が通常使われているとみられる(出汁・味付けの基本)。グルテンフリー希望は事前相談が必要。

vegan_cross_contact.text: おばんざいや出汁には魚介・かつお出汁など動物性食材が使われることが多く、完全ヴィーガンには事前のカスタマイズ相談が前提。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 地元食材を使ったメニューはリクエストに応じてヴィーガン・グルテンフリーにカスタマイズ可能と案内されている。

positives.text: 野菜料理にこだわり、ベジタリアンメニューがあると明記。塩分2g以下の減塩ランチなど健康配慮メニューが充実。
料理研究家が営む個人店で、玄米(美人玄米)や20品目以上の食材を使った体にやさしいおばんざいが看板。

cuisine: Seafood — grilled and raw oysters, oyster rice, anago (conger eel) rice and bowls, oyster/anago onigiri; eat-in plus walk-and-eat street snacks
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Core dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings (which typically contain wheat), and grilled/glazed anago and oyster rice use soy-based sauces. Onigiri and rice bowls are rice-based but the seasonings are not gluten-free, and no gluten-free preparation or labeling is documented. A celiac traveler should not rely on this spot.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu is built around oysters (raw and grilled), anago (conger eel), scallops and other seafood, with dashi and fish-based seasonings throughout. There are no documented plant-based main dishes.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町港町1162-6
geocode_note: GSI geocode for the chō-banchi address landed at lat 34.348637, lng 132.331451, inside the Hiroshima validity box and on Miyajima near the ferry terminal as expected.
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small casual shop near the ferry terminal with limited eat-in space alongside its takeaway counter.
hours_raw: 10:30-20:00 daily (primary listing). A separate listing shows 10:30-18:00 with Tuesdays closed, so closing time and a possible Tuesday rest day can vary seasonally.
closed_days: Open daily per primary listing; one source indicates Tuesdays may be closed — confirm seasonally.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up oyster and anago shop right by the ferry where you can point at what you want and eat on the go, so a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way even if no English menu is guaranteed.
neighborhood: Miyajima island, by the ferry terminal at the entrance to Omotesando shopping street
caveats: Posted hours and closing day differ between sources (10:30-20:00 daily vs 10:30-18:00 closed Tuesdays) — verify the day you plan to visit, especially in the off-season. Seat count is not published; expect mostly counter/standing and takeaway. No official website was found.

bio.background: Shofuku opened in 2017, with its main shop in Hiroshima city's Horikawa-cho district; the Miyajima shop is its second location, set right in front of the ferry terminal at the entrance to the Omotesando approach. It is a small independent operation rather than a chain.
bio.origin: Hiroshima city (Horikawa-cho main shop), expanding onto Miyajima island.
