bio.specialty: Local oysters served raw and grilled, including large rock oysters prized for sweetness and umami, plus oyster rice and fresh anago (conger eel) dishes. The shop leans on oysters that are described as non-frozen and not treated with chlorinated water, prized for clean, clear flavor.
bio.philosophy: A focus on fresh, carefully handled local oysters and anago, served in both sit-down and grab-and-go formats so visitors can taste Miyajima's signature seafood while strolling.

anecdotes.text: Diners highlight the friendly, knowledgeable hospitality, with staff happy to share local tips alongside the seafood.
The shop is praised for fresh oysters that are not frozen and not disinfected with chlorinated water, giving them a notably clean, transparent taste.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as glazed/grilled anago, oyster rice and onigiri rely on Japanese soy- and dashi-based seasonings, which conventionally contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is entirely seafood-based (oysters, anago, scallops) with dashi seasonings, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable across the whole operation.

cuisine: 牡蠣料理・タパス・地酒のバル／居酒屋（牡蠣入りピザ、日替わりパスタ、小皿料理）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Signature dishes are wheat-based: the Hiroshima oyster margherita pizza and the daily-changing pasta both contain wheat. Tapas and light fare are not described as gluten-free, and no gluten-free menu or preparation is mentioned anywhere. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should treat this venue as unsuitable unless they confirm specific options directly.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on oysters (oyster pizza), and the documented tapas include low-temperature salmon and roast beef; pizza and pasta typically use cheese and dairy. No vegan dishes or plant-based options are documented. The draw is local Hiroshima sake, shochu, wine, and craft beer paired with seafood and meat small plates.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町1165 ホテル宮島別荘内
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町1165 via GSI; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box. Coordinates reflect the chō-level point near Hotel Miyajima Besso, about one minute from the Miyajima ferry pier.
neighborhood: 宮島島内（宮島桟橋から徒歩約1分、表参道近く）
seats_text: 19 seats (counter and table seating inside the hotel bar lounge)
closed_days: 不定休（無休に近いが不定休。営業状況は要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: As a stylish bar inside a hotel right by the Miyajima ferry pier, it's used to receiving travelers, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome even with limited Japanese.
caveats: Located inside Hotel Miyajima Besso on Miyajima island. Open to non-hotel guests as well as hotel guests. Closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. Not suitable for strict vegan or gluten-free diners: the headline dishes (oyster pizza, daily pasta) are wheat-based, and tapas feature seafood and meat.

bio.origin: Miyajima island, Hiroshima — set just inside the main entrance of Hotel Miyajima Besso, about a one-minute walk from the ferry pier
bio.background: 蔵VITTO is the bar of Hotel Miyajima Besso, a modern property produced by Kinsuikan, a long-running Miyajima hot-spring inn founded in 1902. The name plays on 蔵 (kura, a sake brewery storehouse), a nod to the idea that you have arrived at the gateway of Miyajima with Hiroshima's celebrated local brews waiting for you.
bio.specialty: A relaxed adult bal showcasing Hiroshima's drink culture alongside small plates: a generous Hiroshima oyster margherita pizza, a daily-changing pasta, and rotating tapas such as low-temperature salmon and roast beef, served from morning through late evening.
bio.philosophy: Celebrating Hiroshima as one of Japan's great sake regions and pairing local craft drinks with cuisine built on fresh produce from regional contract farms — an everyday oasis for grown-ups that welcomes both hotel guests and passing travelers.

anecdotes.text: The bar pours Sakurao Gin distilled in nearby Sakurao (Hatsukaichi) and local Miyajima craft beer, with signature cocktails like a Miyajima Gin Tonic and Hiroshima Spritz developed under bartender Shingo Noma.
You can enjoy fresh bread bought from the hotel's own bakery, Shimaumai PAN (島旨PAN), right at the bar counter.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（宮島産ブランド牡蠣を使った牡蠣料理・あげおこ）
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki house. The okonomiyaki batter and crepe layer are made from wheat flour, the layered noodles (そば) are wheat, the signature あげおこ is deep-fried battered okonomiyaki, and dishes are finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki/Otafuku sauce and soy-sauce-based seasonings. The fried oysters (牡蠣フライ) use wheat breadcrumbs. No gluten-free menu, preparation, or accommodation is documented anywhere. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg-and-pork okonomiyaki, oysters (Miyajima brand 牡蠣), squid, shrimp, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, with sauces that are not vegan. The closest non-meat item documented is an oven-baked potato-salad-and-cheese dish (ポテサラチーズ焼, dairy, not vegan). No vegan or fully vegetarian options are documented, and no vegan accommodation is mentioned. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町70-2
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県廿日市市宮島町70-2 via GSI; first feature coordinates [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
neighborhood: 宮島（廿日市市宮島町）／厳島神社・表参道商店街エリア
seats_text: About 21 seats; table seating for roughly 4-5 groups plus one single seat (Tabelog lists 24 seats / 5 tables). Small house-converted space.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu (and holidays): 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00). Fri, Sat, Sun: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Tuesdays. May close early when sold out.
closed_days: Tuesday (火曜日); may also close early on sellout
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual family-run okonomiyaki spot in a converted old house just off the Itsukushima Shrine path; English is not advertised, but pointing at the picture menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get a foreign student through comfortably.
caveats: Menu is overwhelmingly wheat- and seafood-based; no gluten-free or vegan options are documented. Hours differ on weekdays vs. Fri-Sun (dinner service only Fri-Sun), and the shop can sell out and close early. Seat counts differ slightly between the official site (~21) and Tabelog (24).

bio.background: Tucked into a quiet alley just steps from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine on Miyajima, this Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop occupies a converted house more than twenty years old, giving it a calm, lived-in feel away from the bustle of the main shopping street.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and Miyajima oysters. The standout is あげおこ, a deep-fried okonomiyaki made for eating on the move, offered in soba, squid, oyster, and cheese versions. The kitchen also leans hard into local oysters, using the premium Hiroshima 'Gokusen' brand for dishes like the oyster special (five large oysters piled on the okonomiyaki) and oyster ponzu with a house-blended ponzu.
bio.philosophy: Pairing honest, classic Hiroshima okonomiyaki with the best local Miyajima oysters, so the place works equally for a quick walking snack and for diners who come specifically for the oysters.

anecdotes.text: The owner's own favorite build is an okonomiyaki with shrimp, squid, shiso (oba), and squid tempura bits, and he personally recommends the seseri (chicken neck) with grated-radish ponzu.
あげお, short for 'fried okonomiyaki,' is finished with a choice of Otafuku sauce or a green-onion ponzu jelly and is a popular grab-and-go item for shrine visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes (okonomiyaki batter, layered wheat noodles, deep-fried あげおこ, and breaded fried oysters) are all wheat-based and cooked on shared teppan/fryer surfaces; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki/Otafuku sauce and soy-sauce-based seasonings (e.g., ponzu), which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around egg, pork, oysters, squid, shrimp, and fish-based dashi/toppings cooked on a shared griddle; no vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: Miyajima bar — oyster-topped pizza, tapas (small plates), and local drinks including Sakurao (Hatsukaichi) gin and Miyajima craft beer
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature dishes are oyster-topped pizza and bread from the hotel bakery, both wheat-based. Tapas and other small plates are not documented as gluten-free, and there is no indication of GF accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu centers on oysters (oyster pizza), tapas, and hotel bakery bread; oysters and typical tapas use animal products. No vegan dishes are documented. The draw is local Hiroshima/Miyajima alcohol (Sakurao gin, local beer, sake, wine), which a vegan could drink, but there is no confirmed vegan food.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町1165 ホテル宮島別荘1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 廿日市市宮島町1165. Result (lat 34.301968, lng 132.323425) lands on Miyajima island, just below the central-Hiroshima bounding box; kept per Miyajima guidance since the address contains 宮島町. The venue sits inside Hotel Miyajima Villa near the Miyajima ferry pier / Omotesando entrance. Note: target brief listed 宮島町407, but Tabelog and the official site both give 宮島町1165.
neighborhood: Miyajima (near the ferry pier and Omotesando entrance), Hatsukaichi
seats_text: 19 seats
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: As the casual bar of a design-forward resort hotel right by the ferry pier, it is used to visitors and is an easy, relaxed spot to drop in for a drink or a slice, though menus and staff conversation are mainly in Japanese.

bio.origin: Miyajima, inside Hotel Miyajima Villa, a few steps from the Miyajima ferry pier and the entrance to the Omotesando shopping street
bio.background: Kura VITTO is the casual stand-bar of Hotel Miyajima Villa, an adults-oriented resort that opened in front of the Miyajima ferry terminal. The bar is open to non-guests and runs from 9 in the morning, so it works as a morning coffee stop, a daytime lunch break over pizza, or an evening drink after sightseeing on the island.
bio.specialty: Oyster-topped pizza and a rotating selection of tapas (small plates) made by the hotel's kitchen, paired with locally rooted drinks — Sakurao gin distilled in Hatsukaichi, Miyajima craft beer, regional sake and wine.
bio.philosophy: The concept is to let visitors enjoy Hiroshima- and Miyajima-made drinks in a relaxed, stylish setting from early morning onward, showcasing local producers.

anecdotes.text: The bar features original cocktails supervised by bartender Shingo Noma, including a 'Miyajima Gin Tonic' and a 'Hiroshima Spumoni' built on locally made Sakurao gin.
Although it is the bar of Hotel Miyajima Villa, it is open to the public (you do not need to be a hotel guest), serving from 9:00 to 23:00.

caveats: Address in the source brief (宮島町407) does not match the address confirmed on Tabelog and the official site (宮島町1165); recorded the confirmed 1165 address. Seat count (19) is from Tabelog. Hours are 9:00–23:00 daily with irregular (不定休) closures, so any given day may be closed without notice. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation information is documented.

cuisine: 寿司 / Sushi (seasonal Seto Inland Sea fish, omakase)
neighborhood: 宮島 表参道商店街そば（厳島神社・宮島桟橋エリア）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町810-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町810-1 returned [132.309982, 34.272606], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Counter only; official site lists 14 counter seats (a review platform lists 16). Small intimate counter.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (last entry 13:00, L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (last entry 20:30, L.O. 21:30). Closed Wednesdays, and the 1st and 3rd Tuesday of each month.
closed_days: Wednesdays; plus 1st and 3rd Tuesdays of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A reservation-only counter sushi-ya where the chef serves omakase course by course; a little Japanese and an understanding of sushi-counter etiquette will help, though staff are used to visitors to Miyajima.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional sushi counter built around soy sauce (the kitchen pairs sushi with four soy-sauce styles including tamari and a tsuyu/dashi-soy blend, all conventional wheat-containing or dashi-based seasonings), plus dishes like seared Hiroshima beef and oyster soup. No gluten-free menu or wheat-free soy sauce is documented, and there is no dedicated fryer or GF handling on record.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire menu centers on raw and grilled Seto Inland Sea fish, Miyajima oysters, and Hiroshima beef, with fish-based dashi and soy sauces throughout. No vegan or vegetarian course is documented; meals are chef's-choice omakase.
caveats: Seat count differs by source (official site 14 counter seats; one reservation platform lists 16). Reservation-only and omakase-driven; expect a per-person budget in the roughly 8,500 yen and up range for courses. Tuesday closures rotate (1st and 3rd Tuesdays), so confirm before visiting.

bio.background: A small hideaway-style sushi counter tucked into a quiet alley just off Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the Miyajima ferry pier. It opened in 2017 and seats guests at an intimate counter where the chef serves an omakase progression.
bio.specialty: Seasonal fish of the Seto Inland Sea, served as nigiri and small plates, alongside signature local items such as seared Hiroshima beef and a soup made with Miyajima oysters.
bio.philosophy: A devotion to local sourcing: the seasonal Seto Inland Sea fish, the wasabi, the rice, and the sake are all from Hiroshima Prefecture. The sushi rice is seasoned with a blend of rice vinegar and red vinegar, and rather than one all-purpose soy sauce the counter pairs each piece with one of four styles - sudachi soy, Tosa soy, tamari, and a tsuyu-style blend - chosen to match the ingredient.

anecdotes.text: Seasonal menus rotate with the catch; a winter lineup featured yellowtail, sea cucumber, oysters, and Japanese jack mackerel.
The kitchen deliberately switches between four different soy sauces - sudachi, Tosa, tamari, and a tsuyu blend - depending on which fish is being served.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant pairs sushi with four soy-sauce styles (sudachi, Tosa, tamari, and a tsuyu/dashi-soy blend); these are conventional Japanese soy-based and dashi-based seasonings, none documented as gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely fish-, shellfish-, and meat-based (Seto Inland Sea fish, Miyajima oysters, Hiroshima beef) with fish dashi, so no separation from animal products is possible.

lead: 広島つけ麺 ぶちうま
name_ja: 広島つけ麺 ぶちうま
cuisine: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (spicy cold dipping noodles)
neighborhood: Tatemachi, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Hondori area)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町6-1 ウイングビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区立町6-1; result lat 34.393272, lng 132.461685 falls within the Hiroshima box and matches the central Tatemachi location near Hatchobori/Hondori.
seats_text: Counter seating only; a small lunch-counter shop (exact seat count not documented).
closed_days: Saturday and Sunday (土曜日・日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny lunch-only counter noodle shop where ordering is simple (point to the tsukemen and tell them your spice level); a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa will carry you through with no English expected.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is tsukemen — wheat-flour noodles dipped in a sauce built on soy sauce (which contains wheat). There are no documented gluten-free options.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The dipping broth is a three-blend of chicken-and-pork bone (鶏ガラ豚骨) with seafood (魚介) and tomato, and the dish is served with chashu (roast pork). The wheat noodles and soy-sauce-based dare make this unsuitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Open weekday lunch only (11:00–15:00, closed weekends) — go before 13:00 to be safe, as a small popular shop can sell out or queue. Spice is adjustable; the chili level scales steeply (10辛 and beyond), so first-timers should order mild.

bio.background: A Hiroshima-style tsukemen specialist tucked into the Wing Building on Tatemachi, a short walk from the Hatchobori and Hondori shopping districts and just steps from Tatemachi tram stop. It is reported to be the Hiroshima arrival of a well-known Tokyo tsukemen name, bringing the format to central Hiroshima.
bio.origin: Tokyo-origin tsukemen brand brought to Hiroshima's Tatemachi
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style spicy tsukemen: chewy wheat noodles served with a three-blend dipping sauce of chicken-pork bone, seafood, and tomato, topped with juicy chashu, with diner-selectable chili heat

anecdotes.text: The signature is a three-blend dipping sauce combining chicken-and-pork-bone richness with seafood umami and tomato, paired with juicy chashu, with spice levels customizable to the diner's preference.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle tsukemen shop with soy-sauce-based dipping broth; gluten is central to the menu and there is no indication of gluten-free handling or separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce is soy-sauce based (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat); the broth is a chicken-pork-bone, seafood and tomato blend.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is animal-based (chicken-pork bone plus seafood) and the dish is topped with pork chashu; no vegan or vegetarian preparation is documented.

cuisine: 郷土料理・甘味（もみじ饅頭製造販売、もみじクロワッサン「もみクロ」などの食べ歩きグルメ、穴子飯・牡蠣料理）
gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature momiji croissant ("Momikuro") is made from wheat croissant dough, and momiji manju are wheat-and-egg sponge cakes. Local dishes such as anago-don (conger eel rice) and oyster dishes are served with standard wheat-based soy sauce and sauces. No dedicated gluten-free items or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Sweets contain dairy and egg (cream, cheese, chocolate croissant varieties; momiji manju use egg). Savory menu centers on conger eel (anago) and oysters, which are not vegan, and uses fish/wheat-soy-based seasonings. No documented plant-based options.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町492
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 132.331451, lat 34.348637, within the Hiroshima box. Located on Miyajima island (宮島町) along the Omotesando shopping arcade between the ferry terminal and Itsukushima Shrine.
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（厳島神社参道沿い）
seats_text: A multi-floor tourism center: ground floor is a souvenir shop and momiji-manju production/sales counter with the food-walk croissant sold at the storefront; group dining rooms occupy the upper floors. Exact seat count not documented.
closed_days: No regular closing day (open daily); opening times vary seasonally.
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, tourist-friendly arcade shop where you can simply point at a croissant and pay at the counter, so a few words of Japanese and a smile are all you need to enjoy a food-walk snack.
caveats: Exact seat count and detailed founding history are not documented in available sources. Upper-floor sit-down meals are noted as currently limited to domestic tour groups during busy periods, but the ground-floor shop and food-walk croissant counter remain open to all visitors.

bio.background: Toriiya sits right on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping arcade, on the lively path that runs from the ferry pier toward the great torii and Itsukushima Shrine. It works as a tourism center: the ground floor is a souvenir shop where momiji manju (maple-leaf-shaped cakes) are made and sold, while upper floors host dining rooms for groups who come for Hiroshima specialties like anago-don and oysters.
bio.specialty: The shop is best known for the 'momiji croissant' nicknamed 'Momikuro' (もみクロ) sold at its storefront for snacking as you stroll. A square of crisp croissant dough frames a soft maple-leaf-shaped center, and it is filled with the shop's own house-made fillings such as red bean, custard, apple, chocolate, cheese and a rotating seasonal flavor.
bio.philosophy: The croissant is meant to be eaten leisurely while wandering the arcade rather than rushing through, encouraging visitors to slow down and enjoy the island. Fillings use the shop's own house-made an (sweet bean paste).

anecdotes.text: The Momikuro is hugely popular at the counter, reportedly selling as many as 2,000 to 3,000 pieces a day at peak, and is described as palm-sized so it satisfies without filling you up before other island treats.
The croissant is offered in six standard flavors built on the shop's own house-made anko, distinguishing it from ordinary momiji manju.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory island dishes such as anago-don and oyster dishes are served with standard Japanese seasonings, which typically include wheat-based soy sauce.

cuisine: 和菓子・もみじ饅頭 (Japanese sweets / momiji manju)
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街入口（厳島神社参道）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町浜之町844-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県廿日市市宮島町浜之町844-1 returned [132.309982, 34.272606], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: No eat-in seating documented; this is primarily a takeout confectionery shop where you can watch momiji manju being made at the storefront.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular; generally open daily, hours extend in spring/autumn peak season)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly traditional sweets shop on the Miyajima approach where you can point at what you want and buy individual momiji manju; a smile and basic Japanese greetings go a long way, and English is limited.
gf_detail: Momiji manju are made from a wheat-flour batter, so they are not gluten-free. No gluten-free products are documented at this shop.
vegan_detail: The classic red-bean (koshi-an / tsubu-an) momiji manju centers on sweet adzuki paste, but the manju batter and cake-style varieties typically contain egg, and the shop also makes cheese and cream fillings that are not vegan. No certified vegan items are documented; a strict vegan would need to ask whether any specific manju is made without egg, dairy, or honey.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (9:00-17:00 with peak-season extension to 21:00 on one source, 9:30-18:30 on another); confirm seasonal hours on arrival. Closures are irregular. No eat-in seating was documented; treat as a takeout sweets shop. No official website was found.

bio.background: Founded in 1933, Kimuraya Honten is a family-run momiji manju shop standing on a prominent corner right where the Omotesando shopping street begins on the approach to Itsukushima Shrine. For generations it has made its maple-leaf cakes by hand, and visitors can watch the manju being baked right at the storefront.
bio.specialty: Momiji manju with a notably firm, chewy dough that sets it apart from the softer versions sold elsewhere on the island, filled with a sweet, sticky adzuki bean paste made to the original recipe. Beyond the classic red-bean version, the shop offers fillings such as soy-milk cream, cheese, raisin, pumpkin, and chestnut.
bio.philosophy: The shop deliberately keeps its original formulation and traditional dough, making its momiji manju without emulsifiers or additives — reportedly one of the only makers on Miyajima to do so, which appeals to families and additive-conscious visitors.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor describes keeping the recipe unchanged: making the manju with the traditional dough and without emulsifiers ('配合を変えず昔ながらの生地にこだわり、乳化剤等を使用せず製造').
The firm dough and unchanged-since-opening sweet bean paste give the cakes a distinctly old-fashioned Miyajima character, best enjoyed with green tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All momiji manju are baked from a wheat-flour batter, so the entire product line contains gluten; there is no gluten-free option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop makes dairy-based fillings (cheese, soy-milk cream) alongside the bean-paste varieties in the same small operation, so cross-contact for a strict vegan cannot be ruled out.

positives.text: Momiji manju here are made without emulsifiers or additives, keeping the original traditional recipe.

cuisine: Anagomeshi (conger eel rice), udon, Setouchi seafood (grilled oysters)
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Omotesando area, in front of Itsukushima Shrine's Karamon gate
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町102
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 宮島町, landing on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Seating includes tatami-room areas; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00 - 16:00 (daily); may close early once sold out (売り切り次第終了する場合もあります)
closed_days: No regular closed days listed (open 7 days/week)
cultural_comfort_note: A small tourist-area eatery with a short, photo-friendly menu (anagomeshi, oysters); basic Japanese greetings help but pointing at the limited menu works fine, and walk-in only means no reservation hurdle.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The signature anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) is glazed/seasoned with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare, and udon is a wheat noodle, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No dedicated GF preparation or fryer documented.
vegan_detail: Menu centers on conger eel (anago), grilled oysters, and seafood; no vegan dishes documented. Soy-sauce-based seasonings and fish-based dishes make this unsuitable for vegan diners.
caveats: Detailed backstory (Taisho-era mansion building, established 2004, staff of four) appears in a third-party summary of listing content and could not be independently confirmed on the primary fetched pages; treat as low confidence. Kitchen may close before 16:00 once the day's anago sells out.

bio.background: Yamashiroya sits right at the heart of Miyajima's approach, directly in front of the Karamon gate of Itsukushima Shrine, occupying a renovated old house with tatami-room seating that gives the place a calm, traditional feel amid the busy Omotesando shopping street.
bio.specialty: Anagomeshi — locally caught conger eel grilled and laid over rice — is the house dish, served alongside grilled Setouchi oysters and udon.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is housed in a renovated former residence and keeps a deliberately short menu focused on anagomeshi, large portions, and grilled oysters; it has been described as established in 2004 with a small staff.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Anago is finished with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare and udon is a wheat noodle, so gluten is present throughout the core menu; no GF handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anagomeshi glaze/seasoning is soy-sauce based, which in Japan is typically brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on conger eel and oysters; no vegan-safe preparation indicated.

cuisine: Momiji-manju and wagashi (traditional Japanese sweets)
gf_detail: Momiji-manju are made from a wheat-flour castella sponge, and the seasonal and salted-butter varieties are likewise wheat-based, so the core menu is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options or allergy accommodation are documented.
vegan_detail: The classic anko momiji uses house-made azuki bean paste, which can be plant-based, but the castella sponge of momiji-manju typically contains egg and the signature salted-butter momiji contains dairy butter. No ingredient list confirms egg-free batter, so a vegan visitor would need to ask directly. Treat as ask, not confirmed.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町455
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町455 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 14 table seats in the eat-in tea space
hours_raw: Shop 09:00-17:00; tea space 10:00-16:00 (opens around 12:00 on days following a rest day)
closed_days: Irregular (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run sweets shop tucked off the main arcade; a friendly greeting and pointing at the freshly baked momiji you want will carry you through, though detailed allergy questions may be hard without some Japanese.
neighborhood: Miyajima island, on a side street one block off the Omotesando shopping arcade, about a 10-minute walk toward Itsukushima Shrine
caveats: Hours are irregular (不定休) and the tea space keeps shorter hours than the shop; on days following a rest day opening can be delayed to around noon. Confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: Sakamoto Kashiho is a family-run sweets shop on Miyajima that has been baking momiji-manju since 1955, more than seventy years of single-family craft. It sits a block off the bustling Omotesando arcade, a quieter spot where visitors can step away from the crowds about ten minutes from Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Hand-baked momiji-manju with house-made azuki bean paste, and a signature filling-free salted-butter momiji.
bio.philosophy: Rather than buying in pre-made fillings, the shop makes everything by hand: the father personally crafts both the bean paste and the castella sponge, carefully removing the skins of the azuki beans to produce a refined paste with a gentle sweetness and smooth texture.

anecdotes.text: The signature salted-butter momiji breaks from tradition with no anko at all, instead kneading French sea salt and New Zealand butter into the fluffy castella sponge for an entirely different flavor; it is the shop's most popular item.
Seasonal momiji rotate through the year: cherry-leaf-scented sakura anko in spring, Uji matcha with whole azuki in summer, chestnut-cream Mont Blanc in fall, and roasted sweet-potato paste in winter.
A small tea space lets guests eat the momiji fresh from the oven, warm and fluffy, with complimentary self-serve tea.

lead: つけ麺本舗 辛部 十日市店
name_ja: つけ麺本舗 辛部 十日市店
cuisine: Hiroshima tsukemen (spicy cold dipping noodles) and ramen
neighborhood: Naka-ku Tokaichimachi, central Hiroshima (near Tokaichimachi tram stop, ~5 min from Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区十日市町1-4-29 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.448776, 34.396957] for 広島県広島市中区十日市町1-4-29, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 25 seats (13 counter + 12 table) per Tabelog; one local blog cites 28 (12 counter + 16 table). Small counter-and-table noodle shop.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:45), 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:45); evening service ends when sold out. 不定休 (irregular closures).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed weekly closed day documented.
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is by ticket machine and the menu is mostly in Japanese, so glance at the posted menu outside and learn the spice scale before stepping up to order.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle tsukemen shop; the dipping noodles are wheat, and the spicy dipping broth uses soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and other seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiac/strict GF diners.
vegan_detail: The tsukemen comes standard with chashu pork, and the dipping broth is built on a meat/fish base with wheat-based soy seasoning. Toppings are cabbage, cucumber, negi, nori, and dried bonito. No vegan option is documented; not suitable for vegans or strict vegetarians.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly between sources (25 vs 28). Evening service can end early once the day's noodles sell out. Closures are irregular, so check before a special trip. No official website confirmed; the brand has branch Instagram accounts.

bio.background: Karabu (辛部) is one of the standard-bearers of Hiroshima-style tsukemen, the city's spicy cold dipping-noodle dish. Though it arrived after the genre's pioneers, it won a following on the strength of its noodles and broth and grew into a small chain with several Hiroshima locations; the Tokaichi shop is the original main store.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima tsukemen: firm, springy wheat noodles served cold alongside a clean, spicy dipping broth, dressed with cabbage, cucumber, negi, nori or bonito, and chashu, with a customizable heat scale.
roles: Original main store of the Karabu group

anecdotes.text: The spice level is set on a wide scale (commonly cited from 0 up to around 30), with a signature stick-style heat gauge; locals note that even '10' lands only just short of medium-hot.
The noodles are described as firm with a crunchy bite and the broth as simple and clean enough to drink down, drawing many female customers and strong local support.
Regarded as one of the representative Hiroshima tsukemen names, comparable in recognition and scale to other major local specialists, having expanded to multiple branches across the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are the core product, so flour and wheat are pervasive in the kitchen; no GF preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is seasoned with soy sauce, which in this style is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free soy alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on meat/fish broth and topped with pork chashu and bonito, so shared cookware and broth make vegan service impractical.

cuisine: Hiroshima tsukemen (spicy dipping noodles)
neighborhood: Takanobashi shopping street, Otemachi, Naka-ku
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町5丁目5-10
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [132.451736, 34.383869] for 広島県広島市中区大手町5丁目5-10; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 12 counter seats
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-14:30, 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat 11:30-14:30; Sun closed. Also closed during Obon and New Year holidays.
closed_days: Sundays (also closed during Obon and New Year holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat counter shop run by the owners; ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English, so knowing a few Japanese words for ordering and your desired spice level helps.
gf_detail: This is a tsukemen shop: the noodles are wheat and the spicy dipping broth uses soy sauce (wheat-based) and fish dashi, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The signature spicy dipping broth is built on bonito/seafood (fish) dashi, so the tsukemen is not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; vegetable toppings exist but the broth itself contains fish.
caveats: Cash plus QR-code payment (PayPay, d-barai) only; no credit cards or e-money. Very small 12-seat counter, so expect to wait at peak lunch and dinner. Spice can be ordered from 0x up to extremely high challenge levels.

bio.background: A small counter-only tsukemen shop tucked into the Takanobashi shopping street in central Hiroshima, opened as Hiroshima-style spicy dipping noodles grew in popularity. It is run as a family operation, with the proprietress tending the broth and a partner helping with service.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tsukemen: thin wheat noodles served with a cold, seafood-and-bonito dipping broth that you can order at a customizable heat, from no spice up to eye-watering challenge levels.
bio.philosophy: A restrained, seafood-forward dipping broth with the spice dialed exactly to the diner's request, served in an intimate counter setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop is famous for its escalating spice levels; diners who conquer the most extreme heat have their names posted in the shop, and a 'Hell Ramen' at the top spice level is offered as a challenge even regulars find brutal.
The broth leans on bonito dashi with restrained acidity, and the noodles are noticeably thinner than at rival Hiroshima tsukemen shops.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat noodles boiled in shared water and a soy-sauce-based dipping broth, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima tsukemen dipping broth is soy-sauce based, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is made with fish/bonito and seafood dashi, so all tsukemen contains animal-derived stock.

lead: インド料理 ルーパリ 本店（若草店）
name_ja: インド料理 ルーパリ 若草町店（本店）
cuisine: インド料理（北インド系カレー・タンドール料理。本場インド出身のシェフ・スタッフによる地元密着の独立店）
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. Naan and the breads are wheat-based, and the popular set lunches center on naan, so they are not gluten-free. Turmeric/saffron rice with a curry could be a starting point, but Indian curries here may use wheat-thickened or dairy bases and no celiac-safe handling is stated. A coeliac should phone ahead (082-264-1333) and confirm dish by dish; assume cross-contact in a busy shared kitchen.
vegan_detail: As a North Indian restaurant it carries genuine meat-free items: vegetable salad with beans, vegetable pakora (Indian-style vegetable tempura), and vegetable/chickpea-style curries. However these are not certified vegan — paneer (cheese) pakora is on the menu, and curries and naan commonly use ghee, butter, cream, yogurt or milk. Ask for a fully plant-based vegetable or dal curry without dairy, and pair with plain rice rather than naan (naan often contains milk/yogurt). Vegetarian is easy here; strict vegan requires asking the staff to omit dairy.
address_ja: 広島県広島市東区若草町14-32
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [132.479126, 34.397625] for 広島市東区若草町14-32, within the Hiroshima box. Location is just east of JR Hiroshima Station (Shinkansen/Wakakusa side), about a 5-minute walk.
neighborhood: 広島市東区若草町（広島駅 新幹線口・若草町側、徒歩約5分）
seats_text: About 30 seats (Tabelog lists 30; a sit-down Indian dining room).
closed_days: No regular closing day; closed only over the New Year holidays (年末年始).
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, foreigner-friendly Indian restaurant where much of the staff is from India and English is often understood, so a foreign student can order easily; a few words of Japanese or simple English both work fine.
caveats: Naming: the shop's own site labels a "honten" with a Kaitaichi-station description, but the Tabelog page for this address (若草町14-32, tel 082-264-1333, near Hiroshima Station) is the Wakakusa branch the user targeted. Seat count (30) is from the listing and may vary. Vegetarian items are clearly present; vegan and gluten-free require confirming dairy/wheat with staff. This sits near Hiroshima Station rather than the A-Bomb Dome or Miyajima, but it is in central Hiroshima.

bio.background: A long-running Indian restaurant a short walk from Hiroshima Station, where much of the kitchen and floor staff are from India, giving the food and the room an authentic feel. It has become a neighborhood fixture, especially at lunch, when a queue often forms for its generous curry sets.
bio.specialty: North Indian curries cooked with Indian spices, served with freshly baked naan and turmeric rice. The set lunches let you choose from several curries (a popular nine-curry-pick lunch and the Roopali Special Set with tandoori items) with free refills of naan and rice.

anecdotes.text: At lunchtime a line forms outside for the choose-your-curry sets, a sign of how the spot has been embraced locally.
Diners pick their favorites from several curries and the naan and rice can be refilled, which is part of its everyday appeal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan and the bread-based sets are wheat; in a shared Indian kitchen with flour and fried items, cross-contact with wheat is likely and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable pakora is fried alongside paneer (cheese) pakora, and curries/naan commonly use ghee, butter, cream or yogurt, so plant-based dishes may share oil and dairy unless you ask staff to omit them.

positives.text: Genuine meat-free items exist, including vegetable salad with beans, vegetable pakora, and vegetable/chickpea-style curries, so vegetarians have real choices.

cuisine: Mexican / California-style Mexican-American (tacos, burritos, enchiladas, fresh salsa & guacamole)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Core dishes (burritos, soft tacos, enchiladas, chimichangas) are built on wheat-flour tortillas; fried items like fish tacos and chimichangas use battered/deep-fried components, and there is no documented dedicated fryer or gluten-free protocol. No gluten-free menu or claims found on any source. Corn-tortilla or naturally GF options may be possible to ask about in person, but cross-contact cannot be assumed safe.
vegan_detail: An omnivore, meat-forward diner (beef, chicken, catfish), but it carries several vegetarian dishes and staff have said they can make them vegan on request. Everything (salsa, guacamole) is made from scratch, which helps with customization. There is no dedicated vegan menu, so vegans should communicate clearly when ordering and confirm no animal products (cheese, sour cream, lard) are used.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町6-4 エクサビル4F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区袋町6-4 returned [132.45961, 34.39167], within the Hiroshima box. Location is central Naka-ku near Fukuromachi Park / Namiki-dori, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park and A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 袋町・並木通り (中区), central Hiroshima near Fukuromachi Park
seats_text: Around 41-45 seats: counter ~9, bar counter ~5, tables ~32; casual space with sofa seating by the windows. On the 4th floor of the Exa Building.
hours_raw: Mon 17:00-00:00; Tue closed; Wed 17:00-00:00; Thu 17:00-22:00; Fri 17:00-23:00; Sat 11:30-14:00 (LO 13:15) & 17:00-23:00; Sun 11:30-14:00 (LO 13:15) & 18:00-23:00; holidays lunch 11:30-14:00. Lunch service is sometimes closed for food prep; dinner is the main service. (Sources vary slightly on exact closing times.)
closed_days: Tuesdays. Lunch can also be closed on short notice for food preparation.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, expat-friendly Mexican diner where the staff are known to be very welcoming and English is workable, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about vegetarian or vegan adaptations.
caveats: Meat- and fish-heavy menu; no dedicated vegan or gluten-free menu, so dietary needs must be communicated when ordering. Hours are irregular and lunch may close unexpectedly for prep, so it is safest to visit for dinner or call ahead. Closed Tuesdays.

bio.background: Graffity is a San Diego-inspired Mexican and California-style diner in central Hiroshima, run by the Nakashima brothers and going strong for around 17 years. Chef Kai developed his Mexican and Tex-Mex cooking during five years working in the San Diego area before bringing that style home to Hiroshima.
bio.origin: California / San Diego-style Mexican and Tex-Mex, adapted in Hiroshima
bio.specialty: Burritos, enchiladas and soft tacos with grilled chicken or beef, fish tacos and Cajun-grilled catfish, alongside the kitchen's own fresh salsa and guacamole
bio.philosophy: A from-scratch kitchen: every dish and condiment is made in-house so the flavors are as full and fresh as they should be, exemplified by the super-fresh salsa and guacamole.

anecdotes.text: Chef Kai picked up his ideas about Mexican and Tex-Mex food during five years working in the San Diego area, and makes every dish and condiment from scratch.
The restaurant is run by the friendly Nakashima brothers and has been a popular, expat-loved spot for around 17 years, with a fun casual space, sofas by the windows, and counter seating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dishes are built on wheat-flour tortillas (burritos, soft tacos, enchiladas) and several items are battered and deep-fried (fish tacos, chimichangas), so gluten exposure and shared-fryer cross-contact are likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian dishes can reportedly be made vegan on request, but Mexican dishes here commonly include cheese and sour cream and the kitchen is meat/fish-heavy, so confirm omission of dairy and animal fats when ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as very friendly and have indicated they can adapt vegetarian dishes to vegan, suggesting willingness to accommodate requests communicated at the table.

positives.text: Everything is made from scratch in-house, including the fresh salsa and guacamole, which makes plant-based customization easier to request.

lead: あなごめしうえの 宮島口本店
name_ja: あなごめしうえの 宮島口本店
cuisine: Anagomeshi (conger eel over soy-seasoned rice); also bento and donburi
neighborhood: Miyajimaguchi (mainland gateway opposite Itsukushima Shrine), steps from JR Miyajimaguchi Station and the Miyajima ferry terminal
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島口1-5-11
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島口1-5-11 returned [132.303543, 34.311611], within the Hiroshima box; lands at Miyajimaguchi on the mainland.
seats_text: 32 seats (7 tables of 4 plus 4 counter seats)
hours_raw: Dining hall 10:00-19:00 (Wed until 18:00); bento counter 9:00-19:00 (Wed until 18:00); last order ~30 min before closing; closes early when sold out. Open year-round.
closed_days: None (open year-round, 年中無休); may close early when sold out
cultural_comfort_note: A famous tourist-area institution where you can usually get by with a few Japanese words, pointing at the menu, and simple gestures; English support is limited but the dish choices are simple.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature anagomeshi is built on rice cooked with soy sauce (which contains wheat) and conger-eel bone stock, and the eel is glazed with a soy-based tare. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The restaurant is centered on conger eel (anago) served over rice cooked with eel bones and soy sauce; there are no documented plant-based dishes.
caveats: Hours can end early once the day's anago sells out. The Tanikichi sit-down restaurant operated by the same family keeps different hours (lunch 11:00-15:00; dinner Fri-Sun) and is closed Tue-Wed, separate from the main shop verified here.

bio.background: Founded in 1901 (Meiji 34), the shop began when the Ueno family, who had dealt in rice on Miyajima, opened a tea stand at Miyajimaguchi station and created anagomeshi as a railway station bento. Sold along the Sanyo Main Line, it grew famous over more than 110 years and is regarded as the birthplace of conger-eel rice.
bio.specialty: Anagomeshi: thick layers of grilled conger eel over rice that is cooked with conger-eel bones and soy sauce, giving the rice a deep savory flavor rather than serving it plain white.
bio.philosophy: The current fourth-generation owner emphasizes guarding the traditional family taste while adapting to modern challenges such as the declining conger-eel catch.

anecdotes.text: The dish was originally devised as a station boxed lunch (ekiben) sold at Miyajimaguchi and along the Sanyo Main Line, and the recipe has been carried on for over 110 years.
What distinguishes the dish is cooking the rice with conger-eel bones and soy sauce so the base itself is richly flavored.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature rice is cooked with soy sauce and conger-eel bone stock, and the eel is finished with a traditional soy-based tare; soy sauce contains wheat.

lead: お好み焼き・鉄板串焼き やまだ
name_ja: お好み焼き・鉄板串焼き やまだ
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppan kushiyaki (grilled skewers); a small owner-run teppan izakaya
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and topped with fresh wheat noodles (肉玉そば) and okonomiyaki sauce, which is typically wheat/soy-based. No gluten-free batter, noodle, or sauce alternatives are documented, and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free preparation area.
vegan_detail: Largely not vegan. Core dishes use egg, pork, beef, chicken, and local oysters, and okonomiyaki includes egg plus likely fish/bonito-derived sauce. The menu page notes some vegetarian options exist, but nothing is documented as confirmed vegan (no specified plant-based broth or sauce), so a vegan diner would need to ask in person about specific items.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-29 ファブリカビル 101
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-29 returned [132.455719, 34.39521], within the Hiroshima bounding box; consistent with a location near Kamiyachō-nishi tram stop, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区大手町（紙屋町西／平和記念公園から徒歩約5分）
seats_text: 37 seats (counter 5, tables 32)
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, locally run evening teppan izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will go a long way; note one order per person is required.
caveats: Wheat-heavy okonomiyaki and noodle dishes make this unsuitable for strict gluten-free diners, and meat/seafood-centered cooking on shared teppan makes it a difficult choice for vegans. Open evenings only (from 17:30), closed Sundays, one order per person required.

bio.background: A small independent teppan izakaya that opened in 2018, tucked into an alley behind the Edion main-store west building and about a minute on foot from the Kamiyachō-nishi tram stop, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki paired with a wide range of teppan-grilled skewers, letting guests enjoy two of the city's signature ways of cooking on the iron griddle in one sitting.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes its own touches on the classics, using fresh noodles seasoned with an original paitan (chicken-bone) broth that are meant to stand up well to okonomiyaki sauce.

anecdotes.text: The house okonomiyaki, やまだ焼き, layers in squid tempura, shiso (perilla) leaf, and green onion, with deluxe and special versions adding cheese, garlic chips, kimchi, and scallion.

Skewers run from shiso-wrapped pork and chicken-cheese to beef harami and ponzu-dressed gizzard, and a casual 'choi-nomi' set pairs a drink with a small dish and three skewers for easy after-work grazing.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter and the topping noodles are wheat-based and cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside many other dishes, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is typically wheat/soy-based; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, beef, chicken, egg, and local oysters are cooked on the same shared teppan as any vegetable dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

positives.text: The menu page notes that some vegetarian options are available, so a flexitarian diner may find a few plant-leaning dishes by asking.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (savory layered pancake with noodles)
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀（八丁堀・流川エリア、繁華街中心部）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀7-2 YGビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [132.465027, 34.390129] fall within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 36 seats total (8 counter, 8 table, 20 tatami)
hours_raw: Tue-Thu 17:00-04:00; Fri-Sat 17:00-06:00; Sun & holidays 17:00-01:00; Monday closed
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a lively, late-night local okonomiyaki spot where ordering is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help a first-time foreign visitor.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. This is not a gluten-free-friendly venue and no gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki centers on egg, pork, and wheat noodles, and dishes use animal ingredients and wheat-based sauce. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop that opened in 2013 in the heart of Hiroshima's downtown nightlife district, run by an energetic owner with a small staff. It keeps long late-night hours, drawing locals after work and after a night out.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles, prized for a pleasantly crisp finish; the soba-niku-tama (noodle, pork and egg) is a house favorite.

anecdotes.text: Known among regulars for a lively, welcoming atmosphere and a crisp-edged okonomiyaki, served late into the night in the Yagenbori entertainment quarter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and wheat noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared on a shared griddle with egg and pork, so cross-contact with animal products is expected.

caveats: Address is in 薬研堀 (Yagenbori) within the broader Hatchobori/Nagarekawa entertainment district, slightly different from the target's stated 胡町. Menu data on Tabelog dates to 2013 and may be outdated; the listing advises confirming current offerings by phone (082-542-5889). No official website found. No specific allergy-handling or dedicated-fryer information is documented.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (個人経営の広島風お好み焼き／お好み焼き・ステーキハウス)
neighborhood: 中区鉄砲町（八丁堀近く、広電・胡町電停から徒歩約3分）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町7-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.465317, 34.395042] for 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町7-2; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Small shop centered on counter seating along the teppan griddle; intimate, low-seat-count layout typical of a family-run okonomiyaki spot (exact count not documented).
hours_raw: Tabelog (current): Tue/Wed/Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); closed Mon, Thu, Sat, Sun, and holidays. (An older blog noted lunch 11:30-13:30 plus dinner 17:30-19:30 with noodle dishes from 13:00 — hours have varied.)
closed_days: Monday, Thursday, Saturday, Sunday, and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only, no-reservation local counter shop with limited open days and no English menu noted, so some basic Japanese and flexibility with the short hours will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is built on a wheat-flour batter, topped with wheat noodles (fresh ramen from Iso Seimen), tenkasu (fried wheat batter bits), and finished with wheat-containing okonomiyaki/Carp-brand sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation, and the single shared teppan means heavy cross-contact with wheat.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki is made with generous fish powder (魚粉), pork belly, and egg, and the noodles are seasoned with dashi on the griddle; the sauce is standard (non-vegan) okonomiyaki sauce. The menu centers on pork-and-egg-and-noodle okonomiyaki, with no documented vegan option.
caveats: Open only three days a week (Tue/Wed/Fri) for a short lunch window; cash only and no reservations, so arrive early. Hours have varied over time, so confirm before going.

bio.background: A long-standing, family-run okonomiyaki and steak-house counter in central Hiroshima's Teppocho, just a short walk from the Hatchobori district. It is run as a small team built around the owner, with a daughter and a couple of staff working the griddle.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked with a distinctive light touch: a slightly thick batter is laid down and topped with plenty of fish powder, cabbage, salt and pepper, tenkasu, rinsed bean sprouts, and two slices of pork belly. The cook uses no binding agents, spreads lard onto the griddle rather than over the food, and famously does not press the okonomiyaki down with the spatula, instead letting high heat steam it so it stays voluminous and never squeezed dry. The fresh noodles (from Iso Seimen) are seasoned right on the griddle with dashi, salt, and garlic powder rather than being pre-sauced.
bio.philosophy: A minimal, ingredient-forward approach described as okonomiyaki 'made with the eater in mind' rather than relying on heavy technique.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known as a place former Prime Minister Fumio Kishida frequents when in Hiroshima, and his visits have been featured on national news.

Reviewers note the okonomiyaki is never pressed flat with the spatula, leaving it light and full-bodied rather than dense, and the griddled noodles are seasoned with dashi, salt, and garlic powder instead of sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan griddle used for wheat-batter okonomiyaki, wheat noodles, and tenkasu (fried wheat bits), creating heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Finished with standard okonomiyaki/Carp-brand sauce, which is wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Okonomiyaki uses fish powder, pork belly, and egg, and the noodles are seasoned with dashi on the same griddle, so no dish is free of animal products.

lead: お食事処 新京本店
name_ja: お食事処 新京本店
cuisine: Showa-era late-night diner serving home-style set meals (teishoku) and the house specialty "curry soup" (kare-jiru)
cultural_comfort_note: A deep-night counter spot run by an elderly proprietor where regulars order in Japanese by pointing at rows of pre-made side dishes; manageable by pointing but a very local, no-English atmosphere.
neighborhood: Nagarekawa-cho, Naka-ku (Nagarekawa entertainment district, walkable from the Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町5-9
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 広島県広島市中区流川町５番９号; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Counter seating only (small; exact seat count not documented)
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature curry soup uses a wheat-flour curry roux, and side dishes use wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The curry soup contains beef and beef tendon and a fish-based dashi (kombu, iriko, katsuo); side dishes include meat, fish roe and egg. No plant-based options documented.

bio.background: The shop began in 1945 as a nameless stall a couple opened in the black market in front of Hiroshima Station right after the atomic bombing. After an eviction it moved to the Nagarekawa entertainment district, and after a fire it settled at its current location, where it has now run for about 41 years. Third-generation owner Kono Noriaki worked as a banker until 55, then took early retirement to carry on the family business, standing at the counter twelve hours a day except Sundays.
bio.specialty: Kare-jiru (curry soup): a light, soothing thinned curry simmered with potato, onion, carrot, beef and beef tendon, finished with a closely guarded house dashi.
bio.philosophy: Old-fashioned home cooking with simple seasoning that never tires the palate, so regulars keep returning; about forty pre-made side dishes are served instantly, a style born from early customers who were rushed taxi drivers and nightlife workers.

anecdotes.text: The curry soup was born about forty years ago when the owner's brother-in-law, a heavy drinker, asked for curry thinned out because curry rice was too heavy; the owner's sister Tomoko recalled a school-lunch curry soup and finished it by thinning with dashi. Turmeric is added generously as a hangover soother for the late-night drinking crowd.

The kitchen effectively runs around the clock: the owner's sister preps from 6 a.m. to evening, then hands off to Kono for the 6 p.m.-6 a.m. service, with clientele shifting from salarymen to bar staff to small-business owners through the night.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Home-style braised side dishes and dashi-based dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature curry soup contains beef and beef tendon and a fish-based dashi (kombu, iriko, katsuo), so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

caveats: Exact seat count not documented (counter-only). No official website found. Hours confirmed 18:00-06:00 Monday-Saturday, closed Sundays/holidays. No gluten-free or vegan handling documented. Confirmed currently operating with no closure notice.

name_ja: みのる食堂 エキエ広島店
cuisine: 広島・中国四国地方の地場食材を使った定食食堂（みのる定食、広島和牛・豚のハンバーグ、ステーキ、季節の天ぷらなど）
neighborhood: 南区松原町（JR広島駅ビル ekie 1F）
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町1-2 広島駅ビル ekie 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市南区松原町1-2 (GSI). Lands at JR Hiroshima Station / ekie, within the Hiroshima box. Note: this is by Hiroshima Station, not near the A-Bomb Dome or Miyajima.
seats_text: 40 table seats
hours_raw: 11:00～22:00（出典により11:00-23:00表記あり）。不定休（ekieに準ずる）
closed_days: 不定休（ekie 商業施設の営業に準ずる）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, easy-to-enter set-meal restaurant inside the Hiroshima Station ekie complex with picture-friendly teishoku menus, so a foreign student can manage with basic Japanese greetings and pointing, though staff English may be limited.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on teishoku with hamburg steak, fried items, tempura, and soy-sauce/miso-based seasonings, all of which typically contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer is indicated.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The concept and signature dishes are built around meat and fish — Hiroshima wagyu and pork hamburg, wagyu steak, and other animal-based mains — with soy-sauce and dashi-based seasoning. While some vegetable dishes (e.g., tomato-miso roll cabbage, vegetable tempura) exist, there is no documented vegan menu or vegan-safe preparation.
caveats: This is a chain location operated by JA Zennoh as part of the みのりみのるプロジェクト, not an independent local eatery. It sits at JR Hiroshima Station (ekie 1F), not near the A-Bomb Dome or on Miyajima. Hours and closed days follow the ekie facility and are irregular; sources vary between 11:00-22:00 and 11:00-23:00. No vegan or gluten-free accommodations are documented.

bio.origin: Opened March 29, 2018 at the ekie commercial complex inside JR Hiroshima Station, on the first floor.
bio.background: The restaurant is part of the みのりみのるプロジェクト (Minori Minoru Project), launched in 2009 by JA Zennoh, the national federation of Japan's agricultural cooperatives. The project was created so that producers and consumers could connect through food and farming, showcasing domestic, seasonal regional produce.
bio.specialty: Teishoku set meals built around local ingredients — the signature みのる定食 lets diners choose two mains from a rotating selection, served with rice and miso soup. Standout dishes include a Hiroshima wagyu-and-pork hamburg developed with JA Zennoh Meat Foods and steak/rice bowls featuring Hiroshima wagyu branded 'Motonari'.
bio.philosophy: Built on the idea of 'producers and consumers thinking of one another through food and agriculture,' emphasizing local-production-for-local-consumption (地産地消) and regional revitalization, here highlighting Hiroshima and the broader Chugoku and Shikoku regions.

anecdotes.text: When the Hiroshima location opened in 2018, the concept was to start with Hiroshima Prefecture ingredients and progressively expand to feature products from across the Chugoku and Shikoku regions.
The flagship みのる定食 is a build-your-own set: pick two mains from a lineup that mixes everyday staples with seasonal specials, paired with rice and miso soup.

cuisine: 家庭的な定食・お惣菜の食堂（地元米を使った唐揚げ・定食）
neighborhood: 中区紙屋町・立町（本通／パルコ東館近く、平和記念公園から徒歩圏）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-5-3 P.S平野ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-5-3 returned [132.459656, 34.393921], inside the Hiroshima box; lands in central Naka-ku Kamiyacho, a short walk from the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: 21 seats, counter-style seating
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat 11:00-22:00; Sun 11:00-21:30; Wed & Thu closed (per Tabelog). Note: Gurunavi lists 11:00-21:40 (L.O. 22:00) Mon-Sat and 11:00-21:10 (L.O. 21:30) Sun/holidays with no fixed closed day — hours and closed days vary by source.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday (per Tabelog; another listing shows no fixed closing day, so confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local set-meal counter where you order at a touch-panel ticket machine and pay cash, so a foreign student can manage with minimal Japanese, but staff communication is in Japanese and there is no English menu, so bringing a translation app or this guide helps.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu centers on breaded chicken cutlet, karaage and tempura-style fried chicken (wheat batter and flour coating), and dishes are seasoned with regular wheat-based soy sauce and sauces. There is no dedicated fryer and no gluten-free menu, so this is not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. The menu is built around chicken (karaage, cutlet, chicken nanban), meat-and-vegetable, and fish (hokke/mackerel) set meals, with egg used in some dishes and animal-based dashi and soy sauce throughout. No documented plant-based set meal.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ between listings (Tabelog: Wed/Thu closed, close 22:00; Gurunavi: open daily, slightly different last-order times) — confirm the day's schedule before visiting. The official website was reachable in search but blocked direct fetching (server-side 403), so on-site details came from listing pages. No allergy/gluten-free/vegan information is documented for this venue.

bio.background: A small, home-style set-meal counter in the heart of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park, built around carefully cooked locally grown rice served fresh each day with free refills alongside its set meals.
bio.specialty: Set meals of salt-marinated karaage (fried chicken), chicken cutlet, chicken nanban, and grilled fish, served with generous rice that can be refilled.
bio.philosophy: A calm, welcoming everyday table where even solo diners, including women eating alone, feel comfortable, with rice treated as the centerpiece of the meal.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Hiroshima-grown rice cooked with care daily, with free rice refills on set meals, framing the spot as an honest neighborhood table rather than a destination restaurant.
Ordering runs through a touch-panel ticket machine before you sit, keeping the busy lunch rush moving quickly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried chicken, karaage and breaded cutlets are cooked in shared oil with wheat-coated items, so gluten cross-contamination in the fryer is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals are seasoned in the standard Japanese teishoku style with wheat-based soy sauce and sauces.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is dominated by chicken, meat and fish dishes with egg and animal dashi; no plant-based separation is documented.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (savory griddle-cooked pancake layered with cabbage, noodles, egg, and pork or seafood)
neighborhood: Miyajima, Machiya-dori (about a 10-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry terminal / Itsukushima Shrine)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町483-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県廿日市市宮島町483-2 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island and within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 12 seats (small counter/table shop)
hours_raw: Mon-Wed 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Sat-Sun 11:00-14:00; closed Thursday and Friday. (Some sources note possible evening hours on Fridays/holidays and weekend evening service; weekday lunch is generally not offered.)
closed_days: Thursday and Friday
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the two women who run the shop are used to foreign visitors, so a student with no Japanese can order comfortably here.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and crepe, includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and is topped with Otafuku-style sweet sauce that contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature niku-tama-soba uses pork and egg, other versions add squid, shrimp and other seafood, and the dishes are finished with bonito/fish-based dashi and Otafuku sauce. No vegan version is documented.
caveats: Hours are irregular and differ between weekdays (evenings) and weekends (lunch); confirm before visiting. Cash only and no card/IC/QR payment. Reservations generally not taken, though a phone call may sometimes work.

roles: proprietress (okami) and her daughter run the shop together
bio.background: A small, family-feeling okonomiyaki shop tucked one block off Miyajima's busy Omotesando shopping street, on quieter Machiya-dori. It is run day to day by two women, described as the proprietress and her daughter, and keeps the relaxed, unhurried feel of a neighborhood place rather than a tourist counter.
bio.specialty: Authentic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki; the standard niku-tama-soba (pork, egg, cabbage, bean sprouts and noodles) is the house favorite, cooked slowly for a fluffy rather than crisp-edged result, and finished with sweet Otafuku sauce against crunchy vegetables.
bio.philosophy: A homey spot where you can eat at a leisurely pace; the owners chat casually with guests and the place is built around regulars and an easygoing local atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The shop is run by two women, described as the okami and her daughter, who keep an at-home, leisurely atmosphere and chat casually with customers.
Because most shops on Miyajima close by early evening, Kishibe's weekday hours running later in the evening make it a handy option for a meal at the end of a day on the island.
The okonomiyaki is cooked slowly for a fluffy texture rather than pressed to crisp edges, with generous cabbage, bean sprouts, green onion and seafood toppings available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Everything is cooked on a single shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with sweet Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle and dishes routinely involve pork, egg and seafood, so vegan cross-contact cannot be avoided.

cuisine: Japanese (washoku) — Miyajima specialties: braised anago (conger eel) rice and fried oysters
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), off Omotesando shopping street near Miyajima Post Office
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町531-4
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 宮島町531 (chō/banchi level); within Hiroshima box and consistent with a location on Miyajima island just inland of Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: 9 seats total (6 counter, 3 at tables)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 16:00-18:30 (listed daily; one review noted dinner to 21:00 — hours may vary)
closed_days: No regular closed day listed (open daily per current listings); confirm by phone as small shops vary.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter-style local spot where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner go a long way; English support is uncertain, so pointing at the set menu works well.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature braised anago and anago-tororo rice rely on a soy-sauce-based braising sauce (soy sauce typically contains wheat), and the fried oysters are breaded in wheat flour/panko. No gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu centers on conger eel (anago) and oysters, with rice and sauces prepared using fish-based dashi and soy sauce. No vegan dishes are documented.
caveats: Hours differ slightly across listings (one review cites dinner until 21:00 vs. 16:00-18:30 on the main listing); the second-floor entrance is down a side alley and can be easy to miss. Verify hours and any closed days by phone before visiting.

bio.background: A small, hidden-gem eatery tucked into a quiet side street off the bustling Omotesando approach to Itsukushima Shrine, near the island's post office. Set on the upper floor of a traditional folk-house-style building, it has an intimate, calm room with just a handful of counter and table seats and warm indirect lighting.
bio.specialty: Braised conger eel (anago) over rice, including an anago-and-grated-yam (tororo) rice set, and fried local oysters. Unlike the grilled anago common elsewhere on the island, the kitchen here slow-braises the eel until it is soft and tender.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described it as a quiet hidden find off the crowded main approach, praising the soft braised anago over rice and noting the lunch sets run well under 2,000 yen, roughly half what comparable Miyajima specialty restaurants charge for similar dishes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters are breaded in wheat flour and deep-fried; no information indicates a separate gluten-free frying setup.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature anago dishes use a soy-sauce-based braising/seasoning sauce, which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around conger eel and oysters with fish-based stocks; no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

lead: Cafe Lente（カフェ レンテ）
cuisine: カフェ／軽食。オーガニックブレンドコーヒー、自家製チーズケーキ、トマトカレー（鶏もも肉とマッシュルーム）、赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾットなどの軽食を提供する、宮島・厳島神社近くのオーシャンビューカフェ。
gf_detail: 米を使った「赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾット」がグルテンフリーとして紹介されており、複数の媒体で外国人観光客にも人気と記載。ただし専用調理環境や交差汚染対策に関する情報は確認できず、コーヒー中心のカフェのため小麦使用メニュー（自家製チーズケーキ、ホットサンド等）も併存する。GFを要する場合はリゾットなど該当メニューを指定し、店に直接確認するのが安全。
vegan_detail: 「赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾット」は味噌・豆乳・オリーブオイルを使い、ベジタリアン・ヴィーガンでも食べられると複数媒体で紹介。一方でトマトカレーは鶏肉入り、チーズケーキやカフェオレは乳製品使用で、メニュー全体がヴィーガン対応ではない。ヴィーガンを希望する場合はリゾット等の対応メニューを確認のこと。
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町北大西1167-3
geocode_note: GSIジオコーディングで宮島町北大西1167-3が[132.32164, 34.299492]に解決。広島ボックス内かつ宮島島内に整合。
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島神社エリア、北大西。宮島桟橋から徒歩約15分、五重塔・厳島神社出口近く）
seats_text: 店内約25〜35席（媒体により25席/35席との記載差あり）に加え、オーシャンビューのオープンテラス席あり。席間隔は広めに取られている。
hours_raw: 11:00〜16:00（食べログ）。公式サイトは「11:00〜／土・日・祝日をのぞく不定休」、別媒体では「11:00〜18:00／不定休」との記載もあり、営業時間・定休日に媒体間で差がある。電話 0829-44-1204。2階は宿泊（private inn lente）。
closed_days: 水曜（食べログ記載）。ただし公式は「不定休（土日祝を除く）」とし、定休日は媒体により異なるため要確認。
cultural_comfort_note: 外国人観光客に人気でヴィーガン・グルテンフリー対応メニューが知られており、簡単な日本語と指差し・メニュー名の提示があれば留学生でも安心して利用できる落ち着いたカフェ。
caveats: 営業時間・定休日が媒体間で食い違う（11:00-16:00／11:00-18:00、定休日 水曜／不定休）。訪問前に電話0829-44-1204または公式サイトで確認推奨。パキスタン風スパイスチキンカレーは今回確認した情報源では裏付けが取れず（確認できたのは鶏もも肉とマッシュルームのトマトカレー）。座席数は25席と35席の記載差あり。

bio.background: 宮島・厳島神社のすぐ近く、賑わう商店街から少し離れた静かな一角に建つ古民家を生かしたオーシャンビューのカフェ。古い梁や柱を残した趣ある空間に、アンティーク家具やソファ、洗練されたテーブルを配し、全席から大鳥居や瀬戸内の海を望める。1階がカフェ、2階は2室の宿泊施設『private inn lente』として営まれている。
bio.specialty: オーガニックのオリジナルブレンドコーヒーと、パルメザン・クリームチーズを使い自家製ブルーベリーソースを添えた自家製チーズケーキ。軽食では鶏もも肉とマッシュルームのトマトカレーや、赤米・ほうれん草・豆乳・味噌・オリーブオイルを使ったリゾットが知られる。
bio.philosophy: 席間隔を広く取り、世界遺産の島の風景を眺めながらゆったり寛いでほしいという思いで運営されている。

anecdotes.text: 赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾットはグルテンフリーで、ベジタリアン・ヴィーガンの人でも食べられるとして外国人観光客に人気を集めている。
商店街の喧騒から離れた静かな場所にあり、全席から厳島神社の大鳥居を望めるオープンテラスが魅力と紹介されている。

positives.text: 赤米とほうれん草の豆乳リゾットはグルテンフリーで、ベジタリアン・ヴィーガンの人でも食べられると複数媒体で紹介されている。
野菜をたっぷり使った健康志向のメニューで、外国人観光客にも人気と記載されている。

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（鉄板焼き・居酒屋メニューあり）
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-soy-sauce based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made with egg, pork, squid, shrimp and bonito/dashi-based sauces; the menu centers on meat and seafood (Hiroshima Special, Deluxe with squid and shrimp) plus izakaya side dishes from the chef's Chinese-cuisine background. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5番13号 新天地プラザ4F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (広島市中区新天地5-13); lands in central Hiroshima within the expected box, at the Okonomimura building / Shintenchi Plaza area near Hatchobori.
neighborhood: 広島市中区新天地（お好み村4F、八丁堀駅から徒歩約4分）
seats_text: Counter seating (around 15-16 seats) at the teppan griddle; one published listing states 15, another 16.
hours_raw: 11:30-翌0:00（日曜・祝日は11:30-24:00との記載あり）年中無休
closed_days: 年中無休（None / open daily）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, lively counter okonomiyaki stall inside the famous Okonomimura food building; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way, and English support is limited.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly between listings (15 vs 16). Sunday/holiday closing time is listed as 24:00 while weekdays run to roughly midnight/after; treated as ~midnight close daily. Official okonomimura.jp page was intermittently unreachable at verification time; chef-story details cross-confirmed via search snippet quoting that official page and via Tabelog.

bio.background: A counter okonomiyaki shop on the 4th floor of Okonomimura, Hiroshima's famous building packed with okonomiyaki stalls. The owner came to okonomiyaki after a career as a Chinese-cuisine cook, and that background shows up in a fuller-than-usual lineup of side dishes alongside the griddle work.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on the teppan in front of you and kept hot to the last bite; signature builds include the Hiroshima Special (noodles, meat, egg, squid tempura, green onion) and the Deluxe (noodles, meat, egg, fresh squid, shrimp), plus Chinese-influenced sides such as shrimp chili.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor moved into okonomiyaki after working as a Chinese-cuisine cook, and the shop is described as a noted spot that has won over many entertainers and celebrities.
Okonomiyaki is cooked on the iron griddle right in front of the guest at the counter and held warm on the teppan until the end of the meal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a single-griddle teppan okonomiyaki counter where wheat batter and wheat noodles are cooked on the same surface, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki/soy-based sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle cooks pork, squid, shrimp and egg, so any item would have animal cross-contact.

lead: Caff'e Ponte ITALIANO（カフェポンテ イタリアーノ）
name_ja: カフェポンテ イタリアーノ
cuisine: Italian / cafe
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on wood-fired pizza, fresh pasta, and bread, all wheat-based, so this is not a suitable destination for someone requiring gluten-free dining. No celiac-safe preparation or dedicated handling is mentioned anywhere.
vegan_detail: The restaurant lists vegetarian options, but vegetarian here likely includes dairy and eggs (cheese pizza, gelato), and no fully vegan dish is documented. Much of the menu uses oysters, fish-based sauces, and cheese. A vegan diner would need to ask directly about plant-only preparations; a simple marinara pizza or vegetable pasta might be possible but is not confirmed.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-9-21
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lat 34.394184, lng 132.454117, within the Hiroshima box and consistent with a riverside location along the Motoyasu River facing Peace Memorial Park near the Genbaku Dome-mae tram stop.
neighborhood: 中区大手町（元安川沿い・原爆ドーム前駅すぐ、平和記念公園を望む水辺）
seats_text: 70 seats total (about 30 indoors, 40 on the riverside terrace)
hours_raw: Weekdays 10:00-22:00; weekends and holidays 08:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). In August, weekdays also open from 08:00. Open year-round, no regular closing day.
closed_days: None (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly open-air cafe right beside Peace Memorial Park with multilingual menus, so a foreign student can comfortably walk in and order in English.
caveats: Vegetarian options are advertised but not detailed, and there is no confirmed vegan or gluten-free dish; dietary travelers should ask on arrival. Pizza, pasta, and bread are wheat-based.

bio.background: An open-air riverside Italian cafe that opened on August 1, 2008, on the green riverbank at the eastern end of the Motoyasu Bridge, directly across the water from Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and within sight of the Atomic Bomb Dome. Its white parasols and terrace under the trees, with fresh oranges stacked at the entrance, have made it a landmark stop for both local visitors and travelers from abroad.
