bio.philosophy: The name comes from the Italian word ponte, meaning bridge, reflecting the founders' wish to offer a riverside place of rest and connection, a calm and refreshing waterside spot for people to gather and exchange.
bio.specialty: Authentic Italian cooking built on carefully chosen seasonal ingredients: wood-fired pizzas, fresh pasta, escargot-style roasted Hiroshima oysters, and house gelato including a Hiroshima lemon sherbet, plus takeaway gelato, freshly squeezed orange juice, coffee, and beer.

anecdotes.text: The cafe takes its name from the Italian word for bridge, ponte, chosen because it sits at the foot of the Motoyasu Bridge and was conceived as a place of rest and exchange along the water.
Its white parasols and terrace seating beneath lush trees, with fresh oranges displayed at the entrance, overlook Peace Memorial Park and have made it popular with international visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen runs a wood-fired pizza oven and fresh pasta service, so wheat flour is central to the cooking and gluten cross-contact should be assumed; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes commonly use cheese, oysters, and fish-based sauces; no separate vegan preparation is described, so a vegan diner should confirm ingredients and shared cooking surfaces directly.

positives.text: The restaurant advertises vegetarian options and provides menus in English, Chinese, and Korean, which makes it easier to discuss dietary needs.

lead: Cafe & Space Link（カフェ アンド スペース リンク）
name_ja: Cafe & Space Link（カフェ アンド スペース リンク）
cuisine: アレルギー対応ヴィーガン＆グルテンフリーカフェ（米粉ガレット・米粉パンケーキ・グルテンフリー麺/ラーメン/パスタ・ヴィーガンオムライス等）
vegan_detail: The cafe's own concept is meals made without any animal products. The owner states lunches are prepared "without animal-based foods and wheat products," and the menu (vegan omelet rice, rice-flour galettes, sweets, plant-based dishes) is fully plant-based. No specific notes on cross-contact with non-vegan ingredients were documented, but the kitchen does not appear to use animal products at all.
gf_detail: A dedicated gluten-free kitchen: the owner developed a gluten allergy at age 40 and built the cafe specifically so allergy-affected diners could eat safely. Dishes avoid wheat entirely, using rice flour and soy flour (galettes, pancakes, gluten-free ramen/pasta/yakisoba noodles, rice-flour bread by reservation). No documented detail on dedicated fryer or wheat-free soy sauce, so confirm those directly if highly sensitive.
address_ja: 広島県福山市新涯町1-3-44
geocode_note: Address geocodes to [lng 133.388992, lat 34.465401] in Fukuyama City — outside the Hiroshima target box (lat 34.26–34.42, lng 132.28–132.50), so coordinates set to null. This cafe is NOT in central Hiroshima (Naka-ku) and NOT on Miyajima; it is in Fukuyama City, roughly 100km east. The target's stated area (広島市中区/中心部) is incorrect.
neighborhood: 福山市新涯町（広島県東部）
seats_text: Seat count not documented on available pages.
hours_raw: 11:00–16:30（L.O. 16:00）。定休日：日・月・火（その他、不定休やイベント営業の案内あり）。
closed_days: 日曜・月曜・火曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run cafe where menus and signage are primarily in Japanese, so a phrase or two of Japanese (or a translation app) will help, though the all-vegan, all-gluten-free kitchen makes ordering safely much simpler.
caveats: LOCATION MISMATCH: This cafe is in Fukuyama City (新涯町), in eastern Hiroshima Prefecture — about 100km from central Hiroshima city. It is NOT near the A-Bomb Dome and NOT on Miyajima, so it does not fit the curated "central Hiroshima / Miyajima" scope. Operating status and exact hours should be reconfirmed before visiting: hours and open-days differed slightly between sources (one listing showed Fri/Sat/holidays 11:00–17:00). Note: the domain cafespace.link is an UNRELATED cafe in Sapporo, Hokkaido and should not be used for this place; the correct info page is select-type.com/p/cafelink.

bio.background: A small independent allergy-conscious cafe in Fukuyama, eastern Hiroshima Prefecture. The founder developed a gluten allergy at age 40, which made eating out difficult and isolating.
bio.origin: The turning point came when a friend admitted they hesitated to invite the founder to restaurants because of those dietary concerns. That remark inspired the founder to open a place where people with allergies could eat freely and without worry.
bio.philosophy: Cooking entirely without animal products and without wheat, so that allergy-affected and plant-based diners face less hardship when dining out. The space doubles as a rentable community/seminar room and hosts wellness practitioners.
bio.specialty: Rice-flour galettes, rice-flour pancakes and bread, vegan omelet rice, and gluten-free noodle dishes (ramen, pasta, yakisoba).

anecdotes.text: The owner developed a gluten allergy at 40; a friend's comment that they couldn't invite the owner to restaurants because of dietary concerns prompted the decision to open an inclusive cafe.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner built the cafe specifically around their own gluten allergy and prepares all dishes without animal products and without wheat, so the entire kitchen is oriented toward allergy-safe cooking.

positives.text: Dedicated vegan and gluten-free kitchen — every dish is made without animal products or wheat, using rice flour and soy flour instead.
Founded by someone with a gluten allergy expressly to give allergy-affected diners a safe place to eat.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き (Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki / teppanyaki)
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀（立町電停そば・本通／八丁堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀8-12 今本ビル2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 八丁堀8-12 returned [132.462036, 34.395878], within the Hiroshima box. Building-level point for the chōme; reliable for the block near Tatemachi tram stop.
seats_text: About 12 seats — a small counter plus two tables for four; an intimate family-run space.
closed_days: 日曜日（祝日は不定休）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood okonomiyaki counter where a few friendly words of Japanese and pointing at the griddle go a long way; an English menu has been noted, so a foreign student can manage comfortably.
vegan_detail: This is a Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppan shop built around pork, egg, squid, shrimp, noodles and a wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce, so there are no vegan or vegetarian options. The batter, sauce and shared griddle all rule it out for plant-based diners.
gf_detail: Okonomiyaki here is made on a wheat-flour batter with wheat noodles (soba/udon) and a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. There is no indication of gluten-free batter or a dedicated cooking surface.
caveats: The hours and 12-seat figure reflect the main Hatchobori 8-12 location near Tatemachi; the owners also run a separate larger branch elsewhere in Hatchobori (9-5) that is cash-only, so confirm which location you are visiting. Card and QR payment are noted as accepted at the 8-12 shop.

bio.background: Kurumiya is a small, family-run okonomiyaki house tucked on the second floor of a building down a side street in Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, a short walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. With only about a dozen seats around a counter and a couple of tables, it has the feel of a neighborhood spot rather than a tourist destination.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked layer by layer on the griddle, with a signature 'special' piling pork, egg, squid, shrimp and noodles together; regulars order the niku-tama soba (pork-and-egg with noodles) and pile on the free green-onion topping.
bio.philosophy: The shop puts its pride in ingredients, using free-range (yusei-ran) eggs and its own green onions, and in a careful steaming-and-griddling technique that brings out the natural sweetness of the cabbage.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen makes a point of using free-range eggs and homemade green onions, and is known for a steaming technique that draws out the sweetness of the cabbage.
The okonomiyaki 'special' brings together meat, egg, squid, shrimp and noodles in one layered Hiroshima-style pancake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and cooked alongside wheat noodles on a shared teppan griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with a Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single griddle is used for pork, egg and seafood, so any vegetable-only order would share a surface with animal products.

lead: 広島牡蠣と鉄板焼き 一娯一笑
name_ja: 広島牡蠣と鉄板焼き 一娯一笑
name_en: Hiroshima Kaki to Teppanyaki Ichigo Ichie
cuisine: Hiroshima oysters and teppanyaki — premium raw and grilled Hiroshima oysters, uni-spinach (uni houren), local conger eel (anago), Hiroshima beef, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Core dishes rely on wheat: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and noodles, and the teppanyaki and oyster dishes are typically finished with soy sauce (e.g., butter-soy or ponzu on grilled oysters), which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is documented. A shared teppan griddle also makes cross-contact likely.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around oysters (raw and grilled), sea urchin, conger eel, and Hiroshima beef, and dishes use butter, soy sauce, and fish/seafood. Okonomiyaki typically contains egg, pork, and a seafood/meat-based sauce. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町7-18 古本ビル
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at 流川町7-18; coordinates [132.462799, 34.389156] fall within the central Hiroshima box (Nagarekawa / Yagenbori nightlife district, Naka-ku).
neighborhood: 中区流川町（流川・薬研堀エリア）
seats_text: 28 seats total — 6 at the counter and 22 at tables; groups up to about 20
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 18:00–23:55 (LO 23:00); Sat 17:00–23:55 (LO 23:00); Sun & Holidays 17:00–22:55 (LO 22:00). Sundays often closed; ¥480 seat charge. (Tabelog lists close at 23:00; some listings show close at midnight.)
closed_days: Sundays often closed (may open the day before a holiday); closed Monday when it falls on a national holiday. Hours are irregular.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small late-night local teppanyaki and oyster spot where some Japanese will help a lot; reservations are recommended, the room allows smoking throughout, and minors are not admitted.

bio.background: A small teppanyaki counter-and-table spot tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, operating since 2009. It was conceived as a place where you can taste 'all the best of Hiroshima in one spot' — pairing the city's signature ingredients with hot-griddle cooking in a relaxed setting suited to casual nights out, light entertaining, and travelers.
bio.specialty: Premium Hiroshima oysters served both raw and grilled on the teppan — including prized 'Kanawa' brand raw oysters by the shell and grilled oysters finished with butter-soy or ponzu — alongside uni-and-spinach (uni houren) eaten with baguette, local conger eel, Hiroshima beef, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.
bio.philosophy: The house keeps three words at its heart: 安心・丁寧・笑顔 — reassurance, care, and a smile. The name 一娯一笑 plays on 'one enjoyment, one smile.'

anecdotes.text: The kitchen highlights 'Kanawa' oysters, described as among the safest oysters in Hiroshima, sold by the individual shell, with a summer seasonal oyster also offered.
A signature uni-and-spinach dish is bound in the house's own mellow sea-urchin sauce and eaten with baguette — presented as a teppanyaki-style Hiroshima specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle, and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (wheat batter plus noodles) is cooked on the same surface as other dishes, making gluten cross-contact likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Grilled oysters are finished with butter-soy sauce or ponzu, and teppanyaki dishes use soy-based seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on oysters, sea urchin, conger eel, and Hiroshima beef cooked on a shared griddle with butter and soy seasonings; no plant-based or vegan options are documented.

caveats: Hours vary by source: the official site lists weekday close at 23:55 (LO 23:00) while Tabelog shows close at 23:00 and one aggregator shows close at midnight; hours_week reflects the official site. Sundays are frequently closed but the shop may open the day before a holiday, so confirm before visiting. A ¥480 seat charge applies, the room permits smoking throughout, and minors are not admitted. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

cuisine: あなごめし・牡蠣など瀬戸内の郷土料理（釜飯・海鮮）
gf_detail: This is a traditional anago (conger eel) and oyster eatery. The signature anagomeshi (eel over rice) and oyster dishes are prepared with Japanese soy sauce and sauces that contain wheat, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen handling. Not suitable for someone who needs to avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around conger eel and oysters, with seafood and wheat-based soy sauce throughout. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町856
geocode_note: Geocoded from the 宮島町 (Miyajima island) address via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima bounding box and land on Miyajima island as expected.
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道/厳島神社周辺
hours_raw: 10:30 - 15:00（年中無休、ランチ営業のみ）
closed_days: 不定休（基本的に年中無休との記載）
cultural_comfort_note: A reservation-only, cash-only sit-down restaurant where lunch runs about 3,000-4,000 yen and middle-school-age and younger children are not admitted, so a foreign student should arrange a booking in advance and come prepared with cash and basic Japanese or a translation app.
caveats: Cash only (no cards, e-money, or QR payment) and reservation required. Lunch service only (10:30-15:00). Children of junior-high age and younger are not permitted. Prices run higher than nearby alternatives.

bio.background: Hanabishi is a sit-down eatery just off the seafront promenade near the entrance to Miyajima's main approach to Itsukushima Shrine, about a five-minute walk from the ferry pier. It specializes in the island's two signature dishes: anagomeshi (conger eel over rice) and locally prized oysters.
bio.specialty: Anagomeshi made with wild conger eel, and oyster dishes including oyster clay-pot rice.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the wild conger eel as having firm flesh that is fragrant, light, and clean-tasting.
The interior is often noted as refined and cafe-like, and the room tends to be calmer than the busier shops along the main approach street.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anagomeshi and oyster dishes at this kind of eatery are conventionally prepared with Japanese soy sauce / tare, which contains wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

lead: アールカフェ キッチネッテ
name_ja: アールカフェ キッチネッテ
cuisine: ポートランド風ビストロカフェ。十五穀米のふわとろオムライス、デミグラスハンバーグ、グラスフェッドビーフのベーグル、パスタなど。野菜中心・健康志向のメニューとスペシャルティコーヒー。
gf_detail: Documented gluten-free option: all of the cafe's cakes are made without wheat flour (小麦粉不使用), offered for health-conscious customers. The savory menu, however, is not gluten-free — pasta, bagels, French toast, sandwiches and breaded cutlets all contain wheat, and there is no stated celiac-grade kitchen protocol. Coffee and tea are made with naturally alkaline spring water the owner sources himself. Confirm cross-contact directly if you have celiac disease.
vegan_detail: This is a health-leaning but omnivore cafe, not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The signature dishes center on egg (the fluffy 15-grain-rice omurice), dairy (grass-fed butter, Hiroshima Sunatani-dairy milk), and meat (demi-glace hamburger, grass-fed beef bagel). There is no advertised vegan menu. A vegetarian could likely find vegetable-forward plates or pasta, but most items use egg/dairy/meat, so a strict vegan should ask staff about specific dishes before ordering.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町2-6-25 アールカフェビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市中区大手町2-6-25 to lng 132.455429, lat 34.392445 — within the central Hiroshima box, in the Otemachi district a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 60 seats total: 1F has 7 tables of 4; 2F has 6 tables of 4 (2F noted as currently unavailable).
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Fri-Sat 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sun & holidays 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Lunch served 11:30-16:00 (L.O. 15:00, while supplies last).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, stylish cafe in central Hiroshima where a foreign student can order comfortably with a few Japanese words and gestures; a picture menu and well-known dishes like omurice make ordering easy, though staff may have limited English.

bio.background: Kitchenette is the sister cafe to R[a:r] cafe, and its name means 'small kitchen.' It was conceived as an open, light-filled space inspired by the cafes of Portland, Oregon — a city admired as a place creators from around the world aspire to. The interior was designed by Makoto Tanijiri of Suppose Design Office, the Hiroshima-based architecture studio.
bio.specialty: The fluffy-and-runny omurice built on nutritious 15-grain rice is the signature dish, alongside a hand-formed demi-glace hamburger plate and a grass-fed-beef bagel. Cooking leans health-forward: grass-fed butter, naturally leavened bread, locally produced Sunatani milk and seasonal Hiroshima vegetables.
bio.philosophy: The cafe embraces a local-production, local-consumption (地産地消) ethos and aims to be a crossroads where people meet and a platform from which Hiroshima's culture can reach the wider world.

anecdotes.text: The owner personally drives to Kanagi in Shimane Prefecture several times a month to collect naturally alkaline spring water (pH 8.2), which is then used to brew the cafe's coffee and tea.
The coffee program centers on Q-grade specialty beans (origins such as Brazil, Ethiopia and Costa Rica) served as hand drip.

positives.text: All cakes are made without wheat flour, offered as a gluten-free sweet option for health-conscious guests.
Health-forward sourcing: grass-fed butter, naturally leavened bread, local Sunatani-dairy milk and seasonal Hiroshima vegetables.

caveats: Confirmed open and operating as of the latest available listings; closures are irregular (不定休), so a same-day check or call (082-258-3200) is wise. The 2F seating was noted as currently unavailable. Gluten-free applies specifically to the cakes — the savory menu contains wheat — and no celiac-grade cross-contamination protocol is documented, so anyone with celiac disease should confirm in person. Not a vegan/vegetarian destination; most dishes use egg, dairy or meat.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (savory layered wheat-batter pancake with cabbage, pork, egg and yakisoba noodles)
neighborhood: Minamimachi (Minami-ku), south-central Hiroshima — roughly a 10-minute walk from the Minamimachi 6-chome tram stop; not in the immediate A-Bomb Dome / Peace Park core but in the wider central-south part of the city
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区皆実町4-24-12
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50). Point falls in Minamimachi, Minami-ku.
seats_text: 17 seats, mostly counter seating; small solo-run shop
hours_raw: 11:00-13:30, 17:00-20:00 (sources vary on lunch close, 13:30 per Tabelog / 14:00 per other listings; open until sold out in evening). Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warm neighborhood counter shop run single-handedly by a chatty local owner; little to no English is likely, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe batter and includes yakisoba wheat noodles, plus wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce. There is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The okonomiyaki centers on pork, egg, and the shop's signature dishes use pork fat for cooking; sauce and toppings are not plant-based. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Lunch closing time differs between listings (13:30 vs 14:00); evening service runs until ingredients sell out. Hiroshima box geocode is fine, but note this shop sits in the southern Minamimachi district rather than the A-Bomb Dome core or Miyajima specified in the brief.

bio.background: A neighborhood okonomiyaki shop in the Minamimachi district that opened in 1989 and has been run, for more than three decades, by its founder Hirano-san, who cooks alone behind the counter. Warm and talkative, she has become as much of a draw as the food, and the shop has the easy, lived-in feel of a true local institution.
bio.specialty: The house signature is 'soba rice' (そばライス): a hearty okonomiyaki layered with plenty of cabbage, pork, egg and yakisoba noodles, with sauce-coated rice folded in. It was born in the early years when a hungry high-school boy told her the standard size 'won't fill me up,' prompting her to build a more filling version that became the shop's defining dish.
bio.philosophy: Her stated outlook is simple: as long as customers enjoy eating it, that's enough for her. She cooks without added oil, relying on the rendered fat from quality Hiroshima (Sera) pork, and uses firm noodles chosen to keep the dish from turning soggy, aiming for something filling yet light.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'soba rice' was created after a hungry local high-school student complained that the regular okonomiyaki wouldn't fill him up, so the owner devised a bigger, rice-loaded version.

With the football powerhouse Hiroshima Minami High School nearby, the shop draws many students, and over the years athletes connected through sport — including players from Sanfrecce Hiroshima, the Hiroshima Carp, and the Hiroshima Dragonflies basketball team — have become regulars, making it a quiet hangout for local sports figures.
The owner cooks using no added oil, drawing only on the fat rendered from quality pork raised in Sera, Hiroshima, and selects firm noodles so the dish stays light rather than greasy or soggy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle and built from wheat batter and wheat yakisoba noodles, so the cooking surface is heavily exposed to gluten; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce is a wheat-containing sauce and the noodles are wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked using rendered pork fat on a shared griddle and center on pork and egg, so all items have animal-product contact.

lead: うずしお（本家うずしお）
name_ja: 本家うずしお（うずしお）
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き（牡蠣入りあり）・鉄板焼き
neighborhood: 中区大手町（本通・原爆ドーム/平和記念公園 徒歩約3分）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-5-15-201
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (大手町1-5-15) via GSI; lands in central Naka-ku near Hon-dori, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 約40席
closed_days: 無休（定休日なし）
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, tourist-friendly counter okonomiyaki spot where a smile and a few Japanese words go a long way, but don't expect detailed English allergy support.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a thin wheat-flour crepe and layered noodles, and the finishing sauce is a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The okonomiyaki contains egg and pork and is cooked on a shared griddle, oysters feature prominently, and the sauce is fish/animal-derived; no vegan options are documented.
caveats: Hours vary by source (09:30-21:30, 10:00-22:30, and 10:00-23:00 all appear); confirm on arrival. Cash only and no reservations. The "201" suite number indicates a 2nd-floor location above a shop.

bio.background: A long-running Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop tucked on the upper floor near the west end of the Hon-dori shopping arcade, just a few minutes' walk from the Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. It has served locals and visitors for roughly a quarter-century.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles, with a popular oyster (kaki) version using plump Hiroshima oysters and oyster-butter as a signature add-on.

anecdotes.text: The walls are covered with autographs and signed photos from the many celebrities and Hiroshima Carp players who have eaten here over the years, giving the small dining room a cluttered, well-loved feel.
The shop was featured on a televised national restaurant-tour program, which helped build its reputation among visitors to the Peace Park area.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and noodles, so any non-wheat order would face heavy cross-contact; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, which contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg, oysters and the same griddle are used across dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cuisine: Robatayaki izakaya — straw-seared, primitive-fire, wood-fired and charcoal grilling of Hiroshima/Seto Inland Sea seafood (oysters, local fish) and wagyu beef
neighborhood: 中区本通り (near the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a robata grill built around soy-sauce-based seasonings (which contain wheat) and grilled seafood; the menu (straw-seared sashimi, charcoal-grilled dishes, izakaya plates) is not designed for gluten avoidance and no GF accommodation is mentioned on the official site or listings.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The restaurant centers on seafood (oysters, Seto Inland Sea fish), wagyu beef, and seafood/meat-based robata dishes, with soy-sauce and fish-based seasonings throughout. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are advertised.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通6-14
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 広島県広島市中区本通6-14, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 82 seats across the 1F counter and tables and 2F semi-private and private rooms (4- and 8-person rooms available)
closed_days: 不定休・年末年始 (irregular closures; closed over the year-end/New Year period). Check the official site or Instagram for current closure dates.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed local robata izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the open hearth go a long way; online reservation is available if you'd rather avoid a phone call.
caveats: Course meals require a reservation for 2 or more guests. Closures are irregular, so confirm the day before visiting. Not appropriate for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diets.

bio.background: Sasa occupies a roughly 70-year-old building dating to Hiroshima's post-war reconstruction. Rather than tear it down, the owners renovated it, keeping the original brick-walled, nostalgic character so that diners step into a space that feels removed from the everyday while remaining warm and comfortable.
bio.specialty: Robata hearth cooking using four distinct fire techniques — straw-flame searing (wara-yaki), primitive open-fire grilling, wood-fired cooking, and charcoal grilling — applied to fresh Seto Inland Sea fish, Hiroshima oysters, local vegetables, and charcoal-grilled wagyu. Straw-seared sashimi is a signature.
bio.philosophy: Let the fire and the season lead: source local, fresh ingredients from the Seto Inland Sea and adjust the offering with the seasons, cooking simply at the counter hearth so guests can watch, hear, and smell the food as it cooks.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is built around an irori hearth and a stone/wood-fired kiln at the counter, where guests can watch the flames and catch the aroma of food cooking over straw and charcoal.
The restaurant opened on June 1, 2020 inside a vintage 1940s-50s reconstruction-era building reworked into a brick-walled, nostalgic space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned with soy-sauce-based seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is centered on a shared hearth, kiln and charcoal grill used for fish, oysters and meat, so vegetable items would share fire and surfaces with animal proteins.

lead: おばんざい処 やましたさん家
name_ja: おばんざい処 やましたさん家
cuisine: Japanese home-style obanzai set-meal lunch (vegetable-forward, Shimane produce)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment and no GF accommodation is documented. The obanzai lunch is built around many small side dishes that in traditional home cooking typically use soy sauce (wheat-containing) and dashi; a panko-breaded salt-koji pork cutlet (wheat) is a regular item. No dedicated fryer or GF protocol is mentioned anywhere. A celiac traveler would need to ask in detail in Japanese and should not assume any dish is safe.
vegan_detail: Strongly vegetable-centered: the daily lunch features 10+ small dishes built from fresh Shimane (Iinan-cho) vegetables with house-made condiments (mayonnaise, sesame dressing). However it is not a vegan restaurant. The set includes animal items (salt-koji pork cutlet, dashi-maki tamago egg roll, Onan-cho eggs), and traditional obanzai commonly uses fish-based dashi and soy sauce. There is no documented vegan set. Because the kitchen is small and ingredient-focused, a vegetarian who eats egg may fare reasonably by asking; a strict vegan would need to confirm dashi and individual dishes directly and may have limited options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区猫屋町2-14 グローバル猫屋町1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区猫屋町2-14 returned [132.447708, 34.395477], within the Hiroshima box; lands in Naka-ku Nekoya-cho just west of the Peace Memorial Park, consistent with the listed location near Tokaichi-machi tram stop.
neighborhood: Naka-ku, Nekoya-cho (just west of Peace Memorial Park; ~2 min from Tokaichi-machi tram stop)
seats_text: 7 seats total (3 counter seats plus one table for 4)
hours_raw: 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 14:30); closed Wednesdays. Lunch-only operation.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warm family-run lunch spot where English is unlikely, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the day's set and use a few words of Japanese, but the welcoming "like visiting a country relative" atmosphere makes it forgiving.
caveats: Lunch-only (11:30-15:30, last order 14:30), closed Wednesdays, and very small (7 seats), so it can fill up; payment is assumed cash (electronic payment not documented). The single rotating obanzai set changes daily, so specific dishes vary. It rebranded from a vegetable shop to a restaurant in September 2025, so it is newly established.

bio.background: The shop began in 2021 as 「旬菜マルシェやました」, a direct-sales outlet for vegetables from the owner's home town of Iinan-cho in Shimane, and in September 2025 it was reborn as a sit-down obanzai lunch spot run by a husband-and-wife pair with one additional staff member. The cook, Yumi, holds a food-education (shokuiku) instructor qualification and still drives back to Iinan-cho two or three times a week to hand-pick seasonal vegetables, rice and other produce from a circle of named local farmers.
bio.specialty: A single daily 「ゆみちゃんのおばんざいランチ」 — a tray of ten-plus small home-style dishes that change every day, built around Shimane vegetables, locally milled rice, and house-made seasonings like mayonnaise and sesame dressing.
bio.philosophy: The owners want guests to feel as if they have come home to a relative's house in the countryside, and they cook with additive-free, health-minded seasonings (cane sugar, mineral-rich brown sugar, rice oil) so people can eat with peace of mind.

anecdotes.text: The owner drives to her home town of Iinan-cho in Shimane two or three times a week to personally choose the day's vegetables from local farmers.
On the menu philosophy the cook explains she wants customers to be able to enjoy many different things, each in small portions, all at once.
The name was chosen to evoke the feeling of returning to a rural relative's home, and the kitchen deliberately uses additive-free seasonings so guests can dine safely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is traditional home-style obanzai with many small dishes, which conventionally rely on soy sauce and dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free preparation is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The daily set combines vegetable dishes alongside animal items such as a salt-koji pork cutlet and a dashi-maki egg roll on the same tray, so vegan and non-vegan dishes are prepared and plated together.

positives.text: The kitchen emphasizes additive-free, health-conscious seasonings (cane sugar, mineral-rich brown sugar, rice oil, house-made mayonnaise and sesame dressing) rather than packaged sauces.
Cooking is vegetable-forward, using fresh Shimane (Iinan-cho) produce hand-selected by the owner, with tofu and eggs also sourced from nearby Onan-cho.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き (Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki)
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop. The dish is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter, layered with soba (wheat) noodles, and finished with a sweet okonomiyaki sauce that is wheat-and-soy based. There is no indication of gluten-free batter, noodles, sauce, or any allergy accommodation. Not suitable for someone with celiac disease or a strict gluten-free need.
vegan_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki here is made with egg in the batter, pork (the signature 肉玉そば / niku-tama-soba is a pork-and-egg style), and bonito-derived sauce/seasonings; griddle is shared with meat. The other popular item is a grilled mochi-and-cheese dish (dairy). No vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザ お好み村 3F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the chō-level address 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13; result lng 132.461609 / lat 34.391186 falls within the central Hiroshima box (Shintenchi, Naka-ku), consistent with the Okonomimura building near Hatchobori and Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区新天地 (Shintenchi) — Okonomimura building, near Hatchobori / Peace Memorial Park
seats_text: 15 counter seats only
closed_days: Not clearly documented; closing days follow the Okonomimura building schedule and may vary. Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: The owner, known as Ron, speaks English and is used to welcoming foreign visitors, so an exchange student can order and chat comfortably at the counter.
caveats: Tiny 15-seat counter, cash only, no reservations. Okonomiyaki here is wheat- and pork-based, so it is not an option for gluten-free or vegan diners. Exact closing days are not clearly published; the shop follows the Okonomimura building's schedule, so confirm before a special trip. The official okonomimura.jp floor page was unreachable at verification time; hours and contact were confirmed via Tabelog and the directory listing.

bio.background: Ron was born and raised in Hiroshima and loved okonomiyaki from a young age. Opening this shop on the third floor of the famous Okonomimura building fulfilled a childhood dream.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, with the niku-tama-soba (pork, egg and soba) as the house favorite, plus a popular griddle-seared mochi-and-cheese dish; a broad lineup of teppanyaki sides rounds out the menu.
bio.philosophy: Sticking to the old-fashioned, traditional Hiroshima flavor while keeping a warm, friendly welcome at the counter.

anecdotes.text: The owner, Ron, speaks English and the shop draws a large number of overseas visitors, who feel at ease chatting across the griddle.
The house favorite is the niku-tama-soba: soba griddled until fragrant and crisp, then coated generously in the shop's special sauce — a natural match for a cold beer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat batter and soba noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a sweet okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce that is wheat- and soy-based; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared griddle cooks pork, egg and seafood-derived items, so any dish will have animal-product cross-contact.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き (Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki & teppanyaki)
neighborhood: 中区新天地 お好み村4F（八丁堀／平和記念公園エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザ4F お好み村内
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 returned [132.461609, 34.391186], within the Hiroshima box. This is the Shintenchi Plaza / Okonomimura building; the shop is on the 4th floor.
seats_text: 17 seats, counter/griddle-side seating only (no private rooms)
hours_raw: Mon–Sat & holidays: 11:30–24:00; Sun: 11:30–15:00. (If a Monday is a public holiday, the shop stays open Sunday and closes Monday instead.) Note: Tabelog lists Sunday as closed except for reservations, while HotPepper lists Sunday 11:30–15:00 — hours are somewhat irregular and worth confirming by phone.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day; Sunday hours are shortened (11:30–15:00). Holiday schedule can shift the short day to Monday. Sources disagree on whether Sunday is closed or open short hours, so confirm by phone.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run griddle counter inside the Okonomimura okonomiyaki hall with about half regulars; a little Japanese helps, but pointing at menu items and watching the chef cook on the iron plate in front of you makes ordering manageable.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce typically contains wheat-derived ingredients. Squid tempura (ika-ten) topping is also wheat-battered. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Dishes are cooked on a shared griddle and the standard build includes egg and pork, with toppings such as Hiroshima oysters (with bacon), scallop, squid, shrimp, and tuna-mayo; okonomiyaki/teppanyaki sauces commonly contain fish/bonito and wheat-soy. No documented vegan options.

bio.background: Kaeruttei has run its single griddle counter inside Okonomimura — the original multi-shop okonomiyaki hall in central Hiroshima — since 1991, operated by the owner with one other staff member.
bio.origin: Hiroshima, in the Okonomimura building at Shintenchi.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked in front of you on the iron plate, featuring the local touches of squid tempura and thin bean sprouts, plus seafood toppings like fresh Hiroshima oysters and scallops.
bio.philosophy: The shop leans toward lightly-seasoned cooking and good-quality, largely domestic and local ingredients, with about half its customers being regulars.

anecdotes.text: The name comes from the owner's wish that customers will come back again — a play on the Japanese verb kaeru, 'to return home.'

A visitor described picking the shop on a whim inside Okonomimura and watching the okonomiyaki — with its squid tempura and bean sprouts, alongside oysters — cooked right in front of them on the big iron griddle, calling the result delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made on a shared iron griddle with wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable in this style of cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are cooked together on one communal griddle alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so plant-only preparation cannot be assured.

positives.text: Ingredients are largely domestic/local and the cooking is described as lightly seasoned, and the food is prepared on the griddle in front of you so you can see exactly what goes in.

caveats: Two sources disagree on Sunday hours (Tabelog: closed except for reservations; HotPepper: 11:30–15:00) and on payment (Tabelog notes credit cards accepted; HotPepper says cash only). I treated it as effectively cash-only and confirmed irregular hours — verify both by phone (082-247-8014). The official Okonomimura page would not load (connection refused) so background facts rely on HotPepper and a visitor's note.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き (Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki)
neighborhood: 中区新天地（八丁堀／平和記念公園エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-23 お好み共和国ひろしま村2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区新天地5-23 via GSI; coordinates [132.462067, 34.39143] fall within the Hiroshima box. This is the building (お好み共和国ひろしま村) location; the shop is on the 2F.
seats_text: 7 seats (counter), per Tabelog listing
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 16:00-23:00 / Sat-Sun 12:00-23:00 (per Tabelog). Irregular closures.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 7-seat counter okonomiyaki stall inside a multi-shop building, where you watch the cook work the griddle right in front of you — a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, and pointing at what others are eating works fine.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, which is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat noodles (soba/udon), and finished with okonomiyaki sauce (Otafuku-style) that contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is standardly made with egg, pork, and seafood, and uses bonito/fish-based elements and wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: 姉妹 is one of the small okonomiyaki stalls inside お好み共和国ひろしま村, a Shintenchi food complex devoted to Hiroshima's post-war okonomiyaki culture. The shop is known for a former sumo wrestler who works the teppan, grilling each Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki by hand in full view of the counter.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — the layered, wheat-crepe-and-noodle griddle dish that the surrounding complex is built around.
bio.philosophy: The building it sits in espouses okonomiyaki that evokes nostalgia without heavy seasoning, rooted in Hiroshima's postwar food culture.

anecdotes.text: The cook at the griddle is a former sumo wrestler, and customers come to watch him grill the okonomiyaki right in front of them at the counter.
The shop sits inside お好み共和国ひろしま村, a Shintenchi okonomiyaki complex focused on unfussy, nostalgic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki.

caveats: Tabelog lists the area tag as 八丁堀 but the address is 中区新天地; both refer to the central Hiroshima area near the building お好み共和国ひろしま村. Hours and seat count are from the Tabelog listing and may vary; the official complex site notes an allergy-info section exists but did not expose dish-level allergen details in the fetched content. Owner/cook identity (元力士 寺尾海) is supported by Instagram posts but not by an official corporate bio.

name_ja: 汁なし担々麺専門店 楽 小網町店
cuisine: 汁なし担々麺 (brothless dandan noodles)
neighborhood: 中区小網町（土橋・本通エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区小網町7-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区小網町7-1 returned [132.446075, 34.393528], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Koamicho/Tsuchihashi area near the Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: 12 counter seats only
closed_days: Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat counter shop run single-handedly with a ticket-machine ordering system, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the menu) helps; just pick your spice level (0 to MAX) and hot or cold.
gf_detail: The signature dish is wheat-noodle dandan noodles, and the savory tare typically relies on soy sauce (which contains wheat) plus meat- and sesame-based seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free option, so this is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-forward noodle shop — the dandan noodles and the other recommended bowls (chicken soba) are built on chicken/pork umami and animal-based broths and toppings. No vegan option is documented.

bio.background: A small specialist shop devoted to shirunashi tantanmen — Hiroshima-style brothless dandan noodles — that opened on September 29, 2014. It sits a one- to two-minute walk from the Hiroden Tsuchihashi tram stop in the Koamicho district just west of the Peace Memorial Park, and is run single-handedly as a 12-seat counter operation.
bio.origin: Hiroshima, central Naka Ward near Tsuchihashi/Dobashi
bio.specialty: Brothless dandan noodles (汁なし担々麺) served at 800 yen, with the eater choosing both a spice level (0, 1, 2, 3, or MAX) and a temperature (hot or cold).
bio.philosophy: The owner frames the shop around offering something other places do not, building the bowl on chicken-derived umami, chewy noodles, generous pickled mustard greens (zaasai), and crunchy garlic chips for layered texture.

anecdotes.text: A distinctive feature of the bowl is the unusual sweetness of the pickled mustard greens, which gives the dish more richness and depth than a typical brothless dandan noodle.
Regulars treat a small portion of rice as essential to soak up the seasoned sauce left at the bottom of the bowl, and table-side garlic-chip chili oil lets you shift the flavor partway through.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single-counter shop centered entirely on wheat noodles, so gluten is present throughout the cooking line; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The savory tare for brothless dandan noodles characteristically uses soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes use chicken/pork umami and animal toppings; a shared single-counter kitchen means no separation from animal ingredients.

caveats: No official website found; identity, hours, seats, and dish details confirmed via Tabelog and two independent Hiroshima food blogs. Dietary judgments (not GF, not vegan) are inferred from the documented wheat-noodle, meat-based dish composition; the shop publishes no allergen or accommodation information.

cuisine: Sichuan Chinese cuisine; specialty brothless tantanmen (shirunashi tantanmen)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. The signature dishes are wheat-noodle tantanmen and brothless tantanmen, and Sichuan stir-fries here use soy sauce and likely wheat-based seasonings. No dedicated GF menu or preparation is documented, so this is not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu centers on meat-based Sichuan dishes (mapo tofu typically with pork, karaage, beef oyster-sauce stir-fry) and pork/sesame-based tantanmen. No documented vegan options or plant-based preparation. Vegetable dishes likely contain meat-derived stocks, soy sauce, or oyster sauce.
address_ja: 広島県広島市東区光町1-6-22 光町ハイム 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島市東区光町1-6-22 returned [132.480911, 34.399765], within the Hiroshima box. Location is about a 5-7 minute walk from JR Hiroshima Station Shinkansen exit.
neighborhood: 東区光町 (Hikarimachi, Hiroshima Station area)
seats_text: 26 seats (6 counter, 20 table)
hours_raw: Tue-Sun 11:30-14:00, 17:30-21:00; closed Monday
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual neighborhood Chinese restaurant where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome and a great bowl of noodles.
caveats: Dietary information is inferred from documented menu items; travelers with strict gluten or vegan needs should not rely on this spot. Cards are accepted but QR-code payment is not.

bio.background: The owner-chef built his craft over a long apprenticeship in authentic Sichuan cooking before opening this small restaurant a short walk from Hiroshima Station, where he turns out classic Sichuan dishes alongside the city's beloved brothless tantanmen.
bio.specialty: Brothless tantanmen (shirunashi tantanmen) and Sichuan classics such as mapo tofu
bio.philosophy: Bringing genuine Sichuan technique to an everyday neighborhood setting, and giving the local brothless tantanmen a personal twist with added vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef is described as having trained for 21 years under celebrated Sichuan master Chen Kenichi at Shisen Hanten, grounding the kitchen in serious Sichuan technique.
His brothless tantanmen stands out by including bean sprouts, where most Hiroshima versions use only green onion as a topping, adding a fresh vegetable element to the dish.
It is cited as one of the closest places to Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit to get a proper bowl of brothless tantanmen, about a 5-7 minute walk away.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The brothless tantanmen is described as having a soy-sauce-based seasoning, and Japanese soy sauce is typically brewed with wheat.

lead: やまだ屋 宮島本店
name_ja: やまだ屋 宮島本店
cuisine: もみじ饅頭・和菓子（老舗和菓子店・カフェ併設）
cultural_comfort_note: A famous, tourist-friendly confectionery shop on Miyajima's main approach where you can buy momiji manju, watch them being made, try a hand-baking experience, or relax in the attached cafe; pointing works fine and no Japanese is needed to order.
gf_detail: Their signature momiji manju and kiriyoka are wheat-flour confections (castella-style sponge), so the core products are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or dedicated allergen handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Momiji manju is a castella-batter cake that traditionally contains eggs (and commonly dairy/honey in modern varieties), so it is not vegan. No vegan products or labeling are documented for this shop.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町835-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町835-1 returned [132.309982, 34.272606], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; the main store has a retail shop plus an attached cafe (RAKU山田屋) and a second-floor hand-baking experience space.
hours_raw: 9:00～18:00／年中無休（直営店舗・カフェとも）
closed_days: 年中無休（無休）
neighborhood: 宮島 表参道商店街（厳島神社近く）
caveats: Hours sources vary slightly: the official store page and Tabelog list 9:00-18:00 year-round, while one search aggregator listed 9:00-19:00; confirm seasonal hours on arrival. Primarily a confectionery/souvenir shop with a cafe rather than a full restaurant. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners given wheat- and egg-based products.

bio.background: Yamadaya is a long-established Miyajima confectioner founded in 1932 as a maker of momiji manju, the maple-leaf-shaped cakes that are the island's signature sweet. Its main store sits along the busy approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Momiji manju in a wide range of flavors (more than a dozen year-round, over twenty including seasonal varieties) and its own popular kiriyoka (a soft cake filled with chestnut-and-bean paste).
bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes that its confections are handmade by artisans, and at the main store visitors can watch the sweets being made and even bake their own.

anecdotes.text: The Miyajima main store doubles as an experience destination: a second-floor workshop offers hand-baking of momiji manju, and an attached cafe (RAKU山田屋) serves the brand's sweets on site.
Yamadaya developed kiriyoka, a soft chestnut-and-bean cake, which has become one of the brand's signature products alongside momiji manju.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature products (momiji manju, kiriyoka) are wheat-flour cakes made in a shared confectionery production environment; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Momiji manju is an egg-based castella cake; no vegan products or egg/dairy-free handling are documented.

lead: 大衆食堂 遊のすけ
name_ja: 大衆食堂 遊のすけ
cuisine: Town-Chinese comfort food and set meals (machi-chuka) with a neo-izakaya drinking menu; daily set meals, meat shumai, big portions
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. This is a meat-and-fish-forward town-Chinese shokudo and izakaya (shumai, fried dishes, set meals with soup, pickles and salad). Dishes are built around pork and other meats and are seasoned with soy-sauce-based seasonings; no plant-based menu or vegan accommodation has been described.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen is built around wheat-based soy sauce, fried foods and dumpling-style items like shumai, so gluten is pervasive and there is no documented gluten-free option or allergy handling. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should avoid.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通1-22 佐藤ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the building address (中区本通1-22); landed in central Hiroshima within the expected box, consistent with the Hondori/Tatsumachi/Hatchobori area.
neighborhood: Central Hiroshima, Hondori arcade area near the Tatsumachi and Hatchobori streetcar stops (Naka-ku)
seats_text: 33 seats (6 counter, 27 table)
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sun 11:30-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Tue closed. (Some sources list weekday lunch service 11:30-15:00; hours appear to vary, so confirm before a daytime visit.)
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, welcoming local eatery where a foreign student will be comfortable, though menus and staff are mostly in Japanese, so a translation app or pointing at dishes helps.
caveats: Address and access differ slightly from the requested 八丁堀 location: the venue is on 本通 (Hondori), about a 2-3 minute walk from the Tatsumachi/Hatchobori streetcar stops. Reported hours vary between sources (some list weekday lunch service, others evenings only), so confirm before a daytime visit. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Opened on April 25, 2023, this is a modern take on the old-school neighborhood Chinese diner, billed as a town-Chinese (machi-chuka) eatery crossed with a neo-izakaya. It is the kind of place built for everything from a midday set meal to an early, casual drink with friends.
bio.origin: Hiroshima, in the Hondori arcade district of central Naka-ku.
bio.specialty: Hearty daily set meals (around 21 rotating varieties) and town-Chinese standards, with the meat shumai set a signature plate; portions are known for being generous.
bio.philosophy: An easygoing, anyone-welcome shokudo atmosphere where you can eat well and drink casually from daytime onward.

anecdotes.text: The shop has a retro bathhouse-tile look outside with white square tiles and a white noren curtain, and inside, cream walls, a wooden counter and a big TV for catching baseball games.
The signature meat shumai set (about 900 yen) comes with rice, soup, pickles and salad; the shumai has a dense, slightly chewy texture and arrives fast and piping hot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on fried town-Chinese dishes, dumplings/shumai and soy-sauce-seasoned plates, so wheat and shared frying are expected throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Town-Chinese set meals and izakaya dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese/Chinese soy-sauce-based seasonings, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are meat- and fish-forward (pork shumai, set meals with soup); no plant-based or vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: 広島中華そば (Hiroshima-style soy/pork ramen) and oden
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Chinese-style wheat ramen noodles in a pork-bone soy-sauce broth; the soy sauce contains wheat. The oden is simmered in the same soy-based ramen stock and includes wheat-containing items (fish cakes, etc.). There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a long-simmered pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy-sauce base, topped with house chashu (pork) and the oden is built on the same animal stock. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市西区東観音町3-25
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [132.440445, 34.392788] fall within the Hiroshima box. Address per Tabelog is 東観音町3-25; the official site lists 3-2 — same block near Temmachō tram stop along the Hongawa river.
seats_text: Tabelog lists 30 seats (counter plus tables); the shop's own site states around 45 seats including a 3-seat counter. Small, casual neighborhood ramen shop either way.
closed_days: Sundays (and year-end/New Year, Dec 31 - Jan 4)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a no-frills, cash-only local ramen counter where ordering is in Japanese; a foreign student comfortable pointing at the menu and knowing a few basic phrases will do fine, but little to no English support should be expected.
neighborhood: 西区東観音町／天満町 (Higashi-Kannonmachi / Temmachō), central-west Hiroshima, near the Temmachō tram stop along the Hongawa river, about 2 km from the A-Bomb Dome
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and closed Sundays. Seat count differs between sources (Tabelog ~30, shop site ~45). Prices and hours can shift; the broth recipe and family operation are well documented but exact current pricing beyond the ~¥600-650 ramen should be confirmed on arrival.

bio.origin: Hiroshima, started as a street-stall (yatai) ramen operation
bio.background: A family-run Hiroshima ramen shop that has been serving the same pork-bone, soy-sauce-based chuka soba for around 40 years. The business has carried on across three generations, with the previous-generation owner still involved in preparing the broth, keeping the recipe and methods unchanged from the shop's street-stall beginnings.
bio.specialty: Classic Hiroshima-style chuka soba (~¥650): a pork-bone and soy-sauce broth with house-made chashu, green onion, and bean sprouts. Alongside the ramen, the shop simmers oden in a stock based on the same ramen broth, offering about eight varieties; the daikon radish is a standout, made by sourcing large radishes and using only the center portions, simmered for a precise time.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to keeping the time-honored taste consistent rather than chasing trends - no substitute noodles or oversized portions, just the same broth done carefully, with a welcoming neighborhood atmosphere.
roles: broth still prepared by previous-generation owner

anecdotes.text: The pork-bone soy-sauce broth has been kept unchanged for about 40 years, passed down across three generations, with the former owner still helping make the soup today.
The oden is built on the ramen's own soup stock; the daikon is prepared from large radishes using only the center sections, simmered for a precise duration for the best flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-containing soy-sauce broth are central to the menu, and oden shares the same soy-based stock, so gluten is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce (shoyu) base, which in standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with pork chashu, and oden uses the same animal stock and fish-based items, so no dishes are free of animal products.

cuisine: 昭和レトロな老舗喫茶店 (Showa-era retro kissaten / coffee shop)
neighborhood: 中区本通 (Hondori / Tatemachi, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通3-17
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区本通3-17 via GSI; first feature coordinates [132.459778, 34.393276], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in the Hondori shopping arcade area, consistent with the documented location.
seats_text: 26席 (テーブル中心)。ビルの2階。
closed_days: 火曜日 (Tuesdays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, regulars-oriented retro coffee shop with no indication of English support, so a foreign student is best off going with a Japanese-speaking guide or being prepared to point at the menu and use simple Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a traditional kissaten whose menu centers on omelette rice (omurice), hamburger steak, cakes and morning toast sets — wheat-based dishes and bread dominate, and there is no evidence of any gluten-free preparation or awareness. Celiac travelers should treat it as unsuitable.
vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Signature dishes (omurice made with egg, hamburger steak with demi-glace, cake sets, morning sets with toast and egg) rely on egg, dairy and meat. There is no evidence of plant-based menu items, so vegans should treat it as unsuitable.
caveats: Operating status confirmed as open. Management description varies across sources (one Tabelog-style summary says solo-run; a blog describes roughly four family members), so the exact staffing is uncertain. The hand-roasted coffee and Blue Mountain-bean claims in the target brief could NOT be confirmed from the pages fetched — sources describe a retro kissaten and food menu but do not document the coffee sourcing or roasting, so those specifics are omitted. hours_week is approximated as a continuous 08:00-17:00 block since morning (8:00-11:00) and lunch (11:00-17:00) services run back-to-back.

bio.background: A long-running retro coffee shop tucked on the second floor of a building along the bustling Hondori shopping arcade in central Hiroshima, said to date back several decades. It is the kind of nostalgic neighborhood kissaten where regulars gather for conversation away from the crowds of the street below.
bio.specialty: Old-fashioned coffee-shop fare, with omelette rice (omurice) — often topped with a hamburger steak and demi-glace sauce — as a signature plate, alongside a morning toast set and cake sets.

anecdotes.text: The interior leans into Showa-era nostalgia: cream-toned walls hung with artwork, red velvet chairs and a calm, old-fashioned atmosphere, with fresh flowers set on the tables.
Tables are finished with thin wooden strips about 5mm thick that give a subtle texture, and the upstairs room stays quiet and relaxed despite sitting right above the busy arcade.

cuisine: 江戸前寿司 (Edomae sushi)
neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-11 ヤオキンビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区本通8-11 returned [132.459183, 34.392559], within the Hiroshima bounding box. Lands on Hondori shopping street, consistent with the address.
seats_text: 9 counter seats (all counter)
closed_days: Sundays and national holidays
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, higher-end omakase sushi counter where the chef sets the pace; some Japanese-language ability and reservation etiquette help, and it suits a special occasion rather than a casual drop-in.
gf_detail: Edomae sushi here is finished with wheat-based soy sauce (nikiri) and uses cooked/cured fish; no gluten-free program is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: An omakase sushi counter built around fish and seafood, with wheat-soy seasonings throughout. No vegan offering is documented.
caveats: Omakase-only; courses are in the roughly 22,000–33,000 yen range per current sources, so this is a special-occasion destination. Reservation strongly advised. Course pricing listed elsewhere as 15,000–25,000 appears outdated.

bio.background: A Hiroshima native who fell for cooking as a child and discovered sushi during his university years, Yoshimura trained in Tokyo to learn the techniques and discipline of Edomae sushi before returning home. He opened his own counter at age 35 and has run it for nearly three decades.
bio.specialty: Edomae sushi using seasonal fish from the Seto Inland Sea and the Sea of Japan, prepared with classic techniques of simmering, marinating (zuke), and aging. The rice is seasoned with red vinegar and salt rather than sugar, to draw out its natural sweetness and umami.
bio.philosophy: He works by the motto 'opening for the first time today' (本日開店) — approaching each day's service with fresh sincerity, aiming for craftsmanship and hospitality that leave a lasting impression on guests.

anecdotes.text: Ingredients are chosen by the chef himself, with the day's fish selected fresh each morning before service.
Yoshimura opened the restaurant at age 35 after his Tokyo apprenticeship and has built it into one of the region's most respected sushi counters.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Edomae nigiri is conventionally brushed with nikiri (a wheat-based soy sauce reduction); no gluten-free soy sauce is documented for this restaurant.

lead: 瀬戸内グリル じぱんぐ
name_ja: 瀬戸内グリル じぱんぐ
cuisine: あなご・牡蠣料理／ダイニングバー (anago-meshi, grilled and tempura oysters, Seto Inland Sea seafood grill)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature dishes are anago-don and oyster/anago tempura — tempura batter is wheat-based, and grilled anago and donburi are glazed with a wheat-soy-based tare. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, separate fryers, or allergy handling.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood grill specializing in conger eel (anago) and oysters; the menu centers on fish and shellfish and uses wheat-soy tare and tempura. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町中江町306-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box; located on Miyajima island (宮島町), on the quiet Taki-koji slope near the Itsukushima Shrine approach, diagonally opposite Iwamura Momiji-ya.
seats_text: Approximately 10 seats: counter seating on the first floor plus three table seats on the second floor overlooking the street.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00; 不定休 (irregular closures, phone confirmation recommended)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular / no fixed closing day; call ahead to confirm)
neighborhood: 宮島 中江町 (Miyajima, Nakae-chō — near the Itsukushima Shrine approach)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual family-run grill on a quiet Miyajima back lane — a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though staff may have limited English.
caveats: Hours are short (lunch only, roughly 11:00-15:00) and closures are irregular, so call ahead. Phone listed on local guides is 070-5524-3191. Cards and e-money are not accepted; QR-code payment is accepted, so bring cash to be safe.

bio.background: A small, owner-run grill tucked onto Taki-koji, a quiet sloping back lane on Miyajima near the Itsukushima Shrine approach, sitting diagonally across from the well-known Iwamura Momiji-ya. Away from the busiest tourist streets, it has an intimate, low-key feel, with counter seats downstairs and a few table seats upstairs looking out over the street.
bio.specialty: Anago-don (conger eel bowl) and oyster tempura made from Miyajima-grown oysters, alongside other Seto Inland Sea seafood — fried small sardines (karaage), gansu (white-fish cake fry), and a Hiroshima lemon pork bowl.
bio.philosophy: Known locally for conscientious, honest pricing — serving Seto Inland Sea anago and Miyajima oysters at prices noticeably more reasonable than surrounding shops, so visitors can enjoy local specialties without the tourist markup.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen brings in Seto Inland Sea anago fresh each morning and pairs dishes with Koshihikari rice grown in eastern Hiroshima; oysters are pre-boiled before being fried into tempura.
Regulars praise it as a hidden-gem spot to enjoy anago bowls and oyster tempura at gentle prices on a calm slope just off the shrine approach.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around tempura (oyster, anago) and fried small fish, so the fryer handles wheat batter; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Anago-don and grilled anago dishes use a standard sweet-soy tare, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All signature dishes are fish or shellfish based (anago, oysters, sardines); no vegan or vegetarian items are documented.

lead: 台湾薬膳料理 青葉
name_ja: 台湾薬膳料理 青葉
cuisine: Taiwanese herbal-medicine cuisine and hot pot (薬膳・火鍋)
neighborhood: 中区広瀬町 (Hirosecho), near Tokaichimachi Station and just west of Peace Memorial Park, central Hiroshima
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. As a Taiwanese kitchen, dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce and many fried/braised items (e.g. the signature braised pork rib rice with sweet-savory sauce); the herbal hot pot broth and sauces are not confirmed GF. A gluten-free diner would need to ask carefully about every dish, and no dedicated GF preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian by default. The kitchen is built around the medicinal hot pot, whose broth and ingredients include chicken (silkie/烏骨鶏), duck, pork and seafood (crab, shrimp, squid), so the signature dish is not vegan. However, there are genuine plant-forward dishes on the menu — stir-fried Chinese greens (炒青菜), dried-daikon omelette, and other vegetable plates — so a vegetarian can eat well by ordering selectively. A strict vegan should confirm broth, egg and sauce ingredients with staff before ordering.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区広瀬町7-25
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; result lat 34.399487 / lng 132.446472 falls within the central Hiroshima box, consistent with Hirosecho near Tokaichimachi Station.
seats_text: 35 seats; semi-private rooms available for groups of roughly 10-20; all tables fitted with a tabletop burner for hot pot.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Lunch served weekdays only.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (Wednesdays are most often closed). Weekend and holiday lunch service is not offered.
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, locally-run Taiwanese spot where service is in Japanese and staff are attentive and welcoming, so a foreign student comfortable pointing at the menu and using a little Japanese will be looked after kindly.
caveats: Lunch is weekday-only; the place closes irregularly (often Wednesdays), so confirm the day before visiting. The signature herbal hot pot is meat/seafood based and is typically ordered for two or more, making it less suited to solo vegan/GF diners — focus on the à la carte vegetable plates if avoiding animal products.

bio.background: A long-standing Taiwanese restaurant tucked into the Hirosecho district just west of central Hiroshima, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park. The dining room is lined with rows of jars and bottles of dried herbs, signaling at a glance that this is a kitchen devoted to medicinal cooking. Every table is set with its own burner for the house specialty, a herbal hot pot.
bio.specialty: The signature is a medicinal herbal hot pot (薬膳鍋 / 沙茶火鍋) built on a broth simmered with around twenty-five kinds of herbs and roots, served with a generous spread of ingredients. Beyond the pot, the kitchen turns out genuinely accomplished everyday Taiwanese cooking — braised pork rib rice with an addictive sweet-savory sauce, stir-fried Chinese greens, and a much-praised dried-daikon omelette.
bio.philosophy: The cooking follows the East Asian idea of 医食同源 — that food and medicine share one source — using herbs and roots to make nourishing, body-warming dishes.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is filled with rows of jars of herbal ingredients on display, and the daikon omelette is singled out by diners as a standout dish that shows the kitchen is far more than just its famous hot pot.
Regulars describe warm, personable service, including staff quietly giving younger diners a larger portion of rice — a small gesture of genuine hospitality rather than rote routine.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Taiwanese kitchen, braised and sauced dishes such as the signature braised pork rib rice use a sweet-savory soy-based sauce, which in Taiwanese cooking is normally wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house herbal hot pot broth is made with chicken, duck, pork and seafood, so vegetable items cooked or served alongside the pot are not free of animal-product contact.

positives.text: Several clearly vegetable-forward dishes are on the menu, including stir-fried Chinese greens and a dried-daikon omelette, giving vegetarians real options outside the meat/seafood hot pot.

lead: コトノハ ヴィーガングルテンフリースイーツ
name_ja: コトノハ ヴィーガングルテンフリースイーツ
cuisine: ヴィーガン＆グルテンフリー焼き菓子工房（米粉のクッキー・シフォン・パウンドケーキ・マフィン）
gf_detail: Fully dedicated gluten-free bakery. Every item is made with rice flour and no wheat flour at all (the shop's stated policy is "without wheat flour, eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar"). Because the entire kitchen is wheat-free, the risk of gluten cross-contact is very low, though the shop has not published a formal allergen-segregation protocol.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan. All baked goods are made without eggs or dairy (no animal products), using rice flour and plant-based ingredients with restrained sweetness and no refined white sugar. Products include weekly-rotating muffins (chocolate, berry, matcha), chiffon cakes, pound cakes, cookies, and granola.
address_ja: 広島県広島市安佐南区上安2-16-18
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned [132.446487, 34.472851]; lat 34.4729 is north of the Hiroshima validation box (34.26–34.42), as expected since Asa-Minami ward (Kamiyasu) sits in the city's northern suburbs outside the central 3mi core. Coordinates set to null per the box rule. Approximate location: ~5 min walk from Kamiyasu Station on the Astram Line.
neighborhood: 安佐南区上安（Asa-Minami ward, Kamiyasu — northern Hiroshima suburbs near Kamiyasu Station）
seats_text: Takeout bakery only — no eat-in seating. Two parking spaces in front of the shop. Pre-orders accepted by phone, email, or Instagram DM, with nationwide shipping available.
hours_raw: 水〜土 10:00-16:00（売り切れ次第終了）／定休日：日・月・火
closed_days: Sunday, Monday, Tuesday (open Wednesday–Saturday; may close earlier if sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny suburban takeout sweets shop with no eat-in seating and likely Japanese-only service, so a foreign student is best off knowing a few Japanese phrases or bringing a translation app and treating it as a grab-and-go bakery stop.
caveats: Located in Hiroshima's northern suburbs (Asa-Minami ward), well outside the central A-Bomb Dome area — reachable via the Astram Line to Kamiyasu Station. It is a takeout-only bakery, not a sit-down eatery, and items sell out, so going earlier in the day is safer. Open only Wednesday through Saturday.

bio.origin: Opened in December 2019 in Kamiyasu, in Hiroshima's Asa-Minami ward, taking over a space that had previously been a takoyaki shop.
bio.background: A small, independently run vegan and gluten-free baking studio in the northern suburbs of Hiroshima. Everything is baked in-house from rice flour, with no wheat flour, eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar.
bio.philosophy: The founders set out 'to make snacks that anyone can safely eat' (誰でも安心して食べられるおやつを作りたい) — sweets gentle on the body that people with allergies, dietary restrictions, nursing mothers, and the health-conscious can all enjoy with peace of mind, keeping sweetness restrained and ingredients carefully chosen.
bio.specialty: Rice-flour baked goods: weekly-rotating muffins (chocolate-banana, blueberry, matcha), chiffon cakes (plain, coffee, berry marble, lemon tea), pound cakes (chocolate, matcha-chestnut, lemon-apricot), cookies, and granola.

anecdotes.text: A local food blogger sampled the chocolate-banana and blueberry muffins and called the quality 'unbelievable given the ingredient restrictions,' finding them hard to distinguish from conventional baked goods despite using no wheat, egg, or dairy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen uses rice flour and no wheat flour in any product, so the bakery is dedicated gluten-free and the chance of wheat cross-contact is inherently very low; however, the shop has not published a formal allergen-segregation protocol.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All products are made without eggs or dairy and the shop is fully vegan, so animal-product cross-contact risk is inherently low across the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop's founding aim is to make sweets 'that anyone can safely eat,' explicitly catering to people with allergies and dietary restrictions, indicating allergy-awareness — though no specific handling procedure is documented.

positives.text: Fully vegan and dedicated gluten-free: no wheat flour, eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar in anything sold, using rice flour as the base.
Pre-orders are accepted and nationwide shipping is available, so travelers can arrange items in advance.

cuisine: おでん (oden)
gf_detail: This is a traditional oden house whose broth is built on dashi and soy sauce, and the menu centers on fish cakes (hanpen, etc.), fish, beef and other simmered items. Wheat-based soy sauce and fish stock run through nearly every dish, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation or accommodation. Not suitable for those requiring gluten-free.
vegan_detail: An oden specialist with 70-80+ ingredients including fish cakes, oysters, conger eel, stonefish (okoze), kasubi shellfish and Miyazaki beef shank, all simmered in fish-based dashi (with a Fukui white-miso dashi option). The broth itself relies on fish stock, so even vegetable items are not vegan. No vegan offering is documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀1-23
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.466278, 34.391041] for 広島県広島市中区薬研堀1-23, within the Hiroshima box. Located in the Yakkenbori/Nagarekawa district of central Hiroshima.
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀（流川・薬研堀エリア、銀山町電停近く）
seats_text: カウンター30席、座敷60席 (30 counter seats, 60 tatami seats); counter on 1st floor, counter plus private rooms on 2nd floor
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays; also closed during Obon and New Year periods
cultural_comfort_note: A century-old local oden house where ordering is in Japanese and payment is cash only, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app, and pointing at the simmering pot of ingredients works well.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payment. Closed Sundays and holidays. Reserving a seat may carry a small otoshi/appetizer charge (around ¥220). Dinner-only, opening at 17:00. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Founded in the late Taisho era (around 1924), this oden specialist in Hiroshima's Yakkenbori district has been simmering its pots for close to a century, making it one of the city's enduring old-guard eateries.
bio.specialty: Oden featuring an unusually broad lineup of 70 to 80-plus ingredients at a time, from everyday fish cakes and oversized hanpen to seasonal and regional items such as oysters, conger eel, stonefish (okoze), kasubi shellfish, and Miyazaki beef shank. Diners can choose between a standard dashi and a white-miso-based broth made with miso from Fukui.

anecdotes.text: The shop has been running since the closing years of the Taisho period and is approaching its 100th anniversary as a neighborhood oden institution.

On any given evening the pot holds 70-80 or more kinds of ingredients, with two broth choices including a Fukui white-miso dashi, and signature items running to oysters, conger eel, stonefish and kasubi shellfish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden broth and simmered items rely on dashi and soy sauce (wheat-based), so gluten is present throughout the cooking liquid and there is no separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional oden house, the seasoning is built on standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which contain wheat and fish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ingredients share a common fish-based dashi pot, so even vegetable items are cooked in animal stock; no vegan broth is offered.

cuisine: Oden (Japanese simmered hot pot) — refined Kyoto-style dashi, plus local Hiroshima specialties such as koune (beef neck fat) oden
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Oden is simmered in a soy-sauce-and-dashi broth; Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, and many oden items (chikuwa, hanpen, satsuma-age and other fish cakes, plus the dish often eaten with karashi mustard) contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Travelers with celiac disease should avoid this venue.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The defining element is the dashi broth (made from fish/bonito stock), and most oden items are fish cakes, eggs, beef tendon/koune, or other animal products.
