There is no documented vegan menu or vegetable-stock option. Plain vegetable items (daikon, konnyaku, potato) are simmered in the same fish-and-soy broth, so they are not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町13-9 カノープス銀山 101
geocode_note: Geocoded from current relocated address (銀山町13-9). The shop moved here in 2025 from its former premises inside the 薬研堀 Hiroshima General Entertainment Center; both locations are within a few minutes' walk of each other near the Ginzancho tram stop in central Naka-ku. Coordinates fall within the Hiroshima bounding box.
neighborhood: Ginzancho / Yagenbori area, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (near Ginzancho tram stop, walkable to the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Park)
seats_text: 14 seats total — counter plus two small tables of four. A small, intimate counter-focused shop.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00–22:00; Sundays and public holidays closed.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, chatty owner-run counter oden bar where conversation in Japanese is part of the charm; a foreign student with some Japanese will feel welcome, but expect little to no English and a budget of roughly 4,000–8,000 yen per person.
caveats: Evening-only (dinner) and closed Sundays/holidays. The shop relocated in 2025, so older listings show a different address inside the Yagenbori entertainment center — the current location is Canopus Ginzan, unit 101. Phone reservations recommended for the small counter. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: A small, owner-run oden counter in central Hiroshima. The proprietor, Kurokawa Masanori, worked his way through a series of restaurant jobs before finally opening the shop he had long dreamed of. He named it after a wish to bring customers a little everyday happiness.
bio.specialty: Carefully made oden built around an elegant, deeply flavored dashi broth, alongside Hiroshima touches like koune (beef neck fat) oden. The owner says his goal is to make 'the most delicious oden in Hiroshima,' with the broth as the heart of everything.
bio.philosophy: Inspired by manga depictions of old-fashioned oden stalls, Kurokawa set out to become the kind of quiet, story-bearing counter host who looks after each guest — though he cheerfully admits that once he is behind the counter he tends to talk far more than he listens.

anecdotes.text: The owner says he dreamed of being a 'cool, taciturn proprietor with a story to tell,' modeled on oden stalls he saw in manga — but laughs that in practice he talks too much once the shop gets going.
When the shop moved, the owner carried over fittings such as the doors and furniture from the original premises to preserve the familiar atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All oden is simmered together in a single shared soy-sauce-and-dashi broth, so wheat from soy sauce and from the many fish-cake items is present throughout the pot — even vegetable items share that broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The oden broth is a soy-sauce-based dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the broth is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a fish/bonito dashi and most items are animal-based; vegetable items (daikon, konnyaku, potato) are simmered in the same fish broth, so they are not vegan.

cuisine: 海鮮・小料理居酒屋（瀬戸内の鮮魚、刺身・寿司・魚料理）
neighborhood: 中区袋町（本通・八丁堀から徒歩圏、並木通り周辺）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町2-29
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja 広島県広島市中区袋町2-29 via GSI; coordinates [132.459595, 34.392384] fall within the central Hiroshima box. Note: actual address is 袋町 (Fukuromachi), not 立町 as listed in the target brief, but it sits in the same Hondori/Namiki-dori walking area of central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 15席（カウンター席と奥に座敷）
hours_raw: 月～土: 11:30-14:00, 17:30-22:00 / 日曜・祝日: 定休。ランチは不定休・売り切れ次第終了。夜営業は寿司の提供なし。
closed_days: 日曜日・祝日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 15-seat couple-run spot with a Showa-era feel and cash-only service; basic Japanese helps a lot, and lunch can sell out early, so go with a friend who speaks some Japanese if you can.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya specializing in fresh Setouchi fish, sashimi, and sushi; dishes rely on fish, dashi, and wheat-based soy sauce, so there are no documented vegan options.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Japanese soy sauce (used widely for sashimi/sushi and seasoning) contains wheat, and there is no indication of GF preparation or a dedicated setup.
caveats: Target brief listed the area as 中区立町, but the verified address is 中区袋町2-29 (adjacent neighborhood in the same central Hondori/Namiki-dori area). Lunch hours are irregular and end when sold out; sushi is served only at lunch, not in the evening.

bio.background: A small, family-run seafood eatery in central Hiroshima that has been operating for about 17 years under its current husband-and-wife proprietors. The space carries a nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere and previously housed a sushi restaurant before the current couple took it over.
bio.specialty: Fresh Setouchi (Seto Inland Sea) seafood — sashimi, fish dishes, and sushi at lunch.

anecdotes.text: The couple has run the place for around 17 years; the room was originally a sushi shop before they made it their own, and it keeps a warm, old-fashioned Showa feel with counter seats and a small tatami area in the back.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a sashimi/sushi and Japanese seafood izakaya, dishes are served and seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on fish and seafood with fish-based dashi; shared seafood preparation means high cross-contact for anyone avoiding animal products.

cuisine: 居酒屋・焼き鳥（カウンター中心の小規模個人店）
neighborhood: 中区流川（流川・薬研堀の繁華街）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町4-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町4-5 returned [132.464325, 34.390549], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 8 seats, counter only
closed_days: Sundays (or Mondays if Sunday is a holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat late-night counter izakaya where regulars gather and smoking is allowed indoors; warm and welcoming but with no indication of English support, so basic Japanese helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a yakitori/izakaya whose grilled skewers, tsukune, and oden rely on wheat-based soy sauce and tare; tsukune and other dishes typically contain wheat. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on chicken yakitori, hand-made chicken tsukune, wagyu beef, and oden simmered in bonito-and-kelp (fish) dashi. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: Born in 1968 in Hiroshima Prefecture, Nakahara Junji entered the food world after starting work at a friend's restaurant. Over roughly 38 years he worked in several izakayas and yakitori shops, learning both the craft of grilling skewers and the basics of running a small place. He has been the owner-manager of this counter since it opened in April 2011.
bio.specialty: Yakitori and grilled skewers; he is described as someone who knows grilling inside and out. Signature plates include hand-made chicken tsukune in a sweet-salty glaze, flame-grilled wagyu kouné with salt and pepper, and oden simmered in bonito-and-kelp broth (the daikon is a standout). Drinks lean to easygoing pours including a lemon sour made with Hiroshima lemons.
bio.philosophy: He set out to build an izakaya anyone can casually drop into, and says he wants to keep prioritizing a warm, homey atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: He opened the shop in April 2011 after about 38 years in the trade and has run the tiny counter himself ever since.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare, tsukune, and oden broth at a standard izakaya rely on wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden is simmered in bonito-and-kelp (fish) dashi and the menu is built around chicken and beef, so vegan separation is not feasible.

caveats: Tabelog and Hitosara disagree slightly on opening time (Tabelog 19:00, Hitosara 18:00) and budget; hours_week reflects Tabelog's 19:00-03:00. Smoking is allowed indoors. No official website found; reservations via phone (082-242-0181) or net booking.

lead: 海鮮酒場 牡蠣いかだ屋
name_ja: 海鮮酒場 牡蠣いかだ屋
cuisine: Oyster and seafood izakaya
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a seafood izakaya where dishes rely on wheat-based Japanese soy sauce, ponzu, and miso-based oyster hot pots, and tempura/fried items use wheat flour. There is no indication of gluten-free menus, ingredient swaps, or allergen handling. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe here.
vegan_detail: Oyster and seafood izakaya; no plant-based options documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町1-33 沖田ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the confirmed street address 広島市中区袋町1-33; lands in central Naka-ku near Namiki-dori, within the Hiroshima bounding box. Note: the target brief listed 立町 but every fetched source (Tabelog, Gnavi, HotPepper) gives the address as 中区袋町1-33, which is the adjacent block just off Namiki Rotary, a short walk from Hondori/Hatchobori.
neighborhood: 中区袋町（並木ロータリーすぐ、本通・八丁堀から徒歩圏、立町駅 徒歩約4分）
seats_text: Around 28–30 seats, all table seating (sources vary between 28 and 30)
hours_raw: 火～日・祝日 17:00～翌2:00（料理L.O. 翌1:00、ドリンクL.O. 翌1:30）。定休日：月曜（月曜が祝日の場合は翌火曜休）。生牡蠣は安全上の理由により4月～9月は提供なし。
closed_days: Mondays (if Monday is a public holiday, the following Tuesday is closed instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual late-night seafood izakaya geared toward locals — a little Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, and there is no clear sign of English-language service.
caveats: The brief listed the area as 立町, but all fetched sources place the actual address at 中区袋町1-33 (2F, Okita/Okida Building), one block over near Namiki Rotary. Raw oysters are not served April–September for food-safety reasons, so the signature raw-oyster experience is a winter/cold-season offering. Opened April 2024.

bio.background: A casual seafood drinking spot in central Hiroshima that opened in spring 2024, built around the city's most famous local product: oysters. The name — roughly 'the oyster raft house' — nods to the floating rafts (ikada) on which Hiroshima's oysters are farmed in the bays around the city.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-grown brand oysters served several ways — raw (in the cold-weather months), grilled, and in a warming miso-style hot pot — rounded out with seasonal sashimi platters and a rotating selection of local sake.
bio.philosophy: Cooking around the seasons and the day's catch, leaning on Hiroshima's prized oysters as the centerpiece while letting fresh fish and good sake do the rest.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen builds its lineup around premium Hiroshima brand oysters and seasonal seafood, with daily specials reflecting what the cooks source that day, paired with more than ten local sake.
Raw oysters are served only in the colder months — they come off the menu roughly April through September for food-safety reasons — making this very much a winter oyster destination.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese seafood izakaya, dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce, ponzu, and miso seasonings, all of which are wheat-based; no gluten-free alternatives are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around oysters, sashimi, and fish, and fish-based dashi is standard in izakaya cooking, so plant-based diners cannot rely on dishes being free of animal products.

lead: 魚地心 広島八丁堀店
name_ja: 魚地心 広島八丁堀店
cuisine: Seafood izakaya — Seto Inland Sea fish, Hiroshima oysters, regional Japanese dishes
gf_detail: This is a seafood izakaya where the menu centers on dishes that rely on wheat-based seasonings and gluten. Sashimi and grilled oysters are served with standard soy sauce (which contains wheat), fried items like sweet-shrimp karaage and fried oysters use wheat batter, and the house ganso (fried fish cake) and tempura-style dishes contain wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no published allergen handling, so a strict gluten-free diner cannot be safely accommodated here. Not recommended for celiac/gluten-free travelers.
vegan_detail: A dedicated seafood/oyster izakaya. The entire concept is fresh Seto Inland Sea fish and Hiroshima oysters (raw, grilled, steamed, fried), plus sashimi, fish cakes, and fish-dashi dishes such as oyster-dashi chazuke. Dashi stocks are fish/bonito based and there is no documented plant-based or vegan menu. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町5-14 荘保ビル2F～4F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-banchi address 広島市中区堀川町5-14 via GSI; result 34.392811, 132.462585 falls within the central Hiroshima box, near Hatchobori (Ebisu-dori / Nagarekawa area).
seats_text: 106 seats across floors 2F–4F, with private tatami/sunken-hearth rooms for groups from 2–6 up to 30+, and floor rental for up to about 45 guests.
hours_raw: Sun, Tue–Thu, holidays: 17:00–24:00; Mon: 17:00–22:00; Fri, Sat, day before holidays: 17:00–25:00. Open year-round (年中無休).
closed_days: None — open year-round (年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local-oriented seafood izakaya geared to Japanese group dining and banquets, so some Japanese-language ability or a translation app will help; dinner-only, and reservations are wise for a private room.
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀（堀川町・えびす通り/流川エリア、八丁堀電停徒歩約3分）
caveats: Dinner-only izakaya (opens 17:00). Multi-floor venue (2F–4F) built around group banquets and private rooms; no documented allergen or dietary accommodation, so unsuitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: 魚地心 (Uochishin) is a seafood izakaya in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, built around the catch of the Seto Inland Sea and the city's famous oysters. The kitchen keeps a live fish tank on the premises so guests can enjoy seasonal fish at its freshest, and spreads its dining rooms across three floors of a building near Ebisu-dori, with private tatami and sunken-hearth rooms suited to everything from a quiet pair to a large banquet.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters prepared raw, grilled, steamed, and fried; fresh sashimi from the live tank; and local Hiroshima dishes such as hand-made ganso (fried fish cake), seared kōne, and oyster-dashi chazuke.
bio.philosophy: A focus on carefully selected ingredients and the seasonal seafood of the Seto Inland Sea, served in a setting designed for gatherings — welcome and farewell parties, year-end celebrations, and business entertaining.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant keeps a live fish tank (生簀) in-house so diners can be served seasonal fish at peak freshness.
Oysters, a Hiroshima signature, are offered in four styles — raw, grilled, steamed, and fried — alongside local specialties like hand-made ganso and oyster-dashi chazuke.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried dishes (fried oysters, sweet-shrimp karaage, hand-made ganso) use wheat batter and share fryers in a kitchen with no documented gluten-free protocol.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled oysters are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on fish, oysters, and fish/bonito dashi (e.g. oyster-dashi chazuke); no plant-based options are documented.

cuisine: Fried oyster skewers, anago skewers, anago-meshi skewers and Miyajima beer
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando Shopping Street (Itsukushima)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町1133 錦水館 1F
geocode_note: Address geocoded to 広島県廿日市市宮島町1133 on Miyajima island; coordinates fall outside the Hiroshima-city box but are correct for a 宮島町 address, kept per instructions.
seats_text: Takeout / food-stand style for eating while walking; no documented seating.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual order-and-go skewer stand on the main Miyajima shopping street where a smile and pointing at the skewer count (2, 3, or 5) is all you need — just bring cash, as no cards are accepted.
gf_detail: The signature item is breaded, deep-fried oyster on a skewer; the breading/batter is wheat-based and the items are deep-fried, so they are not gluten-free. Anago and anago-meshi skewers also involve wheat (breading and/or wheat-based sauce). No gluten-free options or accommodation are documented.
vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on oysters (shellfish) and anago (conger eel), both animal products; sauces typically include fish/wheat-soy bases. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
caveats: Hours listed as 11:00-17:00 daily with occasional irregular closures (不定休); confirm on the day of visit. Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payment. The Tabelog listing now shows the renamed form 牡蠣フライ串専門 (formerly 牡蠣フライ串と麦酒).

bio.background: This small skewer stand sits on the first floor of Kinsuikan, a Miyajima inn with roughly 120 years of history, about a five-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry pier along the bustling Omotesando shopping street. It opened in March 2022 as the inn's casual, walk-and-eat offshoot, bringing the island's famous oysters into a hand-held form.
bio.specialty: Freshly fried, piping-hot oyster skewers, served two, three, or five pieces at a time so couples, families, or groups can share while strolling.
bio.philosophy: To let visitors taste Miyajima's signature oysters and anago in an unfussy, eat-as-you-walk format paired with local Miyajima beer.

anecdotes.text: The shop also serves anago (conger eel) skewers and a limited-run anago-meshi skewer, putting two of Miyajima's celebrated specialties onto a single stick for easy snacking.
Run as a lean, often single-staff operation, the stand pairs its oyster skewers with Miyajima craft beer for a quick break during a walk down the shopping street.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All signature items are wheat-breaded and deep-fried; no gluten-free preparation or separate fryer is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built entirely around oysters and conger eel; no vegan items or separate vegan handling are documented.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki (with vegetarian, vegan, and gluten-free dishes available on request)
gf_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe and the regular menu uses wheat-based okonomi/soy sauces, so the default dishes are not gluten-free. However, the Hiroshima city tourism office lists this shop as able to accommodate gluten-free diets (alongside vegetarian and vegan), and the listing notes general food-allergy accommodation. There is no documented dedicated fryer or certified GF kitchen, so a celiac or gluten-sensitive diner must call ahead, explain the requirement clearly, and confirm a wheat-free preparation before relying on it.
vegan_detail: This is primarily an omnivore okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop whose standard menu centers on pork, beef, oysters, squid, shrimp, egg and bonito/wheat-soy sauces, none of which are vegan. That said, the official Hiroshima tourism feature explicitly states the shop can accommodate vegetarian and vegan diners and that it is well received by international visitors, and there are plant-forward teppan options (eggplant, mushrooms, asparagus, garlic chives, edamame, cold tofu). A vegan or vegetarian version is made to order rather than being a fixed menu, so notify them in advance and confirm that egg, dashi/bonito and animal sauces are omitted.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町9-26 トミヤビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; result lng 132.459961 / lat 34.389599 falls within the Hiroshima box, in central Naka-ku just east of Peace Memorial Park near Hondori/Hatchobori.
neighborhood: 中区中町（中央／平和記念公園の東、本通・八丁堀の近く）
seats_text: 16 seats (4-tops x2, 2-tops x2, plus a 4-seat counter); up to about 20 for private events
hours_raw: Lunch (Tue–Fri/Sat) 11:30–14:00; Dinner (Tue–Sun & holidays) 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Closed Mondays. Tabelog lists dinner as 17:30–22:00; Hitosara/Gnavi list 17:00–23:00.
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: English-language support is advertised and the shop is noted as popular with international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about vegetarian, vegan, or gluten-free needs.
caveats: Source pages disagree on exact dinner hours (Tabelog 17:30–22:00 vs Hitosara/Gnavi 17:00–23:00) and lunch may run Tue–Fri or Tue–Sat depending on the source; confirm current hours before visiting. Vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free meals are made on request rather than printed on the standard menu, so contact the shop ahead of time. A one-drink (or 200-yen water charge) order per guest is requested.

bio.background: A compact 16-seat okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop on the second floor of the Tomiya Building in central Hiroshima, a short walk from Peace Memorial Park and the Hondori shopping arcade, marked by a yellow signboard. It pairs classic Hiroshima-style layered okonomiyaki and a teppan grill with an unusually welcoming approach to visitors, advertising English-language support and a willingness to adapt dishes for different diets.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (the house 'Jirokichi' and the 'Jirokichi roll'), grilled koune (beef-cheek) and a range of teppan meats, seafood and vegetables.
bio.philosophy: Making Hiroshima's signature dish approachable for everyone, including vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free guests and travelers from abroad.

anecdotes.text: The Hiroshima city tourism office highlights the shop as able to serve vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free diners and notes it is very well received by visitors from overseas.
The shop's Instagram handle marks a 10th-anniversary, indicating it has operated for roughly a decade.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat-batter pancakes and wheat-soy sauces, so cross-contact with gluten is likely unless a specific gluten-free preparation is arranged in advance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard okonomiyaki sauce and soy-based seasonings used here are wheat-based; the menu does not indicate a wheat-free sauce by default.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The same griddle and tools are used for pork, beef, oysters and other seafood, so a vegan order should be requested ahead so it can be cooked separately and without egg, dashi or animal sauces.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop is listed as offering food-allergy accommodation ('フードアレルギー対応可') and advertises English-language support.
Hiroshima's official tourism site lists the shop as able to accommodate vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free diets.

positives.text: Recognized by the city's tourism office as a welcoming choice for vegetarian, vegan and gluten-free travelers and praised by international visitors.
Plant-based teppan items such as eggplant, mushrooms, asparagus, garlic chives, edamame and cold tofu are on the regular menu.

cuisine: ベジタリアン／ヴィーガン中華（精進スタイル）
neighborhood: 中区中町
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町4-14-104 チュリス中町ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded at chō-level (中町4-14) via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Hondori/Fukuromachi.
seats_text: Around 12 seats — roughly 6 counter seats plus several tables (some tatami seating reported); small individually-run shop.
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00, 17:00–22:00 (lunch reported by some sources as 11:30–14:00, dinner 18:00–22:00)
closed_days: Regular closed days not clearly documented (reported as possibly none); confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual ticket-machine shop run by a couple of staff; no English signage is documented, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the menu) will help, but the all-plant-based concept makes ordering low-stress for vegans.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based Chinese cooking in a shojin (Buddhist temple) style. The shop signage states no meat or fish ("肉・魚不使用") and that it also omits the five pungent vegetables — chives, garlic, onion and scallion ("ニラ・にんにく・玉ねぎ・ねぎも不使用") — with a plant-based broth. Soy meat stands in for pork and chicken (e.g. soy-sauce ramen, mapo tofu, fried "chicken"). Eggs and dairy are not mentioned on the documented menu and the concept is described as vegan/vegetarian; travelers strictly avoiding eggs/dairy should still confirm individual dishes.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is Chinese noodles, ramen, dumplings and soy-meat dishes — wheat noodles and wheat-based soy sauce/seasonings are central, so this is not suitable for gluten-free diners. No GF accommodation is documented.
caveats: Regular closed days are not clearly documented and hours vary slightly between sources; confirm by phone (080-4264-7758) before visiting. No official website found.

bio.background: 桂愛 opened on April 14, 2024 in central Hiroshima's Nakamachi, taking over a space that had previously been a ramen shop. It is run as a small independent operation, with reviewers noting two women working the shop — one cooking in the kitchen while also chatting with guests about how spicy to make their dishes.
bio.specialty: Plant-based Chinese cooking in a shojin style — soy-meat soy-sauce ramen, mapo tofu over rice, plant-based fried 'chicken', and a daily-changing set meal that pairs a noodle or rice dish with a fried item and dessert such as almond tofu.
bio.philosophy: The shop bills itself as possibly the only vegetarian Chinese restaurant in the city, cooking without meat, fish, or the five pungent vegetables (garlic, onion, scallion, chives) and using a fully plant-based broth — an approach rooted in Buddhist temple cuisine.

anecdotes.text: A storefront whiteboard cheekily advertises the place as '市内唯一？ベジタリアン中華' ('the city's only vegetarian Chinese?'), and the kitchen staff personally advise diners on spice levels for the mapo tofu.
The menu runs to around 48 ticket-machine buttons spanning daily set meals, about a dozen noodle dishes, a dozen rice dishes, drinks and small plates.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles, dumplings and wheat-based soy sauce are used throughout, so a fully shared kitchen handles gluten; not suitable for celiac/GF diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as the soy-sauce ramen and mapo tofu rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and Chinese seasonings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs or the five pungent vegetables are reported on the menu — so risk of animal-product cross-contact is low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Kitchen staff engage directly with diners, e.g. advising on spice levels, suggesting an approachable setting for asking ingredient questions.

positives.text: An entirely plant-based menu cooked without meat, fish, the five pungent vegetables, and with a plant-based broth makes this a reliable, low-stress choice for vegans.
Soy meat is used as a meat substitute across signature dishes like ramen, mapo tofu and fried 'chicken'.

cuisine: ビストロフレンチ・サンドイッチ (bistro French; sandwiches at lunch, French cuisine at dinner)
neighborhood: 宮島 (Miyajima)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町681-2
geocode_note: GSI geocoder matched at 宮島町681 (chō-level number 681), coordinates within Hiroshima box; lands on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: 22 seats (per tabiiro listing)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, France-trained-chef bistro tucked in a Miyajima back alley; a foreign student can manage with basic greetings, though a reservation via the website (which supports some English) makes a visit smoother.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The kitchen centers on house-made bread sandwiches at lunch and French dishes at dinner, so wheat is pervasive; a celiac/GF traveler must ask directly and should not assume safe options exist.
vegan_detail: The restaurant's own site states a vegan/vegetarian menu is available (ビーガンメニュー取り揃えております), but no dish-level detail or ingredient/cross-contact information is published. Confirm specifics when booking.
caveats: Whether the place is run by a husband-and-wife couple could not be confirmed from the sources fetched; the only documented detail is that the chef trained in France. Seat count (22) comes from a single listing and was not cross-confirmed. Reservation and payment policies are not explicitly published; reservations are accepted online up to one month ahead but walk-ins appear possible.

bio.background: A French bistro tucked away in a quiet back alley off Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the island's pier. The owner-chef trained in France and was inspired by the regional dishes and traditional confections encountered during that apprenticeship.
bio.specialty: By day, sandwiches built on house-made bread with carefully chosen fillings; by night, leisurely, authentic French cooking. Dishes draw on Hiroshima and Japanese seasonal ingredients and have included oysters with herb butter, fish papillote, mussels marinière, beignets, and seasonal fruit tarts and panna cotta. A notable lunch sandwich pairs house bread with sweet red bean from Iwamura Momiji-ya, a long-established Miyajima mochi shop.
bio.philosophy: Showcase local and seasonal ingredients through honest, unhurried French cooking, with everything from the bread up made with care.

anecdotes.text: The name Cuillere is French for spoon.
A red-bean-butter sandwich uses an from Iwamura Momiji-ya, a long-established Miyajima mochi maker, tying the bistro to the island's local food culture.

positives.text: A vegan/vegetarian menu is offered.

cuisine: すき焼き・和食（広島牛のすき焼き専門）
neighborhood: 宮島（フェリー桟橋・厳島神社 表参道エリア）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町772-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 廿日市市宮島町772-3 within the Hiroshima box, on Miyajima island near the ferry pier / Itsukushima Shrine omotesando area.
seats_text: Seat count not documented on any fetched page. Described as a small place that serves one group at a time, so capacity is limited; reservations recommended.
closed_days: 月曜・火曜（Mondays and Tuesdays）
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run sukiyaki spot near the Miyajima ferry pier; staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a reservation (online via their booking page) and a few basic Japanese phrases will make the visit smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is sukiyaki, whose warishita simmering sauce is made with soy sauce and mirin (wheat-containing), and accompaniments include a wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a Hiroshima-beef sukiyaki specialist; sets center on beef and include egg (for dipping), and the broth/sauce uses soy sauce. No plant-based options are documented.
caveats: Seat count not published; "regular" hours assume the listed lunch/dinner windows apply on all open days (Wed–Sun) — a recently opened spot, so confirm current hours when reserving. No Tabelog page surfaced in searches.

bio.background: A small, recently opened sukiyaki house tucked just a few minutes' walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, in the streets leading toward Itsukushima Shrine. It takes its name from the historic Rokumeikan, evoking a spirit of people gathering, talking, and being warmly received.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-beef sukiyaki, served as a quiet, sit-down meal. Sets pair the beef with carefully chosen vegetables, grilled tofu, a local egg, rice, and miso/aka-dashi soup; dinner adds a beef-broth starter.
bio.philosophy: Sourcing as much as possible from Hiroshima — the beef, vegetables, eggs, and even seasonings — and cooking to let the ingredients' natural flavors come through, serving one group at a time so the meal feels unhurried and personal.

anecdotes.text: The house describes preparing sukiyaki carefully for one group at a time, aiming for a meal that quietly stays in your memory.
Listed by the Miyajima tourism association among small spots that take reservations for parties of just a few people, located near the pier.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on sukiyaki, traditionally simmered in warishita made from soy sauce and mirin, and served with soy sauce — standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sets are built around Hiroshima beef and include a local egg; no plant-based or vegan preparation is documented.

lead: 宮島ゲストハウス三國屋
name_ja: 宮島ゲストハウス三國屋
cuisine: 手作り野菜カレー（動物性不使用のヴィーガンカレー）/ Handmade vegan vegetable curry
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島神社から徒歩約5分、紅葉谷公園近く）/ Miyajima
vegan_detail: The dinner offering is a fully vegan handmade curry. The official meals page states "動物性食材は一切使用しておりません" (no animal-derived ingredients are used at all) and describes the food as suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. The owner explains they made it a vegan curry "anyone can eat" specifically to accommodate the dietary diversity of international guests. The dinner (vegan curry) is served daily roughly 18:30-20:30 and is offered to the general public, not only overnight guests. Served self-service / communally.
gf_detail: The official meals page indicates gluten-free can be accommodated with advance notice (グルテンフリー対応は事前連絡が必要). Because this is a small family-run kitchen serving a single daily curry, GF eaters should contact ahead. No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or dedicated fryer is documented, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out — confirm directly before relying on it.
address_ja: 〒739-0523 広島県廿日市市宮島町327
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 〒739-0523 広島県廿日市市宮島町327 via GSI; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50).
seats_text: Communal/shared dining at a large table designed to encourage interaction among diners; exact seat count not documented. Self-service style.
hours_raw: 夕食（ヴィーガンカレー）18:30-20:30、基本的に毎日提供（変更はInstagramのストーリーズで告知）
closed_days: 基本的に無休（営業日・時間の変更はInstagramストーリーズで告知。事前確認推奨）/ Basically no fixed closed days, but service can vary — check ahead.
cultural_comfort_note: Run by a welcoming, internationally-minded family with an English website and experience hosting guests from around the world, so a foreign student can comfortably visit and ask about the vegan or gluten-free options in English.
caveats: This is primarily a family-run guesthouse whose handmade vegan curry dinner (18:30-20:30, roughly daily) is open to the general public, not only lodgers. Because it is a small home-style kitchen with self-service communal dining and variable hours, you should contact ahead (Instagram or the website contact form) to confirm availability that evening, the price, and especially any gluten-free request. Pricing and exact seat count are not publicly documented. There are few morning/evening eateries on Miyajima, which is partly why they began serving meals.

bio.background: Mikuniya occupies a quiet corner of Miyajima island, about a five-minute walk from Itsukushima Shrine and near Momijidani Park. The main building was once a retreat house for the government Mint (造幣局), and the family later restored a roughly 100-year-old traditional house — designated within the island's important traditional building district — as an annex. It opened in 2013.
bio.origin: Founded in 2013 by the current host's father, a former social studies teacher who wanted to share the charms of Miyajima with visitors from Japan and abroad.
bio.specialty: A daily handmade 'Mikuniya Original Curry' — a vegetable curry made with no animal ingredients at all, served in the evening.
bio.philosophy: The host, Junya, frames travel as a path to peace — 旅をする人が増えると世界は平和につながるんじゃないかな ('if more people travel, the world moves toward peace') — believing that encounters while traveling deepen understanding between cultures. The curry was intentionally made fully vegan so that anyone, whatever their dietary needs, can share the same meal.

anecdotes.text: The host says they made the dinner a vegan curry 'anyone can eat' specifically to honor the food diversity of guests arriving from all over the world: せっかくなら、世界中から来る方々の食の多様性にも応えたいと考え、どなたでも食べられるビーガンカレーにしました.
The house's unofficial 'matron' is Miku, a rescued cat (around 10 years old) who greets guests and embodies the home's welcoming spirit.
Because Miyajima has few places open for breakfast or dinner, the family began serving simple morning fare and an evening curry so guests would not go hungry.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gluten-free can be accommodated with advance notice, but there is no documented dedicated GF prep area in this small home kitchen, so cross-contact cannot be ruled out — confirm directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single daily curry is prepared with no animal-derived ingredients at all (動物性食材は一切使用しておりません), so vegan cross-contact risk is low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free / dietary needs are handled by advance request, indicating the cook will adjust for individual guests when notified ahead.

positives.text: Fully vegan menu (no animal ingredients), made by a host who deliberately chose a curry that 'anyone can eat' to suit international guests' dietary diversity.
English-language website and an internationally experienced family host make it easy to communicate dietary needs.

lead: ちんちくりん 薬研堀本店
name_ja: ちんちくりん 薬研堀本店
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house. The signature dishes are built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles (the famous crispy "Paris" noodles from Isono Seimen), and the okonomiyaki is finished with Otafuku sauce, which contains wheat and soy. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or separate cooking surface. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on okonomiyaki containing egg, pork, squid and other seafood, plus teppanyaki of oysters, offal (horumon) and beef. Dishes use fish-based and wheat-soy sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented on the official site or listings.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区田中町6-3 音戸温泉ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box (Naka-ku, Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district near Ginzancho tram stop).
neighborhood: 中区田中町（薬研堀／流川・胡町エリア、胡町電停近く）
seats_text: 45 seats total: 6 counter, 15 tatami/zashiki, 24 table seats; all tables fitted with built-in teppan grills.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, welcoming okonomiyaki spot used to tourists and solo diners; a foreign student will be fine with a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu, though detailed allergy questions may be hard to get answered.
caveats: Wheat- and seafood-heavy menu with no documented gluten-free or vegan options; one drink per person is required (elementary-age children exempt). Evenings-only, opens 17:00, closed Tuesdays.

bio.background: This is the flagship (honten) of the Chinchikurin okonomiyaki and teppanyaki group, set on Yagenbori Street in the heart of Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district. It anchors the brand that has spread across the city, but the original Yagenbori shop keeps the boisterous, friendly counter atmosphere that built its reputation.
bio.specialty: The house signature is okonomiyaki built on so-called 'Paris noodles' from Isono Seimen: the noodles are boiled thoroughly to draw out their sweetness, then seared in soybean oil so they come out crisp on the outside and chewy inside. The flagship 'Chinchikurin-yaki' layers in egg and squid tempura in the classic Hiroshima style, finished with Otafuku sauce. Beyond okonomiyaki, the teppan turns out Hiroshima specialties like grilled oysters with butter, sea-urchin-and-spinach, and offal.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its mission as inheriting and developing Hiroshima's food culture, with diners seated at table-top grills to share a sense of unity with the cooks as the food sizzles in front of them.

anecdotes.text: The crispy-yet-chewy noodle texture comes from a deliberate technique of fully boiling the Isono Seimen noodles to bring out their sweetness, then searing the surface in soybean oil before they go into the okonomiyaki.
Every table is fitted with its own teppan grill so guests, from solo diners to families, eat alongside the grill cooks in a shared, energetic atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter and the signature seared noodles are wheat-based and cooked on a shared teppan grill alongside many other dishes; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki is finished with Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which contains wheat and soy.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The teppan is used continuously for pork, seafood (oysters, squid), egg and offal; no separate vegan cooking area is documented.

lead: お好み・鉄板焼道場 三箆
name_ja: お好み・鉄板焼道場 三箆
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Nobori-cho, Naka-ku (Hatchobori/Ebisu-cho area, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町7-11 ヤマダビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区幟町7-11 returned [132.46582, 34.394661], within the Hiroshima box. Note: the address is Nobori-cho (幟町), not Teppo-cho as in the target hint; both are adjacent in central Naka-ku near Hatchobori, roughly 1.5km from the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: About 22 seats, counter plus a few teppan-equipped tables; can accommodate small groups.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:45); Dinner 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Wednesdays. (Tabelog lists lunch/dinner with a 17:00 dinner start on some sources; official/HotPepper list dinner from 18:00.)
closed_days: Wednesdays (plus year-end/New Year holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, small counter okonomiyaki spot where the cook prepares each pancake in front of you; basic Japanese greetings help, and pointing at the menu works fine for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour crepe base, wheat noodles (soba/udon), and a wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki contains egg and is cooked on a shared teppan with lard, and dishes feature pork, seafood, oysters and fish-based sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: Dinner start time differs between sources (some list 17:00, official/HotPepper list 18:00); hours_week uses the 18:00 dinner start. Seat count is listed as ~21-22 depending on source.

bio.background: A small, owner-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house that opened in 2020 in the Nobori-cho area of central Hiroshima, within easy walking distance of the Hatchobori shopping district.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built on a signature noodle technique, plus a range of teppanyaki dishes.
bio.philosophy: The cook spreads the boiled noodles wide across the hot griddle and seals the surface with a thin film of lard so each strand turns crisp and stands up, aiming for a pancake made with care, one at a time.

anecdotes.text: The house signature is a noodle method where the boiled noodles are spread thinly on the teppan and given a lard glaze so every strand comes out crisp, finished as a generous okonomiyaki.
Beyond okonomiyaki, the teppan menu runs to local-leaning items like sea-urchin-and-spinach (uni-horen) and garlic shrimp, alongside an original Setouchi-lemon sour.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan griddle used for wheat-flour okonomiyaki crepe and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with a wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauce; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodles are griddled in lard and dishes use pork, seafood and oysters, all cooked on the same teppan as any other order.

lead: お好み焼 鉄板焼 やつなみ
name_ja: お好み焼 鉄板焼 やつなみ
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き (Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki)
neighborhood: 中区上八丁堀（縮景園前駅・女学院前駅、広島城近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区上八丁堀7-27 藤岡ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 上八丁堀7-27 returned [132.46347, 34.399258], within the Hiroshima box; matches the office district near Hiroshima Castle and Shukkeien.
seats_text: Around 20-21 seats: teppan counter seats plus a few teppan table seats (sources vary between 20 and 21).
hours_raw: Mon-Sat: lunch 11:30-14:00/14:30 (L.O. ~13:30-14:00), dinner 17:00/17:30-22:00/23:00 (L.O. ~21:30-22:00). Closed Sundays (some listings also note holidays / day before holidays). Hours differ slightly across sources.
closed_days: 日曜日（祝日・祝前日も休みとする情報あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run neighborhood teppan shop where you sit at the counter; staff are welcoming but service is mostly in Japanese, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help a foreign student feel comfortable.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and wheat noodles (Isono noodles), and finished with Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on pork/egg okonomiyaki (肉玉そば), grilled oysters, pork rib stew, salt-grilled beef and other teppanyaki, plus bonito/fish-based dashi and Otafuku sauce. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Opening hours and seat count vary slightly across listings (lunch ends 14:00 or 14:30; dinner 17:00/17:30 to 22:00/23:00; 20 vs 21 seats). The hours_week reflects the most commonly cited weekday/Saturday hours. Confirm current hours and any holiday closures before visiting.

bio.background: A Hiroshima local who first fell for okonomiyaki during a student part-time job, then trained at well-regarded Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops and at teppanyaki restaurants in Tokyo before opening his own place in November 2019.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — including the signature 'Yatsunami-yaki' with crisp noodles — alongside teppanyaki such as salt-grilled corn and beef, butter-grilled oysters, and a much-loved slow-cooked pork rib stew.
bio.philosophy: To keep serving the unchanging, old-fashioned flavors of Hiroshima, rooted in the neighborhood and the community.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef discovered okonomiyaki through a student part-time job and went on to apprentice at established Hiroshima shops and Tokyo teppanyaki kitchens before opening Yatsunami in November 2019.
He describes his aim as continuing to offer the unchanging traditional Hiroshima taste, rooted in the local community.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle used for wheat-flour okonomiyaki batter and wheat noodles, so high cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with Otafuku okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The same griddle is used for pork, egg, oysters and beef, so any vegetable item would share surfaces and fish/meat-based dashi.

lead: 中華そば うぐいす
name_ja: 中華そば うぐいす
cuisine: 広島中華そば・おでん（ライト豚骨醤油ラーメン）
neighborhood: 中区流川町（新天地公園そば、流川・胡町電停近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16 returned [132.463654, 34.391228], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in the Nagarekawa nightlife district, consistent with the late-night location.
seats_text: カウンターのみ。約10席（うち実際に使えるのは6席程度との来店記録あり）。
hours_raw: 20:00～翌2:00（月～土）／日曜・祝日 定休。営業時間は時折変動することがある。
closed_days: 日曜日・祝日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, late-night, counter-only local ramen counter where ordering is simple ("中華そば") but little English is expected, so a few Japanese words and quiet confidence go a long way for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles (sourced from a local noodle maker) in a soy-sauce-based pork broth, and the oden uses a soy/dashi broth. Wheat-containing soy sauce is integral, and there is no documentation of gluten-free options or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy base, the signature bowl is topped with chashu pork, and the oden includes fishcakes, beef tendon and egg. No plant-based options are documented.
caveats: Late-night only (roughly 8pm to 2am), closed Sundays and holidays, and hours can shift. Counter seats are very limited (about 6 usable), no reservations, and it functions mainly as an after-drinks "finishing" ramen spot.

bio.background: Uguisu traces back to 1948, when it was one of the three pioneering food stalls that gave birth to Hiroshima's distinctive 'chuka soba' Chinese-noodle tradition in the years after the war. The founder is said to have learned the art of soup-making in Manchuria before setting up a noodle cart in central Hiroshima. The original stall ran for 67 years before closing around 2015; the lineage carried on through a noren-wake (branch) arrangement, and this Nagarekawa shop continues the name in the heart of the city's nightlife quarter.
bio.specialty: Light pork-bone-and-soy 'chuka soba' served late into the night, with the negi-chashu (green onion and char-siu) bowl as the house specialty, plus a small counter selection of oden.
bio.philosophy: A no-frills, late-night noodle counter built for the quiet ritual of finishing the evening with a comforting, nostalgic bowl.

anecdotes.text: Uguisu belongs to Hiroshima's 'kotori-kei' (little-bird-named) family of late-night ramen shops, serving a lighter pork-bone soy broth that locals favor as a gentle, guilt-free way to cap off a night of drinking.

Founded in 1948 as one of three original Hiroshima noodle stalls, with a founder who learned soup-making in Manchuria; the original shop closed after 67 years and the name lives on through a family/noren-wake spinoff.
A 2025 visit found the snug counter still busy with customers late at night, with the 800-yen chuka soba and 1,100-yen negi-chashu anchoring a deliberately tiny menu alongside oden.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in shared water and served in a soy-sauce-based broth, so the entire kitchen workflow involves gluten; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce-forward pork-bone base and the oden uses a soy/dashi broth, both of which rely on standard (wheat-containing) Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork-bone broth, char-siu pork, and oden items such as fishcakes, beef tendon and egg are prepared on the same small counter, making any plant-only request impractical.

lead: 中華そば 千番 流川店
name_ja: 中華そば 千番 流川店
cuisine: 広島中華そば・ラーメン居酒屋 (Hiroshima soy-sauce ramen and late-night izakaya)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop. The signature 中華そば uses wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce-based broth (Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat), so the core dishes are not gluten-free. There is no documented gluten-free menu, GF noodle option, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish ramen izakaya. The broth is a soy/animal-style ramen stock and the bowls feature chashu (braised pork); the popular "moyacha" is bean sprouts topped with pork. With 20+ izakaya side dishes built around meat and seafood and no documented vegan offering, there is nothing reliably plant-based here.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町7-8
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町7番8号 returned [132.463272, 34.389118], within the Hiroshima bounding box in the Nagarekawa nightlife district of central Naka-ku.
neighborhood: 中区流川町（流川・繁華街、胡町電停近く）
seats_text: Roughly 30-35 seats with counter and tatami seating; sources vary (Tabelog lists 30, the official site lists 35 with a private tatami room for up to 12).
hours_raw: 火～土・祝日 19:00～翌3:00（L.O. 2:30）／定休日 月・日（不定休あり）。営業時間・定休日は変更となる場合があるため来店前に要確認。
closed_days: Monday and Sunday (with occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A late-night, locals-loved ramen izakaya where ordering will likely be in Japanese and English support is unlikely, so a translation app and pointing at the menu help; cash or PayPay only.

bio.background: A Hiroshima institution that opened in 1951 (Showa 26) and has been serving its 中華そば for around seven decades, becoming the late-night ramen many locals reach for after a night of drinking in the Nagarekawa entertainment quarter.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style soy-sauce 中華そば: a broth simmered for more than 12 hours paired with a secret tare aged about three weeks. The kitchen is also known for its dramatic noodle-draining flourish and a signature bowl of bean sprouts and pork nicknamed 'moyacha'.
bio.philosophy: A 'ramen you want to eat every day' served as part ramen shop, part izakaya, with 20-plus generous side dishes to go with a drink.

anecdotes.text: Open into the small hours, it has become the go-to closing-meal (shime) ramen for Nagarekawa's nightlife crowd.
Beyond ramen it runs as a 'ramen izakaya' with over 20 hearty side dishes, unusual for a ramen shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce broth; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is soy-sauce based (Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat), aged with a secret tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on animal/soy ramen stock and topped with pork chashu; the kitchen is meat- and seafood-centric with no plant-based option noted.

bio.background: A small late-night ramen counter in Hiroshima's Yakaregbori entertainment district. The shop originally served a tonkotsu-shio ramen, then reworked the menu to a simple Hiroshima-style chuka-soba after the pandemic.
bio.specialty: Light soy-based Hiroshima chuka-soba with a gently sweet broth, served as a late-night finishing bowl.
bio.philosophy: A no-frills, comforting bowl meant to round off the night.

anecdotes.text: The owner switched from a pork-bone salt ramen to a simpler Chinese noodle soup, citing COVID and other considerations.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The bowl is a soy-tare Chinese noodle soup; standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and wheat ramen noodles are central to the dish.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is meat/fish based and side dishes include fried chicken and oden.

cuisine: Gyoza specialist (pan-fried dumplings)
neighborhood: Naka-ku, Kanayama-cho (Ginzan-cho), near the Nagarekawa nightlife district, ~1.5km from Peace Memorial Park
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町13-11
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 広島県広島市中区銀山町13-11; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 8 counter seats only
hours_raw: Tue–Sat 17:00–21:00; closed Sunday, Monday and holidays. May close earlier when sold out.
closed_days: Sunday, Monday, and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter run single-handedly by the proprietress where ordering is in Japanese and you follow her lead on how to eat; a friendly but immersive spot best suited to those comfortable with a little Japanese or a phrasebook.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The only food served is gyoza, whose thin wrappers are made from wheat flour. The complimentary bean sprout starter is dressed in ponzu (which contains wheat-based soy sauce). There are no gluten-free options.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The sole dish is pork gyoza (double-ground pork with garlic and Chinese chives), pan-fried in lard. The bean sprout starter is dressed in ponzu containing fish-derived dashi/soy sauce. There are no plant-based options.
caveats: Year of founding varies by source: Tabelog lists 1964 while feature articles describe "over 50 years" and the proprietress as having run it for ~58 years (implying ~1967). Hours per Tabelog are Tue–Sat 17:00–21:00; one food blog cites 17:00–22:00. The shop closes when sold out, so arriving early or reserving by phone is essential. Cash only.

bio.background: Sei-chan is a minuscule eight-seat counter tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment quarter, run single-handedly for well over half a century by its proprietress, Chizuko. She has been wrapping and frying dumplings here for around 58 years, opening up alone each evening and presiding over the counter like the host of an intimate little theater.
bio.specialty: Pan-fried pork gyoza, served with a complimentary starter of chilled, crisp bean sprouts in ponzu
bio.philosophy: An almost monastic focus: the menu is essentially only gyoza and beer. Chizuko begins her prep at 6:30 every morning, double-grinding the pork to build the right texture, folding in Chinese chives from Kochi and garlic from Aomori, and hand-wrapping each dumpling in specially commissioned ultra-thin skins. She speaks of the wrapper as 'living' and delicate, so thin that frying it well demands real skill; the dumplings are cooked in lard-greased iron pans with a signature swirling motion.

anecdotes.text: The bean sprouts arrive first as a free starter and the proprietress shows newcomers the proper way to eat: perch a hot gyoza on top of the cold, crunchy ponzu-dressed sprouts for contrast.
The counter doubles as a neighborhood living room — regulars have been known to celebrate birthdays by sticking candles into a gyoza in place of a cake.
Beer is Kirin Lager only, and a small azuki (red-bean) bar is handed out as a sweet after the meal.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only food is wheat-flour gyoza fried in shared lard-greased iron pans; there is no gluten-free preparation or separate cooking surface.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The complimentary bean sprout starter is dressed in ponzu, which contains wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish centers on pork and the dumplings are fried in lard; the starter's ponzu also contains fish-derived dashi, so nothing is free of animal products.

cuisine: 町中華 (town Chinese) — ramen, gyoza, stir-fries, char siu noodles
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀 (Yakkenbori, near Ebisucho/Ginzancho tram stops)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations. As a Chinese eatery, dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce, and the ramen/wonton noodles and spring roll/gyoza wrappers are wheat flour. No dedicated fryer or GF menu is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on meat and animal-based town-Chinese dishes (char siu noodles, gyoza, liver-and-chive stir-fry, wonton ramen with meat/pork broth). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀1-13
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 広島市中区薬研堀1-13; within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Counter seating; exact seat count not published. No private rooms.
closed_days: Mondays (with occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A late-night, locals' counter spot in the entertainment district with Japanese-only signage and menus, so a translation app and some basic Japanese will help a foreign student feel comfortable ordering.
caveats: Late-night hours only (opens 17:00, runs past midnight). Closed Mondays plus occasional irregular days, so confirm before a special trip. Counter-only seating; reservations not accepted. Smoking is permitted throughout.

bio.background: Ryuoh is a long-running town-Chinese restaurant tucked into Hiroshima's Yakkenbori entertainment district, founded in 1965. It has spent decades as the kind of unpretentious counter spot where locals end a night out, and it is known affectionately as a neighborhood Chinese place ('広島なじみの中華屋さん').
bio.specialty: Char siu noodles (チャーシュー麺) and an 'easy-on-the-palate' ramen, alongside town-Chinese staples like gyoza, spring rolls, and liver-and-garlic-chive stir-fry (ニラ肝炒め).
bio.philosophy: The kitchen describes its ramen as having a clean, pleasant aftertaste ('あと味が良いラーメン'), suited to late-night eating.

anecdotes.text: A classic late-night town-Chinese spot in the Yakkenbori nightlife area, open until the small hours and treated as a familiar go-to by locals winding down their evening.
Founded in 1965, making it a long-established fixture of the neighborhood.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared wok/kitchen for Chinese dishes; wheat noodles and wheat-soy-based sauces are central, so cross-contamination with gluten is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a standard Chinese eatery, dishes use ordinary (wheat-based) soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-forward (char siu, gyoza, liver stir-fry, meat/pork-broth ramen); animal products and shared cooking surfaces make vegan dining impractical.

lead: 大衆ぎょうざ倶楽部 餃夢中 広島本通店
name_ja: 大衆ぎょうざ倶楽部 餃夢中 広島本通店
name_en: Taishu Gyoza Club Gyomuchu Hiroshima Hondori-ten
cuisine: 餃子居酒屋（一口の焼き餃子・水餃子に加え、抹茶・トムヤム・麻婆・パクチーなどの変わり種餃子。安価で気軽な大衆ぎょうざ酒場）
gf_detail: グルテンフリー対応の店ではありません。看板メニューの餃子は小麦粉の皮を使い、つけだれ・味付けには小麦を含む醤油が使われます。台湾唐揚げなどの揚げ物も小麦の衣を使用します。セリアック病・小麦アレルギーの方には適しません。
vegan_detail: ヴィーガン対応の店ではありません。看板の夢餃子は国産豚肉（三河豚など）が主材料で、料理全般に肉・魚介・小麦入り醤油が使われます。豚肉中心の餃子酒場であり、植物性のみで構成できるメニューは確認できません。
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通4-1
geocode_note: GSI地理院ジオコーダーで「広島県広島市中区本通4-1」を検索した第1候補の座標。広島ボックス内に収まり、本通商店街・立町電停付近に一致。
neighborhood: 中区本通（本通商店街、立町電停・紙屋町東駅至近、平和記念公園から約0.8km）
seats_text: 60席（2名〜50名規模の宴会にも対応）
closed_days: 不定休（公式情報では恒常的な定休日の記載なし。特定日の臨時休業あり）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a lively, casual, cheap-and-cheerful gyoza-and-beer tavern where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way, though pointing at the picture menu makes ordering easy for a foreign student.
caveats: 広島本通店は2025年12月10日オープンの比較的新しい店舗で、「大衆ぎょうざ倶楽部 餃夢中」ブランド（運営：有限会社モワ企画）の系列店。営業時間は曜日で大きく異なり、平日は夜のみ、土日は昼から営業。深夜営業が中心で、料理ラストオーダーは閉店の約1時間前。座席数は系列ページ記載の60席。

bio.background: A buzzy, budget-friendly gyoza tavern that opened on Hiroshima's covered Hondori shopping arcade in December 2025, run under the local 餃夢中 (Gyomuchu) brand. The name plays on being so 'crazy about gyoza' you lose yourself in it.
bio.specialty: Hand-made dumplings sold at giveaway prices, headlined by the 'dream gyoza' (yume gyoza) made with domestic pork and Aomori garlic, alongside playful flavored versions like matcha, tom yum, mapo and coriander.
bio.philosophy: An accessible 'cheap, tasty, fast' neighborhood drinking spot where anyone can drop in for plates of gyoza and very low-priced beer.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans into novelty, offering offbeat dumpling flavors such as tom yum, mapo and coriander alongside the classic pan-fried and water gyoza.
Pricing is the hook: dumplings from around 99-109 yen a piece and beer advertised from about 190 yen, making it an easy, low-cost stop after sightseeing.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core menu is wheat-based dumplings made and cooked in-house, so flour and gluten are present throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping sauces and seasonings for the gyoza rely on Japanese soy sauce, which standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature gyoza are filled with domestic pork and the kitchen centers on meat dishes, so vegan dishes and separation from animal products are not provided.

cuisine: Miyajima street-food stall — anago (conger eel) tempura skewers and snacks for eating while strolling
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street (Hatsukaichi)
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町1131
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chōme-level point for 広島県廿日市市宮島町1131; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box, consistent with the Omotesando shopping street location about an 8-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry terminal.
seats_text: Small takeout food stall with a small eat-in space (イートインスペース); exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 10:00–18:00 (per listing information)
closed_days: No regular closed day documented; hours may vary with season and ferry/tourist traffic on Miyajima.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual grab-and-go counter where you can simply point at the skewer and pay; a smile and a few Japanese words are plenty, so it is very easy for a foreign student to use.
gf_detail: The signature anago tenkushi is whole conger eel deep-fried in a wheat-flour tempura batter, so it is not gluten-free. No gluten-free preparation or dedicated fryer is documented at this small fried-snack stall.
vegan_detail: The shop centers on a whole-conger-eel (anago) tempura skewer — a fish dish — so the headline item is not vegan, and no vegan options are documented.
caveats: Hours (approx. 10:00–18:00) come from listing aggregators rather than an official schedule and may vary by season; no regular closed day is documented. Seat count is unconfirmed — treat primarily as a takeout/eat-while-walking stall.

bio.background: A small individual stand on Miyajima's lively Omotesando shopping arcade, the lane that leads visitors from the ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is the kind of compact local stall, with just a little standing/eat-in nook, that gives the shopping street its food-walking character.
bio.specialty: Anago tenkushi — a whole conger eel battered and deep-fried onto a skewer, served piping hot to eat as you stroll.
bio.philosophy: Turning Miyajima's famous anago into a generous, one-handed snack so visitors can enjoy a whole eel's worth of flavor while walking the arcade.

anecdotes.text: The skewer uses an entire conger eel, fried so it comes out crispy outside and fluffy inside ('サクッ！フワッ！') — a hearty, filling take that lets you taste plenty of anago in handheld form.
It sits among the Omotesando shopping-street eateries roughly an 8-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry terminal, made fresh to order for passing visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The stall deep-fries wheat-battered conger eel, so any shared fryer would carry gluten; no separate gluten-free frying setup is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking centers on fish (anago) cooked in a fryer, so cross-contact with animal products is inherent at this stall.

lead: 牡蠣フライ串専門（牡蠣フライ串と麦酒）
name_ja: 牡蠣フライ串専門（牡蠣フライ串と麦酒）
name_en: Kaki Fry Kushi Senmon (Kaki Fry Kushi to Bakushu)
cuisine: 牡蠣フライ串・あなごめし串・宮島ビールを供する食べ歩き向けの揚げ物テイクアウトスタンド
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町1133 宮島錦水館
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (廿日市市宮島町1133) via GSI. Coordinates land on Miyajima island within the accepted Hiroshima bounding box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50).
seats_text: テイクアウト・食べ歩き専門のスタンドで、店内の座席はなし（0席）。
hours_raw: 11:00～17:00（年中無休/祝日も営業）。不定休。
closed_days: 不定休（定休日は決まっておらず、不定期に休業）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up takeout stand where you can point at the skewers and pay cash, so a few words of Japanese and a smile are all you need.
gf_detail: The signature dishes are deep-fried oyster skewers (牡蠣フライ) coated in wheat-flour breading/panko, so they are not gluten-free. The あなごめし串 (anago rice skewer) is dressed with a soy-and-mirin tare that typically contains wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free preparation or a dedicated fryer.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fried oysters and grilled conger eel (anago) over rice — both animal products — plus beer. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian items.
caveats: Cash only; no cards, e-money, or QR payment. Hours are 11:00-17:00 daily but with irregular closures (不定休). The popular あなごめし串 is made in limited daily quantities and can sell out, so visit earlier in the day, especially on weekends. No seating — it is strictly takeout/eat-while-walking.

bio.background: A walk-up fried-skewer stand on the first floor of the historic Miyajima Kinsuikan ryokan, set along the island's Omotesando shopping arcade about a five-minute walk from the ferry terminal. It opened on March 28, 2022, taking over a space that had previously housed a steamed-bun (manju) vendor.
bio.specialty: Fried oyster skewers (牡蠣フライ串) sold in 2, 3, or 5-piece portions, and an あなごめし串 — Miyajima's famous anago (conger eel) rice reimagined as a kiritanpo-style skewer you can eat with one hand — finished with tare and optional sansho pepper. Local craft beer rounds out the food-while-walking theme.
bio.philosophy: Reworking the island's traditional anago-meshi into a one-handed, eat-while-strolling skewer reflects a clear effort to make Miyajima's classic flavors easy for visitors to enjoy on the move.

anecdotes.text: The shop turned Miyajima's signature anago-meshi into a 500-yen skewer of seasoned rice shaped like kiritanpo, generously sauced and toppable with sansho — a creative reinvention of the local classic that a reviewer praised as real development effort, while noting it sells out fast on weekends.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago rice skewer is finished with a soy-based tare, which in Japan customarily contains wheat; no allergen information is published for this stand.

cuisine: Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet)
neighborhood: Nagaregawa-cho, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima entertainment district, near Ebisucho / Hatchobori)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町5-19 カサブランカビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町5-19 returned [132.463669, 34.389946], within the Hiroshima box; matches the central-Hiroshima entertainment-district location near Ebisucho.
seats_text: 6 counter seats only (a single circular counter)
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny six-seat counter run by a couple where reservations are essentially required and the experience is intimate, so a little Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking friend to book) will make a visit much smoother.
gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu specialist: the pork is coated in wheat flour and panko (breadcrumbs) and deep-fried, so the core dish is not gluten-free. Accompaniments include a house tonkatsu sauce and traditional soy sauce, both wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: A pork-cutlet restaurant whose entire menu centers on branded pork fried in a blend of Dutch and house-made lard (animal fat). There are no vegan options; even the sides involve miso soup and animal-based seasonings.
caveats: Tabelog lists operating hours as 11:30–14:30 / 17:30–21:30 (with last orders at 14:00 and 21:00); some blog write-ups cite slightly earlier last-order/closing times. With only six counter seats, reservations are strongly advised and walk-ins are often turned away. The prompt described the area as Nobori-cho/幟町, but the confirmed address is Nagaregawa-cho (流川町) in Naka-ku.

bio.origin: The chef-owner is a Tokyo native who made an I-turn move to Hiroshima to open his own shop.
bio.background: Before launching this place, the proprietor came from a family with tonkatsu roots and honed his craft at respected Tokyo cutlet houses, including Butagumi in Nishi-Azabu and Takenobu in Yoyogi-Uehara.
bio.specialty: Multiple branded, single-origin pork varieties served as tonkatsu, with diners choosing both the cut (loin or fillet) and the specific pork breed. Loin options have included Amagi kurobuta, and fillet options have included Umeyama pork.
bio.philosophy: An obsessive, detail-first approach to frying: coarse panko from a noted bread-flour maker, a blend of Dutch and house-rendered lard for the frying fat, and a lineup of finishing options (sea salt, a traditional soy sauce, a house sauce, and a house dressing) all chosen to bring out the natural sweetness and umami of each pork.

anecdotes.text: Run as a husband-and-wife operation around a single circular counter of just six seats, it has become widely regarded as one of the most sought-after cutlet shops in the city despite opening only a few years ago.
The owner moved from Tokyo to Hiroshima and trained at well-known cutlet restaurants such as Butagumi in Nishi-Azabu before going independent.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature dish is wheat-flour-and-panko-breaded pork deep-fried in lard; no gluten-free preparation is offered, so cross-contamination with wheat is inherent to the cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Finishing options include a traditional brewed soy sauce (and a house tonkatsu sauce), both of which are wheat-based and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cutlets are fried in a blend of Dutch lard and house-made lard (animal fat), so the frying medium itself is non-vegan.

cuisine: Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlets)
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀・幟町 (near Goncmachi/Hatchobori, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町7-7
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町7-7; result lat 34.3949, lng 132.4650 falls within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 25 seats (counter and solid-wood tables); one listing notes 27 seats.
closed_days: Sundays and national holidays (日曜・祝日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, popular local tonkatsu shop with no English menu noted and frequent lunchtime queues, so a foreign student is best off pointing at a set meal and going with a Japanese-speaking guide or some basic Japanese.
gf_detail: The entire menu centers on tonkatsu and other cutlets, which are coated in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce and miso soup typically contain wheat-based soy sauce/miso. There is no documented gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: A pork- and chicken-cutlet specialist; core dishes are deep-fried meat served with rice, miso soup, and pickles. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
caveats: Hours and seat count vary slightly between listings (25 vs 27 seats; some pages list dinner service Monday-Friday with Saturday lunch only). No official website exists; details drawn from aggregator listings.

bio.background: A neighborhood tonkatsu shop in central Hiroshima, a short walk from the tram stop, that has been frying cutlets since around 2000. It is run by the owner alongside a handful of staff, and the dining room is built around a large wooden counter with solid-wood tables that give it a warm, natural feel.
bio.specialty: Crisp panko-breaded pork cutlets with tender, juicy meat, using domestic Japanese pork prized for its juiciness and clean sweetness; loin and fillet sets and a chicken-cutlet set come with rice, miso soup, and pickles.

anecdotes.text: Known locally as a popular spot that draws a queue at lunchtime.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All mains are breaded in wheat flour and panko and deep-fried, so the kitchen and fryer are not gluten-free; assume heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkatsu sauce and the accompanying miso soup typically use wheat-containing soy sauce/miso; no wheat-free condiments are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built on pork and chicken cutlets fried in shared oil, so no dish can be considered free of animal products.

cuisine: Tonkatsu (low-temperature-cooked fried pork cutlet) and fried-food set meals / donburi, lunch only
neighborhood: Fukuromachi, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (near Hondori)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町2-17 袋町共栄ビル101
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区袋町2-17 via GSI; result lng 132.45845 / lat 34.392502 falls within the Hiroshima bounding box, central Naka-ku near Hondori.
seats_text: Small counter-style lunch operation run by a single proprietor inside a shared space; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: Lunch only 11:30-14:00. Closed Tuesday and Saturday.
closed_days: Tuesday and Saturday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny one-person lunch counter with no English menu indicated, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the set you want) will make ordering smoother.
vegan_detail: This is a fried-pork (tonkatsu), chicken karaage and horse-mackerel fry specialist; dishes come with rice and miso soup and there are no vegan offerings. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: The core dishes are breaded and deep-fried (wheat flour, egg, panko breadcrumbs), so they contain gluten by definition. Shared frying oil and a wheat-based menu make this unsuitable for gluten-free diners.
caveats: Recently relocated (effective July 1, 2025) from the Nekoyacho building to share the Lemon Stand Hiroshima space in Fukuromachi, operating only during lunch (11:30-14:00) before Lemon Stand takes over the venue in the evening. Closed days (Tuesday/Saturday) per Tabelog; the official site lists only the lunch hours. Seat count not published.

bio.background: Tsukanto is a one-person fried-food specialist that previously operated in the Nekoyacho building before relocating on July 1, 2025 to share a space in Fukuromachi with the daytime slot of Lemon Stand Hiroshima — Tsukanto runs the lunch service (11:30-14:00) and the venue then transforms into the Lemon Stand bar in the evening.
bio.specialty: Low-temperature-cooked pork cutlet: the pork is first cooked sous-vide-style at low temperature, then plunged into high-temperature oil so the center stays rare and tender while the panko coating turns crisp — described as 'rare inside, crispy outside, the best of both worlds' (中はレア、外はカリ). The proprietor frames the difficulty as nailing that balance.
bio.philosophy: The motto is 揚げて、揚げて、アゲまくる ('fry, fry, and keep frying'), serving crispy, juicy fried food with freshly cooked rice and white miso soup, prepared 心を込めて ('with heartfelt dedication').

anecdotes.text: The shop reinvented itself as 'one place with two faces': Tsukanto took over the lunch slot that Lemon Stand Hiroshima had previously used to serve curry, and after 14:00 the same room becomes the Lemon Stand bar.
Beyond the signature pork cutlet sets (fillet and loin), the menu runs to katsudon, Kagoshima free-range chicken karaage sets, and horse-mackerel fry sets, all served with miso soup.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All signature dishes are panko-breaded and deep-fried in shared oil; the menu is built around wheat-coated fried items, so cross-contamination with gluten is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Katsudon and tonkatsu accompaniments typically rely on standard Japanese sauces (tonkatsu sauce / soy-based seasonings) which contain wheat; no gluten-free seasoning is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is dedicated to frying pork, chicken and fish, with meals served alongside miso soup; there are no plant-only preparations and animal products are pervasive.

lead: とんかつ かつまる
name_ja: とんかつ かつまる
cuisine: Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet); lunch sets and evening drinks
neighborhood: 中区本通 (Hondori), near 立町駅 / Tatemachi Station, central Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通4-25
geocode_note: GSI address search on 広島県広島市中区本通4-25 returned [lng 132.45961, lat 34.393425], within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 26 seats total (6 counter, 20 table)
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:30, 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed only for year-end/New Year.
closed_days: No regular closing day; closed only over the year-end/New Year period.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, modern downtown cutlet shop with set meals and digital payments; a little Japanese helps but you can order comfortably by pointing at the picture menu.
gf_detail: This is a breaded-cutlet specialist: the panko coating on the pork, fish and chicken katsu is wheat, and tonkatsu sauce and curry roux typically contain wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or preparation, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around pork, fish, chicken and oyster cutlets, plus beef-tendon curry; even the frying oil is custom-blended with pork back fat. No vegan dishes are documented, so it is not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Frying oil is blended with pork back fat, so even non-pork items (fish/chicken/oyster katsu) are cooked in pork-derived oil. Weekday midday break between lunch and dinner service.

bio.background: Katsumaru opened on July 30, 2024 on Hondori shopping street in central Hiroshima, taking over a space that had previously housed a mackerel restaurant. It was conceived as a relaxed,街ナカ (in-town) spot where people can enjoy tonkatsu casually, with a compact room of counter and table seating and takeout available straight from the storefront.
bio.specialty: Breaded cutlets fried to a crisp, golden, juicy finish, alongside a slow-cooked curry. Premium options use Shimane Iwami pork (a rare Kenborough breed) and Seto Inland Sea oysters from Hiroshima.
