bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for deep umami, rich flavor and a fragrant, crisp crust, using a custom-blended frying oil made with pork back fat to get a crunchy exterior and juicy interior with a beautiful golden color.
roles: Casual lunch and evening dining with all-you-can-drink

anecdotes.text: The signature Katsumaru curry is simmered over three days until the vegetables dissolve into a smooth, mellow sweetness, and is finished with beef tendon for depth while staying mild.
A visiting reviewer described the cutlet as light and crisp with a clean, well-cut bite, and praised the ¥950 katsu-curry bowl set that comes with miso soup and pickles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are panko-breaded (wheat) and share the same fryer; tonkatsu sauce and curry roux also typically contain wheat, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The frying oil is custom-blended with pork back fat, so any item from the fryer carries animal fat regardless of the main ingredient.

cuisine: Yoshoku (Japanese-style Western cuisine) — hamburger steak, beef cutlet, omelette rice, meat spaghetti, beef stew
gf_detail: This is a classic yoshoku house whose signature dishes are built on wheat: hamburger steak, breaded beef cutlet, from-scratch demi-glace sauce (roux-based), omelette rice and meat spaghetti all contain wheat flour. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and dairy — hamburger steak, beef cutlet, beef/tongue stew, omelette rice (egg) and demi-glace made from meat broth. No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町4-19 アウトサイダー並木1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区中町4-19 returned [132.459274, 34.390453], within the Hiroshima box; central Naka-ku near Fukuromachi, a short walk from the Peace Park area.
neighborhood: 中区中町 (Nakamachi, central Hiroshima) — about a 4-minute walk from Fukuromachi tram stop, near Peace Memorial Park
seats_text: 21 seats (sources vary: Tabelog lists 21, a diner blog counts ~20 — roughly 6 counter and 14 table seats); small intimate room
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00) / 18:00-21:30 (L.O. 20:30); closed Tuesday and Wednesday
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: Both Japanese and English menus are available and the room is small and welcoming, so a foreign student can order comfortably; reservations are wise as lunch can fill up.
caveats: Reservations are accepted and recommended, especially for dinner and weekend lunch, as the small room fills up. As a meat-and-dairy yoshoku kitchen, it is not a fit for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diets. Seat count varies slightly between sources.

bio.background: Kokemomo is the sister restaurant of Hirotei Tanaka (広亭タナカ), a beloved Hiroshima yoshoku house founded in 1991. The original is run by a second-generation chef who inherited it from the founder, and Kokemomo brings that same old-line Western cooking into the city center near Fukuromachi.
bio.specialty: Hand-crafted yoshoku: hamburger steak with a crisp exterior and dense, refined interior; beef cutlet; omelette rice; meat spaghetti; and slow-cooked stews, finished with a from-scratch demi-glace sauce.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen carries the founding chef's maxim, 'to remain unchanged, you must keep changing' — protecting the original flavors while quietly refining them, with stocks, soups and demi-glace all made by hand.

anecdotes.text: The hamburger steak is praised for a crisp seared surface over a dense, delicate interior, with a sauce that leans sweet with a gentle bitter undertone.
The parent restaurant Hirotei Tanaka, established in 1991, makes its demi-glace, broths and soups entirely from scratch, a practice carried into Kokemomo.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling is documented; core dishes (breaded beef cutlet, roux-based demi-glace, omelette rice, meat spaghetti) all contain wheat, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable in this kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is meat- and dairy-based (hamburger steak, beef stew, demi-glace from meat broth, egg-based omelette rice); no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling information is published online; the parent restaurant directs diners with dietary concerns to phone ahead, so the same is advisable here.

cuisine: Old-style Japanese yoshoku (Western-style) grill — demi-glace tongue stew, beef stew, oxtail stew, hamburger steak, filet steak, hayashi rice
gf_detail: This is a classic yoshoku house built around long-simmered demi-glace sauce and stews; demi-glace is thickened with wheat flour (roux) and the kitchen makes no documented gluten-free accommodation. Hamburger steak, hayashi rice and the stews all rely on this wheat-based sauce. Not a gluten-free destination; no dedicated handling confirmed.
vegan_detail: A meat-focused Western grill: the signature dishes are beef tongue stew, beef stew, oxtail, hamburger steak and steak, and the house demi-glace is built on chicken stock and sauteed vegetables simmered over many hours with meat. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区土橋町2-18 ダイヤパレス土橋1F北側
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 広島県広島市中区土橋町2番18号; result lat 34.3933, lng 132.4475 falls within the Hiroshima box, roughly 1 mile west of the Peace Park / A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: Tsuchihashi, Naka-ku (west-central Hiroshima, near Tsuchihashi tram stop)
seats_text: 37 seats (6 at the counter, 31 at tables)
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, traditional sit-down yoshoku restaurant where ordering is straightforward by pointing to set dishes, but staff communicate mainly in Japanese, so a translation app helps; reservations can be made online.

bio.background: A neighborhood Western-style eatery in Hiroshima's Tsuchihashi district, founded in 1991 and beloved for more than thirty years. The founding chef built the place around painstaking French-influenced sauce work, and today his daughter runs the kitchen as the second-generation proprietor, carrying his recipes forward.
bio.specialty: Beef tongue stew (tan stew) and other dishes bathed in a house demi-glace; the sauce is made by sauteing vegetables, simmering them in chicken stock and straining the result, repeated four times to draw out a deep vegetable umami. Other mainstays include beef stew, oxtail stew, hamburger steak, filet steak and hayashi rice.
bio.philosophy: The founder's motto was to never spare time and never cut corners. His daughter inherited a companion creed: to stay the same, you have to keep evolving — preserving the original flavors while continually refining them.

anecdotes.text: The founding chef was unusual for his generation in placing family ahead of career, and it was his daughter who took over the kitchen and continues his recipes as the second-generation owner.
The signature tongue stew arrives in a demi-glace built through four full rounds of sauteing, simmering in chicken stock and straining, so that the vegetables' umami gently floods the palate.
The dining room seats around 37, with classical music playing, plants throughout and a small flower arrangement on each table, giving it a homey, welcoming feel.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are built on a wheat-flour-thickened demi-glace and other Western sauces, so gluten is pervasive across the menu; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The house demi-glace is simmered on chicken stock with meat, and signature dishes center on beef tongue, beef stew, oxtail and hamburger steak, so plant-based diners would find essentially no suitable mains.

caveats: Seat count differs by source (Tabelog lists 37; one feature article cites ~33); 37 is used as the more authoritative figure. Phone reservations and online reservation (via the official site) are available but not mandatory. Lunch is run in two timed seatings. The chef's personal name was not published in the sources reviewed.

name_ja: 中華そば 千番 流川店
cuisine: 中華そば（醤油ラーメン）・ラーメン居酒屋
gf_detail: A traditional soy-sauce (shoyu) ramen shop. The broth is built on a soy-sauce-based tare, and Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the soup is not gluten-free. Ramen noodles are wheat, and the popular fried-rice and izakaya side dishes also typically use soy sauce/wheat seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The signature broth is a meat/animal-based stock simmered 12+ hours with a soy-sauce tare, and the menu centers on ramen with pork-style toppings plus izakaya sides. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町7-8
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町7-8 returned [132.463272, 34.389118], within the Hiroshima box; lands in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Hiroshima, consistent with the listed address.
seats_text: Around 30 seats (Tabelog lists 30; the shop's own page describes roughly 35 seats including counter and tatami, with a private room for up to ~12).
hours_raw: 19:00～翌3:00（L.O.2:30）／定休日：月曜・日曜（公式・ぐるなび）。タベログ表示では曜日により昼11:00–16:00や深夜20:00–04:00帯の記載もあり。
closed_days: Mondays and Sundays (per official site and Gurunavi). Note: Tabelog's day-by-day display differs.
cultural_comfort_note: A late-night, cash-leaning local ramen counter in the Nagarekawa nightlife district; a little Japanese helps and there is no English menu documented, but pointing at the ramen works fine.
neighborhood: 流川（中区流川町、胡町・八丁堀方面）
caveats: Sources disagree on hours and seat count: the shop's own page and Gurunavi list 19:00–03:00 (L.O. 02:30), closed Monday and Sunday, while Tabelog's display shows some daytime (11:00–16:00) and late-night windows. Treat as a late-night spot and confirm on arrival. Cash-based (PayPay accepted); cards and e-money not accepted.

bio.background: A Hiroshima chuka-soba institution dating to 1951, Senban carries on a recipe handed down from the founding generation. The shop moved from its original Fujimi-cho location across Heiwa-dori to its current Nagarekawa address in October 2011, planting itself in the heart of the city's nightlife district.
bio.specialty: Soy-sauce-based chuka soba whose broth is simmered for over 12 hours and paired with a secret tare aged about three weeks; the deeply popular fried rice (yakimeshi) is the most-ordered side and a regulars' staple.
bio.philosophy: Keeping the predecessor's flavor unchanged through trial and refinement over the decades.

anecdotes.text: Known as a 'ramen izakaya,' Senban is unusual for a ramen shop in offering more than 20 generously sized side dishes, making it a late-night spot for the 'shime ramen' (finishing-the-night bowl) culture.
The broth is soy-sauce based and simmered more than 12 hours, with a secret tare matured for about three weeks.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and a soy-sauce-based broth are central to the menu, so a shared kitchen handling wheat is unavoidable; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is built on a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the soup contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is an animal-based stock simmered 12+ hours; no vegetable-only broth or vegan option is documented.

cuisine: Soba and katsudon, signature chili-oil soba
neighborhood: Naka-ku, Horikawa-chō (near Hatchobori tram stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町6-5 セイコウビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the chō-level address 広島県広島市中区堀川町6-5 via GSI; result lat 34.392673, lng 132.462479 falls within the Hiroshima box, in the Horikawa-chō block just east of the Hatchobori shopping district.
seats_text: Small counter-style shop; exact seat count not published for the Horikawa branch.
hours_raw: Official site (Horikawa branch): 11:00–20:00, last order 19:30, open year-round. (A separate listing shows 11:30–19:00; the official site is used as primary.)
closed_days: Open year-round (no regular closed day per the official site). Some third-party listings note occasional irregular closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, cash-only neighborhood soba counter where ordering is simple but staff are unlikely to speak much English, so pointing at the picture menu and basic Japanese phrases help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is buckwheat soba, which in Japan is almost always cut with wheat flour, and the chili-oil/dipping sauces use wheat-based soy sauce. Fried-cutlet (katsudon) items are breaded in wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The signature rāyu soba is topped with sweetly simmered shredded pork, and the soba broth/dipping sauce uses wheat-based soy sauce and most likely fish dashi. The other headline dish is katsudon (pork cutlet over rice with egg). No documented plant-based options.

bio.background: A small Hatchobori-area shop that opened in 2014, tucked into the Seikō Building in Horikawa-chō a short walk from the tram stop. It belongs to a local Hiroshima food group whose roots reach back to the early 20th century, but the shop itself reads as a compact, everyday neighborhood noodle counter.
bio.specialty: Its calling card is 'rāyu soba' — thick buckwheat noodles paired with a chili-oil dipping/seasoning sauce, finished with shredded nori, green onion, sesame, and sweetly simmered pork. It serves the noodles both warm and cold by season, and also offers katsudon.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the draw as 'addictive': thick noodles bound up in a chili-oil sauce, generously portioned, in the under-1,000-yen range.
The storefront is recognizable for its black walls, wooden fixtures, and glass sliding doors with bold signage advertising the chili-oil soba.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles are cooked in shared water with wheat-containing noodles, and the kitchen also breads and fries pork cutlets, so gluten cross-contact is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping and seasoning sauces are based on standard Japanese soy sauce/tare, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature soba is topped with sweetly simmered pork and the broth is not plant-based, so dishes share meat and likely fish-dashi preparation.

caveats: Hours differ between sources: the official store directory lists 11:00–20:00 (LO 19:30) year-round for the Horikawa branch, while Tabelog lists 11:30–19:00. The official site is used as primary. Seat count is not published. The operating company also runs a separate 紙屋町 (Kamiyachō) branch — the geocode and details here are for the Horikawa-chō (Hatchobori) location matching the target Tabelog URL.

cuisine: 和カフェ・甘味処 (traditional Japanese tea house / sweets — matcha, zenzai, kakigori, wagashi)
neighborhood: 宮島・滝小路（厳島神社〜大聖院の参道沿い）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町260
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町260 via GSI; coordinates land on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box, consistent with the café's location on Taki-koji near Itsukushima Shrine and Daishoin.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on matcha, zenzai (sweet azuki bean soup), and kakigori (shaved ice with homemade syrup), several of which can be naturally wheat-free, but wagashi sweets and cake/coffee sets are also served and no ingredient or cross-contamination details are published. A celiac or gluten-sensitive guest would need to ask in person about each item.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment, but several offerings lean plant-based: matcha, kakigori with homemade plum syrup, and zenzai (azuki beans, sugar). However zenzai and accompanying sweets may include mochi or dairy/egg-based wagashi and cake sets, and no vegan labeling exists. A vegan guest should confirm ingredients (especially for sweets and any cream items) directly with staff.
seats_text: No published seat count. Seating includes indoor tables, tatami-mat seating, and engawa (veranda) seats overlooking the garden, where small trays (zen) serve as tables; intimate small-scale old-house café.
hours_raw: 11:00〜17:00（水曜・木曜定休、不定休あり）
closed_days: 水曜日・木曜日（加えて不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional old-house tea house tucked away from the tourist crowds; English support is unlikely and it is cash-only, so a foreign student should bring yen and be ready to point at the menu or use simple Japanese/a translation app.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. Closed Wednesday and Thursday plus occasional irregular days, so check before visiting. Located on the quiet Taki-koji lane toward Daishoin rather than on the main shopping street, so allow time to find it. No official website; dietary (GF/vegan) details are not published and must be confirmed in person.

bio.background: A hidden-gem Japanese tea house and sweets shop set inside a roughly 170-year-old machiya (traditional townhouse) on Miyajima. It sits along Taki-koji, the quiet lane running between Itsukushima Shrine and Daishoin temple, away from the bustle of the main shopping street, surrounded by old historic homes.
bio.specialty: Matcha served with wagashi, zenzai (sweet azuki bean soup, including a chestnut version), and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice) made with the owner's homemade plum syrup; also coffee and cake sets and cream-azuki.
bio.philosophy: A calm, refined refuge from the tourist crowds, built around handmade, attentive hospitality.

anecdotes.text: Behind a formal white-walled fence and gate, guests are welcomed into a serene old house with table, tatami, and engawa (veranda) seating overlooking a well-tended garden; on the veranda small trays double as tables.

The hospitality is personal and handmade — the owner makes the shaved ice with homemade plum syrup and serves chilled herb-infused water, and reviewers note touches such as lending blankets to guests.

positives.text: The menu is centered on simple Japanese sweets and tea — matcha, zenzai (azuki beans and sugar), and shaved ice with homemade plum syrup — several of which are naturally plant-based, though ingredients should be confirmed in person.

cuisine: 手打ち蕎麦 (handmade soba)
neighborhood: 宮島口・大野浦エリア（廿日市市大野中央）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市大野中央4-5-20
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box; lands in Ono Chuo, Hatsukaichi, on the mainland across the strait from Miyajima.
seats_text: 25 seats: counter 5, tables 12, tatami 8. A converted single-family house with a glass-walled view onto the stone mill and soba-making station.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:30-15:00, 17:00-20:00; Sun & holidays 11:30-15:00; closes when soba sells out (無くなり次第終了). Closed Monday (open if Monday is a public holiday).
closed_days: Monday (open if Monday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small walk-in-only soba house run by a master soba artisan; a little Japanese and patience for possible queues and sell-outs will help, and there is no English-language ordering support documented.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. While buckwheat is the headline grain, handmade soba is typically cut with wheat flour and the dipping tsuyu is made from bonito dashi and wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The soba tsuyu is built on katsuo (bonito) dashi and soy sauce, and signature dishes such as kamo nanban use duck. No vegan options or plant-based dashi are documented.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations; the shop closes early when the day's soba runs out, so go early. The address is on the mainland in Ono (Hatsukaichi), not on Miyajima island itself, despite the name.

bio.background: Tanaka Kunio trained under the legendary soba master Takahashi Kunihiro at the famed Daruma Sekkasanbo in the mountains of Kitahiroshima, a shop so popular it capped service at around 300 customers a day amid long lines. When that shop closed in May 2015, Tanaka was given permission to carry the name forward (norenwake) and opened Miyajima Daruma in Ono, Hatsukaichi, on July 13, 2015 - the group's first shop in Hiroshima Prefecture.
bio.specialty: Self-milled, handmade soba; zaru soba is the centerpiece, alongside dishes like oroshi soba, tororo soba with Tanba mountain yam, kamo nanban, and sobagaki.
bio.philosophy: Buckwheat is selected from multiple farmers and milled entirely in-house from the whole groats; the noodles are firm and aromatic, and the refined tsuyu carries a higher proportion of dashi than usual, with condiments so good they are said to be worth eating on their own.

anecdotes.text: Master Takahashi Kunihiro himself occasionally visits the shop to help out and demonstrate his soba-making.
A reviewer noted the condiments (yakumi) are delicious enough to enjoy on their own, a hallmark of this lineage's standards.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Handmade soba in this style is generally cut with wheat flour, and the tsuyu uses wheat-based soy sauce, so gluten is present; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping tsuyu is a dashi-and-soy-sauce broth (described as carrying a higher dashi ratio than usual), which uses standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tsuyu is based on bonito (katsuo) dashi and the menu includes duck (kamo nanban), so animal products are integral to the cooking.

cuisine: 抹茶カフェ・甘味（抹茶わらび餅、抹茶ソフトクリーム、かき氷などの和スイーツ／テイクアウト系カフェ）
vegan_detail: A matcha-sweets shop whose lineup is mostly soft serve, matcha milk drinks, and warabi mochi. Soft serve and matcha-milk drinks contain dairy and are not vegan. The warabi mochi (lotus-root and bracken starch dusted with matcha and kinako) appears plant-based by ingredients, but no vegan labeling or allergen statement is published, so a strict vegan should ask directly. Treat as "ask" rather than a vegan destination.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. Warabi mochi is made from lotus-root and bracken starch dusted with matcha and kinako (roasted soybean flour), which is naturally wheat-free, but it is dusted/handled alongside baked goods such as matcha financiers and baumkuchen that contain wheat, so cross-contact is possible. Soft serve cones and any baked items contain wheat. A celiac should confirm ingredients and handling in person before eating.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町553-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町553-5 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box. Consistent with a location on the Miyajima Omotesando shopping street near Itsukushima Shrine.
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（嚴島神社へ徒歩圏、表参道沿い）
seats_text: Small standing/takeout-oriented shop with a small counter and a few bench seats in a tea-house-style nook at the back for a quick break; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: Not specifically documented on the official brand page or aggregator listings reviewed; hours not confirmed.
closed_days: Not documented on sources reviewed.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual counter-and-takeout matcha sweets stand on the main shopping street where you can point at the matcha soft serve or warabi mochi and pay cash, so a foreign student gets by easily with a smile and a few words.

bio.origin: A matcha-sweets shop on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, within walking distance of Itsukushima Shrine, run as one of the Sawawa-brand matcha shops by Telacoya Co., Ltd., which operates matcha and themed sweets shops across Japan from Hokkaido to Tokyo, Kanazawa and Arashiyama.
bio.background: Sawawa positions itself as a specialist in quality matcha sweets. The Miyajima shop greets visitors with an eye-catching warabi-mochi tower at the entrance and keeps a consistent lineup across its branches rather than region-only items.
bio.specialty: Matcha warabi mochi made from a blend of lotus-root and bracken starch, hand-cut and generously dusted with matcha and kinako, alongside matcha soft serve and, in summer, fluffy matcha kakigori.
bio.philosophy: A focus on good-quality matcha and approachable, affordable sweets you can grab and enjoy while strolling the shrine approach.

anecdotes.text: The signature warabi mochi has a soft, melting texture and is sold very affordably (around 300 yen for several pieces), with takeout available so visitors can eat it on the go.
The shop uses Kyoto Uji matcha and offers a range from matcha soft serve and matcha milk in clear cups to warabi mochi, daifuku, and summer-only kakigori.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Warabi mochi (lotus-root/bracken starch, matcha, kinako) is naturally wheat-free, but the shop also sells wheat-based baked goods (matcha financiers, baumkuchen) and soft serve cones, so shared surfaces and dusting create possible cross-contact; no GF handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soft serve and matcha milk drinks contain dairy and are made on the same equipment as other items; warabi mochi appears plant-based by ingredients but no vegan/allergen labeling is published.

positives.text: Warabi mochi is made from lotus-root and bracken starch dusted with matcha and kinako (roasted soybean flour), with no wheat in the core recipe.

caveats: Hours, closed days, and exact seat count are not published on the official brand page or the aggregator/travel pages reviewed; confirm hours and seating on arrival or by phone (0829-40-2888). Cash-only per Tabelog. Dietary handling (GF/vegan) is inferred from ingredient descriptions, not from any official allergen program, so travelers with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should verify in person.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Kamiyacho, Naka-ku (near Hondori, east of the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町2-3-10 リブレ紙屋町ビル 302
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市中区紙屋町2-3-10 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 18 seats total: 8 counter seats and 10 table seats (one 2-person table, two 4-person tables).
hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sun: 12:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Fri-Sat: 12:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). Irregular closures (不定休).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and staff appreciate English conversation, so a foreign student can order and dine comfortably here.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. Okonomiyaki batter is wheat-based and the savory okonomiyaki sauce is also wheat-based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Hiroshima okonomiyaki typically layers pork, egg, and noodles, and teppanyaki dishes use meat/seafood; the okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-and-soy based. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented on the official site or listings.
caveats: Cash only; no card, e-money, or QR payment. Reservations are not accepted except for private group events. Hours are subject to irregular closures, and the shop limits daily capacity (about 40 customers) and does not serve large tour groups. No takeout. After 5 PM and all day on weekends/holidays, each guest must order at least one paid drink.

bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Kamiyacho, Naka-ku), a short walk from the Hondori shopping arcade and near the Peace Memorial Park.
bio.background: A small, owner-run okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot tucked on an upper floor of a building near Hondori. Stepping in feels less like a typical restaurant and more like an enthusiast's hideaway, with anime posters and figures decorating the walls and friendly, costumed hall staff greeting guests.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki griddled to order, alongside teppanyaki dishes.
bio.philosophy: The owner has said the goal is to give attentive service to every guest and to cook each okonomiyaki carefully to perfection rather than mass-produce. To protect that hands-on approach, the shop deliberately caps how many people it serves each day and declines large tour-group bookings.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen and floor are run by just two people, and the owner is described as playful and easygoing, keeping the place casual and personal.

In 2023 the shop introduced daily capacity limits, explaining that it typically serves around 40 guests a day and cannot take on large groups the way big tourist restaurants do, precisely so it can keep cooking each okonomiyaki with care.

cuisine: 純喫茶・自家焙煎珈琲（サイフォン、モーニング） / Traditional kissaten serving house-roasted siphon coffee, morning sets, toast and sandwiches
gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house whose food offerings are toast, ham toast, sandwiches and French bread — all wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no gluten-free bread, and no indication of allergy accommodation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive visitor can drink the black house-roasted coffee, but none of the food is gluten-free and there is no information on cross-contamination handling.
vegan_detail: Morning sets and the food menu center on toast, ham, bacon-and-egg, sandwiches and homemade yogurt, all of which contain animal products. There is no documented vegan or plant-based option. Black coffee and the retail coffee beans are plant-based, but milk-free/plant-milk alternatives are not documented, so a vegan visitor is effectively limited to black coffee.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀12-21
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区八丁堀12-21 returned [132.463486, 34.395618], within the Hiroshima box; lands in Naka-ku Hatchobori, roughly 1.5km east of the Peace Memorial Park, consistent with the stated location.
seats_text: 47 seats (7 counter seats facing the siphon brewing area, plus around 40 table seats)
hours_raw: Weekdays open 07:30, Saturdays open 08:00, closing around 19:30; morning service runs until roughly 11:00–11:30.
closed_days: Closed Fridays per recent visitor reports; Tabelog lists Sunday instead. Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a quiet, traditional Japanese coffee house run by its owner-roaster with a no-chatter counter area, so a little spoken Japanese and a calm, respectful manner will go a long way; English menus or staff are not documented.
caveats: Cash only and no English service is documented. Closed-day information conflicts between sources (Friday in recent reports vs. Sunday on the Tabelog listing) — confirm by phone (082-227-7488) before a Friday or Sunday visit. Not suitable as a dietary destination: all food is wheat- and animal-product-based, with no documented gluten-free or vegan options.

bio.background: A one-person traditional coffee house in the heart of Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, founded in 1984. Behind a bright, unassuming orange storefront hides a hushed, glamorous interior of dim lamplight, brass-topped tables and a large mirrored wall that makes the snug room feel open. It is the kind of place locals call a true junkissa — a pure coffee house dedicated entirely to the craft of the cup.
bio.origin: Hatchobori, central Hiroshima, about 1.5km east of the Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: House-roasted coffee brewed by siphon. The shop roasts its own beans, offering around eight single-origin coffees and five blends, and also sells beans to take home. Mornings bring a beloved set of crisp-outside, fluffy-inside toast with bacon and egg, salad, homemade yogurt and coffee.
bio.philosophy: A devotion to freshly roasted aroma and the meditative ritual of siphon extraction. The counter seats look directly onto the brewing, and that front area is kept quiet so guests can simply watch the coffee come to life, while a back section allows conversation.

anecdotes.text: Regulars are given their own personalized ceramic cups, and visitors describe the slow theatre of watching siphon coffee rise and fall at the counter as quietly meditative.

One writer stumbled on the cafe by chance near their hotel and called it 'lighthouse below is dark' — the gem hiding right under your nose — praising how a casual orange storefront opens into a retro, glamorous interior.
Hatchobori is so dense with old coffee houses that enthusiasts jokingly call it the neighborhood's 'coffee war,' and this long-running roaster is one of its quiet stalwarts, dating to 1984.

positives.text: House-roasted single-origin and blend coffees are served black, and beans are sold to take home, giving gluten-free and vegan visitors a safe drink option.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (savory layered pancake with cabbage, pork, soba noodles and egg)
neighborhood: Takeyacho, Naka Ward (near Nisseki Byoin-mae tram stop, about 1.5km southeast of Peace Memorial Park)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter (here mixed with egg and mirin) and includes wheat soba noodles, plus a sweet okonomi-style sauce that typically contains wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner cannot eat the core dish here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature and essentially only dish is okonomiyaki made with pork and egg, cooked on a shared griddle, and finished with okonomi sauce (typically not vegan). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区竹屋町5-21
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned the exact street address (竹屋町5番21号); coordinates fall well within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: About 7 counter seats around the griddle.
hours_raw: 10:30-14:30 (sources vary; closes when sold out, generally early-mid afternoon). Closed Sundays and public holidays.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, cash-only counter shop run by locals where little to no English is likely spoken, so be ready to order simply (point and say "soba niku tamago") and eat at the griddle counter.

bio.background: Ganso Hassho opened in 1965, founded by Takanori Furuta, the eldest son of Shozaburo Furuta, who served as the very first 'village chief' of Hiroshima's famed Okonomimura (Okonomiyaki Village). The 'Ganso' (meaning 'the original') name nods to its place at the root of the well-known Hassho okonomiyaki lineage. The shop has been led by the second-generation owner, Takanori's daughter Yuka, since 2016.
bio.specialty: Soba-iri niku-tama (okonomiyaki with soba noodles, pork and egg).
bio.philosophy: The owner treats the batter as a 'tsunagi' (binding element) rather than just a base. A thin batter enriched with egg and mirin is used to deepen flavor and let the cabbage, pork and noodles each shine instead of overwhelming the palate; the noodles are prized for being fragrant yet chewy.

anecdotes.text: The founding family's roots reach back to the very beginnings of Hiroshima's Okonomimura, as the founder's father was its first 'village chief'.

The second-generation owner uses a batter made with wheat flour, egg and mirin as a flavor 'connector', and finishes with a slightly spicy locally made sauce, a deliberate choice to build flavor complexity.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All okonomiyaki is cooked on a single shared griddle and the dish itself contains wheat batter and wheat soba noodles, so there is no way to avoid gluten or cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Finished with a sweet okonomi-style sauce (a locally made Mitsuwa sauce), which is typically wheat-containing and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes include pork and egg cooked on a shared griddle; no vegan or vegetarian preparation is documented.

caveats: Hours vary across sources (Tabelog lists 10:30-13:30; other listings 10:30-14:30 or until ~16:00); the shop is small and often closes early when sold out, so go for an early lunch. Closed Sundays and holidays. Cash only, no reservations, very few seats. Address and existence confirmed; no official website found.

lead: 鉄ぱん屋 弁兵衛 八丁堀店
name_ja: 鉄ぱん屋 弁兵衛 八丁堀店
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き・居酒屋
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀（八丁堀駅徒歩約3分）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀16-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.39, lng 132.46), central Naka-ku near Hatchobori.
seats_text: Approximately 68-73 seats including counter seating along a roughly 6-meter teppan griddle and private tatami rooms (banquet rooms accommodate groups up to about 30).
closed_days: Open year-round except the year-end/New Year period (around Dec 31 - Jan 2).
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, mainstream teppan izakaya with picture-friendly menus and counter cooking; basic Japanese greetings and pointing will get you by, and reservations can be made online.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The core dishes are Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles), gyoza (wheat wrappers), and teppan dishes typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and okonomi sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on Hiroshima-grown oysters, Seto Inland Sea fish, pork and egg in the okonomiyaki, and meat/seafood teppan courses. No vegan menu or documented plant-based options.
caveats: This is one branch of a multi-location restaurant group (9 locations across Hiroshima, Tokyo, Fukuyama and Okayama operated by the Balcom Group), so it is not an independent single-owner shop. Seat count differs slightly between listings (about 68-73). No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented; travelers with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should look elsewhere.

bio.background: A Hiroshima teppan house built around a long iron griddle where okonomiyaki and seasonal dishes are cooked in front of guests. The room pairs lively counter seats facing the griddle with private cottage-style tatami booths set with their own teppan, so groups can grill and dine in a relaxed traditional setting.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with house-made noodles, Hiroshima-grown oyster dishes, the signature Benbei gyoza served with a special soup, and teppan dishes featuring fresh Seto Inland Sea fish.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated aim is to serve food at its best straight off the hot iron plate, cooking directly in front of diners so each dish can be savored freshly made.

anecdotes.text: Cooking happens on a roughly 6-meter teppan counter where the grilling doubles as part of the dining entertainment.
The house philosophy is summed up as serving freshly made food hot off the iron plate so guests can take their time enjoying it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is pervasive in the kitchen: okonomiyaki batter and noodles, gyoza wrappers, and standard soy/okonomi sauces all contain wheat, and dishes are cooked on a shared teppan, making cross-contact very likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppan and okonomiyaki dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce, which are wheat-based; no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared teppan is used for oysters, fish, pork and egg, so any vegetable items would be cooked on the same surface as animal products.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き（鉄板居酒屋）
neighborhood: 中区流川（流川・薬研堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町1-20 田中ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島市中区流川町1-20 via GSI; coordinates within Hiroshima box. Lands in the Nagarekawa nightlife district, central Hiroshima.
seats_text: Roughly 12-14 seats, mostly counter (about 10 counter seats) with one small table; intimate, counter-focused space. Sources vary between 12 and 14.
hours_raw: 18:00-翌5:00（L.O. 4:30）。公式サイトでは無休、グルメ媒体では日曜定休の記載もあり。
closed_days: Official site states no regular closing day (無休); some listing sites note Sunday closed. Confirm directly as hours/closures may vary.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, late-night counter teppanyaki izakaya where ordering and conversation run in Japanese; a foreign student would be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a Japanese-speaking companion, though the homey atmosphere is welcoming to solo diners.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature Hiroshima okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles (soba/udon), and teppanyaki dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on beef (steaks, beef koune, beef-stock dashi egg), seafood, and okonomiyaki cooked on a shared griddle with meat; dishes use fish-derived stocks and wheat-soy sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: Seat count and closing days differ between the official site (14 seats, no closing day) and grocery/listing sites (12 seats, Sunday closed). Hours run very late (until 5:00 the next morning), suiting after-dinner or late-night visits rather than early evenings. Verify current closures directly.

bio.background: A small, owner-run teppanyaki izakaya tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, opening only in the evening and serving deep into the early morning. The owner shops the market daily, so the day's best ingredients turn up on a handwritten blackboard of specials.
bio.philosophy: The aim is a warm, homey counter where a single traveler or a small group can settle in and watch their food cooked right in front of them, with off-menu requests honored when the ingredients are on hand.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki where the onions are slowly sweated on their own first to coax out their sweetness, alongside hearty teppanyaki such as beef sirloin and fillet steaks, thick-cut beef tongue, and the local rarity beef koune (a cut from the neck/shoulder).

anecdotes.text: The cook chooses seasonal ingredients at the market each day and grills them in front of guests at the counter, with a blackboard of daily specials greeting you on arrival.
For the okonomiyaki, the onions are sauteed slowly and alone first to draw out maximum sweetness and aroma before the rest is built up.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle; wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared on the same surface as other dishes, making cross-contamination likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking happens on a single shared griddle used for beef, seafood and egg, so any vegetable item shares contact with animal products.

cuisine: 和菓子・元祖もみぢ饅頭（手焼き）
gf_detail: The signature momiji manju is a baked cake made from a wheat-flour batter, so it is not gluten-free. No gluten-free items are documented, and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation. Travelers needing strict GF should avoid.
vegan_detail: Momiji manju is a wheat-flour cake whose batter typically contains egg, and the standard fillings are sweet red bean paste with seasonal varieties (custard/melon/etc.). With egg in the batter the core product is not vegan, and no vegan items are documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島口西2-6-25
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in Miyajimaguchi on the mainland (not on Miyajima island), about a 5-minute walk from JR Miyajimaguchi Station.
seats_text: About 20 seats (roughly 12 indoors, 8 outdoors); primarily a confectionery shop with a small eat-in/rest area.
hours_raw: 8:00 - 19:00（年中無休、時期により変動あり）
closed_days: No regular closing day (open year-round; hours may vary seasonally)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, family-run sweets shop where you can watch momiji manju being hand-baked; English is limited, so pointing at the freshly made cakes works fine for a foreign student.
neighborhood: 宮島口（Miyajimaguchi）
caveats: The Ito Hirobumi naming legend appears in some listings but is not consistently sourced; a better-documented account credits the innkeeper Eiko of the Iwaso ryokan as the inspiration, so the Ito Hirobumi claim is omitted. Seat split and exact seasonal hours may vary.

bio.background: Takatsudo is the birthplace of momiji manju, the maple-leaf-shaped cake that became Hiroshima's most famous souvenir. The shop's founder, Takatsu Josuke, began making the leaf-shaped baked cakes in 1906 and obtained a trademark/patent as 'maple-leaf-shaped baked manju' around 1910. The original creation is tied to nearby Momijidani (Maple Valley) on Miyajima, where the proprietress Eiko of the historic Iwaso inn is said to have asked Josuke for a sweet befitting the autumn-leaf setting.
bio.specialty: Hand-baked momiji manju, made one at a time, with a smaller size, pronounced leaf veins, and a moist texture; seasonal fillings such as custard, melon, cherry blossom, chestnut, and ramune appear alongside the classic red-bean version.
bio.philosophy: Each cake is baked individually by hand so that every customer receives something made with care, keeping the original recipe and craft alive across generations.

anecdotes.text: The line of momiji manju went dormant after the second generation, and the shop was effectively lost; the founder's grandson, Kato Hiroaki, revived it in his fifties, firing up the irons again and reopening on July 18, 2009 as 'the original momiji manju shop.'

Documented accounts trace the cake's origin to a request from Eiko, proprietress of the venerable Iwaso inn near Miyajima's Momijidani, who wanted a matcha-friendly sweet suited to the maple valley, leading Josuke to create the leaf-shaped manju.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's core product is a wheat-flour baked cake (momiji manju), so wheat is central to the kitchen and cross-contact with gluten should be assumed throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Momiji manju batter is wheat-flour based and commonly contains egg; no vegan-segregated preparation is documented.

cuisine: 甘味処・茶屋 (traditional teahouse: kinako mochi, house-made amazake, matcha, tea)
neighborhood: 宮島・大町（五重塔下／千畳閣のすぐそば）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町419
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町419 returned [132.320145, 34.297195], on Miyajima island near the five-story pagoda/Senjokaku; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Small teahouse with outdoor tatami-mat seating (roughly 4 low table sets plus an elevated tatami area) and some indoor tables; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 10:00-16:30 (LO 16:00) per Rurubu; Tabelog lists 10:00-17:00. Daily, irregular closures (不定休).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays; no fixed weekly closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional Japanese teahouse with no English menu noted, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, but the menu is short (mochi, amazake, tea) and pointing works fine.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature 太閤力餅 is kinako (roasted soybean powder) mochi made from rice, which is typically wheat-free, and the house-made koji amazake is rice-based — but the kitchen makes no GF claims and cross-contact is unverified, so a sensitive celiac should ask directly (Japanese helpful).
vegan_detail: No vegan program is documented. The kinako mochi (rice + roasted soybean flour + sugar) and the rice-koji amazake appear plant-based, but ingredients are not confirmed vegan by the shop and other items (e.g., udon broth) may use fish/dashi, so confirm on site.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog 10:00-17:00 vs Rurubu 10:00-16:30 LO 16:00); used the earlier/conservative close. Closures are irregular (不定休) so confirm before visiting. Seat count not published. No official website found.

bio.background: Tonooka Chaya is a traditional teahouse set on the slope below Miyajima's five-story pagoda and Senjokaku (the Hall of a Thousand Tatami), where guests sit on outdoor tatami mats and look out over the pagoda while they eat. A pine tree said to have been planted around 200 years ago, nicknamed the Ryugen-no-matsu (Dragon's-Whisker Pine), stands in front of the shop as its living landmark.
bio.specialty: Taiko no Chikara Mochi (太閤力餅) — kinako-dusted rice mochi — and house-made koji amazake, served with matcha and tea.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signature Taiko no Chikara Mochi is tied to the building of nearby Senjokaku: the hall was commissioned by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and the sugar-rich mochi is said to have its roots in sweets given to the laborers to restore their strength during construction (Hideyoshi died before the hall was finished).
A roughly 200-year-old, carefully tended pine in front of the shop, called the Ryugen-no-matsu (Dragon's-Whisker Pine) for its resemblance to dragon whiskers, serves as the teahouse's symbol.

positives.text: The signature dish is kinako (roasted soybean) mochi made from rice, and the house-made amazake is rice-koji based, so the core sweets are plant-based and wheat-free in their basic ingredients — though not certified by the shop.

cuisine: 和菓子・スイーツ／カフェ（もみじバターサンド・焼き栗・安納焼き芋ソフト）
neighborhood: 宮島・紅葉谷（厳島神社から鏡の池・紅葉谷公園方面）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町389-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI address search for 広島県廿日市市宮島町389-2; first feature coordinates [132.309982, 34.272606]. Lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50).
seats_text: 小規模なテイクアウト・軽食スイーツ店で、紅葉谷の土産物店「佐々木八重子の店」の一画に入る形態。固定の席数は公表されておらず、座席はごく少数または立ち寄り中心。
hours_raw: 10:00〜18:00（紅葉谷エリアの目安。表参道商店街は概ね10:00頃〜17:00頃で、店舗・季節により変動）
closed_days: 不定休（決まった定休日なし。季節・天候・宮島の人出により変動）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up sweets counter where you can simply point at the soft-serve or butter sandwiches and pay, so very little Japanese is needed, but staff communication in English is not guaranteed.
gf_detail: This is a confectionery shop built around wheat-based items: the signature momiji butter sandwich is a maple-leaf cookie (flour), and momiji-manju style sweets use wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. The roasted chestnuts (くり香) and roasted sweet potato are naturally gluten-free as ingredients, but there is no documented allergen control or dedicated preparation, so a strict gluten-free diner cannot rely on this place.
vegan_detail: No vegan program is documented. The headline items are not vegan: butter sandwiches use butter cream, and the popular sweet-potato treat is paired with dairy soft-serve ice cream. The plain roasted chestnuts (くり香) and roasted sweet potato are plant-based on their own and may be a possible option for a flexible vegetarian, but a vegan should ask directly before assuming anything is suitable.
caveats: Operates as a small satellite counter within a Momiji Valley souvenir shop rather than a full sit-down café; offerings and opening can vary with season and foot traffic. Hours are an estimate based on the Miyajima shopping-street norm and the brand's stated 10:00-18:00; confirm on the day. No published phone or seat count for this specific branch.

bio.background: A Hiroshima/Miyajima confectionery maker, 株式会社風籟堂, that grew up around the island's maple-leaf sweets tradition and now runs a handful of small shops along the route to Itsukushima Shrine. The Momiji Valley branch sits on the walking path from the shrine entrance toward Mirror Pond and Momijidani Park, tucked into a corner of a local souvenir store.
bio.specialty: Their calling cards are the もみじバターサンド (a maple-leaf-shaped butter-cream cookie sandwich finished with lemon or caramel), 国産焼き栗『くり香』 (roasted domestic chestnuts), and a much-photographed pairing of warm 安納 roasted sweet potato with cold vanilla soft-serve ice cream.
bio.philosophy: The chestnut line くり香 is promoted as made from pesticide-free domestically grown chestnuts, reflecting a focus on Japanese ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The brand and its sweet-potato soft-serve have been featured on Japanese television, including the program マツコの知らない世界.
Visitors describe the Momiji Valley spot as an easy stop on the stroll from the shrine to Momijidani Park, with a colorful five-color set of the maple butter sandwiches sold as a souvenir.
Some passers-by note the branch tucked beside a souvenir shop near the path along Mirror Pond, where the warm sweet potato and cold soft-serve combination is the draw.

positives.text: Roasted domestic chestnuts (くり香) and roasted Anno sweet potato are offered as simple, single-ingredient style items that are naturally plant-based, though no allergen handling is documented.

name_ja: ベーカリーカフェ SAN-HITORI（サン ヒトリ）
cuisine: Bakery / cafe — cream pan, savory rolls (惣菜パン), soft-serve ice cream, coffee, light lunch and bento
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery; bread, rolls, and cream pan are all made with wheat. No gluten-free items or accommodations are documented, and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free preparation area. Not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A traditional-style bakery whose signature products are dairy-cream pastries (the house custom cream, cookie-topped cream pan, soft-serve ice cream). No vegan or plant-based options are documented; expect butter, milk, and eggs throughout the bread and pastry lineup.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀13-16
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box; matches the Hatchobori location about one minute from the Hatchobori tram stop.
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, Naka Ward)
seats_text: 14-seat eat-in space at the back of the shop (tables); bread is sold at the front.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 07:00–19:00; Sat 08:00–18:00; Sun & holidays closed (also closed year-end/new-year).
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays (also closed during the year-end/new-year period)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual neighborhood bakery where you can simply point at what you want and pay with cash, IC card, or QR code, so a few words of Japanese and a smile are plenty; no English menu is documented.
caveats: No English menu is documented. The shop does not accept credit cards (electronic money and QR-code payment are accepted), and there is no parking. Vegan and gluten-free travelers should note this is a conventional wheat-and-dairy bakery with no documented allergen accommodations.

bio.background: A small independent bakery-cafe that opened in February 2019 in the Hatchobori shopping district of central Hiroshima, about a minute on foot from the Hatchobori tram stop. The owner was drawn into baking because his own father ran a bakery, and he set out to recreate that kind of warm, old-fashioned neighborhood bread shop.
bio.specialty: House cream pastries — above all the cookie-topped 'cream pan jun,' a chilled, dessert-like roll whose silky house cream was developed over roughly three months in collaboration with a pastry chef. A second version is packed with around 100 grams of golden, almost drinkable cream, about triple a normal cream pan. The shop also makes savory rolls, fried chicken and bento, soft-serve ice cream, and coffee.
bio.philosophy: To be a warm, nostalgic bakery in the traditional style, with the handcrafted custom cream as its signature point of pride.

anecdotes.text: The owner says he was led to baking because his father ran a bakery, and aims for the warm, nostalgic feel of an old-fashioned bread shop.
The signature cream filling was developed over about three months together with a pastry chef; reviewers compare biting into the cream pan to eating a choux cream rather than ordinary cream bread.

A morning bread set is offered for around 300 yen from 7–10 a.m. and a lunch set around 550 yen, drawing local office workers and shoppers; the eat-in area has Wi-Fi and outlets and staff will reheat your bread.

gf_cross_contamination.text: An open wheat-flour bakery where bread and pastries are made and displayed together, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination very likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items are dairy-cream pastries and soft-serve ice cream, and savory items include fried chicken, so dairy, egg, and animal ingredients are pervasive in the kitchen.

lead: あなご料理廣島 紅緒
name_ja: あなご料理廣島 紅緒
cuisine: Anago (conger eel) specialty Japanese restaurant
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature anago dishes are prepared with soy sauce and sake (the simmered anago uses soy sauce; the raw anago is served with ponzu), and the menu features tempura, all of which contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or protocols are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The restaurant is built around fresh conger eel (anago) and other Seto Inland Sea seafood and oysters; dishes use fish, soy sauce, and dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀2-11
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 広島県広島市中区薬研堀2番11号; within the Hiroshima box, in the Yakkenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district near Ebisucho Station.
neighborhood: 薬研堀 (Yakkenbori), Nagarekawa nightlife district, central Naka-ku; near Ebisucho/Okamachi tram stops, about 2km east of the Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome
seats_text: Around 25-30 seats: about 10 counter seats on the first floor plus tatami seating upstairs for groups (a private room seats roughly 8-16).
hours_raw: 18:00～23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Sundays. Generally open on public holidays.
closed_days: Sundays (generally open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small owner-run local spot in a nightlife district where some basic Japanese will help; counter seating is open to walk-ins of two or more, while group tatami rooms upstairs need a phone reservation.
caveats: Seat count and payment details vary slightly between listings (around 25-30 seats; one source notes cash-only while the official site mentions PayPay is accepted) - confirm by phone if paying by card or app. Evening-only, dinner-focused izakaya-style dining.

bio.background: Nakahara grew up in Hiroshima and traces his culinary spark to watching a skilled fishmonger at work at an izakaya where he worked part-time in his youth. After high school he trained at a culinary school, then spent more than a decade as a Japanese-cuisine chef at a Hiroshima hotel before opening his own place, Benio, in 2003.
bio.specialty: Conger eel (anago), Hiroshima's celebrated regional specialty - especially raw anago sashimi (nama-chiri) made from morning-killed live eel, and slow-simmered anago.
bio.philosophy: He wants to make Hiroshima's prized anago accessible and affordable to a wide range of diners, personally handling everything from sourcing to preparation while keeping the atmosphere warm and approachable.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a rustic traditional townhouse roughly 50-60 years old, giving it a charming old-Hiroshima feel.

His signature raw anago sashimi uses eel killed the same morning and meticulously cleaned so there is no off-odor, served with ponzu for a crisp texture and refined umami; another favorite is anago tempura topped with scallions.
Diners are said to travel considerable distances to eat his anago because of its quality and value.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes tempura and dishes seasoned with soy sauce and sake; wheat is present throughout, so heavy cross-contamination risk for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Simmered anago is prepared with soy sauce, sake, and a little sugar; raw anago is served with ponzu - standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on fish (anago), oysters, and dashi; no vegan-safe preparation or separation is documented.

cuisine: Japanese home-style obanzai and seafood izakaya — Setouchi seasonal fish and Hiroshima flavors prepared with a refined, gentle touch in a small, intimate hideaway run by a husband-and-wife team.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu is built around obanzai home-cooking and seafood seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) and dishes such as tempura and assorted fried items. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce is documented. A traveler with celiac disease should treat this as unsafe without direct, careful confirmation in Japanese.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen centers on Setouchi seafood and fish, and the standard opening dishes (otoshi) and many obanzai plates use fish, dashi, and animal ingredients (e.g., small shrimp karaage, chikuwa, cold tofu with kombu tsukudani). Soy-sauce and dashi-based seasoning is pervasive. No vegan menu is documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地1-3 広島第3ビル新館7F
geocode_note: Geocoded from the chō-level address (中区新天地1-3) via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Hatchobori/Ebisucho, in the Nagarekawa-Shintenchi entertainment district about 2 km from Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区新天地・流川（胡町駅／八丁堀電停付近）
seats_text: 20 seats total — 8 counter seats plus one private table room seating about 4.
hours_raw: 火・水・木・金・土 18:00-23:00／月曜は不定期営業（前日午前中までに要予約確認）／日曜・祝日定休
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays closed; Mondays are irregular (open only by advance reservation, confirmed by the morning before).
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, mostly Japanese-language counter spot run by a couple, so a foreign student will be most comfortable arriving with some basic Japanese (or a translation app) and a reservation, especially since seating is very limited.
caveats: Cash only is reported on the primary listing, though one secondary aggregator claims cards are accepted — bring cash to be safe and confirm by phone. Single-item (non-course) diners are charged a 2,000 yen set fee that includes assorted otoshi appetizers. Hours and the irregular Monday schedule should be confirmed when reserving, as a secondary source lists later closing times. Very small room of about 20 seats.

bio.background: A small hideaway obanzai izakaya that opened in summer 2023 in central Hiroshima's Shintenchi/Nagarekawa district, run as a two-person husband-and-wife operation.
bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai paired with Setouchi seasonal seafood — sashimi, a mini seafood rice bowl, agedashi tofu, tempura assortments, and rotating daily specials — seasoned in a gentle, refined Hiroshima style.
bio.philosophy: Handmade, home-cooking-style obanzai served in a calm, welcoming room, with daily-changing dishes and warm, easygoing hospitality.

anecdotes.text: The couple greets guests with friendly, approachable service in a quietly elegant room of uguisu (warbler-green) walls and wooden furnishings, with a counter and a single private table.
Each seated guest is welcomed with a trio of otoshi opening dishes such as cucumber with chikuwa and mayonnaise, small shrimp karaage, and chilled tofu topped with kombu tsukudani.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes deep-fried items such as tempura assortments and small shrimp karaage, indicating shared frying with wheat batter; no dedicated gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and tsukudani-style seasonings (e.g., kombu tsukudani on tofu), which contain wheat; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even otoshi appetizers include fish (chikuwa) and shrimp, and the kitchen centers on seafood and dashi, so vegan separation is not provided.

cuisine: Bakery / freshly baked bread and sandwiches
neighborhood: Funairi-naka-machi, Naka-ku (about 2 km from Peace Memorial Park, near Funairimachi tram stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区舟入中町3-7 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded the chō-banchi address 広島市中区舟入中町3-7 to [132.443848, 34.387791], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 8-seat eat-in space (note: one recent visitor report says the eat-in seating was temporarily suspended at the time of their visit).
hours_raw: 7:00-19:00; closed Wednesday, Sunday, and public holidays.
closed_days: Wednesday, Sunday, and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood bakery where you can browse and point at what you want, so a friendly greeting and a few basic Japanese words are plenty to get by.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery: loaves, rustic campagne, sweet buns, savory pastries, and sandwiches are all made with wheat flour. There are no documented gluten-free items, so it is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A standard bakery whose range leans heavily on butter, egg, and meat/seafood fillings (meat-cutlet sandwiches, shrimp gratin bread, wagyu burgers, custard buns). Plain bread loaves may be free of animal products but this is not documented; a strict vegan would need to ask about each item's ingredients.

bio.background: A small independent neighborhood bakery in the Funairi district of central Hiroshima that opened in June 2015. Despite operating for about a decade, the shop still looks fresh, and staff keep the racks restocked with newly baked bread throughout the day.
bio.specialty: A wide everyday range of breads and sandwiches: pain de mie loaves, rustic campagne, sweet buns, and savory deli-style breads, with crowd-pleasers like shrimp gratin bread, meat-cutlet sandwiches, pirozhki, garlic-salt-butter shrimp bread, and a wagyu cutlet burger.
bio.philosophy: An approachable everyday bakery: most items sit in the 200-300 yen range, making it easy to drop in regularly and try a bit of everything.

anecdotes.text: The shop opens early, at 7:00 in the morning, serving as a local go-to for fresh bread to start the day.
The interior runs deep with bread displayed along both sides, and staff continuously refill the shelves as items sell through the day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working wheat-flour bakery; airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination unavoidable for anyone with celiac disease or a wheat allergy.

caveats: Official website not found; hours and seat count are from the Tabelog listing. One visitor report notes the 8-seat eat-in area may be temporarily suspended, so confirm in person if you intend to eat in.

cuisine: American-casual cafe & bar: sandwiches (katsu sandwich), steak plates, coffee, with live music events
neighborhood: Ginzan-cho, Naka-ku (Hatchobori / Peace Memorial Park area, steps from the Ginzan-cho tram stop)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The signature dishes are breaded-and-fried katsu sandwiches (wheat bread and wheat-flour coating) and sandwiches generally, so the core menu is wheat-based. Sauces likely use standard wheat-containing soy sauce. Treat as not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. The menu centers on meat (steak plates, pork-cutlet katsu sandwiches) and a coffee/bar lineup; nothing on the page indicates plant-based dishes. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町1-2 銀山町アパート 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 広島県広島市中区銀山町1-2 returned [132.468506, 34.391869], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 14 seats (6 counter, 8 table)
hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Fri-Sun 08:00-22:00; Thursday closed. (One local write-up notes hours can be irregular.)
closed_days: Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood cafe-bar where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; some basic Japanese helps as it is a small local spot rather than a tourist-facing place.
caveats: Opening year and owner name appeared in a search snippet (run by Gakuyu Yamamura, opened 2012) but could not be independently confirmed on a fetched page, so they are omitted from the bio. Hours are listed as a fixed window on the listing but one local write-up describes them as irregular; confirm before a special trip. The official site uses a self-signed certificate and could not be fetched.

bio.background: A small, casual American-style cafe and bar tucked into the Ginzan-cho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, an easy walk from the Hatchobori area and within the Peace Memorial Park orbit. It sits right by the Ginzan-cho tram stop near the Kyobashi River, in a low-key local pocket rather than a tourist strip.
bio.specialty: Hearty, value-minded plates with an American-casual feel: katsu sandwiches and other sandwiches, a popular steak lunch served as a one-plate 'grown-up kids' meal, plus coffee and a bar menu. Morning sets are also served.

anecdotes.text: Locals point out the shop name 銀山 is read 'Kanayama' (not 'Kinzan'), a quirk noted for visitors from outside the prefecture.
Known for offering a satisfying steak lunch for around 1,000 yen, described as a lean red-meat plate.

lead: 出雲そば大黒屋 本店
name_ja: 出雲そば大黒屋 本店
cuisine: 出雲そば（手打ち蕎麦・居酒屋）
neighborhood: 中区銀山町（八丁堀近く、平和記念公園圏内）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町5-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県広島市中区銀山町5-3 returned [132.467316, 34.392178], within the Hiroshima validity box.
seats_text: 30席（カウンター7席、座敷18席含む）。座敷あり、全席禁煙。
closed_days: 日曜・月曜・祝日（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school local soba shop where ordering is simple and the staff are warm, but little to no English is likely, so knowing a few Japanese menu words (割子そば, 肉そば) helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Soba here is not certified gluten-free (most soba contains wheat flour as a binder), and the dipping/pouring tsuyu is a standard soy-and-dashi sauce containing wheat. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The tsuyu (sauce) is dashi-based, typically made with bonito/fish stock, and signature dishes such as 肉そば (meat soba topped with raw egg) contain meat and egg. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Business hours differ slightly across listings (one source gives 11:00-14:00 / 18:30-late, another 11:00-15:00 / 19:00-late); confirm before visiting. Closed days reported as Sun/Mon/holidays with possible irregular closures. No official website found.

bio.background: A long-established Izumo soba house in central Hiroshima's Ginzan-cho, founded in 1961 (Showa 36). It has quietly served handmade soba in the Izumo style for over six decades, just a short walk from the Hatchobori area and within the central district near the Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: The house specialty is wariko soba (割子そば), served in stacked wooden tiers: diners eat the top tier, then cascade the leftover sauce down to the next, enjoying subtly shifting flavors as they go. A three-color wariko (三色割子) and a meat soba (肉そば) crowned with a raw egg are also signatures.

anecdotes.text: Regulars and first-timers alike are met by the motherly staff who warmly welcome guests; one visitor recalled being told with a smile, 'You didn't know about us until now? Please come again soon!'
Open late into the small hours, it has long been a favorite spot to finish off an evening with a comforting bowl of soba after drinks.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba tsuyu (dipping/pouring sauce) is a standard soy-and-dashi sauce, which contains wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soba broth is dashi-based and signature dishes include meat soba with raw egg, so animal products are integral to the menu.

cuisine: 中華麺・担仔麺（タンツーメン）の個人店 / Chinese-style noodles, specializing in tan tsu men (Taiwanese-style meat-miso noodle)
neighborhood: 中区銀山町（八丁堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区銀山町12-22 松家ビル3F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島市中区銀山町12-22 returned [132.466293, 34.391426], within the Hiroshima box; consistent with the Ginzancho/Hatchobori area near the Peace Memorial Park district.
seats_text: Seat count not published; the venue is a small 3rd-floor space (a night bar/lounge called Mazan) that serves noodles at lunch, so seating is limited.
hours_raw: 火〜土 11:00-15:00（ランチ営業）/ 定休日 月曜・日曜
closed_days: 月曜・日曜 (Monday and Sunday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, locals-only lunch counter tucked on the third floor of a building (a night lounge by evening), with little to no English, so basic Japanese and a sense of adventure help a lot.
gf_detail: The single signature dish is tan tsu men — a noodle bowl built on wheat ramen noodles with a ground-meat (pork) topping, so it is not gluten-free, and there are no documented gluten-free options.
vegan_detail: The dish is a meat-miso noodle in a chicken-based broth with a ground-meat topping; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: A one-trick lunch spot: essentially the only thing served is a 700-yen bowl of tan tsu men, offered Tuesday through Saturday, 11:00-15:00 only. The room doubles as a nighttime lounge (Mazan), so the daytime atmosphere is unconventional and smoking is permitted indoors.

bio.background: An offbeat little lunch operation in central Hiroshima's Ginzancho, a short walk from the Hatchobori shopping district. By night the third-floor room runs as a lounge called Mazan; at midday it transforms into a no-frills noodle counter that does essentially one thing.
bio.specialty: Tan tsu men (担仔麺) for 700 yen — a light, gentle bowl described as a meat-miso ramen: thin wheat noodles in a chicken-based broth topped with seasoned ground meat, with a distinctive note of celery. Diners are invited to brighten it with added sesame and vinegar.

anecdotes.text: The space leads a double life: a nighttime lounge that opens its doors at lunch purely to serve its single signature bowl of tan tsu men.
Regulars describe the broth as light and not spicy, carrying clear chicken flavor with celery as a key accent, and finished to taste with sesame and vinegar.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only dish is built on wheat ramen noodles, so gluten is intrinsic to the meal and there is no gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The bowl uses a chicken-based broth and a ground-meat (pork) topping, making it unsuitable for vegans/vegetarians; no plant-based version is documented.

cuisine: Hiroshima regional izakaya — seafood (oysters, conger eel, sashimi), local specialties
neighborhood: Tachimachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, within the Hondori / Peace Memorial Park area)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町5-12 ホテル山城屋B1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates (lat 34.39365, lng 132.461044) fall within the Hiroshima bounding box. Building-level point for the Tachimachi block.
seats_text: Approximately 61–80 seats; three private rooms with tatami and sunken-kotatsu (horigotatsu) seating available.
hours_raw: Mon–Fri 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat / holiday eves / holidays 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (when Sunday is a holiday, open Sunday and closed Monday).
closed_days: Sundays (open Sunday and closed Monday when Sunday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed local izakaya where a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps; the menu and staff are oriented toward Japanese diners, but it is a welcoming, low-pressure spot for a foreign student wanting to try Hiroshima seafood.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a Japanese izakaya, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based soy sauce, and fried items (kaki-fry, tempura) use wheat batter and shared fryers. Not a safe choice for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood (oysters, conger eel, sashimi, sea urchin) and meat, with wheat-soy-based seasonings and likely dashi (fish stock) throughout. No vegan dishes or accommodation are documented; not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: A Hiroshima izakaya tucked into the basement of a hotel building just off the Hondori shopping arcade, styled as a Showa-retro 'adult hideaway' with vintage wall clocks and an old phonograph setting the mood. It serves Hiroshima regional cooking centered on seafood: large shell-on grilled oysters available year-round, conger eel (anago) prepared several ways, fresh seasonal sashimi platters, and local touches like rare beef koune.
bio.specialty: Year-round large grilled oysters, conger eel dishes, and a popular uni-and-spinach dish, alongside seasonal sashimi.

anecdotes.text: Large shell-on oysters are brought in year-round from Miyajima-area seafood distributors, and the uni-and-spinach dish is a favorite ordered by many out-of-prefecture visitors.

caveats: Seat count varies by source (about 61 usual, up to 80). Phone and hours confirmed across Tabelog and the official site. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere; treat as unsuitable for strict GF/vegan diners.

cuisine: 自家焙煎の珈琲専門店・喫茶（ハンドドリップ珈琲、珈琲ソフト、パニーニなどの軽食）
neighborhood: 中区幟町（銀山町電停すぐ、八丁堀・平和記念公園圏内）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町9-17
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [132.467621, 34.394299] for 広島県広島市中区幟町9-17; within Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 18席（カウンター4席、テーブル14席）
closed_days: 日曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A small, welcoming neighborhood coffeehouse where staff are used to helping newcomers pick a bean or brew; a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way, and you can point at the menu, though detailed allergy discussion in English may be limited.
gf_detail: This is a traditional coffeehouse, not a gluten-free destination. The signature café items — panini, butter toast and other light meals — are wheat-based, and there is no documented gluten-free menu or handling. Black hand-dripped coffee from the roughly 30 single-origin beans is naturally gluten-free, but no celiac-safe preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan spot. Black coffee (hand drip, about 30 beans) is the obvious plant-based option, but the house specialties are dairy-based — the famous 珈琲ソフト (coffee soft serve) and 珈琲ゼリーソフト, plus smoothies and toast that typically use milk and butter. No documented plant-milk option or vegan menu; ask if a soy/oat milk substitute is available.
caveats: Posted hours differ slightly between sources (9:00-18:30 vs 9:00-18:00); hours_week uses the later close. Seat count is from the listing page.

bio.background: A small, old-fashioned coffeehouse rooted in central Hiroshima. The parent company began in 1947, just after the war, selling coffee and provisions and supplying roasted beans to local cafes and hotels; this Noboricho shop has anchored the neighborhood near Peace Memorial Park since 1952. For more than seventy years the house has bought green beans from around the world and roasted them itself, chasing a mild, approachable cup.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee brewed by hand from about thirty single-origin beans, the shop-only 'Noboricho Soft Blend' (light bitterness, soft mouthfeel, gentle acidity), and the long-loved coffee soft serve and coffee-jelly soft serve.
bio.philosophy: To remain the authentic, everyday taste loved by the local community — keeping older roasting equipment in careful working order while welcoming newcomers and helping each guest find their own favorite cup.

anecdotes.text: The cafe brand is nicknamed after pigeons, a symbol of peace in Hiroshima, and the shop even runs a mobile roasting vehicle to bring fresh coffee to neighborhood events.

A longtime manager recalled first tasting the house coffee at a lunch spot years before ever working there, and that a love of traditional kissaten drew him in even though he once needed milk in his coffee.

positives.text: Staff are accustomed to advising guests on bean selection and brewing, so it is a comfortable place to ask questions about what is in your cup.

lead: つぼやきカレー キートン 幟町店
name_ja: つぼやきカレー キートン 幟町店
cuisine: Pot-baked curry (tsubo-yaki curry); Japanese-style curry specialty house
neighborhood: Naka-ku, Noboricho (near Hatchobori, within the central Peace Memorial Park area)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町12-20
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 広島県広島市中区幟町12-20; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Not published. Described as a calm, retro interior centered on two large U-shaped counters plus table seating.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 18:00-20:00 (per Tabelog); some listings show continuous 11:00-21:00. Weekday 13:00-18:00 entry includes a free mini-salad or soft drink time service.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); some listings state open year-round (無休). Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, easygoing curry counter where a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the picture menu will get you through comfortably; bring cash, as cards are not accepted.
