gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Japanese-style curry roux is conventionally thickened with wheat flour, and the menu features breaded katsu (chicken cutlet, winter oyster fry) cooked in shared oil. No gluten-free preparation or labeling is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu centers on meat and seafood (chicken cutlet, corned beef, special beef, winter oyster fry); the curry base is not documented as plant-based and Japanese curry typically uses meat/animal stock. Some vegetable-forward toppings exist (mushroom, lotus root and eggplant, natto), but there is no confirmed vegan curry base, so a strict vegan cannot rely on this place without asking in detail.

bio.background: Keaton (Keyton / 亀屯) began in 1985 in Higashi-Hiroshima at the height of the family-restaurant boom, when its owner bucked the trend and opened a dedicated curry specialist, convinced that to truly compete in food service you should be a specialist rather than a generalist. The Noboricho branch carries that same kitchen into central Hiroshima, a short walk from Hatchobori.
bio.specialty: Tsuboyaki (pot-baked) curry: each order is simmered and finished to taste in its own specially made 4mm-thick iron pot, brought sizzling to the table so the curry stays piping hot and its spice level can be tuned for each guest. It is served alongside turmeric (saffron-yellow) rice plated in a ring on a black dish, a presentation unchanged since the founding.
bio.philosophy: The house treats curry as Japan's everyday national dish, something equally for children and the elderly, and keeps refining the recipe over the decades so the flavor quietly evolves while staying approachable across a range of spice levels.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained as a painter before going into food, and personally selected the antiques and furnishings that give the dining room its calm, lived-in, grown-up atmosphere.
Rather than holding curry in one large pot all day, every serving is finished individually in its own iron pot so the taste and heat can be dialed in order by order.
Diners describe an unhurried, retro interior built around two big U-shaped counters, with toppings you can mix and match freely.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Breaded fried items (chicken katsu, winter oyster fry) are on the menu, indicating wheat breading and likely shared frying oil; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen prepares meat (beef, corned beef, chicken katsu) and seafood (oyster) curries on the same line; no separation or vegan base is documented.

positives.text: Because each portion is finished individually in its own iron pot rather than ladled from a communal pot, spice level (and potentially other adjustments) can be tailored per order if you ask.

caveats: Tabelog lists hours as 11:00-15:00 and 18:00-20:00 with irregular closures, while Hot Pepper/other listings show a continuous 11:00-21:00 with no regular closing day; hours_week reflects the broader split but call ahead. Seat count is not published. This is one branch of an independent Hiroshima-region curry chain (Keyton/亀屯, ~6+ stores plus Matsue/Fukuoka/Tokyo), so it is locally rooted but not a single-location mom-and-pop. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented; treat dietary claims as unconfirmed and ask staff directly.

cuisine: 牡蠣料理・あなごめし中心の小さな食堂（カキフライ定食、牡蠣うどん、穴子飯など）
neighborhood: 宮島町（厳島神社・表参道周辺、中江町）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町中江町319-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.331451, 34.348637] for 広島県廿日市市宮島町中江町319-3; within the Hiroshima box and located on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: 座席数は確認できず（テラス席ありとの記載あり）。小規模な食堂。
hours_raw: 10:30～売切れ次第
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A small, off-the-main-route local food stop on Miyajima; little evidence of English support, so a foreign student is best off pointing at photos of the set meals or going with a guide or Japanese-speaking friend.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation documented. Core dishes — カキフライ (fried oysters, wheat-flour batter and breadcrumbs), 牡蠣うどん (wheat udon), and あなごめし/穴子飯 served with a soy-based tare (typically wheat-containing soy sauce/mirin glaze) — all involve gluten. Not a gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on seafood — oysters (カキフライ, 牡蠣うどん) and conger eel (あなごめし/穴子飯) — and uses fish-based dashi and soy-based sauces. No documented plant-based options.
caveats: Hours are listed only as "10:30 until sold out" with irregular closures, so the per-day 10:30-15:00 window is an approximation of a lunch-only operation, not a confirmed schedule — call ahead. Seat count is not published. There is an apparent positioning difference between sources: one sister-restaurant introduction frames it as an anago-rice specialist, while the listing and customer photos also show oyster dishes (fried oysters, oyster udon); treat it as a small seafood shokudo serving both. No official standalone website was found; it is operated as a sister establishment of 芝居茶寮 水羽 (Shibai Saryo Mizuha).

bio.background: 瑠羽樹（ルウジュ）は宮島・中江町の路地にある小さな食堂で、古民家を改装した、格子戸や土間を残す落ち着いた空間で宮島名物を出している。芝居茶寮 水羽の姉妹店として営まれている。
bio.specialty: 宮島名物のあなごめし（穴子飯）と牡蠣料理。カキフライ定食や牡蠣うどんなど、地元の海の幸を気取らない定食で味わえる。
bio.philosophy: あなごめしについては『穴子・秘伝のタレ・選び抜いた米の三位一体』を掲げ、宮島の穴子料理を知り尽くした客も満足させることを目指すとされる。
roles: small independent seafood shokudo on Miyajima
sister restaurant of Shibai Saryo Mizuha

anecdotes.text: 古民家を改装した造りで、格子戸や土間の食事スペースなど伝統的な意匠を残している。
宮島の主要な観光動線から少し外れた路地に位置する隠れ家的な食堂。

cuisine: Yakiniku and beef dishes; lunch tonteki/ginger-pork set meals using Hiroshima sangen pork, dinner A5 black wagyu yakiniku in private rooms
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀13-18
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature for 広島市中区八丁堀13-18; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box, about 1km from the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 50 seats; upstairs has eight 2-8 person sunken-table (horikotsu) private rooms that combine to seat up to 32, plus three 2-4 person table rooms.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling local yakiniku and set-meal spot a few minutes from Hatchobori; menus and staff are mostly in Japanese, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help, though pointing at photo menus works for lunch.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The kitchen is built around yakiniku tare and set meals; tonteki and ginger-pork sauces and yakiniku dipping sauces are soy-sauce based (wheat), and breaded/fried items are also on the menu, so this is not a safe choice for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused restaurant centered on pork tonteki at lunch and A5 wagyu yakiniku at dinner; no vegan or vegetarian menu is documented and even sides rely on meat-based broths and wheat-soy sauces.
caveats: Listed as part of the Balcom Group (an eight-restaurant group), so despite the small dual-concept feel it is not an independent or family-run shop. Phone numbers vary across listings (082-962-5858 / 050 booking lines). No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published.

bio.background: A dual-identity beef house in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, within walking distance of the Hondori shopping street. By day it works as a hearty, value-driven lunch spot; by night it becomes a refined wagyu yakiniku restaurant served in private sunken-table rooms suited to anything from after-work gatherings to family celebrations.
bio.specialty: At lunch the draw is tonteki and ginger-pork set meals made with Hiroshima-raised sangen (three-breed) pork, with small, regular, and large portions all priced the same. At dinner the focus shifts to A5-grade kuroge wagyu, and the kitchen serves only beef rated BMS 10 or higher.
bio.philosophy: The head chef deals with suppliers personally every day to select the beef, keeping sourcing standards high and the offering tightly curated around premium A5 wagyu.

anecdotes.text: The upstairs floor is built almost entirely from sunken-table private rooms whose sliding partitions can be removed to open into a single space seating up to 32 guests.
Lunch leans openly toward working diners, with the tonteki priced the same whether you order the small, regular, or large size.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku dipping sauces (tare) and the tonteki/ginger-pork sauces are soy-sauce based, which in Japan normally contains wheat; no gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire concept is pork and wagyu beef cooked on shared grills, so vegan diners face pervasive meat contact and no plant-based menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation information is published on the official site or reservation page.

cuisine: Japanese washoku / kappo — Seto Inland Sea wild fish and local seasonal vegetables, served as kaiseki courses and a la carte, with an affordable seafood lunch
neighborhood: Naka-ku, Teppocho / Noboricho (near Ebisucho and Hatchobori tram stops), central Hiroshima, about 1.2 km east of the A-Bomb Dome
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町4-10 シティコープ幟町1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 41 seats total: counter 5, tables 2-12, plus 4 private sunken-kotatsu tatami rooms seating up to 6 each (up to about 20-24 for banquets). The official site describes 4 counter and 12 table seats plus a private room for up to 12.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat (and holidays / day before holidays): 11:30–14:30 (lunch L.O. 14:00), 17:30–22:00 (dinner L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (open if Monday is a public holiday).
closed_days: Sundays (open when the following Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional Japanese kappo restaurant where dinner is a higher-end kaiseki experience; the affordable weekday lunch is the easiest entry point, but little English is documented, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help, especially for any dietary requests.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. As a traditional washoku/kappo kitchen, dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce and other wheat-containing seasonings, and there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not assume safe options here.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around Seto Inland Sea wild fish (sashimi, grilled, aged) and seafood such as hamo (pike conger) and seasonal fugu; courses are fish- and dashi-centric. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and dishes use fish, fish-based dashi, and wheat-soy seasonings, so it is not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: A family-run Japanese kappo restaurant in central Hiroshima where the head chef trained for about ten years at a renowned Osaka kaiseki house before opening here. He visits the local markets almost daily to hand-pick quality fish.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (central Naka-ku, near the Ebisucho/Hatchobori tram stops)
bio.specialty: Wild fish from the Seto Inland Sea — served as sashimi, grilled, or aged — alongside seasonal vegetables from contracted local farms; seasonal highlights include hone-nuki hamo (deboned pike conger) and wild fugu.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen works in the spirit of ichigo-ichie (treating each guest's visit as a once-in-a-lifetime encounter), sourcing premium seasonal ingredients daily and letting Seto Inland Sea fish lead the menu while occasionally drawing on Western and Chinese touches.

anecdotes.text: The chef spent roughly a decade training at a well-known Osaka kaiseki restaurant and now selects fish himself at Hiroshima markets nearly every day.
Seasonal specialties such as deboned hamo (pike conger) and wild fugu rotate through the menu with the four seasons.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional washoku/kappo kitchen, seasoning relies on Japanese soy sauce, which is wheat-based; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus are fish- and dashi-centric (sashimi, grilled fish, hamo, fugu), so vegan diners face pervasive seafood and fish-stock contact with no vegan options documented.

caveats: Seat count differs between sources: Tabelog lists 41 seats with 4 private rooms; the official site describes 4 counter + 12 table seats plus a private room for up to 12. Dinner runs roughly ¥8,000–10,000 per person, while lunch is approximately ¥1,000–2,000 (basis for under30=yes). No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere; assessments above are inferred from the documented fish/dashi/soy-sauce-based cuisine.

cuisine: Organic French (cuisine terroir), locally-sourced ingredients and pesticide-free vegetables, owner-chef course menu
gf_detail: No dedicated or documented gluten-free program. This is a classic/natural French course restaurant whose dishes routinely involve flour-based sauces, bread, and pastry desserts, so it is not inherently gluten-free. As a fully reservation-only, course-based kitchen, the chef may be able to adapt dishes if you discuss gluten avoidance in detail when booking, but no published GF assurance exists. Confirm directly in advance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant. While it emphasizes organic, pesticide-free local vegetables (e.g. from Watanabe Farm), the menu is built around animal products — signature dishes include Sakaki-yama A5 beef, Hiroshima red chicken, foie gras, caviar, and seafood, and French cooking here uses butter, cream, and stocks. A vegetable-forward course might be possible by special arrangement given the reservation-only format, but there is no published vegan/vegetarian menu. Ask when reserving.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区上八丁堀4-1 アーバンビューグランドタワー12F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市中区上八丁堀4-1 to lat 34.399822, lng 132.46521 — within the Hiroshima box. Near Shukkeien Garden, about 1.5km from the A-Bomb Dome.
neighborhood: 中区上八丁堀 (Kamihatchobori, near Shukkeien Garden)
seats_text: 24 seats (can accommodate standing events for roughly 25-35 people)
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-14:30 (L.O. 13:00); Dinner 18:00-22:00 (L.O. ~19:30). Closed Mondays (if Monday is a holiday, closed the following Tuesday or Wednesday instead); also closed Dec 31-Jan 3. Complete advance reservation only.
closed_days: Mondays (closed following day if Monday is a public holiday); year-end/New Year Dec 31-Jan 3
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, completely reservation-only fine-dining French course restaurant where interaction is mostly in Japanese, so it suits a special occasion rather than a casual drop-in, and you should arrange any dietary needs in Japanese when booking.

bio.background: Shimamura decided to become a chef after a part-time job during high school, which led him to France. He graduated from the Tsuji Culinary Institute's France school, then gained experience at top hotels in Japan before returning to France to train in Lyon and Cannes. Back in Hiroshima, he was involved from the opening of Mikuni Hiroshima, then opened his own restaurant, 'Restaurant Shimamura L'Esprit de Mikuni.' In 2017 he renamed it Shimalabo, and in 2020 opened a sister restaurant, Shimalabo Bis.
bio.specialty: A vegetable- and terroir-driven natural French course built on local Hiroshima ingredients and organic, pesticide-free produce, with signature dishes such as Sakaki-yama A5 beef, a terrine of Hiroshima red chicken and foie gras with winter truffle, and a blue-crab dish.
bio.philosophy: Shimamura inherited the spirit of his mentor, chef Kiyomi Mikuni: 'Cooking is determined by the ingredients — knowing the roots of an ingredient, and the passion to understand it, is what matters.' He aims for an approachable, bistro-rooted style that evolves with each ingredient, each day, and each table, serving food meant to be good for both body and spirit.

anecdotes.text: Shimamura trained directly under French master chef Kiyomi Mikuni and was part of the founding team of Mikuni Hiroshima before striking out on his own; his original restaurant carried the name 'L'Esprit de Mikuni' before being renamed Shimalabo in 2017.

Set on a high residential floor of the Urban View Grand Tower with sweeping daytime and evening city views, the kitchen is known locally as one of Hiroshima's most generously portioned French restaurants, blending classic technique with hearty plates.

positives.text: Because the restaurant is run as a completely advance-reservation, owner-chef course kitchen, there is genuine scope to discuss dietary needs in detail when booking — though no specific gluten-free or vegan handling is documented.

caveats: Chef bio (born 1972, Tsuji France school, Lyon/Cannes training, Mikuni lineage, 2017 rename to Shimalabo, 2020 Shimalabo Bis) comes from a search-result summary of the Hitosara profile; the Hitosara page itself returned 404 on direct fetch, so those specific biographical dates should be treated as well-supported but not directly page-verified. Dinner last-order time varies by source (L.O. 19:00 per Tabelog vs ~19:30 per Gurunavi). No published GF/vegan/allergy program found — dietary safety details are unconfirmed and must be arranged when reserving.

lead: 宮島咖喱麵麭研究所 宮島本店
name_ja: 宮島咖喱麵麭研究所 宮島本店
cuisine: Oyster curry bread (牡蠣カレーパン) takeout / street-food specialty stand
neighborhood: Miyajima (Hatsukaichi), about 3 minutes on foot from the Miyajima ferry pier on the approach toward Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: This is a deep-fried curry bread (karē-pan) shop; the bread is made from wheat flour and items are fried, so the core product is not gluten-free. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: The signature and most products contain animal ingredients: the famous oyster curry bread contains whole Hiroshima oysters, and other varieties use Hiroshima beef, cheese, or egg. No vegan or vegetarian curry bread is documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町浜之町853-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the full street address; lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Takeout / eat-while-walking stand; no seating documented.
hours_raw: 08:00-18:00 (closes early when sold out)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); otherwise open daily but may close early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual grab-and-go counter on the Miyajima tourist street where pointing at the oyster curry bread and a friendly greeting is all you need, though staff may have limited English during busy periods.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations. The shop often sells out and closes before the posted 18:00, so visit early; closures are irregular. The headline oyster curry bread contains shellfish and the bread is wheat-based and deep-fried, making it unsuitable for gluten-free, vegan, or shellfish-allergic visitors.

bio.background: Hiroshima's first curry-bread specialty shop, run by a bakery with more than 40 years of history. The Miyajima main store sits a few minutes from the ferry pier on the approach toward Itsukushima Shrine, with additional branches around Hiroshima Prefecture and one in Kyoto.
bio.specialty: Oyster curry bread (牡蠣カレーパン) made with two plump, whole Hiroshima oysters tucked into a freshly deep-fried curry bun, served hot.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing a local Hiroshima ingredient in an easy eat-while-walking street-food form, with a mild, low-spice curry the oyster umami melts into so it is approachable for children and older visitors and even for people who normally avoid oysters.

anecdotes.text: At peak the Miyajima store has sold as many as 2,500 pieces in a single day, and it frequently sells out, so early visits are recommended.
Beyond the oyster version, the shop makes more than 15 original curry breads including Hiroshima beef, stretchy mozzarella cheese, and creamy soft-boiled egg.
The mild curry roux is infused with the oysters' essence to spread a rich umami while keeping the spice low.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Products are deep-fried wheat-flour curry breads, so the core menu is not gluten-free and frying introduces gluten throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature item contains whole oysters (shellfish); other varieties contain beef, cheese, or egg, and there is no documented vegan option.

cuisine: もみじ饅頭・和菓子（焼きたて販売・併設イートイン菓寮あり）
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（厳島神社参道沿い）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町幸町東浜1129
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 広島県廿日市市宮島町1129; result [132.32135, 34.298687] lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: Approx. 70 seats total, including the adjoining in-store tea room where you can eat freshly baked momiji manju with tea.
hours_raw: 09:30-17:00 (open daily; some sources list seasonal hours around 08:30-18:00 with no regular closing day)
closed_days: No regular closing day (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, tourist-accustomed confectionery on Miyajima's main approach where you can simply point at the freshly baked momiji manju and pay by card, though deeper conversation may be in Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature momiji manju is a castella-style cake batter made from wheat flour and eggs, so it contains gluten. No dedicated gluten-free items or handling are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The momiji manju castella batter uses fresh eggs, and several varieties contain custard cream or chocolate (dairy). The plain red-bean (koshi-an / tsubu-an) filling itself is made from Hokkaido azuki and may be plant-based, but the surrounding cake batter contains egg, so even the bean-paste varieties are not vegan. Travelers would need to confirm ingredients directly.
caveats: Hours vary between sources (09:30-17:00 vs roughly 08:30-18:00 seasonally); confirm on the day of visit. This is a confectionery and tea room rather than a meal restaurant.

bio.background: Fujiiya is a long-established Miyajima confectioner founded in 1925 (Taisho 14), making momiji manju on the island since its earliest days. The Miyajima main store sits midway along the roughly 350-meter Omotesando shopping street that runs from the pier toward Itsukushima Shrine, where visitors can watch the maple-leaf cakes being made on the line and buy them hot off the mold.
bio.specialty: Freshly baked momiji manju with a crisp, fragrant castella shell made with local fresh eggs and a piping-hot center of bean paste; varieties include koshi-an, tsubu-an, matcha, custard cream and chocolate, plus seasonal editions.
bio.philosophy: The house's signature is its 'wisteria-colored' (fujiiro) smooth red-bean paste, achieved by an old-school method of peeling the skins from Hokkaido azuki and simmering only the centers, aiming for a gentle sweetness that lets the true flavor of the beans come through.

anecdotes.text: When momiji manju first appeared, makers used various names such as 'Miyajima manju' and 'maple-shaped manju' to avoid infringing the original trademark; by the end of the Taisho era the name had been unified as 'momiji manju.'
The signature pale 'wisteria-colored' koshi-an comes from peeling the azuki skins and cooking only the insides, a hallmark of the house's craftsmanship.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core product is a wheat-flour castella batter, so the kitchen environment is inherently wheat-heavy; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Momiji manju batter uses eggs and some fillings use dairy (custard, chocolate), with no separation documented for vegan needs.

positives.text: Made fresh on site so ingredients of each variety can be asked about at the counter.

lead: 自然食レストラン かんな
name_ja: 自然食レストラン かんな
cuisine: Natural-food / organic daily-changing lunch (無農薬・有機野菜中心の自然食ランチ)
vegan_detail: A vegetable-forward natural-food kitchen using pesticide-free and organic produce, organic-rice miso, and traditional wood-barrel-fermented seasonings; the menu centers on plant-based dishes and desserts like a pesticide-free lemon cake. It is not certified fully vegan, and the daily-changing set may include fish, egg, or dairy, so confirm by phone (082-248-4221) and request a plant-only plate when you reserve.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a Japanese natural-food kitchen, seasonings such as miso and soy sauce typically contain wheat, and items like the lemon cake are wheat-based. There is no stated dedicated fryer or allergen protocol, so a celiac diner must call ahead and explain wheat strictly.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区住吉町5-7 広島療院 5F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 住吉町5-7 (chō-level match); within Hiroshima box. The building 広島療院 sits near Funairi-honmachi tram stop, just southwest of Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区住吉町（舟入本町・平和記念公園の南西）
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small in-building lunch restaurant on the 5th floor of 広島療院.
closed_days: Tuesday and Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet local lunch spot tucked inside a wellness building; little to no English is likely, so a translation app and a phoned-ahead plant-based request will make your visit smooth.
caveats: Tabelog lists the address as 中区住吉町5-7 (the 広島療院 building), while the target brief described it as 舟入本町 — these refer to the same location, as 舟入本町 is the nearest tram stop. Lunch-only, with last orders at 13:30. Vegan/GF accommodation is not certified; call 082-248-4221 to confirm.

bio.background: Kanna is a natural-food lunch restaurant on the fifth floor of the Hiroshima Ryoin wellness building, run in the spirit of the MOA natural-food movement that promotes eating in harmony with nature. It is the kind of quiet, word-of-mouth place locals describe as 'known to those in the know.'
bio.specialty: A daily-changing organic lunch built around pesticide-free and organic vegetables grown by Hiroshima-area farms, finished with a homemade pesticide-free lemon cake for dessert.
bio.philosophy: Cooking that lets the ingredients speak: organic produce, organic-rice miso from Aomori made by traditional wood-barrel fermentation, and seasonings chosen without unnecessary additives.

anecdotes.text: The miso soup is made with organic-rice miso fermented in traditional wooden barrels, with no unnecessary additives.
Dessert features a lemon cake made with pesticide-free lemons.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese natural-food kitchen, miso and soy-sauce-based seasonings used here typically contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

positives.text: Cooking emphasizes pesticide-free and organic vegetables and additive-free, traditionally fermented seasonings, which appeals to clean-eating diners.

cuisine: 和食・食堂（炭火焼きの地あなご、あなごめし、牡蠣など）
neighborhood: 宮島（表参道商店街周辺・厳島神社近く）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町中之町浜504-2
geocode_note: GSI geocoder matched the chō-level point for 宮島町中江町 (closest match to 中之町浜); falls within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: 座席数は未確認。個人経営の小規模な食事処。
closed_days: 火曜日（Tuesdays）
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run Japanese eatery on Miyajima where some basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) and cash will make ordering smoother, as English support is not documented.
gf_detail: The house specialty is charcoal-grilled local anago and anagomeshi, which are glazed with a wheat-based soy-sauce tare; no gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented, so this is not a gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on conger eel (anago), oysters and other fish-based set meals seasoned with wheat-soy sauce; no vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: No Tabelog detail page or restaurant-run official website was located; details (address, phone, hours, specialty) are drawn from the Miyajima Tourism Association listing and HitoSara. Seat count and exact menu/prices are unconfirmed. A separate crayonsite page for a "食事処 きらく" turned out to be an unrelated, now-closed shop in Saitama and was disregarded.
sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=広島県廿日市市宮島町中之町浜504-2

bio.background: A small, independently run eatery on Miyajima, just off the path between the Omotesando shopping arcade and Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled local anago (conger eel) and anagomeshi, a Miyajima signature dish.
bio.philosophy: The shop invites visitors to try its charcoal-grilled local anago at least once.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes itself simply by its specialty: charcoal-grilled delicious local anago that it hopes guests will try at least once.

name_ja: 御食事処 山一本店
cuisine: あなご料理・海鮮の食堂（自家製タレのあなごめし、焼き牡蠣などの宮島郷土料理）
neighborhood: 宮島（桟橋前・表参道商店街周辺）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町港町1162-3
geocode_note: Geocoded from address; lands on Miyajima island near the ferry pier, within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 1階はテーブル席、2階・3階は座敷の大広間で総席数およそ190席（団体・宴会対応）。口コミでは1階に5〜6席のテーブル、2階に50〜60人収容の大広間との記載もある。
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00（公式サイト）。一部の情報源では10:30-17:00、不定休の記載あり。
closed_days: 公式サイトでは年中無休。一部情報源では不定休との記載あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly dining hall right by the Miyajima ferry pier where pointing at the anago-meshi or oyster set on the menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" is enough; a few words of basic Japanese help but are not required.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature anago-meshi is glazed with a soy-sauce-based tare that contains wheat, and dishes such as tempura and grilled-oyster sauces also typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Celiac travelers should treat this place as unsafe without direct confirmation.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around seafood — Miyajima anago (conger eel) and oysters — and dishes use fish-based dashi and wheat-soy-based tare. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Official website returns a TLS certificate mismatch when forced to HTTPS; the HTTP version is reachable. Hours vary slightly across sources (10:00-17:00 official vs 10:30-17:00 elsewhere) and closed-days status is inconsistent (年中無休 vs 不定休), so confirm before a visit. Payment: credit cards accepted, electronic money not accepted.

bio.background: 山一本店 began not as a restaurant but as a fish market run by local Miyajima fishermen. To share the oysters and conger eel pulled from the waters around the island with the steady stream of visitors arriving by ferry, the family opened an eatery directly in front of the Miyajima pier, just a minute's walk from where the boats land.
bio.specialty: 宮島産の穴子と牡蠣を使った郷土料理。看板はあなごめし、焼き牡蠣、穴子丼、天ぷらなど。
bio.philosophy: 宮島の海で獲れた新鮮な穴子と牡蠣を、訪れる人にその場で味わってもらうこと。

anecdotes.text: The shop traces its roots to a fish market operated by fishermen, and opened a dining room right in front of the pier so ferry arrivals could eat the day's Miyajima oysters and conger eel.

Set across multiple floors with table seating downstairs and large tatami banquet rooms above, it has long served as a roomy, accessible stop for both individual travelers and tour groups near Itsukushima Shrine's approach.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature anago-meshi is finished with a soy-sauce-based tare, which in Japan virtually always contains wheat; tempura and oyster dishes likewise involve wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on fish and shellfish (conger eel, oysters) and fish-based dashi, so cross-contact with animal products is pervasive throughout the menu.

cuisine: 和食・食堂（冬は新鮮な牡蠣料理、軽食・ソフトクリームなど）
neighborhood: 宮島（表参道・厳島神社周辺）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町久保町279-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 132.309982, lat 34.272606, within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: About 10 seats (small local eatery).
hours_raw: Roughly 10:00/11:00–17:00 daily; the two association listings differ slightly on opening time. Closures are irregular.
closed_days: Irregular closures (no fixed closing day).
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, affordable local spot where a few words of Japanese and a friendly smile go a long way; the association notes some foreign-language support, but expect a casual, mostly Japanese setting.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a small Miyajima eatery serving oyster and local dishes, sauces and seasonings typically use wheat-based soy sauce, and there is no indication of gluten-free menu items or allergy handling.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The kitchen centers on fresh oysters (winter) and other local Miyajima fare; dishes rely on seafood and wheat-soy seasonings, with no documented plant-based options.
caveats: Hours and opening time vary between sources (around 10:00/11:00 to 17:00) and closures are irregular, so confirm before visiting. Cash only. This is a casual, small local shop rather than a sit-down seafood specialist; winter is when fresh oyster dishes appear on the menu.

positives.text: A small, affordable, casual local eatery near the approach to Itsukushima Shrine that serves fresh oyster dishes in winter alongside light bites.

lead: コトノハ vegan & gluten-free sweets 工房
name_ja: コトノハ 〜Vegan & Gluten-free 工房〜
cuisine: Vegan & gluten-free baked sweets workshop (rice-flour cakes, muffins, cookies; no wheat, egg, dairy, or refined white sugar)
gf_detail: Dedicated gluten-free workshop. Every item is made with rice flour (米粉) and contains no wheat flour at all. The shop explicitly advertises コンタミ対応 (cross-contamination control), and multiple writeups describe it as a safe choice even for people with serious wheat allergies. Treat any claim below the celiac 20ppm threshold as unverified by lab testing, but the entire kitchen is wheat-free by design.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. All baked goods are made without eggs or dairy (卵・乳不使用) as well as without refined white sugar. Drinks include coffee served with plant-based milk. There is no animal product on the menu, so the entire output is vegan-friendly.
address_ja: 〒731-0154 広島県広島市安佐南区上安2丁目16-18
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [132.446487, 34.472851]. Latitude 34.4729 is north of the Hiroshima validation box (34.26-34.42), which is expected because the shop sits in Asaminami-ku (Kamiyasu), well north of central Hiroshima. Coordinates set to null per spec; the geocoded point is plausible and lies a 5-minute walk from Astram Line Kamiyasu Station.
neighborhood: Kamiyasu, Asaminami-ku (Hiroshima City) - about a 5-minute walk from Kamiyasu Station on the Astram Line, north of the city center
seats_text: Takeout-only workshop (工房). No eat-in seating; 2 parking spaces in front of the shop.
hours_raw: 営業日: 水・木・金・土 / 営業時間: 10:00-16:00（売り切れ次第閉店）
closed_days: Sunday, Monday, Tuesday (open Wed-Sat only); may close early once sold out
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny rice-flour sweets workshop where English is unlikely, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the display case or use a translation app, and ideally bring a Japanese-speaking friend or pre-translated notes if buying a custom whole cake.
caveats: This is a takeout baked-goods workshop in Asaminami-ku, north of central Hiroshima - it is NOT near the A-Bomb Dome or on Miyajima, and requires an Astram Line trip (alight at Kamiyasu Station). Open only Wednesday-Saturday, 10:00-16:00, and closes early when sold out. Whole cakes and pound cakes need ordering about 2 business days ahead. There is no dine-in seating.

bio.background: Kotonoha is a small independent baked-sweets workshop in the Kamiyasu neighborhood of Hiroshima's Asaminami ward, making cakes, muffins, cookies and granola entirely from rice flour with no wheat, no eggs, no dairy and no refined white sugar.
bio.specialty: Rice-flour (komeko) baked goods - weekly muffins, chiffon and sponge whole cakes, pound cakes, sables and cookies - all vegan and gluten-free.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its founding wish as wanting to make snacks that anyone can eat with peace of mind, welcoming people with food allergies, those on restricted diets, breastfeeding mothers and the health-conscious, while keeping sweetness gentle.

anecdotes.text: The shop explicitly practices cross-contamination control (kontami-taio), and local food writers describe it as a place where even people with serious allergies can buy treats safely.

Founded with the mission of making snacks anyone can safely enjoy, it serves people with allergies, restricted diets, nursing mothers and health-conscious customers, using rice flour in place of wheat and keeping sweetness modest.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop explicitly advertises cross-contamination control (コンタミ対応); the entire kitchen is wheat-free since all products are made with rice flour and no wheat flour is used anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No eggs or dairy are used anywhere in the workshop, so there is no animal-product cross-contact risk in the baked goods.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Founded to make snacks anyone can eat with peace of mind, the shop caters specifically to customers with food allergies and dietary restrictions and labels everything as wheat-, egg-, dairy- and white-sugar-free.

positives.text: 100% vegan and gluten-free output: every item uses rice flour and excludes wheat, eggs, dairy and refined white sugar.
Local allergy-focused writeups call it effectively a perfect shop for allergy sufferers thanks to its cross-contamination measures.

name_ja: 中華そば うぐいす
cuisine: Hiroshima-style chuka soba (Chinese-style ramen) — light tonkotsu-shoyu (pork-bone soy) broth with thin firm noodles; a classic late-night post-drinking ramen
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a soy-sauce-based broth, and Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat. There are no gluten-free options and no indication of allergen accommodation at this small counter shop.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature broth is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and soy-sauce base, and bowls are typically topped with chashu pork and other animal products. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16 returned [132.463654, 34.391228], within the Hiroshima bounding box, in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Naka-ku.
neighborhood: Nagarekawa-cho, Naka-ku — central Hiroshima nightlife district (Nagarekawa/Yagenbori), beside Shintenchi Park, about a 3-minute walk from Ebisucho streetcar stop
seats_text: About 10 counter seats only; no tables or private rooms
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 20:00–翌2:00 (8:00 PM – 2:00 AM); closed Sundays and public holidays
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, late-night locals' counter shop where little or no English is spoken and the menu is in Japanese, so knowing a few basic phrases (or pointing at chuka soba) helps a foreign student feel comfortable.

bio.background: Uguisu is one of the original postwar Hiroshima chuka soba shops, founded in 1948. It is counted among the city's so-called 'kotori-kei' (small-bird-named) ramen lineage, said to descend from stalls that defined Hiroshima's light tonkotsu-shoyu ramen after the war. The founder is described as having learned soup-making at a small eatery in Manchuria before opening a stall in Hiroshima's old Naka-Shintenchi area.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style chuka soba: a light, nostalgic pork-bone-and-soy broth with slightly firm thin noodles and bean sprouts; a beloved late-night bowl after a night out in the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife area.
bio.philosophy: Keeping a traditional, unfussy Hiroshima chuka soba alive at a tiny counter that has watched over the Nagarekawa district for generations.

anecdotes.text: The shop has long served as a go-to 'shime' (closing) ramen for people finishing a night of drinking in Nagarekawa, and the hotel owner who blogged about it recalled frequenting it in his younger years.

The founder is said to have learned how to make soup at a small restaurant in Manchuria before starting a stall in Hiroshima's Naka-Shintenchi area, contributing to the city's distinctive light tonkotsu-shoyu ramen tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop using soy-sauce-based broth; gluten is central to the cuisine and there is no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a pork-bone soy-sauce (tonkotsu-shoyu) base; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are pork/animal-based; vegan diners cannot be accommodated and cross-contact is unavoidable.

caveats: Cash only per the HotPepper listing; an older Tabelog listing notes some credit cards (JCB/AMEX/Diners) may be accepted, so carry cash to be safe. Open only late at night (8 PM–2 AM), closed Sundays and holidays. Very small counter (about 10 seats), Japanese-only menu, walk-in only. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners — wheat noodles and pork-bone soy broth are fundamental to every bowl.

lead: 広島風お好み焼き くらわんか
name_ja: 広島風お好み焼き くらわんか
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (savory layered wheat-batter pancake with cabbage, noodles, egg); signature oyster-topped "Kurawanka-yaki" using local Miyajima oysters
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and includes wheat noodles (soba/udon), and the finishing okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based. Everything is cooked on a shared communal iron griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki uses egg and pork, the signature dishes feature oysters, and the standard okonomiyaki sauce typically contains fish-derived and other animal ingredients. Dishes are cooked on a shared griddle alongside meat and seafood. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町甲589-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address returned [132.309982, 34.272606], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando shopping street / ferry terminal area.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Omotesando shopping street), Hatsukaichi — a few minutes' walk from the Miyajima ferry pier toward Itsukushima Shrine
seats_text: Counter seating along the long iron griddle plus table seating in a back area; small shop, exact seat count not documented
hours_raw: 11:00–18:00 (some sources list close at 17:00). Closed Wednesdays (open if Wednesday is a holiday; no regular closures in May, August, and November).
closed_days: Wednesday (open when Wednesday falls on a holiday; no closures during May, August, and November)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-frequented griddle shop on Miyajima's main shopping street where staff are used to visitors; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome, and staff will ask whether you'd like to eat straight off the hot griddle or from a plate.
caveats: Closing time varies by source between 17:00 and 18:00; seat count is not published. Founding year (1991 / Heisei 3) and family-run character come from tourism-guide summaries rather than an official restaurant page (no official website found). Hours_week reflects the documented 11:00 open with the latest listed close.

bio.background: Kurawanka opened in 1991 on Miyajima island's busy Omotesando shopping street, a short walk from the ferry pier on the way toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is a small, independently run okonomiyaki shop that has spent decades feeding both visitors making the pilgrimage to the famous floating torii and locals.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki — a thin wheat crepe layered with mounded cabbage, pork, egg and griddled noodles. The house signature is the 'Kurawanka-yaki,' and the most eye-catching version tops it with plump local Miyajima oysters, letting you taste the griddle-grilled oyster and the okonomiyaki layers both together and on their own. Noodle portions can be adjusted (half-size available), and grilled oysters are offered as a standalone small plate.
bio.philosophy: A lively, hands-on griddle experience: a long iron teppan runs along the counter so guests can watch their okonomiyaki cooked in front of them, and staff invite you to eat it the traditional Hiroshima way — straight off the hot griddle — or from a plate.

anecdotes.text: The shop's signboard is shaped like a shamoji (the wooden rice paddle that is a famous Miyajima craft), tying the restaurant to the island's identity.
On the oyster okonomiyaki, the oysters sit like a topping so you can enjoy the okonomiyaki and the grilled oysters as distinct flavors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a single long communal iron griddle shared across all dishes (wheat batter, wheat noodles, pork, seafood), so gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is finished with a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce; no gluten-free sauce option is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items are cooked on the same griddle as pork and oysters, and standard okonomiyaki includes egg, making any vegan preparation impractical.

lead: 穴子天丼・天丼 天ぷら みしなや
name_ja: 穴子天丼・天丼 天ぷら みしなや
cuisine: 天ぷら・穴子天丼・あなごめし（天ぷら専門店）
neighborhood: 宮島・大願寺裏（厳島神社出口から徒歩約2分）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町北大西町1167-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode resolved to the chō-level point 広島県廿日市市宮島町北之町浜 on Miyajima island (lat 34.2995, lng 132.3216), within the Hiroshima box. This is a Miyajima address so the geocode is kept per instructions; it represents the general island block, not the exact storefront.
seats_text: 約28席（座敷：4人掛けタタミ席5卓・2人掛けタタミ席1卓、カウンター6席）。小さなカウンターも備えた個人店。
hours_raw: 11:00～16:00（ランチのみ、ディナー営業なし。食事ラストオーダーは14:30前後。なお食べログ掲載では11:00～15:00表記もあり、提供時間は時期により変動の可能性）
closed_days: 不定休（営業日・休業日が一定しないため、訪問前に確認推奨）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly island tempura shop where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; point at the photo menu and you'll be fine, but staff may not speak much English.
gf_detail: Mishinaya appears in gluten-free dining guides and is repeatedly described by GF travelers as serving "good gluten-free tempura" with accommodating service. However, it is NOT a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, and no documented details exist on the batter (rice flour vs. wheat), frying oil sharing, or cross-contamination controls. The signature anago-meshi and anago glaze use a sweet tare that is very likely soy/wheat-based and not confirmed GF. Anyone with celiac disease should confirm the batter and fryer setup directly before ordering.
vegan_detail: This is an anago (conger eel) and tempura specialist; the headline dishes are eel over rice and tempura sets that include shrimp. Fish, fish-based dashi, and wheat-soy glazes are central to the menu, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (11:00-16:00 on a detailed guide vs. 11:00-15:00 on Tabelog); treated as lunch-only with an irregular closing day. Phone number is not publicly listed. Gluten-free reputation rests on community reviews, not on documented kitchen protocols. Geocode is chō-level (island block), not the exact storefront.

bio.background: Mishinaya is a small, owner-run tempura specialist tucked behind Daisenji temple on Miyajima, just a couple of minutes' walk from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine. It opened in mid-April 2024 in a spot that previously housed another tempura restaurant, quietly continuing the building's frying tradition. A yellow noren curtain marks the entrance, and window seats look out toward the great floating torii gate.
bio.specialty: Made-to-order tempura and anago (conger eel): the standout is the anago-meshi, soft-simmered eel given a light caramelized char, brushed with a sweet glaze and laid over rice with miso soup and a small side; tempura sets pair shrimp and seasonal vegetables with a generous piece of anago tempura.
bio.philosophy: A focus on freshly fried, cooked-to-order tempura (each order takes around 20 minutes), prized as one of the few dedicated tempura houses on the island.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for its resident dogs, including a friendly mascot named Lucky, and for relaxed weekday afternoons when window tables with a torii-gate view are easiest to get.
Each dish is fried to order and can take about 20 minutes, a deliberate trade-off for crisp, just-cooked tempura.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago is finished with a sweet glaze (amadare) typical of anago-meshi, which is generally soy/wheat-based; the menu offers two dipping sauces with tempura sets. None are confirmed gluten-free, so a celiac diner should verify before ordering.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Gluten-free travelers describe the shop as 'very accommodating' for gluten-free tempura, but no specific allergy-handling procedure is documented.

positives.text: Listed in gluten-free dining guides with reviewers noting 'good gluten free tempura' and a great view of the torii gate.

cuisine: 鉄板焼き（A4広島牛ステーキ・瀬戸内海鮮）/ Teppanyaki — Hiroshima beef steak and Seto Inland seafood
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a teppanyaki steakhouse where dishes are cooked on a shared iron griddle and seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat). There is no mention of a dedicated fryer, gluten-free soy sauce, or cross-contamination controls. A celiac or strictly gluten-free diner should not assume any dish is safe without speaking directly to the kitchen.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around A4-grade Hiroshima beef steak and Seto Inland seafood (including live lobster). Sauces and seasonings rely on standard soy sauce and the dishes center on meat and fish, so there is no documented vegan offering. A vegan or vegetarian diner would find essentially nothing suitable and should not rely on this venue.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀8-11
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区八丁堀8-11 returned [132.463043, 34.396107], within the Hiroshima box; located near the Hatchobori tram stop in central Naka Ward.
seats_text: 43 seats total (counter 9, tables 34); private rooms accommodating 2–20 guests are available.
hours_raw: Mon–Wed, Sun, holidays: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Fri, Sat, day before holidays: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Closed Thursday.
closed_days: Thursday (note: the official site states year-round operation, but the detailed listing specifies Thursday closed — confirm before visiting on a Thursday)
neighborhood: 広島市中区八丁堀（八丁堀電停近く）/ Hatchobori, Naka Ward, central Hiroshima
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed teppanyaki steakhouse in central Hiroshima where a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way; no English menu is documented, so a translation app helps when discussing cuts or ingredients.
caveats: Dinner budget runs roughly ¥8,000–10,000 per person, so this is a splurge rather than a budget meal. Not suitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diners. Hours vary by day and there is a conflict between the official site (year-round) and the detailed listing (Thursday closed) — confirm directly before a Thursday visit.

bio.background: The owner discovered a passion for hospitality during his student years and founded Aohige with a simple mission: to energize people through good food. Over time he refined his teppanyaki craft, pouring the same care into the cooking and the interior so the place feels like a quiet hideaway for grown-ups in the middle of the city.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant aims for fully local sourcing, choosing Hiroshima-grown vegetables and seasonings, and deliberately recommends A4-grade beef rather than the very fattiest cuts so guests taste the genuine balance of marbling and lean meat. Local drinks round it out, including Saijo's Kamotsuru sake and wines from Miyoshi.
bio.specialty: A4-grade Hiroshima beef steak cooked on the iron griddle, with a choice of sirloin, fillet, or lean cuts, alongside fresh Seto Inland seafood such as live lobster kept in tanks.

anecdotes.text: The founder's love of food service began in his student days, and that spark grew into a steakhouse devoted to celebrating Hiroshima's own beef, produce, sake, and wine under one roof.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan (iron griddle) where beef, seafood, and seasonings are prepared together, which creates a high risk of gluten cross-contact for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned in the standard Japanese teppanyaki style, which uses ordinary soy sauce containing wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on beef and seafood cooked on a shared griddle, so any vegetable items would be prepared alongside animal products.

cuisine: 寿司・鮨（瀬戸内前鮨、地魚おまかせ）
neighborhood: 広島市中区胡町（八丁堀・胡町エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町5-7 ウィンザービル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区胡町5-7 via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box (central Naka-ku, near Ebisu-cho).
seats_text: 16 seats total — 12 counter seats plus a private room for up to 4
hours_raw: Tue–Fri 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat–Sun 12:00–14:00 (L.O. 13:00) and 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); closed Mondays. Lunch served weekends only.
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only counter sushi bar where the chef serves an omakase course, so a foreign student should be ready to book ahead and converse mostly in Japanese, though pointing and simple words usually work fine at the counter.
gf_detail: This is a traditional omakase sushi counter. Nigiri is brushed with or served alongside soy sauce, which in Japan is almost always brewed with wheat, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Treat it as not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: A fish-and-seafood omakase sushi restaurant built around Seto Inland Sea local fish; the meal is centered on raw fish, anago (eel), and seafood, so it is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: The Tabelog URL provided in the target (.../34023207/) is the chef's OLD location in 銀山町 and is marked 移転 (relocated). The restaurant now operates at 胡町5-7 ウィンザービル 2F under a newer Tabelog listing (.../34030280/), which matches the target address. Hours and lunch availability can vary; lunch is weekend-only. Confirm current hours and pricing when booking.

bio.background: The chef trained in both Hiroshima and Osaka in Japanese cuisine and sushi before opening his own counter, going independent in March 2014. Rather than coming up through a famous Tokyo edomae house, he built his style around his home waters, and his skills have grown markedly in the years since opening.
bio.specialty: Setouchi-mae sushi — omakase built around local Hiroshima and Seto Inland Sea fish, including rotating white-fish selections, anago (sea eel), and seasonal catch, finished as nigiri over a distinctive shari.
bio.philosophy: He prioritizes quality while maximizing the use of local fish, sourcing directly from island fishermen in his home region partly in response to declining local fish stocks. The shari is made from Hiroshima koshihikari blended with aged rice and seasoned with two local red vinegars (from Onomichi and the Tosoki area), reflecting a deliberately Hiroshima-rooted take on edomae sushi.

anecdotes.text: The chef sources fish directly from fishermen on the islands of his hometown, leaning into Seto Inland Sea local fish as both a flavor signature and a response to dwindling regional catches.
His rice is a blend of Hiroshima koshihikari and aged rice, seasoned with two local red vinegars from Onomichi and Tosoki, giving the shari a regional character distinct from Tokyo-style sushi.
After relocating, the shop moved to a counter in central Naka-ku and is regarded locally as one of Hiroshima's standout sushi destinations.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional omakase sushi counter, dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is typically brewed with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is an omakase centered on raw fish and seafood; there is no documented vegan or vegetarian course, so vegan diners cannot be safely accommodated.

cuisine: 牡蠣（焼きかき・殻付き生かき）／郷土料理
neighborhood: 宮島口
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島口1-1-13
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県廿日市市宮島口1-1-13 to [132.331451, 34.348637]; within Hiroshima box and consistent with the Miyajimaguchi ferry-terminal area.
seats_text: Small eat-in counter with about 8 seats, attached to the oyster shop.
hours_raw: 09:00-18:00（食べログ／ホットペッパー掲載。定休日の記載なし）
closed_days: No fixed closed day listed; hours may vary seasonally with the oyster season, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a working oyster shop with a tiny standing counter where staff speak mostly Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable pointing at the grilled oysters and paying at the counter rather than expecting English service.
gf_detail: The simplest items, fresh oysters grilled in the shell and seasoned with seaweed salt (藻塩), are naturally free of wheat, but other preparations such as soy-glazed oyster pasta use wheat-based soy sauce and wheat noodles. No gluten-free protocol or allergy handling is documented, so a celiac visitor would need to ask and stick to the plain salt-grilled or raw oysters.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated oyster (shellfish) shop; every menu item centers on oysters, so there are no vegan or vegetarian options.

bio.background: Sakuma Kaisan is a Miyajima-oyster farming and sales house in Miyajimaguchi, just by the ferry pier to Itsukushima. Its roots reach back to 1673, when five pioneers of Kusatsu, among them an ancestor remembered as Gohee of the Koshiya house, perfected a bamboo-raft method of cultivating oysters and began selling them by boat, eventually shipping all the way to Osaka. The family has stayed close to Hiroshima's oyster trade ever since, and today still farms, sells, and grills its own oysters at the shop.
bio.origin: Miyajimaguchi, Hatsukaichi, Hiroshima (the mainland gateway to Miyajima)
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled oysters in the shell and fresh shucked oysters from their own Miyajima beds
bio.philosophy: Cultivating quality oysters with methods suited to each era, carried on across more than three hundred years.

anecdotes.text: The business dates its founding to 1673, when local pioneers established oyster-boat commerce and later won trading privileges in Osaka, tying the family to the rise of Hiroshima oyster farming for over 300 years.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu includes a soy-glazed oyster pasta, indicating use of wheat-based soy sauce and wheat noodles alongside the simpler salt-grilled oysters.

positives.text: Oysters grilled in the shell and seasoned only with seaweed salt (藻塩) are a naturally wheat-free preparation available here.

caveats: Hours and seasonal availability are not fully documented and oyster shops often operate on a seasonal calendar, so confirm opening before a trip. No allergy or dietary protocol is published; treat GF/vegan suitability as something to confirm in person.

cuisine: ステーキ・鉄板焼き（広島牛、瀬戸内海鮮）
neighborhood: 広島市中区大手町（本通駅近く、平和記念公園圏内）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-7-23 ラフォーレビル2F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 広島県広島市中区大手町1-7-23 returned [132.454971, 34.394154], within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26-34.42, lng 132.28-132.50). Lands in central Hiroshima near Hondori, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: 23 seats total (counter 9, tables 14)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Wednesdays (open during year-end/New Year period)
closed_days: 水曜日（年末年始期間を除く）
cultural_comfort_note: A polished local steakhouse where a few Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will get you by; staff are used to serving visitors but an English menu is not guaranteed, so a translation app helps.
vegan_detail: This is a beef-and-seafood steakhouse built around Hiroshima beef, Seto Inland Sea abalone, oysters, conger eel and spiny lobster cooked on an iron griddle. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and dishes are centered on meat, fish and dairy (the signature grilled potato salad includes egg, mayonnaise, bacon and cheese). Not a suitable destination for vegan diners.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Plain grilled Hiroshima beef and seafood off the teppan could in principle be naturally gluten-free, but standard teppanyaki seasonings and dipping/grill sauces in Japan typically contain wheat-based soy sauce, and the shared L-shaped griddle handles many dishes. A celiac diner would need to confirm ingredients and cross-contact directly with the kitchen; nothing on the sources addresses this.

bio.background: Aohige is a Hiroshima beef specialist run by a local operator surnamed Tani, who says he discovered the appeal of the food business as a student and built the restaurant around a mission to energize people through food. Beyond this Otemachi steakhouse, the Aohige group also runs a butcher shop and a bento/prepared-foods business, and in March 2025 opened a location at the Minamoa complex by Hiroshima Station.
bio.specialty: Grade A4 Hiroshima beef (choice of loin, sirloin or fillet) and Seto Inland Sea seafood, grilled on what the restaurant calls Japan's first seamless large L-shaped iron griddle.
bio.philosophy: A strict local-production-for-local-consumption ethos: showcasing Hiroshima Prefecture ingredients to guests from the neighborhood, the rest of Japan and overseas, while lowering the intimidation factor of a steakhouse so younger diners and after-work guests can enjoy Hiroshima beef comfortably.

anecdotes.text: The owner describes choosing A4 Hiroshima beef for its elegant, easy-to-eat fat, and lets guests choose among loin, sirloin and fillet so they can compare cuts.
A house favorite is a warm grilled potato salad finished on the iron griddle with thick- and thin-cut bacon and melting cheese, described as mild and gentle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a single large shared L-shaped iron griddle used for beef, seafood and side dishes, which implies shared cooking surfaces; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on beef, abalone, oysters, conger eel and lobster cooked on a shared griddle; the signature potato salad uses egg, mayonnaise, bacon and cheese, so plant-based separation is not supported.

caveats: No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan/vegetarian accommodations are documented on any fetched source; dietary suitability would need direct confirmation with the restaurant. Phone reservations are taken (050-5597-4341); advance notice is requested for lobster/abalone. Lunch ~1,000-1,999 yen, dinner ~5,000-5,999 yen.

cuisine: Japanese & Western confectionery — handmade momiji manju and chiffon-based sweets, with eat-in cafe
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The shop's specialty is momiji manju and chiffon-cake sweets, both made from wheat-flour batter, and the cafe focuses on Western pastries. There is no documented gluten-free option and no gluten-free handling described.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature chiffon-style momiji manju and cakes are built on fresh eggs and dairy (cream, cheese, butter), so the core menu is unsuitable for vegans. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町592
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県廿日市市宮島町592 via GSI. Result lng 132.331451 / lat 34.348637 lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box; consistent with a location on the Omotesando shopping street.
seats_text: Small eat-in cafe with roughly 13 seats (about 5 counter and 8 table seats) following the May 2025 cafe renovation. An earlier listing recorded 6 seats.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 daily; closed Thursdays. (An earlier listing also noted Fri/Sat 19:00-22:00 evening hours.)
closed_days: Thursdays
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（厳島神社方面）
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, tourist-frequented sweets shop on Miyajima's main shopping street; menu items are visible from outside and ordering is easy, though deeper conversation will be in Japanese.

bio.background: Kimura began as a Western-style cake shop run by a pastry chef who graduated from the Tokyo Confectionery School (Tokyo Koto Seika Gakko) and trained in Tokyo before returning to open a patisserie in his home of Miyajima. The shop later branched into making and selling its own momiji manju, blending Japanese confectionery with Western pastry technique. In May 2025 it was renovated into a stylish eat-in cafe while keeping the character longtime fans loved.
bio.specialty: Handmade chiffon-style momiji manju that stays moist and soft for days rather than drying out, plus original flavors and Western sweets.
bio.philosophy: Apply Western pastry craft and carefully selected ingredients to a Hiroshima classic, so the souvenir sweet tastes freshly made even after travel.

anecdotes.text: The owner was inspired to create the chiffon-cake momiji manju after a school-trip student remarked that ordinary momiji manju goes dry and crumbly after a few days; using fresh-egg chiffon batter keeps it moist even when cold.

The signature five momiji manju flavors include tsubu-an, cream, chocolate, an original cheese (blending Danish and processed cheese), and a popular apple made with apples sauteed and simmered in wine for a refined taste with crunchy fruit pieces.

After a May 21, 2025 renovation the shop reopened as a modern cafe pairing Western pastries with Japanese tea service, with offerings like a Miyajima omelet and an original maple-themed non-alcoholic drink.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core products (momiji manju and chiffon cakes) are made from wheat-flour batter, so the kitchen routinely handles gluten; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sweets are built on fresh eggs, cream, cheese and butter; egg and dairy are central to the menu, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

caveats: The shop's listed official domain (kashidokoro-kimura.net) now 301-redirects to an unrelated site, so it is omitted as the current official URL. Seat count and evening hours differ between sources: an older listing shows 6 seats with Fri/Sat 19:00-22:00 evening service, while the post-May-2025 cafe renovation is reported at about 13 seats with 10:00-17:00 hours closed Thursdays; the current record reflects the renovated cafe. No gluten-free or vegan handling is documented for travelers with dietary needs.

cuisine: Home-style Japanese teishoku (set meals) and noodle dishes — a casual lunch shokudo
neighborhood: Ebisucho / Hatchobori, central Hiroshima (Naka Ward)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町6-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町6-2 returned [132.465439, 34.39547], inside the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26–34.42, lng 132.28–132.50). Lands in Teppocho, Naka Ward, near Ebisucho tram stop and Hatchobori.
seats_text: Counter seating (about 5 seats x 2 rows) plus tables (4 seats x roughly 10 tables); approximately 50 seats total per the Tabelog listing. Note: this is larger than a typical tiny diner, though it operates as a casual home-style lunch spot.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays; also closed on the 2nd and 4th Saturdays of each month (other Saturdays open).
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local lunch diner where ordering is done in Japanese with no English menu, so a foreign student would do best with a translation app or a Japanese-speaking companion to navigate the daily set meals.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese home-style teishoku menu (fried mains like chicken nanban, stir-fried vegetable sets, miso soup, rice). Dishes rely on regular Japanese soy sauce and seasonings, which contain wheat, and fried items share batter/oil. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish home-cooking diner: chicken nanban, home-style mains, miso soup (almost certainly dashi-based with fish), and wheat-soy seasonings. A vegetable stir-fry set (野菜炒め定食) exists but typically includes meat and non-vegan seasonings, and no vegan preparation is documented. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
caveats: Seat count and some prices differ between listings (one source describes around 50 seats and cards accepted; another describes it as a small home-style lunch counter). Daily set meals rotate, so specific dishes vary. Dinner service hours are listed but this is primarily a lunchtime spot popular with local office workers; confirm evening availability before visiting. No official website found.

bio.background: A modest, unpretentious set-meal diner tucked into the Teppocho block near Ebisucho and the busy Hatchobori district of central Hiroshima. It serves the kind of home-style cooking that draws local office workers at lunch, built around rotating daily set meals.
bio.specialty: Home-style daily set meals (kawari-banawari teishoku): a main dish with a few small sides, miso soup, and rice. Familiar plates such as a stir-fried vegetable set and chicken nanban set appear on the menu.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is a plain, simple space — white ceiling and walls, a reed-panel half-wall, and wood-plank flooring — with counter seats and tables, an everyday neighborhood eatery rather than anywhere fancy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu of home-style set meals relies on standard Japanese soy sauce and seasonings, which contain wheat; no gluten-free options are documented.

lead: 牡蠣と肉の店 ‐IPPO‐ 薬研堀店
name_ja: 牡蠣と肉の店 ‐IPPO‐ 薬研堀店
name_en: Kaki to Niku no Mise -IPPO- Yagenbori-ten
cuisine: Izakaya specializing in Hiroshima oysters and charcoal-grilled yakiton using local brand pork "Seto Momiji"
neighborhood: 広島中心部 薬研堀・流川（八丁堀／胡町近く）
address_ja: 〒730-0028 広島県広島市中区流川町5-32 イワタビルB1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区流川町5-32 via GSI; first feature coordinates within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: About 52 seats (sources vary 51–58); private rooms for groups of 4–18, can accommodate up to ~60 for private events.
hours_raw: 17:00–23:00 (Food L.O. 22:00, Drink L.O. 22:30). Official site lists 17:00–22:00; reservation platforms list 17:00–23:00. Closed Mondays. Closed Dec 30–Jan 3.
closed_days: Mondays; year-end/new-year (Dec 30–Jan 3)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will carry you; reservations can be made online, which helps if you don't speak Japanese.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on fried oysters (wheat batter), yakiton (grilled pork) and oyster dishes typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and ponzu; no gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is oysters (shellfish) and pork; signature dishes include grilled/raw/fried oysters, yakiton, and offal hot pot. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: The linked Tabelog listing is currently 掲載保留 (suspended) and shows a former name (骨付豚 弌歩); the restaurant is confirmed actively operating under the current name "牡蠣と肉の店 ‐IPPO‐ 薬研堀店" via its official site and reservation platforms. Stated hours and seat counts vary slightly across sources (official site lists 17:00–22:00 / closed Mon; reservation platforms list 17:00–23:00 / closed Mon); verify on arrival.

bio.background: A Hiroshima oyster-and-meat izakaya in the Yagenbori/Nagarekawa nightlife district, tucked into the basement of the Iwata Building. It is run by the local company Dot One (株式会社ドット・ワン), which operates several food businesses in Hiroshima under the -IPPO- brand, a name meaning "one step forward."
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters served many ways (grilled, raw, fried, carpaccio, Korean-style chanja) alongside charcoal-grilled yakiton made from the local brand pork Seto Momiji, a pig raised on feed that includes crushed Hiroshima oyster shells.
bio.philosophy: The company describes its mission as creating smiles for both producers and customers through creative cooking, sharing heritage Hiroshima ingredients that have been cultivated over generations.
roles: operator: 株式会社ドット・ワン (Dot One)

anecdotes.text: The kitchen prizes freshness: the Seto Momiji pork is ground/butchered fresh each morning ("asabiki") before being grilled over binchotan charcoal.
The oysters are a branded Hiroshima variety from Maruken Suisan, cultivated so they can be served year-round rather than only in winter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried oysters (kaki fry) use a wheat-flour/panko batter, and the fryer is shared with other breaded items; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oyster and grilled-pork dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and ponzu, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around shellfish (oysters) and pork/offal; broths, grills and fryers are not vegetarian, so cross-contact is unavoidable.

cuisine: Gluten-free, additive-free daily plate lunch with enzyme brown rice (酵素玄米) and koji (麹) fermentation; plant-forward Japanese
neighborhood: 中区土橋町（土橋電停周辺、平和記念公園の西側）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区土橋町4-28
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.446808, 34.393387] for 広島県広島市中区土橋町4-28; within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 18 seats (per reservation listing)
closed_days: Monday and Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A small Japanese-speaking lunch spot; a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the day's plate) will go a long way, and the gluten-free/additive-free focus makes it welcoming for cautious eaters.
gf_detail: Positions itself as a gluten-free and additive-free kitchen (the delivery listing is explicitly tagged 「グルテンフリー 無添加」), centered on enzyme brown rice (酵素玄米) and koji fermentation rather than wheat. Confirm the specific day's plate when you visit, as full per-dish gluten-free verification was not available from published pages.
vegan_detail: The menu is plant-forward and built around enzyme brown rice and koji, but published pages did not confirm that every component is fully vegan (dashi, eggs or dairy in sides were not documented). Ask staff which items on the day's plate are fully plant-based.
caveats: No Tabelog page was found for this Hiroshima location; identity confirmed via HotPepper Gourmet, a delivery listing, and the shop's Instagram. The address (土橋町) sits at the western edge of central Naka-ku near the Peace Park rather than directly on 本通り/八丁堀. Vegan completeness and the owner/background story were not documented in fetched pages; verify on site.

positives.text: Describes itself as a gluten-free and additive-free kitchen, with meals based on enzyme brown rice and koji fermentation instead of wheat.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀11-4 島村ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区八丁堀11-4 via GSI; lat 34.395485 / lng 132.462769 falls within the Hiroshima box. About a 2-minute walk from Hatchobori tram/station (approx. 164m per listing).
seats_text: Seat count not published; the shop has counter seating, table seating, and a back room that operates izakaya-style in the evenings (small independent shop).
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sun 16:00-22:00; Tue closed (定休日)
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small neighborhood okonomiyaki shop where staff likely speak mainly Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help; the counter seating makes it a warm, casual place to watch your okonomiyaki cooked in front of you.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter/crepe and the okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based, and dishes like beef-tendon curry okonomiyaki are served with wheat noodles (udon/soba). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on pork, beef tendon, beef koune (neck), eggs and a wheat-soy-based sauce; the signature suji curry okonomiyaki is topped with eggs. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: Yakeppachi carries a family name across two generations: the owner's father ran an izakaya also called Yakeppachi, and the son branched out on his own to open this Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop, where the building bears the family name (Shimamura Building).
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, with a signature suji curry okonomiyaki — curry-braised beef tendon folded into okonomiyaki and served with udon noodles, topped with two eggs and sauce.

anecdotes.text: The now-quietly-popular suji curry okonomiyaki was actually born out of a local television feature, and it stuck around to become one of the shop's signature dishes.
The shop is the second-generation continuation of the Yakeppachi name: the owner's father ran an izakaya of the same name, and the son set up his own okonomiyaki shop after going independent.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter cooked on a shared teppan griddle alongside wheat noodles, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; no wheat-free or tamari sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Everything is cooked on a single teppan griddle shared with pork, beef tendon and eggs, so vegan separation is not possible.

caveats: Seat count is not published. The official-site field is blank because no dedicated website was confirmed; details are drawn from the listing and a local TV feature. Note there is a separate same-name location near Hakushima — this record is specifically the Hatchobori (八丁堀11-4) shop.

cuisine: Spicy soupless dandan noodles (汁なし担担麺), tsukemen, and spicy noodles (辛めん)
neighborhood: Hatchobori, Naka-ku, Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀6-4 スカイパークビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; result lat 34.395664, lng 132.463913 falls within the central Hiroshima bounding box, consistent with a Hatchobori location near Hatchobori Station.
seats_text: Roughly 32 seats: about 15 counter seats used at lunch, plus two 4-person tables in the evening.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-22:00 (Mon-Sat); closed Sundays and holidays. (Lunch-only 11:30-15:00 also reported on one source.)
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: Ordering is via a ticket machine and the menu is in Japanese, so a little prep or a translation app helps, but the staff are described as friendly and responsive.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle shop. The dandan and tsukemen noodles are wheat-based (reportedly from Harada Seimen), and the spicy sauce and broth are very likely to contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no indication of any gluten-free option or accommodation.
vegan_detail: A vegetable-only ("野菜のみ") noodle variant is mentioned, but the standard bowls are topped with chashu pork, and the noodles are wheat. No source confirms the broth/sauce is free of animal products or that a genuinely vegan preparation exists, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly. Treat as not reliably vegan.

bio.background: A small, stylish noodle counter that opened in Hatchobori in February 2023. The space has a cafe-like feel built around wood and black tones, green plants, and music, a contrast to the typical noodle shop.
bio.specialty: Spicy soupless dandan noodles, tsukemen (dipping noodles), and spicy noodles, all built around a 'secret spicy sauce' the shop says took around four years to develop. Heat is adjustable from level 0 up through 4-plus, with level 2 as the house standard, so both chili lovers and the spice-shy can order comfortably.
bio.philosophy: The shop openly draws on the lineage of Hiroshima's legendary soupless-dandan tradition while developing its own identity through that house-made spicy sauce. Bowls come topped with boiled cabbage, cucumber, green onion, chashu pork, and Japanese pepper, and are meant to be mixed thoroughly before eating.

anecdotes.text: The signature spicy sauce was reportedly researched and refined over about four years before the shop opened.
The shop is described as clearly respecting Hiroshima's foundational soupless-dandan style while building its own take on it.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The spicy sauce and noodle broth are very likely to use standard wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.
