vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard bowls are topped with chashu pork and share a kitchen and water with meat-based dishes, so a vegetable variant would carry cross-contact risk.

positives.text: Heat level is fully adjustable from 0 upward, so the spiciness can be tailored to the diner.

caveats: Reported hours vary slightly across sources: one source lists lunch-only 11:30-15:00 while others list 11:00-15:00 and 17:00-22:00; the dual-service hours are used here as the more detailed/consistent listing. Seat count (32) comes from a listing and may combine lunch counter and evening table configurations. No source explicitly documents allergen handling, a dedicated fryer, or a confirmed vegan preparation.

cuisine: Seafood eatery — Miyajima anago (conger eel) rice, grilled and fried oysters, udon/soba, curry
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on conger eel and oyster rice sets, fried oysters/shrimp/chicken (breaded), udon and soba noodles, and curry — all of which typically involve wheat (batter, noodles, roux) and wheat-based soy sauce/sauces. There is no mention anywhere of gluten-free preparation, separate fryers, or allergen handling.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused eatery. Signature dishes are conger eel (anago) rice and oysters; other items include fried shrimp/chicken, tempura udon/soba (dashi typically made with fish), and meat/seafood curries. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and broths/sauces are not plant-based.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町南大西町52
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō-level address (宮島町52); coordinates fall within the Hiroshima/Miyajima box, near the island as expected.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), near the foot of ryokan Jukeiso, diagonally across from the Miyajima History & Folklore Museum; about a 2-minute walk from Itsukushima Shrine's Karamon gate toward Miyajima Aquarium.
seats_text: 43 seats total: 3 at the counter, 32 at tables, and an 8-seat tatami room.
hours_raw: Official site lists hours as 不定期 (irregular); no fixed daily hours published.
closed_days: Irregular / not fixed. The operator (ryokan Jukeiso) describes opening times as irregular, so confirm in advance before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual island eatery with a picture/set-meal menu where pointing works fine, but staff are unlikely to speak much English and dietary accommodation is not offered, so bring a guide or translation app if you have allergies.
caveats: Opening days and hours are irregular per the operator — it is best to confirm before going. No documented gluten-free or vegan/vegetarian options; menu is built around eel, oysters, fried/breaded items, and wheat noodles.

bio.background: A casual dining spot on Miyajima run as the down-to-earth counterpart to the long-standing island ryokan Jukeiso, sitting at the foot of the inn beside the staircase that leads up to it and just across from the Miyajima History & Folklore Museum. While the ryokan offers a formal stay, Petit Heike opened (around 2016) to let day visitors and passers-by enjoy the island's home cooking without staying over.
bio.specialty: Miyajima-style anago (conger eel) rice — the eel is lightly grilled then steamed and served as a set with side dishes, miso soup, pickles, and a mandarin orange; the eel is supplied through its affiliated ryokan Jukeiso. Local oysters, served grilled or fried, are the other house draw.

anecdotes.text: The eatery sources its conger eel through its parent ryokan Jukeiso and serves it in the regional grill-then-steam style as a complete set meal.
Beyond eel and oysters, the kitchen rounds out the menu with udon and soba, curries, and fried oyster/shrimp/chicken plates, making it an easy all-day stop for sightseers on the island.

cuisine: Craft drink bar / Hiroshima lemon beverages (frozen, highball, soda, cocktails)
gf_detail: This is a drink-focused bar, not a food restaurant, so there is no kitchen frying or wheat-based dishes to worry about. The lemon sodas, lemon frozen drinks, and most cocktails are built from house lemon syrup, soda, spirits and juice and are naturally free of wheat. However, beer-based drinks (such as the Hiroshima lemon beer) contain barley/wheat and are not gluten-free, and the venue makes no formal gluten-free claims or allergen guarantees. A gluten-sensitive traveler can likely enjoy the non-beer lemon drinks but should ask staff to confirm ingredients of any specific item.
vegan_detail: As a drinks-only bar, the core offerings—Hiroshima/Setoda lemon sodas, the signature lemon frozen, lemon gin cider, seasonal lemon mojito and similar drinks made from house lemon syrup (whole peel and juice), soda, spirits and citrus—contain no animal products and are suitable for vegans. There is no documented use of honey or dairy in the standard lemon line, but this is not a certified vegan establishment, so a strict vegan should confirm individual drinks (especially any cream, milk or honey-based seasonal specials) with staff.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町528-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町528-3 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando shopping street toward Itsukushima Shrine.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima island), Omotesando shopping street area, Hatsukaichi
seats_text: Small standing/counter drink bar with limited counter seating and a take-out front counter; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 10:00–17:00 (daily; seasonal variation, some sources to 18:00)
closed_days: No regular closing day documented; hours can vary seasonally.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly walk-up drink stand on Miyajima's main street where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the lemon-drink menu will get you served easily, though it is cash-only so bring yen.
caveats: Cash only (no cards, e-money, or QR payment). It is a drinks bar, not a food restaurant—do not expect a meal. Hours can shift with the season. There are multiple GEBURA locations on Miyajima; this record is the head store (本店) at 宮島町528-3.

bio.background: GEBURA opened in February 2020 on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, set inside a renovated old townhouse. A local architect reworked the space into a pared-back, concrete-toned interior with a long counter, blending contemporary design with the island's traditional streetscape and adding a terrace looking onto the shopping street.
bio.specialty: Drinks built around Hiroshima lemons—using the whole fruit, peel and juice—turned into house-made lemon syrup. The lineup runs from the popular lemon frozen and lemon soda to lemon highballs, gin cider, and seasonal cocktails, with lemons sourced from contract growers on Setoda in the Seto Inland Sea.
bio.philosophy: A spirit of playfulness and hospitality paired with environmental mindfulness: guests get a reusable cup and refills cost only a couple hundred yen, encouraging visitors to slow down, taste several drinks, and enjoy the island rather than rushing through.

anecdotes.text: After your first drink you keep a reusable cup, and any refill across the GEBURA shops costs just 200 yen—an unusual system meant to cut waste and let you sample multiple lemon drinks.
The bar grew into a small family of locations along the Miyajima shopping street and by the ferry terminal, all built on the same idea of whole-lemon, house-syrup drinks.

positives.text: Drinks-only bar with no kitchen frying or wheat-based cooked dishes; the core lemon drinks are made from whole-lemon house syrup, soda, citrus and spirits, so most options are naturally free of wheat and animal products (excluding beer-based drinks).
Signature drinks use Hiroshima/Setoda lemons with the whole peel and juice, plus house-made lemon syrup, making the standard lemon line transparent and plant-based.

cuisine: 手網焙煎コーヒースタンド・カフェ（プラントベース／グルテンフリー）
gf_detail: All sweets and food are made gluten-free (wheat-free). The wife develops every recipe in-house without wheat, dairy, eggs, refined sugar, artificial coloring, or preservatives. Items include carrot cake, a cheese-free "cheesecake," and canelés. This is a genuinely dedicated gluten-free kitchen, not an options menu. Note: as a coffee/sweets stand they do not serve savory mains.
vegan_detail: All food and sweets are plant-based with no animal products (no eggs, dairy, or butter). Lattes use soy milk or oat milk. Caffeine-free options include decaf coffee, genmai tea, and amazake. Coffee is exclusively Ethiopian beans, hand-net roasted (手網焙煎) in tiny batches in-house. Effectively a fully vegan menu.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区白島北町12-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 広島市中区白島北町12-2, within the Hiroshima box. Matches the documented location immediately by JR Shin-Hakushima / Astram Line Hakushima station.
neighborhood: 白島北町（新白島駅すぐ）
seats_text: About 9 seats; small intimate space, all non-smoking, no private rooms.
hours_raw: 8:00-18:00（出典により8:00-17:00との記載もあり）。定休日：木曜・不定休
closed_days: 木曜日、および不定休（Thursdays plus occasional irregular closures）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warm husband-and-wife coffee stand where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the menu is fully plant-based and gluten-free so dietary needs are already covered, but the owners speak primarily Japanese.
caveats: Hours vary slightly by source (closing reported as both 17:00 and 18:00) and there are occasional irregular closures in addition to the regular Thursday closure, so check their Instagram before visiting. Small seat count means it can fill up. As a coffee-and-sweets stand it does not serve full meals.

bio.background: ninin is a small coffee stand opened on March 27, 2023 in the quiet Hakushima-Kitamachi neighborhood of central Hiroshima, a short walk from Shin-Hakushima station. It is run by a husband and wife; the name 'ninin' (two people) comes from their wish to build something together as a couple. The husband, who has a background in construction, handcrafted much of the interior himself, from the wooden tables and chairs to the concrete counters, giving the little space a warm, made-by-hand feel that visitors say looks far from amateur.
bio.specialty: Ethiopian coffee, hand-net roasted (手網焙煎) in very small batches, paired with plant-based, gluten-free sweets made entirely in-house.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made without animal products, wheat, refined sugar, artificial coloring, or preservatives. The wife developed all the dessert recipes herself, and one motivation she has shared is the wish for her husband to live a long, healthy life, which guides the gentle, additive-free baking. They aim for sweets so good 'you wouldn't know they're plant-based.'

anecdotes.text: The wife created the gluten-free, plant-based dessert recipes herself, motivated in part by wanting her husband to enjoy a long and healthy life.
The husband, who comes from a construction background, built much of the cafe's furniture and fixtures by hand, including wooden tables, chairs, and concrete counters.
Hand-net roasting is rare in Hiroshima and lets them roast only small quantities at a time, carefully watching the sound, color, and weight of the beans, focusing exclusively on Ethiopian coffee.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen is run entirely on a plant-based, gluten-free basis: all sweets and food are made in-house without wheat, dairy, eggs, refined sugar, artificial coloring, or preservatives, with the wife developing each recipe herself.

positives.text: Entire food and dessert menu is plant-based with no animal products, so no eggs, dairy, or butter are used.
All sweets are gluten-free (wheat-free) and additive-free, made by hand on site.
Plant-based milk options (soy milk, oat milk) are offered for lattes, plus caffeine-free options like decaf coffee, genmai tea, and amazake.

cuisine: Onomichi-style ramen (independent local ramen shop)
neighborhood: Tepuomachi, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori Station)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町8-24 にしたやビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of street address landed in central Naka-ku within the Hiroshima box; consistent with the Hatchobori-area location.
seats_text: 18 seats (10 counter, 8 table). One blog notes the counter feels more like ~10 with a couple of small tables; Tabelog lists 18 total.
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closes when sold out (売り切れ次第終了)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual solo-run counter ramen shop where a smile, a "konnichiwa," and pointing at the menu will get you through; you pay in cash after eating, and no English service should be expected.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses flat wheat noodles (from Inoue Noodle Factory in Onomichi) in a soy-sauce-based broth, both of which contain wheat. There is no indication of any gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature Onomichi ramen is built on a pork-and-chicken broth flavored with scorched soy sauce and topped with rendered pork back fat and chashu (pork). No plant-based options are documented.

bio.background: Akatsuki opened on February 12, 2015 to bring authentic Onomichi-style ramen to central Hiroshima. Real Onomichi is a 1.5-hour-plus trip each way, so the shop exists to let locals and visitors near Hatchobori taste the hometown style without making the journey.
bio.specialty: Onomichi ramen with a deeply colored soy-sauce broth so dark it can be mistaken for Toyama black ramen, yet surprisingly light (assari) on the palate. The broth blends scorched soy sauce with pork and chicken dashi and is kept piping hot under a layer of rendered pork back fat; it is paired with medium-thin, flat wheat noodles made by the Inoue Noodle Factory in Onomichi.
bio.philosophy: Honest, traditional ramen at a kind price — keeping a bowl around 750 yen in an era of 1,000-yen ramen, served the old-fashioned way (no meal-ticket machine; you simply pay after eating).

anecdotes.text: The shop is run single-handedly by its owner, a small counter-and-tables operation where you order, eat, and pay afterward rather than buying a ticket up front.
The Onomichi style here is described as faithfully carrying on the lineage of the famed Shukaen shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in the shared pot and the broth is soy-sauce based, so a shared-equipment, wheat-heavy environment is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth's defining flavor comes from scorched soy sauce (and a dark soy-sauce tare), which is wheat-containing.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is made from pork and chicken stock with rendered pork back fat; not suitable for vegans and pork/chicken fat is present throughout.

caveats: Hours are irregular (不定休) and the shop closes early when sold out, so timing is uncertain. Cash only, no reservations, no card/IC/QR payments. No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and a local food blog.

lead: 広島風お好み焼 大福
name_ja: 広島風お好み焼 大福
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (old-school crispy press-style), teppanyaki
neighborhood: Ekinishi / Osugacho, Minami-ku (west side of Hiroshima Station)
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区大須賀町12-7
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box, near Hiroshima Station west side (Ekinishi).
seats_text: Around 28 seats: counter seating around the teppan griddles plus tatami seating on the 2nd floor. Some older sources describe it as a smaller counter-only shop (about 10 seats), so the intimate teppan counter is the heart of the place.
hours_raw: 18:00-22:00 (older listings note 17:30-22:30); closed Sundays and holidays.
closed_days: Sundays (and holidays). Hours vary between sources, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, old-fashioned local teppan shop where ordering is casual and likely in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or pointing at what others are eating will go a long way.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour, the okonomiyaki includes soba (wheat) or udon noodles, and it is finished with Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The standard okonomiyaki contains pork (meat), egg, and is cooked on a shared griddle, and the shop also serves horumon (offal). The sauce and preparation are not plant-based, and no vegan options are documented.
caveats: Seat count and exact hours differ between listings (Tabelog ~28 seats / 18:00-22:00 vs. older reviews ~10 counter seats / 17:30-22:30). No official website found; no phone reservation requirement confirmed. Confirm hours before visiting.

bio.background: Daifuku is a long-running, old-school okonomiyaki shop tucked into the lively backstreet district on the west side of Hiroshima Station. It has the retro, lived-in feel of a neighborhood teppan joint, with seats gathered around hot iron griddles where the okonomiyaki is cooked right in front of you.
bio.specialty: Its calling card is genuinely old-fashioned, crispy press-style Hiroshima okonomiyaki: ingredients are layered and weighted down with an iron press while cooking, then an egg is spread on the griddle and the stack is flipped onto it and finished with sauce. The classic order is soba-meat-egg, generously seasoned with garlic, kept honest and affordable.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen sticks to a 'mukashi-nagara' (the way it has always been done) approach, prizing the thin, crisp texture that the iron press gives over flashier modern styles.

anecdotes.text: A local food blogger describes the signature soba-meat-egg okonomiyaki at just 500 yen, cooked in the traditional pressed style with a heavy iron weight and a soft (not runny) finished egg, calling it satisfyingly old-fashioned Hiroshima okonomiyaki.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle that also handles wheat batter, wheat noodles, meat, and offal, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with Otafuku-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle is used for pork, egg, and offal, so all surfaces have animal-product contact.

lead: カフェ クラシック
name_ja: カフェ クラシック
cuisine: Cafe / kissaten (coffee shop)
neighborhood: Hondori / Tatemachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通9-21 静嘉棟ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区本通9-21 via GSI; first feature coordinates [132.460007, 34.392555], within the Hiroshima box. Lands on Hondori shopping arcade, central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 17 seats total (6 counter, 11 table).
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 10:00-18:00; Sat, Sun, Holidays 11:00-18:00; Wednesday closed.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run neighborhood kissaten where a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way; staff are warm but an English menu is unlikely.
gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee shop whose signature items are hot cakes/pancakes, toast sets, and sandwiches, all wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for someone needing strictly gluten-free food.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented.
caveats: No documented gluten-free or vegan accommodations; menu is wheat-, egg-, and dairy-based. Owner/origin story beyond the mother-daughter operation and 2017 opening is not documented on sources fetched.

bio.background: A compact, old-fashioned coffee shop on Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from PARCO, opened on Christmas Eve 2017 and run by a mother and daughter together.
bio.specialty: Homemade pudding with a nostalgic, comforting taste, and fluffy hot cakes that are crisp outside and soft inside, served alongside carefully made coffee.
bio.philosophy: A warm, homey kissaten welcoming all generations, from solo coffee drinkers to families with children.

anecdotes.text: Known on Hondori as a coffee shop with many repeat regulars, beloved for its old-fashioned menu and warm atmosphere.
Run as a two-person mother-daughter team since opening on December 24, 2017, with attentive service and a sense of home.

name_ja: アルヒ モリノナカ
cuisine: Cafe / dining bar (projection-mapping themed; curated dishes and sweets)
neighborhood: Hondori, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, near Hatchobori/Tatemachi and the A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通1-5 4F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区本通1-5 via GSI; coordinates [132.462021, 34.393105] fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 58 seats; private rooms available, can accommodate groups/private events up to roughly 50 people.
hours_raw: Mon-Wed, Sun & holidays 11:00-22:00; Fri-Sat & day before holidays 11:00-00:00; Thursday closed. (Lunch 11:00-14:00, cafe 14:00-17:30, dinner from 17:30.)
closed_days: Thursdays (note: official site mentions irregular closures may also occur)
cultural_comfort_note: A modern, casual themed cafe in central Hondori with card and QR payment and online reservations; a few words of Japanese helps but the relaxed, all-ages atmosphere is welcoming to foreign students.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu is advertised. The official site states allergy accommodations are possible if you give advance notice, so a celiac/GF diner would need to call ahead (090-4392-5102) and confirm. Several menu items (onigiri aside, dishes like tile soba/kawara soba and Basque cheesecake) contain wheat, so GF cannot be assumed.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The menu centers on curated meat/fish dishes (e.g. roast beef salad supervised by a Japanese kappo restaurant), Greek yogurt sweets, and cheesecake. No vegan menu is documented. Allergy/dietary accommodation is said to be possible with advance notice, so a vegan would need to inquire directly before visiting.
caveats: Target listing labeled the area as Hatchobori/Tatemachi, but the confirmed address is 本通 (Hondori), one block over in the same central shopping district near the A-Bomb Dome. Listing also tagged it a small atmospheric local cafe; in reality it is a larger (58-seat) themed projection-mapping cafe/dining bar that opened April 2024, hence categorized OMNI rather than MOM_AND_POP. Hours vary by day and irregular closures occur; confirm before visiting.

bio.background: Opened in April 2024, this is billed as Hiroshima's first projection-mapping cafe and dining bar. Set on the fourth floor of a building in the Hondori shopping arcade, the room is wrapped in projected imagery so that guests feel transported into a forest, with seasonal scenes shifting from spring cherry blossoms to ocean views.
bio.specialty: An immersive, themed dining experience pairing projected visuals and sound with food and sweets curated from well-known local establishments.
bio.philosophy: The concept is to create a non-everyday, fairytale 'inside the forest' space engaging all five senses, where the look, taste and temperature of the food are crafted alongside the video and sound for a multisensory escape.

anecdotes.text: The space is described as revealing its forest world the moment you step out of the elevator, with projection mapping turning the room into a forest that can transform into the sea or, in spring, a field of cherry blossoms.

Rather than relying solely on original recipes, the cafe features dishes supervised by established local names, including a kappo restaurant (Nagayama) for items like roast beef salad and onigiri, kawara-soba (tile soba), and a Basque cheesecake from another local maker.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states that allergy accommodations are possible when advance notice is given.

positives.text: Willing to accommodate allergies with prior notice, so calling ahead to discuss dietary needs is encouraged.

cuisine: Cafe / dessert and cake specialty (chiffon cake), with light lunch and dining-bar fare
neighborhood: 中区本通 / 八丁堀・本通 (central Hiroshima, near Hondori arcade and Hiroshima PARCO)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通9-2 ル・フェール広島本通りⅡ 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区本通9-2; within Hiroshima box, lands on Hondori arcade in central Chuo-ku.
seats_text: 56 seats
hours_raw: Sun–Thu 11:30–22:00; Fri, Sat & day before holidays 11:30–00:00
closed_days: No regular closed day documented
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, stylish café in the central Hondori shopping arcade where a basic Japanese greeting is enough; menus are in Japanese, so a translation app helps for dessert and lunch choices.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The house specialty is chiffon cake and other cakes/pastries, which are wheat-flour based, and lunch buffet/main-dish fare is not advertised as gluten-free. Treat as not gluten-friendly unless you confirm directly with staff.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling is documented. The signature chiffon cakes, parfaits and other desserts typically contain eggs and dairy, and the café/dining menu is omnivore-oriented. A vegan visitor would need to ask staff directly, and options are likely very limited.

bio.background: 47 (Quarante Sept) is a café and dessert spot tucked on the second floor of a building along Hiroshima's central Hondori arcade, near Hiroshima PARCO. It is operated as a brand within the Diamond Dining / DD Holdings restaurant group rather than as a solo independent shop.
bio.specialty: The signature item is a house chiffon cake (known as the べべシフォン / bébé chiffon), alongside parfaits, other cakes and a lunch service with main-dish options.
bio.philosophy: The concept evokes a small hideaway palace in the woods, said to be inspired by Marie Antoinette's Petit Trianon; the interior uses antique wooden furniture and tree motifs to create a calm, forest-like retreat where guests can relax.

anecdotes.text: The space is themed as a secret little palace in the forest inspired by Marie Antoinette's Petit Trianon, with tree imagery and antique wooden interiors giving it a softly rustic, woodland feel; it is popular for after-parties such as wedding second-receptions and casual gatherings with friends or coworkers.
A local food feature highlights the café's chiffon cake as a draw, describing it as a treat to enjoy in the relaxed hideaway café just in front of Hiroshima PARCO.

caveats: This is a brand operated within the Diamond Dining / DD Holdings restaurant group, so it is not an independent owner-run shop. No gluten-free or vegan menu is documented; desserts are wheat/egg/dairy based. Allergy handling and cross-contamination practices are not documented, so any GF/vegan traveler should confirm directly with staff.

cuisine: Teppanyaki izakaya — oysters (kaki butter), wagyu koune, Hiroshima okonomiyaki, yakitori, hormone/offal
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around grilled meats, offal (hormone), wagyu koune, oysters (kaki butter), pork-and-egg teppanyaki, and yakitori. Okonomiyaki is made with wheat batter, noodles, egg, pork and seafood. No vegan dishes are documented, and dashi/sauces throughout are fish- and meat-based.
gf_detail: Not suitable for gluten-free diners. Okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter plus wheat noodles (soba/udon), tonpei-yaki is topped with tempura scraps (tenkasu, wheat), and Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce and izakaya seasonings typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区大須賀町10-5
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [132.473145, 34.398235] for 広島県広島市南区大須賀町10-5, within the Hiroshima box; lands in the Ekinishi area just west of Hiroshima Station.
neighborhood: 広島駅西（エキニシ） / 南区 大須賀町
seats_text: Small two-floor izakaya: counter seating on the 1st floor (popular for solo and casual visits) and table seating on the 2nd floor that can be booked privately for roughly 12-18 people. Exact total seat count not published.
hours_raw: 17:00 - 00:00 (per Tabelog); the official site lists 18:00～0:00. Open daily.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local teppanyaki izakaya where some basic Japanese (or a translation app and pointing at the grill) will go a long way, and a foreign student will be welcomed at the counter for a casual solo meal.
caveats: Payment method is inconsistent across sources: the Tabelog listing states cards and electronic money are not accepted (cash only), while the official site lists VISA/JCB/Diners/UC/AMEX/Master as accepted — bring cash to be safe. Opening time differs by source (17:00 on Tabelog vs 18:00 on the official site); closures are irregular, so confirm before visiting. Not appropriate for vegan or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Shonenba is a small two-floor teppanyaki izakaya on the bustling west side of Hiroshima Station (the Ekinishi district), run by an owner-cook who draws on years of experience working the teppan. The ground floor is a counter where solo diners and small groups watch the grilling up close, while the second floor has tables that can be reserved for parties.
bio.origin: 広島駅西（エキニシ）, 南区 大須賀町, Hiroshima
bio.specialty: The house signature is 'Shonenba-yaki' — domestic beef hormone and short rib grilled in a rich, secret sweet-spicy miso sauce. Alongside it are Hiroshima staples done with care: salt-grilled wagyu koune (the prized neck/brisket cut), kaki (oyster) butter, Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with selected noodles, and tonpei-yaki layered with pork belly, scallions and Onomichi-sourced tempura scraps. Yakitori is made one skewer at a time from carefully chosen domestic chicken.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is to gather 'delicious things' and serve standout teppan dishes that suit any occasion, from a quick solo drink to a group gathering.

anecdotes.text: The owner emphasizes using wagyu for the koune cut, saying the marbled fat and firm lean meat give a quality a step above the standard Hiroshima version, and sources the tempura scraps for the tonpei-yaki from Onomichi for richer oil and umami.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki (wheat batter plus soba/udon noodles), tonpei-yaki topped with wheat tempura scraps, and teppan dishes share the same iron griddle, so heavy gluten cross-contact is expected.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce and standard izakaya seasonings used here are typically wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce or soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetables and any non-meat items are cooked on the same teppan as beef, offal, oysters, pork and egg, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

name_ja: CAFE HAYASHIYA（カフェ ハヤシヤ）
cuisine: Café / Japanese sweets — kakigōri (shaved ice) with homemade Setouchi-citrus syrups and matcha parfaits
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Machiya-dōri
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町町屋通504-5
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; landed on Miyajima island, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 26 seats (10 counter, 16 table)
closed_days: Wednesdays; irregular Tuesday closures
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed sweets café in a restored townhouse where pointing at the picture menu of kakigōri and parfaits works fine, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The kakigōri (shaved ice with homemade citrus and amazake-milk syrups) and matcha/azuki components are not inherently wheat-based, but granola toppings on parfaits contain grains and roll cakes/gelato cones are wheat. There is no published allergen or cross-contamination information, so a gluten-free visitor must ask in person and confirm each item.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment and no vegan menu is documented. Many sweets use dairy (milk gelato, amazake milk, cream) and the parfaits/roll cakes likely contain eggs and dairy. A plain citrus-syrup kakigōri made with Setouchi lemon/yuzu/navel could potentially be plant-based, but this is unconfirmed; vegans should ask which syrups and bases avoid dairy.

bio.background: CAFE HAYASHIYA is the sister shop of Yakigaki-no-Hayashi, a grilled-oyster restaurant founded in 1948 that is credited as the originator of grilled-oyster cuisine on Miyajima. The café occupies a traditional townhouse on Machiya-dōri, the island's historic former main street one block back from the busy Omotesandō shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: Kakigōri (shaved ice) topped with over a dozen rotating homemade syrups made from Setouchi citrus, plus organic matcha parfaits
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as a space to convey the culture of the island, set in a sukiya-style townhouse that blends Japanese and Western touches through handmade sweets and drinks made with local Hiroshima and Setouchi ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The owner crafts more than ten homemade kakigōri syrups, including ones built from Setouchi citrus such as shiranui, yuzu and navel orange, plus amazake-milk versions; diners can upgrade to 'Miyajima pure ice' made with water from the primeval forest of Mount Misen for a softer, gentler-cold texture.
Its signature tea's parfait layers three teas — matcha, hōjicha and genmaicha — with granola and house-made azuki bean filling.
The lunch service that once featured oysters from the parent Yakigaki-no-Hayashi is currently not offered, with the café now focused on sweets.

positives.text: Many sweets are built from naturally gluten-light bases such as shaved ice, Setouchi-citrus syrups, matcha and azuki, which can give a gluten-free or dairy-conscious visitor a starting point to ask about.

caveats: No published allergen, gluten-free or vegan information; dietary suitability must be confirmed in person. Lunch (oyster) service is currently suspended; the menu is sweets-focused. Tuesday closures are irregular, so confirm before visiting.

lead: 麺屋台 我馬 紙屋町店
name_ja: 麺屋台 我馬 紙屋町店
cuisine: Hiroshima tonkotsu ramen
neighborhood: Kamiyachō, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima (near the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-6-12
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box for 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-6-12.
seats_text: 36 seats
hours_raw: 11:00～24:00 (L.O. 23:30); closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a public holiday)
closed_days: Tuesdays (open when the Tuesday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, casual ramen counter where ordering is straightforward and a few words of Japanese plus pointing at the menu will get you a great bowl; no special etiquette beyond ordering and slurping.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop; the standard ramen uses wheat noodles and there is no documented gluten-free option. Not suitable for someone requiring strictly gluten-free food.
vegan_detail: The house specialty is tonkotsu (pork-bone) broth simmered from pork heads, and toppings are meat-based. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian option, so this is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Part of the Hiroshima-based ラーメン我馬 (Gaba) chain (13+ locations across Hiroshima and Kobe), so not an independent one-off shop. Tonkotsu broth and wheat noodles make it unsuitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: Gaba (我馬) began in 2000 when its founder, having trained at a renowned tonkotsu shop in Hakata (Fukuoka), opened the original Misasa shop in Hiroshima. The pork-bone ramen caught on quickly and is often credited with helping spark a tonkotsu boom in Hiroshima; the brand has since grown into a well-known local chain with this Kamiyachō branch among its city-center outlets.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (founded 2000, original shop in Misasa, Nishi-ku)
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style tonkotsu ramen, offered as 白うま (shiro-uma, the classic creamy white pork-bone bowl) and 赤うま (aka-uma, a spicier red version).
bio.philosophy: The shop describes its broth as made using only pork heads (豚頭) slow-simmered for a full day and night to extract a rich, aromatic, creamy soup without the fattiness or off-smell of typical bone cuts; the company frames itself as offering not just noodles and broth but the whole experience of space, time, and service.

anecdotes.text: The founder trained at a famous tonkotsu specialist in Hakata before bringing the style to Hiroshima, and the broth is simmered from pork heads alone over a full day to keep it creamy yet clean-tasting.

lead: 宮島かき氷処 幸氷
name_ja: 宮島かき氷処 幸氷
cuisine: Kakigori (shaved ice) specialty stand with savory tempura rice-ball snacks
neighborhood: Miyajima Omotesando shopping street, near Itsukushima Shrine
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free offering. The savory counter items are wheat-battered tempura (bite-sized shrimp tempura and anago/conger-eel tempura rice balls, plus fried oysters), which are not gluten-free. Kakigori syrups are not documented as GF, and there is no information about gluten-free handling. Treat as not suitable for strict gluten-free needs unless you confirm a plain ice-and-fruit option directly with the shop.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The signature kakigori uses locally grown Hatsukaichi strawberries, but several flavors are described as milk-based (e.g. ichigo-milk / strawberry-milk), and the savory items contain seafood (shrimp tempura, anago tempura, fried oysters). A fruit-syrup-only shaved ice with no dairy might be available, but this is not documented, so a vegan would need to ask which ices are made without milk or condensed milk.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町50-1128-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町50-1128-1 returned [132.309982, 34.272606], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the Omotesando arcade / Itsukushima Shrine area.
seats_text: Small takeout-style stand on the Omotesando arcade; ordering is via a front counter / vending machine and items are sold to go. No documented indoor seating.
hours_raw: Specific opening hours are not published on any source fetched; the shop posts current operating days on its Instagram (@sachigoori.miyajima). Closures are irregular.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny grab-and-go shaved-ice stand on the busy Miyajima shopping street where you order at a counter/machine — a few words of Japanese and pointing are plenty, but bring cash since cards are not accepted.
caveats: Cash only. Opening days are irregular and not posted on listing sites, so check the shop's Instagram before visiting. Hours could not be reliably confirmed across sources, so the weekly schedule is left blank rather than guessed.

bio.background: A shaved-ice specialty stand that opened on the Miyajima Omotesando shopping arcade in April 2024, a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine. It was created by the team behind the Hiroshima wagashi (Japanese confectionery) maker Shungetsu Kagura, bringing a sweets-shop sensibility to summer kakigori.
bio.specialty: Fluffy, melt-in-the-mouth shaved ice featuring locally grown Hatsukaichi strawberries, such as the strawberry-milk bowl, alongside bite-sized savory snacks like shrimp-tempura and conger-eel (anago) tempura rice balls and fried oysters for walking the arcade.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Miyajima and Hatsukaichi local produce in a casual, takeaway-friendly treat for shrine visitors.
roles: produced by the Shungetsu Kagura wagashi maker

anecdotes.text: Opened in April 2024 on the Omotesando arcade and is run by the people behind the Hiroshima sweets brand Shungetsu Kagura.
Its strawberry-milk kakigori is built on Hatsukaichi-grown strawberries with notably fluffy ice.

cuisine: Café — hot sandwiches, pasta (carbonara), baked curry, salads, fried chicken, and desserts (Basque cheesecake), with coffee and tea.
neighborhood: Kamiyachō / Hondori, Naka-ku, central Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町2-3-20 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 紙屋町2-3-20 returned [132.456146, 34.393833], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Hondori/Kamiyachō location ~0.4 mi east of the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: Spacious "large-box" café with high ceilings; mix of counter seats (good for solo diners), sofa seats, 2- and 4-person tables, and wall-aligned seating; wide aisles that fit strollers. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 (irregular closures)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed weekly closed day published.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, modern open-plan café right off Hondori where a foreign student can comfortably order by pointing at the menu or photos; a few words of Japanese help but are not essential, and card/QR payment is accepted.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or documented plant-based options. The menu centers on dishes that typically contain animal products — carbonara and pasta (cream, egg, cheese), ham-cheese-and-tomato sandwiches, baked curry with soft-boiled egg and cheese, fried chicken, and dairy-based desserts like Basque cheesecake. A plain salad or coffee/tea may be the only options; vegans would need to ask directly about each item.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering and not safe for celiacs. Core dishes are wheat-based: pasta/carbonara, bread for the hot sandwiches, and curry typically thickened with wheat flour roux; fried chicken (唐揚げ) is usually wheat/potato-starch coated. No allergen or gluten-free information is published. Treat as not gluten-free.

bio.background: J CAFE is a revival of a beloved Hiroshima café brand. The original was born as 'J CAFE 富士見町店' in July 1999 under local company Double O, and through the 2000s the brand grew into one of the city's signature spacious, stylish 'large-box' cafés where people came for both meals and sweets. After the original locations eventually closed, the brand was brought back, reopening as the Kamiyachō branch on January 6, 2026, just off the Hondori shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: Comfortable all-day café fare — ham, cheese and tomato hot sandwiches, pasta such as a creamy carbonara, and a baked curry with cheese and a soft-boiled egg that is described as a hidden signature dish — alongside salads, fried chicken, and desserts like Basque cheesecake.
bio.philosophy: The reborn space is designed to feel 'nostalgic yet contemporary' — bright natural light from big windows, simple exposed-concrete interiors, greenery, sofa seating and even a working disco ball, with wide aisles that welcome solo diners, groups, and families with strollers from lunch through late evening.
roles: operator: Double O Co. (founder/representative Jun Oyama)

anecdotes.text: The 2000s, when J CAFE first thrived, were the heyday of Hiroshima's spacious, fashionable 'large-box cafés,' and the new Kamiyachō branch deliberately revives that mood while feeling modern.
The baked curry with cheese and soft-boiled egg is highlighted as a 'hidden signature' (隠れた名物) of the café.
The brand traces back to J CAFE 富士見町店, opened in July 1999 by local Hiroshima company Double O.

caveats: Hours (11:00–23:00) and the irregular-closure schedule come from a launch feature and listing; seat count and a formal allergen/dietary policy are not published, so GF/vegan travelers should confirm by phone (082-298-0805) or in person. No gluten-free or vegan accommodations are documented anywhere.

cuisine: Traditional Miyajima Japanese seafood — anago-meshi (conger eel rice), oyster don, clam dishes
neighborhood: Miyajima island (Itsukushima), Omachi — near the Omotesando shopping street and Itsukushima Shrine
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町1-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode resolved to Miyajima-cho Omachi 1; coordinates fall within the expected Hiroshima/Miyajima box and land on the island as expected.
seats_text: Around 80 seats (one source lists 95); a fairly roomy converted townhouse with tatami rooms, private rooms, and wheelchair access.
hours_raw: 10:00–17:00 (Tabelog); some listings show 11:00–16:00. Closing days not fixed.
closed_days: No fixed regular holiday (irregular closures); confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly teahouse-style spot in central Miyajima where pointing at the picture menu and a few polite Japanese words will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English allergy discussion may be limited.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is advertised. The menu centers on anago-meshi, oyster don, clams and similar dishes that are simmered or seasoned with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce and tare, and fried items (oyster fry, tempura) use wheat batter. Treat the kitchen as not gluten-free and not equipped for celiac-level needs.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused restaurant — its signature dishes are conger eel rice, oyster bowls and clam dishes, and seasonings rely on fish-based dashi and wheat-soy-sauce. No vegan menu is offered; not suitable for strict vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (10:00–17:00 on Tabelog vs 11:00–16:00 on a travel listing); seat count also varies (~80 vs 95). Closures are irregular. Confirm hours and opening directly, especially off-season. Official site is served over plain HTTP and may trigger browser security warnings.

bio.background: Mizuha occupies the site of a former Edo-period theater teahouse on Miyajima, set inside a renovated old machiya (wooden townhouse) just off the Omotesando approach toward Itsukushima Shrine. The building keeps its period character with exposed beams and pillars, latticed doors and decorative sliding screens, giving a quiet, historic backdrop for a meal away from the busy main street.
bio.specialty: Traditional, handmade Miyajima dishes built around local seafood — anago-meshi (conger eel over rice), kaki-don (oyster rice bowl), fried oyster sets, and seasonal clam preparations.
bio.philosophy: The owners frame the place around the old teahouse spirit: just as people once paused to eat and rest at a teahouse before a play began at the neighboring theater, Mizuha aims to be a calm spot where today's visitors can sit, slow down, and enjoy Miyajima's specialties.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant stands on the ground of a former kabuki/theater playhouse, and the name and concept deliberately echo the teahouses where audiences would eat and rest before performances began.
The kitchen emphasizes traditional handcrafted cooking using Miyajima's signature ingredients — anago, oysters and asari clams.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes (anago-meshi, oyster don, clam dishes) are prepared with traditional Japanese seasonings, which in this style of cooking rely on wheat-based soy sauce and tare; no gluten-free seasoning is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is seafood-centric (eel, oysters, clams) with fish-dashi-based cooking, so plant-only dishes would share equipment and stocks with animal ingredients.

cuisine: 手握りおにぎり専門店（広島米・地元食材使用。煮穴子、瀬戸内海牡蠣のオリーブオイル漬け、塩麹鮭など）
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島）・五重塔のふもと／町家通り
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町408
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町大町408 returned [132.320145, 34.297195], within the Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine and the five-story pagoda.
seats_text: Small shop with an eat-in space of about 10 seats; primarily takeout-focused.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (Tabelog); Miyajima Tourism Association lists 10:30-16:30.
closed_days: No fixed closing day listed (Tabelog shows open daily); the tourism association notes irregular closures (不定休).
cultural_comfort_note: A foreign-language (English) menu is available and the shop is used to tourists, so ordering is easy even with little Japanese.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The documented fillings rely on animal products and fish — simmered conger eel (anago), Seto Inland Sea oysters in olive oil, salted-koji salmon, salmon roe, and bonito-based tuna — so most onigiri are not vegan. Miso soup is also sold. No plant-only or clearly vegan-labeled option is documented; ask in person if any plain or pickled-plum (ume) rice ball can be made without animal ingredients.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. While rice itself is naturally gluten-free, the shop makes no gluten-free claim and several fillings/seasonings (salted koji, soy-sauce-koji, simmered anago, miso soup) typically involve wheat-based soy sauce or wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or allergen separation is documented. A celiac traveler should not assume safety here.

bio.origin: Hiroshima Prefecture — the brand began as a handmade-onigiri kitchen car in the Saijo area before opening this Miyajima shop in autumn 2024.
bio.background: Founder Michiko Yokote previously ran an izakaya in Tokyo together with her husband. After moving to Saijo for her husband's work, she started fresh with a handmade onigiri business, first as a mobile kitchen car and developing some of her menu in collaboration with university students, then opening the Miyajima shop.
bio.specialty: Generous, ingredient-packed handmade rice balls using Hiroshima-grown rice, with the nori served separately so guests wrap it just before eating to keep it crisp. Signature fillings include simmered conger eel (anago) and Seto Inland Sea oysters preserved in olive oil.
bio.philosophy: Yokote says she wants all kinds of people to come, and that it makes her happy when someone tastes an onigiri and smiles; she was drawn to onigiri because the fillings allow for endless variation.

anecdotes.text: The Miyajima shop occupies a restored historic building near the base of the five-story pagoda that formerly housed the old 'Yamamoto Torakichi Shoten,' now marked by purple noren curtains and flags.
Rice is cooked to be fluffy and the nori is handed over separately so it stays crisp until the moment you eat; the shop also sells miso soup, beer, and sake alongside the onigiri.
The brand started as a handmade-onigiri kitchen car, with the founder choosing carefully selected ingredients and developing experimental fillings together with Hiroshima University students.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Fillings and accompaniments such as salted-koji salmon, soy-sauce-koji, simmered conger eel, and miso soup typically use standard wheat-based Japanese seasonings; the shop publishes no gluten-free or wheat-free claim.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Onigiri are prepared in a small shared workspace and the menu centers on fish and animal fillings (anago, oysters, salmon, salmon roe, tuna), so cross-contact for any plain rice ball is likely; no vegan separation is documented.

positives.text: An English/foreign-language menu is available, which makes it easier for travelers to ask about ingredients.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog 10:00-17:00 vs. Miyajima Tourism Association 10:30-16:30); closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. Seat count of ~10 and credit-card acceptance are from the Miyajima Tourism Association listing. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan handling is documented — diners with celiac disease or a vegan diet should ask directly or treat this as unsuitable.

cuisine: 炭火焼きの穴子を使ったミニあなご丼・うどん・甘味を出す、宮島の小さなお茶処
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島）・大願寺〜大聖院エリア（厳島神社の奥側）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町東大西町71
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町東大西町71 returned [132.309982, 34.272606], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box, near the Daiganji/Daisho-in temple path just inland of Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: About 28 seats — 2 table sections and 2 tatami (zashiki) areas; a small tea house.
hours_raw: 11:00–16:00; closed Tuesdays (per Miyajima Tourism Association listing). Tabelog notes irregular closures (不定休), so hours can vary seasonally.
closed_days: Tuesdays (with occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual island tea house where a guidebook or translation app helps, since menus and service are mostly in Japanese and the staff may speak little English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The mini anago bowl is glazed with a soy-based tare (Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), the udon noodles are wheat, and sweets like dango and zenzai are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature dish is charcoal-grilled anago (conger eel), and the udon is typically served in a fish-dashi broth. No vegan-specific options are documented; even the sweets and tea are not verified as animal-free in preparation.
caveats: The Tabelog listing name is ふじの茶屋 里の店. Hours and closed days are taken from the Miyajima Tourism Association member listing (11:00–16:00, closed Tuesdays); Tabelog marks closures as irregular, so confirm before a long detour. No official website was found. Payment is assumed cash-based as no card acceptance is documented — confirm on arrival.

bio.background: A small, old-fashioned tea house tucked along the quiet path between Daiganji and Daisho-in temples on Miyajima, a little inland from the bustle of the main approach to Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: A mini anago (conger eel) rice bowl built around eel grilled over charcoal, alongside simple udon and traditional Japanese sweets such as zenzai (sweet red-bean soup) and seasonal matcha shaved ice.
bio.philosophy: The eel is grilled over charcoal with care — described in their own listing as あなごはこだわりの炭火で焼いております ('our conger eel is grilled over charcoal with particular care').

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe a cozy, retro Showa-era atmosphere — a quiet spot to rest with a small anago bowl or a sweet between temple visits.
The shop's own listing emphasizes charcoal-grilled anago and seasonal sweets like zenzai and matcha shaved ice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The anago bowl is finished with a soy-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the dish is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Udon is typically served in fish dashi and the headline dish is grilled eel, so vegan diners cannot rely on the kitchen being free of fish/animal contact.

cuisine: 昭和レトロな喫茶店（コーヒー・ホットケーキ）／個人経営
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島）・表参道／宮島桟橋エリア
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町858
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.322113, 34.300591] for 広島県廿日市市宮島町858; within Hiroshima box and on Miyajima island near the ferry terminal.
seats_text: 22 seats total — three 4-seat tables and one 10-seat table.
hours_raw: 営業時間 不定（要問い合わせ）／定休日 水曜日
closed_days: Wednesdays (定休日 水曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, old-school family-run coffee shop with no English menu and irregular hours, so a foreign student is best off coming with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend and pointing at what they want.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. Core menu is hotcakes and toast (wheat) plus shrimp pilaf; nothing indicates gluten-free preparation or awareness.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature items — hotcakes and toast — use eggs, dairy and butter, and other dishes include shrimp pilaf and zenzai; no documented plant-based options.
caveats: Hours are listed as irregular (不定) — call ahead before visiting. Closed Wednesdays. All-seating smoking permitted. Payment method not explicitly published; treated as cash to be safe.

bio.background: The shop began life as 'Ebisuya Ryokan,' a small inn, before being reborn as a coffee shop in 1975 under proprietor Yuko Uchiyama. It sits right by the Miyajima ferry terminal, a few steps from the island's main approach toward Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Showa-era kissaten classics — hand-poured coffee, fluffy hotcakes, shrimp pilaf, and a coffee-and-momiji-manju set that nods to the island's famous maple-leaf cakes.

anecdotes.text: A warm, retro refuge for travelers waiting on a ferry, the cafe carries its old-inn lineage into a relaxed coffee-shop atmosphere.

cuisine: 純喫茶（コーヒー専門の喫茶店）— ネルドリップ・サイフォンで淹れる本格珈琲と軽食
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島）・町家通り／表参道エリア
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町853-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.322113, 34.300591] for 廿日市市宮島町853-2. Within the Hiroshima box and located on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: 16 seats, fully non-smoking.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, welcoming vintage coffee house that offers a foreign-language menu and has barrier-free access, so a foreign student can order comfortably even with little Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house, not a gluten-free destination. Plain brewed coffee (nel drip / siphon) and tea are naturally gluten-free, but the café also serves light snacks and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or cross-contamination control. A celiac traveler should stick to black coffee/tea and ask directly about any food item.
vegan_detail: Black coffee and tea are vegan, but milk-based drinks and most kissaten snacks/sweets typically contain dairy or egg, and no vegan menu is documented. A vegan visitor can order black coffee safely but should ask about milk, butter, and egg in any food before ordering.
caveats: Cash only (no credit cards). Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays. No Tabelog or official website could be confirmed; details are drawn from the Miyajima Tourism Association member listing. The same building/address (宮島町853-2) has also been listed as "昭和カフェ 松大," which appears to be a predecessor or related operation.

bio.background: A small vintage coffee house on Miyajima that keeps the spirit of a Showa-era junkissa alive, just off the temple-town lanes near Itsukushima Shrine. It serves authentic coffee brewed slowly by nel (flannel) drip and siphon, with takeout also available for visitors wandering the island.
bio.specialty: Hand-brewed coffee — nel drip and siphon — in a nostalgic Showa atmosphere.
bio.philosophy: Carefully brewed, traditional coffee served in an unhurried, retro setting.

anecdotes.text: The café is described as a junkissa that retains a Showa-era feel, where you can enjoy authentic coffee brewed by nel drip or siphon, with takeout available.

positives.text: A foreign-language menu is available and the café has barrier-free access.

name_ja: お好み焼き 桜まる
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with Miyajima oysters and seafood
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), Omotesando shopping street near Itsukushima Shrine
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町510-2
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町510-2; coordinates [132.309982, 34.272606] fall within the Hiroshima box and land on Miyajima island as expected.
seats_text: About 26 seats; counter seating plus a small tatami room.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (official site notes 11:00-15:00 L.O., subject to change); closed Wednesdays, with some Fridays also closed.
closed_days: Wednesdays (some Fridays also closed)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual island okonomiyaki counter where a friendly greeting and pointing at the photo menu will get you by, though English support is limited so a few Japanese words help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and layered with wheat soba/yakisoba noodles, and the okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on oysters, mixed seafood (clams, shrimp, squid, scallops), pork/beef and egg in the okonomiyaki, plus bonito/fish-based sauces. No vegetable-only or vegan option is listed.
caveats: Hours are subject to change and some Fridays close in addition to the regular Wednesday closure, so confirm before a long trip out to the island. The official site lists last order at 15:00 while listing hours run to 16:00.

bio.background: A small, independent okonomiyaki shop on Miyajima island, set along the route toward Itsukushima Shrine. It serves Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki paired with the island's famous oysters in an unpretentious local-eatery setting of a counter and a small tatami room.
bio.specialty: Oyster okonomiyaki -- a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki topped with Miyajima oysters, the shop's signature and most popular dish, alongside seafood okonomiyaki and foil-grilled oysters and scallops.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around sharing Hiroshima's everyday home-style food with visitors, inviting travelers to enjoy okonomiyaki and oysters together as part of the pleasure of the trip.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes itself with the sentiment that beautiful scenery and delicious food are the joys of travel, and invites guests to enjoy Hiroshima's popular everyday tastes -- okonomiyaki and oysters -- together.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle and built from wheat batter and wheat noodles, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The griddle is shared with oysters, seafood, pork/beef and egg; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

name_ja: 宮島 Cuillere（キュイエール）
cuisine: French bistro using Hiroshima/Setouchi ingredients — grilled oysters, eel (anago) confit, oyster bourguignon, plus homemade-bread sandwiches and house-made sweets
vegan_detail: The restaurant's official site states a vegan menu is available alongside the main French menu ("ビーガンメニュー取り揃えております"). However, no specific vegan dishes are itemized publicly and the broader menu is meat- and seafood-forward (charcuterie, oysters, anago/eel confit, mussels). Vegan diners should confirm dishes and preparation in advance, ideally when reserving.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or protocols are documented. This is a French bistro whose signatures lean on wheat — homemade bread and sandwiches, pasta, beignets/pastries and French desserts. Gluten-free travelers have no confirmed safe path here and would need to discuss options directly with the kitchen.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町681-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to [132.323181, 34.30014]; within the Hiroshima box and correctly on Miyajima island near the Omotesando shopping street.
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島）・表参道商店街エリア（宮島桟橋から徒歩約7分）
seats_text: About 22 seats; small intimate French bistro, no private rooms.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:00) / Dinner 18:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:00); irregular closures (不定休)
closed_days: Irregular (不定休) — no fixed weekly closing day; confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small chef-run French bistro on Miyajima's main shopping street that sees plenty of tourists; staff are used to visitors, but for a vegan meal or any dietary need it helps to phone or use the website reservation form ahead so the kitchen can prepare.
caveats: Credit cards are not accepted (electronic money is); bring cash or e-money. Closing days are irregular, so check the official site before going. A vegan menu is advertised but not detailed publicly — confirm specifics in advance. Not suitable for gluten-free diners without prior arrangement.

bio.background: A French bistro opened on Miyajima in spring 2023, set in the island's traditional Omotesando merchant district about a seven-minute walk from the ferry pier and within the historic streetscape near Itsukushima Shrine.
bio.specialty: Casual but authentic French cooking built around carefully selected local ingredients — grilled oysters with herb butter, eel (anago) confit, and oyster bourguignon, alongside homemade-bread sandwiches, beignets, and French pastries and sweets.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to seasonal Hiroshima and Setouchi ingredients from 'the mountains and the sea,' prepared with meticulous care by a French-trained chef and served in an unhurried atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen turns Hiroshima's famous oysters into French preparations such as oyster bourguignon and grilled oysters with herb butter, and the celebrated local anago (sea eel) into a French-style confit.
Drinks lean local too, with Hiroshima craft beers, regional sake, and sommelier-selected wines offered to pair with the food.

positives.text: The restaurant advertises a dedicated vegan menu in addition to its main French menu, so plant-based diners have at least some accommodation if they confirm details when reserving.

lead: ひろしま きんちゃい屋
name_ja: ひろしま きんちゃい屋
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き
neighborhood: 中区銀山町（流川・薬研堀エリア）

closed_days: 水曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny six-seat counter run by a friendly, chatty owner where you watch your okonomiyaki cook in front of you; little to no English is documented, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house. The okonomiyaki crepe batter is wheat flour, the dish is layered with wheat-based yakisoba/udon noodles, and standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation, so it is not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: Dishes center on egg, pork, squid tempura, bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings, and wheat-soy-based sauce. The signature "名月そば" includes squid tempura and a fried egg. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: Closing time is listed as 02:00 on Tabelog while one early blog noted a 17:00 opening; treat the late-night 18:00–02:00 schedule as the working hours but confirm on arrival. Reservation and payment details are based on Tabelog's listing.

bio.background: A small okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop that opened on August 23, 2023, tucked just off the street near Hiroshima's Butsudan-dori in the lively Nagarekawa-Yagenbori district. The whole place is a single counter of six seats, so every guest sits right at the griddle.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, with a house dish called the "名月そば" (meigetsu soba) layered with squid tempura, seaweed, and an egg cooked firm for a mellow finish.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the owner as a genuinely friendly, easygoing guy who keeps up good conversation while he cooks, making the tiny counter feel welcoming.

neighborhood: Nakamachi, Naka Ward — central Hiroshima, within the Hondori / Peace Park area, about 5 min walk from Fukuromachi tram stop

geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区中町7-16 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near the building location.
seats_text: 28 seats total — 6 at the counter and 22 on raised tatami seating.
hours_raw: 17:30–23:00 (last order 22:00). Sources differ on closures: one listing shows closed Tuesday and Wednesday, while the official page states irregular holidays (不定休) with weekend/holiday time-slot seating.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休); some listings indicate Tuesday and Wednesday closed. Confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, homey local izakaya where ordering and conversation will mostly be in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes that typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce and sauces, and signature items such as fried oysters (kaki fry) are breaded with wheat, so this is not a suitable option for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya built around oysters and anago (conger eel), with wagyu beef and fish dishes; no vegan menu is offered and the core offerings are animal-based, so it is not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary by source (official page lists irregular holidays; another listing shows Tuesday/Wednesday closed). Weekend and holiday seating may be on timed slots. Confirm hours and reserve ahead for busy periods.

bio.background: A homey, independently run Seto Inland Sea cuisine spot in central Hiroshima where the focus is on letting guests enjoy the region's signature flavors in one place.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima brand oysters marketed as "Kaki Komachi" (large 150g-plus oysters served raw, grilled, or fried) and white-grilled (shirayaki) locally caught anago (conger eel), paired with an extensive regional sake list.
bio.philosophy: An at-home, welcoming atmosphere centered on showcasing fresh Hiroshima oysters and Setouchi seafood.

anecdotes.text: The house highlights premium "Kaki Komachi" Hiroshima brand oysters, large at 150g or more, which can be ordered raw, grilled, or fried.
A signature dish is the white-grilled (shirayaki) anago caught in local waters, served alongside a deep regional sake selection.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀（八丁堀・流川エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀4-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区薬研堀4-13 returned [132.465393, 34.390884], within the Hiroshima validity box. The registered address is 薬研堀, which sits inside the Hatchobori/Nagarekawa nightlife district adjacent to Ebisu-cho.
seats_text: 10 seats, counter only
hours_raw: 月・火・水・木・金・土・祝日・祝前日 18:00-02:00（L.O. 01:30）／日・祝後日 定休 ※年始休（出典により18:00-翌3:00・無休とする情報もあり）
closed_days: 日曜日、祝日の翌日（年始休）。※一部情報源では「無休」とされており要確認。
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter where you sit elbow-to-elbow at the griddle and the friendly owner chats as he cooks, so a little Japanese (or willingness to gesture and smile) makes the late-night visit far more enjoyable.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe layered with wheat noodles, and savory dishes are seasoned with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce and Worcestershire-style sauce. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of separate preparation, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg, pork, Wagyu offal, oysters, and the signature uni (sea urchin) and spinach dish, and the batter and broth use bonito/fish dashi. There are no documented vegan options, so this spot is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

bio.background: A small counter-only okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop in central Hiroshima's lively Yakkenbori area, run for well over a decade by an owner-cook known for his warm, energetic hospitality. With only ten seats facing the griddle, it has the intimate feel of a neighborhood regulars' haunt that also welcomes curious visitors late into the night.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki with thin noodles tossed in a house broth and griddled slowly until the outside is crisp and the inside stays moist, alongside the locally loved uni-horen (sea urchin with a whole bundle of spinach).
bio.philosophy: A commitment to Hiroshima-sourced ingredients (kodawari Hiroshima-san) and cooking everything to order right in front of each guest.

anecdotes.text: Now in its fourteenth year, the shop is described as a popular long-running local institution whose draw is as much the owner's lively, conversational service at the counter as the food itself.

The signature okonomiyaki uses thin noodles coated in a special house broth and griddled for over ten minutes for a crisp-outside, tender-inside finish, and the well-known uni-horen pairs umami-rich sea urchin with a whole bundle of spinach.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made on a shared teppan griddle from a wheat-flour batter layered with wheat noodles; no gluten-free preparation is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and okonomi sauce, both of which normally contain wheat. No wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a single shared griddle alongside pork, Wagyu offal, oysters, sea urchin, and egg, so vegan separation is not possible.

hours_raw: Tue-Sat 18:00-03:00; Sun & holidays 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Monday
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, late-night neighborhood okonomiyaki and teppanyaki counter where ordering in basic Japanese will go most smoothly; a friendly atmosphere but likely little English support.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and topped with wheat-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, and the menu includes noodles (soba), so the signature dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: A teppanyaki and okonomiyaki house: dishes use egg, pork, seafood, dashi and wheat-soy sauces. Even simpler grilled-vegetable teppanyaki items typically share the griddle and sauces with animal products. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: The target listed the area as 胡町; the current operating address is 流川町2-18 (a short walk from Ojimachi/Hatchobori), with the restaurant having relocated from its earlier 18-year location near Ebisu-dori. An older Tabelog listing (34000170) and an ekiten listing at 胡町2-17 reflect the pre-relocation venue. Opens evenings only and runs late.

bio.background: A family-run, late-night okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot in central Hiroshima. After about 18 years serving regulars near Ebisu-dori, the shop relocated roughly eight years ago to its current fifth-floor home in the Nagarekawa/Hatchobori entertainment district, carrying its longtime following with it.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (layered batter with cabbage and noodles) alongside teppanyaki griddle dishes; grilled scallions/onions and a well-regarded omurice are house favorites.

anecdotes.text: Spent about 18 years at its original location near Ebisu-dori before moving to the current spot around eight years ago, keeping the same intimate counter feel.
The thinly sliced grilled onions/scallions and the omurice are singled out as standout, popular plates from the teppanyaki side of the menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and the menu includes soba noodles, with shared griddle preparation, so cross-contact with gluten is inherent to the cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce, which is wheat-soy based; no wheat-free sauce alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Single shared teppan griddle cooks pork, egg, seafood and batter, so any vegetable items would share surfaces and fats with animal products.

lead: 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ 離れ
name_ja: 廣島牡蠣 みやまえ 離れ
cuisine: Seto Inland Sea cuisine / oyster izakaya (Hiroshima brand oysters, conger eel, teppanyaki, local sake)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a Seto Inland Sea izakaya where most dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce, miso, dashi, batter and fried items. Fried dishes (karaage, ham cutlet), the finishing udon, and standard izakaya seasonings all contain wheat. No allergen menu is published; a traveler needing strict GF would need to call ahead (082-569-6711), but the cuisine is not structured for gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The restaurant centers on oysters, conger eel (anago), small sardines, seafood sashimi and A5 beef, and uses fish dashi (e.g. dashi-rolled egg) widely. A few vegetable items appear (chilled tomato, moro-kyu cucumber with miso, bagna cauda, potato salad) but these typically contain miso, mayonnaise, anchovy or other animal products, and no dedicated vegan dishes are advertised.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区小町3-6 メイゾン小町1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区小町3-6 via GSI; coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box and match central Nakaku (Komachi / Jizō-dōri), near Chūden-mae tram stop and within the Peace Park area.
neighborhood: 中区小町・じぞう通り (near Chūden-mae tram stop, within the Peace Park area)
seats_text: 26 seats total; private rooms available, banquets up to 26 people
hours_raw: 月、火、木～日、祝日、祝前日 17:00～23:00（料理L.O. 22:00、ドリンクL.O. 22:00）。週末・連休中は時間制(2時間)となる場合あり。定休日：水曜日
closed_days: Wednesday (per official site and current listings; an older Tabelog listing showed Tuesday+Wednesday — official site is treated as authoritative)
cultural_comfort_note: A small local oyster izakaya with no documented English support and an evening-only, sit-and-order seafood-and-sake format, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some Japanese or a translation app and a willingness to dine izakaya-style.

bio.background: A small, intimate evening izakaya in the Komachi / Jizō-dōri quarter of central Hiroshima, opened in June 2023 as the 'hanare' (annex) of the Miyamae oyster house. With just 26 seats and private rooms, it is built around the flavors of the Seto Inland Sea.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima brand oysters 'Kaki Komachi' (served raw and grilled), Hiroshima conger eel served white-grilled (shiroyaki), small sardines, teppanyaki, and A5 Japanese beef, paired with a deep lineup of local Hiroshima sake (around 30 varieties).
bio.philosophy: To be an intimate place where guests can savor Hiroshima's regional specialties of the Seto Inland Sea alongside its local sake, with fresh brand oysters brought in daily.

anecdotes.text: The house describes itself as an intimate spot for Hiroshima specialties, with brand oysters delivered fresh and roughly 30 kinds of local sake on offer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or gluten information is published; the menu includes fried items and a finishing udon course, so shared fryer/wheat cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard izakaya seasoning with soy sauce and miso is used throughout (e.g. moro-kyu with miso, plum-wasabi), and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood (oysters, conger eel, sardines, sashimi) and fish dashi are central to the kitchen; dashi-rolled egg explicitly uses fish stock, so vegetable dishes are prepared in a seafood-heavy kitchen.

lead: 中華そば まる 舟入店
name_ja: 中華そば まる 舟入店
cuisine: 広島ラーメン・中華そば (Hiroshima-style chuka soba)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses wheat-based straight noodles and a tonkotsu-shoyu broth; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The broth is pork-bone (tonkotsu) plus soy sauce, and dishes are topped with chashu (pork). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区舟入中町3-6 パートナー広島1階
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島市中区舟入中町3-6 via GSI; within the Hiroshima box, matching the Funairi neighborhood west of Peace Park.
neighborhood: 中区舟入中町 (Funairi, west of Peace Memorial Park)
seats_text: 12 seats total (10 counter, 2 tables); small shop, single diners welcome.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 18:00-23:00; closed Wednesdays. A local listing shows lunch starting as early as 11:00.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only counter ramen shop where ordering is simple but staff likely speak little English, so a few Japanese words for your order help.

bio.background: Chuka Soba Maru is a long-loved Hiroshima ramen shop. The Funairi location was renovated and reopened in February 2020 under new ownership, which carried on the recipe handed down from the previous proprietor so longtime locals can keep tasting the flavor they grew up with.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style chuka soba with a tonkotsu-shoyu broth balancing rich pork-bone umami and the depth of soy sauce, served with medium-thin straight noodles, crisp bean sprouts, green onions, and tender chashu.
bio.philosophy: Preserving and passing on a beloved local taste for families and customers who have eaten it since childhood.

anecdotes.text: The shop reopened at its renovated Funairi location in February 2020, keeping the flavor inherited from the previous owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is centered on wheat ramen noodles cooked in shared water; high gluten exposure.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is tonkotsu-shoyu (pork bone and soy sauce); standard soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based and dishes are topped with chashu pork; no plant-based preparations documented.

cuisine: Western-style (yoshoku) and tonkatsu, butcher-shop-run; sukiyaki, beef cutlet, steak
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori, Naka-ku)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀14-12
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区八丁堀14-12 returned [132.463165, 34.394508], within the Hiroshima box; about a 2-3 minute walk from the Hatchobori tram stop, within the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Park area.
seats_text: Large multi-floor restaurant: roughly 32 table seats and 24 tatami seats plus around 150 seats each on the 2nd and 3rd floors (about 356 total). Seating spread across multiple floors; can be crowded at lunch.
