hours_raw: 11:00-20:45 (1F); 11:00-21:00 (2F). Closed Wednesdays and the second Tuesday of each month.
closed_days: Wednesdays and the second Tuesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual old-school eatery where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; menus are in Japanese and staff may have limited English, but the famous bargain tonkatsu is easy to point to and order.
vegan_detail: This is a butcher-shop-run meat restaurant; the core menu is pork cutlets, beef cutlets, steak, sukiyaki and other meat dishes. The frying oil is house-rendered lard and beef tallow (animal fat), and the signature demi-glace sauce is simmered from meat trimmings and bones, so even side components are animal-based. There are no documented vegan options.
gf_detail: The signature dishes are breaded, deep-fried cutlets (tonkatsu, beef cutlet), which use wheat flour and panko breading, so they are not gluten-free. The demi-glace/Western sauces and likely the sukiyaki seasoning involve wheat-based ingredients. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
caveats: Closed days are irregular (Wednesdays plus the second Tuesday of each month), so confirm before visiting. Floors keep slightly different closing times. The famous 380-yen service tonkatsu is limited and very popular (up to ~200 servings a day) and can sell out. No vegan or gluten-free accommodations are documented; everything is meat- and wheat-based.

bio.background: A longstanding Western-style eatery in central Hiroshima's Hatchobori district, founded in 1956 (Showa 31) and run as an annex to the family's own butcher shop, which sits in the same building with its own entrance. Having direct access to the meat counter has been the heart of the operation for nearly seventy years.
bio.origin: Hiroshima, Hatchobori. The business grew out of the family meat shop, with the restaurant pairing fresh-cut meat with classic Japanese-Western home cooking.
bio.specialty: The legendary 'service tonkatsu' - a large, freshly fried pork cutlet served with rice, boiled cabbage and potato salad for a famously low price (around 350-380 yen). Beyond that, the kitchen turns out beef cutlets, hamburg steak, omelets, Kansai-style tableside sukiyaki and substantial steaks.
bio.philosophy: The service tonkatsu was created by an earlier generation out of a wish to let students and people without much money eat a big plate of good meat cheaply, and that generous spirit still defines the place. Nothing from the butcher shop goes to waste: the demi-glace sauce is simmered from meat trimmings, bones and vegetable ends, and the frying fat is house-rendered lard and beef tallow made from the shop's own off-cuts.

anecdotes.text: The bargain service tonkatsu is so popular that the kitchen prepares it in advance, and on busy days they sell as many as 200 plates.
The demi-glace sauce is made entirely from the butcher shop's leftover meat scraps, bones and vegetable ends, born from the founder's refusal to waste good ingredients.
Across two retro floors, vintage signage and a settled old-shop atmosphere reflect the restaurant's long history.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on breaded, deep-fried cutlets, so the shared fryer and prep surfaces carry a high risk of wheat/gluten cross-contact for fried items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific allergen or soy-sauce ingredient disclosure was found; however the menu includes sukiyaki and Western sauces that conventionally use wheat-containing soy sauce and wheat-based demi-glace.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Frying oil is house-rendered lard and beef tallow (animal fat), and the demi-glace is made from meat trimmings and bones, so even non-meat-looking components are animal-derived.

cuisine: とんかつ (tonkatsu / breaded pork cutlets)
neighborhood: 南区金屋町（稲荷町電停すぐ・広島駅周辺）
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区金屋町2-8
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市南区金屋町2-8 returned [132.472488, 34.392124], within the Hiroshima box; lands near Kanaya-cho / Inari-cho tram stop in Minami-ku.
seats_text: 48 seats total: roughly a 22-seat counter plus two 4-person tables and a tatami room for up to about 16 people.
closed_days: 日曜・祝日・祝前日・祝後日 (Sundays, public holidays, and the days immediately before/after holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A long-running neighborhood tonkatsu shop where ordering is simple (point to a set meal) but staff likely speak little English, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.
gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu specialist: cutlets are coated in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the core dishes contain wheat gluten and are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on breaded, deep-fried pork cutlets fried in 100% lard (animal fat), served with rice and miso soup. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
caveats: Tabelog lists 48 seats, which exceeds the typical under-30 threshold for a small mom-and-pop venue; it is nonetheless an independent, family/owner-run local institution. No official website was found. Closed not only Sundays/holidays but also the days before and after holidays, so check the calendar before visiting.

bio.background: Tonki is a tonkatsu specialist in the Kanaya-cho area of Minami-ku, a short walk from the Inari-cho tram stop and within reach of Hiroshima Station. It opened in 1981 and has become enough of a fixture that locals treat the name Tonki as shorthand for tonkatsu in Hiroshima. A second shop in the Nishi-ku commercial center operates as a noren-wake (name-sharing) offshoot; only these two carry the Tonki name.
bio.specialty: Tonkatsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlets) — loin (rosu) and fillet (hire) cutlet set meals, plus a value katsu-don rice bowl.
bio.philosophy: The owner describes aiming to make food that is delicious to themselves and that anyone would find delicious: "自分が食べておいしいもの。誰が食べてもおいしいと思ってもらえるようなものを目指しています".

anecdotes.text: The kitchen fries with four separate oil pots, rotating them and resting the oil so the temperature never drops, and avoids crowding cutlets into a single pot — all to keep the crust crisp.
Cutlets are fried in 100% lard to concentrate umami, the rice is Hiroshima-grown Koshihikari, and the first refill of rice and miso soup is free.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are coated in wheat flour and panko and deep-fried, so the frying oil and breading station carry wheat; there is no gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Frying is done in 100% lard (pork fat), so even non-meat items cooked here would contact animal fat.

cuisine: Curry / music café (handmade oil-free vegetable and fruit curry, classical-music kissaten)
vegan_detail: A health-conscious curry café whose curry is made without oil, built on lots of vegetables, fruit and spices. There is a tofu curry and a vegetable-forward style that suits vegetarians, but the menu also includes beef, egg and seafood ("海の幸") curries, so it is not a vegan establishment. The curry roux is not stated to be free of dairy or other animal products, so a strict vegan should confirm specific dishes directly. No explicit vegan labeling was found.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The dishes are curries served as a café/curry-house; curry roux conventionally contains wheat flour, and there is no statement that any dish is gluten-free. Travelers requiring gluten-free meals should not rely on this spot without confirming, and should assume wheat is present.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-4-6 アウルスタイル紙屋町ビル 3F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-banchi address (紙屋町1-4-6) via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Kamiyacho, close to Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区紙屋町（紙屋町東駅 徒歩約2分・平和記念公園至近）
seats_text: 40 seats
closed_days: Wednesdays; also closed during Obon and New Year holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, grown-up classical-music café where curry is the draw; staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help, but the calm atmosphere makes it easy to linger as a solo diner.
caveats: Cash only (no credit cards, though QR-code payment is accepted). Curry roux should be assumed to contain wheat; no gluten-free or certified-vegan options are documented, so allergy-sensitive diners must confirm specific dishes in person.

bio.background: A long-running curry music café in central Hiroshima's Kamiyacho district, a short walk from Peace Memorial Park. It pairs handmade curry with classical music: grand piano, cello and violin sit in the room, classical BGM plays, and on some days there are live lunchtime performances and occasional salon concerts by professional musicians.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated creed is playful and humble: "The most delicious curry is the one made at home, so we make the second-most delicious curry." The curry is made without oil, built from plenty of vegetables, fruit and spices for a light, health-conscious result.
bio.specialty: Oil-free handmade curry in many varieties, including a tofu curry and fruit curry, served in a refined, salon-like café setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop's tagline is that home cooking is the best curry, so they aim to make "the second-most delicious curry," prepared daily without oil from vegetables, fruit and spices.
The interior holds a grand piano, cello and violin, with classical background music and, on some days, live piano and violin performances during lunch.
Staff confirm the curry roux is made without fat/oil, giving it a fragrant, non-greasy character that appeals to health-conscious diners.

positives.text: Curry is made without oil from vegetables, fruit and spices, and a tofu curry is offered, making the menu friendlier to lighter and vegetable-forward eating.

cuisine: 洋食 (yoshoku) — rice gratin / doria specialist
neighborhood: 中区立町（中の棚商店街・本通至近、立町電停近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町5-7 Gハウス 3F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box (Tatemachi area, near the shopping arcades). Building is on the 3rd floor.
seats_text: 46 seats total (6 counter, 40 table)
hours_raw: Mon & Thu: 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00), lunch only. Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun: 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00) and 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:45). Dinner service is closed on Mondays and Thursdays.
closed_days: Open daily for lunch; dinner service is closed on Mondays and Thursdays. No full weekly closing day documented.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, long-loved local lunch spot where a friendly greeting and pointing at the picture menu will get you by; little to no English is documented, so basic Japanese helps.
gf_detail: This is a rice gratin (doria) specialist whose signature dishes are built on a wheat-flour béchamel sauce, so the core menu is not gluten-free. Cream croquettes and curry roux also typically contain wheat. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The gratins are dairy-heavy (béchamel sauce, cheese) and the signature varieties feature chicken, shrimp, clams, ham, squid, white fish, or egg. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Founder/representative name appears in Japanese as 吉川 裕仁; romanization is approximate. Dinner is unavailable Mondays and Thursdays — verify on the day you visit.

bio.background: Founded in 1975 in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district, Resort is a rice gratin specialist that has guarded the same recipe for roughly half a century. Tucked on the third floor of a building near the Nakanotana arcade and a short walk from the Hondori shopping street, it became a fixture for local students and office workers and is now enjoyed by three generations of the same families.
bio.philosophy: Preserve a single, comforting flavor without change. The béchamel sauce has used the same recipe since 1975 — 'a nostalgic, remembered taste' that the kitchen layers with rice and cheese and bakes in a custom lidless oven so each gratin arrives piping hot.
bio.specialty: Rice gratin (doria) baked in a custom lidless oven, served with the house onion salad. Signature varieties carry European names: Monaco (ketchup rice + chicken), Oscar (butter rice + shrimp), André (curry rice + clams), Versailles (butter rice + ham and mushrooms), and Resort (butter rice + squid, white fish, boiled egg). Shrimp cream croquettes and house spiced curry round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The béchamel sauce recipe has remained unchanged since the 1975 founding, and the gratin is baked in a specially commissioned lidless oven so it reaches the table bubbling hot.
Once especially popular with students, the rice gratin is now loved across generations — from the original customers to their children and grandchildren.

lead: インド料理ラソイ 本通り店
name_ja: インド料理ラソイ 本通り店
cuisine: インド料理・カレー（Indian curry）
neighborhood: 中区本通（本通商店街・本通駅 徒歩3分）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-18 4F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区本通8-18 returned [132.458542, 34.392769], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in the Hondori shopping arcade area in central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 30 seats
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), Dinner 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); open year-round (年中無休)
closed_days: None (open year-round / 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual modern Indian curry house in the central Hondori arcade where you can choose your spice level and ask staff about which curries are vegetarian; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu works fine, and English may be limited.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Rice-based curry plates are naturally wheat-free, but naan is wheat and no information confirms whether sauces, thickeners, or fryers avoid gluten or cross-contact. A GF diner would need to ask directly and stick to rice plus rice-based dishes.
vegan_detail: The restaurant states it flexibly accommodates vegetarian and vegan requests, and set menus include a vegetable curry option. Documented vegetable dishes include palak paneer, paneer in tomato-butter sauce, lentil (dal) curry, and stir-fried okra with onion and potato; of these, paneer and butter-based dishes contain dairy, so a true vegan should specify no dairy/ghee/cream and choose dal or vegetable curries prepared with oil. Confirm dairy-free preparation when ordering.
caveats: Vegetable curries include both vegan-friendly (dal, okra) and dairy-containing (paneer, butter sauce) options — vegans must specify no dairy/ghee. Naan is wheat-based; no gluten-free or dedicated-fryer information is published. The shop opened in September 2025, so menu and hours may still be settling.

bio.background: A recently opened branch of the Rasoi family of Indian restaurants, this Hondori location brought authentic Indian curry to the heart of Hiroshima's main shopping arcade in September 2025. It is run as a small, two-person operation serving lunch and dinner sets daily.
bio.specialty: Authentic North Indian-style curries served with naan or rice, with diners choosing their spice level from a range of intensities and getting staff guidance on curry choices.
bio.philosophy: Flexible, made-to-order cooking that adapts to each guest, including vegetarian and vegan requests.

anecdotes.text: Opened in the central Hondori arcade in September 2025, a short walk from the station, bringing a compact authentic Indian kitchen run by a small team.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff help guests choose curry type and spice level and the kitchen says it flexibly accommodates vegetarian and vegan requests, so allergy and diet needs can be discussed at ordering.

positives.text: A dedicated vegetable curry is offered within the set menus, and meat-free dishes such as lentil (dal) and okra curries are available.
The kitchen states it can flexibly adapt dishes for vegetarian and vegan diners.

cuisine: 純喫茶・洋食 (traditional kissaten / Japanese-style Western food) — known for old-fashioned ketchup napolitan with choice of toppings, katsu curry spaghetti, and meshikatsu
neighborhood: 中区大手町（市役所前電停 徒歩約4〜5分）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町4-2-16
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [132.452454, 34.386272] for 広島県広島市中区大手町4-2-16; within the Hiroshima validity box.
seats_text: Seat count not documented on sources fetched; described as a small Showa-era kissaten that draws lunchtime lines.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-16:00; Sat 11:00-14:00; Sun & public holidays closed (per Tabelog).
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills neighborhood kissaten with a Japanese-only menu and a long-time local crowd; come prepared to order in Japanese or by pointing, and note children are not permitted.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation documented. The signature dishes are wheat-based spaghetti (napolitan, curry spaghetti) and breaded/fried cutlets (tonkatsu, mince cutlet), all of which contain wheat. Not suitable for strict gluten-free needs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on meat and animal products — napolitan made with bacon and sausage, tonkatsu and mince-cutlet toppings, hamburger, egg topping, and katsu curry. No vegan dishes are documented.
caveats: Hours differ slightly across listings (one search snippet cited 07:00-16:00); the Tabelog listing's 11:00-16:00 (Sat to 14:00) is used here. No official website found. Seat count and any formal allergy handling are undocumented. Cards are not accepted but PayPay QR payment is.

bio.background: Tsuta is a long-running Showa-era kissaten tucked in the Otemachi district of central Hiroshima, a short walk from City Hall and the peace-memorial area. The ivy-draped exterior (its name, Tsuta, means ivy) and a nostalgic Showa interior have made it a fixture for generations of locals, with regulars recalling it operating since at least the late 1980s.
bio.specialty: Old-fashioned ketchup napolitan that diners can dress up with a choice of toppings — egg, tonkatsu, hamburger, or mince cutlet — alongside hearty house favorites like katsu curry spaghetti (spaghetti layered with curry, tonkatsu, and more curry) and meshikatsu, a cutlet over rice.

anecdotes.text: A local writer recalls that in their college days, returning home to Hiroshima, this was the first place they would visit — eating here was a personal reward, and even on an early visit a line of about ten people had already formed.
The napolitan is the classic ketchup style and can be ordered with added toppings (egg, tonkatsu, hamburger, or mince cutlet).

cuisine: 小さなカウンター割烹・日本料理（旬の魚介、刺身、煮物、天ぷら、茶碗蒸し、豆の前菜）
gf_detail: A traditional Japanese kappo counter serving sashimi, simmered dishes, tempura and chawanmushi. Cooking relies on dashi and Japanese seasonings, and tempura is wheat-battered, so dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume safety here without a direct Japanese-language conversation, and even then options would be very limited.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fish and seafood — sashimi (kanpachi, sea bream, raw oysters from Jigozen, tuna, scallop), simmered dishes and tempura, with dashi (typically fish-based) throughout. While there is a notable bean-and-root-vegetable side to the cooking (the chef once ran a place called 豆と根菜 いし井, and a praised bean-jelly appetizer exists), there is no documented vegan menu or vegan accommodation. Not suitable as a vegan destination.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町6-5 GAOビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区流川町6-5 via GSI; result (34.39016, 132.463638) falls within the central Hiroshima box, near the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife area and Gonomachi tram stop. Building floor varies across listings (2F/4F).
seats_text: 8-seat counter (sunken kotatsu / horigotatsu style); a small private room is also available, seating up to about 8 (12 if packed).
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, owner-run Japanese counter restaurant where the chef cooks and serves alone — a phone reservation in Japanese is expected and basic Japanese will make the evening far smoother for a foreign student.
neighborhood: 中区流川町（流川・薬研堀の繁華街、胡町電停近く）

bio.background: A small, intimate Japanese kappo restaurant tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, run single-handedly by its chef-owner, who cooks, plates and serves the whole room himself. He earlier ran a place known for beans and root vegetables (豆と根菜 いし井) before opening this counter as his own independent venture.
bio.philosophy: Turning everyday, seasonal ingredients into refined, carefully composed dishes, and pairing them with a thoughtfully chosen sake list that leans on premium and local Hiroshima labels.
bio.specialty: Seasonal sashimi (kanpachi, sea bream, local Jigozen raw oysters, tuna, scallop), simmered dishes, tempura and chawanmushi, plus a standout bean-jelly (豆のジュレ寄せ) appetizer that regulars single out as the best bite of the meal.

anecdotes.text: Because the chef does everything himself, he stretches the all-you-can-drink to a generous three hours so no one feels rushed while he cooks — yet diners say the food still arrives without noticeable delay.
A visiting diner named the bean-jelly appetizer the single best dish of the evening, praising its pronounced, clean bean flavor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cooking is traditional Japanese kappo style (sashimi, simmered dishes, chawanmushi), which conventionally uses dashi and wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce or accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is seafood- and dashi-centric (sashimi, raw oysters, fish-based dishes), so vegan cross-contact would be essentially unavoidable; no vegan handling is documented.

cuisine: Izakaya focused on Japanese sake (Hiroshima local sake), with teppanyaki, meat dishes, simmered dishes, and sashimi
neighborhood: Nagarekawa, Naka-ku (near Ebisu-cho / Hatchobori, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町1-7 セントラルゲート5ブロック
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区流川町1-7 via GSI; coordinates [132.464569, 34.39127] fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 70 seats across two floors: counter and table seating on the first floor; sunken (horigotatsu) and table seating plus private rooms on the second floor. Group bookings for 30-40 people accepted.
closed_days: Sundays (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local-style sake izakaya where staff and menus are likely Japanese-only, so a phrase book or translation app will help, though card and PayPay payment make ordering and paying straightforward.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a sake izakaya serving teppanyaki, meat, simmered dishes and sashimi, dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings; sake itself is rice-based but the food is not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. The menu centers on meat dishes, teppanyaki and sashimi, with wheat-soy-sauce-based seasoning typical of izakaya cooking, so it is not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Newly opened (March 2026) sake-focused izakaya. Hours and offerings should be reconfirmed directly, as a recently opened venue may adjust its schedule.

bio.background: A sake-focused izakaya in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, built around Hiroshima regional sake alongside sake gathered from across Japan, with an all-you-can-drink selection of more than twenty kinds.
bio.specialty: Japanese sake (especially Hiroshima local brews) paired with teppanyaki, generous meat dishes, simmered dishes and sashimi

anecdotes.text: The two-floor space pairs a casual counter and tables downstairs with sunken seating and private rooms upstairs, making it as suited to a quiet drink as to a group gathering.

name_en: Seto no Sakana to Kaki AVLI
cuisine: 瀬戸内の魚介・牡蠣の炙り小料理／オイスター居酒屋 (Setouchi seafood and oyster izakaya specializing in seared dishes)
gf_detail: This is a Setouchi seafood izakaya whose cooking leans heavily on Japanese soy sauce, dashi, and seared/marinated preparations, all of which typically contain wheat. There is no published gluten-free menu, no indication of wheat-free soy sauce, and no documented allergen accommodation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should not assume any dish is safe here.
vegan_detail: The entire concept centers on Seto Inland Sea seafood: fresh oysters, raw shirasu (whitebait), raw shrimp, seared fish, and seared wagyu beef, finished with clay-pot rice. Dishes rely on fish-based dashi and seafood, so there are no documented vegan options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町4-24 クリタビル 10F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区胡町4-24 via GSI; coordinates [132.465378, 34.393166] fall within the central Hiroshima box. The venue is in the Ebisu-chō/Nagarekawa-Yagenbori entertainment district, on the 10th floor of the Krita Building.
neighborhood: 中区胡町（流川・薬研堀エリア）
seats_text: Roughly 28-32 seats including counter seating and several private/semi-private room sections (sources vary: 28 per one listing, 32 per another).
hours_raw: 火～日・祝・祝前日 17:00～00:00（料理L.O. 23:00、ドリンクL.O. 23:30）。定休日：月曜（月曜が祝日の場合は営業し不定休）。
closed_days: Mondays (open if Monday falls on a public holiday, in which case the closing day shifts irregularly)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a Japanese-language evening izakaya in a nightlife district where some basic Japanese (or a translation app) will make ordering the seared seafood and oyster dishes much smoother; reservations can be made online.
caveats: Listings disagree slightly on seat count (28 vs 32) and on the closed day (one platform lists no regular holiday while others specify Monday). Address is 胡町 (Ebisu-chō), part of the broader Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife area rather than 流川 proper. Official website is the Instagram account; no standalone homepage was confirmed.

bio.background: AVLI is an adults' izakaya in central Hiroshima built around the bounty of the Seto Inland Sea, which it treats as a natural fish pen on the city's doorstep. The kitchen's signature is aburi (searing): a quick char that crisps the surface while keeping the inside moist, drawing out the natural sweetness and umami of each ingredient.
bio.specialty: Seared Setouchi seafood and fresh oysters: raw shirasu (whitebait), sweet raw shrimp, just-shucked oysters carrying the scent and salt of the sea, and seared wagyu, with meals rounded off by rice cooked in a traditional clay pot.
bio.philosophy: Let the freshness of the Seto Inland Sea speak for itself, using searing to concentrate rather than mask each ingredient's flavor.

anecdotes.text: The house calls the nearby Seto Inland Sea a tennen no ikesu, a natural live-fish tank, framing its oysters and shellfish as straight-from-the-sea catches.
Diners can be seated in private and semi-private room sections, making it a calm spot for a relaxed evening over sake and Setouchi lemon sours.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese seafood izakaya, dishes are prepared with standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which normally contain wheat; no wheat-free alternative is documented.

lead: 大衆肉割烹 肉じるしるし
name_ja: 大衆肉割烹 肉じるしるし
name_en: Taishu Niku Kappo Niku Jirushi Rushi
cuisine: 肉割烹・大衆居酒屋（焼き鳥・ステーキ・肉煮込み）
neighborhood: 中区流川（流川・薬研堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町8-27 Wakeビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of street address 広島県広島市中区流川町8-27 returned [132.464218, 34.389507], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Nagarekawa nightlife district in Naka-ku.
seats_text: 56 seats total: counter seating (up to 10), table seats (16), plus tatami private rooms suited to groups (rooms for roughly 6-10 and 12-20+). Solo diners welcome at the counter.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 18:00-00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30). Closed Sundays (open if the following Monday is a national holiday).
closed_days: Sundays (open when Monday is a national holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, smoking-permitted local meat izakaya where staff and menu are Japanese-centric, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on grilled skewers, braised meat in house demi-glace, steak, and motsu hotpot, all typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce and tare; fried items and shared grills mean cross-contamination is likely. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated meat (niku kappo) izakaya — beef skirt steak, skewers of chicken/pork/beef, horse liver sashimi, motsu hotpot, and demi-glace braised meat are the core menu. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented, and broths/seasonings rely on meat and fish. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
caveats: All seats permit smoking, which may be a consideration for sensitive diners. Listed phone numbers vary across directories (050-5457-6491, 070-8326-0866, and an older 082-247-2919). No dietary-accommodation information is published, so gluten-free and vegan assessments are inferred from the documented meat-centric menu rather than a stated allergy policy.

bio.background: A casual meat-focused kappo (niku kappo) izakaya tucked into Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, marked by retro lanterns at the entrance just off the central Chuo-dori area. It works through every cut and method — grilling, simmering, frying — across chicken, pork, beef and more.
bio.specialty: Beef skirt steak (gyu-harami / sagari), assorted grilled skewers, horse liver sashimi, motsu (offal) hotpot, and a signature slow-braised meat simmered in house-made demi-glace sauce.
bio.philosophy: Built around fresh meat and unusual cuts you don't easily find elsewhere, served in a relaxed setting that suits both solo diners at the counter and groups gathering in the tatami private rooms.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen's signature is meat braised slowly in a house-made demi-glace sauce, a dish regulars single out alongside the beef skirt steak.
The shop is recognizable from the street by the retro lanterns hung at its entrance near Hiroshima's central avenue.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried meat items and grilled skewers cooked on shared equipment, so cross-contact with wheat-containing batters and sauces is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as grilled skewers and braised meats use standard Japanese tare and soy-sauce seasonings, which are wheat-based; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every documented dish is meat- or offal-based and broths rely on animal stock, so no vegan-safe preparation is available.

cuisine: 昔ながらの地元食堂・大衆酒場（うどん・そば・おでん・定食）
neighborhood: 中区弥生町（流川・薬研堀エリア／銀山町電停近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区弥生町4-11
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区弥生町4-11 returned [132.466736, 34.390129], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Seat count not documented on any listing; described as a small, intimate old-style eatery.
hours_raw: Business hours not published on any listing; closed Sundays. Phone reservations sometimes possible. Confirm hours by phone (082-245-1802) before visiting.
closed_days: 日曜日（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, old-fashioned neighborhood eatery with no documented English support, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, respectful attitude will go a long way.
gf_detail: The core menu is udon, soba, nabeyaki udon and rice dishes simmered in a dashi-and-soy-sauce broth, plus tempura. Wheat-based noodles, wheat soy sauce and wheat-batter tempura mean there are no gluten-free options, and no GF accommodation is documented anywhere.
vegan_detail: Dishes such as niku-udon (beef), onigiri, simmered squid, tempura and zosui rely on fish dashi, meat and wheat soy sauce. No plant-based dishes or vegan accommodation are documented.

bio.background: A long-running, old-school neighborhood eatery tucked into central Hiroshima's Nagarekawa/Yagenbori district, a short walk from the Ginzan-cho tram stop. It keeps an unmistakably Showa-era, retro atmosphere: a calm, lived-in room where the food is honest, homestyle Japanese fare.
bio.specialty: Homemade-style onigiri and a lineup of udon dishes, including niku-udon (beef udon) and nabeyaki udon, alongside zosui (savory rice porridge) and simple set dishes.
roles: 小規模の地元食堂・大衆酒場

anecdotes.text: Regulars note the dashi broth is light and gentle, making it a favorite spot to settle the stomach after a night of drinking in the nearby entertainment district.
The room is described as an old-fashioned, Showa-nostalgic space with a calm, settled ambiance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on udon, soba and dishes simmered in a dashi/soy-sauce broth, which in traditional Japanese cooking uses wheat-based soy sauce.

caveats: Hours and seat count are not published on any listing; verify by phone (082-245-1802). Note there are several unrelated establishments named 赤のれん in Hiroshima (e.g. near Hiroshima Station and in Yagenbori); this record is the 弥生町4-11 eatery near the Ginzan-cho tram stop. Cash-only assumed for a small old-style shop but not explicitly confirmed.

lead: まいちえキッチン（Mai+Chie kitchen）
cuisine: Vegan organic takeout cafe and bakery (vegan bread, baked goods, weekend lunch plates and drinks)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The bakery centers on wheat-based items such as an organic-wheat melon pan, so flour is used and present in the shared kitchen. Some baked goods or seasonal items may be wheat-free, but no dedicated gluten-free program or cross-contamination controls are documented; a celiac or strongly gluten-sensitive diner should ask item-by-item and treat the kitchen as wheat-exposed.
vegan_detail: Fully (100%) vegan. Described as an entirely vegan, organic takeout cafe with no animal products. Documented items include soy-meat (daizu-meat) karaage, a soup-less tantanmen, melon pan, baked sweets and drinks. Weekend lunch plates and seasonal menus are offered, all plant-based.
address_ja: 広島県広島市西区三滝本町2丁目1-8 ミタキシェアキッチン B-2
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島市西区三滝本町2-1-8 to [132.449142, 34.418846], within the Hiroshima box. This is in Nishi Ward near Mitaki Station, northwest of the city center (roughly 4-5 km from the A-Bomb Dome), not in the immediate central tourist area.
neighborhood: 西区三滝本町 (Mitakihonmachi, Nishi Ward) — about a 5-minute walk from Mitaki Station on the JR Kabe Line, ~10 minutes by train from Hiroshima Station
seats_text: Primarily a takeout operation housed in a shared kitchen (Mitaki Share Kitchen B-2); seating is minimal to none and not documented. Plan for takeout or delivery.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (per Vegewel). Open Friday, Saturday and Sunday; closed Monday through Thursday, plus additional days when the shop is out participating in markets.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday (and occasional extra closures for market events)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly family/solo-run vegan takeout spot where a little Japanese helps, but ordering and pickup are simple and the all-vegan menu makes it easy for a foreign student to choose without worry.
caveats: Open only Friday through Sunday (11:00-16:00) and may close extra days for market events, so check Instagram before going. It is a takeout/delivery operation in a shared kitchen with little or no seating, and payment is cash only. It is northwest of the city center near Mitaki Station, a short train ride from Hiroshima Station rather than walkable from the A-Bomb Dome. Delivery is available via Uber Eats and Wolt.

bio.background: A small, fully plant-based kitchen that opened in June 2025, run as an independent operation out of a shared kitchen space in the Mitaki neighborhood of Hiroshima's Nishi Ward. It works as a takeout and delivery cafe rather than a sit-down restaurant, baking vegan, organic breads and sweets and serving weekend lunch plates and drinks.
bio.philosophy: Everything is vegan and made with organic ingredients, aiming to make plant-based eating approachable and tasty through familiar comfort foods reimagined without animal products.
bio.specialty: Vegan organic bakery items (such as an organic-wheat melon pan) plus plant-based comfort dishes like soy-meat karaage and a soup-less tantanmen.

anecdotes.text: Opened in June 2025 as an entirely vegan, organic takeout cafe operating from a shared kitchen near Mitaki Station, offering seasonal limited menus and weekend lunch plates.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The bakery makes wheat-based products such as an organic-wheat melon pan, so wheat flour is handled in the same kitchen; no gluten-free separation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is vegan/organic, so there is no animal-product cross-contact risk in the kitchen.

positives.text: 100% vegan, organic menu means no animal ingredients anywhere, removing meat, fish, dairy and egg cross-contact concerns for plant-based diners.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (広島風お好み焼き)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter, and the house specialty adds wheat noodles (udon or soba) plus tempura bits (wheat) and a wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce. No gluten-free options are documented and the kitchen is a single shared teppan griddle.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The okonomiyaki is layered with pork, egg, squid and other seafood toppings and cooked on a shared griddle; the signature dishes (e.g. meat-egg-squid-tempura udon, pork-jowl ponzu) all contain animal products. No vegan version is documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区富士見町14-11
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [132.459747, 34.386211], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in Naka-ku Fujimicho on the Jizo-dori shopping street, roughly 1 km east of the Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区富士見町・地蔵通り商店街 (Fujimicho / Jizo-dori shopping street, Naka-ku)
seats_text: 9 seats total — a small counter facing the teppan griddle (about 4 griddle-side counter seats plus 5 more counter seats).
hours_raw: Wed–Sun & holidays 11:00–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) and 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Mon & Tue.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (one source: every Monday and the 2nd Tuesday)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only neighborhood counter where ordering and chatting happen mostly in Japanese; a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, and pointing at the popular griddle dishes works fine.
caveats: Only 9 counter seats, cash only, and no reservations — expect a wait at peak times and bring cash. Closed Mondays and Tuesdays. Heavily wheat- and seafood-based; unsuitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.origin: Fujimicho on the Jizo-dori shopping street in central Hiroshima
bio.background: A family-run Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki shop founded in 1962 (Showa 37) that has stood on the old-downtown Jizo-dori shopping street for more than half a century. It is now run by the third-generation owner, Miho Matsuoka, who grew up in the shop and was making her own okonomiyaki on the griddle from elementary-school age.
bio.philosophy: Matsuoka calls her cooking 'subtraction okonomiyaki' (引き算のお好み焼き): vegetables hand-picked from local grocers, carefully cleaned, and seasoning kept to a minimum so the ingredients speak for themselves. As she puts it, 'I want people to remember our taste, not just our name.'
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles — the udon version with meat, egg, squid and tempura is a signature — plus side dishes like pork-jowl (kone) with ponzu.

anecdotes.text: When she took over the shop, Matsuoka asked her grandmother, the founder, for the batter recipe. The answer was simply, 'There isn't one — just go by feel' (そんなもんない。ええかげんよ). Rather than seeing this as a gap, she treated it as freedom to tune the batter to the day's temperature and water and develop her own flavor.
In 2020 the shop launched a frozen mail-order okonomiyaki, with Matsuoka personally cooking each piece at a new production kitchen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single shared teppan griddle used for all wheat-batter okonomiyaki, noodles and tempura, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce (and a pork-jowl ponzu option), which are wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking happens on one griddle shared with pork, egg and seafood, so vegan cross-contact cannot be avoided.

name_ja: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き 蔵屋 本店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Otemachi, Naka-ku (near Hiroshima City Hall / about 700m from Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町3-8-4 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町3-8-4 returned [132.454605, 34.38707], within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 40 seats total, including counter seats and sunken-kotatsu private rooms (each fitted with its own teppan grill); private spaces accommodate 2-20 guests.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat, holidays and days before holidays: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (LO 13:30), Dinner 17:00-23:30 (LO 22:30). Closed Sundays.
closed_days: Sundays (occasionally open; confirm in advance)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming local okonomiyaki and teppanyaki house where counter seats let you watch the grilling; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English support is limited.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dishes are Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki, built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce. Teppanyaki seasonings and izakaya sides also typically use wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature dishes center on pork, egg, Hiroshima beef, oysters, and sea urchin (uni horen), and okonomiyaki batter typically contains egg and dashi. No vegan menu or plant-based accommodation is documented.

bio.background: Kuraya's main shop sits in Otemachi, a short walk from Hiroshima City Hall and the Peace Memorial Park area, serving Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki in a Japanese-styled space of counter seats and sunken-kotatsu private rooms, each fitted with its own teppan grill so dishes can be cooked and served hot at the table.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built layer by layer with cabbage and noodles, plus teppanyaki dishes such as uni horen (spinach sauteed in butter and salt-and-pepper, crowned generously with sea urchin), a crispy-edged teppan potato salad, A5 sirloin steak, and oysters; an assortment of local Hiroshima sake rounds out the meal.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen treats yaki-kagen, the precise control of heat and timing on the grill, as the heart of the craft, aiming to send out plates that bring a smile to each guest.

anecdotes.text: Katsushi Shoga, born 1982 in Hiroshima, came to cooking after starting in an unrelated line of work; he spent about eleven years at an okonomiyaki restaurant, rising to manager, before joining Kuraya, where he was promoted to store manager in his second year, drawing on close to two decades of teppanyaki and okonomiyaki experience.
The uni horen, spinach sauteed in butter and salt-and-pepper and topped boldly with sea urchin, is presented as a Hiroshima regional specialty and one of the house's signature plates.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki here is built on wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles and finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki sauce, so wheat is present throughout the core menu.

caveats: Hours and Sunday closure cross-checked between the official site and Tabelog; the shop notes Sundays are occasionally open, so confirm ahead. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan handling information is published, so cross-contact for sensitive diners cannot be assessed.

name_ja: 広島つけ麺 かず 光町本店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (cold dipping noodles) — an original wheat noodle served with boiled vegetables, cucumber and chashu, dipped in a light, slightly tart dashi made from natural Rishiri kombu and pork chashu, with 16 selectable spice levels.
neighborhood: Hikarimachi, Higashi-ku — within walking distance of Hiroshima Station Shinkansen exit
address_ja: 広島県広島市東区光町2-8-1 第2今井ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市東区光町2-8-1 returned [132.480392, 34.401405], within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.26–34.42, lng 132.28–132.50).
seats_text: 21 seats total — 15 counter seats and a 6-seat table section.
closed_days: Wednesdays (水曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A no-frills local noodle counter where ordering is simple (pick your spice level 1–16); little to no English is likely, so a foreign student should be ready to point at the menu and pay in cash.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The dish is tsukemen built on wheat noodles, and the dipping dashi is made with chashu (pork) and typically contains soy sauce, so wheat is present in both the noodles and the sauce.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature dipping broth is made from pork chashu (and Rishiri kombu), and the noodles are served with chashu, so the core dish contains animal products. No documented vegan option.
caveats: The shop relocated within Hikarimachi (a notice of a 3/24 relocated-location opening appeared on its listing); the address and geocode reflect the current 光町2-8-1 location. Spice levels are documented as 16 on listing pages, though some older review/listing text mentions 10 — confirm in-store. No official website found; the shop uses an Instagram account (@kazu_hikarimachi).

bio.background: 広島つけ麺 かず is a long-established Hiroshima tsukemen specialist that began in 1997 and has become one of the names credited with sparking Hiroshima's tsukemen culture. The original Hikarimachi shop sits just a short walk from Hiroshima Station's Shinkansen exit, a tight counter-and-table room run as a single independent location.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style cold tsukemen: house noodles topped with boiled cabbage, green onion, cucumber and chashu, dipped into a light (あっさり) dashi drawn from natural Rishiri kombu and pork chashu. The dipping sauce is balanced between tartness and chili heat, and diners choose from 16 levels of spiciness.

anecdotes.text: The dashi is described as drawn from natural Rishiri kombu and chashu, giving a light flavor that is rounded out by the sweetness of the boiled vegetables, with 16 selectable spice levels.
Regulars note the dipping sauce strikes a good balance of sourness and chili heat, with complexity beyond simple spiciness.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping dashi for Hiroshima tsukemen is built on chashu and a savory soy-based sauce, which conventionally contains wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature broth is made with pork chashu, so the kitchen handles animal products throughout the core dish.

cuisine: Tempura specialist — freshly fried tempura served piece by piece; affordable lunch teishoku (set meals) and evening sake-paired tempura small plates
gf_detail: This is a tempura restaurant; the defining dish is battered, deep-fried tempura, and wheat-flour batter is unavoidable here. Tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu) and the izakaya dishes also typically use wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for anyone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The kitchen fries vegetables, seafood (shrimp, squid, mackerel) and chicken in the same oil, so vegetable tempura is cross-contaminated with fish/meat. The signature set also comes with miso soup and pickled sides; batter and oil handling are not vegan-controlled. A strict vegan would have very little to eat safely; at most an omnivore-leaning diner could request vegetable pieces, but cross-contact is certain. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町5-8 第2下中町ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区中町5-8 returned [132.45903, 34.389992], within the Hiroshima box. Lands in Nakamachi, central Hiroshima, a short walk from Hondori and the Peace Memorial Park.
neighborhood: 中区中町 (Nakamachi), central Hiroshima — near Namiki-dori/Fukuromachi, walking distance from Hondori arcade and Peace Memorial Park
seats_text: 46 seats including counter seating, tables, and private rooms (one source cites about 40 seats; listing states 46). Counter seating makes it solo-friendly.
closed_days: No fixed closing day documented (open daily per listed hours). Confirm before visiting, as small restaurants may take irregular holidays.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, friendly counter where a foreign student can comfortably get by with a few Japanese greetings and pointing at the set menu; lunch sets are picture-and-name based and easy to order, though detailed allergy questions may be hard without Japanese.

bio.background: An independent tempura spot in central Hiroshima's Nakamachi that opened in December 2020 with a simple idea: make tempura casual and affordable. Instead of the formal, expensive tempura-counter experience, it serves freshly fried pieces one at a time and turns out a generous set lunch that regularly draws a queue.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (Nakamachi, Naka Ward), central city near Hondori and the Peace Park.
bio.specialty: The house すえ廣定食 (Suehiro set), built around tempura fried to order and delivered piece by piece on a personal wire rack so each item arrives hot and crisp. Diners finish each piece with dipping sauce or a choice of house salts (such as Tibetan, curry, and matcha salts). At night the kitchen shifts to sake- and wine-friendly tempura small plates, including playful items like Camembert and Caprese tempura.
bio.philosophy: Tempura should be approachable and free — eaten hot at the counter, paired with a drink, without the formality or price of a traditional tempura house.

anecdotes.text: The signature lunch set bundles seven freshly fried pieces — shrimp, squid, mackerel, chicken and three vegetables (eggplant, pumpkin, mushroom) — with rice (Shimane-grown kinumusume), miso soup, squid salad and pickles, with rice and sides refillable.
Each tempura piece is brought out hot on an individual wire rack and can be seasoned with dipping sauce or one of three house salts.
In the evening the restaurant leans into pairings it calls 'ten-sham' (tempura with champagne), alongside creative tempura like Camembert and Caprese.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat-battered deep-fried tempura, with seafood, meat and vegetables fried in shared oil; gluten exposure is unavoidable and there is no separate fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tempura dipping sauce and izakaya dishes customarily use wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free or tamari alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura is fried in the same oil as shrimp, squid, mackerel and chicken, so vegetable pieces are cross-contaminated with fish and meat.

lead: 出汁とおでん わいく
name_ja: 出汁とおでん わいく
cuisine: Owner-run Japanese oden izakaya — multi-dashi oden, Seto Inland Sea sashimi, grilled simmered anago, Hiroshima red-chicken karaage, local sake
neighborhood: 中区袋町 (Fukuromachi, central Hiroshima, off Namiki-dori near Hondori)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町1-9 髙田ビル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県広島市中区袋町1-9 via GSI; result lat 34.391792, lng 132.460724 falls within the Hiroshima box and matches the central Fukuromachi location.
seats_text: 26 seats — counter plus tables; partitions create semi-private spaces for 3-5 people, with private rental for groups up to 30.
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 17:00–00:00; closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays (月曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, warm neighborhood oden izakaya where a few words of Japanese and an open mind go a long way; staff likely speak limited English, so pointing at the menu and a friendly attitude will make a solo diner welcome.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Oden is simmered in a broth and the menu centers on Japanese izakaya dishes that typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings; nothing on the official site or listings indicates gluten-free options. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not assume safe dishes here without confirming in person.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature dashi is explicitly built from bonito (鰹) and chicken bones (鶏ガラ) alongside kombu and shiitake, so even the oden broth is animal-based. The rest of the menu is centered on fish (Seto Inland Sea sashimi, grilled anago) and chicken. No vegan dishes are advertised.
caveats: Owner/chef's personal name is not published. Two price tiers appear in listings (a lower quoted budget and a higher review-aggregate around 6,000-8,000 yen); plan for a mid-range izakaya dinner. Hours close at midnight (entered as 24:00 for the day's end). No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan handling information is documented anywhere checked.

bio.background: A small, owner-run Japanese oden izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in central Hiroshima's Fukuromachi district, just off the Namiki-dori and Hondori shopping streets. It is the kind of intimate counter-and-table spot built for an after-work solo drink, a quiet dinner date, or a small gathering.
bio.origin: Central Hiroshima — 広島市中区袋町
bio.specialty: Multi-dashi oden simmered in a broth that blends bonito, kombu, chicken bones, and shiitake. Beyond the oden, the kitchen serves sashimi from the nearby Seto Inland Sea, grilled simmered conger eel (anago), and karaage made from Hiroshima red chicken (Takamiya 'Takamiya-dori' from Akitakata). House-made yuzu kosho and miso sauce are points of pride.
bio.philosophy: The owner frames the restaurant around the idea of '和' (wa) — nurturing the bonds between people — and says they are particular about ingredient selection and spare no time or effort in preparing the food.

anecdotes.text: The dashi, which gives the shop its name, is made by layering several stocks together — bonito, kombu, chicken bones, and shiitake — which the owner treats as the foundation of authentic Japanese cooking.
The karaage uses Hiroshima red chicken (Takamiya-dori) raised in Takamiya, Akitakata, and is served with the owner's house-made yuzu kosho and miso sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The oden broth is made with bonito and chicken bones, so it is animal-based and any item simmered in or served with it is not vegan.

cuisine: Independent tsukemen (dipping-noodle) shop — Kanto-style fish-and-seafood pork-bone double broth, also spicy miso tsukemen, abura soba / mazesoba, and ramen
neighborhood: 中区流川／胡町 (Nagarekawa / Ebisucho, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町4-15 パワービル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町4-15 returned [132.463806, 34.390373], within the Hiroshima box; lands in the Nagarekawa nightlife district of central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 14 seats (8 counter, 6 table)
hours_raw: 19:00–04:00 (L.O. around 03:00–03:30). Note: hours were temporarily reduced to 11:30–20:00 during June 1–20.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, late-night counter ramen/tsukemen shop in the Nagarekawa drinking quarter; ordering is in Japanese with no English menu noted, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help, and bring cash.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house noodles are wheat-based, and the dipping broth is a fish-and-seafood plus pork-bone soup typically seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. (A konnyaku/konjac low-calorie noodle swap exists, but the broth and seasonings are not gluten-free.)
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. The core product is a fish-and-seafood (gyokai) plus pork-bone (tonkotsu) double broth, with toppings such as diced chashu pork. No vegan or vegetarian option is documented.
caveats: Late-night hours (opens around 7pm, runs to ~4am) and closed Sundays; hours have been temporarily shortened in some periods, so confirm before a daytime visit. Cash only. Small 14-seat shop that can fill up.

bio.background: A small independent dipping-noodle specialist tucked into central Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife district, open late into the early morning to catch the after-hours crowd.
bio.specialty: Kanto-style fish-and-seafood tsukemen: house-made medium-thick noodles served with a piping-hot, concentrated double broth made from fish/seafood and pork bone, topped with white scallion, bamboo shoots, spinach and tender diced chashu. Spicy miso tsukemen and a fish-powder spicy mazesoba are also signatures.

anecdotes.text: The shop bills itself as a Kanto-style fish-and-seafood tsukemen specialist and serves its noodles with a fish-and-pork-bone double broth, with a konnyaku (konjac) noodle swap offered for lighter, lower-calorie eating.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are cooked and the kitchen is a single small noodle shop, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tsukemen dipping broth is a standard fish/seafood and pork-bone tare-seasoned soup, which in ramen/tsukemen normally contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are animal-based (fish/seafood and pork bone) and dishes are topped with pork chashu, so there is no vegan-safe preparation.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style tsukemen (spicy dipping noodles), broth-less Sichuan-pepper tantanmen, and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Higashi-Hakushima, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区東白島町19-74
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the full address returned [132.462921, 34.404957], within the Hiroshima box; lands in Higashi-Hakushima, Naka-ku, roughly 1.5 mi NE of the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 30 seats (5 counter, 25 table)
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A small local noodle shop where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu) is expected; no indication of English-language service, so a phrase or two helps.
gf_detail: The core menu is wheat-noodle tsukemen, broth-less tantanmen and ramen, all made with wheat noodles, and the dipping sauces rely on standard soy sauce (wheat-based). There is no documented gluten-free option or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Dishes are built on fish/meat-based broths, pork/sesame tantan sauces and soy sauce, with teppanyaki meat plates. No vegetarian or vegan options are documented.
caveats: Cash only. Dietary information is unverified beyond the standard wheat-and-fish/meat composition of the dishes; travelers with celiac disease or strict vegan needs should treat this as unsuitable.
sources: https://msearch.gsi.go.jp/address-search/AddressSearch?q=広島県広島市中区東白島町19-74

bio.background: A modest neighborhood noodle shop run by a husband-and-wife team on the ground floor of a cream-colored three-story building in the quiet Higashi-Hakushima district of central Hiroshima. By day it is a noodle counter; in the evening it shifts into a relaxed izakaya-style spot.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style spicy tsukemen (dipping noodles) and a signature broth-less tantanmen finished with a kick of Sichuan pepper, alongside teppanyaki plates.

anecdotes.text: The couple runs the whole operation themselves, known locally for generous portions and quality toppings at very approachable lunch prices, with a signature Sichuan-pepper broth-less tantanmen.

cuisine: 中華料理（北京・四川）／ラーメン・餃子
neighborhood: 幟町・八丁堀（中央エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町7-23
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区幟町7-23 via GSI; coordinates [132.465775, 34.395046] fall within the Hiroshima box. NOTE: the target brief listed the area as 野白町, but Tabelog, the official site, and all other listings give the actual address as 幟町 (Noboricho) 7-23. Used the verified 幟町 address.
seats_text: 24席（一部資料では21席との表記もあり）
closed_days: 日曜・祝日（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, longtime neighborhood Chinese restaurant where the menu and service are in Japanese, so a basic phrase or pointing at dishes will help; the staff are welcoming but English support is limited.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen centers on ramen, gyoza (dumplings), and Chinese dishes that rely on wheat noodles, wheat dumpling wrappers, and wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is an omnivore Chinese restaurant whose dishes (char siu, meat-based ramen, gyoza, stir-fries) use pork, other meats, and animal-based broths and seasonings. No vegan menu or documented plant-based options.

bio.background: A Chinese restaurant founded in 1984 that has served central Hiroshima for four decades. After a renovation it reopened in April 2018, continuing as a small individually run establishment.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (central, in the Noboricho/Hatchobori district a short walk from the city center)
bio.specialty: Beijing- and Sichuan-style Chinese cooking, alongside ramen and handmade gyoza. House offerings include hearty lunch sets and an appetizer set pairing gyoza and char siu with a draft beer.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes health-conscious, careful cooking: using canola oil, prioritizing domestically sourced ingredients, avoiding frozen products, and preparing dishes by hand from fresh materials.

anecdotes.text: A long-loved local ramen, 'Suzume no ramen,' was revived here and is served alongside the house Chinese noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese restaurant serving ramen, gyoza, and stir-fried dishes, the cooking relies on standard wheat-based soy sauce and wheat noodles/wrappers; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are meat- and broth-based (pork, char siu, animal-based ramen) and prepared in a shared kitchen; no separation for plant-based diets is documented.

caveats: The target brief's stated area (野白町) does not match the verified address; Tabelog, the official site, gnavi, hotpepper, and cookdoor all give the address as 広島市中区幟町7-23 (Noboricho), which was used for geocoding. Seat count varies by source (24 on official/cookdoor, 21 on Tabelog). No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented; treated as an omnivore Chinese restaurant.

cuisine: Ramen (chuka soba / shoyu ramen)
neighborhood: Nishi-Tokaichimachi, Naka-ku (near Tokaichimachi tram stop, walkable to Peace Memorial Park and Hondori)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区西十日市町2-15
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Hiroshima box; lands in Nishi-Tokaichimachi, Naka-ku as expected.
seats_text: 9 counter seats only
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (or until ingredients sell out). Closure dates announced on Instagram.
closed_days: Irregular; closure dates announced via Instagram. No fixed weekly closed day documented.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter shop with a frequent queue; an English menu is available, so a few words of Japanese and patience in line will carry you through comfortably.
vegan_detail: The ramen is built on animal- and fish-based stock (chicken/duck charsiu toppings and an anchovy/iriko broth), and the soy ramen uses regular soy sauce. There are no documented vegan options.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle ramen shop; the noodles are wheat, and the shoyu (soy sauce) broth is made with wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free options are available.
caveats: Lunch only and closes when ingredients run out; expect a 30-60 minute wait in the alley, especially on weekends. Cash only, no reservations, no parking (bicycle parking available).

bio.background: A small, independent ramen counter that opened in late November 2021 in a quiet residential alley of Nishi-Tokaichimachi, a short walk from Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park and the Hondori shopping street. Word of mouth and social media quickly turned it into one of the city's most talked-about ramen destinations, with lines forming in the alley before it opens.
bio.specialty: Refined shoyu (soy) ramen and a special anchovy (iriko) ramen with thin, silky-chewy noodles and a delicate yet deeply umami broth, finished with a quartet of meat preparations: rare charsiu, grilled charsiu, chicken charsiu, and duck loin. A thin-sliced meat rice bowl is the signature side.
bio.philosophy: The name 'Yohaku' (white space / margin) and the calm, clean counter space reflect an aesthetic of restraint and quiet care; the focus is on a single, carefully composed bowl rather than a broad menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in an unassuming alley, and the queue snaking down that lane has itself become part of its reputation; regulars suggest arriving about 30 minutes before opening on a weekday to make the first seating.
In a local survey of more than 200 ramen fans, it was voted Hiroshima's number-one ramen shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the core product, so flour and wheat are present throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature shoyu ramen is built on a soy-sauce-based tare, which in standard Japanese ramen is made with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth and toppings are animal- and fish-based (chicken, duck, anchovy/iriko), with no vegan menu items documented.

cuisine: 中華料理
neighborhood: 広島市中区八丁堀
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区八丁堀6-6
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区八丁堀6-6 returned [lng 132.46376, lat 34.395638], within the Hiroshima box, central Naka-ku near Hatchobori.
seats_text: Roughly 170 seats across multiple floors: table seating on the 2nd floor and tatami private rooms on the 3rd and 4th floors, with private rooms for 4 to 60 people.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (Mon-Sat); closed Sunday
closed_days: Sundays; year-end and New Year holidays (and occasional other days)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, old-school neighborhood Chinese restaurant where pointing at the picture-menu or food samples works fine, but staff are unlikely to speak much English, so a translation app or your guide helps for any dietary questions.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional Chinese restaurant whose dishes (fried chicken/karaage, eight-treasure stir-fry, ramen, sauces) rely on wheat-based soy sauce, wheat batter and flour, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners. Treat everything as containing gluten unless staff confirm otherwise.
vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan venue. The menu centers on meat, egg and seafood Chinese dishes, and stir-fries and sauces typically use chicken/pork stock and wheat-based soy sauce. A few vegetable stir-fries may be adaptable, but you would need to ask in advance about meat broth, oyster sauce and shared woks; no vegan dishes are documented.
caveats: Tabelog lists a much larger, multi-floor operation (around 170 seats with banquet rooms) than a small family counter shop. One Tabelog summary gave a 2012 establishment date, but Japanese listings and the longstanding-shop framing point to 昭和42年 (1967) founding; the exact founding year is not firmly confirmed across sources. No allergen, gluten-free or vegan information was published on any page reviewed.

bio.background: A long-running, old-school Chinese restaurant tucked into the Hatchobori district of central Hiroshima, just steps from the famous Micchan okonomiyaki shop. Opened with the idea of serving authentic Chinese cooking at prices everyday people can afford, it has become a nostalgic neighborhood fixture spread over several floors, with casual table seating downstairs and tatami rooms above for groups and celebrations.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the classic one-plate 'Chinese lunch' (中華ランチ): a round mound of rice crowned with eight-treasure stir-fry (八宝菜) and karaage, rounded out with salad, ham and a soft-boiled egg, served for under about 1,000 yen. The kitchen ranges from hearty home-style staples to more elaborate banquet Chinese dishes.
bio.philosophy: Authentic Chinese food at reasonable, approachable prices.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the place as a wonderfully nostalgic 'town Chinese restaurant,' with vintage decor and plastic food samples in the window, best known for its generous, comforting one-plate Chinese lunch set.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Chinese restaurant, dishes rely on standard soy sauce and seasonings that are wheat-based; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Stir-fries are cooked in shared woks and commonly use meat stock; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

lead: 流川 餃子センター
name_ja: 流川 餃子センター
cuisine: 餃子・中華料理 (gyoza and Chinese)
neighborhood: 広島市中区流川町 (Nagarekawa, central Hiroshima entertainment district, near Ebisucho stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町5-27
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区流川町5-27 returned [132.46431, 34.390064], within the Hiroshima box; lands in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 26 seats: 9 counter seats plus table seating (4 tables) toward the back.
hours_raw: Tue–Thu 17:00–22:30 (L.O. 22:00); Fri–Sat 17:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); closed Sun & Mon. (Earlier post-relaunch listings showed 17:00–22:00.)
closed_days: Sunday and Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-school downtown counter spot where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the gyoza) is easiest; English menus are not assured, so a translation app helps.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is gyoza, whose wrappers are wheat flour, and the shop is run by a company that describes itself as a dumpling-wrapper maker. Ramen and the green-onion dipping sauce (typically soy-sauce based) also contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature gyoza are filled with pork, and the menu centers on pork dishes, fried rice and Chinese fare; dipping sauces are soy-sauce based. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: Cash or PayPay only (no credit cards or e-money). Hours reported slightly differ between sources (a 17:00–22:00 figure appears on some listings versus the official 22:30/23:00 closes); confirm by phone for late visits. Phone-only reservations with limited daily slots; the place is small, so walk-ins may wait.

bio.chef_name: 佐古純一 (Sako Junichi), founding proprietor
bio.background: A neighborhood gyoza and Chinese eatery founded around 1961 in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, run for roughly six decades by founder Sako Junichi and regarded locally as the work of a pioneer of Hiroshima-style gyoza. When the aging owner faced closing the shop, the Hiroshima dumpling company Itsuji Food & Corporation (which runs Gyoza-ya Ryu) took over the business and reopened it on January 20, 2026, keeping the original name, noren and recipe.
bio.specialty: Hand-folded gyoza with a crisp-bottomed, chewy (mochi-mochi) wrapper, eaten with a tangy green-onion dipping sauce you mix at the table; pork dishes and fried rice round out the menu, with grilled gyoza and water gyoza both around 500 yen for eight.
bio.philosophy: Carry on a beloved old shop without letting its flame go out, faithfully inheriting the late-night founder's taste and techniques so longtime regulars find the flavor unchanged.
roles: successor operator: Itsuji Food & Corporation / Gyoza-ya Ryu

anecdotes.text: The shop was set to close when its elderly founder could no longer keep up the physically demanding work, but a local gyoza company stepped in, saying it wanted to preserve the shop of a pioneer of Hiroshima gyoza; young staff took over the taste and the noren to carry the old shop's history forward.

Under the January 2026 relaunch the new operator committed to faithfully inheriting founder Sako Junichi's recipes and techniques so longtime customers would taste the same flavor cherished for years.
The gyoza are praised for a crisp grilled bottom and chewy wrapper, with a sweet pork filling and the punch coming from the tangy green-onion sauce diners assemble themselves at the counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-wrapper gyoza are the central product and the kitchen is run by a dumpling-wrapper maker, so flour is pervasive; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature green-onion dipping sauce is a soy-sauce based tare, which in Japan normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Gyoza filling is pork and the menu is built around pork and Chinese dishes, so vegan diners cannot rely on shared cooking surfaces or sauces being free of animal products.

cuisine: Japanese kappo cuisine specializing in anago (conger eel) and unagi (freshwater eel), with Seto Inland Sea seasonal seafood
gf_detail: This is a traditional eel and kappo restaurant. The signature anago/unagi dishes are charcoal-grilled and basted in a wheat-based soy sauce tare (the proprietary sauce has been continuously replenished since founding). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and the core dishes rely on wheat-containing soy sauce. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: A fish-and-seafood-centered restaurant built around eel, conger eel, sea urchin, and Seto Inland Sea catch. Dishes use fish, wheat-soy-based tare, and dashi. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町4-18
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区堀川町4-18 returned [132.463516, 34.392807], inside the Hiroshima box; lands in central Naka-ku near the Ebisu-dori arcade / Hatchobori area as expected.
neighborhood: 広島市中区堀川町 (near Ebisucho Station / Hatchobori / Nagarekawa)
seats_text: 23 seats, including counter seating where diners can watch and ask the chefs about the cooking
hours_raw: 12:00–14:00, 18:00–22:00; closed Wednesdays
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, long-established family-run kappo restaurant where Japanese-language ability helps a lot; counter seating is welcoming but the atmosphere is traditional, so go with a few polite phrases and be prepared that English may be limited.
caveats: Lunch unaju starts around 6,000 yen and the signature hitsumushi around 4,400 yen, so this is a special-occasion price point rather than a budget meal. Built entirely around eel and seafood with wheat-soy tare; not appropriate for vegan or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: A long-established kappo (traditional Japanese) restaurant in central Hiroshima that has been operating for over 135 years, with roots reaching back before the war. It sits along the Ebisu-dori shopping arcade in the Horikawa-cho district, run as a small husband-and-wife establishment.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and conger eel (anago). The house signature is 'hitsumushi', a steamed eel-over-rice dish served in a wooden box, available with either anago or unagi. The proprietary basting tare has been continuously replenished since the restaurant's founding.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the seasonal bounty of the Seto Inland Sea, adapting the menu to what is freshest each day, and grilling eel over charcoal so the skin turns crisp while the flesh stays soft.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen's tare (basting sauce) has been continuously topped up and carried forward for well over a century, and the eel is charcoal-grilled until the skin is crisp and the flesh stays fluffy.
Counter seating lets diners watch the chefs work and ask questions about the cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The eel dishes are glazed with a proprietary soy-based tare that has been replenished since founding; Japanese soy sauce tare is wheat-based, so the signature dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is built around eel, conger eel, sea urchin and Seto Inland Sea seafood with fish-based dashi; there is no documented vegan preparation.

cuisine: 日本料理・割烹（懐石・会席）
neighborhood: 広島市中区幟町（銀山町駅）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町9-4 藤井ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区幟町9-4 returned [132.467987, 34.394184], within the central Hiroshima box; consistent with a location near Ginzan-machi station and the A-Bomb Dome area.
seats_text: 22 seats total — counter, table, and tatami seating including 2 private rooms (one source cites ~20 capacity)
closed_days: Mondays; year-end/New Year holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A refined, family-run kaiseki house that serves a fixed omakase course by advance reservation only (just a few groups a night), so a foreign student should be prepared for a formal, mostly-Japanese-speaking setting and reserve ahead.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki/kappo restaurant where dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) and seasonings, and items such as tempura use wheat flour batter, so the omakase course is not gluten-free and no GF accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The kaiseki menu is built around dashi (typically fish/bonito stock) and seasonal Setouchi seafood such as sea bream and local oysters, plus Hiroshima beef, so the dishes contain fish, shellfish and meat. No vegan course or accommodation is documented.

bio.background: The chef-owner is the son of a former naval cook, whose lively stories about cooking first drew him toward the kitchen. He began training at age 18, working his way through traditional ryotei, wedding venues, hotels and kappo restaurants, and apprenticed under chef Nakano Isao at the renowned ryotei Soshisuen, building decades of experience in Japanese kaiseki and kappo cooking. He opened his own restaurant in 2007.
bio.philosophy: He believes a dish is more than its flavor and plating, paying close attention even to the timing of when each course is served. To do this properly he deliberately accepts only a few dinner parties each evening so he can pace the meal to each guest. His cooking centers on regional Setouchi ingredients — firm-fleshed sea bream from strong currents, local oysters, and Hiroshima beef.
bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase kaiseki built on Setouchi seafood and Hiroshima beef, served as a multi-course menu

anecdotes.text: Despite its serious reputation, the dining room is repeatedly described as warm and homely (アットホームな雰囲気) — the place is run by the owner together with his family, lending a familial feel to an otherwise refined kaiseki experience.
The chef limits dinner to only about three groups a night so he can personally control the rhythm and presentation of each course for every guest.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese kaiseki/kappo kitchen, seasoning relies on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and Japanese condiments; no gluten-free soy sauce or accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The course is built on fish/dashi, shellfish (oysters) and meat (Hiroshima beef); there is no separate vegan preparation, so cross-contact for a vegan diner is effectively unavoidable.

caveats: Course/omakase only with full advance reservation (about 3 dinner groups per night); not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diners. Seat count is listed as 22 on Tabelog (one directory cites ~20). No official website was confirmed. Chef birthplace appears as Yamaguchi in the chef-profile sources (a Tabelog-aggregator search snippet showed Yamagata; the dedicated profile pages say Yamaguchi, which is used here).

cuisine: Sweets shop / gelato and ice cream (with attached souvenir store)
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), near the shrine exit on Omotesando / Kawabata-suji
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町112
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町112; coordinates land on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: About 10 seats; seating is within the souvenir shop.
hours_raw: Tabelog lists 09:00-18:00 daily; the official site lists 10:00-17:00. Closures are irregular (不定休).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed closed day.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up sweets counter inside a souvenir shop where you can point at the display and order quickly; a few words of Japanese help but it is easy to manage as a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature momiji-shaped ice monaka uses a wheat-flour wafer shell, and the Hiroshima okonomiyaki roll is wheat-based. No gluten-free items or handling are documented.
