vegan_detail: Not vegan. Core offerings are dairy-based ice cream and gelato, and the ice monaka uses dairy ice cream; the okonomiyaki roll and meat-wrapped rice ball stick contain egg/meat. No vegan/plant-based options are documented.
caveats: Hours differ between listings (Tabelog 09:00-18:00 vs official site 10:00-17:00); closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. No allergen or ingredient information is published.

bio.background: A family-run souvenir shop on Miyajima, just a short walk from the exit of Itsukushima Shrine, with a small sweets counter tucked among the merchandise. Visitors stroll in for a quick treat while exploring the shrine approach.
bio.specialty: Their signature is the momiji-shaped 'ice monaka' (maple-leaf wafer filled with ice cream) made with handmade red bean paste and vanilla ice cream in a crisp wafer shell, offered in flavors like vanilla, matcha, chocolate cake, and cherry blossom, alongside other walk-and-eat snacks.

anecdotes.text: Alongside the sweets counter, the shop stocks a wide range of Miyajima keepsakes, from rice-paddle (shamoji) straps and Great Torii replicas to local teddy bears, making it a one-stop stop for both a snack and a souvenir.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based wafer (monaka) and okonomiyaki roll are made and handled in the same small space as other items, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy ice cream and gelato are the core products and share the same counter and scoops, so cross-contact with dairy is likely.

lead: とんかつ料理 さち
name_ja: とんかつ料理 さち
cuisine: Tonkatsu (Japanese breaded deep-fried pork cutlet)
neighborhood: Sakaimachi, Naka-ku (central Hiroshima, about 700m west of Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堺町2丁目4-27 サンピア―白樺1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 広島県広島市中区堺町2丁目4-27 returned [132.444717, 34.395107], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Sakaimachi location near Tosabashi tram stop.
seats_text: 34 seats total (12 counter, 10 table, 12 sunken-kotatsu/zashiki); can host private banquets up to about 30 people.
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), daily. (One listing shows weekend/Sat–Sun dinner closing at 21:00 rather than 22:00.)
closed_days: None (open daily, no regular closing day per listings)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood tonkatsu shop where ordering is straightforward by pointing at set-meal photos, but menus and staff are mostly Japanese, so a translation app helps; note credit cards are not accepted (cash, with PayPay available).
gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu specialist whose core dishes are pork cutlets coated in wheat-flour batter and panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the signature food is not gluten-free. The house dipping sauce and accompaniments would also typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The restaurant is built entirely around deep-fried pork cutlets; sides include miso soup, cabbage and rice, and the signature secret sauce is served with a quail egg yolk. No vegan dishes are documented and the menu is meat-centered.

bio.background: Tonkatsu Ryori Sachi has stood in the Sakaimachi neighborhood of central Hiroshima since 1975, serving the same local crowd for roughly half a century. It is a small, independently run shop a couple of minutes' walk from the Tosabashi/Koamicho tram area, just west of Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: The house style centers on a 'secret' dipping sauce blended with grated daikon radish: rather than the usual thick brown tonkatsu sauce, you dip the freshly fried cutlet into this lighter, refreshing sauce until the breading just softens, which regulars find easy to eat even in summer. A signature option is the specially selected loin cutlet set using domestic three-breed pork, served with free refills of premium Nita rice from Okuizumo, miso soup and plenty of cabbage; you can add extra sauce, more grated daikon, or a quail egg for a small charge.

anecdotes.text: The 'Sachi-style' way to eat is to dip the cutlet into the secret sauce mixed with grated daikon and topped with a quail egg yolk, letting the crust soak slightly for a lighter, more refreshing bite.
Local food bloggers note the shop has built a loyal following specifically for its dip-in-the-sauce style of eating tonkatsu, distinct from other cutlet shops in town.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat-battered, panko-breaded deep-fried pork, so the fryer and kitchen handle gluten heavily; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat-focused tonkatsu kitchen with shared frying oil and pork-based dishes; no vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Cards and e-money are not accepted; bring cash (PayPay is available). Hours are consistent across listings except one source shows a slightly earlier weekend dinner close (21:00 vs 22:00) — confirm on arrival if going late on a weekend. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners, as the entire concept is deep-fried breaded pork. The 34-seat count exceeds the typical small mom-and-pop threshold, so categorized OMNI despite being a family-style independent shop.

cuisine: Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet) and yoshoku-style Western dishes; a long-running family-run shop
neighborhood: Takanobashi, Naka-ku (Kokutaiji-cho), central Hiroshima
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区国泰寺町2-4-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区国泰寺町2-4-4 returned [132.455215, 34.383293], within the Hiroshima box; lands in Kokutaiji-cho near Takanobashi as expected.
seats_text: 36 seats, all table seating
hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); closed Sundays
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A neighborhood tonkatsu shop where ordering is via a QR-code menu and weekday lunch sets are budget-friendly; a little Japanese or pointing at photos goes a long way, as English support is not advertised.
gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet) specialist. The breading is wheat flour and panko, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around pork cutlets and other meat-based fried dishes; tonkatsu sauce, miso soup, and sides typically contain animal products. No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Hours and seat count are from listing data and may change; phone 082-241-6585. Evening reservations and same-day/next-day takeout are available but not required for walk-in dining.

bio.background: This Takanobashi-area institution opened in 1954 as a coffee shop and, over the decades, transformed into a beloved local tonkatsu destination, now in its seventh decade of business. It remains a small, independently run neighborhood shop just south of the city center.
bio.origin: Founded in 1954 (Showa 29) as a kissaten (coffee shop) in Kokutaiji-cho before evolving into a tonkatsu specialist.
bio.specialty: Pork cutlets cut from premium heritage breeds, especially three-breed (sangen) pork and Kinka 'golden' pork from Hirata Farm in Yamagata, plus Shimane (Iwami) pork. The Yamagata pork is prized for its fine fiber, tender bite, and delicately melting fat. Weekday lunch sets are an exceptional value, and lunch comes with complimentary coffee, a nod to the shop's coffee-house roots.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on carefully sourced heritage pork and generous, comforting sets (free refills of rice, miso soup, and cabbage), keeping quality high while staying affordable for regulars.

anecdotes.text: Though it now serves almost exclusively tonkatsu, the shop began life as a coffee shop in 1954, and a free hot coffee still comes with the lunch sets as a quiet echo of that origin.
The signature pork comes from Hirata Farm in Yamagata Prefecture, with the top 'golden' grade described as having a silken texture and marbling that rivals fine beef.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded with wheat flour and panko and deep-fried; no gluten-free preparation or separate fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkatsu sauce and accompanying condiments customarily contain wheat-based soy sauce; no allergen breakdown is published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-centric (pork cutlets); shared frying oil and meat-based sides make it unsuitable for vegans, and no vegan items are documented.

lead: とんかつ専門 とんき
cuisine: とんかつ専門の個人店 (tonkatsu specialist)
gf_detail: A breaded-pork-cutlet specialist. Tonkatsu is pork dredged in wheat flour, egg, and panko breadcrumbs, deep-fried, and served with a wheat-and-soy-based tonkatsu sauce. The core menu is inherently not gluten-free and there is no documented gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiac/gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu centers on deep-fried pork cutlets fried in 100% lard (rendered pork fat), accompanied by pork-based miso/tonjiru soup. Everything is animal-based and fried in lard, so there are no vegan or vegetarian options. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区金屋町2-8
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 広島県広島市南区金屋町2番8号 = [132.472488, 34.392124], within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 南区・金屋町（稲荷町電停近く）
seats_text: 48席。奥に長いつくりで、カウンター約22席、4人掛けテーブル2卓、奥に最大16名収容の座敷あり。
closed_days: 日曜日、祝日、祝前日、祝後日 (Sundays, public holidays, and the days immediately before and after a holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, locally beloved neighborhood tonkatsu shop where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at set-meal names) is easiest; little English is expected, but the menu is simple and welcoming.
caveats: Closed days are unusually broad (Sundays plus holidays and the days before and after holidays), so check the calendar before visiting. No official website was found. Frying is done entirely in lard.

bio.background: A tonkatsu specialist that has been frying pork cutlets in the Kanayacho neighborhood near the Inaricho streetcar stop for around 45 years, long enough that for many locals the name has simply become shorthand for tonkatsu in Hiroshima. The shop runs deeper than it looks from the street, opening into a long counter, a couple of tables, and a tatami room in the back.
bio.specialty: Deep-fried pork cutlets fried in 100% lard for concentrated richness, served with Hiroshima-grown Koshihikari rice and a house-made sauce balanced between sweet and salty. Signature sets include the loin (rosu) and filet (hire) cutlet teishoku.
bio.philosophy: The owner describes his aim simply as wanting to make food that he himself finds delicious and that anyone who eats it will also find delicious.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen runs four separate oil pots, resting the oil between uses and refusing to crowd too many cutlets into one pot so the temperature never drops, which is how the cutlets come out audibly crisp on the outside yet tender inside.

A second shop operating under the same name exists in the western part of the city through a noren-wake (apprentice 'splitting the curtain') arrangement; these are the only two shops carrying the name.
Rice and the pork soup are refilled free on the first serving.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All cutlets are dredged in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried in shared lard oil pots; there is no gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Served with a house-made tonkatsu sauce, which is a standard wheat-and-soy-based sauce; not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Frying is done in 100% lard (rendered pork fat) across all four pots, so nothing fried here is vegan or vegetarian.

lead: ナカノターナーズ 本通り本店
name_ja: ナカノターナーズ 本通り本店
cuisine: 広島発祥の洋食・ステーキ（バル／焼肉）。名物ターナーズステーキを中心に世界各国の肉料理を提供
neighborhood: 中区・本通／立町（国際ホテル向かい、平和記念公園の東約800m）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通4-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区本通4-1 returned [132.459747, 34.393356], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: About 40 seats; 1F counter/terrace seating and a 2F floor that can be reserved for private parties of roughly 20-50 people.
hours_raw: Tabelog: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) / 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), closed Wednesdays. Hitosara lists lunch 12:00-14:00 and dinner 17:00-24:00 — hours vary by source.
closed_days: Wednesdays (per Tabelog; Hitosara lists open year-round except Dec 31 / Jan 1 — confirm directly)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local steak-and-Western spot where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm welcome; English support is limited.
gf_detail: This is a meat-focused Western/steakhouse with no documented gluten-free program. Steaks may be plain grilled beef, but sauces, marinades, and many sides typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce or flour, and no dedicated gluten-free handling or fryer is described. A celiac traveler should not rely on this place without a careful direct conversation.
vegan_detail: The concept is steak and meat dishes from around the world, so there is no documented vegan menu. Any vegetable sides are likely to be cooked with meat-based fats or seasonings. Not a suitable destination for vegan or vegetarian diners.
caveats: Tabelog currently flags this listing as 掲載保留 (listing on hold), but Hitosara, Yahoo Map and gnavi still list it as operating with an active phone number (082-577-5823) and an active Instagram, so it appears open; confirm before visiting. Business hours and closed days differ between sources. Dietary details (GF/vegan, cross-contamination) are inferred from the steak/Western concept, not from any published allergen statement.

bio.background: A central-Hiroshima Western restaurant built around steak, set on the city's lively Hondori shopping street opposite the International Hotel, a short walk east of Peace Memorial Park. It grew into a small local group with additional locations around the city.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the natural flavor of the meat itself, the kitchen gathers signature meat dishes from around the world rather than focusing on any single national style.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the Turners Steak, a generous, juicy cut praised for its hearty, meaty flavor; the menu ranges across steaks and other international meat dishes.

anecdotes.text: The shop's calling card is the namesake Turners Steak, described as a substantial, satisfying cut where the juices burst out as you eat.
Rather than one cuisine, the menu brings together representative meat dishes from countries around the world alongside the steaks.

cuisine: Handmade jūwari (100%) soba
gf_detail: This is a jūwari (100% buckwheat) soba-ya, meaning the noodles themselves use no wheat binder — but it is NOT a gluten-free establishment. The dipping tsuyu and broth are traditional soba sauces typically made with a soy-sauce base (soy sauce contains wheat), and the kitchen handles wheat-based items. There is no documented allergen protocol, dedicated equipment, or gluten-free claim. Celiac/gluten-sensitive diners should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: A traditional soba shop. The tsuyu/broth is standard Japanese soba sauce built on bonito/fish dashi, and signature dishes such as oyster nanban soba (牡蠣南蛮蕎麦) contain seafood. No vegan menu or plant-based dashi is documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区十日市町1-1-21
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address via GSI; result lands in Naka-ku Tokaichimachi, within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 中区十日市町 (Tokaichimachi, central Hiroshima, ~2 min walk from Tokaichimachi tram stop, west of Peace Memorial Park)
seats_text: 18 seats (some sources list 16); small, generously spaced table seating
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 daily; evening service 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30) on Monday and Thursday only (except holidays). Closes early when soba sells out. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesday; may also close early on any day once the day's soba sells out
cultural_comfort_note: A small, busy local soba shop with no reservations and frequent queues — basic Japanese helps, and it is best approached with patience and simple pointing-based ordering of black or white soba.

bio.background: A small independent soba-ya in central Hiroshima's Tokaichimachi district, a short walk from the Peace Memorial Park area, that opened in October 2016 and quickly became one of the city's most sought-after noodle destinations.
bio.specialty: 100% buckwheat (jūwari) soba made with no binder — stone-milled and milled in-house, offered as a fragrant whole-grain black soba (挽きぐるみ) or a refined hulled white soba (丸抜き), each served at a generous ~400g per portion.
bio.philosophy: Every dish is jūwari — the kitchen refuses binding flour and lets pure stone-ground buckwheat carry the flavor and aroma, milling its own grain and making the noodles by hand.

anecdotes.text: Demand is so high the shop runs on a sell-out basis and regularly hits capacity; one regular described finally getting in only on their fourth attempt, having once been cut off at the very person ahead of them in line.
It earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing in 2018, less than two years after opening, and continues to rank among Hiroshima's top soba shops in local polls.
The black (whole-grain) soba is widely recommended for letting diners fully experience the natural aroma of the buckwheat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Although the noodles are jūwari (100% buckwheat, no wheat binder), the shop is a general soba kitchen with no documented gluten-free protocol; cross-contact with wheat-based sauces and broths should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional soba tsuyu and broth are soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are built on fish dashi and the menu includes seafood dishes such as oyster nanban soba, so plant-based diners cannot assume vegan preparation.

positives.text: The soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder, which can be a starting point for a conversation about wheat — though the accompanying sauces are not wheat-free.

caveats: No reservations accepted; runs on a sell-out basis and frequently sells out / closes early, so arrive early. Seat count varies by source (16 vs 18). Evening service is limited to Monday and Thursday. Opening year (Oct 2016) and Michelin Bib Gourmand 2018 come from secondary summaries, not the restaurant's own site (no official site exists).

cuisine: Soba (buckwheat noodles), tempura, and Japanese sake bar; a dashi-forward noodle shop inside Hiroshima Station's ekie complex.
neighborhood: Matsubara-cho / Hiroshima Station (ekie NORTH 1F)
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町1-2 ekie NORTHエリア 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市南区松原町1-2 returned [132.475952, 34.398319], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the Hiroshima Station / ekie location.
seats_text: 38 seats
hours_raw: 月～日・祝日 11:00-21:30（L.O. 21:00）。休業はエキエの施設休館日に準ずる。
closed_days: No fixed weekly closing day; closes only on ekie's facility holidays.
cultural_comfort_note: A bright, casual station-mall soba counter used to tourists and solo diners; a few Japanese phrases help, and a clearly marked plant-based/halal soba option makes ordering easier for foreign visitors.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The noodles are 更科系 (Sarashina-style) soba, which is buckwheat blended with wheat flour, and the standard dashi/tsuyu and many dishes use wheat-based soy sauce; tempura, katsu, and similar fried items are battered/breaded in wheat. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free noodles, or celiac-safe handling is documented anywhere.
vegan_detail: The shop offers a documented plant-based / vegan-and-halal zaru soba: the dashi/tsuyu is made only from kombu, shiitake mushrooms and vegetables with no animal-derived ingredients (動物性不使用). It is available at this ekie Hiroshima Station location. Note that the soba noodles' exact composition and whether the noodle/tsuyu contain wheat are not disclosed, and the kitchen states the plant-based items are fried in the same oil as regular (non-vegan) menu items, so strict vegans should confirm details on-site. The rest of the menu (anago, oyster soba, tempura, pork katsu, bonito-kombu dashi) is not vegan.

roles: Operated by Miyoshiya Foods Co., Ltd. (三吉屋食品株式会社)
bio.background: This soba shop is the modern station-front sibling of a Hiroshima Station institution that dates to 1965, when Café 風車 (Fusha) — known for its parfaits and Western-style comfort food — opened in the original station building. The same family company later launched the soba side, 生そば食事処 水車 (Suisha), and on 29 March 2018 brought a contemporary version, DASHIとSOBA 水車, into the ekie complex right inside Hiroshima Station.
bio.specialty: Dashi-forward soba with a gentle, slightly sweet Kansai-leaning broth that is uncommon in Hiroshima, plus tempura, anago-don soba sets, oyster soba, and a Setouchi lemon soba; an evening side that doubles as a small Japanese-sake bar.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen draws a 一番出汁 (first-press broth) from three kinds of bonito-style shavings from the Kumamoto/Nagasaki seas combined with Rishiri kombu from Hokkaido, and pairs it with smooth, throat-pleasing Sarashina-style noodles — a craft the company says it has carried since its founding.

anecdotes.text: The house broth is built from three varieties of bonito shavings (Kumamoto/Nagasaki waters) plus Rishiri kombu from Hokkaido, drawn as a first-press 一番出汁.

A dedicated plant-based zaru soba, made with a kombu-shiitake-vegetable dashi and promoted as vegan- and halal-friendly, has drawn praise from diners who avoid animal products and from overseas visitors.
The business traces back to 1965, when sibling brand Café 風車 opened in the Hiroshima Station building; the ekie soba shop opened on 29 March 2018.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Noodles are Sarashina-style soba (buckwheat blended with wheat flour) and the standard broth uses wheat-based soy sauce, so a shared wheat-heavy kitchen makes this unsuitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature first-press dashi and tsuyu are built on bonito and kombu with soy-sauce seasoning typical of Japanese soba shops; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The plant-based items are fried in the same oil as the regular (non-vegan) menu: 通常メニューと同じ揚げ油を使用しています.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The company states infant-food and allergen accommodations may be possible on consultation (要相談), indicating staff will discuss dietary needs.

positives.text: A clearly labeled vegan/halal plant-based zaru soba is offered, with a dashi made only from kombu, shiitake and vegetables (動物性不使用), available at the Hiroshima Station ekie shop.

caveats: Vegan diners: a genuine plant-based (kombu/shiitake/vegetable dashi) zaru soba exists and is marketed as vegan/halal, but the noodle composition and tsuyu wheat content are not disclosed, and plant-based food shares fryer oil with non-vegan items — confirm on-site. Not gluten-free: Sarashina soba is wheat-blended and the kitchen is wheat-heavy. Cards, e-money and QR pay accepted.

lead: 朝日珈琲サロン 胡町店
name_ja: 朝日珈琲サロン 胡町店
cuisine: Kissaten (traditional Japanese coffee house) serving nel-drip coffee, morning sets, light meals, and cakes
gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings documented. The menu centers on wheat-based items — toast morning sets, sandwiches, curry with rice or cutlet, and cakes. There is no indication of gluten-free bread, dedicated preparation, or allergy accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Documented dishes rely on animal products — toast topped with nattō and dried shrimp, omelet curry (egg), sandwiches, and dairy-based drinks like milk tea and banana juice. No vegan menu or plant-based options are described.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町5-1 大内ビル 地階
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県広島市中区堀川町5-1 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Hatchobori.
neighborhood: 八丁堀・堀川町（胡町／恵美須通り商店街地階）
seats_text: 72 seats (60 table seats, 12 counter seats), non-smoking throughout
hours_raw: 8:00-19:00 daily. Morning service 8:00-11:30.
closed_days: 2nd and 3rd Wednesdays of each month; closed New Year's Day
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, retro Showa-era coffee house where you can sit alone with a magazine; a friendly greeting in Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will get you through comfortably, though staff are unlikely to speak much English.
caveats: Tabelog lists payment as accepting electronic money and PayPay QR; cash acceptance for a long-running kissaten is expected but not explicitly confirmed on the pages fetched. No English menu confirmed. No parking, Wi-Fi, or power outlets.

bio.background: Asahi Coffee Salon is a long-running Hiroshima coffee house founded in 1950, operated by the Asahi Coffee company. The Ebisucho branch occupies the basement of the Ouchi Building beneath the lively Ebisu-dori shopping arcade, a few steps from the Hatchobori tram stop. Descending below street level reveals a quiet, retro space that feels like a step back into the Showa era, a calm contrast to the bustle above.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as a bridge connecting past and present, delivering wholehearted hospitality and valuing history and storytelling. Its custom-made tables and deep, traditional chairs are built for relaxed, lingering comfort, and the morning service is framed around energizing the people of Hiroshima with a balanced start to the day.
bio.specialty: Nel-drip coffee and an original house blend known for deep flavor with smooth drinkability, served alongside morning sets, light meals, and cakes. A signature breakfast is the Health Set, with toast topped with natto, dried shrimp and green onion plus fresh shiso, cut into bite-size pieces; other favorites include the Asahi Set, sandwich set, omelet curry, cinnamon toast, and fruit sandwiches.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1950, the shop has operated for over 75 years, presenting itself as a place that bridges the past and present.
Beneath the busy Ebisu-dori arcade lies a serene, retro space evoking the Showa era, a stark contrast to the street-level commotion.

cuisine: 喫茶店 (kissaten / café) — morning sets, toast, homemade pudding, cake, coffee
neighborhood: 広島市中区十日市町 (Tokaichimachi, Naka-ku) — about a 5-minute walk west of the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park, near the Tokaichimachi tram stop
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区十日市町1-6-29
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.448639, 34.39608] for 広島県広島市中区十日市町1-6-29; within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Small space, roughly 20-22 seats (a couple of counter seats plus tables, with a small private room). Sources vary slightly (Tabelog lists 20; one blog cites 22).
hours_raw: Reported hours vary by source. Promotional listings (HotPepper / blog): morning 8:30–10:30 (L.O. 10:00), lunch & café 11:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:30), weekdays. Tabelog lists Mon–Sat 8:30–14:30. Other listings cite 8:00–18:00. Closed days also vary: Sundays + national holidays + irregular closures most commonly reported; some older listings cite Wednesday. Because of these discrepancies, confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Sundays and national holidays, plus irregular closures (announced on the shop's social media). Some sources also list Wednesday — hours are unsettled, so check ahead.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood café where Japanese is the working language and little to no English is expected, so come with a translation app or your guide's help — but the welcome is warm and ordering a morning set or pudding is easy enough to point to.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The menu centers on toast and toast morning sets, plus cakes/cheesecake and pudding, all of which are wheat-based. No sources mention any gluten-free options or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Morning and lunch sets feature eggs, bacon/sausage, yogurt, and butter on toast, and the signature dishes (pudding, cheesecake, French toast) are dairy- and egg-based. No vegan items or accommodations are documented.
caveats: Operating hours and closed days differ noticeably between sources (morning-only vs. through afternoon vs. all day; Sunday/holiday vs. Wednesday closures), and the café posts irregular closures on social media — verify the day before visiting. Cash only. The original task premise of "owner plus two female staff" could not be confirmed by any fetched source; staff structure is not documented, so it is not stated as fact below.

bio.background: A small, much-loved independent café in the Tokaichimachi neighborhood, a short walk west of the Peace Memorial Park. It is a renovated space with exposed-concrete floors and plaster walls, decorated with dried flowers, woven baskets, and postcards. Chairs reminiscent of an old elementary-school classroom and a little corner of books give it a quietly nostalgic mood — fitting for a place whose name, 'mekuru,' means to turn the pages of a book.
bio.specialty: Morning sets served on aluminum trays styled like school-lunch trays — thick toast (with butter, or cinnamon-sugar) paired with a boiled egg, a small soup, and homemade-style yogurt — alongside the house signature 'Mekuru pudding,' a firm custard with bitter caramel and cream. Cakes and pudding can be ordered even during morning hours.
bio.philosophy: A cozy, unhurried place to start the day; locals and commuters drop in before work for a relaxed morning.

anecdotes.text: Regulars praise how every small thing is thoughtfully made and charming — even the clip on the bill is described as cute — and the deeply flavored vegetable soup and homemade-style yogurt show care behind the simple presentation.
It sits about a five-minute walk from the A-Bomb Dome, making it an easy, calming morning stop for visitors to the Peace Park.

cuisine: 純喫茶・カフェ（レトロ喫茶、季節のフルーツパフェ・日替わりランチ）
neighborhood: 広島市中区大手町（紙屋町西駅すぐ、平和記念公園・原爆ドーム近く）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kissaten serving parfaits, daily-changing lunches and bento boxes. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation. Standard cafe fare here (lunch plates, bento, baked goods) typically involves wheat, and there is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation. Travelers requiring strict gluten avoidance should not rely on this spot without confirming directly.
vegan_detail: The signature parfaits use house-made dairy soft-serve and the lunches/bento include animal proteins; no documented vegan options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-4
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市中区大手町1-4-4 to [132.456131, 34.39476], within the Hiroshima box, near Kamiyacho-Nishi station and the Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: 55 seats (one source cites 62 total: 42 non-smoking, 20 smoking)
hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30), closed Wednesdays. Lunch bento takeout served roughly 11:00-15:00. (Some listings cite differing hours such as 8:00-21:00; current listing shows 9:00-18:00.)
closed_days: 水曜日 (Wednesday)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, family-friendly retro cafe steps from the Peace Memorial Park; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the photo-filled parfait menu will carry you through comfortably.
caveats: Hours vary slightly across listings (9:00-18:00 vs 8:00-21:00); the parfait selection rotates seasonally and popular fruits can sell out. Not suitable for strict vegan or gluten-free diets.

bio.background: A long-loved kissaten in central Hiroshima, founded in 1976 just a short stroll from the Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome. For nearly half a century it has been a steady neighborhood fixture, its storefront marked by a large white horse figurine that has become a local landmark and meeting point.
bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit parfaits layered with house-made dairy soft-serve (made without heavy cream) and homemade honey-lemon sauce, plus freshly made daily lunches and takeout bento. Seasonal creations have featured Hiroshima lemon, shine muscat, white peach with lavender, fig with Earl Grey, strawberry with pistachio, and chestnut with cassis.
bio.philosophy: An old-school cafe that pairs the calm, settled atmosphere of a veteran tea house with carefully composed, photogenic seasonal desserts meant to delight visitors of all ages.

anecdotes.text: The white horse figurine at the entrance holds a cup, and when children grow tall enough to peer inside, they find a hidden message reading 'You've grown big' (大きくなったね) — a small tradition tying the cafe to generations of local families.
The house soft-serve is made without heavy cream, giving the parfaits a lighter finish that has drawn a steady following, especially among women and dessert lovers.

cuisine: 純喫茶・カフェ（自家製プリン、フレッシュフルーツジュース、トースト・サンドイッチ）
neighborhood: 広島市中区三川町（並木通り・八丁堀エリア）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区三川町4-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県広島市中区三川町4-5 returned [132.4617, 34.390141], within the Hiroshima box. Source addresses vary slightly (4-5 vs 4-4 1F); used Tabelog's 4-5.
seats_text: 18席（全席テーブル）
closed_days: 月曜（Mondays）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, quiet retro coffeehouse run single-handedly, with no English menu and a cash-only, no-reservation, slow-paced style, so a foreign student should come patient, point at the picture menu, and have cash and a few Japanese phrases ready.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The menu centers on toast, sandwiches, and homemade bread (wheat), with no documented gluten-free options or allergen handling.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature items are dairy-rich (hard pudding, fruit yogurt morning set, milk/cream coffee drinks) and sandwiches use ham, egg, and butter; no documented plant-based options. Seasonal 100% fresh fruit juices are the only likely plant-based item, but this is not confirmed as a vegan accommodation.
caveats: Tabelog lists founding 1973 in the target brief, but multiple sources document 昭和50年（1975年）創業; record uses the better-sourced 1975. Address appears as 三川町4-5 (Tabelog) and 三川町4-4 1F (another source). One staff member cooks and serves, so waits can be long. No official website found.

bio.background: A long-loved retro coffeehouse in central Hiroshima's Namiki-dori area, open since 1975, set on the ground floor of a red-brick building marked by a blue-and-white striped awning.
bio.origin: 広島市中区三川町（並木通り）
bio.specialty: Fresh juice pressed from 100% seasonal fruit, paired with toast and sandwiches; a much-loved firm homemade pudding; and a morning set of half-sandwiches with coffee or tea and fruit yogurt.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried, elegant refuge where time slows down — the kitchen and floor are handled by a single person, so service is gentle and leisurely by design.

anecdotes.text: The interior wraps guests in an English-vintage world: distinctive British-fabric-style wallpaper, stained-glass windows, and soft jazz, with coffee served in high-end porcelain such as Royal Copenhagen Flora Danica, Herend Rothschild Bird, and Blue Danube blue onion — drawing ceramics lovers as much as coffee drinkers.
Every window is rendered in a stained-glass style, giving the small room a clean, nostalgic retro feel under quiet jazz.

cuisine: Kissaten (retro Japanese coffee house) — coffee, toast/morning sets, hot cakes, cream soda
gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings. The menu centers on wheat-based items — toast morning sets, hot sandwiches, and hot cakes (pancakes) are all made with wheat flour. No gluten-free preparations or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a traditional kissaten built around coffee and toast: morning sets come with buttered toast (cheese or ham options), salad with house dressing, hot cakes, and cream soda (which uses ice cream/dairy). No documented plant-based or dairy-free options. Black coffee is the only reliably animal-free item.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区立町3-4 returned [132.460434, 34.393627], within the Hiroshima box. Located in central Hiroshima near the Tatemachi tram stop, a short walk from Hondori arcade.
neighborhood: 広島市中区立町 (Tatemachi, Naka-ku) — near Tatemachi tram stop, off Hondori arcade
seats_text: 41 seats (9 counter, 32 table)
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 07:00-19:30; Sat 07:00-17:30; Sun & holidays 08:00-17:30. Morning service 07:00-11:00 (Sun/holidays from 08:00).
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, old-fashioned neighborhood coffee house where a smile and a simple Japanese greeting go a long way; pointing at the menu works fine, but don't expect much English.

bio.background: Bora Bora opened in 1976 and is one of Hiroshima's enduring jun-kissa (classic coffee houses). The chain once had several locations around the city; the Tatemachi shop is the one that remains, tucked down a side street near the Hondori arcade behind a modest storefront that opens into a warm, brick-walled interior.
bio.specialty: Freshly roasted house-blend coffee and inexpensive morning sets. The original blend is made from five carefully selected beans for a mild, well-balanced cup with gentle bitterness and acidity. Toast morning sets, hot cakes, hot sandwiches, and a retro cream soda round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed, unhurried refuge from the morning bustle — opening at 7am so locals, office workers, and early risers can start the day with good coffee in a nostalgic Showa-era room.

anecdotes.text: On weekday afternoons the owner fires up the roaster behind the counter, and the smell of roasting beans drifts out and draws passers-by inside.
The house-made salad dressing that comes with the morning sets became so popular with regulars that the shop began selling it separately by request.
Originally part of a small group of Bora Bora cafes around Hiroshima, the Tatemachi branch is the sole survivor of that era.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is everywhere in this kitchen — toast, hot sandwiches, and hot cakes are all wheat-based and prepared in the same small space, so gluten avoidance is not realistic here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Toast is served buttered and morning/cream-soda items use dairy; no plant-based menu or separate preparation is documented, so cross-contact with dairy and egg should be assumed.

positives.text: House blend coffee is roasted in-house from five selected beans and can be ordered black, making plain coffee a safe choice for most diners.

caveats: Owner details (the husband-and-wife operation noted in the target brief) are not confirmed in the sources fetched; the well-documented facts are the 1976 founding, in-house roasting, and that the Tatemachi branch is the last remaining Bora Bora. Closed days are irregular (不定休) per the morning-cafe source. Payment includes cashless options (PayPay), so not cash-only.

cuisine: Washoku (Japanese) izakaya; lunch specialty of onigiri (rice balls) and miso soup teishoku, dinner of seasonal seafood, vegetables and sake
neighborhood: Kuchi-machi / Ginzan-cho, near Hatchobori, central Hiroshima (Naka Ward)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町2-24 エントリービル5F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市中区胡町2-24; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Hatchobori/Ginzan-cho.
seats_text: Small space of roughly 14-15 seats with counter, table and private-room seating; reported as 14 in one diner account and 15 in the listing.
hours_raw: Lunch Mon-Sat 10:00-14:30 (L.O. 14:00). Dinner: Wed-Thu 18:00-21:00 (L.O.), Fri-Sat 18:00-22:00/23:00 (Fri-Sat per listing). Saturday morning service 7:30-10:00. Closed Sunday. (Official site and Tabelog differ slightly on dinner days; listing shows Mon-Thu dinner 18:00-22:00, Fri-Sat 18:00-23:00.)
closed_days: Sundays (occasional Monday holiday closures); dinner not served every weekday and a Saturday-only morning service make hours irregular.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, popular owner-run spot where most communication is in Japanese and lunch lines form, so basic Japanese and patience help, though the friendly owner will show you how to eat the onigiri.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes rely on Japanese soy sauce and miso (typically made with wheat), an original blended dashi using fish, and seasoned/tempura onigiri (tempura batter is wheat). No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. The kitchen centers on fresh seafood (live anago/conger eel, oysters, prawns), Miyazaki beef and pork, and a fish-based dashi; even the miso soups and onigiri feature meat or fish (e.g. pork-belly miso soup, niku-miso and grilled-mackerel onigiri). Some vegetable side dishes exist but are not prepared as a vegan menu.

bio.origin: A small washoku izakaya in central Hiroshima's Kuchi-machi district.
bio.background: Originally an evening izakaya, the shop pivoted during the pandemic to add morning and lunch service. Its onigiri-and-miso-soup teishoku became so popular that diners now queue outside, where staff hand out cold green tea and a fan while they wait.
bio.specialty: A build-your-own lunch set where you pick from about ten kinds of freshly finished onigiri and miso soups (such as a hearty pork-and-vegetable soup or a summer soy-milk tomato-and-pork-belly soup, and onigiri from niku-miso to corn tempura), served with around five seasonal side dishes. Dinner showcases seasonal seafood and vegetables.
bio.philosophy: The owner prizes ingredients at their fleeting peak (noting asparagus is only at its best for about two weeks) and seasons lightly to let the produce speak. Vegetables are a focal point, seafood is sourced direct from Osaka's central market, beef is exclusively Miyazaki, and the dashi follows an original blended recipe.

anecdotes.text: The owner personally teaches diners the proper way to eat the onigiri: halve it, tuck the fillings inside, wrap it in seaweed, and eat it right away.
On busy days guests waiting outside are looked after with cold green tea and a fan.
The owner illustrates the focus on seasonality by saying asparagus is only at its best for about two weeks.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and miso, which are typically wheat-containing; no gluten-free seasonings are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen uses a fish-based dashi and prepares seafood and meat alongside vegetable dishes, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

caveats: Official site and Tabelog list slightly different dinner days/hours (official: dinner Wed-Thu and Fri-Sat plus a Saturday 7:30-10:00 morning service; Tabelog: Mon-Thu and Fri-Sat dinner). The hours_week reflects a best-effort merge and should be reconfirmed by phone. Seat count reported as 14-15. No documented allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodations; assessments are inferred from documented ingredients.

lead: ピース ブルバード
name_ja: ピース ブルバード
cuisine: Onigiri cafe / shokudo (rice-ball-focused Japanese lunch)
neighborhood: 大手町 (Otemachi), Naka-ku — central Hiroshima, near Peace Memorial Park / A-Bomb Dome
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町2-11-15 新大手町ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address via GSI; coordinates (lat 34.390484, lng 132.454849) fall within the Hiroshima box and match Otemachi 2-chome near Peace Memorial Park.
seats_text: Small casual cafe with counter seating that comfortably accommodates solo diners; exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Weekends/Holidays (Sat-Sun): 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30)
closed_days: Not specified
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly spot right by Peace Park where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; ordering a set lunch is straightforward and the counter seating welcomes solo visitors.
gf_detail: Plain rice onigiri are naturally gluten-free, but many fillings, the side dishes (simmered hijiki, pickles), and accompaniments are seasoned with regular Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. Weekend dishes include items like fried oyster cutlets (breaded, wheat) and conger eel preparations. No dedicated gluten-free menu or preparation is documented, so a celiac or strict GF diner would need to ask carefully and may have very limited safe options.
vegan_detail: Rice itself is plant-based, but the cafe is built around onigiri with typical fillings and Japanese sides that commonly involve fish-based dashi, fish/seafood (Hiroshima oyster cutlets, conger eel), and wheat-containing soy sauce. Pork soup is on the lunch set. A vegan could potentially find a plain or umeboshi/kombu onigiri, but there is no documented vegan menu and dashi/soy use is widespread, so options are limited and require asking.
caveats: Tabelog lists weekday hours as 11:00-14:00 (lunch only) and weekend hours as 10:00-17:00; one blog references earlier evening service to 8 PM, but that appears to predate current operations, so the Tabelog hours are treated as current. Closed days and exact seat count are not documented. Weekday set lunch is roughly 860-990 yen depending on source/date.

bio.background: Peace Blvd is the eat-in cafe arm of the Hiroshima onigiri specialist Nitaya (仁多屋). It opened in 2020 inside the Shin-Otemachi Building, sharing the premises with Nitaya's takeout flagship in Otemachi but operating as its own business. Sitting a short walk from Peace Memorial Park and the A-Bomb Dome, it draws both Hiroshima locals and travelers looking for a calm, sit-down Japanese lunch.
bio.origin: Hiroshima (Otemachi), with rice sourced from Okuizumo, Shimane Prefecture
bio.specialty: Onigiri made with premium 仁多米 (Nita rice), served as carefully composed lunch sets with seasonal sides and soup
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on quality rice and a thoughtfully prepared, homestyle Japanese lunch that elevates the humble onigiri well above convenience-store fare.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's onigiri are made with 仁多米, a prized rice from the Okuizumo region of Shimane, which the shop uses to distinguish its rice balls from ordinary convenience-store versions through superior grain quality and ingredient selection.

Weekday set lunches pair onigiri with homestyle sides such as simmered hijiki, assorted pickles, and a pork/miso soup, while weekends offer a build-your-own Nitaya onigiri set and regional dishes like Hiroshima oyster cutlets and conger eel.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Onigiri fillings and homestyle side dishes (such as simmered hijiki and pickles) are typically seasoned with regular Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu mixes seafood and meat items (Hiroshima oyster cutlets, conger eel, pork soup) alongside rice dishes, so shared preparation and fish-based dashi are likely; no separate vegan handling is documented.

positives.text: Rice is the centerpiece, so a plain or simply-filled onigiri offers a naturally gluten-free, plant-based base if confirmed with staff; the casual counter setting makes it easy to ask about a single item.

cuisine: Bakery-cafe (stone-oven bread, pan gratin, sandwiches, lunch sets)
neighborhood: 中区八丁堀 (Hatchobori intersection, central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町10-15
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町10-15 returned [132.46376, 34.393932], within the Hiroshima box; matches the Hatchobori-intersection location across from the Tenmaya department store.
seats_text: 24-seat basement cafe space (roughly ten tables), all non-smoking
hours_raw: Per official site: 9:00-18:00 (L.O. 16:30); Thursday short closure 14:00-15:00; lunch menu 11:00-14:30. Closed Sundays and holidays. (Tabelog lists weekday 10:00-19:30 / Sat 10:00-19:00 for this branch; hours vary by source.)
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy, unpretentious neighborhood bakery-cafe run by a small team; menus and ordering are in Japanese, so a little basic Japanese (or pointing at the popular gratin ring set) will help, but the welcoming counter staff make it easy for a first-time foreign visitor.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-bread bakery: the signature gratin ring, curry ring, sandwiches, and pasta/lunch sets are all built on wheat flour, and the bechamel-style gratin sauce is flour-thickened. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The signature pan gratin (gratin ring) is filled with shrimp, bacon, dairy bechamel and cheese; other sets include omurice and meat/seafood items, and breads are made with dairy (milk bread). No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Hours differ between the official site (9:00-18:00, with a Thursday 14:00-15:00 break) and Tabelog (10:00-19:30 weekdays); confirm on arrival. Bread is baked at the Minamitakeya main shop and delivered to this branch. Cash only. The Minamitakeya main location was under renovation as of early 2026, but the Hatchobori branch remains open.

bio.background: MON is a long-established Hiroshima bakery founded in 1953 (the brand describes itself as a roughly 65-year-old shop). The Hatchobori branch sits in the basement of a building right at the busy Hatchobori intersection in central Naka Ward, across from the Tenmaya department store, and has been quietly cherished by locals as one of the increasingly rare small neighborhood bakery-cafes.
bio.specialty: Stone-oven bread and the cafe's signature pan gratin: a milk-bread ring hollowed out and filled with rich shrimp-and-bacon bechamel, topped with cheese and baked until golden. The curry ring (bread with a hidden curry filling) is the other house favorite, and the Hatchobori branch alone offers daily pasta lunch sets and omurice.
bio.philosophy: The shop bakes on a French stone oven it has used since its founding, aiming for bread with a crisp crust and moist interior, and frames its bread as a staple to be enjoyed in place of rice, with carefully chosen ingredients and slow, deliberate baking.

anecdotes.text: A visitor described an intimate basement space of about ten tables where a male chef works the counter with a welcoming manner while a single female server runs the unhurried dining room; most lunch guests order the gratin ring set, and the place stays calm despite a steady stream of solo diners and local office workers.

Bread for the cafe is baked at MON's main Minamitakeya shop and delivered to Hatchobori within about 15 minutes, with roughly 30-40 varieties on offer, and weekend takeaway bread sold near street level.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working bakery where wheat bread is the core product and the cafe builds nearly every dish on bread or flour-thickened sauce; flour is pervasive and there is no documented gluten-free handling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use shrimp, bacon, dairy bechamel and cheese, and breads are made with milk; no plant-based or allergen-segregated preparation is documented.

lead: ブランジェリー 101ブリオ
name_ja: ブランジェリー 101ブリオ
cuisine: パン・ベーカリー (boulangerie / French-style bakery)
gf_detail: This is a traditional wheat-flour boulangerie — baguettes, croissants, danishes, cream pan and other breads are all made with wheat. No gluten-free products or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan offerings are documented. Most items use dairy and eggs (e.g. the signature vanilla-bean cream pan, danishes, meat-and-cheese breads). A plain baguette or some lean European-style loaves may happen to be egg/dairy-free, but this is not confirmed, so a vegan diner would need to ask about specific loaves in person.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町1-25
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区鉄砲町1-25 returned [132.465408, 34.397522], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in central Naka-ku near Jogakuin-mae tram stop.
neighborhood: 中区鉄砲町 (Teppocho, near Hatchobori / Jogakuin-mae, central Hiroshima)
seats_text: Takeaway bakery; no seating documented (bread purchased to go).
hours_raw: 月～土 09:00～16:00、日曜定休 (relocated 鉄砲町 location, opened Aug 2025). Note: the former Hashimoto-cho shop was listed as 7:00–20:00; current listing shows 09:00–16:00.
closed_days: 日曜日 (Sunday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood bakery where you simply pick up bread and pay in cash — pointing at what you want works fine, though menus and staff are likely Japanese-only.
caveats: The shop relocated within central Naka-ku (to 鉄砲町) and reopened in August 2025; some online listings still show the former Hashimoto-cho address and older 7:00–20:00 hours. Cash only — no cards, e-money or QR payment. Confirm current hours by phone (082-222-8002) before a special trip.

bio.background: A long-running independent boulangerie founded around 2004 and operated by the small local company Brio, this is a neighborhood institution that relocated within central Hiroshima and reopened in its current spot in 2025. For roughly two decades it has been the kind of everyday bakery locals rely on, turning out a wide range of European-style breads and pastries.
bio.specialty: The signature item is its cream pan — a mushroom-shaped bun filled with vanilla-bean-flecked custard cream — alongside baguettes, croissants, danishes and savory meat-and-cheese breads.
bio.philosophy: Traditional European baking methods with house-made fillings and sauces, aiming for an everyday bakery that offers a full, varied selection rather than a niche.

anecdotes.text: At its previous location the shop was beloved for staying open late, so commuters could still find a wide selection of fresh bread in the evening when most bakeries had sold out or closed.
The cream pan is treated as the shop's emblem, with in-store signage marking it as the signature offering and its cream made with real vanilla beans.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a working wheat-flour bakery; flour dust and shared surfaces make heavy gluten cross-contact unavoidable. Not appropriate for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and pastries are produced together using shared dough, ovens and equipment; dairy- and egg-containing items (cream pan, danishes) share the same production line as any plainer loaves.

lead: 広島赤焼 えん 駅西本店
name_ja: 広島赤焼 えん 駅西本店
cuisine: お好み焼き・牡蠣・鉄板居酒屋
neighborhood: 南区・エキニシ (Ekinishi, just west of Hiroshima Station)
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区大須賀町13-19
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 大須賀町13-19, within the Hiroshima box. Lands in Ekinishi just west of Hiroshima Station, consistent with the listed neighborhood.
seats_text: 51 seats total: 1F has 7 teppan counter seats; 2F has 44 table seats with private rooms (for roughly 24, 12, and 8 guests). Max group reservation around 26.
hours_raw: 月・水〜土 17:30〜22:00 (L.O. food 20:30 area); 火・日 定休。Sources differ slightly: official site lists close at 22:00 / last entry 20:30; aggregator lists 17:30-20:30.
closed_days: Tuesdays and Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local teppan izakaya where a few basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will carry you comfortably; English support is limited.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter and finished with wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauce, and shared dishes like gansu (breaded fried paste) and various teppan items also use wheat. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen centers on oysters, pork, koune (beef neck), gansu (fish-paste fritter), and seafood, with dashi, egg, and wheat-soy sauces throughout. No vegan dishes are documented.

bio.background: A teppan izakaya in the Ekinishi dining alleys just west of Hiroshima Station, set across two floors: a small teppan counter on the ground floor and table seating with private rooms upstairs. The concept is to let visitors enjoy a casual, all-in-one taste of Hiroshima.
bio.specialty: Oyster-topped Hiroshima okonomiyaki is the signature, most famously the 'Mashimashi Kaki' okonomiyaki piled with around 15 to 20 oysters. The kitchen also leans hard into other local favorites: gansu (fried fish-paste fritter), koune (grilled beef neck), and a range of oyster preparations from classic kaki-butter to Hiroshima-lemon steaming.
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is 'kigaru ni oishiku Hiroshima-zukushi' — to casually and deliciously serve a full spread of Hiroshima, focusing on the dishes locals love, with carefully sourced ingredients ('tenshu kodawari no shiire') and local sake to match.

anecdotes.text: The standout dish is the 'Mashimashi Kaki' okonomiyaki, a hot teppan okonomiyaki crowned with a mound of about 15 to 20 oysters.
The restaurant frames itself around 'kaki-oko' (oyster okonomiyaki) and other Hiroshima ground-foods, served teppanyaki style in the Ekinishi alley scene.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki and teppan izakaya dishes here are finished with standard wheat-based okonomiyaki/soy sauces; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

caveats: Closed days and exact last-order times vary slightly between the official site (close 22:00, last entry 20:30) and aggregator listings (17:30-20:30); confirm before visiting. Seat layout described as 7 teppan counter (1F) plus 44 table (2F). No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented, so dedicated_fryer and cross-contamination details are unknown.

lead: たけはらの味 ぴぃす
name_ja: たけはらの味 ぴぃす
cuisine: Owner-run izakaya specializing in Takehara and Hiroshima produce — Etajima oysters, seafood, sashimi, the signature negi-shabu (white-scallion and Seto Momiji pork shabu-shabu), Takehara Toge-shita beef, and local Hiroshima specialties like uni-horen. Sake exclusively from three breweries in the owner's hometown of Takehara.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen builds around a soy-sauce-based bonito dashi (the signature negi-shabu broth), and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat. The uni-horen is served on a toasted baguette (wheat), and izakaya dishes generally use wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on oysters, sea urchin, sashimi and other seafood, pork (negi-shabu), and Takehara beef, with a fish-based (bonito) dashi running through the cooking. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町10-31
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 広島県広島市南区松原町10-31; within the Hiroshima validation box, just east of Hiroshima Station (Ekinishi / station-west area).
neighborhood: 南区松原町・エキニシ（駅西）, about a 5-minute walk from the south exit of Hiroshima Station
seats_text: 14 seats total — 4 at the counter plus two tables seating 2-4 each.
hours_raw: 17:00–24:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drinks L.O. 23:30). Tabelog lists 17:00–23:00. Closed Thursdays.
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny 14-seat owner-run izakaya where ordering and conversation will largely be in Japanese, so basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help; reservations by phone are advisable since seating is very limited.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources: the official site states 17:00–24:00 (food last order 23:00) while Tabelog lists 17:00–23:00 — confirm by phone. Reservations are accepted (and recommended given only 14 seats); same-day cancellations are charged 100% of the meal cost. Card payment (VISA/Master/JCB/AMEX) and cash are both accepted.

bio.background: The owner grew up in Takehara, a historic port town on the Seto Inland Sea, and built this small izakaya in the lively Ekinishi district just west of Hiroshima Station as a way of bringing his hometown's flavors to the city.
bio.specialty: The house signature is negi-shabu: East Hiroshima white scallions paired with Seto Momiji pork, swished through a house-made, ginger-accented bonito dashi with a slightly sweet soy base. Etajima oysters arrive large, intensely flavored and milky; other draws include uni-horen (sea urchin and Hiroshima spinach on toasted baguette) and koune, a rare cut of Takehara beef regarded locally as a hidden signature.
bio.philosophy: Rather than serving generic 'Hiroshima products,' the owner chooses the finest ingredient from the right corner of the prefecture for each dish, because flavor varies by where things are grown and raised. The sake list reflects the same homecoming spirit: only sake from three breweries in his native Takehara, including seasonal limited releases.

anecdotes.text: The negi-shabu broth is a house-made, ginger-spiked bonito dashi with a soy base built to coax sweetness out of East Hiroshima white scallions and Seto Momiji pork.
The sake program stocks only the three breweries of the owner's hometown, Takehara, including seasonal limited bottles for comparison tasting.
Koune, a rare Takehara beef cut, is described as a 'hidden signature dish' of the house.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature negi-shabu uses a soy-sauce-based bonito dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the broth and similarly seasoned dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cooking is built on a fish (bonito) dashi and the menu is dominated by oysters, sea urchin, pork and beef, so vegan dishes are not available and shared broths/equipment make plant-only meals impractical.

lead: メロンパン 広島福屋八丁堀店
name_ja: メロンパン 広島福屋八丁堀店
cuisine: パン・ベーカリー
neighborhood: 中区胡町・八丁堀
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町6-26 福屋八丁堀本店 B1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島市中区胡町6-26 via GSI; lands within the central Hiroshima box near Fukuya Hatchobori department store.
seats_text: No seating — a takeaway bread counter on the basement food floor (B1F) of the Fukuya Hatchobori department store.
hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sun 10:00-19:30; Fri-Sat 10:00-20:00. Closed days follow the Fukuya Hatchobori department store. Note: the signature melon pan is not stocked on Sundays.
closed_days: No fixed regular holiday; follows the Fukuya Hatchobori department store's calendar (occasional irregular closures).
cultural_comfort_note: A simple department-store bread counter where you point at what you want and pay — no Japanese conversation needed, though staff likely speak little English.
vegan_detail: The signature rugby-ball melon pan is filled with homemade custard-style cream and the breads are enriched dough, so they are not vegan; the lineup also includes meat and fish sandwiches (croquette, chicken nanban, ebi katsu, gyu katsu). No vegan items are documented.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery — every product (melon pan, sweet rolls, sandwiches) is made from wheat dough. There are no gluten-free options.
caveats: This is a takeaway bread counter inside a department store basement, not a sit-down restaurant. The melon pan is baked at the Kure main shop and delivered to this counter; note the signature melon pan is not restocked on Sundays. Coordinates are chō-level for the Fukuya building.

bio.background: This counter on the basement food floor of the Fukuya Hatchobori department store sells the breads of a Kure institution founded in 1936 (Showa 11). The shop is approaching its 90th anniversary and is run by the founding family, now in its third generation under Ryuma Nakashio, with bread still baked at the Kure main shop and delivered fresh to outlets across Hiroshima Prefecture.
bio.specialty: The famous rugby-ball-shaped melon pan: a long oval loaf with a crisp biscuit crust outside and soft sweet bread filled with homemade cream inside. The shop now makes around 40 kinds of sweet rolls and sandwiches.
bio.philosophy: Keeping the founder's original method unchanged and aiming for simple, natural, nostalgic flavors.

anecdotes.text: The founder created the distinctive rugby-ball melon pan because melons were an expensive luxury at the time, and he wanted ordinary people to be able to enjoy the idea of melon; the shape was inspired by makuwa-uri (Oriental melon).
The melon pan and related products are registered trademarks, and the bakery is approaching its 90th year in business.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All products are wheat-flour breads made and handled together; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sweet breads use enriched dough and cream fillings, and the counter shares space and handling with meat and fish sandwiches, so no vegan-safe product can be assured.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き (Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki)
neighborhood: 中区幟町 (Noboricho), near Jogakuin-mae / Hatchobori
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町3-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区幟町3-1 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box, roughly 3 min walk from Jogakuin-mae and near the Peace Memorial Park area.
seats_text: 22 seats total: 6 counter seats and 16 seats at tatami (zashiki) tables.
hours_raw: 定休日: 日曜日 (closed Sundays). Open for both lunch and dinner; dinner offers all-you-can-drink courses. Exact daily open/close times are not published on Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, or Hitosara; the listings advise confirming hours with the shop directly (082-222-2139).
closed_days: Sundays (日曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood okonomiyaki shop where staff cook on the teppan in front of you; little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese or pointing at the menu will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and yakisoba/udon noodles, the okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat, and the signature karaage is wheat/starch-battered fried chicken. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. Okonomiyaki here uses egg, pork, and bonito/fish-based dashi and toppings; the menu centers on meat, fried chicken (karaage), shrimp tartar, and beef tendon. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: A small, independent okonomiyaki spot tucked into the Noboricho neighborhood of central Hiroshima, a short walk from Jogakuin-mae and the Hatchobori area and within reach of the Peace Memorial Park district. The staff cook authentic Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki right in front of you on the teppan.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (layered with noodles, cabbage, and egg) is the centerpiece, but the shop is quietly known for its karaage (fried chicken), treated by regulars as a hidden specialty alongside teppan dishes like shrimp tartar and beef-tendon ponzu.

anecdotes.text: Although it is first and foremost a Hiroshima okonomiyaki shop, its karaage (fried chicken) is repeatedly singled out as a surprising hidden specialty.
Beyond okonomiyaki, the shop serves a range of teppan dishes and offers evening all-you-can-drink courses, making it a relaxed spot for groups of family or friends.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The teppan is used for wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles, so any non-batter item cooked on the same surface would share heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard okonomiyaki sauce and seasonings used at Hiroshima okonomiyaki shops are wheat-based; no wheat-free sauce is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared teppan alongside pork, egg, and seafood, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

caveats: Exact daily opening/closing times are not published on any of the listing sites (Tabelog, Hot Pepper, Gnavi, Hitosara); only the Sunday closure is confirmed, so hours_week days are left blank rather than guessed and hours_status is marked irregular. The Facebook page (facebook.com/amanjyaku) is the only official-ish web presence found. Payment: cards and QR-code payments accepted, electronic money not accepted; no documentation of reservation requirement, and price band (~3,000-3,999 yen dinner) suggests it may exceed a 3,000 yen budget, so under30 is unsure. Note: name_en is a romanization (Amanjaku); the kanji 天邪鬼 reads 'amanojaku/amanjaku'.

cuisine: Hiroshima ramen / chuka soba (late-night ramen shop with a chicken-and-pork double broth, plus house-made oden)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles (the shop runs its own noodle-making operation, 隅吉製麺), and the broth and accompaniments rely on standard Japanese seasonings including soy sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Oden is typically simmered in a soy-sauce/dashi broth and is not gluten-free either.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature broth is built on chicken and pork bones, and other menu items (gyoza, oden) contain animal products. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町4-2 新田ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the Nagarekawacho 4-2 block in central Hiroshima; within the expected Hiroshima bounding box.
neighborhood: Nagarekawa / Yagenbori entertainment district, central Hiroshima (Naka Ward), a short walk from the Ebisucho tram stop and within the central downtown area near Peace Memorial Park / the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 17 seats (9 counter seats, 8 table seats)
closed_days: Sundays (also open on public holidays during the week)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, late-night local ramen counter in a nightlife district where staff likely speak mainly Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help, but pointing at the menu works fine.
caveats: Late-night only: opens at 19:00 and runs until 6:00 the next morning, so it is an after-dark spot rather than a lunch option. Closed Sundays. Just 17 seats and no reservations, so expect to wait or share the counter at peak times. The name is written 隅吉 but read "Sumiyoshi."

bio.background: Sumiyoshi opened in 2018 in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa nightlife quarter with the stated goal of creating a new style of Hiroshima ramen. It is an independent shop that even runs its own noodle-making operation (Sumiyoshi Seimen) at a separate location in the city's Minami Ward, so the noodles served are made in-house.
bio.specialty: A double broth simmered from both chicken and pork bones, described as rich but with a clean, moreish finish; alongside the ramen, the shop is known for its carefully developed house-made oden.
bio.philosophy: To create a new kind of Hiroshima ramen rather than copy existing styles, pairing an original double-stock broth with noodles made by the shop itself.

anecdotes.text: As a late-night spot in the Nagarekawa entertainment district, it serves from evening through until early morning, making it a go-to bowl after a night out.
The shop produces its own noodles under the Sumiyoshi Seimen name at a separate facility in Minami Ward.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is centered on wheat ramen noodles boiled in shared water, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ramen and oden in this style are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is made with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature broth is made from chicken and pork bones, so it is not vegan and meat stock is present throughout the kitchen.

name_ja: 壽ゑ廣餃子 立町店
cuisine: Gyoza izakaya — house-made yakuzen (medicinal-herb) fresh dumplings, with brothless tantanmen and izakaya small plates
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is gyoza, whose wrappers are made from wheat flour, and the brothless tantanmen is a wheat-noodle dish. Standard izakaya seasonings (soy sauce, miso) here also typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The house specialty gyoza are filled with pork mince infused with a medicinal-herb broth made with Chinese miso, and the menu centers on meat and izakaya fare. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 〒730-0032 広島県広島市中区立町1-12 イナリビル1F
neighborhood: 中区立町（本通・八丁堀近く）
geocode_note: Geocoded to the chō-level address 広島県広島市中区立町1-12; result is within the Hiroshima box and lands in central Naka-ku near Hondori/Hatchobori, within the A-Bomb Dome area.
seats_text: Listed as 40 seats (one source lists 36); counter and table seating that accommodates solo diners and groups.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:45–13:30), Dinner 17:00–23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30). Sources differ on whether lunch is served daily or weekdays only; one source indicates weekend lunch is not offered and Sunday may be temporarily closed.
closed_days: Officially no regular closing day (無休), though one listing notes Sunday may be temporarily closed and weekend lunch may not be served — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, welcoming gyoza izakaya about a minute from the Tachimachi tram stop where solo diners are common; a little spoken Japanese helps, but pointing at the gyoza menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will get you by.
caveats: Hours and closing days are inconsistent across listings (daily lunch vs. weekday-only lunch; possible temporary Sunday closure) — confirm directly before a special trip. Not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diners.

bio.background: A casual neighborhood izakaya in central Hiroshima, a minute's walk from the Tachimachi tram stop, built around one obsession: house-made fresh gyoza. The dumplings are wrapped by hand each day for a chewy, springy skin.
bio.specialty: The signature is 薬膳生餃子 (yakuzen fresh gyoza): a pork-heavy filling worked together with an original medicinal-herb broth that is slow-simmered from roughly eight spices — star anise, dried citrus peel (陳皮), cinnamon bark (桂皮), Sichuan/flower pepper (花山椒) and more — blended with Chinese miso. Two styles are made for side-by-side tasting: 黒餃子 (with garlic and Chinese chives) and 白餃子 (without). The kitchen also turns out Hiroshima-style brothless tantanmen in three spice levels and a lineup of medicinal-herb sours.
bio.philosophy: A play on 'health and beauty through food' — the herbs and spices folded into both the dumplings and the drinks are meant to make a hearty izakaya meal feel a little restorative.

anecdotes.text: The medicinal broth at the heart of the gyoza is cooked from about eight spices including star anise, dried citrus peel, cinnamon bark and flower pepper, then blended with Chinese miso before being worked into the pork filling, while the wrappers are folded by hand for a chewy bite.
Guests are encouraged to order both the black (garlic-and-chive) and white (no garlic or chive) gyoza to taste them against each other, alongside four signature medicinal-herb sours.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wheat-based gyoza and wheat-noodle tantanmen, so a shared kitchen would involve heavy flour handling; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Gyoza dipping sauce and izakaya seasonings at a shop like this customarily use standard soy sauce and miso, which contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature gyoza are filled with pork and Chinese miso, and the menu is meat-centered, so no vegan-safe preparation is documented.

cuisine: 焼き鳥 (yakitori / charcoal-grilled chicken) and small Japanese plates
neighborhood: 中区立町 (Tatemachi, central Hiroshima, near Hondori/Hatchobori)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町5-20
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区立町5-20 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central-Hiroshima box near Tatemachi tram stop.
seats_text: 10 counter seats only
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sundays plus occasional irregular closures
closed_days: Sundays; also occasional irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter where the chef works right in front of you, so a little Japanese and an advance reservation go a long way; it is an intimate, food-focused spot rather than a casual walk-in.
gf_detail: This is a yakitori specialist: skewers are seasoned with tare (a soy-sauce-and-mirin glaze that contains wheat) or salt, and the meal often finishes with a wheat-noodle chicken-salt ramen. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, so this is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around charcoal-grilled chicken (thighs, offal, skin, tsukune meatballs, hand wings) and a chicken-based broth ramen, all animal-based. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
caveats: Reservations are strongly advised given only 10 counter seats. Closes Sundays with additional irregular days off, so confirm before visiting. Pricing is on the higher side for yakitori. Chef's full name is not documented in available sources.

bio.background: A small counter-only yakitori shop that opened on April 3, 2023 in central Hiroshima's Tatemachi district, a few minutes' walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. The opening was actually pushed back about a week from its planned late-March date because of smoke-management issues, before the doors finally opened.
bio.origin: Central Hiroshima (Tatemachi, Naka-ku), next to a ramen shop in the busy Hondori/Hatchobori entertainment area.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken cooked over high-grade Tosa binchotan charcoal from Kochi, prized for its stronger aroma. The chef sources different rare and regional birds by cut: prized Amakusa Daio thigh and breast (birds raised around 120 days), Hiroshima offal, and Ehime jidori for lighter dishes like toriwasa and tataki. Signatures include sumi-grilled offal, crispy seseri (neck), creamy chi-gimo (liver), shiso-scented tsukune meatballs, hand wings cooked on iron skewers, and a closing bowl of chicken-salt ramen.
bio.philosophy: The room is built as a single counter so guests sit close to the fire and take in the cooking with all five senses, the smoke, the sizzle and the aroma. The chef's attentive technique is part of the experience: misting each skewer at the start and snipping away any char with scissors mid-grill.

anecdotes.text: The owner previously cooked at the yakitori restaurant 'Yakitori Taniji' before opening his own counter.
Regulars note that the seats nearest the entrance are the best value because you can watch the chef's handwork up close.
The opening was delayed by about a week, to April 3, 2023, due to smoke-management issues at the new location.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare glaze is soy-sauce and mirin based, which contains wheat; the meal is also often finished with a wheat-noodle chicken ramen.

name_ja: 酒蔵魚好人 クダコ
cuisine: Seto Inland Sea seafood izakaya (oysters, live octopus sashimi, conger eel, seasonal clay-pot rice)
neighborhood: 中区幟町(八丁堀・銀山町近く)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町9-5 山県ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区幟町9-5 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central-Hiroshima box near Hatchobori/Ginzancho.
seats_text: 65 seats total, including a 30-seat private room and a separate 18-seat fully private room; up to ~30 standing for events.
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-23:00; Fri 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00-00:00; Sat 17:00-00:00; Sun closed. (Some listings note dinner from 17:00, L.O. 23:00, with an irregular schedule.)
closed_days: Sunday (日曜日); listings also note an irregular closing schedule, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local Japanese seafood izakaya where the daily fish and oyster offerings change with the season, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps when ordering and asking what is fresh that day.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a Japanese izakaya, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi-style seasonings, and there are tempura/fried items, so it is not a safe choice for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around fish and shellfish — oysters, live octopus and other sashimi, conger eel, small sardines, and seafood clay-pot rice — and uses fish-based dashi and soy sauce. There is no documented vegan offering; not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: The owner-chef trained extensively in izakayas and Japanese restaurants before opening this place in 2003, and built it around a deep commitment to local Hiroshima ingredients from both the sea and the mountains.
