bio.specialty: Seto Inland Sea seafood — live octopus sashimi, oysters paired with spinach using sun-dried oysters for concentrated umami, seasonal conger eel and small sardines, and made-to-order seasonal clay-pot rice.
bio.philosophy: To bring out the essence of each ingredient through meticulous, unhurried preparation, serving only dishes the kitchen is fully satisfied with.

anecdotes.text: The name refers to Kudako, a renowned fishing ground in the Seto Inland Sea, reflecting the focus on local sea ingredients.
Even a simple potato salad is mixed by hand for about an hour, illustrating the kitchen's painstaking approach.
Oysters are selected at peak season by local distributors and sun-dried to concentrate their umami before being cooked with spinach.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese izakaya, dishes are seasoned with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on fish and shellfish, so vegan dishes would share preparation surfaces and fish-based dashi/soy seasoning.

caveats: The TARGET note described this as a "small oyster izakaya," but listings report 65 seats (with private rooms) and it is not a sub-30-seat solo gem, so MOM_AND_POP was not used. Hours vary by listing and the closing schedule is noted as irregular; lunch is served Mon-Fri only and the restaurant is closed Sundays. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation is documented — it is a seafood-forward omnivore izakaya.

cuisine: Creative Japanese izakaya (washoku/kappo) — seasonal fresh fish, game meat, smoked dishes, and local sake
neighborhood: 中区幟町 (Noborimachi), central Hiroshima, near Hatchobori / Ginzancho
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町8-22
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区幟町8-22 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Around 32-33 seats: counter (8-10), tables (two 4-person tables / ~10 seats), and private rooms including a tatami sunken-kotatsu room for up to 12.
closed_days: Sundays (some sources list it as irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: This is an owner-run Japanese izakaya where ordering and conversation will be in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on dishes built around regular (wheat-based) soy sauce — the signature boar/deer/chicken stew is simmered in a soy-based broth — and fried items such as the yuba-wrapped fried shrimp use wheat batter, so this is not a suitable choice for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around fish from Kusatsu fishing port, game meat (wild boar, deer) and red chicken, smoked eggs/mentaiko/cheese, and fish-and-soy-based seasonings. No plant-based menu is documented.
caveats: Reported opening time and closed days vary across listings (18:00 vs 17:00/17:30 start; Sunday-closed vs irregular closures); confirm directly before visiting. Seat count is approximate (roughly 32-33). No official website was found.

bio.background: Hironaka says his interest in cooking grew under the influence of his mother, a skilled home cook. After graduating from Hiroshima Suishin culinary college, he trained for five years at a Japanese restaurant, five years at a hotel, and served as head chef within the Yamashiroya group, also earning a Hiroshima fugu (pufferfish) preparation license, before opening his own place in December 2017.
bio.specialty: A creative-washoku izakaya in a converted old-house (kominka) style space. Known for an assortment of house-smoked items (boiled eggs, cheese, mentaiko) smoked with different wood chips like oak, cherry and white oak; a signature inoshika-tori stew of wild boar, deer and red chicken simmered a full day in a soy-based broth; and sashimi platters from fish delivered live from Kusatsu fishing port.
bio.philosophy: Carefully made dishes paired with regional sake that impresses connoisseurs — each dish is matched with a complementary local sake, in a relaxed space meant for hosting people who matter, from solo diners to banquets.

anecdotes.text: Hironaka traces his love of cooking to his mother, a talented home cook, before going on to train in restaurants, a hotel, and as a group head chef, then striking out on his own in December 2017.
The smoked platter is made by deliberately switching between oak, cherry and white-oak wood chips for different ingredients, and the namesake boar-deer-chicken stew is simmered for a full day.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature simmered dishes are built on a soy-based broth, indicating standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is central to the cooking.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style noodles — Kure reimen, tsukemen, ramen
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The shop is built around wheat noodles (flat chewy Kure reimen noodles, ramen, tsukemen), and the sauce/broth is described as seafood-and-soy based, which typically contains wheat. Chashu pork, fried rice and other sides further rule out a gluten-free meal. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature Kure reimen is topped with shrimp, chashu pork and boiled egg, and the sauce carries fish/seafood umami. Ramen and the meat/seafood-heavy side menu (steak, senjigara pork) likewise contain animal products. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町1-36 サンプラザビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (堀川町1-36); lands in central Hiroshima Naka-ku near the Hatchōbori/Ebisu-chō entertainment district, within the Hiroshima box.
neighborhood: 中区堀川町（胡町・胡通り、八丁堀／胡町電停すぐ、本通り至近の繁華街）
seats_text: Counter-style seating; small casual shop, no private rooms. Exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: Mon–Thu 19:00–03:00, Fri–Sat 19:00–04:00 (per Tabelog); some listings show closing as late as 03:30/05:30. Closed Sundays. Late-night noodle shop.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, late-night counter noodle spot where you order verbally rather than by ticket machine, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the Kure reimen) will go a long way for a foreign student.

bio.background: Gomenya is a small Hiroshima noodle operation specializing in Kure reimen, a regional cold-noodle dish born in the city of Kure. This branch sits in the heart of Hiroshima's Hatchobori / Ebisu entertainment quarter and runs deep into the night, drawing both locals after work and curious first-timers wanting to try the local specialty.
bio.specialty: Kure reimen — flat, chewy cold noodles dressed in a distinctive sweet-and-sour sauce with a spicy kick and seafood umami, topped with cucumber, shrimp, chashu pork and boiled egg. Selectable spice level. Also serves ramen, tsukemen and hearty sides.
bio.philosophy: Hospitality-first, greeting every bowl with the cheerful call 'はい、お待ちど〜様ですっ！' ('Here you go, thanks for waiting!'), welcoming regulars and newcomers alike.

anecdotes.text: Kure reimen is presented as an independently developed regional cuisine, distinct from typical summer cold ramen or Korean naengmyeon, defined by its sweet-sour-yet-spicy sauce and springy flat noodles.
A diner described the medium-spicy Kure reimen as an 'endless loop' of chili heat giving way to fish-based umami and sweet-sour notes, paired with fluffy, well-separated fried rice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on wheat noodles (Kure reimen, ramen, tsukemen) and a soy/seafood-based sauce; a shared noodle kitchen means heavy gluten presence and high cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The sauce/broth is described as soy- and seafood-based, which in Japanese noodle shops typically uses wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dish and sides include shrimp, pork chashu, egg and fish-derived umami; no vegan preparation documented.

caveats: Closing-time hours conflict across listings (Tabelog detail 03:00/04:00 vs. search snippet 03:30/05:30); confirm current hours before a late-night visit. Exact seat count not published. Not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diners.

name_ja: 湯葉と豆腐の店 梅の花 広島店
name_en: Yuba to Tofu no Mise Ume no Hana Hiroshima-ten
cuisine: 豆腐・湯葉の和食会席（事前予約でヴィーガン・ベジタリアン対応可）
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. The menu is built around tofu and yuba (soy products), but kaiseki courses rely heavily on Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which typically contain wheat, and fried yuba/tempura-style items use wheat batter. The restaurant only handles a fixed list of allergens (eggs, dairy, buckwheat, peanuts, crab, shrimp, walnuts) by advance reservation and explicitly states it cannot accommodate allergens beyond that list — wheat/gluten is not among them. A celiac or strict gluten-free diner should not rely on this restaurant; ask in detail when reserving.
vegan_detail: The food centers on house-made tofu (from Hokkaido Toyomusume soybeans), freshly pulled yuba, yudofu hot pot, and tofu shumai — all soy-based and plant-forward — but the standard kaiseki courses also include seafood, egg, and dairy items, and use dashi/soy sauce that may contain fish and wheat. There is no published vegan or vegetarian set menu. Vegan/vegetarian accommodation may be possible only by arranging it in advance when booking; confirm directly by phone or online reservation before visiting.
address_ja: 〒732-0822 広島県広島市南区松原町9-1 福屋広島駅前店11F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市南区松原町9-1 via GSI; coordinates land at Fukuya Hiroshima Ekimae department store next to JR Hiroshima Station, within the Hiroshima validity box.
neighborhood: 広島駅周辺（南区松原町・福屋広島駅前店11F）
seats_text: Roughly 119 seats total across the dining floor, fully private rooms, and banquet halls; the venue is configured for both individual dining and larger gatherings (one official figure cites about 28 in a single section).
closed_days: Open daily; closed January 1 (New Year's Day). December 31 operates lunch hours only (11:00-16:00).
cultural_comfort_note: A polished department-store kaiseki restaurant where staff are used to guests but English is limited; a few Japanese phrases and an advance reservation noting any dietary needs will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.

bio.origin: A specialist tofu-and-yuba restaurant occupying the 11th floor of the Fukuya department store directly beside JR Hiroshima Station's south side.
bio.background: The restaurant turns two humble soy staples — tofu and yuba (the delicate skin lifted from simmering soy milk) — into a full seasonal kaiseki experience. It makes its own tofu in-house from Hokkaido Toyomusume soybeans and pulls fresh yuba to order.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes include tofu shumai, yudofu (simmered tofu hot pot), deep-fried yuba, and yuba gratin, served as multi-course kaiseki sets.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the craft of fresh, handmade tofu and yuba as the heart of a refined Japanese meal.

anecdotes.text: Diners eat against a wide view over the Hiroshima cityscape, and the floor includes fully enclosed private rooms used for celebrations, memorials, and formal gatherings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soy-sauce-based seasonings and wheat-battered fried yuba/tempura items are used throughout the kitchen, so gluten cross-contact is likely; the restaurant does not list wheat among the allergens it can accommodate.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaiseki dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tofu and yuba dishes share a kitchen with seafood, egg, and dairy preparations; standard dashi may contain fish, so plant-only diners should request accommodation in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodation is offered only on a limited basis to guests who reserve in advance, and only for eggs, dairy, buckwheat, peanuts, crab, shrimp, and walnuts; allergens outside that list cannot be handled.

positives.text: The core ingredients are house-made tofu and freshly pulled yuba, so plant-forward diners have genuinely soy-centered options to build a meal around when they arrange it ahead.

caveats: Seat count varies by source (official site cites about 28 for one section; Tabelog lists ~119 total including banquet halls) — 119 used as the venue total. Vegan/vegetarian and gluten-free handling is not published as a set menu and must be arranged by advance reservation; the documented allergy program does not include wheat/gluten.

lead: おやつカフェ ユルム。
name_ja: おやつカフェ ユルム。
cuisine: Vegan bake café — egg-free, dairy-free, white-sugar-free muffins, scones and other baked sweets, plus smoothies and drinks
vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around plant-based baking: no eggs, no dairy, no white sugar. Sweetness comes from beet sugar and brown rice malt syrup (genmai mizuame), and soy milk replaces dairy. Muffins, scones and similar baked goods are the core offering, alongside fruit smoothies (e.g. organic strawberry). Suitable for vegans, though confirm any specific item as recipes rotate daily.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The baked goods are made with domestic wheat flour (国産小麦粉), so muffins and scones contain gluten. The shop focuses on egg/dairy/white-sugar avoidance, not gluten avoidance.
address_ja: 広島県広島市東区牛田本町2-3-10
geocode_note: GSI geocode of the chōme-level address; lands in Ushida-honmachi, Higashi Ward, within the Hiroshima bounding box.
neighborhood: Ushida-honmachi, Higashi Ward (near Hakushima Station on the Astram Line; about 2 km north of Peace Memorial Park)
seats_text: 3 counter seats; primarily a small takeout-oriented bake shop where you can also eat in
hours_raw: Tuesday–Saturday 11:00–18:00; closed Sunday and Monday
closed_days: Sunday and Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run bake café with only a few counter seats, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps and a friendly konnichiwa goes a long way; staff are used to allergy-conscious customers but English may be limited.
caveats: Very small (3 counter seats), so it functions largely as a takeout sweets shop; the daily lineup of muffins and scones rotates and items can sell out. Vegan but uses wheat flour, so not for gluten-free diners. Closed Sundays and Mondays.

bio.background: The owner, Harada Naomi, was once an ordinary company worker until she developed severe atopic dermatitis. Searching for foods that were gentler on her body, she began baking her own sweets without eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar.
bio.specialty: Plant-based muffins and scones made with domestic wheat flour, beet sugar, brown rice malt syrup and soy milk, plus seasonal fruit smoothies such as one made from pesticide-free organic strawberries from Hatsukaichi.
bio.philosophy: She wants people to care for themselves and let both body and mind 'loosen and unwind' (yurumu) through gently sweet treats made from ingredients that go easy on the body.

anecdotes.text: After sharing her homemade allergy-friendly sweets on social media, she received so many requests from other people with allergies that she eventually opened the café.
The shop's name 'yurumu' (to loosen / relax) reflects her wish for guests to relax body and mind over gentle, body-friendly sweets.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen bakes with domestic wheat flour, so the environment is not gluten-free and cross-contact with wheat is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The whole product line is made without eggs, dairy, or white sugar, so the kitchen does not handle those animal ingredients alongside the baked goods.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner started baking precisely because of her own allergy (severe atopic dermatitis) and built the shop around requests from other allergy-sufferers, so she is highly attuned to egg/dairy/white-sugar avoidance.

positives.text: Fully egg-free, dairy-free and white-sugar-free baking using beet sugar, brown rice malt syrup and soy milk — a reliable vegan-friendly sweets stop.
Prioritizes domestic, organic and pesticide-free ingredients where possible, such as organic strawberries for smoothies.

cuisine: Vegan organic bakery and takeout cafe
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The shop's core offering is bread and baked goods, which are typically wheat-based, so gluten-free travelers should ask directly about specific items before ordering. Vegan listings do not flag a gluten-free menu.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan, organic operation. Everything is plant-based with no animal products — described as an all-vegan organic takeout cafe specializing in vegan bread, baked goods, and drinks, plus vegan dishes such as tantan noodles. Oriental-vegetarian (no five pungent roots) options are also noted.
address_ja: 広島県広島市西区三滝本町2丁目1-8 ミタキシェアキッチン B-2
geocode_note: Geocoded to 三滝本町2-1-8; lat 34.4189 / lng 132.4491 falls inside the Hiroshima box. Located in Nishi-ku (Mitaki area), about a 5-minute walk from JR Mitaki Station and ~10 minutes by train from Hiroshima Station — northwest of the city center, not in the immediate A-Bomb Dome area or on Miyajima.
neighborhood: 三滝本町 (Mitaki, Nishi-ku)
seats_text: Primarily a takeout operation within a shared-kitchen facility (ミタキシェアキッチン); seating not documented.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:00. Closed Mon-Thu and on days when participating in market events. Weekends include a lunch menu.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday; also closed on days when participating in outside market events
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff and an English menu are available, and being takeout-focused with online and delivery ordering, it is very approachable for a foreign student.

bio.background: A small, independent all-vegan and organic takeout cafe that opened in June 2025 inside the Mitaki Share Kitchen, a short walk from JR Mitaki Station in northwest Hiroshima. It focuses on plant-based bread, baked goods such as cinnamon rolls and melon pan, and drinks, with seasonal items and a lunch menu on weekends.
bio.specialty: Vegan organic bread and baked goods, plus drinks and weekend lunch dishes like vegan tantan noodles
bio.philosophy: Fully plant-based cooking using organic ingredients, with no animal products.

positives.text: Entirely vegan and organic kitchen, so there is no animal-product cross-contact to worry about for plant-based diners.
English-speaking staff and an English menu make it easy to confirm ingredients.

caveats: Confirmed open (opened 2025-06-14) and fully vegan/organic. Located in Nishi-ku Mitaki, northwest of the city center — not in the immediate A-Bomb Dome area or on Miyajima. Takeout-focused inside a shared kitchen; seating not documented. Cash only. Hours are limited (Fri-Sun, 11:00-16:00) and irregular due to participation in outside market events, so confirm via Instagram before visiting. No documented owner story or dedicated gluten-free program; baked goods are likely wheat-based.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki batter and the soba/udon noodles layered into it are wheat-based, and the standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free preparation, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for those requiring gluten-free.
vegan_detail: A traditional okonomiyaki house whose signature dishes contain pork, egg, and wheat noodles cooked on a shared teppan with fish/animal-based sauces. No documented vegan or vegetarian options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 お好み村4F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県広島市中区新天地5番13号 via GSI; within the central Hiroshima box, in the Shintenchi/Nagarekawa entertainment district a few minutes from Hatchobori Station.
neighborhood: 新天地・流川エリア（八丁堀駅徒歩約4-5分）
seats_text: Counter seats only (sources list roughly 15-17 counter seats); no tables or private rooms.
hours_raw: 11:00～翌2:00（料理・ドリンクL.O. 翌1:30）。別ソースでは11:00～24:00表記あり。無休。
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-only okonomiyaki stall inside the Okonomimura food building where you sit right at the griddle; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will carry you, but expect little to no English and cash only.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between listings (one source says open until midnight, another until 2:00 a.m.); confirm late-night closing on arrival. Cash only. Located on the 4th floor of the Okonomimura building amid many similar stalls.

bio.background: Atomu is one of the long-running okonomiyaki stalls inside Hiroshima's famous Okonomimura, a building stacked with small okonomiyaki shops in the Shintenchi entertainment district. It has been serving since the 1990s and is a cozy, counter-only spot where you watch the cook work the iron griddle right in front of you.
bio.origin: Hiroshima, within the Okonomimura okonomiyaki building
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on the teppan, including the namesake house okonomiyaki 'Atomu' made with kimchi and garlic for a hearty, stamina-packed version, plus the classic nikutama soba (pork, egg, and noodle) okonomiyaki.

anecdotes.text: The house's signature 'Atomu' okonomiyaki is loaded with kimchi and garlic for a stamina-boosting, full-flavored take on the Hiroshima classic.
Diners sit at the counter and watch the cook finish each okonomiyaki with fluid, practiced hand movements right on the griddle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is cooked on a shared teppan griddle where wheat batter, wheat noodles, and wheat-containing sauce are all used; heavy cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are cooked on the same teppan used for pork, egg, and seafood, so vegan separation is not possible.

lead: 広島 中華そば うぐいす
name_ja: 広島 中華そば うぐいす
cuisine: 中華そば / Hiroshima-style ramen
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature and essentially only dish is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) soy-sauce ramen, topped with chashu pork. The broth is animal-based and the menu offers no plant-based alternatives.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy-sauce broth. There are no gluten-free options and no indication of allergen accommodation.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16
geocode_note: Geocoded from the chōme-level address via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near Hatchobori / Ebisucho.
neighborhood: 流川町 (Nagarekawa), 中区 — central Hiroshima nightlife district, near Hatchobori and Ebisucho stations
seats_text: About 10 counter seats only.
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, smoky, counter-only late-night ramen counter where regulars and tipsy locals slurp in close quarters; a first-time foreign visitor will be most comfortable arriving with a Japanese-speaking friend or guide, ordering simply (中華そば) and following the unspoken order-pay-eat-leave rhythm.

bio.background: Uguisu has been serving Hiroshima's nightlife district since 1948, making it one of the city's oldest surviving chuka soba (Chinese noodle) shops. It belongs to the so-called kotori-kei or small-bird-style lineage of Hiroshima ramen, alongside shops like Tsubasa, characterized by a lighter pork-bone broth. Today it is run as a solo, hands-on operation by a later-generation owner.
bio.specialty: A single, focused dish: chuka soba in a silky pork-bone soy-sauce broth, with customization limited to extra green onion or extra chashu. The shop also keeps old-school ramune soda on hand.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately minimal menu and an unchanging late-night routine: the broth keeps depth while finishing clean and refreshing, built specifically as the perfect bowl to cap off a night of drinking.

anecdotes.text: The shop looks intimidating from the street, but as one regular put it, when you have been drinking you somehow just slip right in.
Open from 8pm until 2am six nights a week, it has long been a go-to spot for a comforting bowl of light, slurpable ramen after the bars close.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The core dish is wheat ramen noodles in a wheat-based soy-sauce pork broth; there is no documented gluten-free preparation or allergen handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is described as pork-bone soy sauce (tonkotsu shoyu), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is pork-bone based and the standard topping is chashu pork, so all bowls contain animal products.

caveats: Confirmed open and operating via Tabelog (no closure/on-hold notice). No official website exists. Founding year of 1948 and the solo-operation detail are drawn from Tabelog's listing summary; treat the exact generation/ownership history as approximate. Hours and the 10-seat count are from Tabelog; payment supports credit cards (JCB/AMEX/Diners) but no e-money/QR, and reservations are not accepted.

cuisine: フレッシュジュースバー／軽食（ライスバーガー）
neighborhood: 宮島 表参道商店街
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町463-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県廿日市市宮島町463-1 via GSI; first feature lands on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Small standing/walk-up juice bar with a few benches inside for resting; no formal seating capacity published.
hours_raw: 10:30-17:30 (official site lists 10:30-17:00; hours vary with ferry operations)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual walk-up juice stand on the main shopping street where pointing at the colorful fruit displays and a few words of Japanese easily get you served.
vegan_detail: The fresh fruit juices (e.g., Hiroshima lemon lemonade/lemon soda, the seasonal Hiroshima Mix, pineapple frozen drinks) are fruit-based and suitable for plant-based diners. The food side (rice burgers / rice-sandwich onigiri) features conger eel (anago) and oysters, which are not vegan; the cheese-filled mofles contain dairy. Stick to the fruit juices and lemonade for a vegan-friendly choice.
gf_detail: Pure fresh fruit juices and lemon soda/lemonade should be naturally gluten-free, but no allergen information is published. The savory items (rice burgers, mofles/waffle pastries) and possible seasonings cannot be assumed gluten-free. There is no dedicated gluten-free handling; a gluten-sensitive diner should stick to plain fruit juices and confirm at the counter.

bio.background: A small fresh-juice bar tucked along the Omotesando shopping street on Miyajima, billed as the island's only dedicated juice bar. It serves cups of juice pressed from seasonal fruit, with a signature Hiroshima Mix that changes with what is in season and Hiroshima-grown lemon turning up in the lemonade and lemon soda. Alongside the drinks it offers Miyajima-style rice burgers and rice-wrapped onigiri.
bio.specialty: Fresh seasonal-fruit juices and Hiroshima-lemon drinks
bio.philosophy: Fresh fruit juice refreshes the throat with the sweetness and acidity unique to each fruit — a vitamin pick-me-up for visitors strolling the island.

anecdotes.text: Positioned as a 'vitamin charge' rest stop for Miyajima sightseers, with the tart Hiroshima-lemon soda recommended as an energizing pick for hot summer visits.
Described as Itsukushima's only juice bar, with the seasonal Hiroshima Mix as its most popular cup.

positives.text: Fresh fruit juices and Hiroshima-lemon lemonade/soda are fruit-based, giving plant-based and gluten-avoiding visitors a simple, naturally suitable option.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog/blog list 10:30-17:30; official site lists 10:30-17:00) and reportedly vary with ferry schedules. Payment data conflicts: the official site states credit cards are not accepted while Tabelog notes electronic money and QR-code payments are accepted, so card acceptance is uncertain. No seating-capacity or allergen information is published.

cuisine: Western / bar / cafe (yoshoku, Spanish & Italian-style small plates) featuring Hiroshima oysters and anago (conger eel)
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), about a 1-minute walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, near Itsukushima Shrine and the Omotesando shopping street
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on oysters and anago dishes such as oyster carbonara, pizza, and fried oysters, which involve wheat (pasta, dough, frying batter) and typically wheat-based soy/seasonings. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would need to ask carefully; nothing on the available pages confirms gluten-free preparation.
vegan_detail: Listings note a vegetarian menu is available ("ベジタリアンメニューあり"), but no detail on what those dishes are and no confirmation they are vegan. The restaurant's signature dishes are oysters, anago, and other seafood/Western fare that use fish and animal ingredients, so a strict vegan should ask in advance which specific items can be made without animal products.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町860-7
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō-level point for 広島県廿日市市宮島町860; lands on Miyajima island, just below the Hiroshima mainland box, which is expected for a 宮島町 address.
seats_text: Listed as 30 seats on Tabelog (a local guide lists 28); a small-to-mid-size single room.
hours_raw: 09:00-15:30 (L.O. 15:00), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Mondays (if Monday is a holiday, closed the following Tuesday instead)
closed_days: Mondays (closed the following Tuesday instead when Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly spot right by the Miyajima pier where pointing at the menu and a few words of Japanese will get you by; staff are used to visitors but full English service is not confirmed.
caveats: Vegetarian menu is advertised but undocumented in detail and not confirmed vegan; gluten-free is not addressed. Confirm specific dietary needs by phone/in person. Seat count varies slightly between listings (28-30).

bio.background: 厳島BARL 厳齋 (Issai) is a casual Western-style bar and cafe that sits just about a minute's walk from the Miyajima ferry pier, making it a natural first or last stop for travelers heading to Itsukushima Shrine. It opened relatively recently as a BARL-style spot offering creative European cooking alongside Hiroshima's signature local ingredients, with sea views from the dining room.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima oysters and anago (conger eel) prepared in inventive ways - including steamed oysters, fried oysters, oyster carbonara, and oyster pizza - paired with local Hiroshima sake and wine.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed place to ease into or wind down a Miyajima visit, serving the area's prized oysters and conger eel in approachable, Western-influenced small-plate and cafe style rather than as a formal seafood restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen reworks Hiroshima's classic oysters and conger eel into bar-style dishes such as oyster carbonara and oyster pizza alongside the more traditional steamed and fried oysters.
Positioned a one-minute walk from the Miyajima pier, it is pitched as a spot to drop into right after arriving by ferry and to relax in before heading home.

positives.text: A vegetarian menu is listed as available, so there are at least some meat-free options on offer.

cuisine: Kyodo ryori / Japanese kaiseki cuisine ryokan — Seto Inland Sea seafood (Hiroshima oysters, anago/conger eel), with on-site lunch dining and a casual eatery; vegetarian cuisine available on advance request
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The kitchen centers on seafood kaiseki and casual set meals (fried oysters/oyster cutlet, tempura/fried items, udon, soba, curry) that use wheat batter, wheat-based soy sauce, and shared frying. Anago-meshi and other dishes rely on soy-based seasonings. Nothing on any fetched page indicates gluten-free preparation, so GF travelers cannot rely on this kitchen.
vegan_detail: The inn states on its official cuisine page that vegetarian (菜食主義者) meals can be prepared with advance reservation (※事前予約要). However, this is described as vegetarian, not vegan, and no plant-only/dairy-and-egg-free detail is published. The default cuisine is heavily seafood-based (oysters, conger eel, sea bream, octopus, squid) with fish dashi and wheat-soy seasonings. A vegan would need to confirm specifics (dashi, egg, dairy) directly and book ahead.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町南大西町50
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the Tabelog address (宮島町南大西町50). Coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box and land on Miyajima island near Itsukushima Shrine / the Miyajima History and Folklore Museum, consistent with the inn's stated hilltop location. Official-site address is the shortened form 廿日市市宮島町50.
neighborhood: Miyajima (Itsukushima), on the hillside near Itsukushima Shrine, diagonally across from the Miyajima History and Folklore Museum
seats_text: Seat count not published. Lodging-side has 20 or fewer guest rooms across 5 room types. The on-site dining room 平家 (Heike) seats diners for reserved lunch courses; the casual eatery プチ平家 (Petit Heike) sits at the base of the inn — capacities not disclosed.
hours_raw: On-site restaurant 平家 (Heike) lunch: 11:30–14:00, advance reservation required. Dinner kaiseki for staying guests served at 18:00 / 18:30 / 19:00 (17:30 on special-event days). Casual eatery プチ平家 (Petit Heike): irregular hours (不定期). Inn check-in 15:00–18:00, check-out by 10:00.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day published; the casual eatery operates on an irregular schedule and lunch requires advance reservation.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a traditional family-run inn with a reserved-course dining room, so booking ahead in Japanese (by phone or through the official site) is expected and a few words of Japanese will smooth your visit.

bio.background: Jukeiso is a traditional cuisine-ryokan founded in 1926 (Showa 1), set on a hillside overlooking Itsukushima Shrine, its great torii gate, the five-story pagoda and the treasure pagoda. Beyond lodging, it serves food: the on-site restaurant Heike offers reserved lunch courses scaled to different budgets, and a casual eatery called Petit Heike at the foot of the inn — diagonally across from the Miyajima History and Folklore Museum, beside the stairway up to Jukeiso — serves everyday Miyajima fare such as anago-meshi (conger-eel rice) and grilled oysters.
bio.philosophy: The house presents its cooking around the four seasons of Miyajima — spring cherry blossoms, summer breezes, autumn foliage, winter snow — aiming for encounters and emotions guests will keep in their hearts, with every dish prepared by hand for both flavor and visual beauty.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Seto Inland Sea kaiseki built on Hiroshima oysters and anago (conger eel), alongside sea bream, yellowtail, flounder, octopus, squid and turban shells.

anecdotes.text: The inn frames Hiroshima's oysters as a point of pride, noting the region accounts for more than half of Japan's oyster production.
At the base of the inn, the old-fashioned spot Petit Heike serves anago-meshi set meals and grilled oysters, sitting just beside the staircase that climbs to Jukeiso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes battered/fried items (fried oysters, oyster cutlet, tempura-style fried fish and shrimp, tonkatsu, fried chicken) and noodles (udon, soba), indicating shared wheat handling and frying.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as anago-meshi rely on standard Japanese soy-based seasonings, which are typically wheat-containing; no gluten-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core kitchen is seafood-based kaiseki (oysters, conger eel, sea bream, octopus, squid) with fish-derived dashi, so shared equipment and stock are likely; a separate vegetarian menu exists only by advance request.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The inn states vegetarian meals can be prepared with prior reservation (ベジタリアン（菜食主義者）の料理もご用意可能です。※事前予約要), showing willingness to accommodate special diets when notified in advance.

positives.text: Vegetarian cuisine is offered on request with advance booking, and lunch courses are tailored to the guest's budget, suggesting flexibility for diners who communicate needs ahead of time.

caveats: 聚景荘 is primarily a cuisine-ryokan (founded 1926) on Miyajima, but it does serve non-staying diners: the on-site Heike restaurant offers reserved lunch courses (11:30–14:00) and the casual Petit Heike at the base serves anago-meshi and grilled oysters on an irregular schedule. hours_week reflects the Heike lunch window as the dining-relevant hours; reservations are required for the lunch courses and the casual eatery's hours are irregular. Seat counts are not published. Vegetarian (not vegan) meals are available only with advance reservation; the default menu is seafood-heavy and uses wheat-soy seasonings and shared frying, so it is not gluten-free and not reliably vegan. Cash-only status not stated; assumed cards accepted as an established inn but unconfirmed.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki / teppanyaki
neighborhood: 流川・薬研堀（中区）/ near Ebisu-chō Station
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16 お好み共和国ひろしま村3F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区流川町3-16 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Small shop with counter seating facing the teppan plus a few tables in front; exact seat count not published.
closed_days: Mondays (月曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual couple-run counter shop where the owners are described as warm and chatty, but expect mostly Japanese-only conversation, so come ready to point and smile or use a few Japanese phrases.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour, the layered noodles (yakisoba) are wheat, and the okonomi sauce contains wheat-based soy sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg-centric okonomiyaki/teppanyaki shop; the signature dish is 肉玉そば (pork, egg and noodles). Batter typically contains egg and dashi, and okonomi sauce is not vegan. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: いちなり is a small okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop run by a husband-and-wife team inside the お好み共和国ひろしま村 okonomiyaki hall, on the third floor in the Nagarekawa entertainment district of central Hiroshima. The couple relocated to this spot a few years ago and keep the place open late into the night, drawing both locals and visitors after dinner.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style 肉玉そば (pork-and-egg okonomiyaki layered with noodles), cooked to order on the teppan.
roles: husband-and-wife owners running the shop together

anecdotes.text: Visitors single out the layered yakisoba noodles as thinner and springier than ordinary supermarket noodles, giving the okonomiyaki extra texture.
The owners are remembered as cheerful and easy to talk to; one guest recounted lingering at the counter for about three hours because the atmosphere was so welcoming.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based okonomiyaki batter and yakisoba noodles are cooked on a shared teppan griddle, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomi sauce is brushed on the finished okonomiyaki and is made with wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork, egg and dashi-based ingredients are cooked together on the same griddle as all dishes.

caveats: Cash only. Open evenings into the early morning (until 04:00 Tue-Sat); closed Mondays. Located on the 3rd floor of the お好み共和国ひろしま村 okonomiyaki hall. Seat count and detailed allergen handling are not published, so GF/vegan travelers should treat this as not suitable. Official website not found.

lead: おばんざい りなさん家
name_ja: おばんざい りなさん家
cuisine: おばんざい居酒屋（家庭料理・おでん・肉吸い）
neighborhood: 薬研堀・胡町（中区）/ 銀山町駅徒歩約5分
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀10-17 幸楽ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded at chō-level (薬研堀); coordinates fall within the Hiroshima box, in the Yakkenbori entertainment district of central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 9席（カウンター7・テーブル2）
closed_days: 日曜、第1・第3月曜
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter izakaya in a nightlife district where the warm owner makes you feel at home, but it is a late-night, Japanese-language local spot, so some basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help a foreign student feel comfortable.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The core menu of obanzai, oden, and nikusui (beef broth) is built on Japanese dashi and soy sauce, which contain wheat, and there is no documentation of gluten-free preparation or accommodation.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Dishes rely on fish/bonito dashi and Japanese soy sauce, and the signature nikusui is a beef-broth dish; no plant-based menu is documented and there is no indication that vegan requests can be accommodated.
caveats: Recently opened (22 January 2026), so hours and the 1st/3rd-Monday closures may shift; confirm via the owner's Instagram before visiting. Late-night hours (until 03:00) and a 9-seat counter mean it can fill quickly.

bio.background: A small, home-style obanzai izakaya in Hiroshima's Yakkenbori nightlife quarter, opened on 22 January 2026 by owner Rina Nakagawa, who previously worked as a nurse.
bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai (a weekly-rotating selection of seasonal side dishes), oden, and nikusui, prepared with a deeply infused dashi that one reviewer described as gently soaking into the body.
bio.philosophy: The owner's idea is a place to unwind at the end of the day, somewhere you naturally drop by after work and feel welcomed home with a sense of tadaima.

anecdotes.text: Run by a former nurse, the place is described by a guest as a shop wrapped in kindness, relaxed and unpretentious, where both mind and body unwind after a tiring day.
The weekly-rotating obanzai means there is something new to enjoy each visit, suiting both a casual drink and a proper meal.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The obanzai, oden, and nikusui menu is dashi- and soy-sauce-based; Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and there is no gluten-free preparation documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes use fish/bonito dashi and the signature nikusui is beef broth; no vegan options or separate preparation are documented.

lead: おばんざいBar うさぎ
name_ja: おばんざいBar うさぎ
cuisine: 手作りおばんざい・創作料理バー（カウンター）
neighborhood: 胡町（中区）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町3-24 ムシカビル 4F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 広島県広島市中区胡町3-24 returned [132.46524, 34.392803], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 9席（カウンターのみ）
hours_raw: 19:00～00:00（火～土・祝前日など）。日曜・月曜定休。
closed_days: 日曜・月曜
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter bar run solo by the owner; you'll be most comfortable with some basic Japanese, and as a cash-only late-night spot it suits an evening sit-down rather than a quick meal.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The obanzai dishes are seasoned with dashi and soy sauce (which contains wheat), and the menu includes items like macaroni salad and fresh bread, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The rotating obanzai spread includes meat and fish-based dishes (chicken dishes, mapo tofu, butter chicken curry) and the home-style seasoning typically relies on dashi and wheat-based soy sauce. There are several vegetable small plates (vinegared vegetables, blanched greens, braised eggplant, simmered pumpkin), but no confirmed fully plant-based preparation; a vegan diner would need to ask the owner directly.
caveats: Cash only and no card payment. Very small (9 counter seats) and open evenings only (from 19:00), closed Sunday and Monday — reservations are recommended even though walk-ins are accepted. Vegan/gluten-free suitability is not documented and would need to be confirmed in person.

bio.background: A small counter bar tucked into the Mushika Building on Hiroshima's lively Mushika dori, run single-handedly by its owner, known to regulars simply as the mama. She starts cooking in the morning and prepares every dish herself, turning out a daily-changing spread of home-style obanzai.
bio.specialty: All-you-can-eat handmade obanzai (Japanese home cooking) served at the counter, alongside a deep drinks list of sake, shochu and whiskey.
bio.philosophy: Offer warm, lovingly prepared home cooking in an intimate, hideaway setting where solo diners feel at ease.

anecdotes.text: The owner prepares each obanzai dish one by one from the morning, and the daily lineup of eight or so small plates rotates constantly, with weekly specials such as butter chicken curry.
The counter has a hidden-gem, Kyoto-alleyway feel and a warm, home-like atmosphere that draws a loyal local crowd, including notable Hiroshima regulars.

lead: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き 蔵屋 紙屋町店
name_ja: お好み焼き・鉄板焼き 蔵屋 紙屋町店
cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き（牡蠣・ウニホーレン・コウネなど広島名物、瀬戸内海鮮、A5和牛）
neighborhood: 大手町・紙屋町（中区）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町2-2-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町2-2-13 returned [132.455582, 34.392929], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in central Hiroshima near the A-Bomb Dome / Hondori, consistent with the listing.
seats_text: 40 seats total, including counter seating for solo diners and several fully private tatami/horigotatsu rooms (4-, 6-, and 8-seat rooms); up to 40 for private events. (Tabelog lists 38; official site lists 40.)
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) / 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 22:30), daily including holidays. Sundays may close earlier depending on customer volume. Closures are irregular.
closed_days: Irregular closures (no fixed regular day off per official site); Sundays may close early.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, mainstream teppan restaurant near the A-Bomb Dome where a foreign student can point at the picture menu and watch dishes cooked on the griddle, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour batter/crepe and noodles, and the savory dishes use standard wheat-based soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on oysters, sea urchin (ウニホーレン), beef (コウネ, A5 wagyu), pork bacon, eggs, and seafood; okonomiyaki uses egg and pork and is cooked on shared griddles with meat and fish. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Seat count and payment details differ slightly between sources: Tabelog lists 38 seats and states no electronic money/QR, while the official site lists 40 seats and a broad list of cashless options (cards, IC transit cards, iD/QUICPay/Apple Pay, PayPay). The more permissive official site is used here. Hours noted as having irregular closures; confirm before visiting.

bio.background: A teppan izakaya in central Hiroshima a short walk from Hondori and the A-Bomb Dome, part of the local Kuraya group. The dining room blends a calm, traditional Japanese feel with private rooms, and every seat - including the counter and the tatami rooms - has its own built-in iron griddle so food is served hot and freshly cooked right in front of you.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked on the teppan alongside Seto Inland Sea seafood and griddle dishes: local oysters, the Hiroshima specialty uni horen (sea urchin with spinach, which has been featured on television), koune (a cut of beef), and premium A5 wagyu, all paired with local Hiroshima sake.
bio.philosophy: Built around the idea that food tastes best straight off the iron plate, the restaurant fits its private griddle into every room so groups, dates, and solo diners alike can eat freshly cooked dishes; counter guests are welcomed by an easygoing owner-chef.

anecdotes.text: The Hiroshima specialty uni horen (sea urchin with spinach) on the menu has been introduced on television.
Counter seats let solo diners chat with the approachable owner-chef while their food is grilled.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki, noodles, and griddle dishes share a common teppan; wheat batter and wheat-based sauces are central to the menu, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce (both typically wheat-containing) are used; no gluten-free sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All seats use a shared iron griddle that cooks oysters, beef, pork, and seafood, so any vegetable item would contact animal products.

lead: 穴子飯 木村屋本店
name_ja: 穴子飯 木村屋本店
cuisine: Anago-meshi (conger eel rice) specialist — three styles (charcoal-grilled "black," sauce-simmered "red," and white-grilled "white"), prepared with sushi-chef technique
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The anago is served with sweet tare/soy-based sauce (the "black" and "red" styles) and the white style is served with a special soy sauce — Japanese soy sauce and sweet tare both contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, no allergen documentation, and the kitchen does not advertise any gluten-free handling. A celiac traveler could not eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. Every main is conger eel (fish), served over rice with miso soup and pickles, and even the white-grilled style uses soy sauce. There are no plant-based mains or documented meat/fish-free options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町1-1-10
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区大手町1-1-10 returned [132.455566, 34.395294], within the Hiroshima box. Location is in Otemachi, central Naka-ku, very close to the A-Bomb Dome and Kamiyacho-nishi.
neighborhood: 中区大手町（原爆ドーム・紙屋町西すぐ、平和記念公園至近）
seats_text: 16 seats (4 at the counter, 12 at tables) per the listing; the official site states 25 seats (5 counter, 20 tables) — likely reflecting a layout difference. Small intimate space either way.
hours_raw: Mon, Thu-Sat: 10:30-15:00 and 17:00-22:00; Wed & Sun: 10:30-15:00; Tue: closed. (Official site notes lunch-focused hours 10:30-16:00 with irregular holidays; evening izakaya service on some days.)
closed_days: Tuesdays, plus irregular holidays (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: An English website and menu make ordering easy for a foreign student, and the simple three-style anago format means you can just point and choose.

bio.background: A specialist devoted to anago-meshi, Hiroshima's century-old conger-eel-over-rice dish, set in Otemachi within sight of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park. The shop is run by owner Souji Kimura, who grew up around the trade — his father was a sushi chef — and the kitchen is led by a head chef with more than twenty years of sushi experience.
bio.specialty: Three contrasting styles of anago-meshi served side by side: 'black,' raw conger grilled over charcoal for a crisp, fragrant, chewy bite; 'red,' simmered then finished with a special sweet sauce until meltingly tender; and 'white,' shirayaki grilled plain to taste the eel itself, served with real wasabi and a special soy sauce. Each set comes with soup and pickles, and the chef oversees every preparation.
bio.philosophy: The owner inherited from his sushi-chef father the idea of preparing every dish 'with love, just as you would cook for your own children and parents,' and the shop commits to serving food as delicious as possible using premium ingredients — pairing Hiroshima's traditional anago-meshi with refined sushi technique to create a 'new Hiroshima taste.'

anecdotes.text: The restaurant frames itself as carrying forward more than 100 years of Hiroshima anago-meshi history while taking on new challenges in a place where World Heritage sites stand.
The owner says the lesson to cook 'with love just like preparing meals for our children and parents' came from his father, who was a sushi chef.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The white-style anago is served with a special soy sauce, and the black/red styles use a sweet tare; standard Japanese soy sauce and tare contain wheat.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen information is published; the site provides no gluten, wheat, or cross-contamination details, so allergy diners would need to phone the shop directly.

caveats: Seat count differs between sources (16 on the listing vs. 25 on the official site). Hours vary by source: the official site emphasizes daytime service (roughly 10:30-16:00) with irregular holidays, while the listing shows split lunch/dinner service Mon and Thu-Sat. Confirm current hours and any closure before visiting.

lead: 汁なし担々麺 一楽 幟町店
name_ja: 汁なし担々麺 一楽 幟町店
cuisine: 汁なし担々麺（三次発祥系のbrothレス担担麺）/ Brothless dandan noodles
neighborhood: 中区幟町（八丁堀／幟町エリア、平和大通り北側）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区幟町6-24 ウェンディ幟町アネクス 1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.466202, 34.395336] for 広島県広島市中区幟町6-24; within Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 13席（L字カウンター9席＋4人掛けテーブル）
closed_days: 日曜（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: You order at a touch-panel ticket machine at the entrance, so a few words of Japanese and pointing will get you through comfortably even with no fluent staff English confirmed.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle dandan-men shop; the noodles, the meat sauce, and the seasoning base are not gluten-free, and no gluten-free option is documented. Not suitable for a strict gluten-free diet.
vegan_detail: The signature bowls are built on a pork/meat sauce with sesame paste, and sets include a soft-boiled egg, so the dishes are not vegan. No vegan option is documented.
caveats: Small 13-seat counter shop, cash only, ticket-machine ordering, closed Sundays. Newly opened (July 2025) second branch of a Miyoshi original; menu may still be expanding.

bio.background: This is the second shop in the Ichiraku family, the central-Hiroshima branch of a brothless dandan-noodle (汁なし担々麺) house that started in Miyoshi in northern Hiroshima. It opened in the Noboricho neighborhood on July 7, 2025.
bio.specialty: Brothless dandan noodles (汁なし担々麺) served with two kinds of green onion, plus a soup version. The brothless set comes with rice and a soft-boiled egg.
bio.philosophy: The cooking aims for a notably light, clean flavor rather than heavy richness; the meat sauce leans gently sweet and the sesame is present but restrained, with adjustable spice levels that stay mild.

anecdotes.text: Both the brothless and soup versions are described as having a characteristic lightness of flavor, with the meat sauce emphasizing sweetness and the sesame kept gentle; even at spice level 2 it reads as mild.
Staff indicated the menu is planned to expand, possibly adding mapo tofu as offered at the original Miyoshi location.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style ramen and tsukemen (chuka soba)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区立町3-21
neighborhood: 立町 (Tatemachi), Naka-ku — between Hondori and Hatchobori, near the Tatemachi tram stop
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 広島県広島市中区立町3-21 to [132.459778, 34.394054], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Counter only, about 5 seats (some sources say up to 7). A tiny shop that fills up quickly, especially at lunch.
hours_raw: Wed–Sat 11:30–14:00 and 18:30–21:00; Sun 11:30–14:00; closed Mon & Tue. Closes early when the soup and noodles run out.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (and earlier whenever the day's soup/noodles sell out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny five-seat local counter where ordering is simple but communication is in Japanese, so a few basic words or pointing at the menu will help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: The ramen and tsukemen use wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-seasoned pork-bone broth, so dishes are not gluten-free and there is no gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: The broth is pork-bone and soy-sauce based and the bowls are topped with char siu (roast pork), so the food is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
caveats: Very small counter shop that sells out and closes early on busy days; cash only; arrive before the lunch rush to avoid a wait.

bio.background: Kiyochan first opened in 1973 and has served Hiroshima-style chuka soba (ramen) for around half a century. After a continuous family lineage, it reopened in 2018 under its third-generation owner, who carries on the shop's classic recipe.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style ramen and tsukemen built on an amber, gently pork-bone-scented soy-sauce broth, with medium-thin firm wheat noodles topped simply with bean sprouts, scallions, and char siu. The char siu is briefly torched before serving for a lean, savory roasted note. Wonton dishes are also offered.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately intimate counter-only shop that has kept its traditional, minimalist approach to classic Hiroshima ramen across three generations.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so small — a counter of only about five seats — that it fills up before noon, with regulars lining up by midday.
Opened in 1973 and reopened in 2018 under third-generation ownership, keeping the long-running family recipe alive.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy sauce broth are central to every bowl, so a shared cooking environment makes the food unsuitable for gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a soy-sauce (shoyu) style broth, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is pork-bone based and bowls are topped with char siu pork, so animal products are integral to the cooking, not just toppings.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki izakaya
neighborhood: Nagarekawa / Yagenbori entertainment district, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori)
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and wheat noodles, and teppanyaki dishes are seasoned with standard soy-sauce-based sauces, so this is not a gluten-free destination. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should not rely on this restaurant.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Hiroshima oysters, koune (beef neck), pork, egg, and seafood-and-wheat-based okonomiyaki, with sauces containing wheat soy sauce and likely bonito/fish stock. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Vegan diners should look elsewhere.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区三川町9-22 第2KTビル 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区三川町9-22 returned [132.462662, 34.388893], within the Hiroshima box; matches the Nagarekawa/Mikawacho location.
seats_text: Around 50-60 seats including counter, tables, and sunken-hearth (horigotatsu) seating; solo diners welcome and private rooms available for larger groups.
closed_days: No regular closed day (年中無休); evening-only opening.
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and solo diners are welcome, so a foreign student can comfortably order Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki here even with limited Japanese.

bio.background: A lively late-night okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot in Hiroshima's Nagarekawa entertainment district, part of the local Negian group of okonomiyaki restaurants.
bio.specialty: The house identity is built around scallions: Hiroshima-grown negi piled generously over Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, alongside regional specialties like Hiroshima oysters and koune (beef neck). The kitchen describes its focus as a commitment to ingredients, noodles, and scallions.
bio.philosophy: A stated devotion to quality in three things: the ingredients, the noodles, and the scallions.

anecdotes.text: The signature is okonomiyaki and teppanyaki heaped with locally grown Hiroshima scallions, served alongside regional teppan dishes such as oysters and koune (beef neck).
A known item is a salt-and-mayo tonpei (egg-and-pork) dish that the group has promoted as a contest winner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles cooked on a shared teppan griddle, making cross-contact with gluten unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Teppanyaki and okonomiyaki dishes are seasoned with standard sauces and soy sauce, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared on a shared griddle with pork, egg, oysters, and beef, so vegan separation is not provided.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which helps travelers identify ingredients before ordering.

caveats: Confirmed open and operating as of the verification date. It is part of the Negian okonomiyaki group (not independent). Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. Evening-only hours with no regular closed day.

cuisine: Sweets / wagashi-yogashi fusion — "ankoronne" (anko-filled puff-pastry coronets), plus sablé, financiers and other confections; takeout walk-around treats with a small upstairs café
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature ankoronne is built on a fermented-butter puff pastry, which is wheat-based; other items (sablé, financiers) are also wheat flour confections. No gluten-free options or allergen handling were documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. While the red bean paste (anko) itself is plant-based, the flagship ankoronne uses fermented butter pastry and a custard cream made from Brown Swiss cow's milk, so it contains dairy. Financiers and other baked goods typically contain butter and eggs. No vegan items were documented.
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町490
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県廿日市市宮島町490 via GSI; coordinates [132.331451, 34.348637] fall on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box, on the Omotesando shopping street near Itsukushima Shrine.
seats_text: Primarily a takeout/walk-around sweets shop with a small second-floor eat-in café space; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 10:00–17:30 daily (one source lists 10:00–18:00); closures irregular (不定休).
closed_days: 不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed counter-style sweets shop where you can point at the pastry you want and pay easily, so a few words of Japanese and gestures are plenty for a foreign student.
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（厳島神社・フェリーターミナル方面）
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (10:00–17:30 vs 10:00–18:00) and closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. Signature items contain wheat and dairy — not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diets; no allergen accommodation is documented.

bio.background: 古今果anco is the casual Miyajima outpost of Fujii-ya (藤い屋), a momiji-manju and mochi maker with roughly 90 years of red-bean-paste tradition. It opened on February 25, 2017 on Miyajima's Omotesando shopping street, directly across from Fujii-ya's main shop, as an offshoot of the 'Coconca/古今果' brand the company first launched in 2015 at Miyajima-guchi as a confectionery workshop and café.
bio.specialty: Ankoronne — a coronet-shaped pastry of fermented-butter puff pastry filled with the shop's artisan anko and a custard cream made from Brown Swiss cow's milk from the Mirasaka Fromage Workshop, finished with Breton sea salt from western France.
bio.philosophy: A 'Japanese-Western fusion' approach to sweets, building modern pastries on the foundation of Fujii-ya's nine-decade-old traditional red bean paste so that the heritage anko is the heart of each creation.

anecdotes.text: The storefront's design echoes the Seto Inland Sea, mirroring the aesthetic of the original 2015 Coconca venue at Miyajima-guchi.
The signature ankoronne layers fermented-butter puff pastry with house anko and Brown Swiss milk custard cream, finished with Breton salt as an accent.

lead: 電光石火 駅前ひろば店
name_ja: 電光石火 駅前ひろば店
cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki, known for a fluffy double-egg-wrapped style
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built on Hiroshima okonomiyaki, which centers on egg, pork, and noodles, with squid tempura, oysters, wagyu offal and other animal toppings. The signature dish layers a double egg with meat and seafood, and the sauce is a seafood-flavored Worcester-style sauce. No vegan or vegetarian dishes or modifications are documented.
gf_detail: Not suitable for gluten-free diners. Okonomiyaki is made from a wheat-flour batter and is layered with wheat noodles (soba or udon). The okonomiyaki sauce is wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or accommodation; everything is cooked together on a shared teppan griddle.
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区松原町10-1 広島フルフォーカスビル6F ひろしまお好み物語 駅前ひろば内
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市南区松原町10-1 via GSI; coordinates 34.39698, 132.473282 fall within the Hiroshima box. Located on the 6F okonomiyaki theme floor (ひろしまお好み物語 駅前ひろば) at Hiroshima Station, about a 3-minute walk from the station.
neighborhood: 広島駅周辺（駅前ひろば／フルフォーカスビル6F）
seats_text: Counter-only seating; official site states 22 seats (Tabelog lists 19). Private events available by arrangement.
hours_raw: 10:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), open daily / year-round
closed_days: No regular closing days (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, tourist-friendly okonomiyaki shop right by Hiroshima Station where you watch the cooking at a counter; a few words of Japanese help but the picture-friendly griddle experience makes ordering easy for a foreign student.
caveats: Part of the Denko-Sekka chain with locations across Japan (Hiroshima, Tokyo, Yokohama, Fukuoka, Nagoya, Hokkaido, Ehime), so not an independent single-owner shop. Seat count differs between sources (official site 22, Tabelog 19). Reservations are accepted on weekdays but not on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays.

bio.background: Denko-Sekka is a Hiroshima okonomiyaki brand whose Ekimae Hiroba branch sits on the 6th-floor okonomiyaki theme floor (Hiroshima Okonomi Monogatari Ekimae Hiroba) of the Full Focus Building, about a three-minute walk from Hiroshima Station. The shop has grown from Hiroshima into a brand with locations across Japan.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki cooked fresh on a hot teppan in front of guests. Their signature 'Denko-Sekka' okonomiyaki is known for an unusually fluffy, tender texture, layering a double egg with meat, noodles, squid tempura, shiso, and green onion.
bio.philosophy: Their stated ethos is to deliver a piping-hot dish overflowing with passion and happiness ('情熱と幸せ溢れるアツアツの一枚を'), prizing the sensory experience of the teppan's heat, the aroma of the sauce, and bright, energetic service alongside quality cabbage, batter, and house sauce.

anecdotes.text: The team describes its approach as cooking 'at full power from 10 in the morning,' with all-counter seating so guests can watch every okonomiyaki being grilled in front of them.
Beyond the classic, the kitchen offers playful original toppings, including an 'ebi-mayo' shrimp-mayonnaise version finished with a house signature sauce they say is found nowhere else.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki is made from a wheat-flour batter and topped with wheat noodles, all cooked on a single shared teppan griddle alongside every other order, so cross-contact with wheat is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki sauce, a wheat-based Worcester-style sauce; no wheat-free or gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared griddle is used continuously for pork, egg, squid, oysters, and other animal ingredients, so any item would have animal-product cross-contact.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki (also Seto Inland Sea seafood)
neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (next to the Okonomi-mura building, near Alice Garden; ~3 min walk from Hatchobori tram stop)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-22
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区新天地5-22 returned [132.461945, 34.391453], within the Hiroshima box, consistent with the Shintenchi entertainment district in central Naka-ku.
seats_text: 68 seats total (counter 10, tables 58); first floor offers counter and table seating where you can watch the okonomiyaki cooked in front of you. Source pages vary (some list ~60), but the detailed listing gives 68.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (food L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-21:00 (food L.O. 20:30); closed Thursdays (open if Thursday is a holiday)
closed_days: Thursdays (open if Thursday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming local okonomiyaki shop where you can sit at the counter and watch your dish cooked; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a long way, though detailed allergy questions may be hard to convey.
gf_detail: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and wheat noodles (soba/udon) and is finished with wheat-and-soy-based okonomiyaki sauce, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Signature dishes contain pork, egg, squid, shrimp, oysters and cheese, and use wheat-and-soy-based sauce; the batter typically includes egg and dashi. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly across listings (68 on the detailed listing vs ~60 elsewhere). Hours and Thursday closure are consistent between the official site and the detailed listing.

bio.background: A long-running okonomiyaki shop in Hiroshima's Shintenchi district, standing next to the famous Okonomi-mura building near Alice Garden. The shop carries on the flavor of one of the founders behind Okonomi-mura and has been beloved by locals for about 50 years.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with soba noodles, plus seafood and teppanyaki; the house Soncho Deluxe (soba, squid, shrimp, cheese, green onion, pork and egg) and the soba-mochi-cheese okonomiyaki are signatures, alongside butter-grilled oysters and other Seto Inland Sea dishes.
bio.philosophy: Hiroshima-specialty okonomiyaki refined over 50 years, cooked on the teppan in front of guests.

anecdotes.text: The shop inherits the flavor of a founder of Okonomi-mura and has been refining its Hiroshima okonomiyaki for about 50 years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Dishes are cooked on a shared teppan (griddle) where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and noodles are prepared, making cross-contact with gluten effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki/Worcestershire-style sauce, which is wheat- and soy-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single shared teppan is used for pork, egg, seafood and cheese dishes, so any item would share the same cooking surface as animal products.

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・鉄板焼き
neighborhood: 中区新天地（八丁堀／新天地）お好み村4F
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 新天地プラザ 4F お好み村内
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 広島県広島市中区新天地5-13 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box, consistent with the Okonomimura building near Hatchobori, ~1km from the A-Bomb Dome.
seats_text: 14席（カウンター中心の小規模店）
closed_days: 日曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A small, late-night counter okonomiyaki spot inside the multi-shop Okonomimura building; staff are used to tourists and you can order by pointing at the menu, but expect a casual local atmosphere with little dedicated English support.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki shop. The okonomiyaki crepe batter is wheat flour, the dishes include wheat noodles (soba/yakisoba), and okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation documented. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Standard Hiroshima okonomiyaki and teppanyaki: dishes are built on pork, egg, and seafood (e.g., uni, squid), with wheat-soy okonomiyaki sauce and a layered egg/meat preparation. No vegan menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Opens evenings only and runs late (17:00 until roughly 03:00); closed Sundays. Budget runs higher than a typical single okonomiyaki because the recommended style is to graze on teppan small plates first and finish with okonomiyaki. The official okonomimura.jp shop page would not load during verification, so the descriptive story below is drawn from the building's directory listing and reservation-site description rather than the shop's own page.

bio.background: 八戒 is one of the individually run okonomiyaki stalls on the 4th floor of Okonomimura, the famous Hiroshima building where dozens of small okonomiyaki shops sit side by side on stacked floors. It is a compact, counter-style place open late into the night.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with crispy noodles, alongside a wide range of teppanyaki small plates; the suggested way to eat is to start with grilled appetizers and finish with the okonomiyaki. Popular orders include soba-niku-tama with cheese and seafood items.

anecdotes.text: Diners are encouraged to graze through teppanyaki small plates first and end the meal with an okonomiyaki featuring crispy pan-fried noodles, rather than ordering just a single pancake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cooking is done on a shared teppan griddle where wheat-batter okonomiyaki and wheat noodles are prepared, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is finished with okonomiyaki sauce, which is wheat-based; no allergen-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared teppan is used for pork, egg, and seafood dishes; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

name_ja: ガーデンハウス ヒロシマ
cuisine: North-California-inspired all-day cafe/dining: wood-fired pizza, pasta, pancakes, salads, using Seto Inland Sea ingredients and natural wine
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or claims found. Core offerings are wheat-based (wood-fired pizza on hand-stretched dough, pasta, ricotta pancakes), and there is no mention of gluten-free preparation, separate equipment, or a dedicated fryer. A celiac traveler should not assume any dish is safe without contacting the restaurant directly (050-1721-6566).
vegan_detail: A vegan/vegetarian menu is offered. Documented plant-based items include a falafel and vegetable plate at lunch, and the listing notes vegan and vegetarian menu options are available. Specific ingredients and cross-contact are not documented, so vegans should confirm details (e.g., dairy, egg, fish-derived stocks) with staff when ordering.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区基町15番地 ヒロパD棟102
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区基町15 returned [132.453812, 34.401646], within the Hiroshima box and consistent with the HiroPa complex in Hiroshima Central Park, a short walk north of the A-Bomb Dome / Peace Memorial Park and beside the Edion Peace Wing stadium.
neighborhood: 中区基町（広島中央公園・ヒロパ内、原爆ドーム／平和記念公園そば、エディオンピースウイング隣接）
seats_text: 66 seats total (about 46 indoor, 20 on the open terrace); the lawn terrace is pet-friendly and private events for up to ~70 can be accommodated.
hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (food L.O. 21:00, drink L.O. 21:30). Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a relaxed, modern park-side cafe used to tourists and casual walk-ins, with online reservations and card/QR payment, so a foreign student can comfortably drop in and point at the menu.
caveats: Closed-day sources mostly agree on Tuesday (Tabelog and official store page); one secondary article mentioned Wednesday, which appears to be outdated — treat Tuesday as the regular closing day but verify online if visiting on a Tuesday/Wednesday. Lunch L.O. and a shorter Monday schedule have been reported in some sources, so confirm same-day hours.

bio.background: GARDEN HOUSE began in Kamakura and grew into a small group of garden-side, all-day dining cafes. This Hiroshima outpost is the brand's seventh location and its first in the Chugoku region, opening inside the HiroPa complex in Hiroshima Central Park within easy walking distance of the A-Bomb Dome and Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: North-California-style all-day fare: hand-stretched pizza fired in a wood oven at around 450C, pasta, ricotta-cheese pancakes, and seasonal plates, paired with Japanese natural wine and Seto Inland Sea ingredients.
bio.philosophy: Built around 'local sourcing (chisan-chisho)' and 'handmade,' the cafe treats the surrounding park as one large garden, aiming for a seamless connection between the dining space and nature so guests can eat amid greenery.

anecdotes.text: The design intentionally blurs the line between the restaurant and the park, framing the green space as 'a large garden' so the shop and garden feel like one continuous, nature-filled place.
The signature Bismarck pizza is built on hand-stretched dough wood-fired at about 450C for roughly a minute, topped with cream-cheese sauce, egg, cheese and prosciutto.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on a wood-fired oven for pizza and serves pasta and pancakes, so flour is used heavily throughout; no gluten-free handling is documented.

positives.text: A vegan/vegetarian menu is offered, including a falafel and vegetable plate, giving plant-based diners a clear option.

cuisine: Health-conscious fermented-food cafe (yogurt, fermented dishes; some plant-based items, but menu includes chicken curry and dairy yogurt)
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or labeling is documented on the official site, menu page, or listing. The cafe centers on fermented foods and yogurt, with curry and rice-bowl dishes; whether any item is gluten-free and how cross-contamination is handled is not stated, so a celiac traveler would need to ask directly.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The signature products are dairy yogurt and smoothies made with milk (recreating a 1917 Hiroshima yogurt recipe), and lunch items include amazake butter chicken curry, so meat and dairy are present. There may be some plant-forward fermented or vegetable dishes, but no items are documented as vegan and none are labeled as such; confirm in person.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-1-13 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区紙屋町1-1-13 returned [132.459152, 34.394566], within the Hiroshima box; near Kamiyacho / the A-Bomb Dome area, consistent with the listed location (2-min walk from Hiroden Kamiyacho-Higashi).
neighborhood: 中区紙屋町（紙屋町東・胡町東、原爆ドーム近く）
seats_text: 40 seats (16 counter, 24 table)
hours_raw: Tue-Fri 11:00-18:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00-17:00; Closed Mondays. (Listing also breaks weekday service into 11:00-14:00 and 14:00-18:00 blocks.) Open 11:00-21:00 on event days per some sources.
closed_days: Mondays (irregular additional closures possible)
cultural_comfort_note: A bright, modern, family-friendly cafe in central Hiroshima where a few words of Japanese help with ordering, though the casual counter-and-table setup is easy to navigate for a foreign student.
caveats: Operated by Swedish probiotics company BioGaia, so it is a corporate-backed venue rather than an independent local shop. It doubles as a talk/seminar space, and hours can shift on event days. Dairy yogurt is central; no vegan or gluten-free labeling is documented, so dietary travelers should confirm specifics directly.

bio.background: BioGaia TALKS is a fermented-food cafe and dialogue space in central Hiroshima's Kamiyacho district, near the A-Bomb Dome. It is run by the Swedish probiotics maker BioGaia, built around the idea that good gut health and good conversation nourish people together. The cafe's signature is yogurt made with L. reuteri lactic-acid bacteria, including a plain yogurt that faithfully recreates what is described as Japan's oldest yogurt recipe, born in the Nomura family of Hiroshima in 1917 and revived by the fifth generation.
bio.philosophy: The space runs on the motto that 'knowledge is the finest entertainment,' pairing a healthy, fermentation-forward menu (kin-katsu, or 'bacteria wellness') with talk events where experts and guests share ideas face to face across medicine, science, food, and art. Greenery, a nursing room, and diaper-changing facilities make it a calm, family-friendly place to eat, read, work, or attend an event.
bio.specialty: L. reuteri yogurt and smoothies, plus body-friendly fermented lunch dishes such as amazake (sweet-sake) butter chicken curry and a neba (sticky-fermented) rice bowl, available for dine-in or takeout.

anecdotes.text: The plain yogurt recreates a recipe said to be Japan's oldest, born in Hiroshima's Nomura family in 1917 and faithfully revived by the fifth generation.
A new lunch lineup featuring fermentation-based dishes like amazake butter chicken curry and a neba rice bowl launched in September 2025, available both for dine-in and takeout.

positives.text: The kitchen is built around fermented foods and a body-friendly, health-conscious concept, so wholesome ingredients are a stated focus.

lead: もみじ饅頭 木村屋
name_ja: もみじ饅頭 木村屋
