cuisine: もみじ饅頭の小さな手焼き個人店（クリームチーズ・レーズン等のバリエーション、店頭で焼き立て、おこもみじ）
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町838
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 広島県廿日市市宮島町, landing on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box (lat 34.272606, lng 132.309982). Banchi-level (838) precision not available; this is the chō-level centroid on the island, which is expected for a 宮島町 address.
seats_text: 小規模な手焼きの店頭販売店。店頭で焼き立てを購入でき、イートインも可能とされるが、明確な座席数は確認できず。
hours_raw: 10:00〜17:00（不定休）。一部の情報源では9:00〜17:00との記載もあり。
closed_days: 不定休（決まった定休日なし）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter-service sweets stall on the main shopping street where you point and pay; no Japanese needed beyond a smile, and the freshly grilled cakes are easy to order on the go.
gf_detail: Momiji manju are made from a wheat-flour, egg-rich castella-style batter, so they contain gluten and are not gluten-free. No gluten-free options or claims were found.
vegan_detail: The cakes use an egg-rich wheat batter, and the signature fillings include dairy (Danish cream cheese), custard cream, and chocolate. Even the bean-paste versions are baked in the egg-and-flour shell, so nothing here is vegan. No vegan options were found.
caveats: No dedicated Tabelog detail page could be confirmed for this specific shop; Tabelog search results surface the separate, similarly named 木村家本店 and 菓子処きむら, so no tabelog_url is asserted to avoid misattribution. Hours vary slightly between sources (10:00-17:00 per official/rurubu, 9:00-17:00 per one blog); closures are irregular (不定休), so the weekly grid uses the standard 10:00-17:00 and hours_status is marked irregular. Seating count is unconfirmed. Address 宮島町838 differs from some Google Maps listings per one source.

bio.background: もみじ饅頭 木村屋は、宮島桟橋から表参道商店街に入ってすぐの角にある、もみじ饅頭の手焼き専門の小さな個人店です。店頭で一つひとつ焼き上げており、焼き立てのほかほかをその場で味わえます。
bio.specialty: 看板はデンマーク産ナチュラルクリームチーズを使ったクリームチーズもみじ饅頭で、なめらかで濃厚な味わいが人気を集め、売り切れることも多い一品です。ほかにこしあん・つぶあん・カスタードクリーム・チョコ・ラムに漬けたレーズンなど多彩なバリエーションを揃え、宮島名物のお好み焼きともみじ饅頭を掛け合わせた『おこもみじ』(店の登録商標)も名物です。
bio.philosophy: 厳選した材料と卵をたっぷり使った生地で焼き上げることにこだわっています。

anecdotes.text: デンマーク産ナチュラルクリームチーズを使ったクリームチーズもみじ饅頭が看板商品で、なめらかで濃厚な味わいから売り切れ必至の人気ぶり。
宮島名物のお好み焼きともみじ饅頭を組み合わせた独自のおやつ『おこもみじ』を考案し、店の登録商標としている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All momiji manju are baked from a shared wheat-flour batter on the same griddle, so the entire product line contains gluten with no gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The batter is egg- and flour-based and fillings include dairy cream cheese, custard, and chocolate; bean-paste versions share the same egg-flour shell, so there is no vegan-safe item.

cuisine: もみじ饅頭・和菓子（こし餡もみじ饅頭の老舗専門店）
neighborhood: 宮島・表参道商店街（厳島神社近く、宮島郵便局向かい）
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町541-12
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県廿日市市宮島町541-12 returned [132.331451, 34.348637], on Miyajima island within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Small takeaway-style sweets shop; freshly baked momiji manju sold to go, with a few items also available to eat on-site. Seat count not documented.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open daily).
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny old-fashioned family sweets stall on Miyajima's main street; little to no English, but ordering is simple point-and-pay (cash only), so a foreign student can easily buy a freshly baked momiji manju for about 90 yen.
gf_detail: Momiji manju are made from a wheat-flour castella-style batter, so they contain gluten. This is not a gluten-free option.
vegan_detail: Momiji manju batter is a castella-style sponge that typically contains egg (and often dairy/honey), so these are not vegan. The filling is koshi-an (smooth red bean paste), which is plant-based, but the cake itself is not. No vegan-specific information was found.
caveats: Cash only. Made-to-order and baked fresh through the day, so individual flavors may sell out; the shop specializes solely in koshi-an (smooth red bean paste) momiji manju with no other fillings.

bio.origin: 宮島（広島県廿日市市宮島町）, on Itsukushima island
bio.background: A long-running family momiji manju shop on Miyajima's main shopping street, across from the island post office and a short walk from the ferry terminal toward Itsukushima Shrine. It is believed to have been founded around 1923 (late Taisho era), giving it roughly a century of history, and is often described as one of the island's original momiji manju makers.
bio.specialty: Traditional hand-made koshi-an (smooth strained red bean paste) momiji manju — said to be Miyajima's only shop dedicated solely to the koshi-an style, with a fluffy soft batter and lightly sweetened house-made bean paste.
bio.philosophy: Rather than expanding into many flavors, the shop deliberately keeps to a single, authentic koshi-an momiji manju, baking fresh to order through the day with no leftover stock from the previous day, and keeping prices low (around 90 yen each).

anecdotes.text: The shop bakes its momiji manju fresh through the morning rather than selling pre-made stock; a visitor arriving around 10 a.m. found baking still in progress.
It is known as one of the most affordable momiji manju shops on Miyajima, at roughly 90 yen each, and offers discounts for school-trip students.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a wheat-flour momiji manju bakery, so the environment is inherently flour-heavy and not suitable for gluten avoidance.

lead: そば処 とんぼ 宮島店
name_ja: そば処 とんぼ 宮島店
cuisine: そば (soba / handmade buckwheat noodles)
neighborhood: 宮島（厳島）／中江・厳島神社近く
address_ja: 広島県廿日市市宮島町中江301
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [132.331451, 34.348637] for 広島県廿日市市宮島町中江301; lands on Miyajima island, within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Reported as a counter with about 14 seats on the first floor and a 16-seat tatami room on the second floor (roughly 30 total); seat count not stated on the restaurant's own page.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual tourist-area soba shop on Miyajima where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; do not expect English allergy support.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Soba noodles in Japan are typically blended with wheat flour (nihachi), the dipping/broth dashi uses wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu leans heavily on tempura (wheat batter) such as anago (eel) and shrimp tempura. No gluten-free preparation is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature dishes include anago (conger eel) and shrimp tempura sets, meat soba, duck nanban soba, and steamed/grilled oysters, and soba broth is normally fish-dashi based with wheat soy sauce. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: A soba restaurant set inside a folk house on Miyajima reported to be over 100 years old, in the Nakae area a short walk from Itsukushima Shrine. The aged wooden building is part of the appeal, pairing the island's traditional streetscape with a sit-down noodle meal.
bio.specialty: Soba served alongside hot, freshly fried tempura — including anago (conger eel) and shrimp tempura sets — plus regional touches like steamed oysters and deep-fried momiji manju for dessert. The zaru dipping sauce is described as aged for about two months in earthenware jars.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a reused folk house said to be more than 100 years old, with diners noting its Taisho/Showa-era character.
The zaru dipping sauce is reported to be carefully aged for about two months in earthenware jars, and tempura is served hot, fresh from the fryer.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu centers on soba and tempura-based sets, which in Japan use wheat-based soy sauce and dashi for the broth and dipping sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use fish, eel, duck, meat and oysters, and soba broth is typically fish-dashi based; no separate vegan prep is documented.

caveats: Seat layout (14 counter + 16 tatami) and the two-month sauce aging come from aggregator/review snippets, not the restaurant's own page; confirm on arrival. Hours are listed as a single daily window with irregular closures (不定休) — call ahead, as Miyajima shops often close early or on unannounced days.

cuisine: Korean home cooking and set meals (定食) — samgyeopsal, chijimi (pajeon), gejang, Korean ramen, and lunch teishoku
neighborhood: 広島市中区田中町 (Naka-ku, east of the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori entertainment district)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区田中町5-3
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [132.465454, 34.388157] for 広島県広島市中区田中町5-3, within the Hiroshima box. Location is in central Naka-ku, slightly east of the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori nightlife area, near Hijiyama-shita tram stop.
seats_text: Seat count not documented on any source consulted; described as a small casual local diner.
hours_raw: One source indicates the kitchen serves until around 22:30; full daily hours and closed days are not published on the listings consulted.
closed_days: Not documented on sources consulted.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only neighborhood spot run by what reviewers describe as a Korean couple with no documented English support, so a first-time foreign student would do best visiting with a Japanese- or Korean-speaking friend or a guide.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and seafood — grilled pork belly (samgyeopsal), marinated crab (gejang), and Korean ramen — and Korean staples here rely on wheat-based soy sauce and gochujang and egg-bound pancake (chijimi) batter, so there are no reliably vegan options documented.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly: chijimi (pajeon) pancakes are made from wheat-flour batter, Korean ramen is a wheat noodle, and the soy sauce and gochujang used in Korean cooking typically contain wheat. No gluten-free accommodations are documented.
caveats: Cash only and reservations not accepted. Despite the diner-style name, the cooking is Korean home-style; full opening hours and closed days are not published, so it is worth confirming hours before visiting. The Tabelog area code places it in the Nagarekawa/Yagenbori district, but the actual address (田中町) sits a little east of the nightlife core, closer to the Hijiyama-shita tram stop.

bio.background: A small, casual neighborhood diner in central Hiroshima where the cooking leans into Korean home-style dishes despite the plain shokudo (canteen) name. Reviewers describe it as run by a Korean couple serving generous, everyday Korean fare alongside set-meal lunches.
bio.specialty: Korean comfort dishes — chijimi (pajeon) praised for a crispy outside and chewy inside, samgyeopsal (grilled pork belly), gejang (marinated crab), and Korean ramen, plus lunch set meals.

anecdotes.text: Diners single out the chijimi as a standout — crispy on the surface and chewy inside — with one calling it some of the most delicious Korean food they had had.
A lunchtime chijimi set (チヂミランチ) is a noted draw at midday.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Korean cooking here uses standard soy sauce and gochujang, which typically contain wheat, and the pancake (chijimi) batter and Korean ramen are wheat-based.

cuisine: 郷土料理・海鮮・イタリアン（広島の地物の牡蠣・穴子・たこを和食とイタリアンの両方で）
neighborhood: 広島市中区大手町（本通り・袋町近く）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区大手町2-5-5 青木ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (大手町2-5-5) via GSI; result 34.392391, 132.455292 falls within the central Hiroshima box, near Fukuro-machi / Hondori.
seats_text: About 34 seats (counter 7, tables 18, sunken/zashiki 16); private rooms for 2 to 20+ people. (Some listings state ~40 total.)
hours_raw: Dinner 17:00–24:00 daily (holidays included). May close earlier when ingredients run out. Some aggregator listings additionally show morning/lunch service (9:00–14:30); the primary listing shows evening-only.
closed_days: 不定休（Irregular holidays）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed local izakaya-style spot where some basic Japanese will help, since menus and the owner's off-menu daily specials are described mainly in Japanese; pointing at the seafood and a friendly attitude go a long way.
gf_detail: No gluten-free provisions are documented. The kitchen leans heavily on wheat: Italian pasta and bread, fried oysters (kaki fry) and other breaded/fried items, plus Japanese dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce. No dedicated fryer or GF menu is mentioned anywhere; a celiac traveler should not rely on this place.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood- and meat-centered restaurant. Signature dishes are oysters (raw, grilled, fried), anago (conger eel), octopus, sashimi, T-bone steak, and game meat (venison, wild boar). Stocks and seasonings rely on fish/dashi and wheat soy sauce. There is no documented vegan menu; a strict vegan would find almost nothing suitable and should choose elsewhere.

bio.background: An independent, owner-run Hiroshima eatery in the Otemachi/Fukuro-machi area, a short walk from the Hondori shopping street. Despite a traditional Japanese-looking exterior, the kitchen is led by a trained Italian chef who previously ran a well-known local Italian restaurant for about twelve years before opening here.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Setouchi (Seto Inland Sea) seafood prepared in both Japanese and Italian styles: oysters sourced directly from producers in Kure (Ondo) and Osaki served raw, grilled, or fried; white-grilled local anago with sea salt and wasabi; grilled octopus; and hearty meat dishes like a signature T-bone steak and seasonal game.
bio.philosophy: Let prized Hiroshima land-and-sea ingredients shine, cooking them simply but expertly across two culinary traditions rather than confining the food to one style.

anecdotes.text: Diners expecting straightforward Japanese washoku from the traditional signage are often surprised to find an Italian chef, an extensive wine list, and T-bone steak alongside the oysters and anago.

The owner sometimes offers a generous off-menu set bowl only when the mood strikes him; one visitor was served a mushroom-and-chicken rice with salad, several small side dishes, pickles, and miso soup, the lineup changing with whatever is in stock.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes breaded/deep-fried oysters (kaki fry) and other fried items shared with wheat-based cooking; no dedicated gluten-free fryer or protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese-style dishes use standard soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on seafood (oysters, anago, octopus, sashimi) and meat; vegetable dishes would share equipment and fish/dashi-based seasonings.

caveats: Hours vary by source: the primary listing shows evening-only service (17:00–24:00) while some aggregators also list a 9:00–14:30 morning/lunch block — confirm directly. Closures are irregular (不定休) and the kitchen may close early once ingredients sell out. Seat count is listed as ~34 on the main source and ~40 on another. No vegan or gluten-free accommodations are documented; allergy-sensitive diners should phone ahead.

lead: 餃子家 龍 新天地本店
name_ja: 餃子家 龍 新天地本店
cuisine: Gyoza, Chinese, izakaya
neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (near Hatchobori and Ebisucho)
gf_detail: This is a gyoza and Chinese izakaya, and gluten is everywhere here. Standard gyoza wrappers are made with wheat flour (the official site notes their pan-fried gyoza use 100% domestic wheat). Soy sauce, dipping sauces and most fried/seasoned dishes contain wheat. There is one "rice-flour water gyoza" (米粉水餃子) on the menu, but the broth and seasonings are not documented as gluten-free and the kitchen handles wheat throughout, so cross-contact is very likely. No gluten-free program is advertised. Not suitable for someone with celiac disease or strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood izakaya: the gyoza are filled with pork and Hiroshima ingredients, and signature dishes like mapo tofu and the chili-oil chicken are meat-based. Soy sauce and dashi-style seasonings are used throughout. A few sides could be vegetable-leaning (gyoza-skin chips with lemon pepper, certain vegetable plates), but nothing is confirmed vegan and there is no vegan/vegetarian menu. A vegan would have very few safe choices and should not rely on this place for a meal.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地1-18 立木ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区新天地1-18 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 68 seats (counter seating at the front, table seating in the larger space at the back); the official site phrases it as roughly 58 capacity, but listings consistently give 68.
hours_raw: Sun-Thu & holidays: 16:00-00:00 (L.O. 23:30); Fri-Sat & day before holidays: 16:00-02:00 (L.O. 01:30). Closed Dec 31 and Jan 1.
closed_days: Open daily except Dec 31 and Jan 1 (year-end/New Year closure).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, lively local gyoza izakaya where orders are placed on a touchscreen panel and many payment methods (cards, IC, QR) are accepted, so a foreign student can manage comfortably with minimal Japanese, though the menu and staff are primarily Japanese.
caveats: Seat count varies slightly by source (official site implies ~58, listings say 68). Dietary details (gluten, vegan, allergens, frying setup) are not published anywhere found; assessments above are inferred conservatively from the documented menu and cuisine type.

bio.background: Gyozaya Ryu's Shintenchi honten (flagship) sits on Chuo-dori in the heart of Hiroshima's Shintenchi nightlife district, a few minutes' walk from Hatchobori. It has become one of the city's go-to gyoza spots, the kind of name locals reflexively suggest when asked where to eat dumplings.
bio.specialty: Hand-wrapped pan-fried gyoza made with 100% domestic wheat flour and stuffed with pork and Hiroshima-area ingredients, delivered fresh daily from the group's own gyoza factory. The menu fans out into playful local riffs such as Carp gyoza (red-skinned, a nod to the hometown baseball team) and okonomiyaki-sauce gyoza, alongside Chinese izakaya staples like a Sichuan-pepper-forward mapo tofu and chili-oil chicken.
bio.philosophy: The shop wears its love of dumplings openly, with the slogan 'NO GYOZA, NO LIFE!' and a commitment to making gyoza fresh every day at a dedicated factory.

anecdotes.text: In a crowdsourced poll of dozens of Hiroshima diners naming their favorite gyoza shops, Gyozaya Ryu placed among the top picks, which is what drew one food blogger to visit.
The red-skinned 'Carp gyoza' is described as a team-approved item 'filled with meat juice and Carp love,' tying the shop to Hiroshima's beloved baseball club.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-heavy kitchen producing pan-fried wheat-flour gyoza, fried items and noodle-style dishes; gluten cross-contact is very likely and no gluten-free handling is advertised.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese/Chinese soy sauce and sauces are used across the menu (e.g., okonomiyaki-sauce gyoza); these contain wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Gyoza are pork-filled and most dishes are meat- or seafood-based with soy/dashi seasonings cooked on shared equipment; no vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: Italian-based creative cuisine / bistro / izakaya
neighborhood: Kanayamacho / Hatchobori area, Naka-ku
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. This is an Italian-based bistro/izakaya whose menu centers on pasta, bread, fried items, and dishes plated with wheat-containing sauces; nothing on the official site or listing indicates a gluten-free menu or handling.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian program is documented. Signature dishes are meat- and seafood-forward (beef steak, Iberico chorizo, mussels in white wine, herb-butter beef). While they advertise fresh organic vegetables (e.g. whole roasted broccoli with garlic oil), there is no indication these are prepared without animal products, so a strictly vegan diner cannot be assured here without asking.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区胡町2-11
geocode_note: Geocoded from official/listing address 広島県広島市中区胡町2-11; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box.
seats_text: 54 seats, with counter and table seating; private rooms available and partial/full buyout possible for groups of 20-50+.
hours_raw: 18:00 - 02:00 (L.O. 01:00). Closed Tuesdays. Open year-end/New Year period 12/18-1/2. (Note: official site lists a temporary closure 5/13-15 and states normally open Tuesdays; the listing indicates Tuesday as the regular closed day.)
closed_days: Tuesday (regular). Occasional temporary closures noted on official site.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, owner-run Italian izakaya where ordering and signage are in Japanese; a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese or a translation app will manage fine, and the relaxed atmosphere is welcoming.
caveats: Open late (until 2:00) and oriented toward drinking with food; no documented gluten-free or vegan provisions, so diners with strict dietary needs should ask directly. Note a minor discrepancy between sources on whether Tuesday is the regular closed day.

bio.background: A casual, independently run Italian-based eatery in central Hiroshima near the Hatchobori/Kanayamacho area. It serves original Italian dishes in an unpretentious, everyday setting, with the owner personally selecting the wines.
bio.specialty: Creative Italian home-style cooking such as a whole roasted broccoli with garlic oil, beef steak, Iberico chorizo with dried figs, and mussels in white wine, paired with barrel-aged self-serve wines (red, white, sparkling) kept fresh by a wine-serving system.
bio.philosophy: Casual Italian dining without pretense, using fresh organic vegetables from contracted farmers and accessibly priced wines chosen by the owner.

anecdotes.text: The shop installs a wine-serving system that protects barrel wine from air and light to keep its fresh taste, with self-serve barrel wine available to guests.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu includes a herb-butter soy-flavored beef steak; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so soy-seasoned dishes here should be assumed to contain gluten.

positives.text: The kitchen uses fresh organic vegetables from contracted farmers, so plant-forward sides may be available on request.

lead: 袋町ワイン食堂 LE JYAN JYAN
name_ja: 袋町ワイン食堂 LE JYAN JYAN
name_en: Fukuromachi Wine Shokudo LE JYAN JYAN (Le Jyan Jyan)
cuisine: Wine bistro / izakaya (French-Italian-leaning); handmade hamburger steak, house-made oil sardines, vegetable-forward small plates, 200+ wines
neighborhood: Fukuro-cho, Naka-ku, near Hondori arcade (about 5 min from the A-Bomb Dome)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区袋町4-1 袋町産業ビル 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区袋町4-1 returned [132.458298, 34.39233], within the Hiroshima box; matches the central-Hiroshima Fukuro-cho location near Hondori.
seats_text: 55 seats total, including about 20 counter seats; an oval shared table seats up to about 12.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sunday
closed_days: Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed evening wine spot where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the daily counter display of fish and vegetables will get you a warm welcome, though detailed allergy questions may need a translation app.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a wine bistro built around hamburger steak, pates, oil-cured sardines and wine-friendly small plates, where wheat-based sauces, flour and standard soy sauce are common; nothing on the official site or listings indicates dedicated GF preparation. A GF diner would need to ask in advance and verify each dish.
vegan_detail: The kitchen genuinely emphasizes vegetables, using produce from contract and own farms, and a vegetarian menu is noted in listings, so plant-forward plates do exist. However, the house is meat- and seafood-centric (signature hamburger steak, oil sardines, pates) with no documented fully vegan dishes or vegan-cross-contact controls; butter, cheese, eggs and stock are likely in many vegetable dishes. A vegan should call ahead and confirm preparation.
caveats: No chef/owner name is documented publicly. Vegetarian-menu availability is noted in listings but no fully vegan or gluten-free items are confirmed; dietary travelers should confirm directly. Operated by a restaurant group (in-smart), so not a solo independent.

bio.background: A袋町 (Fukuro-cho) wine shokudo just off the Hondori arcade in central Hiroshima, set on the second floor of the Fukuro-cho Industrial Building. It bills itself as a grown-up wine tavern, the kind of place where you settle in at the counter or around a big shared oval table for an unhurried evening.
bio.philosophy: 肩ひじ張らず、ワインを愉しむ — enjoy wine without pretension. Rather than handing over a fixed wine list, the staff talk with guests to pour something that fits the mood and the food that night.
bio.specialty: Around 200-250 affordably curated wines (15+ by the glass, rotating daily) paired with a vegetable-forward, wine-friendly kitchen. Standout plates include a hand-kneaded hamburger steak that has been on the menu since opening and house-made oil-cured sardines, with daily fish and vegetables displayed market-style at the counter.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on vegetables from contract farms and its own farm, treating produce as a centerpiece alongside the wine rather than an afterthought.
The hand-kneaded hamburger steak and the house-made oil sardines have been signatures since the restaurant first opened, kept on the menu by loyal regulars.

positives.text: Kitchen makes a point of using fresh vegetables from contract and own farms, so plant-based plates are part of the everyday menu.

lead: 広島らーめん たかひろ
name_ja: 広島らーめん たかひろ
cuisine: 広島ラーメン・おでん・居酒屋 (Hiroshima-style ramen, oden, izakaya)
neighborhood: エキニシ (Ekinishi), 広島市南区大須賀町, near JR Hiroshima Station
address_ja: 広島県広島市南区大須賀町12-6
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates [132.472427, 34.398315] for 広島県広島市南区大須賀町12-6, within the Hiroshima bounding box.
seats_text: 1F counter 7 seats; 2F private room(s) seating roughly 5-7
closed_days: Monday and Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 7-seat counter spot run by locals where Japanese is expected; come with a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app, and note it is cash-only.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen uses wheat noodles and a shoyu (wheat-based soy sauce) tonkotsu broth, and the oden and izakaya dishes also rely on soy sauce and other wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature ramen is built on a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with chashu pork, and oyster ramen and oden feature seafood and animal-based dashi. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Documented backstory is limited; opening/renovation around August 1, 2016 and the oyster-ramen specialty come from third-party listings rather than the shop itself. No official website found. Hours and details should be reconfirmed locally as small independent shops change schedules.

bio.background: A small, independent ramen-and-oden counter shop tucked into the lively Ekinishi district just west of Hiroshima Station, a short walk from the platforms. With only seven seats at the counter downstairs and a small private room upstairs, it has the intimate feel of a neighborhood late-night spot where regulars and after-work drinkers gather.
bio.origin: Reported to have opened (with a renovation) around August 1, 2016.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style ramen, with an oyster ramen that draws on the region's famous oysters; the broth is a classic Hiroshima shoyu-tonkotsu (soy and pork-bone) style with a touch of sweetness and straight noodles, topped simply with chashu, green onion, bean sprouts and menma. Oden and izakaya plates round out the menu, making it a popular finishing stop after drinks.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the ramen as gentle and clean-tasting with a slightly sweet broth in the nostalgic Hiroshima style, well suited as a closing dish after a night out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ramen is a Hiroshima-style shoyu (soy sauce) tonkotsu, and soy sauce in Japan is normally wheat-based; oden broth is also soy-seasoned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with chashu pork; oyster ramen and oden include seafood, so animal products are integral throughout the kitchen.

cuisine: Teppanyaki and Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki (seafood and local-beef izakaya)
neighborhood: Shintenchi, Naka-ku (Nagarekawa/Hatchobori nightlife district, within the Peace Park area)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a teppanyaki/okonomiyaki izakaya: the okonomiyaki (ようじろう焼き, 広島スペシャル, 肉玉そば) is built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and savory dishes lean on wheat-containing soy sauce and ponzu. Some naturally gluten-free items exist in principle (sashimi, grilled beef, oysters, grilled vegetables) but there is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation area, and no stated allergy handling. A celiac or strongly gluten-sensitive diner should not rely on this restaurant.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is centered on fresh fish/sashimi (morning-caught Toyoshima fish), oysters, octopus, Sera beef steak/tataki, bacon and egg-based teppanyaki dishes, and okonomiyaki containing egg, pork and wheat. The few plant-based-looking items (grilled potato salad, mushroom butter) use dairy/butter and share the teppan grill with fish and meat. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区新天地1-11 新天地シャトル館 4F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at block level (新天地1-11); coordinates fall within the Hiroshima validity box and land in central Naka-ku, consistent with the Shintenchi entertainment district.
seats_text: 23 seats (7 counter seats facing the teppan grill, 16 table seats); listed maximum capacity around 30.
hours_raw: 17:00 - 02:00 (open daily, including holidays and pre-holiday days)
closed_days: No regular closing day (open daily; opening hours may shift on holidays).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a late-night local teppanyaki izakaya with counter and table seating where ordering is in Japanese; a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) can enjoy it, but no English support is documented.
caveats: The original brief listed the area as 流川町, but the confirmed address is 新天地1-11 (Shintenchi), one block over within the same Nagarekawa/Hatchobori nightlife zone in central Naka-ku. It is a brand-new restaurant (opened May 1, 2026), so hours and menu may still settle. Reservations are accepted and recommended but not strictly required. No official website or SNS was found.

bio.background: 鉄板 ようじろう is a small, independently run teppanyaki and Hiroshima-okonomiyaki spot that opened on May 1, 2026, on the 4th floor of a building in the Shintenchi area of central Hiroshima, deep in the Nagarekawa nightlife district. The chef trained at established teppanyaki and Japanese restaurants before striking out to open this place of his own.
bio.specialty: Casual teppanyaki built around local Setouchi ingredients: morning-caught fish from Toyoshima served as sashimi, Hiroshima oysters prepared butter-sautéed, ponzu or ahijo-style, Sera 'minori' beef as tataki and steak, and house okonomiyaki including the signature ようじろう焼き.
bio.philosophy: An approachable counter-and-table spot where you can watch the cooking on the iron plate and taste Hiroshima specialties paired with local sake, in a relaxed izakaya atmosphere rather than a formal teppanyaki room.

anecdotes.text: The chef honed his craft at major teppanyaki and Japanese restaurants before opening this counter, where he grills morning-caught Toyoshima fish and Sera beef in front of guests.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki uses a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, and is cooked on the same shared iron griddle as all other dishes, so gluten cross-contact on the teppan is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory teppanyaki and ponzu-dressed dishes (e.g., octopus with ponzu) rely on Japanese soy sauce / ponzu, which standardly contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Nearly all dishes involve fish, shellfish, beef, pork or egg, and plant items are cooked on the same teppan as seafood and meat; no separated vegan preparation is described.

cuisine: Relaxed cafe and dining bar serving gentle comfort food and house-made sweets, with buffet-style lunches (rotating pasta, gratin, plate mains), salads, and the signature single-serving BeBe chiffon cakes and parfaits.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on wheat-based items — pasta, bread, gratin, hamburger plates, and especially the signature chiffon cakes (made with wheat flour). No dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer is mentioned anywhere. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would have very little they could safely eat here.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The cafe is built around dairy-rich sweets (chiffon cakes, parfaits, cheese fondue-style dishes) and dishes with meat and fish (hamburger plates, ham-and-spinach cream pasta). No vegan menu or labeling is documented. A vegan would find essentially nothing suitable; a vegetarian could possibly assemble something from the salad buffet but with no guarantees.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通9-2 ル・フェール広島本通りⅡ 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 広島県広島市中区本通9-2 via GSI; result (34.392273, 132.461487) falls within the Hiroshima bounding box, in the Hondori arcade area near Hiroshima PARCO and Tatemachi tram stop in central Naka Ward.
neighborhood: 本通 / Hondori arcade, Naka Ward — in front of Hiroshima PARCO, near Tatemachi tram stop
seats_text: 56 seats, all non-smoking, with a mix of tables and sofa seating.
hours_raw: Sun-Thu: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Fri, Sat & day before holidays: 11:30-00:00 (L.O. 23:00)
closed_days: No regular closed day documented (open daily; hours vary by day).
cultural_comfort_note: A bright, casual cafe in the busy Hondori arcade used to a steady flow of customers, where a foreign student can comfortably get by with basic Japanese greetings, pointing at the menu, and the buffet-style self-serve lunch format.
caveats: This is a branch of the Diamond Dining (DD Holdings) restaurant group, not an independent local shop. Lunch is buffet-style with self-serve salad, bread, and drinks. Tabelog lists it as not taking reservations, though some booking platforms offer online seats; menus and dishes are wheat- and dairy-heavy with no documented vegan or gluten-free options.

bio.background: A relaxed cafe and dining bar in central Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, directly across from Hiroshima PARCO and a short walk from the Tatemachi tram stop. The space is designed with a natural, wood-toned interior meant to feel like a quiet forest retreat in the middle of the city.
bio.philosophy: The cafe describes its aim as offering body-friendly, gentle comfort food and sweets in a natural, relaxing space where conversation can flourish, paired with warm hospitality. Its concept is inspired by Marie Antoinette's private garden retreat, the Petit Trianon.
bio.specialty: The signature is the house-made 'BeBe' chiffon cake — a personal-sized whole chiffon roughly 15cm across that is meant for one person, with seasonal flavors such as strawberry-chocolate and pear with caramel nuts. Buffet-style lunches rotate between pasta, gratin, and plate mains (including a handmade demi-glace hamburger) and come with self-serve salad, bread, and drinks.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's concept draws on Marie Antoinette's secret hideaway, the Petit Trianon, with antique wood and wall decoration meant to evoke a small forest retreat in the heart of the city.
It is best known for the BeBe chiffon, a single-serving whole chiffon cake about 15cm in diameter that one person can finish alone.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki
neighborhood: Shintenchi / Nagarekawa-Yagenbori entertainment district, Naka-ku
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区三川町1-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区三川町1-1 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box, near the Shintenchi/Nagarekawa entertainment area.
seats_text: Around 24-30 seats (counter and sofa seating).
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A lively late-night local teppanyaki spot in the entertainment district where a little basic Japanese will help, as English support is unlikely and the vibe is casual and locally oriented.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The okonomiyaki batter and noodles (soba/yakisoba) are wheat-based, and dishes are finished with wheat-containing okonomiyaki/teppanyaki sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on pork, egg, hormone/offal (パワフル焼き), and fish-based items, with wheat-and-soy okonomiyaki sauce and bonito/fish stock common in this style. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Hours and seat counts are drawn from listing aggregators and may change; confirm directly before visiting. No allergy or dietary accommodation information could be found.

bio.background: A small, independently run teppanyaki and okonomiyaki house tucked into Hiroshima's Shintenchi-Nagarekawa nightlife quarter, just off the bustle of Nagarekawa near Shintenchi Park. It runs late into the small hours, making it a go-to for a hot, sizzling meal after a night out.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki built on the griddle, alongside an unusually broad teppanyaki lineup. Signature plates include the house 川創焼き, hearty 肉玉そば (pork-egg-noodle okonomiyaki), offal-rich パワフル焼き, and inventive griddle dishes like charcoal-infused noodles and curry yakisoba.

anecdotes.text: Regulars treat it as a late-night refuge: it stays open well past midnight, energetically firing the griddle for diners coming off the drinking streets of Nagarekawa.
The kitchen leans into creativity beyond standard okonomiyaki, with playful one-offs such as charcoal-black noodles and a curry yakisoba that have caught the eye of local food bloggers.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki izakaya
neighborhood: Nagarekawa (Hatchobori / Nagarekawa nightlife district), Naka-ku, Hiroshima
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki spot. The okonomiyaki batter and the noodles layered into it are wheat-based, and grilled/teppan dishes are typically seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce and okonomiyaki sauce. No gluten-free menu, preparation, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoiders.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat-and-noodle okonomiyaki, grilled skewers, oysters, beef tongue, and other animal-based teppanyaki dishes; okonomiyaki normally contains egg and the savory sauces are not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区流川町8-11 中川ビル2-1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 広島県広島市中区流川町8-11; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima Nagarekawa nightlife district, inside the expected bounding box.
seats_text: 23 seats total — 7 counter seats facing the teppan plus four 4-person tables.
hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays 18:00–02:00 (food & drink L.O. 01:00); closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a late-night local nightlife-district izakaya where staff cook on the teppan in front of you; English support is not documented and basic Japanese will make ordering much smoother, so it suits an adventurous student comfortable with a Japanese-language setting.
caveats: All seats are smoking and entry is restricted to those 20 and over. It opens only in the evening and runs late (until 2:00 AM). Okonomiyaki/teppanyaki dishes are wheat- and animal-based, so it does not work for gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian diners. No documented English menu or allergy handling.

bio.background: A casual teppanyaki and okonomiyaki spot tucked into the Nagarekawa nightlife quarter of central Hiroshima, the kind of place locals slip into after dark for the city's soul food. Cooks work the iron griddle right in front of guests, so the meal doubles as a bit of theater.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki layered with noodles, plus teppan-grilled skewers and griddle dishes such as roasted tomato topped with melted cheese.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on Hiroshima regional ingredients — noodles from local makers, regional vegetables, and Hiroshima specialties like oysters and beef tongue, paired with a deep list of Hiroshima sake.

anecdotes.text: The drink list leans hard into Hiroshima's own breweries, pouring local sake such as Fukucho from Imada Shuzo in Akitsu, alongside Hoken and Miwazakura.
The counter seats directly facing the teppan are treated as the best seats in the house, where guests can chat with the friendly staff as the food is cooked.

cuisine: 紅茶専門カフェ（個人経営の喫茶店・軽食）
neighborhood: 広島市中区本通（本通商店街）
gf_detail: Tea-focused shop with over 100 varieties of black, flavored, and herbal teas, which are naturally gluten-free. However, the food side leans on wheat: the cafe is known for scones, brownies, macarons, and afternoon-tea pastries, all of which contain wheat flour. Lunch sets include rice-based, vegetable-forward dishes that may be more suitable, but no gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is documented. A celiac or strongly gluten-sensitive guest would need to stick to plain tea and ask carefully about any food item; no allergen handling is published.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. Plain teas (black, flavored, herbal) are vegan, but milk is offered for tea. Lunch is described as vegetable-forward, but documented dishes include a shrimp and eggplant-tomato stew (not vegan), and the signature sweets (scones, brownies, macarons) typically contain dairy and eggs. There is no stated vegan menu. A vegan could likely enjoy a pot of plain tea, but should ask directly about any food item and milk-free options.
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-29 本通赤松薬局ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (chō-banchi level: 広島市中区本通8-29) via GSI. Result lat 34.392925, lng 132.459442 falls within the central Hiroshima box and matches the Hondori arcade location.
seats_text: 28 seats: twelve 2-person tables plus 4 counter seats.
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (daily). Closed January 1st. (Some listings describe a daytime cafe 11:00-17:00 and evening cafe 17:00 onward, but the operating window is 11:00-20:00.)
closed_days: Open daily; closed January 1st (元日).
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, clean second-floor tea salon where staff are described as warm and friendly; a foreign student can comfortably order tea and snacks with a greeting and a few words or by pointing, though no English menu is documented.
caveats: This is a tea (紅茶) specialty cafe rather than a coffee-focused kissaten as the target description suggested; coffee is not its main offering. Located on the 2nd floor (up a staircase). Dietary details below are inferred from menu descriptions; no formal allergen or vegan/GF program is published, so confirm directly.

bio.background: Linden is a small, independently run tea house tucked on the second floor of a building along Hiroshima's Hondori shopping arcade, a short walk from the city center. You climb a staircase to reach a simple, clean, light-filled room of small tables and a short counter, an unhurried space that welcomes solo visitors and pairs alike.
bio.specialty: Tea is the heart of the place: the shop pours from a deep selection of black, flavored, and herbal teas, including its own original blends, and you can buy loose leaves to take home as well as drink in. Alongside the tea, the kitchen sends out afternoon-tea sets and light, vegetable-leaning lunches, with house sweets such as scones, matcha brownies, and macarons.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe an impressively long tea list and order several pots in a sitting, each noticeably different in taste and aroma, paired with savory plates like a shrimp and eggplant-tomato stew with rice.
The room is praised as simple and clean, comfortable for both women and men, with warm, smiling service that makes the climb to the second floor worthwhile.

positives.text: Plain black, flavored, and herbal teas are served by the pot and are naturally free of gluten and animal products, making a tea-only visit straightforward.
Lunch options are described as vegetable-forward, which may give more flexibility for plant-based diners who ask about ingredients.

name_ja: メランジュ ドゥ シュハリ 広島
cuisine: クレープ・マカロン・パンケーキ（フレンチカフェ＆パティスリー）
neighborhood: 広島市中区本通（本通商店街）
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区本通8-8
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島県広島市中区本通8-8 returned [132.459503, 34.392471], within the central Hiroshima box, on the Hontori shopping arcade.
seats_text: Around 28 seats, including sofa areas; tables can be combined for larger groups.
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (food and drink last order 19:00), open daily
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, walk-in-friendly café on the main Hontori arcade where a foreign student can comfortably order pastries and pancakes with a few basic Japanese words and pointing at the display.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan offerings. The menu centers on crepes, pancakes, and macarons made with fermented butter, cream, eggs, and dairy, so the core items are not vegan, and the shop publishes no plant-based options.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature crepes and pancakes are made with French wheat flour, and macarons and cakes are flour-based as well. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented.
caveats: The Tabelog listing (34010946) corresponds to the original MELANGE De SHUHARI 広島 café-patisserie at 本通8-8 (macarons, pancakes, crepes, cakes). The brand also runs a nearby macaron takeout counter at 本通6-8 and a separate Creperie at 本通9-19 3F; details here are for the 本通8-8 flagship.

bio.background: A Hiroshima-born French-style cafe and patisserie on the Hontori shopping arcade, part of a small local group of MELANGE De SHUHARI shops that has grown to include a macaron takeout counter and a separate creperie nearby on the same arcade.
bio.specialty: Colorful macarons, made-to-order crepes and pancakes (using French wheat flour and fermented butter), and seasonal cakes and afternoon-tea sets.

anecdotes.text: The signature crepe is built on a simple butter-and-sugar concept, with add-ons like raw chocolate, custard, whipped cream, and granola, and arrives more voluminous than expected after a short made-to-order wait.
The interior is a white-based, modern and refined space with sofa seating, designed for a leisurely afternoon over pancakes and tea.

cuisine: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki izakaya
neighborhood: Nagarekawa / Shintenchi (Horikawa-cho), Naka-ku
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区堀川町1-29 パリスビル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded to 広島県広島市中区堀川町1-29 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box, in the Nagarekawa/Shintenchi nightlife district.
seats_text: 16 seats, mostly compact counter-style seating.
hours_raw: 16:00-02:00 daily (incl. holidays); closures irregular (不定休)
closed_days: No fixed closing day (irregular closures / 不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny late-night counter okonomiyaki/teppanyaki spot in a busy nightlife district, so a little Japanese helps; staff are unlikely to speak much English and there is no English menu confirmed.
gf_detail: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is built on a wheat-flour crepe batter and finished with wheat-based okonomi sauce, and teppanyaki/izakaya dishes typically use wheat-containing soy sauce, so this is not a safe choice for gluten-free diners. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Hiroshima okonomiyaki (egg, pork, often noodles), teppanyaki, horumon (offal), and seasonal items such as oyster dishes, all typically prepared with egg, meat, seafood, and fish/wheat-based sauces. No vegan options are documented and the kitchen is a shared teppan, so it is not suitable for vegans.
caveats: No official website or detailed chef/owner story could be confirmed from fetched pages; hours and seat count are from the listing and may change. Closures are irregular, so confirm before visiting late at night.

bio.specialty: Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and teppanyaki served late into the night

cuisine: 広島風お好み焼き・もんじゃ
neighborhood: 中区薬研堀／流川
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区薬研堀7-2
geocode_note: Geocoded to street level (薬研堀7-2) via GSI; within Hiroshima box.
seats_text: Counter seating for 7 and table seating for 10 (17 total).
closed_days: 日曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny late-night neighborhood okonomiyaki and monja counter in the Nagarekawa nightlife district; little or no English is likely, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student feel comfortable.
gf_detail: This is a Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki and monjayaki shop. Okonomiyaki batter and the noodles, as well as monjayaki, are wheat-based, and the savory sauces typically contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodation, so it is not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: A traditional okonomiyaki/monja griddle shop where dishes typically include pork, egg, seafood, bonito-based dashi/toppings, and wheat-based savory sauce. No vegan or vegetarian offerings are documented, so it is not suitable for plant-based diners.
caveats: No editorial profile, owner background, or allergy/cross-contamination information was found on the pages reviewed. Hours and seating are from listing data; verify before a late-night visit.

cuisine: Ramen and tsukemen — lunch tsukesoba, dinner miso ramen
neighborhood: Nakamachi, Naka-ku (Fukuromachi/Tatemachi area, east of Peace Memorial Park)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区中町1-13 アルティメイトビル 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 広島県広島市中区中町1-13 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Hiroshima box near the Peace Memorial Park area.
seats_text: 17 seats total — 3 counter seats and 14 table seats
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures — no fixed day off)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small, casual owner-run noodle shop where ordering is straightforward; basic Japanese or pointing at the menu works well, but expect little to no English support.
gf_detail: This is a ramen and tsukemen shop where the core dish is wheat noodles, so the menu is fundamentally not gluten-free. Broths and tare typically contain wheat-based soy sauce and miso, and there is no indication of gluten-free options or accommodations.
vegan_detail: The shop centers on meat-based ramen and tsukemen with chashu pork, and broths for ramen and dipping soup are animal-based. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian option.
caveats: Closed days are irregular (不定休), so check the shop's Instagram before visiting. Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. The lunch and dinner menus differ entirely: tsukemen-style dipping noodles at midday and miso ramen at night.

bio.background: A small noodle shop that opened on December 3, 2024 on the second floor of a building in Nakamachi, central Hiroshima, a short walk east of Peace Memorial Park.
bio.specialty: A two-faced noodle concept: at lunch the shop serves dipping-style tsukesoba, and at dinner it switches entirely to miso ramen, offering white, red, and black miso variations including a garlic miso butter bowl.
bio.philosophy: The owner takes the noodles themselves seriously, sourcing lunch tsukemen noodles from Aichi and the evening miso ramen noodles from Hokkaido to suit each style of dish.

anecdotes.text: The lunch dipping noodles and the dinner miso ramen use different noodles sourced from different regions — Aichi for the lunch tsukemen and Hokkaido for the evening miso ramen.
The dual lunch-tsukemen, dinner-miso-ramen format gives regulars a reason to come back at different times of day to try both sides of the menu.

cuisine: Hiroshima cold noodles (広島冷麺) and Hiroshima tsukemen; izakaya in the evenings
neighborhood: Eba Sakaemachi, Naka Ward (southwest-central Hiroshima)
address_ja: 広島県広島市中区江波栄町1-14 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 広島市中区江波栄町1-14 returned [132.429688, 34.364807], within the Hiroshima box.
seats_text: About 10-12 counter seats in a U/L-shape around the open kitchen, plus one table for 4 (roughly 15 total). 6 parking spaces.
hours_raw: Tue/Thu/Sun/holidays 11:30-14:00; Wed/Fri/Sat 11:30-14:00 and 17:30-21:00; closed Monday; Sunday lunch served on alternating weeks only.
closed_days: Mondays; closed on alternating Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run noodle counter where ordering is straightforward but staff likely speak little English, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will help.
vegan_detail: The cold noodles and tsukemen are built around a soy-sauce-based broth with pork chashu and other meat/fish-derived elements; no vegan offering is documented.
gf_detail: The house specialty is wheat noodles made by the adjacent Harada Seimen, served in a soy-sauce broth (soy sauce contains wheat). This is a wheat-noodle shop with no gluten-free option.
caveats: Sunday lunch runs only on alternating weeks; hours/closures can shift, so confirm via Instagram before visiting. Reading of the name is Bakkajin (ばっかじん).

roles: family-run noodle shop tied to Harada Seimen
bio.background: Opened September 2021 in the EBBA VILLAGE building right beside the Harada Seimen noodle factory in Eba Sakaemachi. It was started by a relative of the Harada Seimen family, with the storefront serving customers and rental units occupying the back of the building.
bio.specialty: Hiroshima cold noodles made with Harada Seimen's own wheat noodles, plus evening izakaya fare
bio.philosophy: Showcasing the family's own Harada Seimen noodles in a friendly, no-frills counter setting.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the broth runs intensely savory and soy-sauce-forward; when one diner found it too salty, the smiling staff repeatedly offered to add more spice rather than dilute it.
A bowl runs about 1,150 yen, roughly 350 yen cheaper than the noodles made from the same Harada Seimen supply served at the well-known Shinkaen.
Visitors describe the kitchen as run by a few cheerful women whose friendliness is part of the draw.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu centers on wheat noodles produced at the adjacent Harada Seimen factory, so the kitchen is a heavy wheat-flour environment.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature broth is described as intensely soy-sauce-forward; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are topped with pork chashu and served in a meat/fish-and-soy-based broth; no plant-only preparation is documented.
